topics.title: Rectangular Coordinate System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rectangular Coordinate System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rectangular Coordinate System (Cartesian Coordinate System)**
> A coordinate system in three-dimensional space defined by **three mutually perpendicular axes** ($x$, $y$, and $z$) that intersect at a common point called the **origin** ($O$). Any point $P$ in space is uniquely described by an ordered triplet $(x, y, z)$ representing its signed perpendicular distances from the coordinate planes $YOZ$, $XOZ$, and $XOY$ respectively.

The system is termed *rectangular* (or *orthogonal*) because the basis vectors are of unit length and intersect at right angles, forming a rectangular volume element $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$. It is the most fundamental and widely used coordinate system in vector analysis and electromagnetic field theory.

## 1.2 The Three Reference Planes

| Plane | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $xy$-plane | $z = 0$ | Horizontal reference plane |
| $yz$-plane | $x = 0$ | Lateral reference plane |
| $xz$-plane | $y = 0$ | Vertical reference plane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The rectangular coordinate system is the *base system* against which all other coordinate systems (cylindrical and spherical) are compared. Mastery of vector operations in this system is a prerequisite for the divergence, gradient, and curl operators covered later in the module.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Corner of a Room**
> Imagine standing at the **corner of a rectangular room** where two walls and the floor meet. The corner itself is the **origin** ($O$).
> - The edge along the floor going to your right is the **$x$-axis**.
> - The edge along the floor going forward (away from you) is the **$y$-axis**.
> - The edge going vertically up the wall is the **$z$-axis**.
>
> Now imagine an ant crawling on the opposite wall. To tell someone exactly where the ant is, you only need **three numbers** — its distance along the floor from one wall, its distance from the other wall, and how high up the wall it has climbed. Those three numbers are the **rectangular coordinates** $(x, y, z)$ of the ant.
>
> This simple "three-measurement" idea is the foundation upon which all of electromagnetic field theory is built, because every field quantity (electric field $\vec{E}$, magnetic field $\vec{H}$, etc.) ultimately needs three numbers to be specified at every point in 3D space.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Number of dimensions:** $\mathbf{3}$ (spatial)
- **Number of unit vectors:** $\mathbf{3}$ ($\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_y$, $\hat{a}_z$)
- **Orthogonality condition:** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$ where $\delta_{ij}$ is the **Kronecker delta** ($\mathbf{1}$ if $i=j$, $\mathbf{0}$ otherwise)
- **Handedness:** **Right-handed** system is the KTU standard (follows the right-hand rule).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Basis Vectors (Unit Vectors)

A point in rectangular coordinates is anchored by three **unit vectors**:

$$\hat{a}_x, \quad \hat{a}_y, \quad \hat{a}_z$$

**Key Properties (the 'Why' and 'How'):**
- They are **constant in direction and magnitude** everywhere in space. Unlike cylindrical or spherical unit vectors, $\hat{a}_x$ in Texas points in the exact same direction as $\hat{a}_x$ in Kerala. This is one of the most exam-favoured properties.
- They are **mutually orthogonal**: any two distinct unit vectors have zero dot product.
- They satisfy the **right-hand rule**: $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ (cyclic permutation).

## 2.2 Position Vector and Vector Representation

A **position vector** $\vec{r}$ extends from the origin $O$ to a point $P(x, y, z)$:

$$\vec{r} = x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z$$

A general vector $\vec{A}$ with components $A_x$, $A_y$, $A_z$ is written as:

$$\vec{A} = A_x\,\hat{a}_x + A_y\,\hat{a}_y + A_z\,\hat{a}_z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why rectangular coordinates matter in engineering:** Nearly all simulation software (ANSYS Maxwell, COMSOL, HFSS) discretizes space into rectangular voxels for FDTD/FEM solvers. Mastering this system is essential for understanding how Maxwell's equations are numerically solved.

## 2.3 Differential Length, Surface, and Volume Elements

These differential elements are the *building blocks* of every integral in vector calculus.

| Element | Differential Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Length | $d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_x\,dx + \hat{a}_y\,dy + \hat{a}_z\,dz$ | Infinitesimal displacement vector |
| Volume | $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ | Infinitesimal rectangular box |

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface elements** depend on orientation. The three fundamental surfaces with outward normals are:
> - $d\vec{S} = dy\,dz\,\hat{a}_x$ (face perpendicular to $x$-axis)
> - $d\vec{S} = dx\,dz\,\hat{a}_y$ (face perpendicular to $y$-axis)
> - $d\vec{S} = dx\,dy\,\hat{a}_z$ (face perpendicular to $z$-axis)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Position vector | $\vec{r} = x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z$ | m |
| Magnitude of $\vec{r}$ | $\vert \vec{r} \vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ | m |
| Vector magnitude | $\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$ | (varies) |
| Dot product | $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_x B_x + A_y B_y + A_z B_z$ | scalar |
| Cross product (determinant form) | $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}$ | vector |
| Distance between two points | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$ | m |
| Differential length | $d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_x\,dx + \hat{a}_y\,dy + \hat{a}_z\,dz$ | m |
| Differential volume | $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ | m$^3$ |
| Orthogonality | $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$ | dimensionless |
| Cyclic unit vector product | $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rectangular coordinate system underpins:
- **Antenna design:** Linear dipole antennas lie along $\hat{a}_z$; field patterns are derived in $(x, y, z)$.
- **PCB layout:** Circuit traces follow $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$ directions for clean EM coupling analysis.
- **Waveguide analysis:** Rectangular waveguides (TE$_{mn}$/TM$_{mn}$ modes) are intrinsically rectangular.
- **Image processing / Medical imaging (MRI/CT):** Voxel data is stored in a rectangular grid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnitude of the Position Vector

**Statement:** Derive the magnitude of the position vector $\vec{r}$ from the origin to point $P(x, y, z)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the position vector definition.**

$$
\vec{r} = x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z
$$

This is the vector from the origin $O(0,0,0)$ to the point $P(x, y, z)$. The components $x$, $y$, $z$ are the projections of $\vec{r}$ onto the three coordinate axes.

**Step 2 — Apply the magnitude formula $\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{A}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{r} \vert^2 &= \vec{r} \cdot \vec{r} \\
&= (x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z) \cdot (x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the dot product using distributivity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{r} \cdot \vec{r} &= x^2(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x) + y^2(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y) + z^2(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z) \\
&\quad + 2xy(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y) + 2yz(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z) + 2zx(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orthogonality condition** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$. All cross terms vanish because distinct unit vectors are perpendicular.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z &= 1 \\
\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and take the square root.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{r} \vert^2 &= x^2 + y^2 + z^2 \\
\vert \vec{r} \vert &= \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The distance of a point from the origin in rectangular coordinates is the Euclidean norm of its coordinate triplet. This result will be reused in the conversion to spherical coordinates later in the module.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Distance Between Two Points

**Statement:** Derive the distance $d$ between two points $P_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2, z_2)$.

**Step 1 — Express the two position vectors.**

$$
\vec{r}_1 = x_1\,\hat{a}_x + y_1\,\hat{a}_y + z_1\,\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\vec{r}_2 = x_2\,\hat{a}_x + y_2\,\hat{a}_y + z_2\,\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 2 — Form the difference vector** $\vec{r}_{12} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$ (vector from $P_1$ to $P_2$).

$$
\vec{r}_{12} = (x_2 - x_1)\,\hat{a}_x + (y_2 - y_1)\,\hat{a}_y + (z_2 - z_1)\,\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude of $\vec{r}_{12}$** using the formula derived in Section 3.1.

$$
d = \vert \vec{r}_{12} \vert = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** This is the 3D generalization of the Pythagorean theorem. In 2D, $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$; in 3D, an additional $\Delta z^2$ term appears.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cross Product via Determinant

**Statement:** Derive the component form of $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ using the cyclic orthogonality of the rectangular unit vectors.

**Step 1 — Write the two vectors.**

$$
\vec{A} = A_x\,\hat{a}_x + A_y\,\hat{a}_y + A_z\,\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\vec{B} = B_x\,\hat{a}_x + B_y\,\hat{a}_y + B_z\,\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the distributive law of cross product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{A} \times \vec{B} &= A_x B_x(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x) + A_x B_y(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y) + A_x B_z(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_z) \\
&\quad + A_y B_x(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x) + A_y B_y(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_y) + A_y B_z(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z) \\
&\quad + A_z B_x(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x) + A_z B_y(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_y) + A_z B_z(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply unit vector cross product identities.**

$$
\hat{a}_i \times \hat{a}_i = 0 \quad \text{(self cross = zero vector)}
$$

$$
\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z, \quad \hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x, \quad \hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y
$$

$$
\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x = -\hat{a}_z, \quad \hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_y = -\hat{a}_x, \quad \hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_z = -\hat{a}_y
$$

**Step 4 — Group the surviving terms by unit vector.**

$$
\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = (A_y B_z - A_z B_y)\,\hat{a}_x + (A_z B_x - A_x B_z)\,\hat{a}_y + (A_x B_y - A_y B_x)\,\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 5 — Write compactly as a determinant.**

$$
\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this form preferred?** The determinant is far less error-prone in exams than expanding 9 terms by hand. KTU examiners reward clean, efficient notation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Coordinate System Foundation

The rectangular coordinate system serves as the *root* of the entire vector analysis framework used in Electromagnetic Theory. The diagram below shows how the concepts build upon each other.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RECTANGULAR COORDINATE SYSTEM] --> B[Three Orthogonal Axes]
    A --> C[Three Unit Vectors]
    A --> D[Position Vector]
    A --> E[Differential Elements]
    B --> B1[X AXIS]
    B --> B2[Y AXIS]
    B --> B3[Z AXIS]
    C --> C1[AX HAT - along x]
    C --> C2[AY HAT - along y]
    C --> C3[AZ HAT - along z]
    D --> D1[Vector from Origin to Point P]
    D --> D2[Components x y z]
    E --> E1[Differential Length dl]
    E --> E2[Differential Volume dV]
    A --> F[Vector Operations]
    F --> F1[Dot Product - scalar]
    F --> F2[Cross Product - vector]
    F2 --> F3[Cyclic Rule AX cross AY equals AZ]
    A --> G[Foundation for Cylindrical Coordinates]
    A --> H[Foundation for Spherical Coordinates]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Steps to Specify a Point in 3D Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Define Origin O] --> S2[Step 2: Draw three perpendicular axes]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify three reference planes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Measure perpendicular distances]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Form ordered triplet x y z]
    S5 --> S6[Point P located uniquely]
    S1 --> N1[O at 0 0 0]
    S2 --> N2[X Y Z axes]
    S3 --> N3[XY plane YZ plane XZ plane]
    S4 --> N4[x from YZ plane, y from XZ plane, z from XY plane]
    S5 --> N5[Coordinates x y z]
    S6 --> N6[Unique location in 3D space]
```

## 4.3 Vector Component Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Vector A in 3D space] --> COMP[Resolve into components]
    COMP --> AX[AX along AX HAT]
    COMP --> AY[AY along AY HAT]
    COMP --> AZ[AZ along AZ HAT]
    AX --> RECOMB[Vector A equals AX AX HAT plus AY AY HAT plus AZ AZ HAT]
    AY --> RECOMB
    AZ --> RECOMB
    RECOMB --> MAG[Magnitude equals square root of AX squared plus AY squared plus AZ squared]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** Each block above corresponds to a single mathematical operation from Section 3. Trace from "Vector A" down to "Magnitude" to visualize the full decomposition pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Rectangular Coordinate System. Mention its basis vectors and their orthogonality property. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Rectangular (Cartesian) Coordinate System is a three-dimensional orthogonal coordinate system consisting of three mutually perpendicular axes — the $x$-axis, $y$-axis, and $z$-axis — intersecting at a common point called the **origin** $O$. Any point $P$ in space is uniquely described by the ordered triplet $(x, y, z)$, where $x$, $y$, $z$ are the perpendicular distances from the $YOZ$, $XOZ$, and $XOY$ planes respectively.

The system is built upon three unit vectors $\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_y$, $\hat{a}_z$ which are:
- **Mutually orthogonal:** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$
- **Of unit magnitude:** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_i = 1$
- Combined via the Kronecker delta: $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$

The system follows the **right-hand rule** with cyclic permutation $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2: State the differential length and volume elements in the rectangular coordinate system. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The differential length element is the elementary displacement vector expressed as:

$$
d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_x\,dx + \hat{a}_y\,dy + \hat{a}_z\,dz
$$

The differential volume element is the infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped (cuboid) defined by the three orthogonal edges $dx$, $dy$, $dz$:

$$
dV = dx\,dy\,dz
$$

These elements are foundational for performing line, surface, and volume integrals of vector fields such as $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ in later modules. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Vector Operations and Distance Calculation

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Given two vectors** $\vec{A} = 3\,\hat{a}_x + 2\,\hat{a}_y - \hat{a}_z$ and $\vec{B} = \hat{a}_x - 2\,\hat{a}_y + 4\,\hat{a}_z$:

**(a)** Compute $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ and $\vert \vec{A} \vert$, $\vert \vec{B} \vert$. Hence find the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ using the determinant method and verify the cyclic unit vector properties. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Compute the dot product.**

$$
\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(1) + (2)(-2) + (-1)(4)
$$

$$
\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 3 - 4 - 4 = -5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitudes.**

$$
\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742
$$

$$
\vert \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + (-2)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 16} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the angle formula** $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \, \vert \vec{B} \vert}$.

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{-5}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{21}} = \frac{-5}{\sqrt{294}} = \frac{-5}{7\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{-5}{17.146} \approx -0.2917
$$

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}(-0.2917) \approx 106.97^\circ
$$

> **Result:** $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = -5$, $\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{14}$, $\vert \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{21}$, $\theta \approx 106.97^\circ$. The obtuse angle confirms that $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ point in generally opposite directions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Set up the determinant.**

$$
\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ 3 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 4 \end{vmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand along the first row.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{A} \times \vec{B} &= \hat{a}_x \bigl[(2)(4) - (-1)(-2)\bigr] - \hat{a}_y \bigl[(3)(4) - (-1)(1)\bigr] + \hat{a}_z \bigl[(3)(-2) - (2)(1)\bigr] \\
&= \hat{a}_x [8 - 2] - \hat{a}_y [12 + 1] + \hat{a}_z [-6 - 2] \\
&= 6\,\hat{a}_x - 13\,\hat{a}_y - 8\,\hat{a}_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify using the formula** $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = (A_y B_z - A_z B_y)\hat{a}_x + (A_z B_x - A_x B_z)\hat{a}_y + (A_x B_y - A_y B_x)\hat{a}_z$:

- $x$-component: $(2)(4) - (-1)(-2) = 8 - 2 = 6$ ✓
- $y$-component: $(-1)(1) - (3)(4) = -1 - 12 = -13$ ✓
- $z$-component: $(3)(-2) - (2)(1) = -6 - 2 = -8$ ✓

**Step 4 — Verify orthogonality of cross product** by computing $\vec{A} \cdot (\vec{A} \times \vec{B})$:

$$
\vec{A} \cdot (\vec{A} \times \vec{B}) = (3)(6) + (2)(-13) + (-1)(-8) = 18 - 26 + 8 = 0
$$

Since the dot product is zero, $\vec{A} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{A}$, confirming the fundamental property. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B: Position Vector and Coordinate Transformations

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**A point $P$ has rectangular coordinates $(3, 4, 12)$ in metres.**

**(a)** Find the position vector $\vec{r}$ from the origin to $P$, its magnitude $\vert \vec{r} \vert$, and the unit vector $\hat{a}_r$ along $\vec{r}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the distance from $P$ to point $Q(-1, 2, 5)$ and the unit vector along the line $PQ$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — Write the position vector.**

$$
\vec{r} = 3\,\hat{a}_x + 4\,\hat{a}_y + 12\,\hat{a}_z \quad \text{(metres)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{r} \vert &= \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 12^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} \\
&= \sqrt{169} \\
&= 13 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unit vector** $\hat{a}_r = \dfrac{\vec{r}}{\vert \vec{r} \vert}$.

$$
\hat{a}_r = \frac{1}{13}(3\,\hat{a}_x + 4\,\hat{a}_y + 12\,\hat{a}_z) = \frac{3}{13}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{4}{13}\,\hat{a}_y + \frac{12}{13}\,\hat{a}_z
$$

> **Result:** $\vec{r} = 3\,\hat{a}_x + 4\,\hat{a}_y + 12\,\hat{a}_z$ m, $\vert \vec{r} \vert = 13$ m, $\hat{a}_r = \frac{3}{13}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{4}{13}\,\hat{a}_y + \frac{12}{13}\,\hat{a}_z$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Position vectors of both points.**

$$
\vec{r}_P = 3\,\hat{a}_x + 4\,\hat{a}_y + 12\,\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\vec{r}_Q = -\hat{a}_x + 2\,\hat{a}_y + 5\,\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 2 — Form the difference vector** $\vec{r}_{PQ} = \vec{r}_Q - \vec{r}_P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{r}_{PQ} &= (-1 - 3)\,\hat{a}_x + (2 - 4)\,\hat{a}_y + (5 - 12)\,\hat{a}_z \\
&= -4\,\hat{a}_x - 2\,\hat{a}_y - 7\,\hat{a}_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance** $d = \vert \vec{r}_{PQ} \vert$:

$$
d = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-2)^2 + (-7)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4 + 49} = \sqrt{69} \approx 8.307 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the unit vector** $\hat{a}_{PQ} = \dfrac{\vec{r}_{PQ}}{d}$:

$$
\hat{a}_{PQ} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{69}}(-4\,\hat{a}_x - 2\,\hat{a}_y - 7\,\hat{a}_z)
$$

$$
\hat{a}_{PQ} \approx -0.4817\,\hat{a}_x - 0.2408\,\hat{a}_y - 0.8430\,\hat{a}_z
$$

> **Result:** Distance $= \sqrt{69} \approx 8.307$ m; $\hat{a}_{PQ} = \dfrac{-4\,\hat{a}_x - 2\,\hat{a}_y - 7\,\hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{69}}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign errors in distance formula:** When computing the distance, students often write $P - Q$ instead of $Q - P$ and then *square* it (which hides the error). Always check whether the difference vector's direction matches the physical setup.
> 2. **Unit vector normalization:** Forgetting to divide by the magnitude when computing $\hat{a}_r$ is the **#1 most common mark-losing error**. A unit vector must have magnitude exactly 1 — verify this as a sanity check.
> 3. **Cross product order:** $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} \neq \vec{B} \times \vec{A}$. Reversing the order negates the result. KTU examiners specifically test for this.
> 4. **Coordinate axis confusion:** $x$, $y$, $z$ axes are *fixed* in rectangular coordinates. Unlike polar coordinates, the unit vectors do not change with position. Mentioning this distinction in answers earns bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rectangular Coordinate System**

- **Origin:** $O(0, 0, 0)$ — common intersection of all three axes.
- **Axes:** $x$, $y$, $z$ are **mutually perpendicular** and form a **right-handed** system.
- **Unit vectors:** $\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_y$, $\hat{a}_z$ are **constant in direction** (this is the defining feature!).
- **Position vector:** $\vec{r} = x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z$
- **Magnitude:** $\vert \vec{r} \vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$
- **Distance formula:** $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2}$ (3D Pythagoras).
- **Orthogonality:** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$ (1 if $i=j$, 0 otherwise).
- **Cyclic cross product:** $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$.
- **Differential length:** $d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_x\,dx + \hat{a}_y\,dy + \hat{a}_z\,dz$.
- **Differential volume:** $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$.
- **Dot product:** $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_x B_x + A_y B_y + A_z B_z$ (scalar).
- **Cross product:** $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}$ (vector).
- **Always normalize** when computing a unit vector: $\hat{a} = \vec{A}/\vert \vec{A} \vert$.
- **Sanity check:** $\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$ — always verify unit vectors satisfy this.
- **Hand-rule check:** For any right-handed triple, $\hat{a}_i \times \hat{a}_j = +\hat{a}_k$ in cyclic order $(x \to y \to z \to x)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinate Systems - Representation of Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinate Systems - Representation of Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Coordinate Systems

In Electromagnetic Theory, physical quantities like electric fields, magnetic fields, and wave propagation are often distributed in three-dimensional space. To describe the position of a point in space unambiguously, we need a **coordinate system**. While the familiar **Cartesian (rectangular) coordinate system** $(x, y, z)$ suffices for many problems, several electromagnetic configurations possess **geometric symmetry** (e.g., a long charged wire is cylindrical, a point charge produces spherical symmetry). Exploiting such symmetry by using an appropriate coordinate system dramatically simplifies the governing equations (Laplace's, Poisson's, Maxwell's).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *represent a point in space* in **Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical** coordinate systems, convert fluently between them, and identify the corresponding **unit vectors**. This topic is foundational to vector calculus operations ($\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$, $\nabla \times \vec{H}$, $\nabla^2 V$) which differ in form for each system.

---

## 1.2 The Three Coordinate Systems — Formal Definitions

### (a) Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinate System
A point $P$ in space is represented by an **ordered triple** $(x, y, z)$ denoting the perpendicular distances from three mutually orthogonal planes (the $yz$, $xz$, and $xy$ planes respectively, intersecting at the origin $O$).

### (b) Cylindrical Coordinate System
A point $P$ is represented by $(\rho, \phi, z)$, where:
- $\rho$ = radial distance of the projection of $P$ on the $xy$-plane, measured from the origin (always **non-negative**, $\rho \geq 0$),
- $\phi$ = azimuthal angle measured from the positive $x$-axis toward the positive $y$-axis in the $xy$-plane ($0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$),
- $z$ = axial height along the $z$-axis (same as in Cartesian).

### (c) Spherical Coordinate System
A point $P$ is represented by $(r, \theta, \phi)$, where:
- $r$ = radial distance of $P$ from the origin ($r \geq 0$),
- $\theta$ = polar angle measured from the **positive $z$-axis** downward to the position vector $\vec{OP}$ ($0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$),
- $\phi$ = azimuthal angle measured from the positive $x$-axis toward the positive $y$-axis in the $xy$-plane ($0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention Used in KTU / Engineering Physics:**
> The symbol $\phi$ denotes the **azimuthal angle** in **both** cylindrical and spherical systems, while $\theta$ is the **polar (zenith) angle** — *exclusive to the spherical system*. This is the physics/mathematics convention (as opposed to the mathematics-only convention where $\theta$ is azimuthal and $\phi$ is polar).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Three Ways to Find Your Seat in a Stadium"**

Imagine a massive, circular stadium with multiple tiers, and you are trying to describe the location of a specific seat to a friend.

- **Cartesian (x, y, z):** You say, *"Walk **10 meters East**, then **5 meters North**, then climb to the **3rd-floor tier**."* This works, but the stadium isn't shaped like a box — it is **round**, so describing it with east/north/up feels unnatural.

- **Cylindrical ($\rho, \phi, z$):** You say, *"The seat is on the **15th row from the center (ρ)**, at an **angle of 60° from the main entrance (φ)**, on the **3rd-floor tier (z)**."* This is more natural for a **round, vertical building** like a stadium, water tower, or a long charged wire.

- **Spherical ($r, \theta, \phi$):** You say, *"The seat is **20 meters from the center spotlight (r)**, tilted **30° down from the ceiling (θ)**, and **60° around from the entrance (φ)**."* This is natural for a **dome-shaped** object like a planet, a soap bubble, or the radiation pattern around an antenna.

Each system is **mathematically equivalent** — they describe the **same 3D space** — but choosing the right system based on the **shape of the problem** makes the math far simpler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in This Topic:**
> - Angle is measured in **radians** (rad) for mathematical derivations.
> - Full revolution = $2\pi$ rad = $360°$.
> - Conversion: $180° = \pi$ rad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of Each System — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Cylindrical Coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$

A point $P$ in space is located at the intersection of three orthogonal surfaces:

1. A **circular cylinder** of radius $\rho$ centered on the $z$-axis.
2. A **half-plane** containing the $z$-axis and making angle $\phi$ with the $xz$-plane.
3. A **plane parallel to the $xy$-plane** at height $z$.

**Why these three surfaces?** Three mutually perpendicular surfaces intersect at a unique point in 3D — this is the geometric basis of *any* orthogonal coordinate system.

The **position vector** of point $P$ in cylindrical coordinates is:
$$\vec{r} = \rho \, \hat{a}_\rho + z \, \hat{a}_z$$

The unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho$, $\hat{a}_\phi$, $\hat{a}_z$ form a **right-handed orthogonal triad**:
- $\hat{a}_z$ points along $+z$ direction.
- $\hat{a}_\rho$ points radially outward (perpendicular to the $z$-axis, in the direction of increasing $\rho$).
- $\hat{a}_\phi$ is tangential, in the direction of increasing $\phi$ (i.e., "wraps around" the $z$-axis).

> [!WARNING]
> **Crucial Difference from Cartesian:** The unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ are **position-dependent** — they change direction as you move through space. $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are **constant everywhere**. This is why vector calculus in cylindrical and spherical systems involves extra terms when differentiating unit vectors.

---

### 2.1.2 Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$

A point $P$ is located at the intersection of:

1. A **sphere** of radius $r$ centered at the origin.
2. A **cone** (half-angle $\theta$) with its apex at the origin and axis along the $z$-axis.
3. A **half-plane** containing the $z$-axis and making angle $\phi$ with the $xz$-plane.

The **position vector** of point $P$ in spherical coordinates is:
$$\vec{r} = r \, \hat{a}_r$$

The unit vectors $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ form a **right-handed orthogonal triad**:
- $\hat{a}_r$ points radially outward.
- $\hat{a}_\theta$ points in the direction of **increasing $\theta$** (i.e., downward from the north pole).
- $\hat{a}_\phi$ is tangential, wrapping around the $z$-axis (same as cylindrical $\hat{a}_\phi$).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master This Table — It Covers 90% of KTU 2-Mark and 7-Mark Questions on This Topic.**

### 2.2.1 Conversion Between Systems

| **From → To** | **Conversion Equations** | **Range of Variables** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical → Cartesian** | $x = \rho \cos\phi$, $\;y = \rho \sin\phi$, $\;z = z$ | $\rho \geq 0$, $\;0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$ |
| **Cartesian → Cylindrical** | $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $\;\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$, $\;z = z$ | $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| **Spherical → Cartesian** | $x = r \sin\theta \cos\phi$, $\;y = r \sin\theta \sin\phi$, $\;z = r \cos\theta$ | $r \geq 0$, $\;0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$, $\;0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$ |
| **Cartesian → Spherical** | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$, $\;\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}}\right)$, $\;\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$ | All real $x, y, z$ |
| **Spherical → Cylindrical** | $\rho = r \sin\theta$, $\;\phi = \phi$, $\;z = r \cos\theta$ | (use $\phi$ common) |
| **Cylindrical → Spherical** | $r = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2}$, $\;\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho}{z}\right)$, $\;\phi = \phi$ | (for $z > 0$; adjust for $z < 0$) |

### 2.2.2 Unit Vector Transformations

| **System** | **Unit Vectors** | **Mutual Orthogonality** |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ (constant) | $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, etc. |
| Cylindrical | $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_z$ (position-dependent) | $\hat{a}_\rho \times \hat{a}_\phi = \hat{a}_z$, etc. |
| Spherical | $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ (position-dependent) | $\hat{a}_r \times \hat{a}_\theta = \hat{a}_\phi$, etc. |

| **Transformation** | **Expressions** |
|---|---|
| **Cartesian → Cylindrical** | $\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y$, $\;\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y$ |
| **Cartesian → Spherical** | $\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y + \cos\theta \, \hat{a}_z$ |
| | $\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y - \sin\theta \, \hat{a}_z$ |
| | $\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y$ |

### 2.2.3 Differential Elements (Used in Volume / Surface / Line Integrals)

| **Element** | **Cartesian** | **Cylindrical** | **Spherical** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length $d\vec{l}$ | $dx \, \hat{a}_x + dy \, \hat{a}_y + dz \, \hat{a}_z$ | $d\rho \, \hat{a}_\rho + \rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi + dz \, \hat{a}_z$ | $dr \, \hat{a}_r + r \, d\theta \, \hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi$ |
| Area $d\vec{s}$ | $dy\,dz \, \hat{a}_x + dx\,dz \, \hat{a}_y + dx\,dy \, \hat{a}_z$ | $\rho \, d\phi \, dz \, \hat{a}_\rho + d\rho \, dz \, \hat{a}_\phi + \rho \, d\rho \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_z$ | $r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r + r\sin\theta \, dr \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\theta + r \, dr \, d\theta \, \hat{a}_\phi$ |
| Volume $dv$ | $dx\,dy\,dz$ | $\rho \, d\rho \, d\phi \, dz$ | $r^2 \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **cylindrical volume element** $\rho \, d\rho \, d\phi \, dz$ is essential when computing the **capacitance of a coaxial cable** or the **magnetic field inside a solenoid**.
> - The **spherical volume element** $r^2 \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi$ is fundamental in **antenna radiation pattern analysis**, **radar cross-section calculations**, and computing the **electric field of a point charge**.

---

## 2.3 Why the Unit Vectors Differ in Nature

### The 'Why' Behind Position-Dependent Unit Vectors

In Cartesian coordinates, the surfaces of constant coordinate ($x = $ const, $y = $ const, $z = $ const) are **planes**. Planes are *flat* — they never curve. Hence, the perpendicular direction to a plane is the same everywhere.

In cylindrical coordinates, the surface $\rho = $ const is a **cylinder** (curved), and in spherical coordinates, $r = $ const is a **sphere** (curved). Because these surfaces curve, the **outward normal direction changes as you move along the surface** — therefore the unit vectors are functions of position.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test:** If you can visualize the coordinate surface, the unit vector is along its outward normal. For cylindrical: $\hat{a}_\rho \perp$ cylinder, $\hat{a}_\phi \perp$ half-plane, $\hat{a}_z \perp$ horizontal plane. For spherical: $\hat{a}_r \perp$ sphere, $\hat{a}_\theta \perp$ cone, $\hat{a}_\phi \perp$ half-plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cylindrical → Cartesian Conversion

**Given:** A point $P$ has cylindrical coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$.

**Goal:** Express the Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in terms of $(\rho, \phi, z)$.

### Step 1 — Project P onto the xy-plane
The point $P$ in 3D space has a vertical projection $P'$ onto the $xy$-plane. The distance from origin $O$ to $P'$ is exactly $\rho$ (definition of cylindrical coordinate). The angle $\angle P'OX$ (where $OX$ is the positive $x$-axis) is $\phi$.

### Step 2 — Use basic right-triangle trigonometry
In the $xy$-plane, $P'$ has Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$ with:
- Hypotenuse: $OP' = \rho$
- Angle with $x$-axis: $\phi$

Therefore:
$$x = \rho \cos\phi$$
$$y = \rho \sin\phi$$

### Step 3 — The z-coordinate is invariant
Since the cylindrical system shares the $z$-axis with the Cartesian system (the height of $P$ above the $xy$-plane is identical in both):
$$z = z$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{x = \rho \cos\phi, \quad y = \rho \sin\phi, \quad z = z}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cartesian → Cylindrical Conversion

**Given:** A point $P$ has Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$.

**Goal:** Find $(\rho, \phi, z)$.

### Step 1 — Square and add x and y
$$x^2 + y^2 = (\rho \cos\phi)^2 + (\rho \sin\phi)^2 = \rho^2(\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) = \rho^2$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$.

### Step 2 — Take the positive square root
$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

(The positive root is chosen because $\rho \geq 0$ by definition.)

### Step 3 — Find the angle $\phi$
$$\tan\phi = \frac{y}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

The correct quadrant for $\phi$ must be determined by the signs of $x$ and $y$ (i.e., use $\text{atan2}(y, x)$ in practice).

### Step 4 — z-coordinate
$$z = z \quad \text{(invariant)}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right), \quad z = z}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Spherical → Cartesian Conversion

**Given:** A point $P$ has spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$.

**Goal:** Express $(x, y, z)$ in terms of $(r, \theta, \phi)$.

### Step 1 — Project OP onto the xy-plane
Let $P'$ be the projection of $P$ onto the $xy$-plane. The distance $OP'$ is the **perpendicular component** of $\vec{OP}$ with respect to the $z$-axis.

In the right triangle $OPP'$ (right-angled at $P'$):
- Hypotenuse: $OP = r$
- Angle at $O$ (between $OP$ and $z$-axis): $\theta$
- Adjacent side: $OP' = r \sin\theta$
- Opposite side: $PP' = z = r \cos\theta$

### Step 2 — Express x and y using φ
$P'$ is at distance $\rho = r\sin\theta$ from the origin, at azimuthal angle $\phi$ in the $xy$-plane. Therefore:
$$x = \rho \cos\phi = r \sin\theta \cos\phi$$
$$y = \rho \sin\phi = r \sin\theta \sin\phi$$

### Step 3 — The z-coordinate
$$z = r \cos\theta$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{x = r \sin\theta \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\theta \sin\phi, \quad z = r \cos\theta}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cartesian → Spherical Conversion

**Given:** Cartesian $(x, y, z)$.

**Goal:** Find $(r, \theta, \phi)$.

### Step 1 — Compute r (the 3D distance from origin)
Square and add the conversions from 3.3:

$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = (r\sin\theta\cos\phi)^2 + (r\sin\theta\sin\phi)^2 + (r\cos\theta)^2$$

$$= r^2 \sin^2\theta (\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) + r^2 \cos^2\theta$$

$$= r^2 \sin^2\theta + r^2 \cos^2\theta = r^2(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) = r^2$$

Therefore:
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the polar angle θ
From $z = r\cos\theta$:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{z}{r} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the azimuthal angle φ
From the cylindrical conversion (Section 3.2), the $\phi$ coordinate is identical in both cylindrical and spherical systems:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}}\right), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** Express the point $P(-3, 4, 12)$ in **(a)** Cylindrical and **(b)** Spherical coordinates.

### Part (a) — Cylindrical Coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$

**Step 1:** Compute $\rho$:
$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\phi$:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{-3}\right)$$

Since $x < 0$ and $y > 0$, the point is in the **second quadrant**:
$$\phi = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 180° - 53.13° = 126.87° = 2.2143 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** $z = 12$ (invariant)

$$\boxed{P \equiv (5, \, 126.87°, \, 12) \text{ in cylindrical coordinates}}$$

### Part (b) — Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$

**Step 1:** Compute $r$:
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\theta$:
$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{r}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) = 22.62° = 0.3948 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** $\phi = 126.87°$ (same as cylindrical, since $\phi$ is common)

$$\boxed{P \equiv (13, \, 22.62°, \, 126.87°) \text{ in spherical coordinates}}$$

**Verification (Spherical → Cartesian):**
$$x = 13 \sin(22.62°)\cos(126.87°) = 13(0.3846)(-0.6) = -3 \; \checkmark$$
$$y = 13 \sin(22.62°)\sin(126.87°) = 13(0.3846)(0.8) = 4 \; \checkmark$$
$$z = 13 \cos(22.62°) = 13(0.9231) = 12 \; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — "When to Use What" (High-Yield for KTU Problems)

> [!TIP]
> This matrix tells you **which coordinate system to choose** based on the symmetry of the source/field configuration. This is a frequently-asked KTU question.

| **Symmetry of the Physical Problem** | **Recommended System** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| Slab, rectangular box, parallel plates | **Cartesian** | Parallel-plate capacitor field |
| Long straight wire, coaxial cable, infinite cylinder | **Cylindrical** | $\vec{E}$ of an infinite line charge, magnetic field of a long current-carrying conductor |
| Point charge, sphere, dipole, antenna | **Spherical** | $\vec{E}$ of a point charge, radiation from a Hertzian dipole |

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Unit Vector Transformation (Cylindrical from Cartesian)

We need to express $\hat{a}_\rho$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ in terms of $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$.

By the geometry of the $xy$-plane:
- $\hat{a}_\rho$ makes angle $\phi$ with $\hat{a}_x$.
- $\hat{a}_\phi$ is perpendicular to $\hat{a}_\rho$, rotated 90° in the direction of increasing $\phi$.

Decomposing onto $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$:

$$\hat{a}_\rho = (\hat{a}_\rho \cdot \hat{a}_x) \, \hat{a}_x + (\hat{a}_\rho \cdot \hat{a}_y) \, \hat{a}_y = \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y$$

Since $\hat{a}_\phi$ is $\hat{a}_\rho$ rotated by $+90°$ (i.e., $\phi \to \phi + 90°$):

$$\hat{a}_\phi = \cos(\phi + 90°) \, \hat{a}_x + \sin(\phi + 90°) \, \hat{a}_y = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Right-Handed System):** $\hat{a}_\rho \times \hat{a}_\phi = \hat{a}_z$
>
> 
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \hat{a}_\rho \times \hat{a}_\phi &= (\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y) \times (-\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y) \\
> &= \cos^2\phi \, (\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y) - \sin^2\phi \, (\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x) \\
> &= \cos^2\phi \, \hat{a}_z + \sin^2\phi \, \hat{a}_z \\
> &= (\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) \, \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z \; \checkmark
> \end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.8 Derivation: Position Vector in Each System (Vector $\vec{r}$)

### Cylindrical Position Vector
The position vector of $P$ has magnitude $r = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2}$ and direction along $\hat{a}_\rho$ (in the $\rho$-$z$ plane). Its components along the cylindrical unit vectors are:

$$\vec{r} = \rho \, \hat{a}_\rho + z \, \hat{a}_z$$

(Note: There is no $\hat{a}_\phi$ component because the position vector lies in the $\rho$-$z$ plane, not in the $\phi$ direction.)

### Spherical Position Vector
By definition, the position vector points radially outward from origin. Its entire magnitude is in the $\hat{a}_r$ direction:

$$\vec{r} = r \, \hat{a}_r$$

### Cartesian Verification
In Cartesian, $\vec{r} = x \, \hat{a}_x + y \, \hat{a}_y + z \, \hat{a}_z$. Substituting the cylindrical→Cartesian conversions and unit vector transformations should reproduce this — this is left as a student exercise and is a common KTU 7-mark derivation question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Cylindrical Coordinate System Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CARTESIAN REFERENCE: Point P in 3D Space"]
    B["x-axis: East"]
    C["y-axis: North"]
    D["z-axis: Up (Vertical)"]
    E["Project P onto xy-plane: P-prime"]
    F["rho = perpendicular distance from z-axis to P"]
    G["phi = angle from +x axis to OP-prime in xy-plane"]
    H["z = vertical height of P above xy-plane"]
    I["P coordinates: rho, phi, z"]
    J["Cylinder of radius rho passes through P"]
    K["Half-plane through z-axis at angle phi contains P"]
    L["Horizontal plane at height z contains P"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    A --> H
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J
    I --> K
    I --> L
```

**Reading the Diagram:** A point $P$ in cylindrical coordinates lies on the intersection of three mutually perpendicular surfaces — a **cylinder** (constant $\rho$), a **half-plane** (constant $\phi$), and a **horizontal plane** (constant $z$).

---

## 4.2 Diagram: Spherical Coordinate System Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SPHERICAL REFERENCE: Point P in 3D Space"]
    B["Origin O is the center"]
    C["Draw straight line OP of length r"]
    D["theta = angle from +z axis down to OP"]
    E["phi = angle from +x axis to projection of OP in xy-plane"]
    F["Sphere of radius r passes through P"]
    G["Cone with half-angle theta and z-axis as axis contains P"]
    H["Half-plane through z-axis at angle phi contains P"]
    I["P coordinates: r, theta, phi"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> I
    E --> I
    C --> I
    I --> F
    I --> G
    I --> H
```

**Reading the Diagram:** A point $P$ in spherical coordinates lies on the intersection of a **sphere** (constant $r$), a **cone** (constant $\theta$), and a **half-plane** (constant $\phi$).

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Functional Block — Conversion Engine Between Coordinate Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT COORDINATES"]
        IC["Cartesian: x, y, z"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["CONVERSION LOGIC BLOCKS"]
        P1["CYLINDRICAL BLOCK: rho = sqrt x2 + y2; phi = atan2 y, x"]
        P2["SPHERICAL BLOCK: r = sqrt x2+y2+z2; theta = acos z divide r; phi = atan2 y, x"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT COORDINATES"]
        OC1["Cylindrical: rho, phi, z"]
        OC2["Spherical: r, theta, phi"]
    end

    subgraph VALID["VALIDATION CHECKS"]
        V1["Check: rho greater or equal 0"]
        V2["Check: 0 less or equal phi less or equal 2 pi"]
        V3["Check: r greater or equal 0"]
        V4["Check: 0 less or equal theta less or equal pi"]
    end

    IC --> P1
    IC --> P2
    P1 --> OC1
    P2 --> OC2
    OC1 --> V1
    OC1 --> V2
    OC2 --> V3
    OC2 --> V4
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This flowchart shows the algorithmic flow for converting from Cartesian to cylindrical or spherical coordinates, with built-in validation checks on the range of each output variable — exactly what a KTU board examiner expects a student to "spell out" in a 7-mark derivation question.

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Unit Vector Orientation (Cylindrical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["UNIT VECTORS AT POINT P in CYLINDRICAL SYSTEM"]
    B["a-rho: points outward perpendicular to z-axis, in direction of increasing rho"]
    C["a-phi: tangent to circle of constant rho, in direction of increasing phi"]
    D["a-z: along positive z-axis, in direction of increasing z"]
    E["Right-Hand Rule: a-rho CROSS a-phi EQUALS a-z"]
    F["Position-Dependent: a-rho and a-phi change with location of P"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram: Unit Vector Orientation (Spherical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["UNIT VECTORS AT POINT P in SPHERICAL SYSTEM"]
    B["a-r: points outward along position vector, in direction of increasing r"]
    C["a-theta: tangent to meridian, points in direction of increasing theta, downward from +z"]
    D["a-phi: tangent to parallel, in direction of increasing phi around z-axis"]
    E["Right-Hand Rule: a-r CROSS a-theta EQUALS a-phi"]
    F["Position-Dependent: all three vary with location of P on the sphere"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram: Decision Flow — "Which Coordinate System Should I Use?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["What is the geometric shape of the source/field?"]
    Q --> S1["Shape is rectangular / planar / slab-like"]
    Q --> S2["Shape is cylindrical / rotational about a long axis"]
    Q --> S3["Shape is spherical / radial from a central point"]

    S1 --> R1["USE CARTESIAN: x, y, z; constant unit vectors; easy flux and gradient in plane geometry"]
    S2 --> R2["USE CYLINDRICAL: rho, phi, z; simplifies problems with line charges, coaxial cables, solenoids"]
    S3 --> R3["USE SPHERICAL: r, theta, phi; simplifies problems with point charges, spheres, antennas, dipoles"]
```

**Engineering Heuristic:** Always pick the coordinate system in which **two of the three coordinates are constants on the boundary of the problem**. Example: For a sphere of radius $R$, choose spherical coordinates with $r = R$ as the boundary — the other two coordinates ($\theta, \phi$) vary freely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the spherical coordinate system. Express the Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in terms of spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The spherical coordinate system represents a point $P$ in 3D space using the radial distance $r$ from the origin, the polar angle $\theta$ measured from the positive $z$-axis, and the azimuthal angle $\phi$ measured in the $xy$-plane from the positive $x$-axis.

**Conversion Equations (2 Marks):**
$$x = r \sin\theta \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\theta \sin\phi, \quad z = r \cos\theta$$

**Ranges:** $r \geq 0$, $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$, $0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Cartesian and cylindrical coordinate systems. What are the unit vectors in each system?**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Cartesian** | **Cylindrical** |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinates | $(x, y, z)$ | $(\rho, \phi, z)$ |
| Unit Vectors | $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ | $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_z$ |
| Nature of Unit Vectors | **Constant** everywhere (independent of position) | **Position-dependent** ($\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi$ vary with location) |
| Surfaces of Constant Coord. | Planes | Cylinder ($\rho$), half-plane ($\phi$), plane ($z$) |
| Best Used For | Planar / slab geometry | Cylindrical symmetry (line charge, coaxial cable) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two full questions (A or B).

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

#### **(a)** Derive the expressions for the cylindrical unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ in terms of the Cartesian unit vectors $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**
In the $xy$-plane, the cylindrical unit vector $\hat{a}_\rho$ points radially outward from the origin and makes an angle $\phi$ with the positive $x$-axis ($\hat{a}_x$).

**Step 2 — Projection of $\hat{a}_\rho$ onto Cartesian axes:**
The component of $\hat{a}_\rho$ along $\hat{a}_x$ is $\cos\phi$ (adjacent/hypotenuse in the right triangle).
The component of $\hat{a}_\rho$ along $\hat{a}_y$ is $\sin\phi$.

$$\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3 — $\hat{a}_\phi$ is $\hat{a}_\rho$ rotated by 90° counterclockwise** (direction of increasing $\phi$):

$$\hat{a}_\phi = \cos(\phi + 90°) \, \hat{a}_x + \sin(\phi + 90°) \, \hat{a}_y$$

Using $\cos(\phi + 90°) = -\sin\phi$ and $\sin(\phi + 90°) = \cos\phi$:

$$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (Right-Hand Rule):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{a}_\rho \times \hat{a}_\phi &= (\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y) \times (-\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y) \\
&= \cos^2\phi \, (\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y) + \sin^2\phi \, (\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x) \\
&= \cos^2\phi \, \hat{a}_z + \sin^2\phi \, \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the right-handed system. ✓

**Final Result (boxed):**
$$\boxed{\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y, \qquad \hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y}$$

#### **(b)** A point is located at Cartesian coordinates $P(2, -2, 5)$. Convert this point to **(i)** cylindrical and **(ii)** spherical coordinates. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x = 2$, $y = -2$, $z = 5$.

### (i) Cylindrical Coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$ — (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $\rho$:
$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\phi$ (point is in 4th quadrant since $x > 0$, $y < 0$):
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-2}{2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-1)$$

In the fourth quadrant: $\phi = -45° = -\pi/4$ rad $= 315° = 7\pi/4$ rad.

**Step 3:** $z = 5$.

$$\boxed{P \equiv (2\sqrt{2}, \, -45°, \, 5) \text{ in cylindrical coordinates}}$$

### (ii) Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$ — (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $r$:
$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 25} = \sqrt{33} \approx 5.745$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\theta$:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{z}{r} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{33}} \approx 0.8704$$
$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8704) \approx 29.50° \approx 0.5150 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** $\phi = -45°$ (same as cylindrical).

$$\boxed{P \equiv (\sqrt{33}, \, 29.50°, \, -45°) \text{ in spherical coordinates}}$$

**Verification (Spherical → Cartesian):**
$$x = \sqrt{33} \sin(29.50°)\cos(-45°) \approx 5.745 \times 0.4924 \times 0.7071 \approx 2.00 \; \checkmark$$
$$y = \sqrt{33} \sin(29.50°)\sin(-45°) \approx 5.745 \times 0.4924 \times (-0.7071) \approx -2.00 \; \checkmark$$
$$z = \sqrt{33} \cos(29.50°) \approx 5.745 \times 0.8704 \approx 5.00 \; \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the cylindrical coordinate system. Derive the conversion relations between Cartesian and cylindrical coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Diagram Description (1 Mark):** *Draw a 3D figure showing the $x$, $y$, $z$ axes, a point $P$ in space, its projection $P'$ on the $xy$-plane, the radial distance $\rho = OP'$, the azimuthal angle $\phi = \angle P'OX$, and the height $z = PP'$. Show the cylindrical unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_z$ at point $P$.*

**Cylindrical Coordinate Definition (1 Mark):** A point $P$ in space is described by the triple $(\rho, \phi, z)$, where:
- $\rho$ = distance from the $z$-axis to $P$ (radial distance), $\rho \geq 0$.
- $\phi$ = angle between the positive $x$-axis and the projection of $P$ on the $xy$-plane, $0 \leq \phi \leq 2\pi$.
- $z$ = height above the $xy$-plane (same as Cartesian $z$).

**Derivation of Cylindrical → Cartesian (2.5 Marks):**
Using the right-triangle geometry in the $xy$-plane with hypotenuse $\rho$ and angle $\phi$:
$$x = \rho \cos\phi, \quad y = \rho \sin\phi, \quad z = z$$

**Derivation of Cartesian → Cylindrical (2.5 Marks):**
Squaring and adding:
$$x^2 + y^2 = \rho^2 \cos^2\phi + \rho^2 \sin^2\phi = \rho^2$$
$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

Dividing:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{y}{x} \implies \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

(Use $\text{atan2}(y, x)$ to get the correct quadrant.)

$$z = z$$

#### **(b)** Two points are given in Cartesian coordinates: $A(1, 0, 0)$ and $B(0, 3, 4)$. Find the distance between them in **(i)** Cartesian and **(ii)** by first converting to spherical coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### (i) Distance in Cartesian (2.5 Marks)

$$d = \sqrt{(x_B - x_A)^2 + (y_B - y_A)^2 + (z_B - z_A)^2}$$

$$d = \sqrt{(0-1)^2 + (3-0)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 9 + 16} = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.099$$

### (ii) Distance via Spherical Coordinates (4.5 Marks)

**For Point A(1, 0, 0):**
$$r_A = \sqrt{1 + 0 + 0} = 1$$
$$\theta_A = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0}{1}\right) = 90° = \pi/2$$
$$\phi_A = \tan^{-1}(0/1) = 0° = 0 \text{ rad}$$
$A \equiv (1, \pi/2, 0)$ in spherical.

**For Point B(0, 3, 4):**
$$r_B = \sqrt{0 + 9 + 16} = 5$$
$$\cos\theta_B = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \implies \theta_B = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87° \approx 0.6435 \text{ rad}$$
$$\phi_B = \tan^{-1}(3/0) = 90° = \pi/2$$
$B \equiv (5, 36.87°, 90°)$ in spherical.

**Note:** Distance is an *invariant* — it is the same regardless of coordinate system. So the spherical coordinates are simply an alternate representation; the distance formula still yields the same answer.

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.099 \text{ units}}$$

This question tests whether students understand that **distance and physical quantities are coordinate-invariant**, even though their *components* change form.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\phi$ and $\theta$:** A frequent error is using $\theta$ as the azimuthal angle and $\phi$ as the polar angle (the "math convention"). In **electromagnetic theory / engineering physics**, the **physics convention is mandatory**: $\phi$ = azimuthal, $\theta$ = polar. Using the wrong convention loses **1–2 marks** consistently.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to choose the correct quadrant for $\phi$:** When $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$, blindly using this gives the **principal value** in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. Always check the signs of $x$ and $y$ to assign the correct quadrant. E.g., $P(-1, -1)$ has $\phi = -135°$ or $225°$, **not** $45°$.
>
> 3. **Treating $\rho$ as a vector:** $\rho$ is a *scalar* magnitude. The vector is $\rho \, \hat{a}_\rho$. Writing $\vec{r} = \rho$ is meaningless and loses marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting that unit vectors are position-dependent:** When asked to compute $\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$ or $\nabla \times \vec{H}$ in cylindrical/spherical coordinates, students often treat $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta$ as constants — this is **wrong** and leads to incorrect answers. Always remember $\partial \hat{a}_\rho / \partial \phi = \hat{a}_\phi$, etc.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $\phi$ in cylindrical and spherical:** Many students think $\phi_{\text{cylindrical}} \neq \phi_{\text{spherical}}$. In fact, they are **identical** — the azimuthal angle is the same in both systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every KTU Exam!**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] A point in 3D space can be uniquely described by an **ordered triple** in any of the three coordinate systems.
- [x] **Cartesian** $(x, y, z)$ → constant unit vectors, best for planar geometry.
- [x] **Cylindrical** $(\rho, \phi, z)$ → position-dependent unit vectors, best for line/axis-symmetric problems.
- [x] **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ → position-dependent unit vectors, best for point/radial-symmetric problems.

### 🔑 Conversion Mastery
- [x] **Cyl → Cart:** $x = \rho \cos\phi$, $y = \rho \sin\phi$, $z = z$.
- [x] **Cart → Cyl:** $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ (check quadrant), $z = z$.
- [x] **Sph → Cart:** $x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi$, $y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi$, $z = r\cos\theta$.
- [x] **Cart → Sph:** $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$, $\theta = \cos^{-1}(z/r)$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$.
- [x] **Cyl ↔ Sph:** $\rho = r\sin\theta$, $z = r\cos\theta$ (so $r = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2}$).

### 🔑 Ranges (Memorize!)
- [x] $\rho \in [0, \infty)$, $\phi \in [0, 2\pi]$, $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$.
- [x] $r \in [0, \infty)$, $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, $\phi \in [0, 2\pi]$.

### 🔑 Position Vectors
- [x] $\vec{r}_{Cart} = x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$.
- [x] $\vec{r}_{Cyl} = \rho\hat{a}_\rho + z\hat{a}_z$.
- [x] $\vec{r}_{Sph} = r\hat{a}_r$.

### 🔑 Unit Vector Tricks
- [x] $\hat{a}_z$ is common to **all three** systems.
- [x] $\hat{a}_\phi$ (cylindrical) = $\hat{a}_\phi$ (spherical).
- [x] Right-hand rule: $\hat{a}_\rho \times \hat{a}_\phi = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_r \times \hat{a}_\theta = \hat{a}_\phi$.

### 🔑 Engineering Heuristic
- [x] **Choose the coordinate system where the boundary surfaces are coordinate surfaces** (e.g., for a sphere of radius $R$, choose spherical with $r = R$ as the boundary).

### 🔑 Frequently Forgotten Formulas
- [x] Differential volume: $dv = \rho\, d\rho\, d\phi\, dz$ (cylindrical) = $r^2 \sin\theta \, dr\, d\theta\, d\phi$ (spherical).
- [x] Arc length on a circle of radius $\rho$: $dl = \rho \, d\phi$.
- [x] Arc length on a sphere of radius $r$ along a meridian: $dl = r \, d\theta$; along a parallel: $dl = r\sin\theta \, d\phi$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Vector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unit Vector # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In vector algebra, a **Unit Vector** is a vector whose magnitude (length) is exactly equal to **one (1)**. It is used purely to specify the **direction** of a physical quantity without contributing any magnitude information. By convention, a unit vector is denoted by placing a caret symbol (^) over the boldface vector symbol, for example $\hat{a}$ or $\hat{u}$. For any non-zero vector $\vec{A}$, its corresponding unit vector $\hat{a}_A$ is obtained by the **normalization** operation: $$\hat{a}_A = \frac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$$ In the Cartesian coordinate system, the three fundamental orthogonal unit vectors are: - $\hat{a}_x$ — directed along the positive **x-axis** - $\hat{a}_y$ — directed along the positive **y-axis** - $\hat{a}_z$ — directed along the positive **z-axis** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Electromagnetic Theory, unit vectors form the **foundational building block** for defining electric fields ($\vec{E}$), magnetic fields ($\vec{B}$), wave propagation directions ($\hat{k}$), and Poynting vectors ($\vec{S}$). Mastery of this topic is mandatory for solving all subsequent modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are giving directions to a friend in a new city. You say, "Walk **North** for **3 kilometers**." Notice the two distinct parts of your instruction: - **"North"** tells your friend **which way to face** — this is the **direction** (the role of a unit vector). - **"3 kilometers"** tells **how far** to walk — this is the **magnitude** (the role of the scalar multiplier). A unit vector is essentially the "**North**" part of that sentence — it carries **only directional information**. Its length is always fixed at exactly **1**, so it never changes how "big" a quantity is, only which way it points. For instance, a wind velocity of $10$ m/s blowing East is written as $\vec{V} = 10\,\hat{a}_E$. The unit vector $\hat{a}_E$ simply says "East," and the number $10$ gives the speed. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Summary:** Think of a unit vector as a **compass needle**. No matter how strong the magnetic field, the needle only points in one direction — it doesn't tell you the strength, only the orientation. The strength comes from multiplying it by a magnitude. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Magnitude of any unit vector:** $\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$ (dimensionless, pure number). - **Coordinate system:** Right-Handed Cartesian (RHC) — standard in all KTU problems. - **Orthogonality constant:** The dot product between any two **distinct** Cartesian unit vectors equals **0**. - **Self-dot product:** The dot product of any Cartesian unit vector with itself equals **1**. - **Notation variants:** Some textbooks use $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ instead of $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$. Both are accepted in KTU answer scripts. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The Three Pillars of Unit Vector Theory To fully understand unit vectors, you must internalize three core principles: ### Pillar 1: Direction Without Magnitude A unit vector $\hat{a}$ is dimensionless and its length is locked at **1**. It acts as a **directional tag** that gets attached to scalar magnitudes via scalar multiplication. For example, in electromagnetic wave propagation, the direction of travel is encoded by a unit vector $\hat{k}$, while the wave number $k$ provides the magnitude. ### Pillar 2: Normalization Procedure Any non-zero vector $\vec{A}$ can be decomposed into a **magnitude part** $\vert \vec{A} \vert$ and a **directional part** $\hat{a}_A$. The act of finding $\hat{a}_A$ is called **normalization** and is performed using the relation: $$\hat{a}_A = \frac{\vec{A}}{\sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}}$$ ### Pillar 3: Orthogonality in Cartesian Coordinates The Cartesian unit vectors $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are mutually perpendicular. This gives rise to two sets of fundamental identities: - **Dot products (orthonormality relations):** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z = 1$$ $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x = 0$$ - **Cross products (cyclic right-hand rule):** $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z, \quad \hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x, \quad \hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$$ Reversing the cross-product order introduces a **negative sign** (anti-commutativity). ## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility Unit vectors are indispensable in: - **Antenna engineering:** Defining polarization direction and radiation pattern orientation. - **Electromagnetic wave propagation:** Specifying the direction of the wave vector $\hat{k}$ for plane waves. - **Electric field lines:** Expressing $\vec{E} = E_0\,\hat{a}_E$ at every point in space. - **Force and torque calculations:** Resolving forces along independent orthogonal axes. - **Coordinate transformations:** Converting vectors between Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical systems. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Identity | Conditions | |---|---------|-------------------|------------| | 1 | Definition of Unit Vector | $\hat{a} = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$ | $\vec{A} \neq \vec{0}$ | | 2 | Magnitude Constraint | $\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$ | Always true | | 3 | Cartesian Decomposition | $\vec{A} = A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z$ | Any vector in 3D space | | 4 | Self Dot Product | $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ | Orthonormal basis | | 5 | Orthogonal Dot Product | $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$ | $x \neq y$ | | 6 | Cross Product Identity | $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ | Right-hand rule | | 7 | Anti-commutative Cross | $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x = -\hat{a}_z$ | Order reversal | | 8 | Magnitude of Cross | $\vert \hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y \vert = 1$ | Perpendicular unit vectors | | 9 | General Unit Vector (Cartesian) | $\hat{a}_A = \dfrac{A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}}$ | Normalized form | | 10 | Position Vector Unit Vector | $\hat{a}_R = \dfrac{\vec{R}}{R}$ | For $\vec{R} = x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$ | | 11 | Dot Product of Parallel | $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ | Same direction | | 12 | Dot Product of Anti-parallel | $\hat{a}_x \cdot (-\hat{a}_x) = -1$ | Opposite direction | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof That the Magnitude of a Unit Vector Equals 1 **Given:** A unit vector is defined as $\hat{a} = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$. **Step 1 — Start with the definition:** $$\hat{a} = \frac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the magnitude of both sides by taking the modulus:** $$\vert \hat{a} \vert = \left\vert \frac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert} \right\vert$$ **Step 3 — Apply the property of magnitude: $\vert c\,\vec{B} \vert = \vert c \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert$ for any scalar $c$:** $$\vert \hat{a} \vert = \frac{\vert \vec{A} \vert}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the ratio (the numerator and denominator are equal non-zero positive real numbers):** $$\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$$ **Conclusion:** Every vector normalized by its own magnitude has unit length. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof of the Orthonormality Relation $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ **Given:** $\hat{a}_x$ is a unit vector along the positive x-axis. **Step 1 — Recall the geometric definition of the dot product:** $$\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q} = \vert \vec{P} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{Q} \vert \cdot \cos\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{P}$ and $\vec{Q}$. **Step 2 — Substitute $\vec{P} = \vec{Q} = \hat{a}_x$:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \cos(0^\circ)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the unit-vector property $\vert \hat{a}_x \vert = 1$ and the trigonometric value $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = (1)(1)(1)$$ **Step 4 — Final result:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$$ **Conclusion:** The dot product of any unit vector with itself equals unity because the angle between a vector and itself is zero and cosine of zero is one. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof of the Orthogonality Relation $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$ **Given:** $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$ are perpendicular Cartesian unit vectors. **Step 1 — Apply the geometric dot product formula:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \vert \hat{a}_y \vert \cdot \cos(90^\circ)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the magnitudes and the angle:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = (1)(1)\cos(90^\circ)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric value $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 1 \cdot 0$$ **Step 4 — Final result:** $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$$ **Conclusion:** Perpendicular unit vectors produce a zero dot product. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Derivation of the Cross-Product Identity $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ **Given:** The cross product of two vectors $\vec{P}$ and $\vec{Q}$ is defined as: $$\vec{P} \times \vec{Q} = \vert \vec{P} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{Q} \vert \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \hat{n}$$ where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal following the right-hand rule. **Step 1 — Substitute $\vec{P} = \hat{a}_x$ and $\vec{Q} = \hat{a}_y$:** $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \vert \hat{a}_y \vert \cdot \sin(90^\circ) \cdot \hat{n}$$ **Step 2 — Apply $\vert \hat{a}_x \vert = 1$, $\vert \hat{a}_y \vert = 1$, and $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$:** $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = (1)(1)(1) \cdot \hat{n} = \hat{n}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the right-hand rule:** Point the right-hand fingers from $\hat{a}_x$ towards $\hat{a}_y$ (i.e., from x-axis to y-axis); the thumb points in the **positive z-direction**. **Step 4 — Therefore $\hat{n} = \hat{a}_z$:** $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$$ **Conclusion:** This result confirms the cyclic ordering $(\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z)$ in a right-handed coordinate system. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Finding the Unit Vector of a Given Cartesian Vector **Problem:** Given the vector $\vec{A} = 3\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y + 0\hat{a}_z$, find the unit vector $\hat{a}_A$. **Step 1 — Write the magnitude formula:** $$\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the components:** $$\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{(3)^2 + (4)^2 + (0)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the squares and the sum:** $$\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 0} = \sqrt{25}$$ **Step 4 — Take the square root:** $$\vert \vec{A} \vert = 5$$ **Step 5 — Apply the normalization formula $\hat{a}_A = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$:** $$\hat{a}_A = \frac{3\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y + 0\hat{a}_z}{5}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify by distributing the scalar $1/5$:** $$\hat{a}_A = 0.6\,\hat{a}_x + 0.8\,\hat{a}_y + 0\,\hat{a}_z$$ **Step 7 — Verification (sanity check):** $$\vert \hat{a}_A \vert = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.8^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1 \checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The unit vector is $\hat{a}_A = 0.6\,\hat{a}_x + 0.8\,\hat{a}_y$. $\blacksquare$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Unit Vector Theory The following diagram maps out the conceptual hierarchy of unit vectors, showing how raw vector data flows through normalization to produce a directional descriptor and how that descriptor interacts with other vector operations. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[RAW VECTOR INPUT
A = Ax ax + Ay ay + Az az]:::input nodeB[MAGNITUDE CALCULATION
vert A vert = sqrt of Ax squared + Ay squared + Az squared]:::process nodeC[DIVISION OPERATION
hat aA = A divided by vert A vert]:::process nodeD[UNIT VECTOR OUTPUT
hat aA = 0.6 ax + 0.8 ay + 0 az]:::output nodeE[CONSTRAINT CHECK
vert hat aA vert must equal 1]:::verify nodeF[ORTHONORMALITY TESTS
hat ax dot hat ax equals 1
hat ax dot hat ay equals 0]:::test nodeG[CROSS PRODUCT TESTS
hat ax cross hat ay equals hat az]:::test nodeH[APPLICATION LAYER
Electric Field E
Magnetic Field B
Wave Vector k
Poynting Vector S]:::apply nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeH classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef process fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef output fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef verify fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef test fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef apply fill:#70ad47,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Cartesian Right-Handed Coordinate System — Orientation Map This schematic illustrates the spatial relationship between the three mutually perpendicular unit vectors that KTU students must master. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeX[X AXIS
hat ax]:::xaxis nodeY[Y AXIS
hat ay]:::yaxis nodeZ[Z AXIS
hat az]:::zaxis nodeO[ORIGIN
0, 0, 0]:::origin nodeR[RULE
hat ax cross hat ay equals hat az]:::rule nodeO --- nodeX nodeO --- nodeY nodeO --- nodeZ nodeX --> nodeR nodeY --> nodeR nodeR --> nodeZ classDef xaxis fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef yaxis fill:#00b050,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef zaxis fill:#0070c0,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef origin fill:#404040,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef rule fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Vector Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeV[VECTOR FAMILY]:::root nodeN[NULL VECTOR
magnitude equals 0]:::branch nodeNV[NON ZERO VECTOR
magnitude greater than 0]:::branch nodeUV[UNIT VECTOR
magnitude equals 1]:::leaf nodeRG[RANGE VECTOR
arbitrary magnitude]:::leaf nodeV --> nodeN nodeV --> nodeNV nodeNV --> nodeUV nodeNV --> nodeRG nodeUV --> nodeOP1[OPERATION 1
NORMALIZATION]:::op nodeUV --> nodeOP2[OPERATION 2
DOT PRODUCT]:::op nodeUV --> nodeOP3[OPERATION 3
CROSS PRODUCT]:::op classDef root fill:#1f3864,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#70ad47,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef op fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEET502]` **Define a unit vector. State any four properties of Cartesian unit vectors $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) **Definition (1 Mark):** A unit vector is a vector whose magnitude is equal to **one (1)**. It is used to indicate direction only and is dimensionless. For any non-zero vector $\vec{A}$, the corresponding unit vector is $\hat{a}_A = \dfrac{\vec{A}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert}$. **Four Properties (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Magnitude unity:** $\vert \hat{a}_x \vert = \vert \hat{a}_y \vert = \vert \hat{a}_z \vert = 1$. 2. **Mutual orthogonality (dot product):** $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x = 0$. 3. **Self dot product:** $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z = 1$. 4. **Right-hand cross product rule:** $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEET502]` **Given the vector $\vec{B} = 5\hat{a}_x - 12\hat{a}_y$, find its magnitude and unit vector.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) **Step 1 — Magnitude (1 Mark):** $$\vert \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{(5)^2 + (-12)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$ **Step 2 — Unit Vector Formula (1 Mark):** $$\hat{a}_B = \frac{\vec{B}}{\vert \vec{B} \vert}$$ **Step 3 — Final Computation (1 Mark):** $$\hat{a}_B = \frac{5\hat{a}_x - 12\hat{a}_y}{13} = \frac{5}{13}\hat{a}_x - \frac{12}{13}\hat{a}_y \approx 0.385\hat{a}_x - 0.923\hat{a}_y$$ --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEET502, Module 1]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **State and prove the orthonormality relations of Cartesian unit vectors. Show that $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ and $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$ using the geometric definition of the dot product.** #### Model Solution **Statement (2 Marks):** In a right-handed Cartesian coordinate system, the three unit vectors $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ satisfy: $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z = 1$$ $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x = 0$$ **Proof of $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ (2.5 Marks):** Using the geometric dot product formula $\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q} = \vert \vec{P} \vert \vert \vec{Q} \vert \cos\theta$ with $\vec{P} = \vec{Q} = \hat{a}_x$: $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \cos(0^\circ)$$ Since $\vert \hat{a}_x \vert = 1$ and $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$: $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = (1)(1)(1) = 1$$ **Proof of $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$ (2.5 Marks):** Using the same formula with the angle $\theta = 90^\circ$ (since x and y axes are perpendicular): $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = \vert \hat{a}_x \vert \cdot \vert \hat{a}_y \vert \cdot \cos(90^\circ)$$ Since $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$: $$\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = (1)(1)(0) = 0$$ This establishes the orthonormality of the Cartesian unit vector basis. $\blacksquare$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Using the right-hand rule, derive the cross-product identities $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$, and $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$.
