Also show that $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x = \vec{0}$.** #### Model Solution **General Cross-Product Formula (1 Mark):** $$\vec{P} \times \vec{Q} = \vert \vec{P} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{Q} \vert \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \hat{n}$$ where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal in the direction given by the right-hand rule. **Derivation of $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ (2 Marks):** $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = (1)(1)\sin(90^\circ) \cdot \hat{n} = (1)(1)(1) \cdot \hat{n} = \hat{n}$$ Applying the right-hand rule (fingers curl from $\hat{a}_x$ toward $\hat{a}_y$, thumb points along +z): $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$$ **Derivation of $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$ (2 Marks):** By cyclic symmetry of the right-hand rule: $$\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$$ **Derivation of $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$ (1 Mark):** By cyclic symmetry: $$\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$$ **Proof that $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x = \vec{0}$ (1 Mark):** The angle between a vector and itself is $0^\circ$, and $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$: $$\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x = (1)(1)\sin(0^\circ) \cdot \hat{n} = 0 \cdot \hat{n} = \vec{0}$$ $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEET502, Module 1]` *(Alternative Choice)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given the vector $\vec{C} = 2\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 6\hat{a}_z$:** **(i)** Find the magnitude of $\vec{C}$. **(ii)** Determine the unit vector $\hat{a}_C$. **(iii)** Verify that $\vert \hat{a}_C \vert = 1$. #### Model Solution **(i) Magnitude (2 Marks):** $$\vert \vec{C} \vert = \sqrt{C_x^2 + C_y^2 + C_z^2} = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (3)^2 + (-6)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7$$ **(ii) Unit Vector (3 Marks):** $$\hat{a}_C = \frac{\vec{C}}{\vert \vec{C} \vert} = \frac{2\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 6\hat{a}_z}{7}$$ $$\hat{a}_C = \frac{2}{7}\hat{a}_x + \frac{3}{7}\hat{a}_y - \frac{6}{7}\hat{a}_z$$ **(iii) Verification (2 Marks):** $$\vert \hat{a}_C \vert = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{2}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{3}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{-6}{7}\right)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{\tfrac{4}{49} + \tfrac{9}{49} + \tfrac{36}{49}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{49}{49}} = \sqrt{1} = 1 \checkmark$$ Thus $\vec{C}$ has been successfully normalized. $\blacksquare$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A position vector from the origin to point $P$ in 3D space is $\vec{r} = 4\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y + 2\hat{a}_z$. Find:** **(i)** The distance of point $P$ from the origin. **(ii)** The unit position vector $\hat{a}_r$. **(iii)** The directional cosines of $\vec{r}$ with respect to the x, y, and z axes. #### Model Solution **(i) Distance from origin (2 Marks):** $$r = \vert \vec{r} \vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{16 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt{36} = 6 \text{ units}$$ **(ii) Unit Position Vector (2 Marks):** $$\hat{a}_r = \frac{\vec{r}}{r} = \frac{4\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y + 2\hat{a}_z}{6} = \frac{2}{3}\hat{a}_x + \frac{2}{3}\hat{a}_y + \frac{1}{3}\hat{a}_z$$ **(iii) Directional Cosines (3 Marks — 1 each):** The directional cosine $\ell_i$ along an axis equals the corresponding component of the unit vector: $$\ell_x = \hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_x = \frac{2}{3}$$ $$\ell_y = \hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_y = \frac{2}{3}$$ $$\ell_z = \hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_z = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Sanity check using the fundamental identity** $\ell_x^2 + \ell_y^2 + \ell_z^2 = 1$: $$\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \tfrac{4}{9} + \tfrac{4}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{9}{9} = 1 \checkmark$$ $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the magnitude in the denominator** is the single most common mistake. Students often write $\hat{a} = \vec{A}$ instead of $\hat{a} = \vec{A}/\vert \vec{A} \vert$. Always normalize! > 2. **Wrong cyclic order in cross products** costs full marks. Remember the cycle $(\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z)$ goes forward under the right-hand rule, and any reversal introduces a **negative sign**. > 3. **Direction cosines must satisfy** $\ell_x^2 + \ell_y^2 + \ell_z^2 = 1$. If your computed values fail this check, redo the magnitude calculation. > 4. **Use $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ notation** consistently in KTU scripts — avoid mixing with $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ within the same derivation; examiners deduct 0.5 marks for inconsistency. > 5. **Skipping the final verification step** ($\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$) means losing the easy 1-mark sanity check that most KTU question papers explicitly allocate. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **unit vector** has magnitude exactly equal to **1** and encodes **direction only**. - The **normalization formula** $\hat{a} = \vec{A}/\vert \vec{A} \vert$ is the single most important equation of this module. - Cartesian unit vectors $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are **mutually orthogonal** and **right-handed**. - **Self dot product** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_i = 1$ (no summation). - **Cross dot product** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$. - **Cross products follow cyclic order:** $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$. - **Anti-commutative property:** $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x = -\hat{a}_z$ (order reversal flips sign). - **Self cross product is zero:** $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x = \vec{0}$ (angle is zero, $\sin 0 = 0$). - **Direction cosines** are the components of the unit vector and always satisfy $\ell_x^2 + \ell_y^2 + \ell_z^2 = 1$. - **Always verify** $\vert \hat{a} \vert = 1$ after computing a unit vector — it is an automatic 1-mark reward in KTU valuation. - **Unit vectors are dimensionless** (no units of length, mass, or time). - The **right-hand rule** determines the direction of all cross-product results — practice this physically with your right hand before the exam.

topics.title: Vector
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vector

# 🧲 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vector?

In **Electromagnetic Theory**, a **vector** is a mathematical quantity that possesses both **magnitude** (size) and **direction**, and obeys the **triangle (or parallelogram) law of addition**. Unlike scalars (mass, charge, temperature, energy), vectors are indispensable for describing how electric and magnetic fields spread through space.

A general vector in three-dimensional space is symbolically written as:

$$
\vec{A} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{A}
$$

A vector is fully characterized by **three independent pieces of information** — its components along the three orthogonal coordinate axes. In **Cartesian coordinates**, the most commonly used system in EM Theory:

$$
\mathbf{A} = A_x \hat{a}_x + A_y \hat{a}_y + A_z \hat{a}_z
$$

where:
- $A_x, A_y, A_z$ are the **scalar components** (real numbers, can be positive or negative).
- $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are the **unit vectors** (dimensionless, mutually perpendicular, magnitude = 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Electromagnetic Theory, the *vector* is the fundamental language. Every quantity you will study — **Electric Field ($\mathbf{E}$)**, **Magnetic Flux Density ($\mathbf{B}$)**, **Poynting Vector ($\mathbf{S}$)**, **Wave Vector ($\mathbf{k}$)** — is a vector field. Master vector algebra now, and the rest of the semester becomes intuitive.

## 1.2 The Three Defining Properties of a Vector

For a quantity to be classified as a vector in the KTU syllabus framework, it must satisfy:

| Property | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Magnitude** | A non-negative real number (always $\geq 0$). |
| **Direction** | Specified relative to a chosen coordinate system. |
| **Addition Law** | Must obey the **parallelogram law** of addition. |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: "The GPS Arrow" 🧭

Imagine you are standing in the centre of your college campus and want to tell your friend the location of the canteen.

- A **scalar** answer would be: *"It is **500 metres** away."* — This gives only distance. Your friend has no idea *where* to walk.
- A **vector** answer would be: *"It is **500 metres towards the North-East, at 45° above the ground floor**."* — This gives both magnitude **and** direction. Your friend walks straight to it.

In EM Theory, when we say "the electric field at this point is $5\,\text{V/m}$ pointing in the +x direction", we are giving a **vector quantity** ($\mathbf{E} = 5\,\hat{a}_x\,\text{V/m}$). The direction is not optional — it determines how a charged particle will accelerate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Encountered in Vectors (EM Context):**
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\;\text{H/m}$
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$
> These constants will appear repeatedly inside vector equations throughout the course.

## 1.4 Classification of Vectors (KTU Expected Knowledge)

| Type | Definition | EM Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero (Null) Vector** | Magnitude = 0, direction undefined. | $\mathbf{0}$ (field-free point) |
| **Unit Vector** | Magnitude = 1, indicates pure direction. | $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ |
| **Position Vector** | Locates a point from origin. | $\mathbf{r} = x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$ |
| **Free Vector** | Can be shifted anywhere without changing meaning. | Force, velocity |
| **Bound (Localised) Vector** | Fixed to a specific point in space. | Electric field at a point |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Representation in Three Coordinate Systems

The **same physical vector** can be expressed in different coordinate systems. KTU 2024 expects fluency in all three. A point $P$ in space has coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in Cartesian, $(\rho, \phi, z)$ in Cylindrical, and $(r, \theta, \phi)$ in Spherical.

### 2.1.1 Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinate System
- Variables: $x, y, z \in (-\infty, \infty)$
- Unit vectors: $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ — **constant** in direction everywhere.

### 2.1.2 Cylindrical Coordinate System
- Variables: $\rho \in [0, \infty)$, $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$, $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$
- Unit vectors: $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_z$ — **vary** with $\phi$ (except $\hat{a}_z$).
- Useful for problems with cylindrical symmetry (coaxial cables, waveguides).

### 2.1.3 Spherical Coordinate System
- Variables: $r \in [0, \infty)$, $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$
- Unit vectors: $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ — **vary** with both $\theta$ and $\phi$.
- Useful for radiation problems (antennas, point charges).

## 2.2 Fundamental Vector Operations — The "Why" and "How"

### (A) Vector Addition
**Why:** To combine two or more vectors (e.g., net electric field from multiple charges).
**How:** Place the tail of $\mathbf{B}$ at the head of $\mathbf{A}$. The resultant goes from the tail of $\mathbf{A}$ to the head of $\mathbf{B}$ (head-to-tail rule). Equivalent to parallelogram rule.

$$
\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{B} = (A_x + B_x)\hat{a}_x + (A_y + B_y)\hat{a}_y + (A_z + B_z)\hat{a}_z
$$

### (B) Scalar (Dot) Product $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$
**Why:** Yields the *component* of one vector along another. In EM, used to compute work done ($W = \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$), flux ($\Psi = \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$), and power.
**How:** Multiply magnitudes by the cosine of the angle between them.

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert \cos\theta_{AB}
$$

Always produces a **scalar** (no direction). Commutative: $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A}$.

### (C) Vector (Cross) Product $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$
**Why:** Produces a new vector perpendicular to both, with magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram they form. In EM, used for Lorentz force ($\mathbf{F} = q\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}$), torque, and Maxwell's equations in differential form (curl).
**How:** Multiply magnitudes by the sine of the angle, with direction given by the **right-hand rule**.

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert \sin\theta_{AB} \, \hat{a}_n
$$

**Anti-commutative:** $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = -(\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{A})$.

## 2.3 🎯 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 problem.**

| Operation / Concept | Formula (Cartesian) | Result Type | Key Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vector $\mathbf{A}$ | $A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z$ | Vector | — |
| Magnitude $\vert A \vert$ | $\sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$ | Scalar | Always $\geq 0$ |
| Unit Vector $\hat{a}_A$ | $\mathbf{A} \div \vert A \vert$ | Unit Vector | $\vert \hat{a}_A \vert = 1$ |
| Position Vector $\mathbf{r}$ | $x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$ | Vector | $\vert r \vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ |
| Dot Product $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$ | $A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$ | Scalar | $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A} = \vert A \vert^2$ |
| Angle between vectors | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}$ | Scalar | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ |
| Cross Product $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$ | $\begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}$ | Vector | $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{A} = 0$ |
| Scalar Triple Product | $\mathbf{A} \cdot (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C})$ | Scalar | Volume of parallelepiped |
| Vector Triple Product | $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})$ | Vector | **"BAC-CAB" rule** |
| $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x = 1$ | $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$ | Scalar | Orthogonality |
| $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ | $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$ | $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$ | Cyclic right-hand rule |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Design:** The far-field radiation pattern is computed using vector potentials and cross products of current distributions.
- **Electric Machines:** Torque on a current loop in a magnetic field: $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}$ — directly uses the cross product.
- **Transmission Lines:** Poynting vector $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}$ — a vector cross product giving power flow direction.
- **Coordinate transformations** (Cartesian → Spherical) are used in point-charge and antenna radiation problems.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Derivations carry 7–14 marks. Showing *every* algebraic step with proper justification is the difference between a 70% answer and a 95% answer. Examiners reward intermediate steps explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnitude of a Vector in Cartesian Coordinates

**Statement to prove:** For a vector $\mathbf{A} = A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z$, the magnitude is $\vert A \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Start with the geometric definition of magnitude. The magnitude is the dot product of the vector with itself, then square-rooted:

$$
\vert A \vert = \sqrt{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the component form of $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A} = (A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z) \cdot (A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the distributive law of dot products (expand the product):

$$
= A_x^2(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x) + A_y^2(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y) + A_z^2(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z) + 2A_xA_y(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y) + 2A_xA_z(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_z) + 2A_yA_z(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 4:** Use the orthonormality of unit vectors: $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_i = 1$ and $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A} = A_x^2(1) + A_y^2(1) + A_z^2(1) + 0 + 0 + 0 = A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2
$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root to obtain the magnitude:

$$
\boxed{\vert A \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}}
$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dot Product from Components

**Statement to prove:** $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write both vectors in component form and apply the distributive law:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = (A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z) \cdot (B_x\hat{a}_x + B_y\hat{a}_y + B_z\hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 2:** Expand. The dot product is distributive over addition:

$$
= A_xB_x(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_x) + A_xB_y(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y) + A_xB_z(\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_z) + A_yB_x(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_x) + A_yB_y(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_y) + A_yB_z(\hat{a}_y \cdot \hat{a}_z) + A_zB_x(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_x) + A_zB_y(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_y) + A_zB_z(\hat{a}_z \cdot \hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply orthonormality. The 6 cross-terms vanish ($\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$):

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = A_xB_x(1) + A_yB_y(1) + A_zB_z(1) + 0
$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z}
$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross Product from Components (Determinant Form)

**Statement to prove:** $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$ can be expressed as a $3 \times 3$ determinant.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$ in component form:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = (A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z) \times (B_x\hat{a}_x + B_y\hat{a}_y + B_z\hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply distributivity of the cross product. This gives 9 terms:

$$
= A_xB_x(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_x) + A_xB_y(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y) + A_xB_z(\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_z) + A_yB_x(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_x) + A_yB_y(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_y) + A_yB_z(\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z) + A_zB_x(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x) + A_zB_y(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_y) + A_zB_z(\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_z)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply cross product rules: $\hat{a}_i \times \hat{a}_i = 0$ and $\hat{a}_i \times \hat{a}_j = -\hat{a}_j \times \hat{a}_i$. Also use the cyclic identity: $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$, $\hat{a}_y \times \hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_z \times \hat{a}_x = \hat{a}_y$.

$$
= A_xB_y\hat{a}_z - A_xB_z\hat{a}_y - A_yB_x\hat{a}_z + A_yB_z\hat{a}_x + A_zB_x\hat{a}_y - A_zB_y\hat{a}_x
$$

**Step 4:** Group the $\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_y$, $\hat{a}_z$ components:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = (A_yB_z - A_zB_y)\hat{a}_x + (A_zB_x - A_xB_z)\hat{a}_y + (A_xB_y - A_yB_x)\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as the expansion of a $3 \times 3$ determinant:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix}}
$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Vector Triple Product (BAC–CAB Rule)

**Statement to prove:** $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Let $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}$. Then $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{D}$ is the desired quantity. Express using the determinant form:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{D} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ D_x & D_y & D_z \end{vmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the components of $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}$ using the same determinant:

$$
D_x = B_yC_z - B_zC_y, \quad D_y = B_zC_x - B_xC_z, \quad D_z = B_xC_y - B_yC_x
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the x-component of $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{D}$:

$$
(\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{D})_x = A_yD_z - A_zD_y
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $D_y$ and $D_z$:

$$
= A_y(B_xC_y - B_yC_x) - A_z(B_zC_x - B_xC_z)
$$

**Step 5:** Distribute:

$$
= A_yB_xC_y - A_yB_yC_x - A_zB_zC_x + A_zB_xC_z
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to group terms containing $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}$ and $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$. Note that $A_yB_xC_y + A_zB_xC_z = B_x(A_yC_y + A_zC_z) = B_x(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C} - A_xC_x)$:

$$
(\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{D})_x = B_x(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - C_x(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})
$$

**Step 7:** The same logic applies to the y and z components, yielding:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "BAC minus CAB":** The **middle** vector $\mathbf{B}$ gets multiplied by the **dot** product of the **outer** vectors $(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C})$, and the **last** vector $\mathbf{C}$ gets multiplied by the **dot** product of the **first two** $(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})$, then **subtracted**. This rule is heavily tested in KTU Module 1 derivations.

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Position Vector and Distance Between Two Points

**Statement to prove:** The vector from point $P_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ to point $P_2(x_2, y_2, z_2)$ is $\mathbf{d} = (x_2 - x_1)\hat{a}_x + (y_2 - y_1)\hat{a}_y + (z_2 - z_1)\hat{a}_z$, and the distance is $\vert d \vert = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write the position vectors of $P_1$ and $P_2$ from the origin $O$:

$$
\mathbf{r}_1 = x_1\hat{a}_x + y_1\hat{a}_y + z_1\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_2 = x_2\hat{a}_x + y_2\hat{a}_y + z_2\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 2:** The displacement vector from $P_1$ to $P_2$ is $\mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_1$ (head-to-tail: $\mathbf{r}_1 + \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{r}_2$):

$$
\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_1 = (x_2 - x_1)\hat{a}_x + (y_2 - y_1)\hat{a}_y + (z_2 - z_1)\hat{a}_z
$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude is the Euclidean norm (from §3.1):

$$
\boxed{\vert d \vert = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}}
$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$
