# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a vector. Distinguish clearly between a scalar quantity and a vector quantity, giving two examples of each from the field of Electromagnetic Theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **vector** is a physical quantity that is characterized by both **magnitude and direction**, and which obeys the **parallelogram law of addition**. In three-dimensional space, a vector $\mathbf{A}$ is represented as:

$$
\mathbf{A} = A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z
$$

| Type | Definition | EM Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scalar** | Has only magnitude, no direction. | Electric potential $V$ (volts), Electric charge $Q$ (coulombs), Energy $W$ (joules), Temperature $T$ (kelvin). |
| **Vector** | Has both magnitude and direction. | Electric field $\mathbf{E}$ (V/m), Magnetic flux density $\mathbf{B}$ (T), Poynting vector $\mathbf{S}$ (W/m²), Lorentz force $\mathbf{F}$ (N). |

**Key distinction:** A scalar is invariant under coordinate rotation; a vector transforms by the rotation rule (its components change, but its magnitude and physical meaning do not). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the right-hand rule for determining the direction of the cross product $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **right-hand rule** gives the direction of the resultant vector $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$:

1. **Point the fingers** of the right hand in the direction of the first vector $\mathbf{A}$.
2. **Curl them** through the smaller angle $\theta$ ($\theta \in [0, \pi]$) towards the second vector $\mathbf{B}$.
3. **The extended thumb** points in the direction of $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$.

Mathematically:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert \sin\theta \, \hat{a}_n
$$

where $\hat{a}_n$ is the unit normal in the direction of the thumb. **Key property:** $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = -(\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{A})$ (anti-commutative). The cross product is **zero** when $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are parallel ($\theta = 0$) or anti-parallel ($\theta = \pi$). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Given the vectors $\mathbf{A} = 3\hat{a}_x + 2\hat{a}_y - \hat{a}_z$ and $\mathbf{B} = -\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y + 5\hat{a}_z$, compute:
- (i) The dot product $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$
- (ii) The cross product $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}$
- (iii) The angle $\theta$ between $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive in detail the **BAC–CAB rule** for vector triple products, and use it to simplify the expression $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C})$ where $\mathbf{A} = 2\hat{a}_x$, $\mathbf{B} = \hat{a}_x + \hat{a}_y$, and $\mathbf{C} = \hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — (i) Dot Product:**

The dot product formula in Cartesian coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = (3)(-1) + (2)(4) + (-1)(5) = -3 + 8 - 5 = 0
$$

**Result:** $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$ — meaning $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are **perpendicular** (orthogonal). **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — (ii) Cross Product:**

Using the determinant form:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ 3 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 4 & 5 \end{vmatrix}
$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$
= \hat{a}_x[(2)(5) - (-1)(4)] - \hat{a}_y[(3)(5) - (-1)(-1)] + \hat{a}_z[(3)(4) - (2)(-1)]
$$

$$
= \hat{a}_x[10 + 4] - \hat{a}_y[15 - 1] + \hat{a}_z[12 + 2]
$$

$$
= 14\hat{a}_x - 14\hat{a}_y + 14\hat{a}_z
$$

**Result:** $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = 14(\hat{a}_x - \hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z)$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — (iii) Angle Between A and B:**

First, compute the magnitudes:

$$
\vert A \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt{14}
$$

$$
\vert B \vert = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 4^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{1 + 16 + 25} = \sqrt{42}
$$

Using $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}$:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{0}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{42}} = 0
$$

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90°
$$

This confirms the dot product result. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of BAC–CAB Rule:**

The full derivation is shown in **§3.4** of these notes. The final rule:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})}
$$

**Applying to the given vectors:**

Given: $\mathbf{A} = 2\hat{a}_x$, $\mathbf{B} = \hat{a}_x + \hat{a}_y$, $\mathbf{C} = \hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C} = (2)(0) + (0)(1) + (0)(1) = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = (2)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(0) = 2
$$

**Step 3:** Apply BAC–CAB:

$$
\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}) = (\hat{a}_x + \hat{a}_y)(0) - (\hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z)(2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = -2\hat{a}_y - 2\hat{a}_z}
$$

**(7 marks — including derivation and application)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **cylindrical and spherical coordinate systems** with neat diagrams. Express the position vector and derive the unit vector transformations between Cartesian and spherical coordinates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two vectors are given as $\mathbf{A} = 4\hat{a}_x - 2\hat{a}_y + 3\hat{a}_z$ and $\mathbf{B} = \hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 2\hat{a}_z$. Find:
- (i) $\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{B}$
- (ii) $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}$
- (iii) The projection of $\mathbf{A}$ on $\mathbf{B}$
- (iv) The component of $\mathbf{A}$ perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinate Systems:**

**Cylindrical coordinates** $(\rho, \phi, z)$ are extensions of 2D polar coordinates into 3D:
- $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ — radial distance from z-axis
- $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ — azimuthal angle from +x axis
- $z$ — same as Cartesian

**Spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$:
- $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ — distance from origin
- $\theta = \cos^{-1}(z/r)$ — polar angle from +z axis (range $0$ to $\pi$)
- $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ — azimuthal angle (range $0$ to $2\pi$)

**Cartesian ↔ Spherical Transformations:**

$$
x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta
$$

**Unit Vector Transformations (Spherical to Cartesian):**

$$
\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y + \cos\theta \, \hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_y - \sin\theta \, \hat{a}_z
$$

$$
\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi \, \hat{a}_x + \cos\phi \, \hat{a}_y
$$

These are derived by differentiating the position vector $\mathbf{r} = x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$ with respect to $r, \theta, \phi$ and normalizing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Vector Operations:**

Given: $\mathbf{A} = 4\hat{a}_x - 2\hat{a}_y + 3\hat{a}_z$ and $\mathbf{B} = \hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 2\hat{a}_z$.

**(i) Addition:**

$$
\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{B} = (4+1)\hat{a}_x + (-2+3)\hat{a}_y + (3-2)\hat{a}_z = 5\hat{a}_x + \hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Subtraction:**

$$
\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B} = (4-1)\hat{a}_x + (-2-3)\hat{a}_y + (3-(-2))\hat{a}_z = 3\hat{a}_x - 5\hat{a}_y + 5\hat{a}_z
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Projection of A on B:**

The **scalar projection** is:

$$
A_B = \frac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert B \vert}
$$

Compute $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = (4)(1) + (-2)(3) + (3)(-2) = 4 - 6 - 6 = -8$.

Compute $\vert B \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 3^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 9 + 4} = \sqrt{14}$.

$$
A_B = \frac{-8}{\sqrt{14}} \approx -2.138
$$

The **vector projection** is:

$$
\mathbf{A}_B = \frac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert B \vert^2} \mathbf{B} = \frac{-8}{14}(\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 2\hat{a}_z) = -\frac{4}{7}(\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y - 2\hat{a}_z)
$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**(iv) Perpendicular Component:**

The component of $\mathbf{A}$ perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$ is:

$$
\mathbf{A}_\perp = \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_B
$$

Using the scalar projection form for clarity, the perpendicular component magnitude is:

$$
\vert A_\perp \vert = \sqrt{\vert A \vert^2 - A_B^2}
$$

Compute $\vert A \vert = \sqrt{16 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{29}$.

$$
\vert A_\perp \vert = \sqrt{29 - \frac{64}{14}} = \sqrt{\frac{406 - 64}{14}} = \sqrt{\frac{342}{14}} = \sqrt{24.428} \approx 4.942
$$

Vector form:

$$
\mathbf{A}_\perp = \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_B = (4\hat{a}_x - 2\hat{a}_y + 3\hat{a}_z) - \left(-\frac{4}{7}\hat{a}_x - \frac{12}{7}\hat{a}_y + \frac{8}{7}\hat{a}_z\right)
$$

$$
= \left(4 + \frac{4}{7}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(-2 + \frac{12}{7}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(3 - \frac{8}{7}\right)\hat{a}_z
$$

$$
= \frac{32}{7}\hat{a}_x - \frac{2}{7}\hat{a}_y + \frac{13}{7}\hat{a}_z
$$

**(2.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in cross product:** Students often forget the alternating signs in determinant expansion. Use the formula carefully: $+\hat{a}_x$ term, then $-\hat{a}_y$, then $+\hat{a}_z$.
> 2. **Confusing dot and cross product results:** Dot product is a **scalar**, cross product is a **vector**. Examiners deduct full marks if you write cross product as a scalar.
> 3. **Right-hand rule violations:** Many students write $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = -\hat{a}_z$ instead of $+\hat{a}_z$. Memorize: $\hat{a}_x \times \hat{a}_y = \hat{a}_z$ (cyclic, right-handed).
> 4. **BAC–CAB misuse:** The rule is **not** associative. $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) \neq (\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}) \times \mathbf{C}$. Always parenthesize the **inner** cross product.
> 5. **Unit vector in coordinate systems:** Cylindrical and spherical unit vectors are **not constant** — they change direction with position. Many students treat them like Cartesian $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$.
> 6. **Projection sign:** A negative projection means the vector points opposite to the reference direction. Do not drop the sign.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Vector = Magnitude + Direction**, obeys the **parallelogram law** of addition.
- ✅ **Magnitude formula:** $\vert A \vert = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2 + A_z^2}$.
- ✅ **Dot product is scalar:** $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z = \vert A \vert \vert B \vert \cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Cross product is vector:** Use the $3 \times 3$ determinant form. Magnitude is $\vert A \vert \vert B \vert \sin\theta$.
- ✅ **Orthonormality:** $\hat{a}_i \cdot \hat{a}_j = \delta_{ij}$ and $\hat{a}_i \times \hat{a}_j = \hat{a}_k$ (cyclic, right-handed).
- ✅ **Anti-commutation:** $\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B} = -(\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{A})$.
- ✅ **BAC–CAB rule:** $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B})$.
- ✅ **Coordinate systems:** Cartesian (constant unit vectors), Cylindrical & Spherical (varying unit vectors — handle with care).
- ✅ **Position vector:** $\mathbf{r} = x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications to recall:** Lorentz force $\mathbf{F} = q\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}$, Poynting vector $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}$, work $W = \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$, flux $\Psi = \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constant Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Constant Surfaces

In the study of Electromagnetic Theory, a **constant surface** (also known as a **level surface** or **equi-surface**) is a geometric locus in three-dimensional space on which a specified scalar or vector field quantity remains invariant. Mathematically, if $F(x, y, z)$ represents either a scalar field or a specific component/property of a vector field, the constant surface is defined as the set of all points satisfying:

$$F(x, y, z) = C$$

where $C$ is a real constant. In the context of vector fields, a constant surface can be defined in three distinct ways corresponding to the three properties of a vector: **magnitude**, **direction**, and **potential**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Constant surfaces are categorized into three fundamental types — *Equipotential Surfaces* (constant scalar potential), *Constant Magnitude Surfaces* (equi-magnitude loci of vector fields), and *Stream Surfaces* (constant direction loci of vector fields). Each plays a critical role in visualizing and solving electromagnetic field problems.

### 1.2 The Three Categories of Constant Surfaces

**Category 1 — Equipotential Surfaces (Constant Scalar Surfaces):**
These are surfaces on which the scalar potential $V(x, y, z)$ is constant. For a point charge, these are concentric spheres.

**Category 2 — Constant Magnitude Surfaces (Equi-Magnitude Surfaces):**
These are surfaces on which the magnitude of a vector field $\vert \vec{A} \vert$ is constant. For a point source, these are also concentric spheres.

**Category 3 — Stream Surfaces (Constant Direction Surfaces):**
These are surfaces generated by streamlines passing through every point of a given curve. On a stream surface, the vector field is everywhere tangent to the surface.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Mountain Landscape:** Imagine standing on a hillside. The **altitude** (height above sea level) is a scalar field. If you walk along a path that keeps your altitude constant, you trace out a **contour line** on a topographic map — this is precisely an **equipotential surface** in 2D (or a level surface in 3D).
>
> Now, imagine a strong wind blowing across the landscape. The **wind speed** (magnitude of the wind vector) varies from point to point. The set of all points where the wind blows at exactly $10$ km/h forms a **constant magnitude surface**. The set of all points where the wind blows due North forms a **constant direction surface** (a stream surface in atmospheric physics).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The gradient of a scalar field is always **perpendicular (normal)** to its constant surfaces.
- The **SI unit** of electric potential $V$ is the **Volt (V)**, equivalent to **Joules per Coulomb (J/C)**.
- The **SI unit** of electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ is **Volts per meter (V/m)** or equivalently **Newtons per Coulomb (N/C)**.
- The constant **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** (permittivity of free space) and **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** (permeability of free space) appear in field-magnitude expressions.

### 1.5 Visualizing Constant Surfaces in Electromagnetics

| Physical Quantity | Field Type | Constant Surface Name | Example Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric Scalar Potential $V$ | Scalar | Equipotential Surface | Concentric spheres (point charge) |
| Magnetic Scalar Potential $\psi_m$ | Scalar | Equipotential Surface | Concentric cylinders (line current) |
| $\vert \vec{E} \vert$ (Electric Field Magnitude) | Vector Property | Equi-Magnitude Surface | Spheres around point charge |
| $\vert \vec{B} \vert$ (Magnetic Flux Density) | Vector Property | Equi-Magnitude Surface | Concentric cylinders (long wire) |
| $\vec{J}$ direction (Current Density) | Vector Property | Stream Surface / Tube of Flux | Cylindrical surface in DC current flow |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Constant Surfaces

A constant surface is the three-dimensional generalization of a level curve. Given a continuously differentiable scalar field $f(x, y, z)$, the level surface (or constant surface) of value $c$ is the locus:

$$S_c = \{(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid f(x, y, z) = c\}$$

For this surface to be a well-defined 2D manifold, the gradient $\nabla f$ must be **non-zero** at every point of $S_c$. This condition is crucial — at points where $\nabla f = \vec{0}$, the level surface either degenerates, has a cusp, or becomes singular.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $\nabla f \neq \vec{0}$ necessary?** The Implicit Function Theorem requires this condition. If $\nabla f = \vec{0}$ at a point, the surface fails to have a well-defined tangent plane, and standard differential geometry tools break down.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Type

#### Type A: Equipotential Surfaces (Scalar Field)

1. Start with a scalar potential function $V(x, y, z)$.
2. Set $V(x, y, z) = k$ (where $k$ is a specified constant).
3. The solution set defines the equipotential surface.
4. **Key Property:** The electric field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ is everywhere **perpendicular** to the equipotential surface.

#### Type B: Constant Magnitude Surfaces (Vector Field Magnitude)

1. Start with a vector field $\vec{F}(x, y, z) = F_x \hat{a}_x + F_y \hat{a}_y + F_z \hat{a}_z$.
2. Compute its magnitude $\vert \vec{F} \vert = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$.
3. Set $\vert \vec{F} \vert = c$ (a specified positive constant).
4. The solution set defines the constant magnitude surface.

#### Type C: Stream Surfaces (Vector Field Direction)

1. Start with a vector field $\vec{F}(x, y, z)$.
2. Find a curve $\vec{r}(t)$ such that $\dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = \lambda \vec{F}(\vec{r})$ for some scalar $\lambda(t) > 0$.
3. This curve is a **streamline**.
4. A **stream surface** is generated by all streamlines passing through a given curve (not parallel to $\vec{F}$).
5. On this surface, the direction of $\vec{F}$ is everywhere tangent.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Equipotential Surface Condition | $V(x, y, z) = C$ | Constant scalar potential locus | V (Volt) |
| 2 | Gradient-Surface Orthogonality | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V \perp S_c$ | Field is normal to equipotential | V/m |
| 3 | Constant Magnitude Surface | $\vert \vec{F} \vert = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2} = k$ | All points where field has magnitude $k$ | depends on $\vec{F}$ |
| 4 | Streamline Differential Equation | $\dfrac{dx}{F_x} = \dfrac{dy}{F_y} = \dfrac{dz}{F_z}$ | Parametric equation of a streamline | — |
| 5 | Point Charge Potential | $V = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ | Constant surfaces are spheres $r = \text{const}$ | V |
| 6 | Point Charge Electric Field | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r$ | Equi-magnitude surfaces are spheres | V/m |
| 7 | Infinite Line Charge Field | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$ | Equi-magnitude surfaces are cylinders $\rho = \text{const}$ | V/m |
| 8 | Dipole Equipotential | $V = \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ | Surfaces $r^2 = k\cos\theta$ | V |
| 9 | Unit Vector of Field | $\hat{a}_F = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{\vert \vec{F} \vert}$ | Defines direction of stream surface tangent | dimensionless |
| 10 | Normal Vector to Surface | $\hat{a}_n = \dfrac{\nabla f}{\vert \nabla f \vert}$ | For surface $f(x,y,z) = C$ | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Constant Surfaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **High-Voltage Insulator Design:** Engineers ensure that no two points at very different potentials are physically close, to prevent dielectric breakdown. Equipotential surfaces guide the placement of insulating materials.
> 2. **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) Design:** The electron beam follows streamlines of the electric field. Constant direction surfaces help predict beam trajectories.
> 3. **MRI Machine Coil Design:** The magnetic field's constant magnitude surfaces determine the imaging "sweet spot" where field uniformity is critical.
> 4. **Lightning Rod Protection:** Equipotential surfaces around a grounded rod dictate the region of protection (the "cone of protection").
> 5. **Microwave Waveguide Analysis:** Constant direction surfaces of the Poynting vector indicate energy flow paths through the guide.

### 2.5 The Deep "Why" Behind Constant Surface Theory

**Why are equipotential surfaces perpendicular to field lines?**
Because the field is the negative gradient: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. The gradient always points in the direction of maximum rate of change of $V$. If motion is constrained to the equipotential surface, $V$ does not change — meaning the directional derivative of $V$ along any tangent direction is zero. This is equivalent to saying $\vec{E}$ has no component tangent to the surface, hence $\vec{E} \perp$ surface.

**Why is the constant magnitude surface for a point charge a sphere?**
For a point charge at the origin, $\vert \vec{E} \vert = \dfrac{\vert Q \vert}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$. Setting this equal to a constant $k$ gives $r^2 = \dfrac{\vert Q \vert}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 k}$, which is the equation of a sphere of fixed radius centered at the origin. The spherical symmetry of the source forces spherical symmetry in its field magnitude distribution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Equipotential Surface of a Point Charge

**Problem:** A point charge $Q$ is located at the origin. Determine the family of equipotential surfaces.

**Step 1:** State the scalar potential of a point charge at the origin. By Coulomb's law integration or the standard result:

$$V(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the spherical radial coordinate $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$. The potential simplifies to:

$$V = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the constant surface condition $V = C$, where $C$ is an arbitrary real constant:

$$\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r} = C$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $r$:

$$r = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 C}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result. The set of all points at distance $r = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 C}$ from the origin forms a sphere of that radius. Thus, the equipotential surfaces are **concentric spheres centered at the origin**.

**Step 6:** Verify orthogonality with the field. The electric field is:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r$$

This field is purely radial. The surface normal of a sphere of radius $r$ is also radial ($\hat{a}_r$). Therefore, $\vec{E}$ is everywhere perpendicular to the equipotential spheres, confirming the orthogonality property.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Constant Magnitude Surface of a Dipole Field

**Problem:** A short electric dipole of moment $\vec{p} = p\hat{a}_z$ is located at the origin. Find the constant magnitude surfaces of its far-field electric field.

**Step 1:** The far-field (radiation zone) electric field of a Hertzian dipole is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0 L \beta \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\cos(\omega t - \beta r)\hat{a}_\theta$$

where $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377~\Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space, $I_0$ is the peak current, $L$ is the dipole length, $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant, and $\theta$ is the polar angle.

**Step 2:** The magnitude of this field (envelope) is:

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \frac{\eta_0 I_0 L \beta \sin\theta}{4\pi r}$$

**Step 3:** Set $\vert \vec{E} \vert = E_0$ (a specified constant):

$$\frac{\eta_0 I_0 L \beta \sin\theta}{4\pi r} = E_0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $r$ in terms of $\theta$:

$$r = \frac{\eta_0 I_0 L \beta \sin\theta}{4\pi E_0}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion: The constant magnitude surfaces are **spheres distorted by a $\sin\theta$ factor** — these are known as the **radiation pattern lobes** of the dipole. Specifically, for a given $E_0$, the surface is a sphere of radius $r(\theta) \propto \sin\theta$, which traces out the classic "donut" (torus-like) radiation pattern.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stream Surface of a Uniform Field

**Problem:** Given the uniform vector field $\vec{F} = F_0 \hat{a}_x$, find the stream surfaces.

**Step 1:** Write the streamline differential equations:

$$\frac{dx}{F_x} = \frac{dy}{F_y} = \frac{dz}{F_z}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the field components $F_x = F_0$, $F_y = 0$, $F_z = 0$:

$$\frac{dx}{F_0} = \frac{dy}{0} = \frac{dz}{0}$$

**Step 3:** The right-hand equalities imply $dy = 0$ and $dz = 0$. Integrating:

$$y = C_1, \quad z = C_2$$

**Step 4:** The streamlines are parallel to the $x$-axis, with $y$ and $z$ fixed at constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively.

**Step 5:** A stream surface is generated by sweeping a curve through the streamlines. If we choose the curve to be the line segment from $(0, 0, 0)$ to $(0, 0, 1)$ (parameterized by $z \in [0, 1]$ with $x = y = 0$), then the stream surface is the $xz$-plane (with $y = 0$).

More generally, **any plane of the form $y = c_1$ (with $z$ free)** is a stream surface of the uniform field $\vec{F} = F_0 \hat{a}_x$. The family of stream surfaces is:

$$S: \quad y = \text{const}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Equipotential Surface of a Line Charge

**Problem:** An infinite line charge with linear charge density $\rho_L$ lies along the $z$-axis. Find its equipotential surfaces.

**Step 1:** The electric potential of an infinite line charge (choosing reference $V = 0$ at $\rho = \rho_0$):

$$V(\rho) = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Set $V = C$:

$$\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right) = C$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\rho$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right) = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 C}{\rho_L}$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \exp\left(\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 C}{\rho_L}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$\rho = \rho_0 \exp\left(-\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 C}{\rho_L}\right) = \text{constant}$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion: The equipotential surfaces are **concentric circular cylinders** of radius $\rho$ centered on the $z$-axis. The electric field $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$ is radial, confirming orthogonality with these cylindrical equipotentials.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Surface Shape vs. Source Type

| **Source Configuration** | **Field Type** | **Equipotential Surface** | **Equi-Magnitude Surface** | **Stream Surface** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Point Charge | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r$ | Concentric Spheres | Concentric Spheres | Radial half-lines from $Q$ |
| Infinite Line Charge | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$ | Concentric Cylinders | Concentric Cylinders | Radial half-planes from $z$-axis |
| Infinite Sheet Charge | $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_s}{2\varepsilon_0}\hat{a}_n$ | Parallel Planes | All planes parallel to sheet | Lines perpendicular to sheet |
| Electric Dipole | $\vec{E} \propto \dfrac{\sin\theta}{r^3}$ | Surfaces $r^2 = k\cos\theta$ | $r = k'\sin\theta$ | Curves in meridian plane |
| Uniform Field $\vec{F} = F_0\hat{a}_x$ | $\vec{F} = F_0\hat{a}_x$ | Planes $x = \text{const}$ | All space (constant everywhere) | Planes $y = \text{const}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Constant Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constant Surfaces in EM Theory] --> B[Scalar Field Derived]
    A --> C[Vector Field Derived]

    B --> B1[Equipotential Surfaces V equals C]
    B1 --> B1a[Point Charge Spheres]
    B1 --> B1b[Line Charge Cylinders]
    B1 --> B1c[Sheet Charge Planes]

    C --> D[Equi Magnitude Surfaces]
    C --> E[Stream Surfaces Constant Direction]

    D --> D1[Point Charge Spheres]
    D --> D2[Line Charge Cylinders]
    D --> D3[Dipole Sin theta Lobes]

    E --> E1[Streamlines Tangent to Field]
    E --> E2[Stream Tubes Bundles of Streamlines]
    E --> E3[Flux Tubes for Vector Flux]

    B1a -. Orthogonal .-> D1
    B1b -. Orthogonal .-> D2
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Equipotential Surface of a Point Charge

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Point Charge Q at Origin] --> node2[Potential V equals Q over 4 pi epsilon_0 r]
    node2 --> node3[Constant Surface Condition V equals C]
    node3 --> node4[Solve r equals Q over 4 pi epsilon_0 C]
    node4 --> node5[Concentric Sphere of Radius r]
    node5 --> node6[Electric Field E is Radial hat a r]
    node6 --> node7[E is Perpendicular to Sphere Surface]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Stream Surface Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Define Vector Field F of x y z] --> stepB[Compute Streamline ODE dx by Fx equals dy by Fy equals dz by Fz]
    stepB --> stepC[Choose Initial Curve C not parallel to F]
    stepC --> stepD[Through Each Point of C Draw Streamline]
    stepD --> stepE[Union of All Such Streamlines equals Stream Surface S]
    stepE --> stepF[On S the Field F is Tangent at Every Point]
    stepF --> stepG[Normal to S is Perpendicular to F]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Orthogonality Principle

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1[Scalar Potential V] --> p2[Gradient Operator Nabla V]
    p2 --> p3[Direction of Steepest Increase of V]
    p1 --> p4[Equipotential Surface V equals C]
    p4 --> p5[Surface has No Variation in V]
    p3 --> p6[Hence Gradient is Perpendicular to Surface]
    p2 --> p7[Electric Field E equals minus Nabla V]
    p7 --> p6
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Constant Magnitude Surface Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Vector Field F of x y z] --> mag[Compute Magnitude Mod F equals sqrt Fx sq plus Fy sq plus Fz sq]
    mag --> cond[Set Mod F equals k where k is a positive constant]
    cond --> solve[Solve for Points x y z satisfying the equation]
    solve --> shape[Identify Geometric Shape: Sphere Cylinder Torus etc]
    shape --> app[Application: Radiation Pattern Field Uniformity Region Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an equipotential surface. Show that the electric field is always perpendicular to the equipotential surface.**

**Model Answer:**

An **equipotential surface** is the locus of all points in space that are at the same electric potential. Mathematically, for a scalar potential function $V(x, y, z)$, the equipotential surface is defined by:

$$V(x, y, z) = C$$

where $C$ is a constant.

**Proof of Perpendicularity:** Consider two points $P$ and $Q$ on the same equipotential surface, separated by an infinitesimal displacement $d\vec{l}$. The potential difference between them is:

$$dV = \nabla V \cdot d\vec{l}$$

Since both points are on the same equipotential, $V_P = V_Q$, hence $dV = 0$. Therefore:

$$\nabla V \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$

The electric field is $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$, so $\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$. Since $d\vec{l}$ is tangent to the equipotential surface, and the dot product with $\vec{E}$ is zero, $\vec{E}$ must be **perpendicular to the surface** at every point.

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a stream surface? How is it different from a streamline?**

**Model Answer:**

A **streamline** is a curve whose tangent at every point is parallel to the direction of the vector field at that point. For a vector field $\vec{F}(x, y, z)$, streamlines satisfy:

$$\frac{dx}{F_x} = \frac{dy}{F_y} = \frac{dz}{F_z}$$

A **stream surface** is a two-dimensional surface formed by the union of all streamlines passing through a given curve (called the *directrix curve*) that is not itself a streamline. Thus, while a streamline is a **1-D curve**, a stream surface is a **2-D surface**. A stream surface is useful in visualizing how the field flows in 3D, especially in fluid dynamics and electromagnetic energy flow (e.g., Poynting vector stream surfaces).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equipotential surfaces of a point charge $Q$ located at the origin. Show that these surfaces are concentric spheres and verify that the electric field is perpendicular to each equipotential surface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a vector field $\vec{F} = y\hat{a}_x - x\hat{a}_y + 0\hat{a}_z$, find the equation of the stream surface passing through the line $x = 1, y = 0, 0 \leq z \leq 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Equipotential surfaces of a point charge:

The electric scalar potential at a point at distance $r$ from a point charge $Q$ at the origin is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$$

For an equipotential surface, $V = C$ (constant):

$$\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r} = C \implies r = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 C}$$

This is the equation of a sphere of radius $r = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 C}$ centered at the origin. Hence the equipotential surfaces are **concentric spheres**.

The electric field is $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. In spherical coordinates:

$$\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r$$

This field is purely radial, while the outward normal to a sphere of radius $r$ is also $\hat{a}_r$. Therefore $\vec{E} \parallel \hat{a}_n$, confirming $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the equipotential surface at every point.

**(b)** Stream surface of the given field:

The streamline differential equations are:

$$\frac{dx}{y} = \frac{dy}{-x} = \frac{dz}{0}$$

From the third ratio, $dz = 0 \implies z = \text{const}$. The streamlines lie in planes of constant $z$.

Cross-multiplying the first two ratios:

$$-x\,dx = y\,dy$$

Integrating both sides:

$$-\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{y^2}{2} + K$$

$$x^2 + y^2 = -2K = C_1$$

Thus, the streamlines are circles of radius $\sqrt{C_1}$ centered at the origin in each $z = \text{const}$ plane.

The directrix curve is the line $x = 1, y = 0, 0 \leq z \leq 1$. Substituting into $x^2 + y^2 = C_1$:

$$1^2 + 0^2 = C_1 \implies C_1 = 1$$

Therefore, the stream surface passing through this directrix is:

$$\boxed{x^2 + y^2 = 1}$$

This is a **circular cylinder** of radius $1$ centered on the $z$-axis. The stream surface is independent of $z$ because the field has no $z$-component.

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** An infinite line charge with uniform linear charge density $\rho_L$ lies along the $z$-axis. Determine the equipotential surfaces and the constant magnitude surfaces of the electric field. Show that they are both coaxial cylinders. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation of the constant magnitude surfaces of a uniform electric field $\vec{E} = E_0 \hat{a}_x$ in free space. Comment on the physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Equipotential and constant magnitude surfaces of a line charge:

The electric field of an infinite line charge along the $z$-axis (in cylindrical coordinates) is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$$

The scalar potential (with reference at $\rho = \rho_0$):

$$V(\rho) = -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho'}\,d\rho' = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right)$$

**Equipotential surfaces** ($V = C$):

$$\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right) = C \implies \rho = \rho_0 \exp\left(-\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 C}{\rho_L}\right) = \text{const}$$

These are **concentric cylinders** of radius $\rho$ about the $z$-axis.

**Constant magnitude surfaces** ($\vert \vec{E} \vert = k$):

$$\frac{\vert \rho_L \vert}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} = k \implies \rho = \frac{\vert \rho_L \vert}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 k} = \text{const}$$

Again, these are **concentric cylinders** of fixed radius. For the line charge, the equipotential surfaces and constant magnitude surfaces coincide because the field has no $\phi$- or $z$-dependence — the symmetry forces both to be coaxial cylinders.

**(b)** Constant magnitude surfaces of a uniform field:

Given $\vec{E} = E_0 \hat{a}_x$ (constant everywhere in magnitude and direction):

$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \sqrt{E_0^2 + 0 + 0} = \vert E_0 \vert$$

Setting $\vert \vec{E} \vert = k$:

$$\vert E_0 \vert = k$$

**Case 1:** If $k = \vert E_0 \vert$, the entire space satisfies the equation — the constant magnitude surface is the **entire 3D space**.

**Case 2:** If $k \neq \vert E_0 \vert$, there is **no solution** — the constant magnitude surface is the empty set.

**Physical Significance:** A truly uniform field is an idealization (achievable approximately between the plates of a large parallel-plate capacitor far from edges). In such a region, the field has the same magnitude everywhere. Hence the only constant magnitude surface is all of space. There are no "isolated" constant magnitude surfaces, in stark contrast to the point charge (spheres) or line charge (cylinders). This reflects the **translational symmetry** of the uniform field — there is no preferred point or axis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always state the SI unit of any physical quantity. Forgetting to write "V" for potential or "V/m" for electric field will cost at least 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Equipotential with Constant Magnitude:** These are different concepts! Equipotential = $V = C$ (constant potential). Constant magnitude = $\vert \vec{E} \vert = C$ (constant field strength). They coincide for highly symmetric sources (point charge, line charge) but NOT in general (e.g., dipole).
> 3. **Skipping the Perpendicularity Proof:** KTU examiners often allocate 2-3 marks specifically for showing that $\vec{E} \perp$ equipotential surface. Simply stating it without proof is insufficient.
> 4. **Stream Surface vs. Streamline Confusion:** A stream surface is **2D**, formed from a **family of streamlines**, not a single curve.
> 5. **Sign Errors in Gradient:** Remember $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. Dropping the negative sign reverses the field direction.
> 6. **Not Identifying the Coordinate System:** Many students write equations in Cartesian form when the geometry clearly demands cylindrical or spherical coordinates. This leads to unnecessarily complex algebra.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A constant surface is the locus of points where a field quantity is constant: $F(x, y, z) = C$.
- **Three Types:** (i) Equipotential surfaces (constant $V$), (ii) Equi-magnitude surfaces (constant $\vert \vec{F} \vert$), (iii) Stream surfaces (constant direction of $\vec{F}$).
- **Gradient Orthogonality:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ is **always perpendicular** to equipotential surfaces. This is the single most important property.
- **Point Charge:** Equipotentials = Spheres (radius $r = Q/4\pi\varepsilon_0 C$). Field is radial $\propto 1/r^2$.
- **Line Charge:** Equipotentials = Cylinders (radius $\rho = \text{const}$). Field is radial $\propto 1/\rho$.
- **Sheet Charge:** Equipotentials = Parallel planes. Field is uniform between sheets.
- **Streamline ODE:** $\dfrac{dx}{F_x} = \dfrac{dy}{F_y} = \dfrac{dz}{F_z}$.
- **Stream Surface:** A 2D surface formed by all streamlines passing through a non-streamline curve.
- **Dipole Radiation Pattern:** Constant magnitude surfaces are toroidal, described by $r \propto \sin\theta$.
- **Uniform Field:** The only constant magnitude surface is all of space (for the correct $k$) or empty set.
- **Engineering Applications:** Insulator design, CRT beam control, MRI coil design, lightning protection, microwave waveguide analysis.
- **Mathematical Requirement:** For a well-defined 2D surface, $\nabla f \neq \vec{0}$ at every point (Implicit Function Theorem).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $V = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ (point charge)
  - $V = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln(\rho_0/\rho)$ (line charge)
  - $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (fundamental relation)
  - $\hat{a}_n = \nabla f / \vert \nabla f \vert$ (unit normal to level surface $f = C$)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformation of Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transformation of Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformation of Points** is the systematic mathematical procedure of converting the spatial coordinates (and their associated unit vectors, differential lengths, surface, and volume elements) of a single geometric point **P** from one coordinate system to another, while preserving the physical position of the point in three-dimensional Euclidean space.

In the context of **Electromagnetic Field Theory (PCEET502)**, a KTU student is expected to master transformations among three primary orthogonal coordinate systems:

1. **Cartesian (Rectangular) Coordinate System** — $(x, y, z)$
2. **Cylindrical Coordinate System** — $(\rho, \phi, z)$
3. **Spherical Coordinate System** — $(r, \theta, \phi)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Range of Variables (KTU Convention):**
> $-\infty \le x, y, z \le +\infty$
> $0 \le \rho < \infty$, $0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$, $-\infty \le z \le +\infty$
> $0 \le r < \infty$, $0 \le \theta \le \pi$, $0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Translator" Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a friend standing at a particular pillar in a large open stadium. You could describe the location in **three different ways**:

- **Cartesian**: *"Walk 3 meters East, 4 meters North, and stay on the ground floor."* (x, y, z)
- **Cylindrical**: *"Stand 5 meters from the center pillar, face 53.13° East of North, on the ground floor."* (ρ, φ, z) — useful if symmetry exists around a central axis.
- **Spherical**: *"You are 7.07 meters away from the stadium's top center, at 45° downward from the zenith."* (r, θ, φ) — useful if symmetry exists around a single point.

> [!TIP]
> **The point hasn't moved at all.** You are simply *describing the same point in three different languages*. This is the essence of coordinate transformation — it changes the **mathematical description**, not the physical reality.

In Electromagnetic Theory, the **choice of coordinate system can reduce a 3-hour integral into a 3-minute calculation.** A point charge at the origin (spherical symmetry), a long straight wire along z-axis (cylindrical symmetry), and a parallel-plate capacitor (Cartesian symmetry) are classical examples of this principle.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Metrics Used in Transformation

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Nature |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Range of azimuthal angle $\phi$ | $\phi$ | **0 to $2\pi$ radians (0° to 360°)** |
| Range of polar angle $\theta$ (from +z axis) | $\theta$ | **0 to $\pi$ radians (0° to 180°)** |
| Geometric basis | — | Right-handed orthogonal system |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Three Coordinate Systems?

A coordinate system is a **mathematical dictionary**. The physical laws of electromagnetics (Maxwell's equations) are **coordinate-invariant**, meaning they hold true regardless of how we describe space. However, the *mathematical complexity* changes dramatically.

| Coordinate System | Best Suited For | Reason |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Cartesian (x, y, z)** | Rectangular geometries (slabs, cubes) | Boundaries align with constant-coordinate surfaces |
| **Cylindrical (ρ, φ, z)** | Cylinders, coaxial cables, long wires | Axis of symmetry matches the z-axis |
| **Spherical (r, θ, φ)** | Spheres, point charges, antennas, Earth | Origin coincides with the center of symmetry |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### 2.2.1 Coordinate Transformation Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these three blocks. They form ~60% of all Module 1 KTU questions.**

#### (A) Cartesian $\longleftrightarrow$ Cylindrical

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}} \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \\
z &= z
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \rho \cos\phi \\
y &= \rho \sin\phi \\
z &= z
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (B) Cartesian $\longleftrightarrow$ Spherical

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}} \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{z}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}\right) \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= r \sin\theta \cos\phi \\
y &= r \sin\theta \sin\phi \\
z &= r \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (C) Cylindrical $\longleftrightarrow$ Spherical

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{\rho^{2} + z^{2}} \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\rho}{z}\right) \\
\phi &= \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= r \sin\theta \\
\phi &= \phi \\
z &= r \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.2 Unit Vector Transformations

#### Cartesian $\longleftrightarrow$ Cylindrical

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{a}_{\rho} &= \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} \\
\hat{a}_{\phi} &= -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} \\
\hat{a}_{z} &= \hat{a}_{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Cartesian $\longleftrightarrow$ Spherical

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{a}_{r} &= \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_{z} \\
\hat{a}_{\theta} &= \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_{z} \\
\hat{a}_{\phi} &= -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.3 Differential Length, Surface, and Volume Elements

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks to derive or write these expressions. The factor $\rho$ in cylindrical and $r^{2}\sin\theta$ in spherical are *favourite* exam topics.

| System | Differential Length $d\vec{L}$ | Differential Volume $dV$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Cartesian** | $dx\,\hat{a}_{x} + dy\,\hat{a}_{y} + dz\,\hat{a}_{z}$ | $dx\,dy\,dz$ |
| **Cylindrical** | $d\rho\,\hat{a}_{\rho} + \rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi} + dz\,\hat{a}_{z}$ | $\rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ |
| **Spherical** | $dr\,\hat{a}_{r} + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_{\theta} + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi}$ | $r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ |

### 2.2.4 Vector Field Transformation Identity

For any vector field $\vec{A}$:

$$
\vec{A} = A_{x}\hat{a}_{x} + A_{y}\hat{a}_{y} + A_{z}\hat{a}_{z} = A_{\rho}\hat{a}_{\rho} + A_{\phi}\hat{a}_{\phi} + A_{z}\hat{a}_{z} = A_{r}\hat{a}_{r} + A_{\theta}\hat{a}_{\theta} + A_{\phi}\hat{a}_{\phi}
$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Design (Spherical)**: Radiation pattern of a dipole antenna is naturally expressed in $(r, \theta, \phi)$ because power radiates spherically.
- **Coaxial Cable Analysis (Cylindrical)**: The electric field between the inner and outer conductor decays as $1/\rho$, a fundamentally cylindrical result.
- **PCB Trace Analysis (Cartesian)**: Microstrip line field distributions are solved in Cartesian coordinates due to planar geometry.
- **GPS & Satellite Communication (Spherical)**: Earth's curvature mandates spherical coordinates for accurate signal path calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Cartesian $\rightarrow$ Cylindrical

### Setup
Consider a point $P$ in 3D space with Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$. The cylindrical coordinates $(\rho, \phi, z)$ are defined as:

- $\rho$ = perpendicular distance of $P$ from the z-axis
- $\phi$ = angle made by the projection of $P$ on the xy-plane, measured from the +x axis
- $z$ = same as Cartesian z

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Project point P onto the xy-plane.**
The projection $P'$ has coordinates $(x, y, 0)$. By the Pythagorean theorem, the distance from origin to $P'$ is:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Find the angle $\phi$.**
Using trigonometry in the right triangle formed by $x$, $y$, and $\rho$:

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{x}{\rho} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos\phi = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{y}{\rho} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin\phi = \frac{y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
$$

(The $\tan^{-1}$ function uses the quadrant of $(x, y)$ to determine the correct branch.)

**Step 3 — Express x and y in terms of $(\rho, \phi)$.**
From the definitions of $\cos\phi$ and $\sin\phi$:

$$
x = \rho\cos\phi, \qquad y = \rho\sin\phi, \qquad z = z
$$

This completes the forward and inverse transformations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Cartesian $\rightarrow$ Spherical

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the radial distance r.**
The straight-line distance from the origin $O$ to point $P(x, y, z)$ is given by the 3D Euclidean distance:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the azimuthal angle $\phi$.**
The projection of $P$ onto the xy-plane gives $\rho = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$. The angle $\phi$ is exactly the same as in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the polar angle $\theta$.**
In the right triangle formed by $\rho$ (the leg in xy-plane), $z$ (the vertical leg), and $r$ (the hypotenuse):

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{z}{r} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{z}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express $(x, y, z)$ in terms of $(r, \theta, \phi)$.**
We already have $z = r\cos\theta$ and $\rho = r\sin\theta$. Substituting $\rho = r\sin\theta$ into the cylindrical inverse formulas:

$$
x = \rho\cos\phi = r\sin\theta\cos\phi
$$

$$
y = \rho\sin\phi = r\sin\theta\sin\phi
$$

$$
z = r\cos\theta
$$

This is the standard set of spherical-to-Cartesian transformation equations used in **all of KTU Module 1, 2, and 3 problems.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Unit Vector Transformation (Cartesian $\to$ Cylindrical)

This is a frequently asked 14-mark KTU question.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the cylindrical unit vectors in terms of Cartesian components.**

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ points radially outward in the xy-plane. From the geometry, its x-component is $\cos\phi$ and y-component is $\sin\phi$:

$$
\hat{a}_{\rho} = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
$$

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\phi}$ is perpendicular to $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ in the direction of increasing $\phi$ (counter-clockwise). Rotating $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ by $+90°$ in the xy-plane:

$$
\hat{a}_{\phi} = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
$$

$$
\hat{a}_{z} = \hat{a}_{z} \quad\text{(unchanged)}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify orthonormality.**
Dot products:

$$
\hat{a}_{\rho} \cdot \hat{a}_{\phi} = (\cos\phi)(-\sin\phi) + (\sin\phi)(\cos\phi) + 0 = 0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
\vert\hat{a}_{\rho}\vert = \sqrt{\cos^{2}\phi + \sin^{2}\phi} = 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Form the rotation matrix.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \hat{a}_{\rho} \\ \hat{a}_{\phi} \\ \hat{a}_{z} \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\phi & \sin\phi & 0 \\
-\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \hat{a}_{x} \\ \hat{a}_{y} \\ \hat{a}_{z} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The inverse transformation is obtained by transposing the matrix (since it is orthogonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{a}_{x} &= \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{\rho} - \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi} \\
\hat{a}_{y} &= \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{\rho} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi} \\
\hat{a}_{z} &= \hat{a}_{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Differential Volume Element

### (A) Cylindrical Volume Element

Imagine an infinitesimal box bounded by constant-coordinate surfaces at $(\rho, \phi, z)$ and $(\rho+d\rho, \phi+d\phi, z+dz)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the three edge lengths.**
- Moving along $\hat{a}_{\rho}$: arc length = $d\rho$
- Moving along $\hat{a}_{\phi}$: arc length = $\rho\,d\phi$ (the famous factor $\rho$ arises because we sweep an arc of radius $\rho$)
- Moving along $\hat{a}_{z}$: arc length = $dz$

**Step 2 — Multiply the three orthogonal edges.**

$$
dV = (d\rho)(\rho\,d\phi)(dz) = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz
$$

### (B) Spherical Volume Element

The arc lengths in spherical coordinates are:

- Along $\hat{a}_{r}$: $dr$
- Along $\hat{a}_{\theta}$: $r\,d\theta$
- Along $\hat{a}_{\phi}$: $r\sin\theta\,d\phi$ (the radius of the parallel circle is $r\sin\theta$)

Multiplying:

$$
dV = (dr)(r\,d\theta)(r\sin\theta\,d\phi) = r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The factor $\sin\theta$** comes from projecting the radius $r$ onto the xy-plane. At the equator ($\theta = 90°$), the parallel circle has maximum radius $r$. At the poles ($\theta = 0°, 180°$), it collapses to zero — hence $\sin\theta$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| KTU Problem Pattern | Input Given | Output Required | Key Formula(s) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Point location conversion | $(x, y, z)$ | $(\rho, \phi, z)$ | $\rho=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$, $\phi=\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ |
| Point location conversion | $(x, y, z)$ | $(r, \theta, \phi)$ | $r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}$, $\theta=\cos^{-1}(z/r)$ |
| Vector conversion | $\vec{A}$ in Cartesian | $\vec{A}$ in Cylindrical | Use $\hat{a}_{\rho}, \hat{a}_{\phi}$ projection formulas |
| Field integral setup | Spherical symmetry, charge at origin | $dV$ for volume integral | $dV = r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ |
| Distance between two points | $(r_1,\theta_1,\phi_1)$ and $(r_2,\theta_2,\phi_2)$ | Chord length | Use Cartesian intermediate form |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometry of the Three Coordinate Systems (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ORIGIN O = 0,0,0"] --> B["POINT P IN 3D SPACE"]
    B --> C["CARTESIAN x,y,z"]
    B --> D["CYLINDRICAL rho,phi,z"]
    B --> E["SPHERICAL r,theta,phi"]
    C --> F["dL = dx ax + dy ay + dz az"]
    C --> G["dV = dx dy dz"]
    D --> H["dL = d rho a rho + rho d phi a phi + dz az"]
    D --> I["dV = rho d rho d phi dz"]
    E --> J["dL = dr ar + r d theta a theta + r sin theta d phi a phi"]
    E --> K["dV = r squared sin theta dr d theta d phi"]
    C -- "rho equals sqrt x2 + y2" --> D
    C -- "r equals sqrt x2 + y2 + z2" --> E
    D -- "rho equals r sin theta" --> E
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Coordinate Transformation

```mermaid
graph LR
    IP["INPUT POINT COORDINATES x,y,z"] --> M1{"SYMMETRY CHECK"}
    M1 -- "AXIAL SYMMETRY" --> OUT1["OUTPUT rho,phi,z"]
    M1 -- "FULL SPHERICAL SYMMETRY" --> OUT2["OUTPUT r,theta,phi"]
    M1 -- "NO SYMMETRY" --> OUT3["RETAIN x,y,z"]
    OUT1 --> VT1["VECTOR TRANSFORM: a rho, a phi, a z"]
    OUT2 --> VT2["VECTOR TRANSFORM: ar, a theta, a phi"]
    VT1 --> SOL["SOLVE MAXWELL OR INTEGRAL"]
    VT2 --> SOL
    OUT3 --> SOL
    SOL --> RES["FINAL FIELD / CHARGE / FLUX"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map of Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["STEP 1: COORDINATE TRANSFORM"] --> S2["STEP 2: UNIT VECTOR TRANSFORM"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: FIELD COMPONENT TRANSFORM"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: DIFFERENTIAL ELEMENT TRANSFORM"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: APPLY MAXWELL OR INTEGRAL EQUATION"]
    S1 --> S1A["x,y,z to rho,phi,z"]
    S1 --> S1B["x,y,z to r,theta,phi"]
    S2 --> S2A["Cartesian to Cylindrical a rho and a phi"]
    S2 --> S2B["Cartesian to Spherical ar a theta a phi"]
    S3 --> S3A["A rho equals A x cos phi + A y sin phi"]
    S3 --> S3B["A phi equals negative A x sin phi + A y cos phi"]
    S4 --> S4A["dV Cylindrical equals rho d rho d phi dz"]
    S4 --> S4B["dV Spherical equals r squared sin theta dr d theta d phi"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**: The arrows indicate *information flow*. A student must complete Step 1 before attempting Step 5. Skipping steps is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks on transformation-based problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between Cartesian and spherical coordinate systems. Define the range of all three spherical coordinates.**

**Model Answer:**

The transformation from Cartesian $(x, y, z)$ to spherical $(r, \theta, \phi)$ coordinates is given by:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}}, \quad \theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}\right), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
$$

The inverse relations are:

$$
x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta
$$

**Range of spherical coordinates:** $0 \le r < \infty$, $0 \le \theta \le \pi$, $0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$.

The angle $\theta$ is the **polar angle** measured from the positive z-axis, and $\phi$ is the **azimuthal angle** measured from the positive x-axis in the xy-plane.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Express the differential volume element $dV$ in (i) cylindrical and (ii) spherical coordinate systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cylindrical:** $dV = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$

**(ii) Spherical:** $dV = r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$

The factor $\rho$ in cylindrical arises from the arc length $\rho\,d\phi$ swept in the azimuthal direction. The factor $r^{2}\sin\theta$ in spherical arises from multiplying the arc length $r\sin\theta\,d\phi$ in the azimuthal direction with $r\,d\theta$ in the polar direction and $dr$ in the radial direction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the transformation equations relating **Cartesian to Cylindrical** coordinates. Express the unit vectors $\hat{a}_{x}$, $\hat{a}_{y}$ in terms of $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ and $\hat{a}_{\phi}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vector field is given by $\vec{A} = 3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{y} + 5\hat{a}_{z}$ at a point $P(3, 4, 0)$. Express $\vec{A}$ in cylindrical coordinates at the same point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Derivation of coordinate transformation:**

Consider a point $P$ in 3D space with coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in Cartesian and $(\rho, \phi, z)$ in cylindrical. The projection of $P$ on the xy-plane gives a point $P'$ at distance $\rho$ from the origin.

By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}
$$

The angle $\phi$ is given by:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
$$

The z-coordinate is identical in both systems:

$$
z = z
$$

**Inverse relations:** From the right triangle in the xy-plane:

$$
x = \rho\cos\phi, \quad y = \rho\sin\phi
$$

**Unit vector transformation:**

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ points radially outward. Decomposing it along the x and y axes:

$$
\hat{a}_{\rho} = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
$$

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\phi}$ is perpendicular to $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ in the direction of increasing $\phi$ (i.e., counter-clockwise):

$$
\hat{a}_{\phi} = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
$$

**Expressing $\hat{a}_{x}$ and $\hat{a}_{y}$ in terms of $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ and $\hat{a}_{\phi}$:**

This is the inverse of the 2x2 rotation matrix. Multiply the first equation by $\cos\phi$ and the second by $\sin\phi$ and add:

$$
\hat{a}_{x} = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{\rho} - \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi}
$$

Similarly, multiply the first by $\sin\phi$ and the second by $\cos\phi$ and add:

$$
\hat{a}_{y} = \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{\rho} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{\phi}
$$

The z-axis unit vector is unchanged: $\hat{a}_{z} = \hat{a}_{z}$.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify $\phi$ at the given point.**

At $P(3, 4, 0)$: $x = 3, y = 4$.

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 53.13°
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6, \quad \sin\phi = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cylindrical field components using the rotation matrix.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} A_{\rho} \\ A_{\phi} \\ A_{z} \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\phi & \sin\phi & 0 \\
-\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each component:

$$
A_{\rho} = \cos\phi \cdot 3 + \sin\phi \cdot 4 + 0 = (0.6)(3) + (0.8)(4) = 1.8 + 3.2 = 5.0
$$

$$
A_{\phi} = -\sin\phi \cdot 3 + \cos\phi \cdot 4 + 0 = -(0.8)(3) + (0.6)(4) = -2.4 + 2.4 = 0
$$

$$
A_{z} = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Write the vector in cylindrical form.**

$$
\boxed{\vec{A} = 5\,\hat{a}_{\rho} + 0\,\hat{a}_{\phi} + 5\,\hat{a}_{z} = 5\hat{a}_{\rho} + 5\hat{a}_{z}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vanishing of $A_{\phi}$ is not a coincidence. The vector $\vec{A} = (3, 4, 5)$ at the point $(3, 4, 0)$ happens to point exactly in the radial direction at that point because the vector from origin to $P$ is also $(3, 4, 0)$, which is parallel to $(3, 4, 0)$.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the transformation equations relating **Cartesian to Spherical** coordinates. Also derive expressions for the unit vectors $\hat{a}_{r}$, $\hat{a}_{\theta}$, $\hat{a}_{\phi}$ in terms of $\hat{a}_{x}$, $\hat{a}_{y}$, $\hat{a}_{z}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point $P$ has spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi) = (5, 60°, 30°)$. Determine (i) the Cartesian coordinates of $P$, and (ii) the distance of $P$ from the z-axis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Derivation of coordinate transformation:**

Let the Cartesian coordinates of point $P$ be $(x, y, z)$ and spherical coordinates be $(r, \theta, \phi)$.

**Step 1 — Radial distance $r$:** Using 3D Euclidean distance:

$$
r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Azimuthal angle $\phi$:** From the xy-plane projection:

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Polar angle $\theta$:** The angle from the +z axis. In the right triangle with hypotenuse $r$ and opposite side $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$:

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{r}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{z}\right)
$$

**Inverse relations:** The z-coordinate is the projection of $r$ onto the z-axis: $z = r\cos\theta$. The projection onto the xy-plane has length $\rho = r\sin\theta$. Using the cylindrical inverse relations $x = \rho\cos\phi$ and $y = \rho\sin\phi$:

$$
x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta
$$

**Unit vector derivation:**

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{r}$ points radially outward. Its components along the three Cartesian axes are obtained by projecting the unit vector onto each axis:

- Along x-axis: $x/r = \sin\theta\cos\phi$
- Along y-axis: $y/r = \sin\theta\sin\phi$
- Along z-axis: $z/r = \cos\theta$

$$
\hat{a}_{r} = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_{z}
$$

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\theta}$ is tangent to the meridian in the direction of increasing $\theta$. It is obtained by differentiating $\hat{a}_{r}$ with respect to $\theta$ (which is the same as partial derivative due to its magnitude being unity):

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{a}_{r}}{\partial \theta} = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_{z}
$$

Since $\partial \hat{a}_{r}/\partial \theta$ is a unit vector perpendicular to $\hat{a}_{r}$ in the direction of increasing $\theta$:

$$
\hat{a}_{\theta} = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_{z}
$$

The unit vector $\hat{a}_{\phi}$ is tangent to the parallel circle in the direction of increasing $\phi$:

$$
\hat{a}_{\phi} = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}
$$

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:** $(r, \theta, \phi) = (5, 60°, 30°)$.

**Step 1 — Compute trigonometric values.**

$$
\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5
$$

$$
\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

**(i) Cartesian coordinates of P:**

$$
x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi = 5 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 5 \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75
$$

$$
y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi = 5 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 2.165
$$

$$
z = r\cos\theta = 5 \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.5
$$

$$
\boxed{P \equiv (3.75,\; 2.165,\; 2.5) \text{ in Cartesian}}
$$

**(ii) Distance of P from the z-axis:**

The distance from the z-axis is the perpendicular distance, which equals $\rho$ in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}} = r\sin\theta = 5 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 4.330 \text{ units}
$$

**Verification:** $x^2 + y^2 = (3.75)^2 + (2.165)^2 = 14.0625 + 4.6872 = 18.7497 \approx 18.75$. $\sqrt{18.75} \approx 4.33$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Polar vs Azimuthal Confusion (–2 marks):** Students often swap $\theta$ and $\phi$ definitions. **Always remember**: $\theta$ is measured from the **+z axis (polar)**, and $\phi$ is measured from the **+x axis in the xy-plane (azimuthal)**. The range $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ is also frequently violated.
>
> 2. **Unit Vector Mistakes (–2 to –4 marks):** The unit vectors $\hat{a}_{\rho}$ and $\hat{a}_{\phi}$ are **not constant**; they depend on $\phi$. So $\partial \hat{a}_{\rho}/\partial \phi \neq 0$. KTU specifically tests this.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\rho$ in Cylindrical $dV$ (–1 mark):** Many students write $dV = d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ instead of $\rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$. The factor $\rho$ comes from the arc length $\rho\,d\phi$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $\sin\theta$ in Spherical $dV$ (–1 mark):** Similarly, $dV = r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$, not $r^{2}\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$.
>
> 5. **Inverse Trigonometric Domain Error (–1 mark):** $\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ returns values in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. You **must manually adjust** the angle based on the quadrant of $(x, y)$. KTU examiners mark this strictly.
>
> 6. **Vector Component Sign Error (–1 mark):** The component $A_{\phi} = -A_x\sin\phi + A_y\cos\phi$. Forgetting the negative sign is a common slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Transformation of Points**

- **Three coordinate systems:** Cartesian $(x, y, z)$, Cylindrical $(\rho, \phi, z)$, Spherical $(r, \theta, \phi)$.
- **Ranges:** $r \in [0, \infty)$, $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$.
- **Foundational transformation equations** (must be memorized):
  - $\rho = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$, $z = z$
  - $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$, $\theta = \cos^{-1}(z/r)$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$
  - $x = \rho\cos\phi = r\sin\theta\cos\phi$, $y = \rho\sin\phi = r\sin\theta\sin\phi$, $z = r\cos\theta$
- **Unit vector formulas** (Cartesian to Cylindrical):
  - $\hat{a}_{\rho} = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}$
  - $\hat{a}_{\phi} = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}$
- **Unit vector formulas** (Cartesian to Spherical):
  - $\hat{a}_{r} = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_{z}$
  - $\hat{a}_{\theta} = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{y} - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_{z}$
  - $\hat{a}_{\phi} = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_{x} + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_{y}$
- **Differential volume elements** (highly tested):
  - Cartesian: $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$
  - Cylindrical: $dV = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$
  - Spherical: $dV = r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$
- **All three coordinate systems are right-handed and mutually orthogonal.**
- **The transformation is invertible** (rotation matrix is orthogonal, so the inverse is the transpose).
- **KTU Hot Spot**: KTU frequently asks (a) a 7-mark derivation + (b) a 7-mark numerical. Practice both the algebraic derivation and at least 3 numerical problems.
- **Engineering Application Trigger Words**:
  - "Point charge at origin" $\to$ use spherical.
  - "Long straight wire along z-axis" $\to$ use cylindrical.
  - "Parallel plates / rectangular slab" $\to$ use Cartesian.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformation of Unit Vectors and Vectors Among the Three Coordinate Systems - Transformation Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transformation of Unit Vectors and Vectors Among the Three Coordinate Systems - Transformation Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Electromagnetic Theory, physical quantities such as electric field intensity $\vec{E}$, magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$, and position vectors $\vec{r}$ must often be expressed in the coordinate system that best matches the geometry of the problem. The **three principal orthogonal coordinate systems** employed in KTU's Electromagnetic Theory syllabus are:

| System | Coordinates | Unit Vectors | Differential Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular (Cartesian)** | $(x, y, z)$ | $\hat{a}_x,\; \hat{a}_y,\; \hat{a}_z$ | $d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ |
| **Cylindrical** | $(r, \phi, z)$ | $\hat{a}_r,\; \hat{a}_\phi,\; \hat{a}_z$ | $d\vec{l} = dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ |
| **Spherical** | $(R, \theta, \phi)$ | $\hat{a}_R,\; \hat{a}_\theta,\; \hat{a}_\phi$ | $d\vec{l} = dR\,\hat{a}_R + R\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + R\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus, Module 1):**
> **Transformation of vectors** is the systematic procedure of expressing a vector $\vec{A}$ (or its unit vectors) defined in one coordinate system as an equivalent expression in another coordinate system, preserving its magnitude and physical direction in 3D space.

**Mathematically**, if $\vec{A} = A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z$ in rectangular form, then the same vector in cylindrical form is $\vec{A} = A_r\hat{a}_r + A_\phi\hat{a}_\phi + A_z\hat{a}_z$ where the new components are obtained using a **transformation matrix** built from the **direction cosines** (dot products) between the unit vectors of the two systems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Weather Reporter's Compass**
>
> Imagine you are a weather reporter describing wind direction:
> - In **Cartesian** terms, you say "wind is blowing 5 m/s East, 2 m/s North, 0 m/s upward."
> - In **Cylindrical** terms (useful for cyclones), you say "wind is 5 m/s radially outward, 2 m/s tangentially (azimuthally)."
> - In **Spherical** terms (useful for global wind patterns from a satellite), you say "wind is X m/s outward from Earth center, Y m/s southward, Z m/s eastward."
>
> All three descriptions refer to the **same physical wind** — we are just changing the *language* of measurement. The **transformation equations** are the dictionary that translates between these languages **without losing any information** about the actual wind vector.

The **direction cosines** (the dot products like $\hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_x = \cos\phi$) are the *translation rules* in this dictionary — they tell us "how much of $\hat{a}_x$ points along $\hat{a}_r$."

### 1.3 Why Transformations Matter in KTU Examinations

- The dot product of a unit vector with another gives the **cosine of the angle between them**, which forms the **direction cosine matrix** (transformation matrix).
- For any vector $\vec{A}$, the component along a new unit vector $\hat{a}_u$ is found by $A_u = \vec{A} \cdot \hat{a}_u$.
- These matrices are **orthogonal**, meaning their inverse equals their transpose: $Q^{-1} = Q^T$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Antenna radiation pattern problems, satellite link budgets, robotic arm kinematics, and stress analysis in mechanical components all rely on these transformation matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Direction Cosines

The transformation between any two orthogonal coordinate systems is governed by the **direction cosines** $\ell_{ij}$, defined as the dot product between a unit vector of the *new* system and a unit vector of the *old* system:

$$\ell_{ij} = \hat{a}_{u_i} \cdot \hat{a}_{v_j}$$

where $\hat{a}_{u_i}$ is the $i$-th unit vector of the *new* system and $\hat{a}_{v_j}$ is the $j$-th unit vector of the *old* system.

The complete transformation matrix $Q$ is a **3×3 orthogonal matrix** whose rows/columns are unit vectors of one system expressed in the other.

---

### 2.2 Case A: Rectangular ↔ Cylindrical Transformation

#### 2.2.1 Coordinate Relationships

The geometric setup places the **z-axis** as the common axis:

$$x = r\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\phi, \quad z = z$$

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right), \quad z = z$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU convention uses $\phi$ (azimuthal angle) measured **from the +x axis toward the +y axis in the xy-plane**. This is consistent with standard engineering texts.

#### 2.2.2 Unit Vector Transformation (Rectangular → Cylindrical)

By taking dot products of $\hat{a}_r$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ with $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$:

$$\hat{a}_r = (\hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_x)\hat{a}_x + (\hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_y)\hat{a}_y + (\hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_z)\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_r = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + 0\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y + 0\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_z = 0\,\hat{a}_x + 0\,\hat{a}_y + \hat{a}_z$$

#### 2.2.3 Inverse Transformation (Cylindrical → Rectangular)

$$\hat{a}_x = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_r - \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\hat{a}_y = \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_r + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

#### 2.2.4 Vector Component Transformation

If $\vec{A} = A_x\hat{a}_x + A_y\hat{a}_y + A_z\hat{a}_z$, then in cylindrical form:

$$A_r = A_x\cos\phi + A_y\sin\phi$$

$$A_\phi = -A_x\sin\phi + A_y\cos\phi$$

$$A_z = A_z$$

---

### 2.3 Case B: Rectangular ↔ Spherical Transformation

#### 2.3.1 Coordinate Relationships

$$x = R\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = R\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = R\cos\theta$$

$$R = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{R}\right), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** $\theta$ is the **polar angle** measured from the +z-axis ($0 \le \theta \le \pi$), and $\phi$ is the **azimuthal angle** measured from the +x-axis in the xy-plane ($0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$).

#### 2.3.2 Unit Vector Transformation (Rectangular → Spherical)

$$\hat{a}_R = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y + 0\,\hat{a}_z$$

#### 2.3.3 Inverse Transformation (Spherical → Rectangular)

$$\hat{a}_x = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_R + \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\theta - \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\hat{a}_y = \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_R + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\theta + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\hat{a}_z = \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_R - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta$$

---

### 2.4 Case C: Cylindrical ↔ Spherical Transformation

#### 2.4.1 Coordinate Relationships

$$r = R\sin\theta, \quad \phi = \phi, \quad z = R\cos\theta$$

$$R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r}{z}\right), \quad \phi = \phi$$

#### 2.4.2 Unit Vector Transformation (Cylindrical → Spherical)

$$\hat{a}_R = \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_r + 0\,\hat{a}_\phi + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_r + 0\,\hat{a}_\phi - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\hat{a}_\phi = 0\,\hat{a}_r + \hat{a}_\phi + 0\,\hat{a}_z$$

#### 2.4.3 Inverse Transformation (Spherical → Cylindrical)

$$\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_R + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta$$

$$\hat{a}_\phi = \hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\hat{a}_z = \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_R - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Transformation | Matrix Equation | Orthogonal Property |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular → Cylindrical | $\begin{bmatrix} A_r \\ A_\phi \\ A_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & \sin\phi & 0 \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A_x \\ A_y \\ A_z \end{bmatrix}$ | $Q^T = Q^{-1}$ |
| Cylindrical → Rectangular | $\begin{bmatrix} A_x \\ A_y \\ A_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -\sin\phi & 0 \\ \sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A_r \\ A_\phi \\ A_z \end{bmatrix}$ | $Q^T = Q^{-1}$ |
| Rectangular → Spherical | $\begin{bmatrix} A_R \\ A_\theta \\ A_\phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta\cos\phi & \sin\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta \\ \cos\theta\cos\phi & \cos\theta\sin\phi & -\sin\theta \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A_x \\ A_y \\ A_z \end{bmatrix}$ | $Q^T = Q^{-1}$ |
| Cylindrical → Spherical | $\begin{bmatrix} A_R \\ A_\theta \\ A_\phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\ \cos\theta & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A_r \\ A_\phi \\ A_z \end{bmatrix}$ | $Q^T = Q^{-1}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These matrices are used in robotics (joint transformations), antenna array design (beam steering), satellite attitude control, and computational electromagnetics (FDTD method boundary handling).

---

### 2.6 Why These Matrices Are Orthogonal

A matrix $Q$ is **orthogonal** if and only if $Q^T Q = I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix. The unit vector transformation matrices satisfy this because:

- Each row has a magnitude of **1** (rows are unit vectors in the old system).
- Each column has a magnitude of **1** (columns are unit vectors in the new system).
- Different rows are **mutually orthogonal** (since the unit vectors of any orthogonal system are mutually perpendicular).
- This means the **inverse equals the transpose**: $Q^{-1} = Q^T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinant property:** $\det(Q) = \pm 1$ (for right-handed systems, $\det(Q) = +1$, indicating a proper rotation preserving handedness).

---
