$$\int_{S_1} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = EA, \qquad \int_{S_2} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = EA, \qquad \int_{S_3} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$$

Total flux:

$$\Phi_E = 2EA$$

**Step 4 — Apply Gauss's Law** with $Q_{enc} = \rho_s A$:

$$2EA = \frac{\rho_s A}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_s}{2\varepsilon_0} \, \hat{n}}$$

This result is remarkable: the field of an infinite sheet is **uniform** and independent of distance.

### 3.4 Derivation: Uniformly Charged Sphere

Consider a sphere of radius $a$ with uniform volume charge density $\rho_v$.

**Step 1 — Compute enclosed charge for $r < a$:**

$$Q_{enc} = \rho_v \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 2 — Choose a spherical Gaussian surface** of radius $r < a$. By symmetry:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = E \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{\rho_v \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the interior field:**

$$\vec{E}_{in} = \frac{\rho_v \, r}{3\varepsilon_0} \, \hat{r} \quad (r < a)$$

**Step 4 — For the exterior ($r > a$)**, the enclosed charge equals the total charge $Q_{tot} = \rho_v \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi a^3$:

$$\vec{E}_{out} = \frac{Q_{tot}}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \, \hat{r} \quad (r \geq a)$$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Charge Distribution | Symmetry | Gaussian Surface | Result for $E$ |
|---------------------|----------|------------------|----------------|
| Point charge $Q$ | Spherical | Sphere (radius $r$) | $E = Q/(4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2)$ |
| Infinite line $\rho_L$ | Cylindrical | Coaxial cylinder | $E = \rho_L/(2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho)$ |
| Infinite sheet $\rho_s$ | Planar | Pillbox | $E = \rho_s/(2\varepsilon_0)$ |
| Uniform sphere (inside) | Spherical | Concentric sphere | $E = \rho_v r/(3\varepsilon_0)$ |
| Uniform sphere (outside) | Spherical | Concentric sphere | $E = Q/(4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2)$ |
| Conducting sphere (surface) | Spherical | Concentric sphere | $E = \rho_s/\varepsilon_0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Gauss's Law Application Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Charge Distribution] --> B{Identify Symmetry Class}
    B -->|Radial| C1[Spherical Symmetry]
    B -->|Axial| C2[Cylindrical Symmetry]
    B -->|Translational| C3[Planar Symmetry]
    B -->|None| Z1[Gauss Law Not Directly Applicable Use Superposition or Numerical Methods]

    C1 --> D1[Choose Spherical Gaussian Surface Radius r]
    C2 --> D2[Choose Coaxial Cylinder Radius rho Length L]
    C3 --> D3[Choose Pillbox Cross Section A Height 2z]

    D1 --> E1[E dot dA simplifies to E times 4 pi r squared]
    D2 --> E2[Split surface into curved plus two caps]
    D3 --> E3[Split surface into top face plus bottom face plus side]

    E1 --> F1[Enclosed charge Qenc depends on r vs a]
    E2 --> F2[Enclosed charge equals rhoL times L]
    E3 --> F3[Enclosed charge equals rhoS times A]

    F1 --> G1[Solve for E vector as function of r]
    F2 --> G2[Solve for E vector as function of rho]
    F3 --> G3[Solve for E vector as uniform constant]

    G1 --> H[Apply Boundary Conditions if Required]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Final Expression for Electric Field]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Conceptual Flow of Gauss's Law

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Charge Distribution Source] --> FLUX[Electric Flux Generation]
    FLUX --> GS[Gaussian Closed Surface S]
    GS --> INT[Surface Integral of E dot dA]
    INT --> REL[Relate to Enclosed Charge]
    REL --> OUT[Solve for E Field Distribution]
    OUT --> APP[Engineering Application Capacitor Cable Shield]

    SRC -.Symmetry Analysis.-> GS
    INT -.Boundary Conditions.-> OUT
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Flux Through a Closed Surface

```mermaid
graph TB
    QPOS[Positive Charge Q Inside] -->|Field Lines Outward| SURF[Gaussian Surface S]
    SURF -->|Net Flux Positive| RESULT1[Phi E greater than zero]
    QNEG[Negative Charge Inside] -->|Field Lines Inward| SURF
    SURF -->|Net Flux Negative| RESULT2[Phi E less than zero]
    SURF -->|If Qenc equals zero| RESULT3[Phi E equals zero]
```

### 4.4 Diagram Note

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above Mermaid block diagrams represent the *functional flow architecture* of how Gauss's Law is systematically applied in engineering analysis. In KTU university examinations, students are expected to draw **neat, labeled Gaussian surfaces** (sphere, cylinder, or pillbox) around the charge distribution and indicate the direction of $\vec{E}$ and $d\vec{A}$. Practice drawing these schematics by hand for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]:**

> State Gauss's Law in integral form. Mention any two limitations of the law.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Gauss's Law (Integral Form):** The total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the total charge enclosed by that surface, divided by the permittivity of the free space.
>
> $$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. It gives the relation between flux and enclosed charge but is most useful only when the charge distribution has **spherical, cylindrical, or planar symmetry**. For irregular distributions, the law is mathematically valid but practically unsolvable in closed form.
> 2. It cannot directly compute $\vec{E}$ when the charge is **outside** the Gaussian surface (the flux is zero, but $\vec{E}$ may still be non-zero at the surface).
> 3. It is a **macroscopic** law and does not describe quantum or atomic-scale charge behavior.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**

> Define electric flux. What is its SI unit? What is the total flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge of $+5\,\mu\text{C}$ and $-3\,\mu\text{C}$?

**Model Answer:**

**Electric Flux ($\Phi_E$):** It is the measure of the number of electric field lines passing through a given surface. Mathematically,

$$\Phi_E = \int_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

**SI Unit:** Volt-meter (V·m) or equivalently Newton-meter-squared per Coulomb (N·m²/C).

**Numerical Computation:**
Net enclosed charge: $Q_{enc} = (+5) + (-3) = +2\,\mu\text{C} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{C}$

$$\Phi_E = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.259 \times 10^{5} \text{ V·m}$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive Gauss's Law in integral form starting from Coulomb's Law for a point charge. (7 marks)

**(b)** A charge of $Q = 8.85\,\mu\text{C}$ is placed at the center of a cube of side $10\,\text{cm}$. Find the total flux through each face of the cube. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above. Outline the derivation:

1. Start with Coulomb's expression: $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{r}$.
2. Construct spherical Gaussian surface, write $d\vec{A} = r^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi\, \hat{r}$.
3. Show $\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$.
4. Integrate: $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi = 4\pi$.
5. Obtain $\Phi_E = Q/\varepsilon_0$.
6. Extend to multiple charges using superposition: $\Phi_E = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon_0$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Total flux through the entire closed surface (cube):**

By Gauss's Law:

$$\Phi_{total} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{8.85 \times 10^{-6}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = 10^{6} \text{ V·m}$$

**Step 2 — Flux through each face:**

By symmetry, since the charge is at the geometric center of the cube, the flux distributes equally among the 6 faces:

$$\Phi_{face} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{6} = \frac{10^{6}}{6} = 1.667 \times 10^{5} \text{ V·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by forgetting to **divide by 6** when the charge is at the center. If the charge is **not** at the center, you cannot assume equal distribution. Also, do not confuse this with the sphere case — the flux is independent of surface shape, but **not** independent of symmetry placement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and explain Gauss's Law. Using it, derive the expression for the electric field intensity due to an infinite line of charge. (7 marks)

**(b)** Two infinite coaxial cylinders carry uniform line charges $\rho_{L1} = +5\,\text{nC/m}$ (inner, radius $a = 2\,\text{cm}$) and $\rho_{L2} = -3\,\text{nC/m}$ (outer, radius $b = 5\,\text{cm}$). Find the electric field at radial distances (i) $\rho = 1\,\text{cm}$, (ii) $\rho = 3\,\text{cm}$, (iii) $\rho = 10\,\text{cm}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.2** for the complete derivation. Key steps:

1. State the law: $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon_0$.
2. Choose cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ and length $L$.
3. Decompose flux into curved surface + two caps.
4. Curved surface contributes $E \cdot 2\pi\rho L$; caps contribute zero.
5. With $Q_{enc} = \rho_L L$, solve: $\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{\rho}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Region (i): $\rho = 1\,\text{cm} < a$ — Inside inner cylinder.**

No enclosed charge (cylinders are line charges, not volume distributions for this problem):

$$Q_{enc} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{E} = 0$$

**Region (ii): $\rho = 3\,\text{cm}$, where $a < \rho < b$ — Between cylinders.**

Enclosed charge per unit length: $\rho_{L,enc} = \rho_{L1} = +5\,\text{nC/m} = 5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{C/m}$

$$E = \frac{\rho_{L,enc}}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.03}$$

$$E = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.669 \times 10^{-12}} = 2996 \approx 3000 \text{ V/m}$$

Direction: outward (positive charge enclosed) — $\hat{\rho}$ direction.

**Region (iii): $\rho = 10\,\text{cm} > b$ — Outside both cylinders.**

Enclosed charge per unit length: $\rho_{L,enc} = \rho_{L1} + \rho_{L2} = +5 - 3 = +2\,\text{nC/m}$

$$E = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.10} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{5.563 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 359.5 \text{ V/m}$$

Direction: radially outward.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign errors:** Always account for the sign of enclosed charge. Negative enclosed charge reverses the direction of $\vec{E}$.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Convert all distances to **meters** before substituting into formulas. Mixing cm and m costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Region selection:** Clearly state which Gaussian surface region applies (inside inner / between / outside) and justify with the inequality $\rho$ vs. $a$ and $b$.
> 4. **Direction:** KTU expects the **unit vector** (e.g., $\hat{\rho}$ or $\hat{r}$) to be explicitly mentioned.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Gauss's Law (Integral Form):**

- **Core Law:** $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \dfrac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$ — memorize the exact form.
- **Sign Convention:** Outward normal convention; positive flux = net positive enclosed charge.
- **Symmetry is Key:** Spherical → sphere; Cylindrical → cylinder; Planar → pillbox. No other symmetries yield clean closed-form solutions.
- **Two Must-Know Results:** $E = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\rho}$ (line) and $E = \dfrac{\rho_s}{2\varepsilon_0}$ (sheet) — both are favorite KTU questions.
- **Uniform Sphere:** $E_{in} = \dfrac{\rho_v r}{3\varepsilon_0}$, $E_{out} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ — remember the **transition** at $r = a$.
- **Gauss's Law ≠ Coulomb's Law:** Gauss's Law is more general and holds even when Coulomb's Law fails (e.g., moving charges in relativistic contexts, with appropriate modifications via Maxwell's equations).
- **Flux through cube face** = (Total flux) / 6, **only if** the charge is at the **center** of the cube.
- **Limitation:** Cannot compute $\vec{E}$ for asymmetric charge distributions using Gauss's Law alone.
- **Boundary Condition:** $\hat{n} \cdot (\vec{D}_2 - \vec{D}_1) = \rho_s$ — the normal component of $\vec{D}$ jumps by the surface charge density.
- **Always include units** in your final numerical answer (V/m for $\vec{E}$, V·m for flux).
- **Practice drawing** the Gaussian surface with proper labeling of $\vec{E}$, $d\vec{A}$, $\hat{n}$, and the symmetry axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Flux Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Flux Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electric Flux Density**, also known as the **Electric Displacement Field** or **D-field**, is a vector field denoted by $\vec{D}$ that represents the density of electric flux lines in a given region of space. It is defined mathematically as the product of the permittivity of the medium and the electric field intensity.

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$$

where:
- $\vec{D}$ = Electric Flux Density (measured in **Coulombs per square meter, C/m²**)
- $\varepsilon$ = Permittivity of the medium (measured in **Farads per meter, F/m**)
- $\vec{E}$ = Electric Field Intensity (measured in **Volts per meter, V/m**)

For a free space (vacuum) scenario, this relation becomes:

$$\vec{D}_0 = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the **permittivity of free space**, with a numerical value of approximately **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** (or more precisely $\frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9}$ F/m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The vector $\vec{D}$ is exclusively defined at a point in a dielectric medium. It is a macroscopic field quantity that excludes the effects of bound (polarization) charges and focuses purely on free charges.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose. The **water pressure** at any point is analogous to the **electric field** ($\vec{E}$), which describes the force a charge would experience. However, the **amount of water actually flowing** through a cross-sectional area depends not only on the pressure but also on the type of soil it passes through — sandy soil allows more water flow than clay.

In this analogy:
- **Water Pressure** $\equiv$ Electric Field $\vec{E}$
- **Type of Soil (permeability to water)** $\equiv$ Permittivity $\varepsilon$ of the medium
- **Actual Water Flow per unit Area** $\equiv$ Electric Flux Density $\vec{D}$

The electric flux density $\vec{D}$ essentially answers the question: **"How much electric flux passes through a unit area perpendicular to the flow of electric field lines?"** It is independent of the medium's polarization response, making it a universal measure of the free charge configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While $\vec{E}$ describes how a medium *responds* to electric charges (including polarization), $\vec{D}$ describes how the electric field *acts* upon free charges, regardless of the medium. This makes $\vec{D}$ particularly useful when applying **Gauss's Law** in materials.

### 1.3 Why is Electric Flux Density Introduced?

The introduction of $\vec{D}$ serves three critical purposes in electromagnetic theory:

1. **Simplification of Maxwell's Equations:** It allows the formulation of Gauss's Law purely in terms of free charges, eliminating the need to separately account for bound charges in dielectrics.
2. **Material Independence:** $\vec{D}$ depends primarily on the *free charge distribution*, not on the bound charges in the material, making it easier to solve problems involving dielectric interfaces.
3. **Symmetry in Maxwell's Equations:** It provides a symmetric counterpart to the magnetic field $\vec{H}$, establishing a parallel structure in Maxwell's equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Physical Significance

The concept of $\vec{D}$ emerges naturally from the behavior of electric fields in dielectric materials. In vacuum, the electric flux density and electric field intensity are directly proportional through $\varepsilon_0$. However, in a dielectric medium, the material becomes polarized when subjected to an external electric field. This polarization creates **bound charges** that partially cancel the original field.

To account for this, we write the total charge density as the sum of free and bound charges:

$$\rho_{total} = \rho_{free} + \rho_{bound}$$

Gauss's law in its general form states:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{total}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Substituting $\rho_{total}$ and using the polarization vector $\vec{P}$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{free} + \rho_{bound}}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{\rho_{free}}{\varepsilon_0} - \frac{\nabla \cdot \vec{P}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Rearranging:

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}) = \rho_{free}$$

The bracketed term is defined as the electric flux density:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most general definition. In a linear, isotropic, and homogeneous (LIH) medium where $\vec{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$, this reduces to the simpler form $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ where $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps

To compute or use $\vec{D}$ effectively in problems, follow this structured approach:

- **Step 1: Identify the Source Free Charges**
  - Locate the free charge distribution $\rho_v$ (volume), $\rho_s$ (surface), or $Q$ (point charges). Bound charges are *not* considered when calculating $\vec{D}$ directly from Gauss's law.

- **Step 2: Construct a Gaussian Surface**
  - Choose a closed surface that exploits the symmetry of the charge distribution (spherical, cylindrical, or planar).

- **Step 3: Apply Gauss's Law in $\vec{D}$ Form**
  - The flux of $\vec{D}$ through the closed surface equals the enclosed free charge:
  
  $$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enclosed, free}$$

- **Step 4: Solve for $\vec{D}$**
  - Use symmetry arguments to extract $\vec{D}$ from the surface integral.

- **Step 5: Determine $\vec{E}$ if Required**
  - Apply the constitutive relation $\vec{E} = \vec{D}/\varepsilon$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ | Definition of $\vec{D}$ in linear medium | C/m² |
| $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$ | General definition including polarization | C/m² |
| $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | Permittivity of medium | F/m |
| $\varepsilon_0 = \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9}$ | Permittivity of free space $\approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| $\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free,enc}$ | Gauss's Law in terms of $\vec{D}$ | C |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{free}$ | Differential form of Gauss's Law | C/m³ |
| $\vec{D}_{1n} - \vec{D}_{2n} = \rho_s$ | Normal boundary condition at surface | C/m² |
| $\varepsilon_1 E_{1n} - \varepsilon_2 E_{2n} = \rho_s$ | Normal $\vec{E}$ boundary condition | C/m² |
| $D_n = \rho_s$ (for conductor) | $\vec{D}$ normal to conductor surface | C/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** When using vertical bars for absolute values, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX. Example: $D = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}\vert_{\text{radially outward}}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of $\vec{D}$ is foundational in:

- **Capacitor Design:** Calculating the electric flux density in dielectric materials used between capacitor plates.
- **Semiconductor Device Physics:** Modeling charge distribution in p-n junctions and MOSFETs.
- **High-Voltage Insulators:** Designing insulators where field uniformity is critical.
- **Antenna Engineering:** Computing the radiated flux density in the near and far fields.
- **Biomedical Applications:** Modeling electric field interactions with biological tissues (which have specific permittivity values).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Gauss's Law to the Definition of $\vec{D}$

We begin with the integral form of Gauss's Law in free space:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{total,enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 1:** Express the total enclosed charge as the sum of free and bound charges:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{free} + Q_{bound}$$

Substituting into Gauss's Law:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{free} + Q_{bound}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 2:** Use the relationship between bound charge and polarization vector $\vec{P}$. The total bound charge enclosed by the surface equals the negative of the polarization flux through the surface:

$$Q_{bound} = -\oint_S \vec{P} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this expression for $Q_{bound}$ into the equation from Step 1:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \left[ Q_{free} - \oint_S \vec{P} \cdot d\vec{S} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $\varepsilon_0$ and bring the polarization integral to the left side:

$$\oint_S \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} + \oint_S \vec{P} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two surface integrals into one:

$$\oint_S (\varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}) \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free}$$

**Step 6:** Define the electric flux density vector $\vec{D}$ as the bracketed quantity:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$$

**Step 7:** The final form of Gauss's Law becomes:

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free,enclosed}$$

By the divergence theorem, the differential form follows as:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{free}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the electric flux density.

### 3.2 Worked Example: D-field of a Point Charge in a Dielectric Medium

**Problem:** A point charge $Q = 5 \, \mu C$ is located at the origin in an infinite dielectric medium with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$. Determine the electric flux density at a distance $r = 2 \, m$ from the charge.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Construct a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r = 2 \, m$ centered at the origin. By symmetry, $\vec{D}$ is radial and has constant magnitude on the surface.

**Step 2:** Apply Gauss's Law in terms of $\vec{D}$:

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free,enclosed} = Q$$

**Step 3:** On the spherical Gaussian surface, $d\vec{S} = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r$, and $\vec{D} = D_r \hat{a}_r$:

$$\oint_S D_r \, \hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_r \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = Q$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the angular integrals:

$$D_r \cdot r^2 \int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = Q$$

$$D_r \cdot r^2 \cdot (2) \cdot (2\pi) = Q$$

$$D_r \cdot 4\pi r^2 = Q$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $D_r$:

$$D_r = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute numerical values. Note: $Q = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, C$ and $r = 2 \, m$:

$$D_r = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi (2)^2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{16\pi}$$

$$D_r = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{50.265} \approx 9.947 \times 10^{-8} \, C/m^2$$

**Step 7:** Express the result in vector form:

$$\vec{D} = 9.947 \times 10^{-8} \, \hat{a}_r \, C/m^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Observation:** The expression for $\vec{D}$ around a point charge does *not* contain the permittivity of the medium! This is because the free charge is the sole source of $\vec{D}$, and the medium's effect appears only when computing $\vec{E}$ via $\vec{E} = \vec{D}/\varepsilon$.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Coaxial Cable with Two Dielectrics

**Problem:** A long coaxial cable has inner radius $a = 1 \, cm$ and outer radius $b = 4 \, cm$. The region between conductors is filled with two dielectrics: $\varepsilon_1 = 2\varepsilon_0$ for $a < r < c$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 4\varepsilon_0$ for $c < r < b$, where $c = 2.5 \, cm$. The inner conductor carries a line charge $\rho_L = 100 \, nC/m$. Find $\vec{D}$ everywhere.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** By cylindrical symmetry, construct a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $L$. The flux of $\vec{D}$ through the curved surface is:

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = D_r \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gauss's Law:

$$D_r \cdot 2\pi r L = \rho_L L$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $D_r$:

$$D_r = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 4:** Since $\vec{D}$ is independent of the permittivity of the medium, the same expression holds in both dielectric regions:

$$\vec{D} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi r} \hat{a}_r = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi r} \hat{a}_r \, C/m^2$$

$$\vec{D} = \frac{1.592 \times 10^{-8}}{r} \hat{a}_r \, C/m^2$$

**Step 5:** If $\vec{E}$ is required in each region:

$$\vec{E}_1 = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon_1} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi r \cdot 2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_r \quad \text{for } a < r < c$$

$$\vec{E}_2 = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi r \cdot 4\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_r \quad \text{for } c < r < b$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: The Relationship Between E, D, and P

The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates the logical flow from free charges to the electric flux density, and how polarization and electric field emerge from it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Free Charge Source] --> B[Gauss Law in D]
    B --> C[Compute D Field]
    C --> D{Select Medium Type}
    D --> E[Linear Isotropic: E = D / epsilon]
    D --> F[Anisotropic: E involves tensor epsilon]
    D --> G[Vacuum: E = D / epsilon_0]
    C --> H[Bound Charges Appear]
    H --> I[Polarization Vector P]
    I --> J[Modified E inside dielectric]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Final Observable Electric Field E]
    K --> L[Engineering Application]
    L --> M[Capacitor Design]
    L --> N[Antenna Radiation]
    L --> O[Insulator Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Solving for D-field in a Problem

This diagram shows the systematic procedure to follow when solving problems involving electric flux density.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Identify Free Charge Distribution] --> S2[Choose Symmetry Type]
    S2 --> S3[Construct Gaussian Surface]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Gauss Law for D]
    S4 --> S5[Evaluate Surface Integral]
    S5 --> S6[Extract D Vector]
    S6 --> S7{E needed?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Use Constitutive Relation E = D/epsilon]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Report Final D]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Verify Boundary Conditions]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram: D-field vs E-field Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FREE_SPACE
        E1[E field: Depends on Q and geometry]
        D1[D field: Depends only on free Q]
    end
    subgraph DIELECTRIC_MEDIUM
        E2[E field: Reduced due to polarization]
        D2[D field: Unchanged from free Q contribution]
    end
    FREE_SPACE --> DIELECTRIC_MEDIUM
    E1 -.Alteration.-> E2
    D1 -.Persistence.-> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram emphasizes a fundamental principle — when the same free charge configuration is placed in vacuum versus a dielectric, the $\vec{D}$-field magnitude is *identical* at corresponding points, but the $\vec{E}$-field is reduced by a factor of $\varepsilon_r$ in the dielectric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Electric Flux Density. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

Electric Flux Density, denoted as $\vec{D}$, is defined as the electric flux per unit area passing through a surface perpendicular to the direction of the electric field. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the permittivity of the medium, $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space, and $\vec{P}$ is the polarization vector.

**SI Unit:** Coulombs per square meter (C/m²)

The vector $\vec{D}$ is also known as the electric displacement vector. It provides a measure of how much electric flux passes through a unit area, and crucially, its divergence is related to free charge density alone:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{free}$$

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for unit and significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023] Differentiate between Electric Field Intensity (E) and Electric Flux Density (D).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Electric Field Intensity $\vec{E}$** | **Electric Flux Density $\vec{D}$** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Force per unit positive charge | Electric flux per unit area |
| Source | Total charges (free + bound) | Free charges only |
| Formula | $\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q$ | $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ |
| Units | Volts per meter (V/m) | Coulombs per square meter (C/m²) |
| Depends on medium | Yes, through permittivity | Indirectly, but the field itself depends on free charges only |
| Boundary condition | Tangential $\vec{E}$ is continuous | Normal $\vec{D}$ has discontinuity equal to surface charge |

**(3 marks — awarded for clear distinction in three or more aspects)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the relationship between $\vec{D}$, $\vec{E}$, and $\vec{P}$ starting from Gauss's Law in a dielectric medium. Explain why $\vec{D}$ is also called the electric displacement vector.

**(b) [7 marks]** A point charge $Q = 8 \, \mu C$ is located at the origin in a medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 4$. Calculate (i) $\vec{D}$ at $r = 1.5 \, m$, (ii) $\vec{E}$ at $r = 1.5 \, m$, and (iii) the total electric flux passing through a spherical surface of radius $1.5 \, m$ centered at the origin.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The derivation begins with Gauss's Law in free space applied to a medium with both free and bound charges:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{Q_{free} + Q_{bound}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

The bound charge enclosed by a Gaussian surface can be related to the polarization vector $\vec{P}$ through:

$$Q_{bound} = -\oint_S \vec{P} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Substituting this into the equation:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left[Q_{free} - \oint_S \vec{P} \cdot d\vec{S}\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\oint_S \left[\varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}\right] \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free}$$

Defining the bracketed term as the electric flux density:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** $\vec{D}$ is called the electric displacement vector because it represents the displacement of free charges in response to an applied electric field, abstracting away the complicating effects of bound charges. In a linear homogeneous medium, $\vec{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$, giving:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0(1 + \chi_e)\vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i)** For a point charge with spherical symmetry, the $\vec{D}$-field at radius $r$ is:

$$D_r = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$$

Substituting $Q = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, C$ and $r = 1.5 \, m$:

$$D_r = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi (1.5)^2} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{28.274} \approx 2.83 \times 10^{-7} \, C/m^2$$

$$\vec{D} = 2.83 \times 10^{-7} \hat{a}_r \, C/m^2$$

**(ii)** The electric field is obtained from:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$

$$E_r = \frac{2.83 \times 10^{-7}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4} = \frac{2.83 \times 10^{-7}}{3.5416 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$E_r \approx 7.99 \times 10^3 \, V/m \approx 7.99 \, kV/m$$

$$\vec{E} = 7.99 \times 10^3 \hat{a}_r \, V/m$$

**(iii)** The total electric flux through the spherical surface equals the enclosed free charge (by Gauss's Law in $\vec{D}$):

$$\Psi = \oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enclosed} = 8 \, \mu C = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Many students incorrectly include $\varepsilon_r$ when computing $\vec{D}$ for a point charge. The $\vec{D}$-field depends *only* on free charges, so its formula is the same in vacuum and in a dielectric.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the total flux with flux density. Flux is a scalar in Coulombs; flux density is in C/m². Read the question carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert units properly. $\mu C$ must be converted to Coulombs before substitution.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 marks]** State and explain Gauss's Law in terms of Electric Flux Density. Derive its differential form. What is the physical significance of $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = 0$ in a region free of charge?

**(b) [7 marks]** A parallel plate capacitor has a separation $d = 2 \, mm$ between the plates. The space between is filled with a dielectric of $\varepsilon_r = 5.6$. If the surface charge density on the plates is $\rho_s = 40 \, \mu C/m^2$, find (i) $\vec{D}$ in the dielectric, (ii) $\vec{E}$ in the dielectric, and (iii) the potential difference between the plates.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Statement:** Gauss's Law in terms of Electric Flux Density states that the total electric flux of $\vec{D}$ passing through any closed surface is equal to the total free charge enclosed by that surface.

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free,enclosed}$$

**Derivation of Differential Form:** Applying the divergence theorem:

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV$$

The enclosed free charge can be expressed as a volume integral of free charge density:

$$Q_{free,enclosed} = \int_V \rho_{free} \, dV$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV = \int_V \rho_{free} \, dV$$

Since this must hold for any arbitrary volume:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{free}$$

**Physical Significance of $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = 0$:** In a region where there is no free charge, the divergence of $\vec{D}$ is zero. This means the field lines of $\vec{D}$ neither originate from nor terminate at any point within that region. The flux lines entering any small volume must equal the flux lines leaving it — analogous to an incompressible fluid flow with no sources or sinks.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i)** Between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor with surface charge $\rho_s$ on the plates, the $\vec{D}$-field is uniform and points from the positive to the negative plate. Applying Gauss's Law with a pillbox surface that cuts through one plate:

$$D_n = \rho_s = 40 \, \mu C/m^2 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \, C/m^2$$

$$\vec{D} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \, \hat{a}_n \, C/m^2 \quad (\text{where } \hat{a}_n \text{ points from positive to negative plate})$$

**(ii)** The electric field in the dielectric is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$

$$E = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.6}$$

$$E = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{4.958 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 8.067 \times 10^5 \, V/m$$

$$\vec{E} = 8.067 \times 10^5 \, \hat{a}_n \, V/m$$

**(iii)** The potential difference between the plates is:

$$V = E \cdot d = 8.067 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V = 1.613 \times 10^3 \, V \approx 1613 \, V$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Students often wrongly assume $\vec{E} = \rho_s / \varepsilon_0$ even in a dielectric. Always use $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** Forgetting that the field between capacitor plates is uniform and perpendicular to the plates. Drawing a Gaussian pillbox that is not aligned with the field will lead to errors.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** Mixing up $\rho_s$ (surface charge density) with $\rho_v$ (volume charge density). They have different units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Core Definition:** $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ (in linear media) or $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$ (general).
- **Physical Meaning:** Electric flux per unit area; depends on free charges only.
- **SI Unit:** Coulombs per square meter (C/m²).
- **Gauss's Law (Integral Form):** $\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{free,enclosed}$
- **Gauss's Law (Differential Form):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{free}$
- **For a Point Charge:** $\vec{D} = \frac{Q}{4\pi r^2} \hat{a}_r$ — independent of medium.
- **For a Coaxial Cable:** $\vec{D} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi r} \hat{a}_r$ — independent of medium.
- **For Parallel Plate Capacitor:** $\vec{D} = \rho_s \hat{a}_n$ (normal to plates, pointing from + to −).
- **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m \approx \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9} \, F/m$.
- **Boundary Conditions:**
  - Normal component: $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_s$
  - For dielectric-dielectric (no free surface charge): $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$
- **Key Insight:** $\vec{D}$ makes Maxwell's equation independent of bound charges, simplifying problems in dielectric media.
- **Relationship to E-field:** $\vec{E} = \vec{D} / \varepsilon$ — the medium reduces $\vec{E}$ by factor $\varepsilon_r$ compared to free space, but $\vec{D}$ remains unchanged.
- **Other Names:** Electric Displacement, D-field, Electric Flux Density — all refer to the same vector $\vec{D}$.
- **Engineering Applications:** Capacitors, transmission lines, antenna radiation patterns, biomedical device design, semiconductor device modeling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Due to Line of Charge, Surface and Volume Charge Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Field Due to Line of Charge, Surface and Volume Charge Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Electromagnetic Theory**, the **Electric Field Intensity (E)** at any point in space is defined as the force experienced by a unit positive test charge placed at that point, assuming the test charge does not disturb the original charge configuration.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_t \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_t} \quad \text{(units: V/m or N/C)}$$

When continuous charge distributions exist (instead of discrete point charges), the total field is obtained by integrating the contribution of every infinitesimal charge element $dQ$ over the entire distribution. The three fundamental continuous charge distributions studied in the **KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 1** are:

| Distribution Type | Symbol | Unit | Differential Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Line Charge** | $\rho_L$ | C/m | $dQ = \rho_L \, dl$ |
| **Surface Charge** | $\rho_S$ | C/m² | $dQ = \rho_S \, dS$ |
| **Volume Charge** | $\rho_V$ | C/m³ | $dQ = \rho_V \, dv$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEET502 - Module 1):** Students must be able to derive and apply the electric field expressions for all three continuous distributions. The constant **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** (permittivity of free space) and **$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C²** appear in nearly every numerical problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sprinkling glitter over a **stretched rubber thread (line charge)**, painting a charge on a **flat balloon (surface charge)**, or filling a **charged sponge (volume charge)**. Each tiny speck of charge acts like a miniature point charge, sending out invisible "force arrows" (electric field lines) in all directions.

- **Line charge** → like charged beads strung along a necklace; the field radiates outward perpendicular to the wire.
- **Surface charge** → like a charged tabletop; field lines push outward normal to the surface.
- **Volume charge** → like a charged cloud; field lines emerge from the bulk in every direction.

By adding up (integrating) the tiny push from every speck, we obtain the **total electric field** at any observation point — this is the essence of Coulomb's Law extended to continuous media.

> [!NOTE]
> The unit vector $\hat{a}_R$ (or $\hat{a}_r$) always points **from the source charge to the field point (observation point)**. Forgetting this directionality is the single most common cause of sign errors in KTU answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Coulomb's Law to Continuous Distributions

Coulomb's Law for a **point charge** $Q$ located at the source point $\vec{r}'$ and observation point $\vec{r}$ states:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\vec{R}}{R^3}$$

where:
- $\vec{R} = \vec{r} - \vec{r}'$ → vector from source to field point.
- $R = \vert \vec{R} \vert$ → scalar distance between source and field point.
- $\hat{a}_R = \frac{\vec{R}}{R}$ → unit vector along $\vec{R}$.

For a **differential charge element** $dQ$, this becomes:

$$d\vec{E} = \frac{dQ}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2}$$

The total field is the **vector sum (integral)** of all such contributions:

$$\vec{E} = \int d\vec{E}$$

## 2.2 The Three Master Integrals

### (a) Line Charge Distribution

A charge distributed along a **line** (e.g., a thin charged wire) with linear charge density $\rho_L$ (C/m):

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \int_{L} \frac{\rho_L \, dl}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2}}$$

### (b) Surface Charge Distribution

A charge spread across a **surface** (e.g., a charged plate) with surface charge density $\rho_S$ (C/m²):

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \int_{S} \frac{\rho_S \, dS}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2}}$$

### (c) Volume Charge Distribution

A charge spread throughout a **volume** (e.g., a charged sphere) with volume charge density $\rho_V$ (C/m³):

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \int_{v} \frac{\rho_V \, dv}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vector $\vec{R}$ and the differential element must be expressed in the **same coordinate system** (Cartesian, Cylindrical, or Spherical). Choosing the wrong coordinate system makes integrals unsolvable in KTU board exams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Coordinate System | Final $\vec{E}$ Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Infinite line charge (linear density $\rho_L$) | Cylindrical | $\dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}$ | Radial ($\hat{a}_\rho$) |
| 2 | Infinite sheet of charge (surface density $\rho_S$) | Cartesian | $\dfrac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0}$ | Normal to sheet ($\hat{a}_n$) |
| 3 | Point charge $Q$ | Spherical | $\dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ | Radial ($\hat{a}_r$) |
| 4 | Finite line segment (length $L$) | Cartesian/Cylindrical | $\dfrac{\rho_L}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 d}\left(\sin\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\right)$ | Perpendicular bisector |
| 5 | Uniform volume charge in a sphere | Spherical (inside) | $\dfrac{\rho_V r}{3\varepsilon_0}$ | Radial ($\hat{a}_r$) |
| 6 | Uniform volume charge in a sphere | Spherical (outside) | $\dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ | Radial ($\hat{a}_r$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Rule (Frequently Asked in KTU):** When the same charge $Q$ exists as line, surface, and volume distributions, their densities relate as:
> $$\rho_L = \rho_S \cdot (\text{width}) = \rho_V \cdot (\text{area}) = \frac{Q}{L}$$
> $$\rho_S = \rho_V \cdot (\text{thickness}) = \frac{Q}{S}$$
> $$\rho_V = \frac{Q}{v}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Distribution Used |
|---|---|
| **High-voltage transmission lines** | Line charge ($\rho_L$) |
| **Parallel-plate capacitors / PCBs** | Surface charge ($\rho_S$) |
| **Coaxial cables / charged spheres** | Volume charge ($\rho_V$) |
| **Cathode-ray tubes (CRTs)** | Mixed line + surface |
| **Cloud-to-ground lightning modeling** | Volume charge in thunderclouds |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electric Field Due to an Infinite Line Charge

**Setup:** Consider an infinite straight line along the z-axis carrying uniform line charge density $\rho_L$ (C/m). The field point $P$ is located at a perpendicular distance $\rho$ from the z-axis (cylindrical coordinates).

**Step 1 — Choose a differential element.**
Take a small charge element at height $z'$ with thickness $dz'$:

$$dQ = \rho_L \, dz'$$

**Step 2 — Express the position vectors.**
- Source point: $\vec{r}' = z' \hat{a}_z$
- Field point: $\vec{r} = \rho \hat{a}_\rho$
- Vector from source to field: $\vec{R} = \vec{r} - \vec{r}' = \rho \hat{a}_\rho - z' \hat{a}_z$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $\vec{R}$.**

$$R = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}$$

**Step 4 — Unit vector $\hat{a}_R$.**

$$\hat{a}_R = \frac{\vec{R}}{R} = \frac{\rho \hat{a}_\rho - z' \hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the differential field equation.**

$$d\vec{E} = \frac{dQ}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2} = \frac{\rho_L \, dz'}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\rho \hat{a}_\rho - z' \hat{a}_z}{(\rho^2 + z'^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $z' = -\infty$ to $+\infty$.**
By symmetry, the $z'$-components cancel out (integrating an odd function over symmetric limits gives zero), and only the $\hat{a}_\rho$ component survives:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L \rho \hat{a}_\rho}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dz'}{(\rho^2 + z'^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral using the standard result.**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dz'}{(\rho^2 + z'^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{2}{\rho^2}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back.**

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L \rho \hat{a}_\rho}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2}{\rho^2}$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} \hat{a}_\rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The field falls off as **$1/\rho$** (not $1/\rho^2$) because charge is distributed along the entire infinite length.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electric Field Due to an Infinite Sheet of Charge

**Setup:** Consider an infinite flat sheet lying in the $xy$-plane with uniform surface charge density $\rho_S$ (C/m²). The field point is at $z = d$ above the sheet.

**Step 1 — Differential element.**

$$dQ = \rho_S \, dx' \, dy'$$

**Step 2 — Position vectors.**
- Source point: $\vec{r}' = x' \hat{a}_x + y' \hat{a}_y$
- Field point: $\vec{r} = d \hat{a}_z$
- Separation vector: $\vec{R} = -x' \hat{a}_x - y' \hat{a}_y + d \hat{a}_z$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $\vec{R}$.**

$$R = \sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Field equation.**

$$d\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S \, dx' \, dy'}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(-x' \hat{a}_x - y' \hat{a}_y + d \hat{a}_z)}{(x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over $x'$ and $y'$.**
The $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$ components vanish by symmetry (integrating odd functions over $-\infty$ to $+\infty$). Only the $\hat{a}_z$ component survives:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S \, d \, \hat{a}_z}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iint_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dx' \, dy'}{(x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the double integral using polar coordinates** ($u^2 = x'^2 + y'^2$):

$$\iint_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dx' \, dy'}{(x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{2\pi}{d^2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back.**

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S \, d \, \hat{a}_z}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{d^2} = \frac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_z$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The field of an **infinite sheet is CONSTANT** (independent of distance $d$). This is a counterintuitive but extremely important result for KTU problems involving parallel-plate capacitors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electric Field of a Uniformly Charged Sphere (Volume Charge)

**Setup:** A sphere of radius $a$ carries uniform volume charge density $\rho_V$ (C/m³). Total charge:

$$Q = \frac{4}{3}\pi a^3 \rho_V$$

**Step 1 — Field OUTSIDE the sphere ($r \geq a$).**
By Gauss's Law (Module 2 in KTU, but derived via Coulomb's law here):

$$\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r = \frac{\rho_V a^3}{3\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r$$

**Step 2 — Field INSIDE the sphere ($r < a$).**
Only the charge enclosed within radius $r$ contributes:

$$Q_{enc} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_V$$

$$\vec{E} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r = \frac{\rho_V r}{3\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_r$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E}_{inside} = \frac{\rho_V r}{3\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_r} \qquad \boxed{\vec{E}_{outside} = \frac{\rho_V a^3}{3\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Distribution

| Problem Signal in Question | Distribution Type | Density Symbol | Differential Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| "thin straight wire", "line charge along z-axis" | **Line** | $\rho_L$ | $dl$ |
| "circular ring", "loop" | **Line** | $\rho_L$ | $dl = a \, d\phi$ |
| "flat infinite sheet", "disc", "plane" | **Surface** | $\rho_S$ | $dS$ |
| "charged sphere (solid)", "cylindrical volume" | **Volume** | $\rho_V$ | $dv$ |
| "point charge" | **Discrete** | — | $Q$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Calculating E from Continuous Distributions

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision-tree workflow** a KTU student should follow when confronted with a charge-distribution problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement Given] --> B{Identify Charge Geometry}
    B -- Thin wire / Loop --> C[Line Charge: rho_L C per m]
    B -- Flat surface / Sheet --> D[Surface Charge: rho_S C per m2]
    B -- Solid volume / Sphere --> E[Volume Charge: rho_V C per m3]

    C --> F[Choose Coordinate System]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G{Check Symmetry}
    G -- Cylindrical --> H[Use Cylindrical rho, phi, z]
    G -- Spherical --> I[Use Spherical r, theta, phi]
    G -- Cartesian --> J[Use Cartesian x, y, z]

    H --> K[Express source position r prime]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Compute R vector: r minus r prime]
    L --> M[Compute R magnitude and unit vector aR]
    M --> N[Write dE from Coulomb Law]
    N --> O[Set up integral over dl, dS, or dv]
    O --> P[Exploit symmetry to eliminate components]
    P --> Q[Evaluate the integral]
    Q --> R[Final Result: Vector E]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic of the Three Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINE[Line Charge Distribution]
        L1[Source: Thin wire along z-axis]
        L2[Field point P at distance rho]
        L3[Vector R from source to P]
        L1 --> L3
        L3 --> L2
    end

    subgraph SURFACE[Surface Charge Distribution]
        S1[Source: Infinite sheet in xy-plane]
        S2[Field point P at height d]
        S3[Normal vector a_n pointing away]
        S1 --> S3
        S3 --> S2
    end

    subgraph VOLUME[Volume Charge Distribution]
        V1[Source: Spherical cloud radius a]
        V2[Field point P at radius r]
        V3[Total charge Q enclosed]
        V1 --> V3
        V3 --> V2
    end
```

## 4.3 Vector Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vector R in 3D Space] --> B[Component along x: Rx]
    A --> C[Component along y: Ry]
    A --> D[Component along z: Rz]

    B --> E[Project onto field direction]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    E --> F{Is component parallel to symmetry axis?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Retain: contributes to E]
    F -- No --> H[Cancel by symmetry: set to 0]

    G --> I[Sum retained components]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Vector E]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, examiners often award **2 marks** simply for the correct vector decomposition diagram. Always include a sketch of $\vec{R}$, $\hat{a}_R$, and the source/field points in your answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define linear, surface, and volume charge densities. Give their SI units and write the relationship between them when they describe the same total charge.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Linear charge density** $\rho_L$ = charge per unit length; SI unit = **C/m**; for a line of length $L$, $Q = \rho_L L$.
- **Surface charge density** $\rho_S$ = charge per unit area; SI unit = **C/m²**; for an area $S$, $Q = \rho_S S$.
- **Volume charge density** $\rho_V$ = charge per unit volume; SI unit = **C/m³**; for a volume $v$, $Q = \rho_V v$.

**Relationship:** $\rho_L \cdot L = \rho_S \cdot S = \rho_V \cdot v = Q$. (1 mark for definitions + units, 1 mark for relationships, 1 mark for clarity)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A point charge $Q = 5 \,\mu\text{C}$ is located at the origin. Find the electric field intensity at point $P(0, 3, 4)$ m.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\vec{R} = (0-0)\hat{a}_x + (3-0)\hat{a}_y + (4-0)\hat{a}_z = 3\hat{a}_y + 4\hat{a}_z \quad \text{m}$$

$$R = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5 \text{ m}$$

$$\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^3}\vec{R} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(125)} (3\hat{a}_y + 4\hat{a}_z)$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E} \approx 1797 \, (3\hat{a}_y + 4\hat{a}_z) \text{ V/m} \approx 1.797 \, \hat{a}_R \text{ kV/m}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electric field intensity at a distance $\rho$ from an **infinite line charge** of uniform linear charge density $\rho_L$ placed along the z-axis. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An infinite line charge of $\rho_L = 10 \,\mu\text{C/m}$ lies along the y-axis. Find $\vec{E}$ at point $P(3, 4, 5)$ m. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider an infinite line along the z-axis. Field point $P$ is at perpendicular distance $\rho$.

**Step 2 — Differential element:**
$$dQ = \rho_L \, dz', \quad \vec{R} = \rho \hat{a}_\rho - z' \hat{a}_z, \quad R = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z'^2}$$

**Step 3 — Field equation:**
$$d\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L \, dz'}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\rho \hat{a}_\rho - z' \hat{a}_z}{(\rho^2 + z'^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Integration limits** $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ along z:

- The $\hat{a}_z$ component cancels by symmetry.
- The $\hat{a}_\rho$ component yields $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dz'}{(\rho^2 + z'^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{2}{\rho^2}$.

**Step 5 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} \hat{a}_\rho}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify geometry.** Since the line lies along the **y-axis** (not z), rotate the standard formula. The perpendicular distance from the line to point $P$ is:

$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.831 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude:**
$$\vert \vec{E} \vert = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(\sqrt{34})}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \vec{E} \vert \approx 3.085 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}}$$

**Step 3 — Direction.** $\hat{a}_\rho$ points from the line to point $P$ in the xz-plane:

$$\hat{a}_\rho = \frac{3\hat{a}_x + 5\hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{34}}$$

**Step 4 — Final vector:**
$$\vec{E} = 3.085 \times 10^{4} \cdot \frac{3\hat{a}_x + 5\hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{34}} \approx (1.587 \hat{a}_x + 2.645 \hat{a}_z) \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the electric field intensity due to an **infinite sheet of charge** with uniform surface charge density $\rho_S$ lying in the xy-plane. State two important conclusions from the result. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A circular disc of radius $a$ carries uniform surface charge density $\rho_S$. Find $\vec{E}$ at a point on its axis at distance $z$ from the centre. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Differential element** $dQ = \rho_S \, dx' dy'$.

**Step 2 — Vector** $\vec{R} = -x'\hat{a}_x - y'\hat{a}_y + d\hat{a}_z$, magnitude $R = \sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Coulomb's law and integrate.** By symmetry, $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$ components vanish:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iint \frac{d \, dx' dy'}{(x'^2 + y'^2 + d^2)^{3/2}} \hat{a}_z$$

**Step 4 — Convert to polar** ($u^2 = x'^2 + y'^2$, $dx' dy' = u \, du \, d\phi$):

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S d}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\infty} \frac{u \, du \, d\phi}{(u^2 + d^2)^{3/2}} \hat{a}_z = \frac{\rho_S d}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{d^2} \hat{a}_z$$

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_n}$$

**Step 5 — Two important conclusions:**
1. The field is **independent of distance** $d$ from the sheet.
2. The field is **always normal** to the sheet, regardless of where the observation point is located.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Setup:** Use cylindrical coordinates. Field point $P$ at height $z$ on axis. Source point at radius $\rho'$ from centre.

$$dQ = \rho_S \, \rho' \, d\rho' \, d\phi', \quad R = \sqrt{\rho'^2 + z^2}, \quad \hat{a}_R = \frac{-\rho' \hat{a}_\rho + z \hat{a}_z}{\sqrt{\rho'^2 + z^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Field equation:**
$$d\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S \rho' d\rho' d\phi'}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 (\rho'^2 + z^2)} \cdot \frac{-\rho'\hat{a}_\rho + z\hat{a}_z}{(\rho'^2 + z^2)^{1/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate** $\phi'$ from $0$ to $2\pi$. By symmetry, the $\hat{a}_\rho$ component vanishes:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S z \hat{a}_z}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi' \int_0^{a} \frac{\rho' d\rho'}{(\rho'^2 + z^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate integral:**
$$\int_0^{a} \frac{\rho' d\rho'}{(\rho'^2 + z^2)^{3/2}} = \left[\frac{-1}{\sqrt{\rho'^2 + z^2}}\right]_0^{a} = \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2 + z^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0}\left(1 - \frac{z}{\sqrt{a^2 + z^2}}\right) \hat{a}_z}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting case check:** As $a \to \infty$, $z/\sqrt{a^2+z^2} \to 0$, recovering the **infinite sheet** result $\rho_S/(2\varepsilon_0)$ ✓.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong direction of $\hat{a}_R$:** It must point FROM source TO field point, not the reverse. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting symmetry:** Noting which components cancel is worth 2 marks by itself in derivations.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing C/m, C/m², and C/m³ without conversion causes full-mark deduction in numerical problems.
> 4. **Sign of $\rho_L$ or $\rho_S$:** A negative density still produces a field magnitude; the sign only affects direction (radially inward vs outward). Do not write a negative magnitude.
> 5. **Integration limits:** When the question says "infinite line", use $-\infty$ to $+\infty$; for a finite segment, carefully read endpoint coordinates.
> 6. **Coordinate system mismatch:** A line along the y-axis requires the perpendicular distance in the xz-plane ($\sqrt{x^2+z^2}$), NOT $\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Coulomb's Law is the foundation: $d\vec{E} = \dfrac{dQ}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{\hat{a}_R}{R^2}$.
- ✅ The vector $\vec{R}$ goes **from source to field point**; magnitude $R = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert$.
- ✅ **Line charge:** $dQ = \rho_L \, dl$; result for infinite line — $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho} \hat{a}_\rho$ (falls as $1/\rho$).
- ✅ **Surface charge:** $dQ = \rho_S \, dS$; result for infinite sheet — $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_n$ (**constant**, independent of distance).
- ✅ **Volume charge:** $dQ = \rho_V \, dv$; result inside uniform sphere — $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\rho_V r}{3\varepsilon_0} \hat{a}_r$ (linear in $r$).
- ✅ Conversion relationships: $\rho_L = \rho_S \cdot w = \rho_V \cdot A$ where $w$ = width, $A$ = cross-sectional area.
- ✅ Always **exploit symmetry** before integrating — this is the mark-distinguishing step.
- ✅ Constants to memorize: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C².
- ✅ Sketch the geometry: source point, field point, vector $\vec{R}$, and unit vector $\hat{a}_R$ — a labeled diagram earns easy marks.
- ✅ Choose coordinate system based on symmetry: **Cylindrical** for line charges, **Cartesian** for planar sheets, **Spherical** for point/sphere problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Fields in Material Media: Gauss's Law - Point Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Fields in Material Media: Gauss's Law - Point Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauss's Law in Point (Differential) Form** states that the divergence of the **Electric Flux Density (D)** at any point in a material medium is exactly equal to the **volume charge density (ρᵥ)** at that point.

The governing mathematical expression is:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v
$$

Where:
- $\vec{D}$ is the **Electric Flux Density** (also called Electric Displacement), measured in **Coulombs per square meter (C/m²)**.
- $\rho_v$ is the **Volume Charge Density**, measured in **Coulombs per cubic meter (C/m³)**.
- $\nabla \cdot$ is the **divergence operator**, which mathematically measures the net outward flux per unit volume at a point.

This is the **point form** (or **differential form**) of Gauss's Law. The subscript "v" denotes volume. It is the local-space equivalent of the integral form $\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Spring" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing in a 3D field of pipes carrying water (representing electric flux $\vec{D}$). 
> - If at the exact point where you stand, water is **spurting outward** in all directions (a source), it means there is a **positive charge** there. The "strength" of the divergence tells you how strong the charge is.
> - If water is **draining inward** from all directions (a sink), it represents a **negative charge**.
> - If water is just **flowing through smoothly** without any source or sink, the divergence is zero — meaning **no free charge** at that point.
>
> The operator $\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$ is essentially a mathematical microscope that tells you, at every infinitesimal point, whether charges are sources or sinks of electric flux.

## 1.3 The Constitutive Relationship

In material media, the three fundamental field vectors are linked by the **constitutive relation**:

$$
\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 \approx \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m}$ is the **permittivity of free space**.
- $\vec{E}$ is the Electric Field Intensity (V/m).
- $\vec{P}$ is the Polarization Vector (C/m²) — the dipole moment per unit volume.

For **linear, isotropic, and homogeneous (LIH) media**, this simplifies to:

$$
\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E}
$$

Where $\varepsilon_r$ is the **relative permittivity** (dimensionless) of the material.

> [!TIP]
> **Why we use $\vec{D}$ instead of $\vec{E}$ in material media:**
> In free space, Gauss's Law reads $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v / \varepsilon_0$. In material media, bound charges appear due to polarization. The vector $\vec{D}$ elegantly combines the effects of **free charges** AND the medium's response, making $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ universally valid — independent of the material.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — From Integral to Point Form

The derivation of the point form proceeds through these logical stages:

1. **Start with the Integral Form:** In material media, the integral form of Gauss's Law is written as:

   $$
   \oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enclosed}
   $$

   The right-hand side accounts only for **free charges** enclosed, since bound charges are already embedded in the definition of $\vec{D}$.

2. **Express Enclosed Charge via Volume Density:**

   $$
   Q_{enclosed} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
   $$

3. **Apply the Divergence Theorem:** This theorem links a closed surface integral to a volume integral:

   $$
   \oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV
   $$

4. **Equate Both Sides:**

   $$
   \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
   $$

5. **Localize the Equation:** Since this holds for **any** arbitrary volume $V$, the integrands must be equal point-by-point:

   $$
   \boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v}
   $$

## 2.2 Physical Significance — The "Why" and the "How"

- **"Why" we need a point form:** The integral form tells you the *total* flux through a closed surface. The point form tells you the *local* behavior of the field at every point in space — essential for solving Maxwell's equations and analyzing field distributions in continuous media.
- **"How" it works in materials:** When a dielectric material is placed in an electric field, its molecules polarize, creating bound charges. The $\vec{D}$ vector automatically incorporates these bound charges. Therefore, the right-hand side $\rho_v$ refers **strictly to free charges** (sources that we can control, like charges on capacitor plates).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ | Point form of Gauss's Law | C/m³ on RHS |
| 2 | $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$ | General definition of $\vec{D}$ | C/m² |
| 3 | $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E}$ | LIH medium simplification | C/m² |
| 4 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_{total} / \varepsilon_0$ | Point form in terms of E (all charges) | V/m |
| 5 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{P} = -\rho_{b}$ | Bound charge volume density relation | C/m³ |
| 6 | $\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV$ | Integral form of Gauss's Law | C |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | Permittivity of free space | F/m |
| 8 | $\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \rho_v$ | Substitution for LIH media | C/m³ |
| 9 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\partial \rho_v / \partial t$ | Continuity equation (for comparison) | A/m³ |
| 10 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = 0$ | Special case: charge-free region | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse $\rho_v$ in $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ with total charge density.** Here, $\rho_v$ refers ONLY to **free charge density**. The total charge density (free + bound) appears in $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_{total} / \varepsilon_0$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacitor Design:** Engineers use $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ to design multilayer capacitors, choosing dielectric materials ($\varepsilon_r$) to achieve desired capacitance.
- **Semiconductor Device Physics:** Used to model p-n junctions, MOSFETs, and charge carrier distributions.
- **High-Voltage Insulation:** Critical for analyzing electric stress in transformer oil, XLPE cables, and SF₆ gas insulation.
- **Antenna & Microwave Engineering:** Foundation for analyzing charge distribution on conductors feeding radiating structures.
- **Biomedical Imaging (MRI/Electroporation):** Understanding electric field divergence in tissue helps in designing safe medical equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Integral Form to Point Form

We will derive $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ rigorously using vector calculus.

**Step 1:** Begin with the integral form of Gauss's Law applied to an arbitrary closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $V$ in a material medium.

$$
\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enclosed,free}
$$

**Step 2:** The total free charge enclosed within the surface $S$ can be expressed as the volume integral of the free volume charge density $\rho_v$ over $V$.

$$
Q_{enclosed,free} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this expression back into Gauss's Law.

$$
\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** (also called Gauss's Divergence Theorem) to convert the closed surface integral on the left-hand side into a volume integral.

$$
\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV
$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two volume integrals obtained from Step 3 and Step 4.

$$
\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 6:** Combine both sides into a single volume integral.

$$
\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D} - \rho_v) \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Since the volume $V$ is **arbitrary**, the integrand must vanish identically at every point.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} - \rho_v = 0
$$

**Step 8:** The final point form of Gauss's Law emerges.

$$
\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Connecting $\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$ to $\nabla \cdot \vec{E}$

**Step 1:** Start with the general relation between $\vec{D}$, $\vec{E}$, and $\vec{P}$.

$$
\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the divergence of both sides.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \nabla \cdot (\varepsilon_0 \vec{E}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{P}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\varepsilon_0$ is a constant in free space, pull it out.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{P}
$$

**Step 4:** Recall that the divergence of polarization gives the negative of bound charge density.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{P} = -\rho_b
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the point form of Gauss's Law and the polarization relation.

$$
\rho_{v,free} = \varepsilon_0 (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \rho_b
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the total charge equation.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{v,free} + \rho_b}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{\rho_{v,total}}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

This proves that the total charge density (free + bound) governs $\vec{E}$, while only the free charge density governs $\vec{D}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of $\vec{D}$ and $\vec{E}$ Equations

| Property | $\vec{D}$ (Electric Flux Density) | $\vec{E}$ (Electric Field Intensity) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{v,free}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_{v,total} / \varepsilon_0$ |
| Source term | Free charges only | All charges (free + bound) |
| Material dependence | Automatically accounts for medium | Requires medium constant $\varepsilon$ |
| Units | C/m² | V/m |
| Origin | Maxwell's definition | Coulomb's law force per unit charge |

## 3.4 Special Case: Charge-Free Region

In a region where there is no free charge ($\rho_v = 0$), the point form simplifies to:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = 0
$$

This means the flux lines of $\vec{D}$ are continuous — they neither originate nor terminate. This is a solenoidal field, analogous to a magnetic field in a region free of magnetic monopoles.

## 3.5 Verification: Spherical Symmetry Example

For a uniformly charged sphere of radius $a$ with free charge density $\rho_0$ in free space, the point form $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ applied at the center yields $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_0$. Solving gives $D_r = \rho_0 r / 3$ for $r < a$, confirming the cubic field inside a uniformly charged sphere — a classic KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Derivation Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the logical transformation from the integral form to the point form, including the role of the Divergence Theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Integral Form of Gauss Law"] --> B["Identify Enclosed Free Charge Q"]
    B --> C["Express Q as Volume Integral of rho_v"]
    C --> D["Substitute into Surface Integral Equation"]
    D --> E["Apply Divergence Theorem to LHS"]
    E --> F["Equate Two Volume Integrals"]
    F --> G["Combine Integrands"]
    G --> H["Volume V is Arbitrary"]
    H --> I["Integrand Must Vanish at Every Point"]
    I --> J["Final Point Form: div D equals rho_v"]
    A --> A1["Surface Integral of D dot dS"]
    A1 --> E
    C --> C1["Integral of rho_v dV over Volume V"]
    C1 --> F
    E --> E1["Volume Integral of div D dV"]
    E1 --> F
    J --> K["Special Case: rho_v equals 0 implies div D equals 0"]
    J --> L["Application: Solve Field in LIH Media"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Field Vectors in a Dielectric Medium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE["Charge Sources"]
        FC["Free Charge Density rho_v_free"]
        BC["Bound Charge Density rho_b"]
    end

    subgraph FIELDS["Field Vectors in Material"]
        DVEC["D Vector: Electric Flux Density"]
        EVEC["E Vector: Electric Field Intensity"]
        PVEC["P Vector: Polarization"]
    end

    FC -->|"Governs div of D"| DVEC
    BC -->|"Arises from medium"| PVEC
    PVEC -->|"P added to eps0 times E"| DVEC
    DVEC -->|"D equals eps times E"| EVEC
    EVEC -->|"E field exerts force on charges"| FORCE["Force F equals q times E"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: D Field vs E Field Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FREEMED["Free Space Behavior"]
        F1["div D equals rho_v / eps0 simplified"]
        F2["div E equals rho_v / eps0"]
        F1 --- F2
    end

    subgraph DIELECTRIC["Dielectric Material Behavior"]
        D1["div D equals rho_v_free only"]
        D2["div E equals rho_total divided by eps0"]
        D3["D continuous across interface, E discontinuous"]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
    end

    FREEMED -->|"Medium Introduced"| DIELECTRIC
```

## 4.4 Application Block Diagram: Capacitor with Dielectric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAP["Parallel Plate Capacitor"] --> PLATE["Conductor Plates with Surface Charge"]
    PLATE -->|Free charge density rho_s| DFIELD["D Field Between Plates: D equals rho_s"]
    DFIELD --> LIH["LIH Dielectric: D equals eps times E"]
    LIH --> EFIELD["Solve E Field: E equals D divided by eps"]
    EFIELD --> VOLT["Integrate to get Voltage V"]
    VOLT --> CAPVAL["Capacitance C equals eps A divided by d"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams above show that the point form $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ acts as the **starting kernel** in a chain of deductions that leads to physical quantities like capacitance, voltage, and field distribution. This is the central role of the point form in KTU module-level problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the point form of Gauss's Law in material media. State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The point form (or differential form) of Gauss's Law in material media is mathematically expressed as the divergence of the electric flux density $\vec{D}$ at any point being equal to the free volume charge density $\rho_v$ at that point.

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v
$$

**Significance:**
- **Marks 1:** It provides a **local (pointwise) description** of how electric flux behaves at every point in space, in contrast to the integral form which deals with total flux through a closed surface.
- **Marks 2:** It is one of **Maxwell's four fundamental equations** for time-invariant (electrostatic) fields, forming the foundation for solving field problems in continuous material media.
- **Marks 3:** It is **independent of the material properties** on the left-hand side — the constitutive behavior of the medium is absorbed into $\vec{D}$, making this equation universally valid for free space, conductors, and dielectrics.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why does Gauss's Law in point form use $\vec{D}$ instead of $\vec{E}$ in material media?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> In material media (dielectrics), the application of an external electric field $\vec{E}$ causes the molecules of the medium to polarize, generating **bound charges** that in turn create their own secondary electric fields.

- **Marks 1:** The equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_{total} / \varepsilon_0$ requires us to know the **total charge density** (free + bound), but the bound charge distribution is often **unknown and difficult to compute** directly in complex materials.
- **Marks 2:** The vector $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$ is defined such that it **automatically accounts for the medium's polarization response**, so $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{v,free}$ involves only the **free (controllable) charges**.
- **Marks 3:** Hence, $\vec{D}$ simplifies the analysis significantly because we do not need to explicitly calculate the bound charge density — the medium's properties (captured in $\varepsilon_r$) handle it implicitly.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is writing $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ with $\rho_v$ representing *total* charge. This will fetch **zero marks** in part (a). Always explicitly state "free charge density" and never skip the divergence theorem step in derivations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Derive the point form of Gauss's Law in material media starting from its integral form. Explain the physical significance of each term in the final equation. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the integral form of Gauss's Law for a closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $V$.

$$
\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enclosed}
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Express the enclosed free charge as a volume integral of free charge density $\rho_v$.

$$
Q_{enclosed} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute back.

$$
\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply the Divergence Theorem on the left side.

$$
\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) \, dV = \int_V \rho_v \, dV
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Combine into a single integral.

$$
\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{D} - \rho_v) \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Argue that since the volume is arbitrary, the integrand must be zero everywhere.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** State the final result.

$$
\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Physical Significance (7 Marks)**

**Significance 1 (2 Marks):** The equation states that the **divergence** of $\vec{D}$ at a point equals the **free charge density** at that point. A positive divergence indicates a source of flux (positive charge), while negative divergence indicates a sink (negative charge).

**Significance 2 (2 Marks):** The vector $\vec{D}$ represents the **electric flux density**, which is the flux per unit area crossing a surface.
