Its divergence being non-zero means flux lines **originate or terminate** at points where free charges exist — a direct visualization of the field-source relationship.

**Significance 3 (2 Marks):** This is the **first of Maxwell's equations** (in static form) and is the foundation for deriving Poisson's and Laplace's equations: $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$, which in turn enables solving for potentials in any geometry.

**Significance 4 (1 Mark):** The equation is **coordinate-system-independent** and works in Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical coordinates with appropriate operator expansion. This universality makes it a powerful tool in engineering electromagnetics.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a) Starting from $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$, derive the point form of Gauss's Law in terms of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ for a linear, isotropic, and homogeneous (LIH) dielectric medium. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A cylindrical conductor of radius $a$ carries a free charge density $\rho_0$ C/m³ uniformly distributed. Using the point form of Gauss's Law, determine $\vec{D}$ and $\vec{E}$ inside the conductor for an LIH medium with permittivity $\varepsilon$. (7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation for LIH Medium (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Recall the general definition.

$$
\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** For an LIH medium, polarization is proportional to the field: $\vec{P} = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \vec{E}$, where $\chi_e$ is electric susceptibility.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute into the definition.

$$
\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \vec{E}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Define relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$, so $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** So the relation becomes $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substitute into $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ and pull out the constant $\varepsilon$ (since the medium is homogeneous).

$$
\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) = \rho_v
$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Final result.

$$
\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Solved Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** By cylindrical symmetry, the field depends only on the radial distance $r$ from the axis, so $\vec{D} = D_r \hat{a}_r$ and $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v = \rho_0$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** In cylindrical coordinates, the divergence operator applied to a purely radial field simplifies to:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} (r D_r)
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Set up the equation.

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} (r D_r) = \rho_0
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Integrate with respect to $r$.

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial r} (r D_r) = \rho_0 r
$$

$$
r D_r = \int \rho_0 r \, dr = \frac{\rho_0 r^2}{2} + C
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Apply boundary condition: at $r = 0$, $D_r$ must be finite, so $C = 0$.

$$
D_r = \frac{\rho_0 r}{2}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Thus, $\vec{D} = \dfrac{\rho_0 r}{2} \hat{a}_r$.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Using $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$, we get:

$$
\boxed{\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_0 r}{2\varepsilon} \hat{a}_r}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Core Equation:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ — remember $\rho_v$ here means **free** charge density only.
- **Constitutive Relation:** $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E}$ for LIH media.
- **Constant to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **For vacuum:** $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$, and $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v / \varepsilon_0$.
- **Derivation steps to memorize:** Integral form → Volume charge expression → Divergence theorem → Equate integrands → Arbitrary volume argument → Point form.
- **Physical meaning:** Positive divergence = source of flux = positive free charge; Zero divergence = solenoidal/continuous field = charge-free region.
- **Coordinate-specific divergence forms (must know for problems):**
   - Cartesian: $\dfrac{\partial D_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial D_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial D_z}{\partial z} = \rho_v$
   - Cylindrical: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r D_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial D_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \dfrac{\partial D_z}{\partial z} = \rho_v$
   - Spherical: $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial (r^2 D_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial (\sin\theta \, D_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{1}{r \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial D_\phi}{\partial \phi} = \rho_v$
- **Common exam trap:** Never equate $\rho_v$ in $\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$ with $\rho_{total}$ — the $\vec{D}$ equation uses **only free charge**.
- **Link to Poisson's equation:** Taking $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ in an LIH medium gives $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$.
- **Charge-free region:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = 0 \Rightarrow$ Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 V = 0$.
- **Engineering applications to mention in answers:** Capacitors, semiconductor junctions, high-voltage insulation, biomedical devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electric Potential** at any point in an electrostatic field is defined as the amount of **work done per unit positive test charge** in bringing that charge from infinity (a reference point of zero potential) to that specific point, against the direction of the electric field, without any change in kinetic energy.

Mathematically, the electric potential $V$ at a point $P$ is expressed as:

$$V_P = \frac{W_{\infty \to P}}{q_0}$$

where:
- $W_{\infty \to P}$ is the work done by an external agent in moving the test charge $q_0$ from infinity to point $P$
- $q_0$ is the magnitude of the positive test charge (in **Coulombs**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Electric potential is a **scalar quantity**, measured in **Volts (V)**. Since $1 \, V = 1 \, J/C$, potential is fundamentally energy per unit charge. It is one of the most heavily tested topics in Module 2, both in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 1.2 Potential Difference

The **potential difference** between two points $A$ and $B$ in an electric field is defined as the work done per unit positive charge in moving the charge from point $A$ to point $B$ against the electric field:

$$V_{AB} = V_A - V_B = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{W_{A \to B}}{q_0}$$

Equivalently:

$$V_{AB} = \int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in $V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ physically represents that the work done by the external agent (in bringing a positive charge against the field) is the negative of the work done by the field itself. This convention is consistent with the conservative nature of the electrostatic field.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Gravitational Hill

Imagine a heavy ball resting on top of a smooth hill. The higher the ball, the greater its **gravitational potential energy**. Now consider a small positive test charge in an electrostatic field generated by a fixed positive source charge.

| Concept | Gravitational Analogy | Electrostatic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Force field | Gravity ($\vec{g}$) | Electric field ($\vec{E}$) |
| Property of object | Mass ($m$) | Charge ($q$) |
| Energy per unit property | Gravitational potential ($gh$) | Electric potential ($V$) |
| Reference point | Ground level ($h = 0$) | Infinity ($r = \infty$) |
| Direction of motion | Rolling downhill | Positive charge moves from high to low $V$ |

Just as a ball naturally rolls **downhill** (from high gravitational potential to low gravitational potential), a free positive charge in an electrostatic field naturally moves from regions of **higher electric potential** to regions of **lower electric potential**. This is because the electrostatic force does positive work on the charge when it moves to a lower potential energy state.

### 1.4 Key Physical Insights

- **Scalar Nature:** Unlike the electric field $\vec{E}$ (a vector), potential $V$ is a scalar. This makes potential calculations significantly easier in problems involving complex charge geometries, since we only deal with algebraic sums rather than vector components.
- **Reference Convention:** In electrostatics, the reference point of **zero potential is taken at infinity**, where the influence of any finite charge distribution vanishes.
- **Independence from Test Charge:** The potential at a point is a property of the source charge distribution **only** — it does not depend on the test charge $q_0$ used to measure it. Doubling $q_0$ doubles both the work done and the charge, leaving the ratio $V$ unchanged.
- **Sign Convention:** For a **positive source charge**, $V > 0$ everywhere around it. For a **negative source charge**, $V < 0$ everywhere around it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The concept of electric potential is rooted in the **conservative nature** of the electrostatic field. This fundamental property means:

1. **Work is path-independent:** The work done in moving a test charge between two points depends only on the initial and final positions, not on the path taken.
2. **Closed-loop integral vanishes:** $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ for any closed path in an electrostatic field.
3. **Existence of scalar potential:** Because the field is conservative, we can define a scalar function $V$ such that $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.

The operational workflow for solving any potential problem follows this logical chain:

- **Step 1: Identify the source charge distribution** — Is it a point charge, line charge, surface charge, or volume charge?
- **Step 2: Choose the appropriate integral formulation** — Direct integration for continuous distributions, superposition for discrete charges.
- **Step 3: Compute the scalar potential** at the field point by integrating over the source.
- **Step 4: (Optional) Recover the electric field** from the potential using $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ if needed.

### 2.2 Potential Due to Various Charge Distributions

#### (a) Point Charge
For an isolated point charge $Q$ located at the origin, the potential at a distance $r$ is:

$$V(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, r}$$

#### (b) System of $N$ Discrete Point Charges
By the **principle of superposition** (potential being a scalar, superposition is purely algebraic):

$$V(P) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{Q_k}{r_k}$$

where $r_k$ is the distance from the $k$-th charge to the field point $P$.

#### (c) Continuous Line Charge Distribution

$$V(P) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{L} \frac{\rho_L \, dl}{r}$$

#### (d) Continuous Surface Charge Distribution

$$V(P) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{S} \frac{\rho_S \, dS}{r}$$

#### (e) Continuous Volume Charge Distribution

$$V(P) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{V} \frac{\rho_V \, dV}{r}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive reference** for solving all potential-related numerical problems in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize this thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_P = \dfrac{W_{\infty \to P}}{q_0}$ | Definition of potential at point $P$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{AB} = -\displaystyle\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Potential difference between $A$ and $B$ | V |
| 3 | $V(P) = -\displaystyle\int_{\infty}^{P} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Potential from field integral | V |
| 4 | $V(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, r}$ | Potential due to point charge | V |
| 5 | $V(P) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{Q_k}{r_k}$ | Superposition for discrete charges | V |
| 6 | $V(P) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \displaystyle\int_{L} \dfrac{\rho_L \, dl}{r}$ | Continuous line charge | V |
| 7 | $V(P) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \displaystyle\int_{S} \dfrac{\rho_S \, dS}{r}$ | Continuous surface charge | V |
| 8 | $V(P) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \displaystyle\int_{V} \dfrac{\rho_V \, dV}{r}$ | Continuous volume charge | V |
| 9 | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | Gradient relation (vector form) | V/m |
| 10 | $E_x = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad E_y = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad E_z = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial z}$ | Cartesian components of $\vec{E}$ | V/m |
| 11 | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ | Conservative field condition | V·m |
| 12 | $W = q \, V$ | Work to bring charge $q$ from infinity to $V$ | Joules (J) |
| 13 | $U = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{\substack{k=1 \\ k \neq j}}^{N} \dfrac{Q_j Q_k}{r_{jk}}$ | Total energy of $N$-charge system | J |
| 14 | $W_{e} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0}{2} \displaystyle\int_{V} \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} \, dV$ | Stored electrostatic field energy | J |
| 15 | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | Permittivity of free space | F/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU:** The constant $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 8.988 \times 10^{9} \approx 9 \times 10^{9} \, N \cdot m^{2/C^{2}}$. Use $9 \times 10^{9}$ unless the problem specifies otherwise.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of electric potential is not merely an academic exercise — it underpins the operation of nearly every modern electronic device:

- **Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs):** Electron beams are steered and accelerated using precisely controlled potential differences between electrodes.
- **Semiconductor Devices:** The built-in potential across p-n junctions in diodes and transistors determines their operational characteristics.
- **Electrocardiograms (ECG/EKG):** The electrical activity of the human heart generates measurable potential differences on the skin surface, used for medical diagnosis.
- **High-Voltage Power Transmission:** Transmission lines operate at potentials of hundreds of kilovolts to minimize $I^{2}R$ losses over long distances.
- **Electron Microscopes and Particle Accelerators:** Electric potential differences accelerate charged particles to relativistic speeds for imaging and research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Potential Due to an Infinite Line Charge

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the electric potential at a perpendicular distance $\rho$ from an infinite straight line charge with uniform linear charge density $\rho_L$ (in $\text{C/m}$), taking the reference point at $\rho = \rho_0$ where $V = 0$.

#### Step 1: Recall the Electric Field of an Infinite Line Charge

From Gauss's Law (already established in Module 2), the radial electric field at perpendicular distance $\rho$ from an infinite line charge is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \rho} \, \hat{a}_{\rho}$$

This field points radially outward (for $\rho_L > 0$) and depends only on the cylindrical radial coordinate $\rho$.

#### Step 2: Set Up the Potential Integral

Using the defining relation $V(\rho) - V(\rho_0) = -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$, where the path is taken radially (along $\hat{a}_{\rho}$):

$$d\vec{l} = d\rho \, \hat{a}_{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \rho} \, d\rho$$

#### Step 3: Perform the Integration

$$\begin{aligned}
V(\rho) - V(\rho_0) &= -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \, \rho'} \, d\rho' \\[8pt]
&= -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{d\rho'}{\rho'} \\[8pt]
&= -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \Big[\ln \rho' \Big]_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \\[8pt]
&= -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \Big(\ln \rho - \ln \rho_0\Big) \\[8pt]
&= \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \ln \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Reference Condition

Since $V(\rho_0) = 0$ by definition (reference point), we get:

$$\boxed{V(\rho) = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \ln \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Unlike the potential of a point charge (which is well-defined with $V(\infty) = 0$), the potential of an *infinite* line charge **diverges at infinity**. Therefore, we **must** specify a finite reference point $\rho_0$. This is a frequent point of confusion and a common exam pitfall in KTU papers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Potential at an Axial Point of a Uniformly Charged Ring

**Problem Statement:** A ring of radius $a$ carries a uniform line charge $\rho_L$ (total charge $Q = 2\pi a \rho_L$). Derive the electric potential at a point $P$ on the axis of the ring at a distance $z$ from its center.

#### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Every infinitesimal charge element $\rho_L \, dl$ on the ring is at the same distance from the axial point $P$:

$$r = \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}$$

This is the key geometric simplification — the distance is **constant** for all elements on the ring.

#### Step 2: Set Up the Scalar Integral

$$V(z) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{L} \frac{\rho_L \, dl}{r} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\rho_L}{r} \int_{L} dl$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate the Line Integral

The integral $\int_{L} dl$ is simply the total circumference of the ring:

$$\int_{L} dl = 2\pi a$$

#### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
V(z) &= \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\rho_L \cdot 2\pi a}{\sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{\sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V(z) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}}}$$

#### Step 5: Limiting Case Verification

At the center of the ring ($z = 0$):

$$V(0) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 a}$$

This equals the potential of a point charge $Q$ at distance $a$, which makes physical sense because every point on the ring is exactly at distance $a$ from the center.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$

**Starting Point:** We wish to show that the electrostatic field can be derived as the negative gradient of a scalar potential.

#### Step 1: Define the Potential Function

For any two points $A$ and $B$ in an electrostatic field:

$$V(B) - V(A) = -\int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

#### Step 2: Take an Infinitesimal Displacement

Let $B$ be displaced by an infinitesimal vector $d\vec{l} = dx \, \hat{a}_{x} + dy \, \hat{a}_{y} + dz \, \hat{a}_{z}$ from $A$. Then the change in potential is:

$$dV = -\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -(E_x \, dx + E_y \, dy + E_z \, dz)$$

#### Step 3: Use the Total Differential of $V(x,y,z)$

By the chain rule of multivariable calculus:

$$dV = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} dz$$

#### Step 4: Compare Coefficients

Since the expressions for $dV$ must be equal for **all** infinitesimal displacements $dx$, $dy$, $dz$, we equate coefficients:

$$E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

#### Step 5: Combine in Vector Form

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_{x} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_{y} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_{z}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The electric field always points in the direction of **maximum decrease** of potential. This is why positive charges (which experience force along $\vec{E}$) accelerate from regions of high potential to regions of low potential.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy of a System of Point Charges

**Problem Statement:** Derive the total electrostatic potential energy of a system of $N$ point charges.

#### Step 1: Bring the First Charge

Bringing the first charge $Q_1$ from infinity requires no work, because there are no other charges present to create a potential:

$$W_1 = 0$$

#### Step 2: Bring the Second Charge

The work done against the potential created by $Q_1$ at distance $r_{12}$ is:

$$W_2 = Q_2 \cdot V_{12} = \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \, r_{12}}$$

#### Step 3: Bring the Third Charge

The third charge $Q_3$ interacts with both $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:

$$W_3 = Q_3 \left(V_{13} + V_{23}\right) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{Q_1 Q_3}{r_{13}} + \frac{Q_2 Q_3}{r_{23}}\right)$$

#### Step 4: Generalize to $N$ Charges

Continuing this pattern, the total work (and hence the total electrostatic potential energy) is:

$$W_e = U = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{\substack{k=1 \\ k > j}}^{N} \frac{Q_j Q_k}{r_{jk}}$$

The constraint $k > j$ ensures each pair is counted exactly once. Equivalently, this can be written as:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{\substack{k=1 \\ k \neq j}}^{N} \frac{Q_j Q_k}{r_{jk}}$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ compensates for the double counting when we sum over all ordered pairs.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Calculation Methods

| Aspect | Direct Integration of $\vec{E}$ | Direct Integration of $V$ | Gradient Method $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quantity computed first | Electric field $\vec{E}$ (vector) | Potential $V$ (scalar) | Potential $V$ (scalar) |
| Mathematical complexity | Higher (vector integrals) | Lower (scalar integrals) | Lowest for symmetric geometries |
| Path dependence | Must be handled carefully | Path-independent, no issue | Path-independent |
| Best suited for | Simple geometries (infinite sheets, cylinders) | Ring, disk, finite geometries | Already-given potential functions |
| Common KTU problem type | "Find $\vec{E}$, then find $V$" | "Find $V$ directly" | "Given $V$, find $\vec{E}$" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Flow — From Charge Distribution to Observable Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Charge Distribution] --> B{Type of Distribution?}
    B --> C[Point Charge Q]
    B --> D[Line Charge rho_L]
    B --> E[Surface Charge rho_S]
    B --> F[Volume Charge rho_V]
    C --> G[Apply V = Q over 4 pi eps_0 r]
    D --> H[Integrate V = integral of rho_L dl over 4 pi eps_0 r]
    E --> I[Integrate V = integral of rho_S dS over 4 pi eps_0 r]
    F --> J[Integrate V = integral of rho_V dV over 4 pi eps_0 r]
    G --> K[Scalar Potential V scalar]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Next Step?}
    L --> M[Compute Electric Field via E = minus grad V]
    L --> N[Compute Potential Energy U = qV]
    L --> O[Identify Equipotential Surfaces V = constant]
    M --> P[Observable Vector Field E]
    N --> Q[Observable Energy U]
    O --> R[Observable Geometric Surfaces]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Potential Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input: Charge Configuration] --> ANALYZE[Analysis Stage]
    ANALYZE --> SYMMETRY{Check Symmetry}
    SYMMETRY --> HIGH[High Symmetry: Point, Infinite Line, Sphere]
    SYMMETRY --> LOW[Low Symmetry: Finite Geometries, Complex Shapes]
    HIGH --> GAUSS[Apply Gauss Law for E field]
    LOW --> DIRECT[Apply Direct Integration for V]
    GAUSS --> INTEGRATE1[Integrate E dot dl from infinity to P]
    DIRECT --> INTEGRATE2[Integrate dQ over 4 pi eps_0 r]
    INTEGRATE1 --> OUTPUT[Output: Scalar Potential V at Field Point P]
    INTEGRATE2 --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> VALIDATE{Validate Result}
    VALIDATE --> CHECK1[Check limiting cases]
    VALIDATE --> CHECK2[Check units and sign]
    VALIDATE --> CHECK3[Check superposition for multiple charges]
    CHECK1 --> FINAL[Final Verified Potential Expression]
    CHECK2 --> FINAL
    CHECK3 --> FINAL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Equipotential Surfaces Around a Positive Point Charge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CENTER[Positive Point Charge Q at Origin] --> SURF1[Equipotential Surface 1: V = V1 at radius r1]
    CENTER --> SURF2[Equipotential Surface 2: V = V2 at radius r2]
    CENTER --> SURF3[Equipotential Surface 3: V = V3 at radius r3]
    SURF1 --> NOTE1[Note: V1 greater than V2 greater than V3 since V proportional to 1 over r]
    SURF2 --> NOTE1
    SURF3 --> NOTE1
    NOTE1 --> PROPERTY[Properties of Equipotential Surfaces]
    PROPERTY --> PROP1[Surface is always perpendicular to E]
    PROPERTY --> PROP2[No work done moving charge along surface]
    PROPERTY --> PROP3[Closely spaced surfaces indicate stronger field]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic (Text Description for Board Drawing)

When drawing on the exam sheet, students should sketch the following for a **positive point charge**:

- Draw concentric circles centered on the charge (these are the equipotential surfaces in 2D cross-section).
- Draw radial arrows pointing outward (these are the electric field lines).
- The angle between field lines and equipotential surfaces must be exactly $90°$ at every intersection.
- Label the surfaces with decreasing potential values as you move outward: $V_1 > V_2 > V_3 > \ldots$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Drawing Tip:** Always use a **ruler** for the radial field lines and a **compass** for the equipotential circles. A sloppy sketch is the fastest way to lose marks on descriptive questions in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define electric potential and potential difference. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

**Electric Potential:** The electric potential at a point in an electrostatic field is defined as the work done per unit positive test charge in bringing the charge from infinity to that point against the electric field, without any change in kinetic energy.

$$V = \frac{W}{q_0}$$

**Potential Difference:** The potential difference between two points $A$ and $B$ is defined as the work done per unit positive charge in moving it from point $A$ to point $B$ against the electric field:

$$V_{AB} = \frac{W_{AB}}{q_0} = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**SI Units:** Both quantities are measured in **Volts (V)**, where $1 \, V = 1 \, J/C$.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the conservative nature of the electrostatic field. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The electrostatic field is **conservative**, meaning the work done in moving a test charge between two points is independent of the path taken. Mathematically:

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$

This implies that the line integral of $\vec{E}$ between two points depends only on the endpoints, allowing the definition of a **scalar potential function** $V$ such that:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

**Significance:** Because the electrostatic field is conservative, we can define a unique potential at every point in space (with reference to infinity), and energy is conserved when charges move in such a field. The work done around any closed loop is zero, meaning no net energy is gained or lost.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the electric potential at a point on the axis of a uniformly charged ring of radius $a$ and total charge $Q$ at a distance $z$ from the center. Hence find the electric field at the same point.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the geometry**

Consider a ring of radius $a$ in the $xy$-plane, centered at the origin. The field point $P$ is on the $z$-axis at height $z$ above the center. An infinitesimal charge element on the ring is:

$$dq = \rho_L \, dl = \frac{Q}{2\pi a} \, dl$$

**Step 2: Distance from charge element to field point**

By Pythagoras' theorem, every element on the ring is equidistant from $P$:

$$r = \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Set up the potential integral**

$$V(z) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int \frac{dq}{r} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}} \int dq$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral**

$$\int dq = Q$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V(z) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the electric field**

Using $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$, and noting that by symmetry the field has only a $z$-component:

$$E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left[\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}(a^{2} + z^{2})^{-1/2}\right]$$

$$E_z = -\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)(a^{2} + z^{2})^{-3/2} \cdot (2z)$$

$$\boxed{E_z = \frac{Qz}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 (a^{2} + z^{2})^{3/2}}}$$

**Step 6: Verification at limiting cases**

- At $z = 0$ (center of ring): $E_z = 0$ ✓ (by symmetry, the field at the center is zero)
- At $z \gg a$ (far field): $E_z \approx \dfrac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 z^{2}}$ ✓ (reduces to point charge field)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Three point charges $Q_1 = +2 \, \mu C$, $Q_2 = -3 \, \mu C$, and $Q_3 = +4 \, \mu C$ are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $10 \, cm$. Find the electric potential at the centroid of the triangle.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Find the distance from each vertex to the centroid**

For an equilateral triangle of side $a = 10 \, cm = 0.1 \, m$, the distance from each vertex to the centroid is:

$$r = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0577 \, m$$

**Step 2: Apply the superposition principle**

By symmetry, all three charges are at the same distance $r$ from the centroid. Therefore:

$$V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \left(\frac{Q_1}{r} + \frac{Q_2}{r} + \frac{Q_3}{r}\right)$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3}{r} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{0.0577} \cdot \left[(+2) + (-3) + (+4)\right] \times 10^{-6} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 3 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0577} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{27000}{0.0577} \\[8pt]
&\approx 4.68 \times 10^{5} \, V
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 4.68 \times 10^{5} \, V = 468 \, kV}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the potential at a point $P$ located at a perpendicular distance $\rho$ from an infinite straight line charge with uniform linear charge density $\rho_L$, taking the reference at $\rho = \rho_0$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Electric field of infinite line charge (from Gauss's Law)**

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_{\rho}$$

**Step 2: Set up the potential difference integral**

$$\begin{aligned}
V(\rho) - V(\rho_0) &= -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} \\[6pt]
&= -\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho'} d\rho'
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Perform the integration**

$$\begin{aligned}
V(\rho) - V(\rho_0) &= -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \Big[\ln \rho'\Big]_{\rho_0}^{\rho} \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} (\ln \rho - \ln \rho_0) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \ln \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply reference condition $V(\rho_0) = 0$**

$$\boxed{V(\rho) = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \ln \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}}$$

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Given the potential function $V(x, y, z) = 2x^{2}y - 3yz^{2} + 5$ Volts in free space, find the electric field at the point $(1, 2, -1)$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivatives**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x &= -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x^{2}y - 3yz^{2} + 5) = -4xy \\[8pt]
E_y &= -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2x^{2}y - 3yz^{2} + 5) = -(2x^{2} - 3z^{2}) = -2x^{2} + 3z^{2} \\[8pt]
E_z &= -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial z}(2x^{2}y - 3yz^{2} + 5) = 6yz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the point $(1, 2, -1)$**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x &= -4(1)(2) = -8 \, V/m \\[6pt]
E_y &= -2(1)^{2} + 3(-1)^{2} = -2 + 3 = 1 \, V/m \\[6pt]
E_z &= 6(2)(-1) = -12 \, V/m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Express the electric field vector**

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = -8\hat{a}_{x} + 1\hat{a}_{y} - 12\hat{a}_{z} \, V/m}$$

**Step 4: Magnitude of the field**

$$|\vec{E}| = \sqrt{(-8)^{2} + (1)^{2} + (-12)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 1 + 144} = \sqrt{209} \approx 14.46 \, V/m$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reference point for infinite distributions:** Unlike point charges, infinite line and surface charges require a finite reference point. Stating $V(\infty) = 0$ for an infinite line charge is a **critical error** and will cost you 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in $V_{AB} = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$:** Many students write $V_{AB} = +\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$. The negative sign is non-negotiable and comes from the convention that work done *against* the field equals $-W_{\text{field}}$.
> 3. **Treating potential as a vector:** Potential is strictly a **scalar**. Do not attempt to find its "direction." When asked for the field at a point, use $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ to get the vector.
> 4. **Not simplifying via symmetry before integration:** Always look for geometric symmetry (axial, cylindrical, spherical) before setting up integrals. Skipping this wastes time and increases the chance of arithmetic errors.
> 5. **Incorrect units:** Potential is in **Volts**, not Joules. Electric field is in **V/m**, not N/C (though they are dimensionally equivalent, KTU expects consistent usage).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electric Potential (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** $V_P = W_{\infty \to P} / q_0$ — work done per unit positive test charge from infinity to point $P$.
- **SI Unit:** **Volt (V)**, where $1 \, V = 1 \, J/C$.
- **Scalar Nature:** Potential is a scalar; superposition is **algebraic** (just add the values).
- **Point Charge:** $V = Q / (4\pi\varepsilon_0 r)$ with reference at infinity.
- **Infinite Line Charge:** $V = (\rho_L / 2\pi\varepsilon_0) \ln(\rho_0 / \rho)$ — requires a finite reference $\rho_0$.
- **Ring on Axis:** $V(z) = Q / (4\pi\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}})$.
- **Gradient Relation:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ — the field points in the direction of **steepest decrease** of potential.
- **Cartesian Form:** $E_x = -\partial V / \partial x$, $E_y = -\partial V / \partial y$, $E_z = -\partial V / \partial z$.
- **Conservative Field:** $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ — path-independent work.
- **Potential Energy of Charge System:** $U = (1/2) \sum_{j} \sum_{k \neq j} Q_j Q_k / (4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_{jk})$.
- **Energy Density:** $w_e = (\varepsilon_0 / 2) |\vec{E}|^{2} \, J/m^{3}$.
- **Equipotential Surfaces:** Surfaces of constant $V$; always **perpendicular** to $\vec{E}$.
- **Reference Conventions:** Use $V(\infty) = 0$ for finite charge distributions; choose a finite reference for infinite distributions.
- **Work Done:** $W = qV$ to bring charge $q$ from infinity to a point at potential $V$.
- **Permittivity Constant:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m$; $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \approx 9 \times 10^{9} \, N \cdot m^{2}/C^{2}$.
- **Sign Convention:** Positive source charge → $V > 0$ around it; negative source charge → $V < 0$ around it.
- **Exam Strategy:** When asked for $\vec{E}$ given $V$, take partial derivatives. When asked for $V$ given $\vec{E}$, integrate along a convenient path. When given a charge distribution, integrate using the appropriate continuous formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation Between E and V
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relation Between E and V

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Electric Field Intensity (E)
The **Electric Field Intensity (E)** at a point in space is defined as the **force experienced by a unit positive test charge** placed at that point, without disturbing the original charge distribution.

$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_t \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_t} \quad \text{(units: V/m or N/C)}$$

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Electric Potential (V)
The **Electric Potential (V)** at a point in an electrostatic field is defined as the **work done per unit positive charge** in moving the test charge from infinity (a reference point of zero potential) to that point against the electric field, **along an arbitrary path**.

$$V = \frac{W}{q} = -\int_{\infty}^{P} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} \quad \text{(units: Volts or J/C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The relationship between **E (vector field)** and **V (scalar field)** is foundational to solving Laplace's and Poisson's equations. Since V is a scalar, it is mathematically easier to solve for it first and then derive E.

### 1.3 The Core Relationship: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$

The electric field intensity is the **negative gradient of the electric potential**.

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\text{grad}(V)}$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The Hill Model
Imagine you are standing on a **smooth, frictionless hill**:
- **V (Potential)** = the **height** of the hill at your location (a scalar number — "how high am I?").
- **E (Electric Field)** = the **steepness and direction** in which a ball would roll downhill (a vector — "which way and how steep?").
- The **negative sign** means: water always flows from **high height to low height** — the electric field always points from **high potential to low potential**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A charge placed in an electric field always moves to **decrease** its potential energy. Since potential energy $U = qV$, a positive charge naturally moves toward **lower V** regions, meaning E points in the direction of **decreasing** V — hence the negative sign.

### 1.5 Equipotential Surfaces
An **equipotential surface** is a locus of points having the **same electric potential** ($V = \text{constant}$). Key properties:
- No work is done in moving a charge along an equipotential surface.
- The electric field is **always perpendicular** to equipotential surfaces.
- Equipotential surfaces **never intersect**.
- The denser the equipotential surfaces, the **stronger** the electric field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gradient Operator: A Structured Breakdown

The gradient $\nabla V$ of a scalar field $V(x, y, z)$ is a **vector** that points in the direction of **maximum rate of increase** of $V$ and has a magnitude equal to that maximum rate of change.

**Why the negative sign?**
1. $\nabla V$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of V.
2. A positive test charge naturally wants to move from **high V to low V** (to minimize potential energy).
3. Therefore, the electric field E, which determines the force on a positive charge, must point in the **direction of decreasing V**.
4. This opposing direction is mathematically captured by the **minus sign** in $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.

**How the gradient works — step by step:**
- Step 1: At any point P, find the spatial rate of change of V along x, y, and z axes.
- Step 2: Combine these partial derivatives into a vector.
- Step 3: Reverse the direction (negative sign) to get E.

### 2.2 The Line Integral Relationship (Reverse Direction)
Starting from the definition of potential, we can **reverse the relationship** to find the potential difference between two points:

$$V_{AB} = V_A - V_B = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

This is the **work done per unit charge** in moving a test charge from A to B.

### 2.3 The Conservative Nature of Electrostatic Fields
A fundamental property of the electrostatic field is that it is **conservative**, meaning:
- The work done in moving a charge between two points is **path-independent**.
- The line integral of E around any **closed loop is zero**:

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0 \quad \text{(Kirchhoff's Voltage Law for electrostatics)}$$

This follows directly from $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ because the curl of any gradient is identically zero: $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs) and Electron Guns:** Electron velocity is controlled via potential difference ($eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$).
- **Van de Graaff Generators:** High potentials create strong E-fields for particle acceleration.
- **Semiconductor Devices:** Band diagrams use potential (V) to visualize E-fields in p-n junctions.
- **High-Voltage Transmission Lines:** Engineers compute E-field from line potential to ensure **insulation breakdown** does not occur in air ($E_{breakdown} \approx 3 \times 10^6$ V/m).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Coordinate System |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | Fundamental relationship | General |
| 2 | $V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Potential from E-field (line integral) | General |
| 3 | $\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x - \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y - \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ | Cartesian form | Rectangular |
| 4 | $E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$ | x-component of E | Cartesian |
| 5 | $E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}$ | y-component of E | Cartesian |
| 6 | $E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$ | z-component of E | Cartesian |
| 7 | $\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\rho - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi - \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ | Cylindrical form | Cylindrical |
| 8 | $\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta - \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$ | Spherical form | Spherical |
| 9 | $V_{AB} = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Potential difference between A and B | General |
| 10 | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ | Conservative field condition | General |
| 11 | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ | Curl of E (electrostatics) | General |
| 12 | $W = qV$ | Potential energy of charge q at potential V | General |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark problems, you will most often be asked to find E from V (differentiation) or find V from E (integration). Always state units clearly: E in **V/m**, V in **Volts (J/C)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Potential Definition to $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$

**Given:** The potential difference between two closely spaced points P and Q separated by an infinitesimal displacement $d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$.

**Step 1:** Start with the line integral definition of potential difference.

$$dV = -\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

*Logic:* The infinitesimal change in potential is the negative of the work done per unit charge by the field.

**Step 2:** Expand the dot product in Cartesian form.

$$dV = -\left(E_x\,dx + E_y\,dy + E_z\,dz\right)$$

*Logic:* Since $d\vec{l}$ has three components and E has three components, the dot product gives a sum of three products.

**Step 3:** Recognize that for a scalar function $V(x, y, z)$, the total differential is given by the chain rule.

$$dV = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}dz$$

*Logic:* This is the multivariable calculus chain rule for a function of three independent variables.

**Step 4:** Equate the coefficients of $dx$, $dy$, and $dz$ from Step 2 and Step 3.

$$E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

*Logic:* This is a standard mathematical technique — if two linear combinations of independent differentials are equal, their coefficients must match.

**Step 5:** Combine the three scalar components into a single vector equation.

$$\vec{E} = E_x\hat{a}_x + E_y\hat{a}_y + E_z\hat{a}_z = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z\right)$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the parenthesized expression as the **gradient operator** $\nabla V$ (or $\text{grad } V$).

$$\boxed{\vec{E} = -\nabla V}$$

*Logic:* The gradient of a scalar is a vector — the negative of this vector is the electric field. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Finding E from a Given V in Spherical Coordinates

**Problem Setup:** Given the potential $V(r) = \frac{A}{r}$ (where A is a constant), find the electric field E.

**Step 1:** Write down the spherical form of the gradient.

$$\vec{E} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta - \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$$

**Step 2:** Identify the partial derivatives. Since $V = \frac{A}{r}$ depends **only on r** (not on $\theta$ or $\phi$):

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial r} = -\frac{A}{r^2}, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi} = 0$$

*Logic:* Holding $\theta$ and $\phi$ constant, the derivative of $r^{-1}$ with respect to $r$ is $-r^{-2}$ by the power rule. The other partials vanish because V is independent of those variables.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the gradient equation.

$$\vec{E} = -\left(-\frac{A}{r^2}\right)\hat{a}_r - 0 - 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify to obtain the final result.

$$\vec{E} = \frac{A}{r^2}\hat{a}_r$$

*Logic:* This is the famous Coulomb field for a point charge, where $A = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Finding V from a Given E (Line Integral)

**Problem Setup:** Given a uniform electric field $\vec{E} = E_0\,\hat{a}_x$ (constant in space), find the potential difference between point A(0, 0, 0) and point B(x, 0, 0).

**Step 1:** Use the line integral formula for potential difference.

$$V_A - V_B = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 2:** Choose a convenient straight-line path from B to A along the x-axis. The differential displacement is:

$$d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x$$

*Logic:* Since the field is uniform and path-independent (conservative), we can pick the simplest path.

**Step 3:** Compute the dot product.

$$\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = (E_0\,\hat{a}_x) \cdot (dx\,\hat{a}_x) = E_0\,dx$$

*Logic:* The dot product of $\hat{a}_x$ with itself is 1; cross terms with other unit vectors are zero.

**Step 4:** Substitute and integrate.

$$V_A - V_B = -\int_{B}^{A} E_0\,dx = -\int_{x}^{0} E_0\,dx$$

*Logic:* Note the limits: B is at $x = x$ and A is at $x = 0$, so we integrate from $x$ to $0$.

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral.

$$V_A - V_B = -E_0[x]_x^0 = -E_0(0 - x) = E_0 x$$

**Step 6:** State the result with units.

$$\boxed{V(x) = E_0 x \quad \text{(taking } V(0) = 0 \text{ as reference)}}$$

*Logic:* The potential increases linearly with distance in a uniform field. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In a uniform field pointing in +x direction, points at higher x have **higher potential**. A positive charge would experience a force in +x and would accelerate from high V to low V — meaning high V is at +x, contradicting intuition? **No** — actually, in our convention, the force is in the direction of decreasing V. Since $\vec{F} = q\vec{E} = qE_0\hat{a}_x$, the force is in +x, meaning V must **decrease** in +x. Therefore $V = -E_0 x$ if we set $V(0) = 0$. The sign convention requires careful tracking of integration limits. Many textbooks take $V_B - V_A$ — always check the convention in your problem.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type 1 | $V(x, y, z)$ | $\vec{E}$ | Differentiate: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ |
| Type 2 | $\vec{E}$ as function of position | $V$ at a point | Integrate: $V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ |
| Type 3 | Two charges $Q_1, Q_2$ at positions | $V$ and $\vec{E}$ at a field point | Superposition principle |
| Type 4 | Equipotential surfaces | $\vec{E}$ direction | E is $\perp$ to equipotentials |
| Type 5 | Work done in moving charge | Potential difference | $W = q(V_A - V_B)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: From Charge Distribution to E-field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source: Charge Distribution rho, Q"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Scalar Potential V using V = integral of rho dV over 4 pi epsilon r"]
    B --> C["Step 2: V is a scalar field easier to compute than E directly"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Apply Gradient Operator nabla to V"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Apply Negative Sign: E = negative nabla V"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Obtain Vector Electric Field E in Cartesian Cylindrical or Spherical form"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Verify by Curl: nabla cross E must equal zero for electrostatics"]
    G --> H["Output: Valid Electric Field E satisfying Conservative Field Property"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Work, Energy, V, and E

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Test Charge q"]
        A2["Path from Infinity to Point P"]
        A3["Reference Point infinity with V = 0"]
    end

    subgraph Process["PHYSICAL PROCESS"]
        B1["External Agent does Work W against E field"]
        B2["Work per unit charge gives Potential V"]
        B3["Field E points in direction of steepest decrease of V"]
    end

    subgraph Output["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Scalar Potential V at P in Volts"]
        C2["Vector Electric Field E = negative nabla V in V per m"]
        C3["Potential Energy U = qV in Joules"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Visualizing the Gradient Concept (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Scalar Field V x y z"] --> B["Compute partial derivative with respect to x"]
    A --> C["Compute partial derivative with respect to y"]
    A --> D["Compute partial derivative with respect to z"]
    B --> E["Combine into Vector with components"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["Apply Negative Sign"]
    F --> G["Final Electric Field Vector E"]
    H["Direction of E: Towards DECREASING V"] -.-> G
    I["Magnitude of E: Rate of change of V per unit distance"] -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** These block diagrams represent the **operational workflow** for KTU exam problems. The typical 14-mark problem flows from a given V (or charge distribution) → apply gradient → get E. The reverse path (E → V) uses line integration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define electric potential. Derive the relationship between electric field intensity (E) and electric potential (V).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Electric potential at a point is defined as the work done per unit positive charge in moving the test charge from infinity to that point against the electric field.

$$V = -\int_{\infty}^{P} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** For an infinitesimal displacement $d\vec{l}$:

$$dV = -\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -(E_x\,dx + E_y\,dy + E_z\,dz)$$

Also, by chain rule: $dV = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}dz$.

Equating coefficients: $E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$, $E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}$, $E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$.

Combining: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What are equipotential surfaces? State any three properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An equipotential surface is a surface on which the electric potential is **constant** at every point.

**Properties (2 marks — any three):**
1. **No work is done** in moving a charge along an equipotential surface since $V$ is constant.
2. The electric field $\vec{E}$ is always **perpendicular** to the equipotential surface.
3. Two equipotential surfaces **never intersect** (otherwise the intersecting point would have two values of V).
4. The **spacing** between equipotential surfaces indicates field strength — closer spacing = stronger field.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** — The most common mark-loss area. Always write $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ explicitly with the negative sign, not $\vec{E} = \nabla V$.
> 2. **Unit Errors** — E must be in **V/m** or **N/C**; V in **Volts**. Mixing units loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the Path Independence Assumption** — When integrating E to find V, you may pick any path, but you MUST mention "since the electrostatic field is conservative."
> 4. **Coordinate System Mismatch** — If V is given in spherical coordinates, the gradient MUST be applied in spherical form. Switching mid-problem loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Improper Limit Notation** — When using $V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$, explicitly state whether the integral is from infinity to P or from P to infinity.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Given the potential $V = 2x^2y - 5z$ volts in free space, find the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ at the point P(1, 2, 3). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two point charges $Q_1 = 5\,\mu C$ at (0, 0, 0) and $Q_2 = -3\,\mu C$ at (4, 0, 0) are placed in free space. Find the potential and electric field at point P(4, 3, 0). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $V(x, y, z) = 2x^2y - 5z$ Volts; Point P(1, 2, 3).

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the Cartesian form of the gradient.

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x - \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y - \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the partial derivatives.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 4xy, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 2x^2, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -5$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute into the gradient expression.

$$\vec{E} = -4xy\,\hat{a}_x - 2x^2\,\hat{a}_y + 5\,\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate at P(1, 2, 3).

$$\vec{E}\big|_P = -4(1)(2)\,\hat{a}_x - 2(1)^2\,\hat{a}_y + 5\,\hat{a}_z = -8\,\hat{a}_x - 2\,\hat{a}_y + 5\,\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the magnitude and state units.

$$|\vec{E}| = \sqrt{(-8)^2 + (-2)^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{64 + 4 + 25} = \sqrt{93} \approx 9.64 \text{ V/m}$$

**Final Answer:** $\vec{E} = -8\hat{a}_x - 2\hat{a}_y + 5\hat{a}_z$ V/m at P(1, 2, 3).

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $Q_1 = 5\,\mu C$ at origin; $Q_2 = -3\,\mu C$ at (4, 0, 0); Field point P(4, 3, 0).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute distances from each charge to P.

$$r_1 = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (3-0)^2 + 0^2} = 5 \text{ m}$$

$$r_2 = \sqrt{(4-4)^2 + (3-0)^2 + 0^2} = 3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the scalar potential using superposition.

$$V_P = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{Q_1}{r_1} + \frac{Q_2}{r_2}\right)$$

$$V_P = 9 \times 10^9 \left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{5} + \frac{-3 \times 10^{-6}}{3}\right) = 9 \times 10^9 (10^{-6} - 10^{-6}) = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute unit vectors from each charge to P.

From $Q_1$ (origin) to P(4, 3, 0):

$$\vec{r}_1 = 4\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y, \quad \hat{a}_{r1} = \frac{4\hat{a}_x + 3\hat{a}_y}{5} = 0.8\hat{a}_x + 0.6\hat{a}_y$$

From $Q_2$ (4, 0, 0) to P(4, 3, 0):

$$\vec{r}_2 = 3\hat{a}_y, \quad \hat{a}_{r2} = \hat{a}_y$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the electric field using superposition.

$$\vec{E}_1 = \frac{Q_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_1^2}\hat{a}_{r1} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{25}(0.8\hat{a}_x + 0.6\hat{a}_y) = 1800(0.8\hat{a}_x + 0.6\hat{a}_y)$$

$$\vec{E}_1 = 1440\,\hat{a}_x + 1080\,\hat{a}_y \text{ V/m}$$

$$\vec{E}_2 = \frac{Q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_2^2}\hat{a}_{r2} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times (-3 \times 10^{-6})}{9}\hat{a}_y = -3000\,\hat{a}_y \text{ V/m}$$

$$\vec{E}_{total} = \vec{E}_1 + \vec{E}_2 = 1440\,\hat{a}_x + (1080 - 3000)\,\hat{a}_y = 1440\,\hat{a}_x - 1920\,\hat{a}_y \text{ V/m}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_P = 0$ V; $\vec{E}_P = 1440\,\hat{a}_x - 1920\,\hat{a}_y$ V/m.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Given $\vec{E} = 5x\,\hat{a}_x + 2y\,\hat{a}_y + 3z\,\hat{a}_z$ V/m, find the potential difference between point A(1, 1, 1) and point B(2, 3, 4). Also determine the potential at the origin if $V(0, 0, 0) = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the conservative property of the electrostatic field. Show that $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ for the field $\vec{E} = 2x\,\hat{a}_x + 2y\,\hat{a}_y$ and comment on whether it can be derived from a scalar potential. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $\vec{E} = 5x\,\hat{a}_x + 2y\,\hat{a}_y + 3z\,\hat{a}_z$ V/m; A(1, 1, 1); B(2, 3, 4).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Use the line integral formula.

$$V_A - V_B = -\int_{B}^{A} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose a straight-line path. Parameterize as $\vec{l}(t)$ for $t \in [0, 1]$ where $\vec{l}(t) = A + t(B - A) = (1+t, 1+2t, 1+3t)$.

$$d\vec{l} = (dt, 2\,dt, 3\,dt)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the dot product.

$$\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = [5(1+t)(dt) + 2(1+2t)(2\,dt) + 3(1+3t)(3\,dt)]$$

$$= [5(1+t) + 4(1+2t) + 9(1+3t)]\,dt$$

$$= [5 + 5t + 4 + 8t + 9 + 27t]\,dt = [18 + 40t]\,dt$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$.

$$V_A - V_B = \int_0^1 (18 + 40t)\,dt = \left[18t + 20t^2\right]_0^1 = 18 + 20 = 38 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Find the potential at origin using $V(0,0,0) = 0$ as reference.

$$V(0,0,0) - V_A = -\int_A^0 \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} \implies V(0,0,0) = V_A - \int_A^0 \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

Alternative direct path A to O: $V_A - V_O = \int_A^O \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$. Parameterize $\vec{l}(t) = (1-t, 1-t, 1-t)$, $t \in [0, 1]$. Then $d\vec{l} = -(dt, dt, dt)$.

$$\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -[5(1-t) + 2(1-t) + 3(1-t)]\,dt = -10(1-t)\,dt$$

$$V_A - V_O = \int_0^1 -10(1-t)\,dt = -10\left[t - \frac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^1 = -10(0.5) = -5$$

$$V_A = V_O - 5 = 0 - 5 = -5 \text{ V (potential at A)}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_A - V_B = 38$ V; $V_A = -5$ V (relative to origin).

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the conservative property.

The electrostatic field is **conservative**, meaning:
- The work done in moving a charge between two points is **independent of the path taken**.
- Mathematically: $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ for any closed path.
- By Stokes' theorem: $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ identically in electrostatics.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Significance — The existence of a single-valued scalar potential $V$ such that $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ is guaranteed only if $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Compute $\nabla \times \vec{E}$ for $\vec{E} = 2x\,\hat{a}_x + 2y\,\hat{a}_y$.

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ 2x & 2y & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$= \hat{a}_x\left(\frac{\partial 0}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial 2y}{\partial z}\right) - \hat{a}_y\left(\frac{\partial 0}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial 2x}{\partial z}\right) + \hat{a}_z\left(\frac{\partial 2y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial 2x}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$= \hat{a}_x(0 - 0) - \hat{a}_y(0 - 0) + \hat{a}_z(0 - 0) = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ is verified, the field can indeed be derived from a scalar potential. We can integrate:

$$E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 2x \implies V = -x^2 + f(y, z)$$

$$E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 2y \implies V = -y^2 + g(x, z)$$

Comparing, $V = -x^2 - y^2 + C$ (constant C).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Relation Between E and V"**

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ — memorize with units (E: V/m, V: Volts).
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Negative sign means E points from **high V → low V**.
- ✅ **Cartesian form:** $E_x = -\partial V/\partial x$, $E_y = -\partial V/\partial y$, $E_z = -\partial V/\partial z$.
- ✅ **Cylindrical form:** $E_\rho = -\partial V/\partial \rho$, $E_\phi = -(1/\rho)\partial V/\partial \phi$, $E_z = -\partial V/\partial z$.
- ✅ **Spherical form:** $E_r = -\partial V/\partial r$, $E_\theta = -(1/r)\partial V/\partial \theta$, $E_\phi = -(1/r\sin\theta)\partial V/\partial \phi$.
- ✅ **Reverse relationship:** $V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ (path-independent for electrostatic fields).
- ✅ **Conservative property:** $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ and $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ — both must be **zero** for electrostatics.
- ✅ **Equipotential surfaces:** $V = \text{const}$, $\vec{E} \perp$ to surface, never intersect, denser = stronger field.
- ✅ **Potential energy:** $U = qV$ (Joules); Work done $W = q(V_A - V_B)$.
- ✅ **Energy density:** $w_E = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ (J/m³) — useful for parallel plate capacitor problems.
- ✅ **Point charge field:** $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}\hat{a}_r$ corresponds to $V = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$.
- ✅ **Infinite line charge:** $\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$ corresponds to $V = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\rho + C$.
- ✅ **Superposition principle:** Holds for **both** V (scalar sum) and $\vec{E}$ (vector sum).
- ✅ **Reference point:** $V(\infty) = 0$ for point charges; $V = 0$ at the conductor surface for grounded conductors.
- ✅ **Always state the coordinate system** before applying the gradient — this is the #1 cause of partial mark deductions in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Due to Electric Dipole
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Field Due to Electric Dipole

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **electric dipole** is a system of two equal and opposite point charges, $+q$ and $-q$, separated by a very small distance $2a$ (or $d$), such that the separation vector is much smaller than the distance of observation point from the center of the dipole. The fundamental property of an electric dipole is characterized by the **electric dipole moment vector**, defined as:

$$\vec{p} = q \cdot \vec{2a}$$

The direction of $\vec{p}$ is conventionally taken from the negative charge to the positive charge. The SI unit of electric dipole moment is **Coulomb-meter (C·m)**, and in Gaussian units it is expressed in **Debye (1 Debye = 3.335 × 10⁻³⁰ C·m)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The dipole is treated as a *point dipole* when $r \gg 2a$. Under this approximation, the field expressions are derived at three canonical positions:
> (i) **Axial position** (end-on / collinear with dipole axis)
> (ii) **Equatorial position** (broadside-on / perpendicular to dipole axis)
> (iii) **General position** (arbitrary point at distance $r$ and angle $\theta$)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine holding a bar magnet — one end is "north," the other is "south." A bar magnet is a *magnetic dipole* because it has two opposite poles. An electric dipole is the exact electrical counterpart: a tiny pair of equal but opposite electric charges bound together.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a water molecule (H₂O). Although the molecule is electrically neutral overall, the oxygen atom carries a slight negative charge while the two hydrogen atoms carry a slight positive charge. This asymmetric charge distribution makes water a *permanent electric dipole*. This intrinsic dipole nature is precisely why water is an excellent solvent for ionic compounds (like salt) and exhibits phenomena such as surface tension and high boiling point.

**Intuitive understanding:** When a dipole is placed in a uniform external electric field, it experiences a *torque* (a twisting force) that tries to align it with the field — just like a compass needle aligns with Earth's magnetic field. The dipole *itself* also produces its own electric field around it, which is what we derive mathematically in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used:**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ (also $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \ \text{N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$)
> - Coulomb's constant $k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$
> - Dipole moment magnitude: $p = q \cdot (2a)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown of the Dipole Field Problem

**Step 1 — Recognize the system geometry:**
The dipole consists of two charges $+q$ at position $+a$ and $-q$ at position $-a$ along an axis. The midpoint is the *center* of the dipole. We want the field at a point $P$ located at distance $r$ from the center, making an angle $\theta$ with the dipole axis.

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of superposition:**
The total field at $P$ is the vector sum of the field due to $+q$ and the field due to $-q$:
$$\vec{E}_P = \vec{E}_{(+q)} + \vec{E}_{(-q)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the point-dipole approximation ($r \gg 2a$):**
Under this limit, we can perform binomial expansions of the form $(r \mp a)^{-2}$ and keep only the leading-order term. This is where the "dipole" $1/r^3$ dependence emerges, distinct from the $1/r^2$ Coulomb dependence of a single charge.

**Step 4 — Resolve the field into components:**
The result is conventionally expressed in spherical coordinates with components along $\hat{r}$ (radial, away from dipole center) and $\hat{\theta}$ (transverse, perpendicular to $\hat{r}$ in the plane of the dipole).

**Step 5 — Identify the two canonical limits:**
- $\theta = 0^\circ$ gives the **axial (end-on) field**
- $\theta = 90^\circ$ gives the **equatorial (broadside-on) field**

### 2.2 Why Dipole Fields Fall Off as $1/r^3$

A single isolated charge produces a field that decays as $1/r^2$ because the field lines spread out over a sphere of area $4\pi r^2$. In a dipole, the two opposite charges *partially cancel* at large distances. The cancellation is not complete — the leading $1/r^2$ monopole terms cancel out exactly (because net charge is zero), leaving the *next-order* term, which is of order $1/r^3$. This is the hallmark of dipole behavior, and the same $1/r^3$ law appears in magnetism (magnetic dipole field) and gravitational physics (tidal forces).

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

The electric dipole concept is foundational in:
- **Antenna theory:** Short linear antennas behave as oscillating electric dipoles.
- **Molecular chemistry:** Polar molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) are characterized by their dipole moments.
- **Dielectric materials:** The polarization of a dielectric medium arises from the alignment of molecular dipoles.
- **Microwave engineering:** Dipole radiation patterns are used in RF and radar systems.
- **Bioelectric phenomena:** Cell membranes exhibit dipole-like charge distributions relevant to ECG/EEG modeling.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Direction / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dipole moment | $\vec{p}$ | $\vec{p} = q \cdot \vec{2a}$ | From $-q$ to $+q$ |
| Axial field (end-on) | $E_{\text{axial}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{2p}{r^3}$ | Parallel to $\vec{p}$ |
| Equatorial field (broadside) | $E_{\text{equat}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p}{r^3}$ | Anti-parallel to $\vec{p}$ |
| Radial field component | $E_r$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{2p \cos\theta}{r^3}$ | Along $\hat{r}$ |
| Transverse field component | $E_\theta$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \sin\theta}{r^3}$ | Along $\hat{\theta}$ (opposite sense) |
| Total field magnitude | $E$ | $\dfrac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3} \sqrt{1 + 3\cos^2\theta}$ | — |
| Electric potential | $V$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{r^2}$ | Scalar field |
| Torque in external field | $\vec{\tau}$ | $\vec{\tau} = \vec{p} \times \vec{E}$ | Tends to align $\vec{p} \parallel \vec{E}$ |
| Potential energy | $U$ | $U = -\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E} = -pE\cos\theta$ | Minimum at $\theta = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall Reminder:** The factor is **2** for the axial field but **1** for the equatorial field. The ratio $E_{\text{axial}} \mid E_{\text{equat}} = 2:1$. Examiners frequently test this ratio question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field on the Axial Line (End-on Position)

**Setup:** Consider a dipole with charges $+q$ at point $A$ and $-q$ at point $B$, separated by distance $2a$. Let $O$ be the midpoint. We want the field at point $P$ on the axis, at distance $r$ from $O$ (on the side of $+q$).

**Geometric reasoning:**
- Distance from $+q$ to $P$ is $r - a$
- Distance from $-q$ to $P$ is $r + a$
- Both charges produce fields along the line, with the field of $+q$ pointing *away* from $+q$ (i.e., from $A$ to $P$), and the field of $-q$ pointing *toward* $-q$ (i.e., from $P$ to $B$).

**Apply Coulomb's law for each charge:**
The field at $P$ due to $+q$ is:
$$E_{(+q)} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{(r-a)^2} \quad \text{(directed from } A \text{ toward } P\text{)}$$

The field at $P$ due to $-q$ is:
$$E_{(-q)} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{(r+a)^2} \quad \text{(directed from } P \text{ toward } B\text{)}$$

Since both fields point in the *same* direction (from $+q$ toward $-q$, which is the direction of $\vec{p}$), they add algebraically:

$$E_{\text{axial}} = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ \frac{1}{(r-a)^2} - \frac{1}{(r+a)^2} \right]$$

**Combine the fractions over a common denominator:**

$$E_{\text{axial}} = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(r+a)^2 - (r-a)^2}{(r-a)^2(r+a)^2}$$

**Expand the numerator using $(r+a)^2 - (r-a)^2 = 4ar$:**

$$E_{\text{axial}} = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{4ar}{(r^2 - a^2)^2}$$

**Apply the point-dipole approximation** $r \gg a$, so $r^2 - a^2 \approx r^2$:

$$E_{\text{axial}} \approx \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{4ar}{r^4} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{4qa}{r^3}$$

**Recognize $p = q \cdot 2a$, so $4qa = 2p$:**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{axial}} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2p}{r^3}}$$

The direction is along $\vec{p}$ (from $-q$ to $+q$, i.e., away from the dipole on the positive side).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Field on the Equatorial Line (Broadside-on Position)

**Setup:** Now consider point $P$ on the perpendicular bisector of the dipole axis, at distance $r$ from $O$.

**Geometric reasoning:**
- Distance from $+q$ to $P$ is $\sqrt{r^2 + a^2}$ (same as from $-q$ to $P$ by symmetry).
- The magnitudes of the individual fields are equal.
- The components of the two fields *along* the dipole axis are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction (they cancel).
- The components *perpendicular* to the dipole axis (along the line from $O$ to $P$) are equal in magnitude and same in direction (they add).

**Magnitude of each field at $P$:**

$$E_{(+q)} = E_{(-q)} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2 + a^2}$$

**Component along $OP$ direction (perpendicular to dipole axis):**
Using $\cos\phi = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{r^2 + a^2}}$ as the angle between the line from $+q$ (or $-q$) to $P$ and the dipole axis:

$$E_{\text{component}} = E_{(+q)} \cos\phi = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2 + a^2} \cdot \frac{a}{\sqrt{r^2 + a^2}} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{qa}{(r^2 + a^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Add contributions from both charges (they point in the same direction along $OP$):**

$$E_{\text{equat}} = 2 \times \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{qa}{(r^2 + a^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2qa}{(r^2 + a^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Apply the point-dipole approximation** $r \gg a$, so $r^2 + a^2 \approx r^2$:

$$E_{\text{equat}} \approx \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2qa}{r^3} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p}{r^3}$$

(The last step uses $p = q \cdot 2a$, so $2qa = p$.)

$$\boxed{E_{\text{equat}} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p}{r^3}}$$

The direction is **anti-parallel** to $\vec{p}$ (i.e., from $+q$ side to $-q$ side along the axis, but the field at $P$ points opposite to the dipole moment direction at this point).

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Result:** $E_{\text{axial}} = 2 E_{\text{equat}}$ at the same distance $r$. This factor of 2 is a frequently asked KTU question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — General Field at an Arbitrary Point

**Setup:** Point $P$ is at distance $r$ from the dipole center $O$, with the line $OP$ making angle $\theta$ with the dipole axis $\vec{p}$.

**Distances from charges to $P$:**
- From $+q$ (at $+a$ on axis): $r_1 = \sqrt{r^2 + a^2 - 2ar\cos\theta}$
- From $-q$ (at $-a$ on axis): $r_2 = \sqrt{r^2 + a^2 + 2ar\cos\theta}$

**Expand under point-dipole approximation** $r \gg a$. Define $\delta = a/r \ll 1$. Then:

$$r_1^2 = r^2(1 - 2\delta\cos\theta + \delta^2), \quad r_2^2 = r^2(1 + 2\delta\cos\theta + \delta^2)$$

To first order in $\delta$ (neglecting $\delta^2$):
$$r_1 \approx r(1 - \delta\cos\theta), \quad r_2 \approx r(1 + \delta\cos\theta)$$

**Potential at $P$ from each charge:**

$$V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q}{r_1} - \frac{q}{r_2}\right]$$

Substitute the approximate expressions:

$$V = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \cdot r}\left[\frac{1}{1 - \delta\cos\theta} - \frac{1}{1 + \delta\cos\theta}\right]$$

Using the binomial expansion $\dfrac{1}{1 \mp x} \approx 1 \pm x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$V \approx \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \cdot r}\left[(1 + \delta\cos\theta) - (1 - \delta\cos\theta)\right] = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \cdot r} \cdot 2\delta\cos\theta$$

Substitute $\delta = a/r$ and $p = 2qa$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p\cos\theta}{r^2}}$$

**Recover the field components** by $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ in spherical coordinates. Since $V$ depends only on $r$ and $\theta$:

$$E_r = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2p\cos\theta}{r^3}$$

$$E_\theta = -\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p\sin\theta}{r^3}$$

**Total field magnitude:**

$$E = \sqrt{E_r^2 + E_\theta^2} = \frac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\sqrt{4\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta} = \frac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\sqrt{1 + 3\cos^2\theta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of canonical cases:**
> - At $\theta = 0$: $E = \dfrac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\sqrt{1+3} = \dfrac{2p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}$ ✓ (matches axial formula)
> - At $\theta = 90°$: $E = \dfrac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}\sqrt{1+0} = \dfrac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}$ ✓ (matches equatorial formula)

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torque on a Dipole in a Uniform External Field

Consider a dipole $\vec{p}$ placed in a uniform external field $\vec{E}$. The force on $+q$ is $q\vec{E}$ and on $-q$ is $-q\vec{E}$. Since the field is uniform, the net force is zero, but the two forces form a **couple** producing a torque.

The torque about the center is:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{r}_{(+)} \times (q\vec{E}) + \vec{r}_{(-)} \times (-q\vec{E}) = q(\vec{r}_{(+)} - \vec{r}_{(-)}) \times \vec{E}$$

Since $\vec{p} = q(\vec{r}_{(+)} - \vec{r}_{(-)})$:

$$\boxed{\vec{\tau} = \vec{p} \times \vec{E}}$$

Magnitude: $\tau = pE\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{E}$.

**Potential energy** (obtained by integrating torque over angle):

$$U = \int_{\pi/2}^{\theta} \tau \, d\theta' = -pE\cos\theta = -\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Electric Dipole Field System

The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional architecture of the electric dipole field problem — the geometric setup, the field calculation modules, the canonical position reducers, and the final result outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Dipole System] --> B[Two Point Charges: +q and -q]
    A --> C[Dipole Moment Vector p = q . 2a]

    B --> D[Field Superposition Module]
    C --> D

    D --> E{Point P Position Selector}

    E -->|theta = 0| F1[Axial End-on Calculator]
    E -->|theta = 90 deg| F2[Equatorial Broadside Calculator]
    E -->|arbitrary theta| F3[General Position Calculator]

    F1 --> G1[Apply r >> a Approximation]
    F2 --> G2[Apply r >> a Approximation]
    F3 --> G3[Binomial Expansion Module]

    G1 --> H1[Output: E_axial = 2p over 4 pi eps0 r cubed]
    G2 --> H2[Output: E_equat = p over 4 pi eps0 r cubed]
    G3 --> H4[Output: V = p cos theta over 4 pi eps0 r squared]

    H1 --> I[Final Result Aggregator]
    H2 --> I
    H4 --> I

    I --> J[Engineering Application Layer]
    J --> J1[Antenna Radiation Patterns]
    J --> J2[Molecular Polar Chemistry]
    J --> J3[Dielectric Material Design]
    J --> J4[Bioelectric Cell Modeling]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map: Field Direction at Canonical Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Axial Point P at theta = 0] -->|E parallel to p| L1[Field points AWAY from +q]
    K2[Equatorial Point Q at theta = 90] -->|E anti-parallel to p| L2[Field points TOWARD -q]
    K3[General Point R at angle theta] -->|E has two components| L3[Er radial plus E theta transverse]

    L1 --> M[Ratio 2 to 1]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> N[Decay law 1 over r cubed]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Dipole Field Component Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Dipole Moment p along z axis] --> S2[Field at point r, theta]
    S2 --> S3[Resolve into Spherical Components]
    S3 --> S4[Radial Component Er = 2p cos theta / 4 pi eps0 r cubed]
    S3 --> S5[Transverse Component E theta = p sin theta / 4 pi eps0 r cubed]
    S4 --> S6[Total Field Magnitude]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[E = p over 4 pi eps0 r cubed times sqrt 1 + 3 cos squared theta]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define electric dipole moment. State its SI unit and write the expression for the electric field at a point on the axial line of a short dipole.

**Model Answer:**
Electric dipole moment is defined as the product of the magnitude of either charge and the vector separation between the charges:
$$\vec{p} = q \cdot \vec{2a}$$
where $q$ is the magnitude of either charge and $\vec{2a}$ is the vector from $-q$ to $+q$. **SI unit: Coulomb-meter (C·m)**.

The electric field at a point on the axial line of a short dipole, at distance $r$ from the center, is:
