> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Many students forget to convert mm to m when computing area. Always convert at the very start.  
> 2. **Vector vs scalar confusion** — Writing $J = \sigma E$ (scalar) when the question asks for the vector form will cost 1–2 marks. Always write $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$.  
> 3. **Skipping the limit step** — In derivations, you must explicitly state "as $\Delta V \to 0$" to convert macroscopic quantities to point quantities.  
> 4. **Forgetting the validity conditions** — Listing $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ without mentioning *linear, isotropic, homogeneous, ohmic* conditions loses 1 mark.  
> 5. **Sign errors in $\vec{v}_d$** — Drift velocity of electrons is *opposite* to $\vec{J}$ and $\vec{E}$ (since charge is negative).  
> 6. **Misinterpreting "loss tangent"** — It is $\sigma / (\omega \varepsilon)$, NOT $\omega \varepsilon / \sigma$. A common trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pin this checklist to your study wall before the exam.**

- ✅ The point form of Ohm's law is $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$, valid *at every point* in a conducting medium.
- ✅ Units: $\vec{J}$ in A/m², $\vec{E}$ in V/m, $\sigma$ in S/m, $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$.
- ✅ Conductivity and resistivity are reciprocals: $\sigma = 1/\rho$.
- ✅ Inverse form: $\vec{E} = \rho \vec{J}$.
- ✅ Derivation uses a cylindrical chunk and the limit $\Delta V \to 0$.
- ✅ Validity requires: **L**inear, **I**sotropic, **H**omogeneous, **T**ime-invariant, **O**hmic (**LIHTO** mnemonic).
- ✅ Anisotropic media need the tensor form: $\vec{J} = \bar{\bar{\sigma}} \cdot \vec{E}$.
- ✅ Vacuum is a perfect insulator: $\sigma = 0$ → $\vec{J} = 0$ always.
- ✅ Drift velocity: $\vec{v}_d = \vec{J} / \rho_v$ (sign depends on carrier charge).
- ✅ Conduction current: $I_c = \int_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$.
- ✅ Loss tangent (for dielectrics): $\tan \delta = \sigma / (\omega \varepsilon)$.
- ✅ This relation is the *starting point* for deriving wave propagation in conducting media, skin depth, and the continuity equation.
- ✅ The point form is more general than $V = IR$ — it works for *any geometry* and *non-uniform materials*.
- ✅ In Module 2, this topic is a *prerequisite* for the Continuity Equation, Relaxation Time, and Boundary Conditions — master it well.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Electromagnetic Field Theory (EMT)**, **Resistance** is defined as a macroscopic circuit parameter that emerges from the fundamental field relationship between the applied electric field, the resulting current density, and the material properties of the medium. From the point of view of fields (as opposed to circuit theory), resistance is the ratio of the total voltage drop across a conducting medium to the total steady current flowing through it, integrated over the geometry of the conductor.

Mathematically, the general expression for resistance of any conducting geometry is:

$$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{\int_{a}^{b} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}} = \dfrac{\int_{a}^{b} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint_{S} \sigma \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

where $\sigma$ is the **conductivity** of the material in **Siemens per meter (S/m)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEET502, Module 2):**
> Resistance is treated as a *derived field quantity* — meaning it is computed by solving for the electric field $\vec{E}$ and current density $\vec{J}$ inside the conductor using **Ohm's Law in Point Form**: $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$. This is the central distinction between *Field Theory* and *Circuit Theory* approaches.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank connected to a long, narrow pipe**. The pipe offers opposition to the water flow. The narrower the pipe, the higher the opposition; the longer the pipe, the higher the opposition; and the rougher the inner surface, the higher the opposition. This opposition is what we call **resistance** in fluid flow — analogous to **electrical resistance** for current flow.

- **Length of pipe** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Length of conductor (l)** — longer path, more opposition.
- **Cross-sectional area of pipe** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Cross-sectional area of conductor (A)** — wider path, less opposition.
- **Roughness of pipe** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Resistivity of material ($\rho$)** — higher resistivity, more opposition.

The flow of water (current) needs a pressure difference (voltage), and the ratio of pressure difference to flow rate gives the resistance. The *EMT perspective* says: "Don't just use the formula $R = l/(\sigma A)$ — derive it from the electric field inside the conductor."

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Quantities Used in Resistance Computations:**
> - **Resistivity** ($\rho$): in **Ohm-meters ($\Omega \cdot m$)**
> - **Conductivity** ($\sigma = 1/\rho$): in **Siemens per meter (S/m)**
> - **Permeability** ($\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m): relevant for surrounding magnetic field
> - **Permittivity** ($\varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m): relevant in related capacitance problems
> - **Current Density** ($\vec{J}$): in **Amperes per square meter (A/m²)**
> - **Electric Field** ($\vec{E}$): in **Volts per meter (V/m)**

### 1.4 Physical Significance of Resistance in EMT

Resistance in EMT is not just a "circuit element" — it represents the **dissipation of electromagnetic energy** into heat (Joule heating). The power dissipated per unit volume is governed by **Joule's Law in point form**:

$$p = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} = \sigma \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} = \vert \vec{J} \vert^{2} / \sigma \quad \text{[Watts/m}^{3}\text{]}$$

This connects resistance directly to the conservation of energy in electromagnetic systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Resistance Concept

The derivation of resistance in field theory follows a strict, four-step logical sequence:

1. **Apply the continuity equation** in steady (DC) state. Since charges do not accumulate at any point in a conducting medium under DC conditions, the divergence of current density is zero:
   $$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$$

2. **Apply Ohm's Law in point form** to relate the current density to the applied electric field through the material conductivity:
   $$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

3. **Substitute into the continuity equation**. Since $\sigma$ is constant inside a homogeneous medium, this reduces to **Laplace's equation** for the electric potential $V$:
   $$\nabla \cdot (\sigma \vec{E}) = \sigma \, \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla^{2} V = 0$$

4. **Solve Laplace's equation** subject to the boundary conditions on the conductor geometry. Once $V$ (and thus $\vec{E}$) is known, compute the total current $I$ by integrating $\vec{J}$ over any cross-section, and divide the total voltage by this current to obtain $R$.

### 2.2 Why This Approach Matters

The "Why" behind using this field-based approach is rooted in **energy conservation**. Circuit theory treats resistance as a black box (with a voltage across and a current through). However, the field approach reveals *where* inside the conductor the energy is being dissipated. For non-uniform geometries (coaxial cables, spherical electrodes, grounding rods, parallel-plate structures with fringing), the simple formula $R = l/(\sigma A)$ is no longer accurate, and only the field approach gives the correct answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Resistance Computations:**
> - **Design of grounding electrodes** in substations and lightning protection systems.
> - **Leakage current analysis** in underground power cables and insulators.
> - **Heating of conductors** in transformers, busbars, and overhead transmission lines.
> - **Return-path resistance** in coaxial cables and PCB traces.
> - **Bio-electromagnetic modeling** (resistance of human tissue to current flow in medical devices).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint \sigma \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}}$ | General field-theoretic definition of resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ | Ohm's Law in point form | A/m² |
| 3 | $p = \sigma \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}$ | Power dissipated per unit volume (Joule's Law) | W/m³ |
| 4 | $P = I^{2} R$ | Total power dissipated in a resistor of value $R$ | W |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{l}{\sigma A}$ | Resistance of a uniform cylindrical conductor (length $l$, area $A$) | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\rho l}{A}$ | Same as above, using resistivity $\rho = 1/\sigma$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{\rho \, \ln(b/a)}{2\pi L}$ | Resistance per unit length of a coaxial cable (inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, length $L$) | $\Omega$/m |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \, d}{A}$ | Resistance of a parallel-plate conductor (separation $d$, plate area $A$, with leaky dielectric of conductivity $\sigma$) | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{4\pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_{a}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{b}} \right)$ | Resistance between two concentric spherical shells (inner radius $r_{a}$, outer radius $r_{b}$) | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)$ | Resistance of earth electrode / grounding rod (length $L$) — approximate form | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $\nabla^{2} V = 0$ | Laplace's equation in a source-free, homogeneous conducting medium | — |
| 12 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ | Steady-state continuity equation (KCL in field form) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Formula #7 (coaxial cable resistance) and Formula #9 (spherical shells) are the two most frequently asked derivations in KTU end-semester exams. Memorize the cylindrical and spherical Laplacian in cylindrical and spherical coordinates — they are essential for solving these.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Resistance of a Uniform Cylindrical Conductor

**Objective:** Derive the familiar formula $R = l/(\sigma A)$ from Maxwell's equations and Ohm's law.

**Step 1 — Assume steady DC conditions.**
There is no time variation, so the displacement current is zero. The continuity equation reduces to:
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law in point form.**
Inside a homogeneous, linear, isotropic conductor:
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

**Step 3 — Express the electric field as the gradient of a scalar potential.**
$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

**Step 4 — Combine the three equations.**
$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma (-\nabla V)) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma \, \nabla^{2} V = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla^{2} V = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation** for the potential inside the conductor.

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary conditions on a uniform cylinder.**
Consider a cylindrical conductor of length $l$ and cross-sectional area $A$, with a potential $V_1$ applied at one end and $V_2$ at the other end. The electric field is uniform along the axis (z-direction) and the potential varies linearly:
$$V(z) = V_1 - \left(\dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{l}\right) z$$

**Step 6 — Compute the electric field.**
$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\dfrac{dV}{dz}\hat{a}_z = \left(\dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{l}\right)\hat{a}_z = \dfrac{V}{l}\hat{a}_z$$

where $V = V_1 - V_2$ is the potential difference across the conductor.

**Step 7 — Compute the current density.**
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \dfrac{\sigma V}{l}\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 8 — Compute the total current by integrating the current density over the cross-section.**
$$I = \oint_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{S} \dfrac{\sigma V}{l} \, dS = \dfrac{\sigma V}{l} \int_{S} dS = \dfrac{\sigma V A}{l}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the resistance.**
$$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{V}{\sigma V A / l} = \dfrac{l}{\sigma A}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{l}{\sigma A} = \dfrac{\rho \, l}{A}}$$

This is the **standard textbook formula**, now derived rigorously from field theory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Resistance of a Coaxial Cable (Leakage Between Conductors)

**Objective:** A coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius $a$, an outer conductor of radius $b$, and a length $L$. The space between the conductors is filled with a material of conductivity $\sigma$. Find the leakage resistance between the two conductors.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry and symmetry.**
By cylindrical symmetry, the potential $V$ depends only on the radial coordinate $\rho$ (using $\rho$ for radial distance to avoid clash with material resistivity, which we denote $\sigma$ as conductivity here). Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates for a function of $\rho$ alone is:
$$\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{d}{d\rho}\left(\rho \dfrac{dV}{d\rho}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once.**
$$\rho \dfrac{dV}{d\rho} = C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{dV}{d\rho} = \dfrac{C_1}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate a second time.**
$$V(\rho) = C_1 \ln \rho + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- At $\rho = a$: $V = V_a$
- At $\rho = b$: $V = V_b$

This gives:
$$V_a = C_1 \ln a + C_2$$
$$V_b = C_1 \ln b + C_2$$

Subtracting:
$$V_a - V_b = C_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\ln(a/b)} = -\dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the electric field.**
$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\dfrac{dV}{d\rho}\hat{a}_\rho = -\dfrac{C_1}{\rho}\hat{a}_\rho = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)}\hat{a}_\rho$$

**Step 6 — Compute the current density.**
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \dfrac{\sigma (V_a - V_b)}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)}\hat{a}_\rho$$

**Step 7 — Compute the total leakage current by integrating over a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ and length $L$.**
$$I = \oint_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{2\pi} \dfrac{\sigma (V_a - V_b)}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)} \cdot \rho \, d\phi \, dz$$
$$I = \dfrac{\sigma (V_a - V_b)}{\ln(b/a)} \cdot 2\pi L$$

**Step 8 — Compute the leakage resistance.**
$$R = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{I} = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\sigma (V_a - V_b) \cdot 2\pi L / \ln(b/a)}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi \sigma L}}$$

This is the **leakage resistance per unit length** of a coaxial cable. The total current is constant for any Gaussian surface chosen between $a$ and $b$ (a direct consequence of $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Resistance of a Parallel-Plate Structure with Leaky Dielectric

**Objective:** Two parallel plates of area $A$ are separated by a distance $d$. The material between them has conductivity $\sigma$. Find the resistance between the plates.

**Step 1 — Symmetry argument.**
The electric field is uniform between the plates, directed perpendicular to them (say, in the $\hat{x}$ direction).

**Step 2 — The potential varies linearly.**
If the left plate is at potential $V_1$ and the right plate is at $V_2$:
$$\vec{E} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{d}\hat{a}_x$$

**Step 3 — Current density.**
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \dfrac{\sigma(V_1 - V_2)}{d}\hat{a}_x$$

**Step 4 — Total current through any cross-section parallel to the plates.**
$$I = \int_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{\sigma(V_1 - V_2)}{d} \cdot A$$

**Step 5 — Resistance.**
$$R = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{I} = \dfrac{d}{\sigma A}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{d}{\sigma A} = \dfrac{\rho \, d}{A}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Resistance Between Two Concentric Spherical Shells

**Objective:** Find the resistance between two concentric conducting spherical shells of radii $r_a$ (inner) and $r_b$ (outer), with the intervening medium having conductivity $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Apply Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates (radial symmetry).**
$$\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r^{2}\dfrac{dV}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — First integration.**
$$r^{2}\dfrac{dV}{dr} = C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{dV}{dr} = \dfrac{C_1}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Second integration.**
$$V(r) = -\dfrac{C_1}{r} + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- $V(r_a) = V_a$
- $V(r_b) = V_b$

$$V_a - V_b = -\dfrac{C_1}{r_a} + \dfrac{C_1}{r_b} = C_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{r_b} - \dfrac{1}{r_a}\right)$$
$$C_1 = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{(1/r_b) - (1/r_a)} = \dfrac{(V_a - V_b) \, r_a r_b}{r_a - r_b}$$

**Step 5 — Electric field.**
$$\vec{E} = -\dfrac{dV}{dr}\hat{a}_r = \dfrac{C_1}{r^{2}}\hat{a}_r = \dfrac{(V_a - V_b) \, r_a r_b}{(r_a - r_b) \, r^{2}}\hat{a}_r$$

**Step 6 — Total current through a Gaussian sphere of radius $r$.**
$$I = \oint \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \sigma E_r \cdot 4\pi r^{2} = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{(V_a - V_b) r_a r_b}{(r_a - r_b) r^{2}} \cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$
$$I = \dfrac{4\pi \sigma (V_a - V_b) r_a r_b}{r_a - r_b}$$

**Step 7 — Resistance.**
$$R = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{I} = \dfrac{r_a - r_b}{4\pi \sigma r_a r_b} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \sigma}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_a} - \dfrac{1}{r_b}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \sigma}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_a} - \dfrac{1}{r_b}\right) = \dfrac{\rho}{4\pi}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_a} - \dfrac{1}{r_b}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The resistance between the spherical shells is *inversely* proportional to the radii — the closer the shells, the smaller the resistance. This is the same result used in **grounding electrode design** and **lightning protection calculations**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Geometry to Resistance Formula

| Geometry | Coordinate System | Boundary Conditions | Resistance Formula |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Uniform cylinder | Cartesian | $V(0) = V_1, V(l) = V_2$ | $R = l/(\sigma A)$ |
| Coaxial cable | Cylindrical ($\rho,\phi,z$) | $V(a) = V_1, V(b) = V_2$ | $R = \ln(b/a)/(2\pi \sigma L)$ |
| Parallel plates | Cartesian | $V(0) = V_1, V(d) = V_2$ | $R = d/(\sigma A)$ |
| Spherical shells | Spherical ($r,\theta,\phi$) | $V(r_a) = V_1, V(r_b) = V_2$ | $R = (1/r_a - 1/r_b)/(4\pi \sigma)$ |

All these derivations fundamentally come from solving **Laplace's equation** in the appropriate coordinate system and then evaluating the ratio $V/I$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Field Quantities to Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Voltage V across conductor"] --> B["Electric Field E inside conductor"]
    B --> C["Current Density J = sigma E (Ohm's Law in Point Form)"]
    C --> D["Total Current I = surface integral of J"]
    D --> E["Resistance R = V / I"]
    F["Conductivity sigma (material property)"] --> C
    G["Geometry of conductor (length, area)"] --> B
    G --> D
    H["Continuity Equation div J = 0"] --> B
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Coaxial Cable Resistance Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Input: Cylindrical geometry, inner radius a, outer radius b, length L, conductivity sigma"] --> P2["Apply Laplace Equation in cylindrical coords: 1 by rho times d by d rho of rho dV by d rho equals 0"]
    P2 --> P3["Integrate twice: V of rho equals C1 ln rho plus C2"]
    P3 --> P4["Apply boundary conditions: V at a equals Va, V at b equals Vb"]
    P4 --> P5["Solve constants C1 and C2"]
    P5 --> P6["Compute electric field: E equals minus gradient of V"]
    P6 --> P7["Apply point form of Ohms Law: J equals sigma E"]
    P7 --> P8["Integrate J over cylindrical Gaussian surface: I equals 2 pi L sigma Va minus Vb over ln b over a"]
    P8 --> P9["Output: Resistance R equals ln b over a divided by 2 pi sigma L"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation: Spherical Electrode Grounding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Inner Spherical Shell: radius ra, potential Va"] -->|radial current flow| S2["Conducting medium: conductivity sigma"]
    S2 -->|radial current flow| S3["Outer Spherical Shell: radius rb, potential Vb"]
    S3 --> S4["Return path for current I"]
    S4 --> S1
    S5["Leakage current density J in radial direction"] --> S2
    S6["Electric field E in radial direction"] --> S2
```

### 4.4 Comparative Geometry Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1["Cylindrical Geometry"] --> R1["R = l divided by sigma times A"]
    G2["Coaxial Geometry"] --> R2["R = ln b over a divided by 2 pi sigma L"]
    G3["Spherical Geometry"] --> R3["R = 1 divided by 4 pi sigma times 1 over ra minus 1 over rb"]
    G4["Parallel Plate Geometry"] --> R4["R = d divided by sigma times A"]
    R1 --> M["All derived from Laplace Equation and Ohms Law"]
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    R4 --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All four geometries (cylindrical, coaxial, spherical, parallel-plate) reduce to the same conceptual pipeline: *Geometry $\to$ Laplace's Equation $\to$ Boundary Conditions $\to$ Electric Field $\to$ Current Density $\to$ Total Current $\to$ Resistance*. The only difference lies in the coordinate system used to express Laplace's equation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the point form of Ohm's law and derive the relationship between current density, electric field, and conductivity. Mention its significance in the computation of resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

Ohm's law in point form relates the current density $\vec{J}$ at a point inside a conducting medium to the electric field $\vec{E}$ at that point through the conductivity $\sigma$ of the medium:

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

where $\vec{J}$ is in A/m², $\vec{E}$ is in V/m, and $\sigma$ is in S/m.

**Significance in resistance computation:**
In the field-theoretic approach, resistance is computed as:
$$R = \dfrac{\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint \sigma \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

Ohm's law in point form allows us to compute the current density at every point in the conductor, which is then integrated to find the total current $I$. This is essential for **non-uniform geometries** where the simple formula $R = l/(\sigma A)$ is invalid.

**Power dissipated per unit volume (Joule's Law):**
$$p = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} = \sigma \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2} \quad \text{[W/m}^{3}\text{]}$$

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the continuity equation for steady current. What condition does it impose on the current density in a homogeneous medium?**

**Model Answer:**

The **continuity equation** in differential form is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\dfrac{\partial \rho_{v}}{\partial t}$$

For **steady (DC) currents**, the charge density does not vary with time at any point:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho_{v}}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$$

This means that the **net current flowing out of any closed surface is zero** — current flows in closed loops, and there is no accumulation or depletion of charge at any point.

**Condition in a homogeneous medium:** Applying Ohm's law $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ and noting that $\sigma$ is constant:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma \vec{E}) = \sigma \, \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla^{2} V = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation** for the scalar electric potential $V$ inside the homogeneous conducting medium — the foundation of all field-based resistance computations.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** As per the 2024 scheme, you must answer ONE full question (with sub-parts a and b) out of a choice of two. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the leakage resistance of a coaxial cable of length $L$, inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, with the dielectric having conductivity $\sigma$.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the problem using cylindrical symmetry.**
The potential $V$ depends only on the radial distance $\rho$ from the axis. Laplace's equation reduces to:
$$\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{d}{d\rho}\left(\rho \dfrac{dV}{d\rho}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — First integration:**
$$\rho \dfrac{dV}{d\rho} = C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{dV}{d\rho} = \dfrac{C_1}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Second integration:**
$$V(\rho) = C_1 \ln \rho + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- $V(a) = V_a$: $V_a = C_1 \ln a + C_2$
- $V(b) = V_b$: $V_b = C_1 \ln b + C_2$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_1$:**
$$V_a - V_b = C_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = -\dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 6 — Electric field:**
$$\vec{E} = -\dfrac{dV}{d\rho}\hat{a}_\rho = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)}\hat{a}_\rho$$

**Step 7 — Current density:**
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \dfrac{\sigma(V_a - V_b)}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)}\hat{a}_\rho$$

**Step 8 — Total current by integrating over a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ and length $L$:**
$$I = \oint_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{2\pi} \dfrac{\sigma(V_a - V_b)}{\rho \, \ln(b/a)} \cdot \rho \, d\phi \, dz = \dfrac{2\pi \sigma L (V_a - V_b)}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 9 — Resistance:**
$$R = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{I} = \dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi \sigma L}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi \sigma L}}$$

**(b) Two concentric spherical conducting shells of radii $r_a$ and $r_b$ ($r_b > r_a$) are filled with a medium of conductivity $\sigma$. Derive the resistance between the two shells.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates with radial symmetry:**
$$\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r^{2}\dfrac{dV}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — First integration:**
$$r^{2}\dfrac{dV}{dr} = C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{dV}{dr} = \dfrac{C_1}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Second integration:**
$$V(r) = -\dfrac{C_1}{r} + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Boundary conditions:**
- $V(r_a) = V_a$
- $V(r_b) = V_b$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_1$:**
$$V_a - V_b = C_1\left(\dfrac{1}{r_b} - \dfrac{1}{r_a}\right) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = \dfrac{(V_a - V_b) \, r_a r_b}{r_a - r_b}$$

**Step 6 — Electric field:**
$$\vec{E} = -\dfrac{dV}{dr}\hat{a}_r = \dfrac{(V_a - V_b) \, r_a r_b}{(r_a - r_b) \, r^{2}}\hat{a}_r$$

**Step 7 — Total current through a Gaussian sphere of radius $r$:**
$$I = \oint \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \sigma E_r \cdot 4\pi r^{2} = \dfrac{4\pi \sigma (V_a - V_b) r_a r_b}{r_a - r_b}$$

**Step 8 — Resistance:**
$$R = \dfrac{V_a - V_b}{I} = \dfrac{r_a - r_b}{4\pi \sigma r_a r_b} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \sigma}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_a} - \dfrac{1}{r_b}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \sigma}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_a} - \dfrac{1}{r_b}\right)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Define resistance in the context of electromagnetic field theory. Starting from the general definition, show that for a homogeneous conductor of length $l$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$, the resistance is $R = l/(\sigma A)$.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Step 1 — General definition of resistance in field theory:**
$$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law in point form:** $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$, so:
$$R = \dfrac{\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\oint \sigma \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

**Step 3 — For a uniform cylindrical conductor with constant cross-section, $\vec{E}$ is uniform along the axis. Take the conductor along the z-axis with $V_1 - V_2$ across the ends separated by $l$:**
$$\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = E \cdot l = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{l} \cdot l = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 4 — Current density is uniform over the cross-section:**
$$\oint \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = J \cdot A = \sigma E \cdot A = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{l} \cdot A$$

**Step 5 — Therefore:**
$$R = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{\sigma (V_1 - V_2) A / l} = \dfrac{l}{\sigma A}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{l}{\sigma A}}$$

**(b) With reference to a parallel plate structure having plate area $A$ and plate separation $d$, filled with a leaky dielectric of conductivity $\sigma$, derive the resistance of the structure and show that it equals $R = d/(\sigma A)$.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume a uniform field between the plates.** If the left plate is at potential $V_1$ and the right plate at $V_2$:
$$\vec{E} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{d}\hat{a}_x$$

**Step 2 — Current density by Ohm's law:**
$$\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E} = \dfrac{\sigma(V_1 - V_2)}{d}\hat{a}_x$$

**Step 3 — Total leakage current through a cross-section parallel to the plates:**
$$I = \int_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{\sigma(V_1 - V_2)}{d} \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Resistance:**
$$R = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{I} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{\sigma(V_1 - V_2)A / d} = \dfrac{d}{\sigma A}$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{d}{\sigma A}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The resistance is directly proportional to the plate separation (longer current path) and inversely proportional to the plate area (larger cross-section for current flow) and the conductivity of the leaky dielectric.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\sigma$ (conductivity) and $\rho$ (resistivity):** Students often write $R = l \sigma A$ instead of $R = l/(\sigma A)$ or $R = \rho l / A$. Remember: $\rho = 1/\sigma$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $2\pi L$ factor in coaxial cable derivation:** A common error is to write $R = \ln(b/a)/(\sigma L)$ — the missing $2\pi$ factor costs 2 full marks.
>
> 3. **Not justifying the symmetry:** In derivations involving coaxial or spherical geometries, you **must** explicitly state that the potential depends only on the radial coordinate and that the Laplacian reduces to its 1-D form. Examiners specifically look for this justification.
>
> 4. **Sign error in the potential difference:** Always verify whether $V_a - V_b$ or $V_b - V_a$ is used, and ensure the final resistance is **positive**.
>
> 5. **Writing the boundary conditions incorrectly:** For the spherical shell problem, the boundary conditions are at $r = r_a$ and $r = r_b$, **not** at $r = 0$ and $r = \infty$. Many students confuse these.
>
> 6. **Skipping the verification step:** Always state at the end that the total current $I$ is the same for any Gaussian surface chosen (a consequence of $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$) — this demonstrates deep understanding and earns bonus marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Resistance is a *field-derived* parameter, given by the ratio of voltage (line integral of $\vec{E}$) to current (surface integral of $\vec{J}$).
- **Point-form Ohm's Law:** $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ — the central equation linking $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{J}$ in a conductor.
- **Steady-state condition:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$, which in a homogeneous medium reduces to **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^{2} V = 0$.
- **Joule's Law (point form):** $p = \sigma \vert\vec{E}\vert^{2}$ = power dissipated per unit volume.
- **Cylindrical conductor:** $R = l/(\sigma A)$
- **Coaxial cable:** $R = \ln(b/a) / (2\pi \sigma L)$
- **Spherical shells:** $R = (1/r_a - 1/r_b) / (4\pi \sigma)$
- **Parallel plates:** $R = d/(\sigma A)$
- **General strategy for any resistance problem:** (1) Identify geometry & coordinate system, (2) Write Laplace's equation, (3) Solve with boundary conditions, (4) Compute $\vec{E}$, then $\vec{J}$, then $I$, then $R = V/I$.
- **Units to remember:** $\sigma$ in S/m, $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot m$, $\vec{J}$ in A/m², $\vec{E}$ in V/m, $R$ in $\Omega$.
- **Physical applications:** Grounding electrodes, cable leakage, PCB trace design, biomedical electrode resistance, and energy dissipation in conductors.
- **Key insight:** All resistance formulas can be derived from a single unifying principle — the solution of Laplace's equation in the appropriate coordinate system, combined with Ohm's law in point form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitance of Parallel Plate Capacitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitance of Parallel Plate Capacitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Plate Capacitor (PPC):** A two-terminal passive energy-storage element consisting of two identical, parallel, plane conducting plates of equal area $A$, separated by a uniform distance $d$, with a dielectric medium of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ filling the space between them. The capacitance $C$ is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the charge $Q$ stored on one plate to the resulting potential difference $V$ established between the plates:
> $$C = \frac{Q}{V}$$

The idealised configuration is shown schematically as **Plate A (+Q)** and **Plate B (−Q)**, where the surface charge density is $\rho_s = Q/A$ (uniformly distributed over the inner facing surfaces when $d \ll \sqrt{A}$ so that fringing is neglected).

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}}$ |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) | Dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Absolute permittivity | $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (F/m) |
| $A$ | Plate area | m² |
| $d$ | Plate separation | m |
| $C$ | Capacitance | Farads (F) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Water-Tank Analogy:** Imagine a cylindrical water tank connected to a pump. The **plates** are like the tank's two parallel walls. The **dielectric** (insulating material) is the rubber seal that prevents electricity (like water) from leaking directly across. The **battery** is the pump that pushes charge onto one plate and pulls it off the other. Just as a wider tank holds more water at the same pressure, a **larger plate area $A$** holds more charge at the same voltage. And just as a *shorter* tank needs *less pressure* to fill it (because the water column is shorter), a **smaller separation $d$** means *less voltage* is required to store the same charge — hence **higher capacitance**. The dielectric itself acts like a sponge that "absorbs" some of the electric stress, multiplying the capacitance by $\varepsilon_r$.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

The parallel plate geometry is the **canonical benchmark** against which all real-world capacitors (MIM, MOS, ceramic disc, PCB-trace, supercapacitors) are analysed. Understanding its physics is a prerequisite for studying **transmission line capacitance**, **microstrip patches**, **VLSI interconnects**, and **high-voltage energy-storage banks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle (Why It Works)

The working principle of a parallel plate capacitor rests on three physical pillars:

- **Charge Confinement:** When a DC source is connected, free electrons migrate from Plate B to the positive terminal of the source, leaving Plate B with net positive charge $+Q$. Simultaneously, electrons accumulate on Plate A, giving it a net charge $-Q$. The dielectric in between physically prevents these charges from neutralising each other.

- **Uniform Electric Field Generation:** The separation $d$ is taken to be much smaller than the plate dimensions ($\sqrt{A}$) so that the electric field lines between the plates are **straight, parallel, and uniformly distributed** — identical to the field of an ideal infinite charged sheet pair.

- **Energy Storage in the Dielectric:** The electric field established in the dielectric stores electrostatic energy. The denser the field (higher $\rho_s$) and the larger the volume ($A \cdot d$), the more energy is stored.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Apply** a DC voltage $V$ across the plates.
2. **Transfer** of charge $Q$ occurs until the induced potential difference equals $V$.
3. **Compute** the surface charge density $\rho_s = Q/A$.
4. **Evaluate** the electric field $E$ between the plates using Gauss's Law.
5. **Integrate** $E$ over the separation $d$ to obtain $V$.
6. **Define** $C = Q/V$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete high-yield reference** for solving any KTU Board question on this topic. Memorise every entry.

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Definition of capacitance | F (Farad) |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ | Parallel plate capacitance (ideal) | F |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | With dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ | F |
| 4 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Surface charge density | C/m² |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Uniform field between plates | V/m |
| 6 | $D = \varepsilon E = \rho_s$ | Electric flux density | C/m² |
| 7 | $V = E d$ | Potential difference across plates | V |
| 8 | $W_E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C} = \dfrac{1}{2} Q V$ | Energy stored | J |
| 9 | $w_E = \dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2$ | Energy density in dielectric | J/m³ |
| 10 | $C' = \dfrac{C}{A}$ | Capacitance per unit area (VLSI metric) | F/m² |
| 11 | $C_{\text{eff,parallel}} = \sum_i C_i$ | Parallel combination | F |
| 12 | $\dfrac{1}{C_{\text{eff,series}}} = \sum_i \dfrac{1}{C_i}$ | Series combination | F⁻¹ |

> **Caution on notation:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ in any KTU answer table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or absolute-value phrasing instead to keep Markdown tables valid.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Decoupling & Bypass Capacitors** on PCBs (place near IC power pins).
- **Sample-and-Hold Circuits** in ADC front-ends.
- **High-Voltage Pulsed Power** in laser drivers and defibrillators.
- **RF/Microwave Tuning** in patch antennas and filters.
- **Energy Storage in SMPS** and DC-link capacitors of VFDs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of an Ideal Parallel Plate Capacitor with Free Space

### Given
- Two parallel plates, each of area $A$, separated by a vacuum gap of thickness $d$.
- A total charge of magnitude $Q$ on the inner surface of each plate.
- Fringing effects are neglected ($d \ll \sqrt{A}$).

### Step 1 — Surface Charge Density
The charge is uniformly spread over the inner face of each plate, so the surface charge density is:

$$
\rho_s = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

### Step 2 — Electric Field Using Gauss's Law
Construct a *Gaussian pillbox* that encloses a portion of the positively charged plate. The flux through the top face (inside the capacitor) is $\rho_s \cdot A_{\text{pillbox}}$, the bottom face flux is zero, and the side flux is zero (field is perpendicular to the plate). Gauss's law gives:

$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{\text{enclosed}}
$$

$$
\rho_s \cdot A_{\text{pillbox}} = D \cdot A_{\text{pillbox}}
$$

Therefore $\vec{D} = \rho_s \, \hat{n}$. Substituting $D = \varepsilon_0 E$ for free space:

$$
E = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}
$$

### Step 3 — Potential Difference Between the Plates
The potential difference is the line integral of $\vec{E}$ from Plate B to Plate A. Since $\vec{E}$ is uniform and parallel to $d\hat{z}$:

$$
V = -\int_{\text{Plate B}}^{\text{Plate A}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = E \cdot d
$$

$$
V = \frac{Q \, d}{\varepsilon_0 A}
$$

### Step 4 — Capacitance by Definition
By the defining relation $C = Q/V$:

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\left( \dfrac{Q \, d}{\varepsilon_0 A} \right)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}
$$

### Result

$$
\boxed{\,C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental PPC formula** for a vacuum dielectric.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PPC with a Dielectric Slab of Permittivity $\varepsilon$

When the gap is completely filled with a homogeneous linear dielectric of absolute permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, the only change is in Gauss's law step:

$$
D = \rho_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon A}
$$

The potential difference becomes:

$$
V = E d = \frac{Q d}{\varepsilon A}
$$

Hence the capacitance is:

$$
\boxed{\,C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}\,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** the dielectric **reduces the internal field** by a factor $\varepsilon_r$, which in turn **reduces the voltage** required to sustain the same charge, **increasing the capacitance** by the same factor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Dielectric Parallel Plate Capacitor

A common KTU variation: two dielectric slabs of thicknesses $d_1$ and $d_2$ (with $d_1 + d_2 = d$) and permittivities $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ are sandwiched between the plates.

### Step 1 — Continuity of $D$
By the boundary condition, the normal component of $\vec{D}$ is continuous across the dielectric interface (no free surface charge at the interface). Thus:

$$
D_1 = D_2 = D = \rho_s
$$

### Step 2 — Fields in Each Region
$$
E_1 = \frac{D}{\varepsilon_1} = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_1}, \quad E_2 = \frac{D}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Voltage
The two regions act like two capacitors **in series** (same $D$, different $E$):

$$
V = E_1 d_1 + E_2 d_2 = \rho_s \left( \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent Capacitance
$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\rho_s A}{\rho_s \left( \dfrac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2} \right)} = \frac{A}{\dfrac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2}}
$$

Equivalent series form:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{1}{C} = \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Edge Cases & Limiting Behaviour

| Configuration | $C$ Expression | Limit Behaviour |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Vacuum gap | $\varepsilon_0 A / d$ | Baseline |
| Single dielectric | $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | $C \uparrow$ by factor $\varepsilon_r$ |
| Two dielectrics (series) | $A / (d_1/\varepsilon_1 + d_2/\varepsilon_2)$ | Dominated by **thicker / lower-$\varepsilon$** slab |
| Two dielectrics (side-by-side, parallel field) | $\varepsilon_1 A_1/d + \varepsilon_2 A_2/d$ | **Sum** of two PPCs |
| Partially inserted slab ($x$) | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[ (1-x)\varepsilon_{r1} + x\varepsilon_{r2} \right] \cdot k$ | Linear interpolation |
| $d \to 0$ | $C \to \infty$ | Theoretical breakdown limit |
| Fringing included (approx.) | $C \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left(1 + \dfrac{d}{\pi A^{1/2}}\right)$ | Edge-effect correction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parallel Plate Capacitor

The following Mermaid flowchart traces the **complete charge-flow and energy-storage pathway** in a parallel plate capacitor, including the role of the dielectric and the boundary conditions at the conductor-dielectric interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Voltage Source V] -->|Current flows briefly| B[Plate A negative charge -Q]
    A -->|Withdraws electrons| C[Plate B positive charge +Q]
    B -->|Surface charge density rho_s = Q/A| D[Inner Face of Plate A]
    C -->|Surface charge density rho_s = Q/A| E[Inner Face of Plate B]
    D -->|Establishes uniform E field| F[Dielectric Medium permittivity epsilon]
    E -->|Establishes uniform E field| F
    F -->|Boundary condition D_n continuous| G[Energy Storage Region A times d]
    G -->|Energy density w_E = 0.5 epsilon E squared| H[Stored Electrostatic Energy W]
    H -->|W = 0.5 C V squared| I[Capacitance C = epsilon A over d]
    I -->|Delivers charge to load| J[External Circuit on Discharge]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    style C fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    style I fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,color:#000
    style H fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#831843,color:#000
```

### Diagram Interpretation Notes for Students

- **Top branch (A → B, C):** The source forces charge separation; this is the **charging transient**.
- **Middle band (D, E → F):** The two opposing surface charge layers jointly produce a **uniform field** in the dielectric — this is the defining physics of the PPC.
- **Energy node (G → H):** Energy is *not* stored on the plates but in the **field within the dielectric**, a fact crucial for understanding EM waveguides and transmission lines.
- **Final node (I):** The single equation $C = \varepsilon A / d$ encapsulates the entire geometry-dependence.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Multi-Dielectric PPC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plate A -Q] -->|Distance d1| K1[Dielectric 1 eps1]
    K1 -->|Interface: D continuous| K2[Dielectric 2 eps2]
    K2 -->|Distance d2| P2[Plate B +Q]
    P1 -->|rho_s| M[rho_s = Q/A uniform]
    P2 --> M
    M -->|E1 = rho_s over eps1| K1
    M -->|E2 = rho_s over eps2| K2
    K1 -->|V1 = E1 d1| SUM[Total Voltage V = V1 + V2]
    K2 -->|V2 = E2 d2| SUM
    SUM -->|C = Q over V| RES[Equivalent Series Capacitance]
    style P1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    style K1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    style K2 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#713f12,color:#000
    style RES fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two-dielectric case is a **very high-yield KTU problem**. The trick to remember is that dielectrics stacked **along the field direction** behave like capacitors in **series**, while dielectrics placed **side-by-side** (splitting the area) behave like capacitors in **parallel**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define capacitance. Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with a dielectric medium.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Capacitance is the ability of a system to store electric charge per unit potential difference. Mathematically, $C = Q/V$, measured in Farads (F).

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$, separation $d$, and dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$:

- Surface charge density: $\rho_s = Q/A$
- Electric field between plates: $E = \rho_s / \varepsilon = Q / (\varepsilon A)$
- Potential difference: $V = E \cdot d = Q d / (\varepsilon A)$
- Hence: $C = Q/V = \varepsilon A / d$

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A parallel plate capacitor has plate area $200 \text{ cm}^2$ and plate separation $2 \text{ mm}$. A dielectric of $\varepsilon_r = 4$ fills the gap. Find the capacitance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$, $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$.

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4)(2 \times 10^{-2})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 8 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.0832 \times 10^{-13}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 3.5416 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F} \approx 354.16 \text{ pF}}
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the dielectric factor** $\varepsilon_r$ when the problem states "with dielectric" is the **#1 mark-loss reason** in this topic. Always check the wording.
> - **Unit conversion errors** between cm², mm and SI units (m², m) cost a full mark band on most KTU papers. Convert *first*, compute *later*.
> - **Mixing series and parallel dielectric combinations** is the second-most common error. Remember: *along the field* = series; *perpendicular to the field* = parallel.
> - **Omitting the sign of surface charge density** in derivations loses a presentation mark.
> - **Assuming uniform field** without stating the assumption $d \ll \sqrt{A}$ leads to a 1-mark deduction.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor partially filled with a dielectric slab of thickness $t$ (where $t < d$) and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, placed adjacent to one of the plates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel plate capacitor of plate area $A = 400 \text{ cm}^2$ and separation $d = 5 \text{ mm}$ has a dielectric slab of $\varepsilon_r = 5$ and thickness $t = 2 \text{ mm}$ placed on one plate. The remaining gap is air. Find: (i) the capacitance, (ii) the electric field in each region when the capacitor is charged to $V = 200 \text{ V}$, and (iii) the energy stored. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Setup (1 mark):** Consider two regions along the field direction:
- Region 1 (dielectric): thickness $t$, permittivity $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.
- Region 2 (air): thickness $(d - t)$, permittivity $\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_0$.

**Continuity of $D$ (2 marks):** The flux density $D = \rho_s$ is the same in both regions.

**Fields and Voltages (3 marks):**

$$
E_1 = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}, \quad E_2 = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

$$
V_1 = E_1 t = \frac{\rho_s \, t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}, \quad V_2 = E_2 (d - t) = \frac{\rho_s (d - t)}{\varepsilon_0}
$$

$$
V = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0}\left[ \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t) \right]
$$

**Capacitance (1 mark):**

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\rho_s A}{\dfrac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0}\left[ \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t) \right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t)}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $A = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$, $d = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 5$, $t = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $V = 200 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Capacitance (3 marks):**

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t)} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4 \times 10^{-2})}{\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{5} + (5 \times 10^{-3} - 2 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

Denominator: $0.4 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 10^{-3} = 3.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$$
C = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-13}}{3.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0416 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 104.16 \text{ pF}}
$$

**(ii) Fields (2 marks):** Since $D = \rho_s = C V / A$:

$$
\rho_s = \frac{(1.0416 \times 10^{-10})(200)}{4 \times 10^{-2}} = 5.208 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/m}^2
$$

$$
E_1 = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{5.208 \times 10^{-7}}{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5)} = 1.176 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}
$$

$$
E_2 = \frac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{5.208 \times 10^{-7}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = 5.882 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}
$$

**(iii) Energy (2 marks):**

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} (1.0416 \times 10^{-10}) (200)^2 = 2.083 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{W \approx 2.08 \ \mu\text{J}}
$$

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor that has two dielectrics of permittivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ with thicknesses $d_1$ and $d_2$ placed one above the other between the plates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel plate capacitor has $A = 1000 \text{ cm}^2$, total separation $d = 6 \text{ mm}$ split equally into two layers: the top layer ($\varepsilon_{r1} = 2$, $d_1 = 3 \text{ mm}$) and the bottom layer ($\varepsilon_{r2} = 6$, $d_2 = 3 \text{ mm}$). Find (i) the capacitance, (ii) the voltage across each layer, and (iii) the energy stored when the capacitor is connected to a $V = 100 \text{ V}$ source. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Already derived in Section 3.3 above. Key results:

$$
D_1 = D_2 = \rho_s
$$

$$
V = \rho_s\left(\frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{C} = \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $A = 1000 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.1 \text{ m}^2$, $d_1 = d_2 = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_{r1} = 2$, $\varepsilon_{r2} = 6$, $V = 100 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Capacitance (3 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_1 = 2 \varepsilon_0 = 1.7708 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = 6 \varepsilon_0 = 5.3124 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{C} = \frac{d_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \frac{d_2}{\varepsilon_2 A} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7708 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.1} + \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{5.3124 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{C} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7708 \times 10^{-12}} + \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{5.3124 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.6941 \times 10^{9} + 5.6470 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{C} = 2.2588 \times 10^{9} \Rightarrow C = 4.427 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F} \approx 442.7 \text{ pF}
$$

**(ii) Voltage across each layer (2 marks):** From $V_i = E_i d_i$ and $E_i = \rho_s / \varepsilon_i$, the charge $Q = C V = (4.427 \times 10^{-10})(100) = 4.427 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$.

$$
\rho_s = Q/A = 4.427 \times 10^{-8} / 0.1 = 4.427 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/m}^2
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{\rho_s d_1}{\varepsilon_1} = \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7708 \times 10^{-11}} = 75.0 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{\rho_s d_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{5.3124 \times 10^{-11}} = 25.0 \text{ V}
$$

(Sanity check: $V_1 + V_2 = 75 + 25 = 100 \text{ V}$ ✓)

**(iii) Energy stored (2 marks):**

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} (4.427 \times 10^{-10})(100)^2 = 2.214 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} \approx 2.21 \ \mu\text{J}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Plate Capacitor**

- ✅ **Defining formula:** $C = Q / V$ — universally applicable.
- ✅ **Ideal PPC formula (free space):** $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$.
- ✅ **With dielectric:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — multiply by $\varepsilon_r$.
- ✅ **Assumption for uniform field:** $d \ll \sqrt{A}$ (fringing neglected).
- ✅ **Field inside:** $E = V / d = \rho_s / \varepsilon$ — uniform, straight, parallel.
- ✅ **Boundary condition:** $D_n$ (normal component) is continuous across dielectric-dielectric interface (no free charge).
- ✅ **Stacked dielectrics (along field):** behave like capacitors **in series** → $\frac{1}{C} = \sum \frac{d_i}{\varepsilon_i A}$.
- ✅ **Side-by-side dielectrics (perpendicular to field):** behave like capacitors **in parallel** → $C = \sum \frac{\varepsilon_i A_i}{d}$.
- ✅ **Energy stored:** $W = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} Q V = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$.
- ✅ **Energy density:** $w_E = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2$ — energy is in the **field**, not the plates.
- ✅ **Constants to memorise:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $1 \text{ pF} = 10^{-12}$ F.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$; $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** If the slab covers *part* of the area, do **not** treat it as series — use the **parallel-slab** formula.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** "Convert units first, then substitute — never the reverse."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coaxial and Spherical Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Coaxial and Spherical Capacitors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Coaxial Capacitor (Cylindrical Capacitor) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > A **coaxial capacitor** (also called a **cylindrical capacitor**) is a two-conductor electrostatic system consisting of two concentric conducting cylinders of finite length $L$, separated by a dielectric medium of permittivity $\varepsilon$. The inner cylinder carries charge $+Q$ uniformly distributed over its outer surface, while the outer cylinder carries $-Q$ on its inner surface. Charge separation is achieved using an **insulating spacer** at the ends. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** The derivation of the electric field $E$ between the conductors, the potential difference $V$, and the final expression for capacitance $C$ are **high-yield topics** and appear almost every university exam cycle. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **drinking straw inserted inside a slightly wider straw** — the gap between them is filled with a thin insulating material (wax paper or air). If you apply a voltage between the inner and outer straw, the system stores electrical energy in the radial electric field that exists in the thin annular gap. The thinner the gap and the longer the straws, the more charge you can store for a given voltage — that is, the higher the capacitance. This is exactly how a **submarine communication cable** or a **high-voltage power line insulator** works in miniature. > [!TIP] > Think of a coaxial capacitor as a **parallel-plate capacitor that has been rolled up into a tube**. Instead of a uniform field between flat plates, we get a **radial field** between two cylinders. ## 1.2 Spherical Capacitor > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > A **spherical capacitor** is a two-conductor electrostatic system comprising two concentric spherical conducting shells of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$ ($b > a$), separated by a homogeneous dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. The inner sphere carries $+Q$ and the outer sphere carries $-Q$, producing a purely **radial electric field** in the region $a < r < b$. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Picture a **tennis ball placed inside a hollow coconut shell** with a thin layer of oil (the dielectric) filling the gap. Apply a battery across the inner and outer shells, and energy is stored in the spherically symmetric radial field. The closer the coconut shell is to the tennis ball (smaller $b-a$), the more charge it stores per volt. This is the foundational geometry behind **Van de Graaff generator domes**, **high-voltage spherical terminals**, and the **classical model of a hydrogen atom** (electron cloud as outer conductor, nucleus as inner). ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Units - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; F/m}$ - Relative permittivity (dielectric constant): $\varepsilon_r$ (dimensionless, $\varepsilon_r \geq 1$) - Permittivity of medium: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ - Capacitance unit: **Farad (F)**; practical sub-units: $\mu F$, $nF$, $pF$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Coaxial Capacitor — Conceptual Breakdown **Why a radial field?** Because of the cylindrical symmetry (infinite length approximation), the electric field must point **radially outward** from the inner cylinder. By Gauss's law, applying a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ ($a < r < b$) and length $L$: - The flux through the curved surface is $E(r) \cdot 2\pi r L$ - The enclosed charge is $\lambda L$ (where $\lambda = Q/L$ is the linear charge density) - Gauss's law gives $E(r) \cdot 2\pi r L = \dfrac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon}$, hence $E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}$ **Key Engineering Insight:** - $E(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$ — the field is **non-uniform** (unlike the uniform field in a parallel-plate capacitor). - The field is **strongest at the inner cylinder** ($r = a$) and weakest at the outer cylinder ($r = b$). This is why **dielectric breakdown** typically initiates at the inner conductor surface. - Practical coaxial cables use a **stranded inner conductor** and a **foamed dielectric** to reduce the maximum field strength. **Real-World Utility:** - **Coaxial transmission lines** (RG-6, RG-58) in RF communication - **High-voltage bushings** in power transformers - **Capacitive voltage dividers** in HV laboratories - **Energy storage** in pulsed power systems (e.g., Z-pinch experiments) ## 2.2 Spherical Capacitor — Conceptual Breakdown **Why a radial field?** Because of perfect spherical symmetry, the electric field is purely radial: $\vec{E} = E(r)\,\hat{a}_r$. By Gauss's law on a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ ($a < r < b$): - Flux through the surface is $E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2$ - Enclosed charge is $Q$ - $E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon}$, hence $E(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}$ **Key Engineering Insight:** - $E(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ — the field falls off **faster** than in the cylindrical case. - The field is **strongest at $r = a$** (inner sphere surface), so the dielectric near the inner sphere experiences the maximum stress. - When $b \to \infty$, the capacitance reduces to that of an **isolated sphere**: $C = 4\pi \varepsilon a$. **Real-World Utility:** - **Spherical electrostatic voltmeters** - **Van de Graaff accelerators** (high-voltage spherical terminals) - **Lightning rod field analysis** (simplified as concentric spheres) - **Atomic physics** (Bohr model of hydrogen) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Coaxial Capacitor | Spherical Capacitor | |---|-----------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Geometry | Two concentric cylinders (radii $a, b$; length $L$) | Two concentric spheres (radii $a, b$) | | 2 | Charge | $+Q$ on inner, $-Q$ on outer | $+Q$ on inner, $-Q$ on outer | | 3 | Linear/Surface charge density | $\lambda = Q/L$ | $\sigma = Q/(4\pi a^2)$ | | 4 | Gaussian surface | Cylinder, radius $r$, length $L$ | Sphere, radius $r$ | | 5 | Electric field (in dielectric) | $E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r} \; \hat{a}_r$ | $E(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2} \; \hat{a}_r$ | | 6 | Region of validity | $a \leq r \leq b$ | $a \leq r \leq b$ | | 7 | Potential difference $V$ | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | $\dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{a}-\dfrac{1}{b}\right)$ | | 8 | **Capacitance $C$** | $\boxed{\dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon L}{\ln(b/a)}}$ | $\boxed{\dfrac{4\pi \varepsilon\, ab}{b-a}}$ | | 9 | Limiting case ($b \to \infty$) | $C \to 0$ (open-ended) | $C \to 4\pi \varepsilon a$ (isolated sphere) | | 10 | Capacitance per unit length | $\dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Not applicable (3-D geometry) | | 11 | Energy stored | $\dfrac{Q^2}{2C} = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | $\dfrac{Q^2}{2C} = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | | 12 | Energy density | $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the **form of the field** ($1/r$ vs $1/r^2$) and the **logarithm vs rational function** in the capacitance. Examiners frequently swap variables or test the limiting case $b \gg a$. > [!WARNING] > In KTU board exams, students frequently lose marks by writing $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ as $\log\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ or by using the **natural logarithm** wrongly. Always use the **natural log ($\ln$)** when SI units ($\varepsilon_0$ in F/m) are used. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance of a Coaxial Capacitor ### Setup Consider two concentric conducting cylinders of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$ (with $b > a$), coaxial with the $z$-axis, of length $L$. The region between them is filled with a linear, homogeneous, isotropic dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$. Charge $+Q$ is placed on the outer surface of the inner cylinder and $-Q$ on the inner surface of the outer cylinder. ### Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law to Find $E$ Choose a cylindrical Gaussian surface $S$ of radius $r$ and length $\ell$ where $a < r < b$. By symmetry, $\vec{E} = E(r)\,\hat{a}_r$. $$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon}$$ The flux through the two flat end caps is **zero** because $\vec{E} \perp d\vec{S}$ there. The flux through the curved surface is: $$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = E(r) \cdot (2\pi r \ell)$$ The enclosed charge is $Q_{\text{enc}} = \lambda \ell$, where $\lambda = Q/L$ is the linear charge density. $$E(r) \cdot 2\pi r \ell = \frac{\lambda \ell}{\varepsilon}$$ Solve for $E(r)$: $$E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}\;\;\;\; \text{for}\;\; a \leq r \leq b$$ > **Logic Note:** The $\ell$ cancels because the field is independent of the length of the Gaussian surface — this is a hallmark of cylindrical symmetry. ### Step 2 — Compute the Potential Difference The potential of the inner conductor (at $r = a$) with respect to the outer conductor (at $r = b$) is: $$V = -\int_b^a \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_a^b E(r)\, dr$$ Substituting the expression for $E(r)$: $$V = \int_a^b \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}\, dr$$ $$V = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\,\Big[\ln r\Big]_a^b$$ $$V = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ > **Logic Note:** The sign convention used here ($V_a - V_b$) ensures $V > 0$ when $a < b$, which is consistent with the inner conductor being at higher potential. ### Step 3 — Compute the Capacitance By definition, $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$. Since $Q = \lambda L$: $$C = \frac{\lambda L}{\dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\,\ln(b/a)}$$ $$\boxed{\;C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon L}{\ln(b/a)}\;}$$ **Capacitance per unit length:** $$C' = \frac{C}{L} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$$ > **Verification (Dimensional Check):** $\varepsilon$ has units of F/m and $L$ has units of m, while $\ln(b/a)$ is dimensionless. So $[C] = $ F/m × m / 1 = F. ✓ ### Step 4 — Limiting Case: $b \gg a$ When $b \gg a$, $\ln(b/a) \to \infty$, hence $C \to 0$. This means an infinitely long coaxial cable behaves like an open circuit at DC, which matches physical intuition (current cannot flow back through the dielectric). ## 3.2 Derivation: Capacitance of a Spherical Capacitor ### Setup Two concentric conducting spherical shells of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$ ($b > a$), separated by a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. Charge $+Q$ is on the outer surface of the inner sphere, $-Q$ on the inner surface of the outer sphere. ### Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law to Find $E$ Choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ where $a < r < b$. By spherical symmetry, $\vec{E} = E(r)\,\hat{a}_r$. $$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon}$$ $$E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon}$$ $$E(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}\;\;\;\; \text{for}\;\; a \leq r \leq b$$ > **Logic Note:** The field outside the outer shell ($r > b$) is zero because the total enclosed charge is zero ($-Q$ on the inner surface of the outer shell cancels the $+Q$). ### Step 2 — Compute the Potential Difference The potential of the inner sphere with respect to the outer sphere: $$V = -\int_b^a \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_a^b E(r)\, dr = \int_a^b \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}\, dr$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\,\left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_a^b = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\,\left(\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{b}\right)$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\,\left(\frac{b-a}{ab}\right)$$ > **Logic Note:** The integrand is a power law, so the antiderivative is straightforward. The result is **symmetric** in $a$ and $b$ in the denominator, as expected from the geometry. ### Step 3 — Compute the Capacitance By definition $C = Q/V$: $$C = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{a}-\dfrac{1}{b}\right)}$$ $$\boxed{\;C = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon\, ab}{b-a}\;}$$ > **Verification (Dimensional Check):** $[\varepsilon] = $ F/m, $[ab] = $ m², $[b-a] = $ m. So $[C] = $ F/m × m²/m = F. ✓ ### Step 4 — Limiting Case: $b \to \infty$ (Isolated Sphere) As $b \to \infty$, $\dfrac{ab}{b-a} = \dfrac{a}{1 - a/b} \to a$. Therefore: $$C_{\text{isolated}} = 4\pi \varepsilon a$$ > **Cross-check with parallel-plate intuition:** This is the **3-D analogue** of the parallel-plate capacitor. Just as $C_{\text{pp}} = \varepsilon A/d$, the spherical result is essentially $\varepsilon \cdot (4\pi a^2)/d$ corrected for the curvature of the field. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Coaxial Capacitor | Spherical Capacitor | |----------|-------------------|---------------------| | Symmetry | Cylindrical (1-D radial) | Spherical (1-D radial) | | Gaussian surface | Cylinder ($2\pi r \ell$) | Sphere ($4\pi r^2$) | | Field dependence | $E \propto 1/r$ | $E \propto 1/r^2$ | | Integration to get $V$ | $\int dr/r = \ln r$ | $\int dr/r^2 = -1/r$ | | $V$ contains | $\ln(b/a)$ | $(1/a - 1/b)$ | | $C$ contains | $\ln(b/a)$ in denominator | $(b-a)$ in denominator | | Number of geometric variables | $a, b, L$ | $a, b$ | | Common exam trick | Substitute $b/a = k$ | Substitute $b - a = d$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Coaxial Capacitor — Architecture & Field Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["INNER CYLINDER
Radius a
Charge +Q"]:::core node2["DIELECTRIC MEDIUM
Permittivity epsilon
Region a to r to b"]:::field node3["OUTER CYLINDER
Radius b
Charge minus Q"]:::core node4["RADIAL E FIELD
E proportional 1 over r
Direction outward from inner"]:::flow node5["LENGTH L along z axis
End insulators separate conductors"]:::geom node6["VOLTAGE SOURCE V
Connected between inner and outer"]:::source node7["CAPACITANCE C
C equals 2 pi epsilon L over ln b divided by a"]:::output node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node4 --> node3 node5 --- node1 node5 --- node3 node6 --> node1 node6 --> node3 node1 --> node7 classDef core fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef field fill:#fde68a,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef flow fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef geom fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef source fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **inner and outer cylinders** (core elements) carry opposite charges. - The **dielectric medium** sits in the annular gap $a < r < b$. - The **radial electric field** points outward from the inner conductor and decreases as $1/r$. - A **voltage source** charges the system; the **capacitance** is the ratio of stored charge to voltage. ## 4.2 Spherical Capacitor — Architecture & Field Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["INNER SPHERE
Radius a
Charge +Q"]:::core nodeB["SPHERICAL DIELECTRIC SHELL
Permittivity epsilon
Region a to r to b"]:::field nodeC["OUTER SPHERICAL SHELL
Radius b
Charge minus Q"]:::core nodeD["RADIAL E FIELD
E proportional Q over 4 pi epsilon r squared
Purely radial direction"]:::flow nodeE["GAUSSIAN SPHERE
Radius r between a and b
Area 4 pi r squared"]:::geom nodeF["VOLTAGE SOURCE V
Connects inner and outer shells"]:::source nodeG["CAPACITANCE C
C equals 4 pi epsilon a b over b minus a"]:::output nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeC nodeE --- nodeB nodeF --> nodeA nodeF --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeG classDef core fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef field fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef flow fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef geom fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef source fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The two **concentric spherical shells** are the energy-storage conductors. - The **spherical dielectric shell** fills the gap $a < r < b$. - The **radial electric field** falls off as $1/r^2$ — much faster than the coaxial case. - A **Gaussian sphere** of radius $r$ is the natural surface to use in the derivation. ## 4.3 Comparative Field-Decay Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeX["RADIUS r increases"]:::axis nodeY["E in coaxial
E proportional 1 over r
Slower decay"]:::cyl nodeZ["E in spherical
E proportional 1 over r squared
Faster decay"]:::sph nodeX --> nodeY nodeX --> nodeZ nodeY --- nodeZ classDef axis fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#475569,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef cyl fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef sph fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Engineering takeaway:** Coaxial geometries retain a higher field over a longer radial distance than spherical ones. This is one reason why **long transmission-line geometries** (coaxial) can store more energy per unit length than compact spherical designs — though spherical designs are mechanically self-contained. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a coaxial capacitor. Derive an expression for the capacitance per unit length of a coaxial cable filled with a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Definition:** A coaxial capacitor consists of two concentric cylindrical conductors of radii $a$ and $b$ ($b > a$), separated by a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. The inner cylinder carries charge $+Q$ and the outer $-Q$. - **(1 mark) Field and Voltage:** By Gauss's law, $E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}$. The potential difference is $V = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\ln(b/a)$. - **(1 mark) Capacitance per unit length:** $C' = \dfrac{C}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\;\text{F/m}$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **A spherical capacitor has inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$. Derive the expression for its capacitance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Field:** By Gauss's law applied to a spherical Gaussian surface, $E(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}$ for $a \leq r \leq b$. - **(1 mark) Potential difference:** $V = \displaystyle\int_a^b E(r)\,dr = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{b}\right)$. - **(1 mark) Capacitance:** $C = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{4\pi \varepsilon ab}{b - a}$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the capacitance of a coaxial capacitor of length $L$ with inner and outer radii $a$ and $b$ filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius 1 mm, an outer conductor of inner radius 4 mm, and length 50 m. The dielectric has $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$. Calculate: (i) the capacitance, (ii) the capacitance per unit length, and (iii) the energy stored when charged to 10 kV. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply Gauss's law. Using a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $\ell$: $$E(r) \cdot 2\pi r \ell = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon L\, r}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute potential difference: $$V = \int_a^b E(r)\,dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon L}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute capacitance: $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon L}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Capacitance per unit length $C' = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)}$. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $a = 1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $b = 4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 50$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$, $V = 10$ kV $= 10^4$ V. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Logarithm:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.3863$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Capacitance:** $$C = \frac{2\pi \times 2.25 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 50}{1.3863}$$ Compute the numerator: $2\pi \times 2.25 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 = 6.260 \times 10^{-9}$ F·m/m = $6.260 \times 10^{-9}$ F. Wait, recompute carefully: $$2\pi \times 2.25 = 14.137$$ $$14.137 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.2517 \times 10^{-10}$$ $$1.2517 \times 10^{-10} \times 50 = 6.258 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$C = \frac{6.258 \times 10^{-9}}{1.3863} = 4.514 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{F} \approx 4.51\;\text{nF}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark) — Capacitance per unit length:** $$C' = \frac{C}{L} = \frac{4.514 \times 10^{-9}}{50} = 9.03 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{F/m} \approx 90.3\;\text{pF/m}$$ **Step 4 (3 marks) — Energy stored:** $$W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.514 \times 10^{-9} \times (10^4)^2$$ $$W = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.514 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{8} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.514 \times 10^{-1}$$ $$W = 0.2257\;\text{J} \approx 226\;\text{mJ}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Unit conversion:** Students frequently write $a = 1$ instead of $a = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m. The capacitance will be wrong by **9 orders of magnitude**. Always convert mm → m. > 2. **Energy formula selection:** Use $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ (since $V$ is given), not $W = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$ (which would require an extra step to find $Q$). > 3. **Logarithm base:** Use **natural log** ($\ln$), not $\log_{10}$. Using $\log_{10}(4) = 0.602$ instead of $1.386$ gives an answer off by ~2.3×. > 4. **Showing intermediate steps:** Even for numerical problems, KTU board examiners award **step marks** for clearly writing the formula, substituting values, and computing — not just the final number. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — Alternative Choice **(a)** Derive an expression for the capacitance of a spherical capacitor with inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$, separated by a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spherical capacitor has inner radius 5 cm and outer radius 10 cm. The dielectric has $\varepsilon_r = 4$. Find: (i) the capacitance, (ii) the charge on the inner sphere when the potential difference is 1000 V, and (iii) the energy stored. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply Gauss's law on a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ ($a < r < b$): $$E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon}\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;
