E(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute potential difference: $$V = \int_a^b \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}\,dr = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_a^b = \frac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon}\left(\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{b}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute capacitance: $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon}{\left(\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{b}\right)} = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon\, ab}{b-a} = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, ab}{b-a}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Limiting case: when $b \to \infty$, $C \to 4\pi \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 a$ (isolated sphere). **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $a = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, $b = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $V = 1000$ V. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Effective permittivity:** $$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 = 4 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{F/m}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Capacitance:** $$C = \frac{4\pi \times 3.5416 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.05 \times 0.10}{0.10 - 0.05}$$ Compute numerator: $4\pi \times 3.5416 \times 10^{-11} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.226 \times 10^{-12}$. $$C = \frac{2.226 \times 10^{-12}}{0.05} = 4.451 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{F} \approx 44.5\;\text{pF}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Charge:** $$Q = CV = 4.451 \times 10^{-11} \times 1000 = 4.451 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{C} \approx 44.5\;\text{nC}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Energy stored:** $$W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.451 \times 10^{-11} \times (10^3)^2$$ $$W = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.451 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^6 = 2.226 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{J} \approx 22.3\;\mu\text{J}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Algebraic simplification error:** Many students write $\dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{b}$ incorrectly as $\dfrac{1}{a-b}$. The correct common denominator is $ab$, giving $\dfrac{b-a}{ab}$. > 2. **Permittivity substitution:** Don't forget $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ (i.e., include $\varepsilon_0$ numerically in the calculation). > 3. **Final answer units:** Always present with SI prefixes: pF, nC, μJ — KTU examiners explicitly check for this. > 4. **Missing limiting-case discussion:** For 1 extra mark, mention what happens as $b \to \infty$ — this is a frequent "bonus" question in KTU. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:** - **Coaxial Capacitor Field:** $E(r) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}$ (inverse-distance law) - **Spherical Capacitor Field:** $E(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi \varepsilon r^2}$ (inverse-square law) - **Coaxial Capacitance:** $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon L}{\ln(b/a)}$ — contains a **logarithm** - **Spherical Capacitance:** $C = \dfrac{4\pi \varepsilon ab}{b-a}$ — contains a **rational function** - **Limiting case ($b \to \infty$):** Coaxial → 0; Spherical → $4\pi \varepsilon a$ (isolated sphere) - **Units:** Always use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; final $C$ in Farads (or pF/nF/μF) - **Logarithm:** Always use $\ln$ (natural log) in SI-based derivations. - **Permittivity:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$, where $\varepsilon_r$ is dimensionless. - **Energy:** $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2}QV$ — three equivalent forms. - **Symmetry selection:** Cylindrical symmetry → cylindrical Gaussian surface; spherical symmetry → spherical Gaussian surface. - **Dielectric strength:** Maximum field occurs at the **inner conductor** ($r = a$) — design accordingly. - **Fringing effects:** Both derivations assume **infinite length** (coaxial) or **exact concentricity** (spherical) — fringing is neglected. - **KTU Board Pattern:** Part A = definition + simple formula (3 marks); Part B = full derivation + numerical (7 + 7 = 14 marks). Always carry units through every step. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "Log for Coax, Reciprocal for Sphere":** > The coaxial capacitor gives a **L**ogarithmic potential because $E \propto 1/r$ integrates to $\ln r$. The spherical capacitor gives a **R**eciprocal potential because $E \propto 1/r^2$ integrates to $-1/r$. Think **L**og → **L**ong cylinder, **R**eciprocal → **R**ound sphere.

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental **conservation law** in electromagnetic theory that mathematically expresses the principle of **conservation of electric charge**. It states that the net current flowing out of any closed surface is equal to the rate of decrease of the charge enclosed within the volume bounded by that surface.

In **point (differential) form**, the continuity equation is expressed as:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

Where:
- $\vec{J}$ is the **current density** (in $\text{A/m}^2$)
- $\rho$ is the **volume charge density** (in $\text{C/m}^3$)
- $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$ is the divergence of current density
- $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ is the time rate of change of charge density

In **integral form**, it is:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Continuity Equation is a direct mathematical consequence of the **immutability of electric charge** — charge can neither be created nor destroyed, only moved from one location to another. This is one of the most rigorously tested laws in physics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tank with a flexible membrane** floating on top:

- **Scenario A (Steady State):** Water flows in through Pipe A at exactly the same rate it flows out through Pipe B. The amount of water in the tank stays constant — analogous to $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$, giving $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$.

- **Scenario B (Filling Up):** More water enters than leaves. The water level rises. The rate of water accumulation inside equals the *net inward* flow. Similarly, if charge is accumulating in a region, the net inflow of current (negative divergence) must equal the rate of increase of charge density.

- **Scenario C (Draining):** Water flows out faster than it flows in. The level drops. This corresponds to positive divergence of current (net outflow) and a decreasing charge density.

**The Continuity Equation is essentially a bookkeeping identity** — it ensures that whenever charge "leaves" a region through current flow, the charge inside that region decreases by exactly the same amount. No charge is ever lost or spontaneously created.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** This equation was formulated within the broader framework of Maxwell's equations (1865) and is sometimes called the **Charge Conservation Equation** or the **Local Conservation Law for Charge**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Units

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Vector/Scalar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\vec{J}$ | Current density | $\text{A/m}^2$ | Vector |
| $\rho$ | Volume charge density | $\text{C/m}^3$ | Scalar |
| $I$ | Electric current | $\text{A}$ | Scalar |
| $Q$ | Electric charge | $\text{C}$ | Scalar |
| $v$ | Drift velocity of charges | $\text{m/s}$ | Vector |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 'Why' Behind the Equation

The continuity equation arises from a single, unbreakable physical rule: **charge is conserved**. To translate this physical rule into a mathematical statement, we follow a logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Quantify the "leaving":** The net current $I$ leaving a closed surface $S$ is the surface integral of current density:
  $$I_{out} = \oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$
  The dot product ensures we only count the component of $\vec{J}$ perpendicular to the surface — the actual flow *across* the boundary.

- **Step 2 — Quantify the "shrinking":** The total charge $Q$ inside the volume $V$ bounded by $S$ is:
  $$Q = \int_V \rho \, dV$$
  If charge is leaving, this integral must decrease with time. The rate of decrease is:
  $$-\frac{dQ}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

- **Step 3 — Equate them (Conservation Law):** Whatever leaves equals what is lost:
  $$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

- **Step 4 — Convert to point form:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the LHS and the **Leibniz rule** (volume fixed) to the RHS to obtain the local form valid at every point in space.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **When to Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ | Integral (scalar) | Net current out = rate of charge loss | Closed surface problems |
| $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV$ | Integral (volume) | Conservation in integral form | Volume-based derivations |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | Point/Differential | Local conservation at a point | Point-wise field analysis |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ | Steady-state (DC) | No sources/sinks of current | Static current problems |
| $\vec{J} = \rho \vec{v}$ | Constitutive | Current density = charge density × velocity | Convection current problems |
| $I = \displaystyle\int_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Definition | Current = flux of $\vec{J}$ | Relating I and J |
| $J = \rho v = \sigma E$ (Ohm's law form) | Conducting medium | Conduction current density | Material media problems |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decision Rule:** If the problem says "time-varying" or "capacitor charging/discharging," use the full form $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$. If it says "steady current" or "DC," you can set $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and reduce to $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Applications

1. **Transmission Line Design:** Engineers use the steady-state continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ to ensure that the current entering a node equals the current leaving it (**Kirchhoff's Current Law** is the circuit-level analog of the continuity equation).

2. **Semiconductor Device Physics:** In PN-junction diodes, MOSFETs, and BJTs, the continuity equation governs the flow of electrons and holes, enabling the design of all modern integrated circuits.

3. **Capacitor Charging Analysis:** When current flows into a capacitor, the continuity equation, combined with Maxwell's displacement current, correctly predicts the magnetic field between capacitor plates — a problem that confused physicists before Maxwell's correction.

4. **Antenna Theory and Electromagnetic Wave Propagation:** The continuity equation underpins the derivation of the **wave equation** and the existence of **time-varying electromagnetic fields**.

5. **Plasma Physics and Fusion Reactors:** Charged particle continuity is central to modeling plasma confinement in tokamaks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles of Charge Conservation (Integral → Point Form)

This is the **primary derivation** most often expected in KTU examinations.

**Given:**
- Total charge $Q$ enclosed in volume $V$ bounded by surface $S$.
- Current density $\vec{J}$ through surface $S$.

**Step 1:** Write the total enclosed charge as a volume integral of charge density:

$$Q = \int_V \rho \, dV$$

*Logic:* Charge is a scalar, distributed continuously through space with density $\rho$. To find the total amount in a region, we integrate the density over the volume.

**Step 2:** Write the net current leaving the closed surface $S$:

$$I_{net} = \oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

*Logic:* Current density $\vec{J}$ is a flux — only its component perpendicular to the surface contributes to actual flow through that surface. The dot product projects $\vec{J}$ onto the outward normal $d\vec{S}$.

**Step 3:** Apply the principle of conservation of charge. The net current leaving per unit time equals the rate of decrease of enclosed charge:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$$

*Logic:* The negative sign is crucial. If $\frac{dQ}{dt}$ is negative (charge is leaving), then $-\frac{dQ}{dt}$ is positive (net outward current is positive). This sign convention enforces physical consistency.

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $Q$:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

*Logic:* We are now expressing the conservation law entirely in terms of the field quantities $\vec{J}$ and $\rho$.

**Step 5:** Since the volume $V$ is fixed (control volume) and time is the only variable, swap the time derivative and volume integral:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV$$

*Logic:* $\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV = \int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV$ because the limits of integration are time-independent (Leibniz rule for fixed limits).

**Step 6:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's Theorem) to the LHS:

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV$$

*Logic:* The Divergence Theorem states $\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{F}) \, dV$. We apply it with $\vec{F} = \vec{J}$.

**Step 7:** Combine the integrals on both sides:

$$\int_V \left( \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \right) dV = 0$$

*Logic:* Both integrands are now inside a single volume integral.

**Step 8:** Since the volume $V$ is **arbitrary** (any region of space), the integrand must vanish identically at every point:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0}$$

*Logic:* This is the **local form** of charge conservation. The argument used is: if $\int_V f \, dV = 0$ for *every* arbitrary volume $V$, then $f = 0$ at every point. (Proof: choose an infinitesimal volume around an arbitrary point.) This step is the heart of converting a global (integral) law to a local (differential) law.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Maxwell's Equations (Alternative Method)

This is the **secondary derivation** linking the continuity equation to Maxwell's framework.

**Step 1:** Start with the **Ampère-Maxwell Law** (one of Maxwell's four equations):

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Take the divergence of both sides:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

*Logic:* The divergence of a curl is always zero (fundamental vector identity). On the RHS, we have used the fact that $\nabla \cdot$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}$ commute for fields with sufficient smoothness.

**Step 3:** Simplify the LHS using the vector identity:

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

*Logic:* $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) \equiv 0$ for any well-behaved vector field $\vec{H}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute **Gauss's Law for Electricity** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$):

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

*Logic:* Gauss's Law tells us that the divergence of the electric flux density equals the local charge density. We substitute $\nabla \cdot \vec{D}$ with $\rho$.

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the standard form:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0}$$

*Logic:* This shows that the continuity equation is **not independent** of Maxwell's equations — it is mathematically embedded within them. The converse is also true: if we assume charge conservation and Gauss's law, we can *derive* the displacement current term in Ampère's law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Insight:** The continuity equation, together with Gauss's law, *demands* the existence of Maxwell's displacement current. Without $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ in Ampère's law, charge conservation would be violated in time-varying situations (e.g., a charging capacitor).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — From Current Density Definition (Convection Current)

This derivation connects the microscopic motion of charges to the macroscopic continuity equation.

**Step 1:** Express current density in terms of charge density and drift velocity:

$$\vec{J} = \rho \vec{v}$$

*Logic:* If charge density $\rho$ moves with velocity $\vec{v}$, the current density is their product. This describes **convection current** (e.g., electron beam in a vacuum tube).

**Step 2:** Take the divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$$

**Step 3:** Use the **product rule** for divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$$

*Logic:* $\nabla \cdot (f\vec{A}) = f(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) + \vec{A} \cdot (\nabla f)$ is the standard vector identity.

**Step 4:** Relate to the material derivative of charge density. From the continuity of a fluid in fluid mechanics:

$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho)$$

**Step 5:** Combine and rearrange to obtain:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{J} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) + \vec{v} \cdot (\nabla \rho) = 0$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the **total (convective) derivative**:

$$\boxed{\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{v}) = 0}$$

*Logic:* This is the **Lagrangian form** of the continuity equation, valid along the flow path of charges.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases

| **Case** | **Condition** | **Reduced Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Example** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Steady-state (DC) | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ | Solenoidal current; no accumulation | Current in a resistor |
| Static electrostatics | $\vec{J} = 0$ | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | Charges are at rest | Charged conductor at equilibrium |
| Time-harmonic | $\rho = \rho_0 e^{j\omega t}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -j\omega \rho_0 e^{j\omega t}$ | Phasor form | AC circuits |
| Incompressible flow analogy | $\rho = $ constant | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | Like incompressible fluid | Vacuum tube electron flow |
| Point source (sink) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -I \delta^3(\vec{r})$ | Singular | Mathematical point charge | Lightning strike model |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** The current density in a region is given by $\vec{J} = 10x \hat{a}_x - 5y^2 \hat{a}_y + 3z \hat{a}_z$ $\text{A/m}^2$. Determine the rate of change of charge density $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ at the point $(1, 2, 3)$ $\text{m}$.

**Step 1:** Write the continuity equation solved for $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = \frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial J_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial J_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate each component:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(10x) = 10$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-5y^2) = -10y$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z}(3z) = 3$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 10 - 10y + 3$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 13 - 10y$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at $(1, 2, 3)$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J}\big|_{(1,2,3)} = 13 - 10(2) = 13 - 20 = -7 \text{ A/m}^3$$

**Step 6:** Apply the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -(-7) = +7 \text{ A/m}^3$$

*Interpretation:* Since $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 7 > 0$, the charge density is **increasing** at this point at a rate of 7 C/m³ per second.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Many KTU problems give $\vec{J}$ and ask for $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$. The trick is to remember the negative sign in $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$. Students often forget this sign and lose a mark.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid

    C --> D[Define Net Outward Current]

    E --> F[Equate: Current Out = Rate of Charge Loss]
    F --> G[Apply Divergence Theorem to LHS]
    G --> H[Swap d/dt and Integral on RHS]
    H --> I[Combine into Single Volume Integral]
    I --> J{Is Volume V Arbitrary?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Integrand Must Be Zero Pointwise]
    J -->|No| L[Equation is Only Global]
    K --> M[Final Point Form: div J + d rho/dt = 0]

    M --> N[Special Case: DC Steady State]
    N --> O[div J = 0]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the continuity equation in point form and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The continuity equation in point (differential) form is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Physical Significance:**

- It is a **mathematical statement of the conservation of electric charge**.
- The term $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$ represents the net outflow of current per unit volume from a point.
- The term $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ represents the rate of change of charge density at that point.
- The equation states that any net outflow of current from a small volume must be accompanied by a corresponding decrease in the charge inside that volume — charge is neither created nor destroyed.
- It is the **local form of Kirchhoff's Current Law** applied at a point in space.
- For steady-state (DC) conditions, it reduces to $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$, indicating that current flow is **solenoidal** (no sources or sinks).

**Mark Distribution:** [1.5 marks equation + 1.5 marks explanation]

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Derive the continuity equation in integral form starting from the principle of charge conservation.**

**Model Answer:**

Consider a closed surface $S$ enclosing a volume $V$ with charge density $\rho(\vec{r}, t)$.

**Step 1:** Total charge inside:

$$Q = \int_V \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Net outward current:

$$I = \oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 3:** By charge conservation, current leaving = rate of charge decrease:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{dQ}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

This is the **integral form of the continuity equation**.

**Mark Distribution:** [1 mark setup + 1.5 marks derivation + 0.5 marks final statement]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Sub-parts (a) and (b)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the continuity equation. Show that charge conservation is embedded in Maxwell's framework.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Maxwell's Equations:**

1. Gauss's Law: $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$
2. Ampère-Maxwell Law: $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$

**Step 1:** Take the divergence of the Ampère-Maxwell Law:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \equiv 0$:

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

**Step 3:** Apply Gauss's Law ($\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$):

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranged:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0}$$

**Conclusion:** Since this equation emerges as a necessary consequence of taking the divergence of Ampère-Maxwell's law and substituting Gauss's law, **charge conservation is automatically embedded** in Maxwell's equations. Conversely, this also demonstrates that the displacement current term $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ is essential — without it, the continuity equation would not follow from Maxwell's equations, leading to a violation of charge conservation in time-varying scenarios (e.g., capacitor charging).

**Mark Distribution:** [2 marks setup + 3 marks derivation + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark conclusion]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) Given the current density $\vec{J} = (2x + y^2)\hat{a}_x + (3y - z)\hat{a}_y + (4z + x^2)\hat{a}_z$ $\text{A/m}^2$, find the rate of change of charge density $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ at the point $(1, 1, 1)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the divergence:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = \frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial J_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial J_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial J_x}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x + y^2) = 2$$

$$\frac{\partial J_y}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3y - z) = 3$$

$$\frac{\partial J_z}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(4z + x^2) = 4$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 \text{ A/m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Apply continuity equation (no spatial dependence, so constant everywhere):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -9 \text{ A/m}^3$$

The charge density is **decreasing** at a rate of $9 \text{ C/m}^3$ per second throughout the region.

**Mark Distribution:** [1 mark equation + 3 marks divergence calculation + 2 marks evaluation + 1 mark final answer with interpretation]

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) State and derive the integral form of the continuity equation. Hence, obtain the point form using the Divergence Theorem.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Statement:
The integral form of the continuity equation is:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

This states that the net current flowing out of a closed surface $S$ equals the negative of the rate of change of charge enclosed within the volume $V$.

**Step 2:** Derivation from charge conservation:
Consider a closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $V$. The net current $I_{out}$ leaving through $S$ is:

$$I_{out} = \oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

The total charge inside is:

$$Q = \int_V \rho \, dV$$

By conservation of charge, if current leaves, charge inside decreases. Therefore:

$$I_{out} = -\frac{dQ}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV$$

This is the **integral form**.

**Step 3:** Convert to point form using Divergence Theorem:
Apply $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV$ on the LHS.

Since the volume is fixed, swap integration and differentiation on the RHS:

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV$$

Combine:

$$\int_V \left( \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \right) dV = 0$$

Since $V$ is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

This is the **point (differential) form**.

**Mark Distribution:** [1 mark statement + 2.5 marks integral derivation + 2 marks divergence theorem application + 1.5 marks final equation]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) For a region where $\vec{J} = 5 \sin(\omega t) \hat{a}_z$ $\text{A/m}^2$ and the charge density is $\rho = \rho_0(x, y, z) e^{j\omega t}$, derive the relationship between $\rho_0$ and the spatial coordinates using the continuity equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Compute the divergence of $\vec{J}$:
Since $\vec{J} = 5 \sin(\omega t) \hat{a}_z$ has no $x$ or $y$ components, and no $z$-dependence in the $z$-component (it's constant in space):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = \frac{\partial J_z}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}[5 \sin(\omega t)] = 0$$

**Step 3:** Compute the time derivative of $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}[\rho_0(x, y, z) e^{j\omega t}] = j\omega \rho_0(x, y, z) e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into continuity equation:

$$0 + j\omega \rho_0 e^{j\omega t} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve:
This requires $\rho_0(x, y, z) = 0$ for all spatial points (assuming $\omega \neq 0$).

**Conclusion:** For this current density to be physically consistent, the charge density must be **zero everywhere**. The current is purely solenoidal — it flows in closed loops without accumulation.

**Mark Distribution:** [2 marks equation setup + 2 marks divergence + 2 marks time derivative + 1 mark conclusion]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Continuity Equation:** Students often write $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = +\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ instead of $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$. The minus sign is non-negotiable. Memorize: "Outflow equals loss."
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply Divergence Theorem:** In integral-to-differential conversion, students often skip the step $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV$ and go directly to point form. Examiners **always** allocate 1–2 marks for this step.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Steady-State Form:** In DC problems, students sometimes write the full continuity equation instead of recognizing that $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ leads to $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Incorrect Sign of $d\vec{S}$:** The surface element $d\vec{S}$ points **outward** by convention in the Divergence Theorem. Reversing it gives the wrong sign and propagates through the entire problem.
> 
> 5. **Mixing Up $\vec{D}$ and $\vec{E}$:** In the Maxwell's-equation derivation, students confuse $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$ with $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\varepsilon_0$. For free-space problems both work, but for dielectric media, $\vec{D}$ is the correct quantity.
> 
> 6. **Missing the "Arbitrary Volume" Argument:** The transition from $\int_V f \, dV = 0$ to $f = 0$ requires stating that $V$ is arbitrary. Without this justification, the step is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation (Point Form):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$
- **Core Equation (Integral Form):** $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV$
- **Physical Meaning:** Local conservation of electric charge — net current outflow equals rate of charge decrease.
- **Steady-State Special Case:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ (current is solenoidal, no accumulation).
- **Key Vector Identity Used:** $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \equiv 0$ (divergence of a curl is always zero).
- **Key Theorem Used:** Divergence (Gauss's) Theorem — converts a surface integral to a volume integral.
- **Connection to Maxwell's Equations:** The continuity equation is a *necessary consequence* of Ampère-Maxwell's law and Gauss's law combined.
- **Sign Convention:** Outward-pointing $d\vec{S}$ and negative sign on $\frac{dQ}{dt}$ are critical.
- **Units Check:** $[\nabla \cdot \vec{J}] = \text{A/m}^3$ and $[\partial \rho/\partial t] = \text{C/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)} = \text{A/m}^3$ — units match.
- **Circuit Analog:** Kirchhoff's Current Law is the integral form of the continuity equation applied to a circuit node.
- **Limitation:** The continuity equation is a *local* and *classical* law. In quantum electrodynamics, it holds as an operator equation, but the physical interpretation is more subtle.
- **Exam Frequency:** This topic appears in KTU examinations at least once every cycle, typically worth 3–7 marks in Part A or as a sub-part of a larger question in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions** in electromagnetic theory are the mathematical constraints that the electric and magnetic field vectors (and their associated flux densities) must satisfy at the **interface** between two dissimilar media (medium 1 and medium 2) having different constitutive parameters (permittivity $\varepsilon$, permeability $\mu$, and conductivity $\sigma$).

These conditions are derived directly from **Maxwell's equations in integral form** and are fundamental to solving boundary-value problems in electrostatics, magnetostatics, and electromagnetic wave propagation. They dictate the behavior of:

- The **tangential** (parallel) components of $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$
- The **normal** (perpendicular) components of $\vec{D}$ and $\vec{B}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Pool Problem**
>
> Imagine two swimming pools placed side-by-side: one filled with **fresh water** (medium 1, $\varepsilon_{r1}$) and the other with **salt water** (medium 2, $\varepsilon_{r2}$). A floating ball pushed from the left pool into the right pool will:
> - Continue to **move smoothly** along the parallel (tangential) direction — there is no sudden change in horizontal motion.
> - But its **vertical (normal) motion** changes sharply because the buoyant force differs between the two liquids.
>
> In the same way, electromagnetic fields at the interface between two media behave: their **tangential components remain continuous**, while their **normal components may "jump"** depending on the surface charge or current present at the boundary.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants govern the behavior of fields at boundaries:

- **Vacuum permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{F/m}$
- **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{H/m}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{m/s}$
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_{0} = 376.73 \;\; \Omega \approx 120\pi \;\; \Omega$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Boundary conditions are derived by applying **Faraday's Law**, **Ampere's Circuital Law**, and **Gauss's Laws** to vanishingly small surfaces and contours at the interface. Mastery of these derivations guarantees marks in any Part B (14-mark) question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Boundary Conditions

The four boundary conditions are obtained by applying two of Maxwell's equations in their **integral form** to infinitesimally thin Gaussian pillboxes and rectangular loops straddling the interface.

### 2.1.1 Condition 1 — Tangential Component of $\vec{E}$ (Continuity)

Derived from **Faraday's Law**: $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$

- **Statement:** The tangential component of the electric field intensity is **continuous** across a boundary.
- **Mathematical Form:** $\hat{n}_{12} \times (\vec{E}_{1} - \vec{E}_{2}) = 0 \implies E_{1t} = E_{2t}$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** No work is done in moving a charge along the boundary itself; the potential is single-valued.

### 2.1.2 Condition 2 — Normal Component of $\vec{D}$ (Discontinuity by $\rho_{s}$)

Derived from **Gauss's Law for Electric Field**: $\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc}$

- **Statement:** The normal component of the electric flux density changes by the amount of **free surface charge density** $\rho_{s}$ at the boundary.
- **Mathematical Form:** $\hat{n}_{12} \cdot (\vec{D}_{1} - \vec{D}_{2}) = \rho_{s}$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** If no surface charge exists ($\rho_{s} = 0$), then $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ (continuous).

### 2.1.3 Condition 3 — Tangential Component of $\vec{H}$ (Discontinuity by $\vec{J}_{s}$)

Derived from **Ampere's Circuital Law**: $\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{L} = I_{enc} + \frac{d}{dt}\int \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S}$

- **Statement:** The tangential component of the magnetic field intensity changes by the amount of **surface current density** $\vec{J}_{s}$ at the boundary.
- **Mathematical Form:** $\hat{n}_{12} \times (\vec{H}_{1} - \vec{H}_{2}) = \vec{J}_{s}$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** If no surface current exists ($\vec{J}_{s} = 0$), then $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$ (continuous).

### 2.1.4 Condition 4 — Normal Component of $\vec{B}$ (Continuity)

Derived from **Gauss's Law for Magnetic Field**: $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = 0$

- **Statement:** The normal component of the magnetic flux density is **always continuous** (no magnetic monopoles exist).
- **Mathematical Form:** $\hat{n}_{12} \cdot (\vec{B}_{1} - \vec{B}_{2}) = 0 \implies B_{1n} = B_{2n}$
- **Engineering Interpretation:** Magnetic field lines are closed loops; they cannot begin or end at a boundary.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Boundary Condition** | **General Form** | **Simplified (No $\rho_{s}$, No $\vec{J}_{s}$)** | **Source Equation** |
|:-----:|:----------------------|:-----------------|:------------------------------------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Tangential $\vec{E}$ | $\hat{n}_{12} \times (\vec{E}_{1} - \vec{E}_{2}) = 0$ | $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ | Faraday's Law |
| 2 | Normal $\vec{D}$ | $\hat{n}_{12} \cdot (\vec{D}_{1} - \vec{D}_{2}) = \rho_{s}$ | $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ | Gauss's Law (Electric) |
| 3 | Tangential $\vec{H}$ | $\hat{n}_{12} \times (\vec{H}_{1} - \vec{H}_{2}) = \vec{J}_{s}$ | $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$ | Ampere's Law |
| 4 | Normal $\vec{B}$ | $\hat{n}_{12} \cdot (\vec{B}_{1} - \vec{B}_{2}) = 0$ | $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ | Gauss's Law (Magnetic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** Note that $\vec{E}$ is **continuous tangentially** but **may be discontinuous normally** (since $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_{s}$ and $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$). Conversely, $\vec{H}$ is **continuous tangentially** in the absence of surface current, and $\vec{B}$ is **continuous normally** always.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Boundary Conditions** |
|:---------------------|:---------------------------------------|
| **Transmission Line Design** | Determining reflection and transmission coefficients of voltage waves at junctions of different dielectric media |
| **Optical Fiber Communication** | Calculating the critical angle of total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface |
| **Antenna Engineering** | Analyzing radiation patterns at the boundary between the antenna substrate and free space |
| **Microstrip Patch Antennas** | Designing substrate materials with specific $\varepsilon_{r}$ to control fringing fields |
| **Transformer Core Design** | Ensuring continuous normal $\vec{B}$ at the lamination-insulation interface to minimize eddy current losses |
| **Semiconductor Device Physics** | Modeling depletion regions and built-in potentials at PN junctions |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tangential Component of $\vec{E}$ (Continuity)

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular loop $abcd$ of infinitesimal height $\Delta h$ and width $\Delta w$, lying half in medium 1 and half in medium 2, with sides $ab$ and $cd$ parallel to the interface and side $bc$ perpendicular to it.

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's Law in Integral Form:**

$$\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Line Integral over the Four Sides of the Loop:**

The line integral decomposes as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} &= \int_{a}^{b} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} + \int_{b}^{c} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} + \int_{c}^{d} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} + \int_{d}^{a} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit $\Delta h \to 0$:**

As $\Delta h \to 0$, the sides $bc$ and $da$ vanish (their length $\to 0$), and the area enclosed $\Delta w \cdot \Delta h \to 0$, so the right-hand side vanishes. The integral reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{a}^{b} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} + \int_{c}^{d} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} &= 0 \\
\vec{E}_{1} \cdot \Delta w \, \hat{a}_{t} - \vec{E}_{2} \cdot \Delta w \, \hat{a}_{t} &= 0 \\
(E_{1t} - E_{2t}) \, \Delta w &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the Boundary Condition:**

$$
\boxed{E_{1t} = E_{2t}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Normal Component of $\vec{D}$ (Discontinuity by $\rho_{s}$)

**Setup:** Construct a "pillbox" Gaussian surface — a small flat cylinder of cross-sectional area $\Delta S$ and infinitesimal thickness $\Delta h$ — placed across the interface between medium 1 and medium 2. Half the pillbox lies in medium 1; the other half in medium 2.

**Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law for Electric Field:**

$$\oint_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc} = \rho_{s} \, \Delta S$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the Surface Integral into Three Parts:**

The pillbox has three surfaces: top face (in medium 1), bottom face (in medium 2), and the curved side wall (negligible in the limit $\Delta h \to 0$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} &= \int_{\text{top}} \vec{D}_{1} \cdot d\vec{S}_{1} + \int_{\text{bot}} \vec{D}_{2} \cdot d\vec{S}_{2} + \int_{\text{side}} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} \\
&= D_{1n} \, \Delta S - D_{2n} \, \Delta S + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit $\Delta h \to 0$:**

The side wall contribution vanishes as $\Delta h \to 0$. Equating with the right-hand side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(D_{1n} - D_{2n}) \, \Delta S &= \rho_{s} \, \Delta S \\
D_{1n} - D_{2n} &= \rho_{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the Boundary Condition:**

$$
\boxed{D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_{s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If no free surface charge is present at the boundary ($\rho_{s} = 0$), then $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$, and the **normal component of $\vec{D}$ is continuous**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tangential Component of $\vec{H}$ (Discontinuity by $\vec{J}_{s}$)

**Setup:** Use the same rectangular loop of width $\Delta w$ and height $\Delta h$ as in Derivation 1, but this time apply **Ampere's Circuital Law**.

**Step 1 — Apply Ampere's Law in Integral Form:**

$$\oint_{C} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{L} = I_{enc} + \frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Limit $\Delta h \to 0$:**

The right-hand side's flux integral $\to 0$ as the area $\Delta w \cdot \Delta h \to 0$. The enclosed current becomes the surface current $I_{enc} = \vec{J}_{s} \cdot \hat{a}_{n} \, \Delta w$ (where $\hat{a}_{n}$ is normal to the loop surface). The line integral simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint_{C} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{L} &= (H_{1t} - H_{2t}) \, \Delta w \\
(H_{1t} - H_{2t}) \, \Delta w &= J_{s} \, \Delta w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the Boundary Condition:**

$$
\boxed{H_{1t} - H_{2t} = J_{s}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Normal Component of $\vec{B}$ (Always Continuous)

**Setup:** Use the same Gaussian pillbox as in Derivation 2, but apply **Gauss's Law for Magnetic Field**.

**Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law for Magnetism:**

$$\oint_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Expand and Take the Limit $\Delta h \to 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} &= (B_{1n} - B_{2n}) \, \Delta S = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the Boundary Condition:**

$$
\boxed{B_{1n} = B_{2n}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Summary of Derived Boundary Conditions

| **#** | **Vector Component** | **Field** | **Result** | **Physical Origin** |
|:----:|:-------------------|:----------|:-----------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Tangential | $\vec{E}$ | $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ | Faraday's Law |
| 2 | Normal | $\vec{D}$ | $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_{s}$ | Gauss's Law (Electric) |
| 3 | Tangential | $\vec{H}$ | $H_{1t} - H_{2t} = J_{s}$ | Ampere's Law |
| 4 | Normal | $\vec{B}$ | $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ | Gauss's Law (Magnetic) |

> [!TIP]
> **The Logical Pattern:** The two **divergence** equations (Gauss's Laws) give conditions on the **normal** components. The two **curl** equations (Faraday's and Ampere's) give conditions on the **tangential** components. This pairing is no accident — it reflects the underlying structure of Maxwell's equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the boundary condition for the tangential component of the electric field at the interface of two media. Mention the Maxwell's equation from which it is derived.*

**Model Answer:**

The tangential component of the electric field $\vec{E}$ is **continuous** across the boundary between two different media. Mathematically:

$$E_{1t} = E_{2t}$$

**Derivation Sketch:** This is obtained by applying **Faraday's Law in integral form** ($\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$) to a small rectangular loop straddling the boundary. As the height of the loop $\Delta h \to 0$, the right-hand side vanishes, leaving the tangential components on either side equal.

**Physical Meaning:** This condition reflects the fact that the electrostatic potential is single-valued — the line integral of $\vec{E}$ around a closed loop is zero in electrostatics.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between the boundary conditions satisfied by the normal component of $\vec{D}$ and the tangential component of $\vec{H}$. When are these conditions continuous?*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Normal Component of $\vec{D}$** | **Tangential Component of $\vec{H}$** |
|:----------|:----------------------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| Mathematical Form | $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_{s}$ | $H_{1t} - H_{2t} = J_{s}$ |
| Source Maxwell Equation | Gauss's Law (Electric) | Ampere's Circuital Law |
| Continuous When | $\rho_{s} = 0$ (no free surface charge) | $J_{s} = 0$ (no surface current) |
| Geometry Used | Gaussian pillbox | Rectangular loop |

The normal $\vec{D}$ condition involves the **free surface charge density** $\rho_{s}$ ($\text{C/m}^2$), while the tangential $\vec{H}$ condition involves the **surface current density** $\vec{J}_{s}$ ($\text{A/m}$). Both reduce to continuity conditions when the respective source densities vanish at the interface.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** State Maxwell's equations in integral form. Using these equations, derive the boundary conditions for the normal component of electric flux density $\vec{D}$ and the tangential component of magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ at the interface between two media with surface charge density $\rho_{s}$ and surface current density $\vec{J}_{s}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The interface between two dielectric media is the plane $z = 0$. For medium 1 ($z < 0$): $\varepsilon_{r1} = 4$, $\mu_{r1} = 1$. For medium 2 ($z > 0$): $\varepsilon_{r2} = 9$, $\mu_{r2} = 1$. The electric field in medium 1 is $\vec{E}_{1} = 5\hat{a}_{x} + 8\hat{a}_{y} + 6\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{V/m}$. If there is **no surface charge** at the boundary, determine $\vec{E}_{2}$ in medium 2. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** State the two relevant Maxwell's equations in integral form.

> Gauss's Law (Electric): $\oint_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc}$
>
> Ampere's Law: $\oint_{C} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{L} = I_{enc} + \frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S}$

**Step 2:** For the **normal component of $\vec{D}$**, construct a pillbox of area $\Delta S$ and thickness $\Delta h \to 0$ across the interface. Apply Gauss's Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} &= D_{1n}\Delta S - D_{2n}\Delta S = \rho_{s}\Delta S \\
\Rightarrow D_{1n} - D_{2n} &= \rho_{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For the **tangential component of $\vec{H}$**, construct a rectangular loop of width $\Delta w$ and height $\Delta h \to 0$ across the interface. Apply Ampere's Law (flux term vanishes as $\Delta h \to 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{L} &= H_{1t}\Delta w - H_{2t}\Delta w = J_{s}\Delta w \\
\Rightarrow H_{1t} - H_{2t} &= J_{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** Interface at $z = 0$, $\varepsilon_{r1} = 4$, $\varepsilon_{r2} = 9$, $\rho_{s} = 0$, $\vec{E}_{1} = 5\hat{a}_{x} + 8\hat{a}_{y} + 6\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{V/m}$.

**Step 1:** Identify tangential and normal components. The normal direction is $\hat{a}_{z}$ (along the interface normal).

- Tangential component: $\vec{E}_{1t} = 5\hat{a}_{x} + 8\hat{a}_{y} \;\; \text{V/m}$
- Normal component: $E_{1n} = 6 \;\; \text{V/m}$

**Step 2:** Apply tangential continuity: $\vec{E}_{2t} = \vec{E}_{1t} = 5\hat{a}_{x} + 8\hat{a}_{y} \;\; \text{V/m}$

**Step 3:** Compute $D_{1n} = \varepsilon_{1} E_{1n} = \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r1} E_{1n} = 4\varepsilon_{0}(6) = 24\varepsilon_{0}$.

**Step 4:** Apply normal condition with $\rho_{s} = 0$: $D_{2n} = D_{1n} = 24\varepsilon_{0}$.

**Step 5:** Compute $E_{2n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{2n} &= \frac{D_{2n}}{\varepsilon_{2}} = \frac{24\varepsilon_{0}}{9\varepsilon_{0}} = \frac{24}{9} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.67 \;\; \text{V/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Assemble the final answer:

$$
\boxed{\vec{E}_{2} = 5\hat{a}_{x} + 8\hat{a}_{y} + 2.67\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{V/m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #1:** A common error is to assume $\vec{E}$ itself is continuous both tangentially and normally. **WRONG!** Only the **tangential component of $\vec{E}$** and the **normal component of $\vec{D}$** are continuous (in the absence of source densities). The **normal $\vec{E}$** jumps because $\varepsilon$ changes between media.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain with neat derivations the boundary conditions satisfied by the tangential component of electric field $\vec{E}$ and the normal component of magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ at the interface of two media. Use diagrams to illustrate the Gaussian surface and the closed loop used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Region 1 ($y < 0$) has $\varepsilon_{r1} = 2.25$ and $\mu_{r1} = 1$. Region 2 ($y > 0$) is free space. The magnetic field in region 1 is $\vec{H}_{1} = (3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{z}) \;\; \text{A/m}$. If $\vec{B}_{1n} = 0$ at the interface, find $\vec{H}_{2}$ in region 2. Assume no surface current at the interface. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Tangential $\vec{E}$ — Derivation:**

Apply Faraday's Law to a small rectangular loop $abcd$ straddling the boundary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{L} &= -\frac{d}{dt}\int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $\Delta h \to 0$, the flux term $\to 0$, so:

$$
E_{1t}\Delta w - E_{2t}\Delta w = 0 \implies \boxed{E_{1t} = E_{2t}}
$$

**Normal $\vec{B}$ — Derivation:**

Apply Gauss's Law for Magnetism to a Gaussian pillbox:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} &= 0 \\
B_{1n}\Delta S - B_{2n}\Delta S &= 0 \implies \boxed{B_{1n} = B_{2n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Include a neat sketch of the loop and pillbox for full marks.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** Region 1 ($y < 0$): $\varepsilon_{r1} = 2.25$, $\mu_{r1} = 1$. Region 2 ($y > 0$): free space ($\varepsilon_{r2} = 1$, $\mu_{r2} = 1$). $\vec{H}_{1} = 3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{A/m}$. No surface current.

**Step 1:** Identify the normal direction as $\hat{a}_{y}$. The normal component of $\vec{H}_{1}$ is $H_{1n} = 0$ (since $\vec{H}_{1}$ has no $y$-component). Thus $B_{1n} = \mu_{0}(1)(0) = 0$.

**Step 2:** Tangential components in region 1: $H_{1t} = 3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{A/m}$. These are in the $xz$-plane.

**Step 3:** Apply tangential continuity (no surface current, so $\vec{J}_{s} = 0$):

$$
\vec{H}_{2t} = \vec{H}_{1t} = 3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{A/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $B_{1n} = \mu_{1}H_{1n} = \mu_{0}(0) = 0$. Apply normal continuity of $\vec{B}$: $B_{2n} = 0 \implies H_{2n} = 0$.

**Step 5:** Assemble the final answer:

$$
\boxed{\vec{H}_{2} = 3\hat{a}_{x} + 4\hat{a}_{z} \;\; \text{A/m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #2:** Many students forget to verify that the tangential condition $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$ is the one that applies when $\vec{J}_{s} = 0$, **not** $H_{1n} = H_{2n}$. The roles of normal and tangential are **inverted** between $\vec{D}/\vec{B}$ and $\vec{E}/\vec{H}$. Always double-check which field is which!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boundary Conditions**

- **Tangential $\vec{E}$ is always continuous** (regardless of $\rho_{s}$ or $\vec{J}_{s}$): $E_{1t} = E_{2t}$ ✔
- **Normal $\vec{D}$ jumps by $\rho_{s}$**: $D_{1n} - D_{2n} = \rho_{s}$. If $\rho_{s} = 0$, then $D_{1n} = D_{2n}$ ✔
- **Tangential $\vec{H}$ jumps by $\vec{J}_{s}$**: $H_{1t} - H_{2t} = J_{s}$. If $\vec{J}_{s} = 0$, then $H_{1t} = H_{2t}$ ✔
- **Normal $\vec{B}$ is always continuous** (no magnetic monopoles): $B_{1n} = B_{2n}$ ✔
- **Pillbox geometry** → used for **normal** components (divergence equations: Gauss's Laws) ✔
- **Rectangular loop geometry** → used for **tangential** components (curl equations: Faraday's & Ampere's) ✔
- **The normal unit vector** $\hat{n}_{12}$ is conventionally taken **from medium 2 to medium 1** (outward from the second medium) ✔
- **Constitutive relations** to remember: $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$, $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$, $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ ✔
- **Engineering applications**: Transmission lines, optical fibers, antennas, transformers, semiconductor junctions ✔
- **For perfect electric conductor (PEC)**: $\vec{E}_{t} = 0$, $\vec{B}_{n} = 0$, and surface currents/surface charges can exist on the conductor's surface ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson's and Laplace's Equations (Solution Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Poisson's and Laplace's Equations (Solution Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poisson's Equation** and **Laplace's Equation** are the two fundamental second-order partial differential equations that govern the behaviour of the **electrostatic scalar potential** $V$ in a region of space, derived directly from Gauss's Law in point form. Together, they constitute the mathematical backbone of static-field analysis in electromagnetic theory.

- **Poisson's Equation** relates the Laplacian of the scalar potential to the local volume charge density in a source-bearing region:

$$\nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

- **Laplace's Equation** is the special source-free (charge-free) case of Poisson's Equation, valid in regions where the volume charge density is identically zero:

$$\nabla^{2} V = 0$$

where $\nabla^{2}$ is the **scalar Laplacian operator**, $\rho_{v}$ is the volume charge density (in $\text{C/m}^{3}$), and $\varepsilon$ is the **permittivity of the medium** (in $\text{F/m}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, only the *derivation* and *statement* of Poisson's and Laplace's equations are required. Students are **not** expected to solve boundary-value problems using these equations — that belongs to advanced courses. Focus your preparation on the derivation chain, the Laplacian in different coordinate systems, and the Uniqueness Theorem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hillscape of Charge"

Imagine the electrostatic potential $V(x,y,z)$ as the **height of a terrain** at every point in space, and the **charge density** $\rho_{v}$ as the presence of "springs" or "valleys" pushing the terrain up or down:

- **Poisson's Equation** describes a *hilly terrain* where springs (charges) are actively pushing up or pulling down the landscape. The curvature of the terrain at any point is directly proportional to the strength of the local charge.
- **Laplace's Equation** describes a *perfectly smooth, springless terrain* — a soap-film-like surface with no sources or sinks. The terrain curves in a perfectly balanced, mean-value manner: the value at any interior point equals the *average* of the values on the surrounding boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The Laplacian $\nabla^{2}V$ is essentially a measure of "how much $V$ at a point differs from the average of $V$ around that point." If $\rho_{v} = 0$, then $V$ must equal its local average — this is the **Mean Value Theorem** of harmonic functions, which is the geometric soul of Laplace's Equation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values appear throughout this topic and must be memorized:

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{F/m} \approx \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}} \;\text{F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{r}$ (dimensionless, $\geq 1$ for all real dielectrics)
- **Absolute permittivity:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}$ (in **F/m**)
- **Volume charge density:** $\rho_{v}$ (in **C/m³**)
- **Potential:** $V$ (in **Volts**, equivalent to **Joules per Coulomb**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Derivation Chain (Top-Down View)

The Poisson and Laplace equations are not standalone results — they are the **culmination** of three foundational electromagnetic relations. The logical chain is:

1. **Gauss's Law in Point Form** (experimental law — encodes the existence of charge):
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_{v}$$

2. **Constitutive Relation** (material property for linear, isotropic, homogeneous media):
$$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$$

3. **Definition of Scalar Potential** (conservative nature of electrostatic field):
$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$$

4. **Substitution Chain**: Combine (1), (2), (3) → Extract Poisson's equation. Then impose $\rho_{v} = 0$ → Extract Laplace's equation.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Gauss's Law in point form?** Because it tells us how charge *spatially* sources the electric flux density field $\mathbf{D}$. Without this, there is no local measure of "how much charge is here."
- **Why the constitutive relation?** Because in a real dielectric medium, the electric field polarizes the material, producing bound charges. The factor $\varepsilon$ accounts for this amplification of the field's source term.
- **Why the gradient of potential?** Because the electrostatic field $\mathbf{E}$ is **conservative** (curl-free, $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = 0$). Any irrotational vector field can be expressed as the negative gradient of a scalar potential $V$.

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions — The Cornerstone of Uniqueness

A differential equation alone admits infinitely many solutions. To obtain a **physically meaningful, unique solution**, the potential $V$ must be constrained on the boundaries of the region of interest. Two standard types:

| Boundary Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dirichlet Condition** | $V$ is specified on the surface $S$ | The potential itself is fixed (e.g., a conductor held at a known voltage) |
| **Neumann Condition** | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial n}$ is specified on $S$ | The normal derivative (proportional to surface charge) is fixed |
| **Mixed (Cauchy) Condition** | Both $V$ and $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial n}$ are partially specified | A combination on different portions of the boundary |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula, operator expansion, and condition** you must know for this topic. **Pay close attention to the LaTeX rendering of absolute values** — vertical pipes have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Coordinate System | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Poisson's Equation (General) | $\nabla^{2} V = -\dfrac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$ | All systems | $\rho_{v}$ in C/m³, $\varepsilon$ in F/m |
| 2 | Laplace's Equation (Source-Free) | $\nabla^{2} V = 0$ | All systems | Valid when $\rho_{v} = 0$ |
| 3 | Laplacian — Cartesian | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$ | $(x,y,z)$ | Most general 3-D expansion |
| 4 | Laplacian — Cylindrical | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$ | $(r,\phi,z)$ | For cylindrical symmetry problems |
| 5 | Laplacian — Spherical | $\dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}$ | $(r,\theta,\phi)$ | For point-charge / antenna problems |
| 6 | Relation: E to V | $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ | All systems | $V$ is volts, $\mathbf{E}$ is V/m |
| 7 | Relation: D to E | $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$ | All systems | LIH medium assumption |
| 8 | Mean Value Theorem | $V(\mathbf{r}_{0}) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi a^{2}}\oiint_{S} V\, dS$ | Spherical surface | Holds for solutions of $\nabla^{2} V = 0$ |
| 9 | Uniqueness Theorem Statement | A solution is unique if $V$ (Dirichlet) or $\partial V / \partial n$ (Neumann) is prescribed on the entire boundary | All systems | The "license" to apply Poisson/Laplace |
| 10 | Boundary Condition on Conductor | $V = $ constant, $E_{t} = 0$, $\rho_{s} = D_{n} = \varepsilon E_{n}$ | All systems | Conductors are equipotential surfaces |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not abstract exercises — they are the working tools behind:

- **Capacitor Design:** Finding the capacitance of arbitrarily shaped electrodes (parallel-plate, coaxial, spherical).
- **Semiconductor Device Physics:** Modelling the built-in potential in p-n junctions and MOSFETs.
- **High-Voltage Engineering:** Designing insulation systems and predicting dielectric breakdown paths.
- **Antenna Theory:** Computing radiated fields in the far-zone using spherical Laplacian.
- **Transmission Line Analysis:** Solving the 1-D Laplace equation for cross-sectional field distribution.
- **Cathode-Ray Tube and Electron Optics:** Tracing electron trajectories through computed electrostatic potentials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Poisson's Equation from First Principles

We begin from Gauss's Law in differential (point) form and apply successive substitutions. Every algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

---

**Step 1 — Statement of Gauss's Law in Point Form**

This is an experimentally established relation (one of Maxwell's four equations for the static case). It expresses that the divergence of the electric flux density $\mathbf{D}$ at any point in space is exactly equal to the volume charge density $\rho_{v}$ at that point:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_{v}$$

> **Justification:** This law is empirically true for all media, irrespective of constitutive properties.

---

**Step 2 — Apply the Constitutive Relation $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$**

For a **Linear, Isotropic, Homogeneous (LIH)** medium, $\varepsilon$ is a constant scalar, so we may pull it outside the divergence operator:

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \mathbf{E}) = \rho_{v}$$

Substituting $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \mathbf{E}) = \rho_{v}$$

Since $\varepsilon$ is a constant (homogeneous medium), we may factor it out:

$$\varepsilon \, (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) = \rho_{v}$$

> **Justification:** $\nabla \cdot (c\,\mathbf{F}) = c\,(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F})$ for any constant scalar $c$.

---

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $\varepsilon$**

Isolating the divergence of $\mathbf{E}$:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

> **Justification:** Simple algebraic division; $\varepsilon \neq 0$ for any physical medium.

---

**Step 4 — Apply the Definition of Scalar Potential**

The electrostatic field is conservative (i.e., $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = \mathbf{0}$), so $\mathbf{E}$ can be expressed as the negative gradient of a scalar field $V$:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$$

> **Justification:** A vector identity guarantees that any irrotational field ($\nabla \times \mathbf{F} = 0$) admits a scalar potential representation. The negative sign is a convention aligned with the physics that the field points from high to low potential.

---

**Step 5 — Substitute $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ into $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}$**

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

The divergence of a gradient is by definition the scalar Laplacian operator:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^{2} V$$

> **Justification:** By definition, $\nabla^{2} \equiv \nabla \cdot \nabla$ when applied to a scalar.

So our equation becomes:

$$-\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

---

**Step 6 — Rearrange to Isolate the Laplacian**

Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$\nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

> **This is Poisson's Equation in its most general form**, valid in any coordinate system, for any LIH medium.

---

**Step 7 — Derive Laplace's Equation as the Source-Free Special Case**

If the region of interest contains **no volume charge** (i.e., $\rho_{v} = 0$), then the right-hand side vanishes identically:

$$\nabla^{2} V = 0$$

> **This is Laplace's Equation.** It governs the potential in charge-free regions such as the space between capacitor plates (outside the fringing region), inside hollow conductors, and in vacuum regions of an electron-optical system.

---

### 3.2 Expansion of $\nabla^{2} V$ in the Three Principal Coordinate Systems

The expression $\nabla^{2} V$ is *coordinate-invariant* in meaning, but its mathematical form depends on the geometry of the problem. Below are the three canonical expansions, derived from the general Laplacian operator applied to a scalar field.

#### 3.2.1 Cartesian Coordinates $(x, y, z)$

The gradient operator in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$\nabla = \hat{\mathbf{a}}_{x}\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + \hat{\mathbf{a}}_{y}\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + \hat{\mathbf{a}}_{z}\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

Applying $\nabla \cdot \nabla$ to a scalar $V(x,y,z)$ gives:

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$

> **Use Case:** Best for problems involving rectangular geometries — parallel-plate capacitors, rectangular waveguides, infinite line charges.

#### 3.2.2 Cylindrical Coordinates $(r, \phi, z)$

Because the unit vectors in cylindrical coordinates ($\hat{\mathbf{a}}_{r}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{a}}_{\phi}$) themselves change direction with position, extra terms appear. The full expansion is:

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$

This can be expanded algebraically for clarity:

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial r^{2}} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$

> **Use Case:** Best for problems with axial symmetry — coaxial cables, cylindrical capacitors, line charges, circular waveguides.

#### 3.2.3 Spherical Coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$

The expansion involves both radial and polar-angle terms and is the most complex:

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2} \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta \frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}$$

> **Use Case:** Best for problems with spherical symmetry — point charges, spherical capacitors, dipole antennas, planetary magnetospheres.

### 3.3 The Uniqueness Theorem — Formal Statement and Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniqueness Theorem Statement:**
> *If a solution $V$ to Poisson's equation (or Laplace's equation) exists in a closed region $\mathcal{V}$ bounded by a surface $S$, and if either:*
> *(a) the value of $V$ is prescribed on every point of $S$ (Dirichlet condition), OR*
> *(b) the normal derivative $\partial V / \partial n$ is prescribed on every point of $S$ (Neumann condition),*
> *then that solution is unique.*

**Why this matters in practice:** Engineers often *guess* a potential function that satisfies the differential equation, then check if it satisfies the boundary conditions. If it does, by the Uniqueness Theorem, it is the **only** correct answer — no need to verify by any other method. This theorem is what permits the powerful method of "image charges" and "separation of variables" in advanced courses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Logical Derivation Chain from Maxwell to Poisson/Laplace

The following block diagram illustrates the exact logical flow used to derive Poisson's and Laplace's equations from Maxwell's static-field relations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MAXWELL EQUATION STATIC FORM"] --> B["Gauss Law Point Form grad dot D equals rho sub v"]
    B --> C["Constitutive Relation D equals epsilon times E"]
    C --> D["Field Equation grad dot E equals rho over epsilon"]
    D --> E["Potential Definition E equals minus grad V"]
    E --> F["Substitute E into Divergence Equation"]
    F --> G["Apply Div Grad Identity yields Laplacian of V"]
    G --> H["Result grad squared V equals minus rho over epsilon"]
    H --> I{"Is rho sub v equal to zero?"}
    I -->|"NO charge present"| J["POISSONS EQUATION grad squared V equals minus rho over epsilon"]
    I -->|"YES charge free region"| K["LAPLACES EQUATION grad squared V equals zero"]
    J --> L["Uniqueness Theorem Dirichlet or Neumann Boundary"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["UNIQUE PHYSICAL SOLUTION FOR V"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Coordinate-System Selection Architecture

Choosing the correct coordinate system for $\nabla^{2}V$ is critical. The following architecture maps problem geometry to the appropriate Laplacian form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["PROBLEM GEOMETRY IDENTIFICATION"] --> S1{"Symmetry Type?"}
    S1 -->|"Rectangular / Cubic / Planar"| S2["USE CARTESIAN LAPLACIAN d2V/dx2 + d2V/dy2 + d2V/dz2"]
    S1 -->|"Cylindrical / Axial / Line Charges"| S3["USE CYLINDRICAL LAPLACIAN 1 over r times d by dr of r dV by dr plus angular plus axial terms"]
    S1 -->|"Spherical / Point / Dipole"| S4["USE SPHERICAL LAPLACIAN 1 over r squared times d by dr of r squared dV by dr plus polar plus azimuthal terms"]
    S2 --> S5["Boundary Conditions: V or dV/dn on Surface S"]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["Solve or Apply Uniqueness Theorem"]
    S6 --> S7["FINAL UNIQUE POTENTIAL FUNCTION V"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Role of Boundary Conditions in Uniqueness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Differential Equation grad squared V equals f of r"] --> P1["Infinite Mathematical Solutions Exist"]
    P1 --> P2{"Boundary Information Provided"}
    P2 -->|"V specified on S"| P3["DIRICHLET CONDITION yields Unique V"]
    P2 -->|"dV by dn specified on S"| P4["NEUMANN CONDITION yields Unique V up to constant"]
    P2 -->|"Mixed on different parts of S"| P5["CAUCHY OR MIXED CONDITION yields Unique V"]
    P3 --> P6["PHYSICAL SOLUTION"]
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The differential equation alone is mathematically under-determined. The boundary conditions supply the missing physics — they encode the actual electrodes, surfaces, and sources that bound the physical region. Without them, even an exact algebraic solution is meaningless.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 Scheme are short-answer questions typically carrying 3 marks. They test conceptual understanding and direct recall of definitions/derivations. Answers should be crisp, well-structured, and self-contained.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define Poisson's equation and Laplace's equation. State the conditions under which each is applicable.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Poisson's Equation** is the second-order partial differential equation governing the electrostatic scalar potential $V$ in regions containing volume charge:

$$\nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}$$

It is **applicable in source-bearing regions** where the volume charge density $\rho_{v}$ is non-zero.

**Laplace's Equation** is the special, source-free case of Poisson's equation, valid in regions where the volume charge density is identically zero ($\rho_{v} = 0$):

$$\nabla^{2} V = 0$$

It is **applicable in charge-free regions**, such as the space between the plates of an ideal parallel-plate capacitor (excluding fringing fields) or inside a hollow conductor. Both equations are derived from Gauss's law combined with the gradient definition of the scalar potential.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Write the Laplacian operator $\nabla^{2} V$ in Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical coordinate systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Cartesian Coordinates $(x,y,z)$:**

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$

**Cylindrical Coordinates $(r,\phi,z)$:**

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$

**Spherical Coordinates $(r,\theta,\phi)$:**

$$\nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}$$

The choice of coordinate system depends on the geometric symmetry of the problem.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE carry 14 marks, typically divided into sub-parts (e.g., 7 + 7). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice). Both options are provided below with exhaustive model solutions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**(a) Derive Poisson's equation for a linear, isotropic, and homogeneous (LIH) medium starting from Gauss's law in point form. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We start from **Gauss's Law in point form**, which states that the divergence of the electric flux density $\mathbf{D}$ at any point in space equals the local volume charge density:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_{v} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

For an **LIH medium**, the constitutive relation $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$ holds with $\varepsilon$ as a constant scalar:

$$\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \mathbf{E}) = \rho_{v} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Since $\varepsilon$ is constant, it can be taken outside the divergence operator:

$$\varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) = \rho_{v} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

The electrostatic field is conservative ($\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = 0$), so it can be expressed as the negative gradient of a scalar potential $V$:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V \quad \text{...(5)}$$

Substituting (5) into (4):

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

Recognizing that $\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^{2} V$:

$$-\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}} \quad \text{...(8)}$$

This is **Poisson's Equation**, valid for any LIH medium in any coordinate system.

---

**(b) State and explain the Uniqueness Theorem for the solution of Poisson's and Laplace's equations. What are Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the Uniqueness Theorem:**

*If a solution $V$ to Poisson's equation (or Laplace's equation) exists within a closed volume $\mathcal{V}$ bounded by a closed surface $S$, and if either the value of $V$ or the value of its outward normal derivative $\partial V / \partial n$ is specified at every point on $S$, then $V$ is uniquely determined throughout $\mathcal{V}$.*

**Explanation:**

Suppose there exist two solutions $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$, both satisfying Poisson's equation inside the volume and the same boundary condition on $S$. Define their difference:

$$W = V_{1} - V_{2}$$

Then $W$ satisfies Laplace's equation inside $\mathcal{V}$ (since the source terms cancel) and $W = 0$ on $S$ (or $\partial W / \partial n = 0$ on $S$). Applying Green's first identity with $W$ as both scalar and vector potential:

$$\int_{\mathcal{V}} \left( \vert \nabla W \vert^{2} + W \, \nabla^{2} W \right) d\mathcal{V} = \oint_{S} W \frac{\partial W}{\partial n} \, dS$$

Since $\nabla^{2} W = 0$ and $W = 0$ (or $\partial W / \partial n = 0$) on $S$, both integrals vanish, leaving:

$$\int_{\mathcal{V}} \vert \nabla W \vert^{2} \, d\mathcal{V} = 0$$

Since the integrand is non-negative, this forces $\nabla W = 0$ everywhere, hence $W = \text{constant}$. With either Dirichlet or Neumann boundary conditions fixing the constant, $W = 0$ identically, proving $V_{1} = V_{2}$.

**Dirichlet Boundary Condition:** The potential $V$ itself is specified on the bounding surface $S$. Example: a conductor held at fixed potential $V_{0}$.

**Neumann Boundary Condition:** The normal derivative $\partial V / \partial n$ is specified on $S$. This is equivalent to specifying the surface charge density $\rho_{s}$ via $\partial V / \partial n = -\rho_{s} / \varepsilon$.

**Engineering Significance:** The theorem guarantees that any "guessed" solution satisfying both the differential equation and the boundary conditions is the correct, unique physical answer — a powerful tool in analytical electromagnetics.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**(a) Starting from first principles, show that the electrostatic field is conservative and hence can be expressed as the gradient of a scalar potential. Use this to obtain Laplace's equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Demonstrating the Conservative Nature of $\mathbf{E}$:**
