> 6. **Common Mistake 6:** In numerical problems, forgetting that $\psi_m$ has units of **Amperes** (not Volts). This is a frequent error that shows conceptual confusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **Definition:** $\vec{H} = -\nabla \psi_m$ — the **magnetic scalar potential** is defined ONLY in current-free regions ($\vec{J} = 0$).
- **Laplace's Equation:** $\nabla^2 \psi_m = 0$ in homogeneous, source-free, current-free regions.
- **Poisson's Equation:** $\vec{A}$ obeys $\nabla^2 \vec{A} = -\mu \vec{J}$ (for vector potential). $\psi_m$ obeys only Laplace, not Poisson — because it cannot exist in current-carrying regions.
- **Units:** $\psi_m$ is measured in **Amperes (A)**, analogous to MMF.
- **Boundary Condition 1 (Tangential):** $\psi_{m1} = \psi_{m2}$ at the interface (assumes no surface current).
- **Boundary Condition 2 (Normal):** $\mu_1 \dfrac{\partial \psi_{m1}}{\partial n} = \mu_2 \dfrac{\partial \psi_{m2}}{\partial n}$ at the interface.
- **Line Integral:** $\int_a^b \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \psi_{m,a} - \psi_{m,b}$ — this is the **Magnetomotive Force (MMF)**.
- **Multi-valuedness:** $\psi_m$ is multi-valued when the region encloses a current. A single-valued $\psi_m$ requires the region to be **simply connected** (no loops around current).
- **Equivalence to Vector Potential:** In current-free regions, $\nabla \times \vec{H} = 0$ implies $\vec{H} = -\nabla \psi_m$, while in general $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$. Both cannot be globally defined simultaneously; $\psi_m$ is the simpler tool for current-free problems.
- **Mathematical Identity:** $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \psi_m) = \nabla^2 \psi_m$ — used to derive Laplace's equation.
- **Methodology:** Solve $\nabla^2 \psi_m = 0$ with boundary conditions using separation of variables, method of images, or conformal mapping (for 2D).
- **Physical Insight:** The magnetic scalar potential is the magnetic analog of electrostatic potential. Equipotential surfaces of $\psi_m$ are everywhere perpendicular to $\vec{H}$ field lines.
- **Engineering Use:** Essential for designing magnetic circuits, transformers, motors, MRI coils, and magnetic shielding.
- **Common Trap:** Don't confuse $\psi_m$ (scalar, A) with $V$ (electric potential, V). They have different units and different physical meanings, even though their mathematical forms are analogous.
- **Validity Checklist:** Before using $\psi_m$, verify: (1) $\vec{J} = 0$ in the region, (2) the region is simply connected, and (3) the medium is linear/homogeneous (for Laplace's equation).
- **Gauge Freedom:** Unlike $\vec{A}$ (which has gauge freedom), $\psi_m$ is uniquely determined (up to an additive constant) by the boundary conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra for Module 2 / Magnetic Scalar Potential:**
> *"If $\vec{J} = 0$ in the region, choose $\psi_m$, apply $\nabla^2 \psi_m = 0$, enforce $\psi_{m1} = \psi_{m2}$ and $\mu_1 \partial_n \psi_{m1} = \mu_2 \partial_n \psi_{m2}$ at the interface, and solve. Always remember — $\psi_m$ lives in Amperes, lives only in current-free regions, and turns magnetostatics into a Laplace-equation problem."*
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Vector Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Vector Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In classical electrodynamics, the **Magnetic Vector Potential** (often denoted as **A**) is a vector field whose curl equals the magnetic flux density **B**. It is one of the two fundamental electromagnetic potentials, the other being the scalar electric potential **V**.

Mathematically, it is defined through the foundational relation:

$$\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$$

where **B** is measured in **Tesla (T)** and **A** is measured in **Weber per meter (Wb/m)** or equivalently **Tesla·meter (T·m)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The magnetic vector potential is not merely a mathematical convenience — it is a *physical* field that becomes directly observable in quantum mechanical experiments such as the **Aharonov–Bohm effect**, where charged particles are deflected in regions where **B = 0** but **A ≠ 0**.

## 1.2 Why We Need a Vector Potential?

The existence of **A** is rooted in a deep Maxwell's equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$$

This is **Gauss's Law for Magnetism**, which states that magnetic field lines have *no beginning and no end* — they always form closed loops. Because the divergence of any curl is identically zero (a vector identity):

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) \equiv 0$$

any vector field that is a curl of another vector automatically satisfies **∇·B = 0**. This is the *mathematical necessity* of introducing **A**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Whirlpool Picture:**
>
> Imagine a still pond. Drop a pebble, and you see *concentric ripples* (this is analogous to the **scalar potential V** for electric fields, which has sources/sinks).
>
> Now stir the water with a stick. You see *swirling vortices* around the stick — there are no sources or sinks, just closed circulating flow. The "swirl" of water is described by a **vector potential A** (whose curl gives the circulating flow velocity). Even if you remove the stick and try to detect the original vortex by looking at *flow alone* at distant points (where flow is zero), quantum particles can still sense the original swirl — this is the **Aharonov–Bohm effect**.
>
> **Key Takeaway:** Just as a whirlpool is more easily described by its "circulation source" rather than the velocity field itself, **A** often provides a simpler description of magnetostatics than **B** does.

## 1.4 Gauge Freedom (A Critical Concept)

The vector potential is **not unique**. If **A** produces a given **B**, then any other vector potential **A'** defined by:

$$\mathbf{A'} = \mathbf{A} + \nabla \psi$$

will produce the *exact same* **B**, because the curl of a gradient is always zero:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A'} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A} + \nabla \times (\nabla \psi) = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$$

This freedom to add the gradient of an arbitrary scalar function **ψ** is called **gauge freedom**, and the choice of **ψ** is called a **gauge choice**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Gauge for Magnetostatics: The Coulomb Gauge**
> $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$$
> Under this gauge, **A** satisfies a clean Poisson-like equation: **∇²A = −μ₀J**

## 1.5 Units, Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** |
| Magnetic flux density | $\mathbf{B}$ | **Tesla (T)** |
| Vector potential | $\mathbf{A}$ | **Weber/meter (Wb/m)** |
| Current density | $\mathbf{J}$ | **A/m²** |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why ∇·B = 0 Forces a Curl

Let us walk through the logical chain:

- **Step 1:** Maxwell's equation for magnetism says **∇·B = 0** (no magnetic monopoles).
- **Step 2:** A fundamental vector identity tells us that **∇·(∇×F) = 0** for *any* vector field **F**.
- **Step 3:** Therefore, if we can find a vector field **A** such that **B = ∇×A**, Gauss's law for magnetism is automatically satisfied.
- **Step 4:** This is *guaranteed to work* at every point in space because the identity is universal — independent of coordinates, sources, or geometry.
- **Step 5:** This **does not** prove that **A** is unique — multiple valid **A** fields exist for one **B** field (gauge freedom).

## 2.2 Connection to Ampère's Law (Curl of Curl Identity)

Starting from Ampère's Law (magnetostatic form):

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

Substituting **B = ∇×A**:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

Applying the vector identity **∇×(∇×A) = ∇(∇·A) − ∇²A**, we get:

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

In the **Coulomb gauge** where **∇·A = 0**, this simplifies beautifully to:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **vector Poisson's equation** for magnetostatics. It is the magnetic analogue of **∇²V = −ρ/ε₀** in electrostatics. Each Cartesian component of **A** satisfies an independent Poisson equation!

## 2.3 Biot–Savart Law Expressed via A

The Biot–Savart Law for **B** due to a current distribution is:

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3} \, dV'$$

By integrating carefully, the vector potential formulation emerges:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \, dV'$$

This is **simpler** than the Biot–Savart law for **B** because there is **no cross product** involved — only a scalar 1/r term.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where is A Used?

- **Antenna theory:** Radiation fields of antennas are derived via **A** before taking the curl to get **E** and **B**.
- **Superconductivity:** London equations are written in terms of **A**; flux quantization uses **∮A·dl = nΦ₀**.
- **Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC):** Inductance calculations for complex geometries use **A**.
- **Particle accelerators & electron microscopy:** Vector potential determines canonical momentum **p = mv + qA**.
- **Quantum devices (SQUIDs, qubits):** Aharonov–Bohm effect relies directly on **A**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Magnetostatics Vector Potential)

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ | Fundamental definition; always true |
| 2 | $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV'$ | Volume current distribution |
| 3 | $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \oint \frac{I \, d\mathbf{l}'}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}$ | Line current (filamentary) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{q \mathbf{v}}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}$ | Point charge in motion |
| 5 | $\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$ | Vector Poisson's equation (Coulomb gauge) |
| 6 | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$ | Coulomb gauge condition (magnetostatics) |
| 7 | $\mathbf{A}' = \mathbf{A} + \nabla \psi$ | Gauge transformation |
| 8 | $\Phi = \oint \mathbf{A} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$ | Magnetic flux via Stokes' theorem |
| 9 | $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^2} \, \mathbf{m} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}}$ | Magnetic dipole at far field |
| 10 | $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2(R^2 + z^2)^{3/2}} \hat{\phi}$ | N-turn finite solenoid on axis |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vector Potential from the Biot–Savart Law

**Goal:** Show that $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV'$ leads to $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ that is consistent with the Biot–Savart law.

**Step 1:** Begin with the vector calculus identity:

$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) = -\frac{(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3}$$

This holds when the gradient is taken with respect to **r** (the observation point), treating **r'** (the source point) as a constant.

**Step 2:** Take the curl of the proposed **A**:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \nabla \times \left( \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) dV'$$

Since **J(r')** is independent of **r**, it can be pulled out of the curl:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) dV'$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the identity from Step 1:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times \left( -\frac{\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}'}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3} \right) dV'$$

**Step 4:** Reverse the cross-product order using **a × (−b) = −(a × b) = b × a**:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3} dV'$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the right-hand side as exactly the **Biot–Savart Law**:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vector Poisson's Equation from Ampère's Law

**Step 1:** Start with magnetostatic Ampère's Law:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute **B = ∇×A** (definition of vector potential):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the BAC-CAB vector identity:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{A}$$

**Step 4:** Impose the Coulomb gauge condition **∇·A = 0** (this is a valid choice because of gauge freedom):

$$\nabla(0) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnetic Flux via Stokes' Theorem

**Step 1:** Magnetic flux through a surface **S** bounded by closed curve **C** is:

$$\Phi = \int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute **B = ∇×A**:

$$\Phi = \int_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) \cdot d\mathbf{S}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stokes' theorem to convert the surface integral of a curl into a line integral:

$$\int_S (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \oint_C \mathbf{A} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$$

**Step 4:** Hence:

$$\boxed{\Phi = \oint_C \mathbf{A} \cdot d\mathbf{l}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relation is the foundation of **magnetic flux quantization in superconductors** and is used in SQUID devices.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Vector Potential of an Infinite Solenoid

**Setup:** Consider an infinitely long solenoid of radius **R**, with **n** turns per unit length, carrying current **I**.

**Step 1:** By symmetry, **A** must point along the **azimuthal direction** $\hat{\phi}$, and depend only on the radial distance **s** from the axis.

**Step 2:** Inside the solenoid (s < R), the magnetic field is uniform: **B = μ₀nI ẑ**.

**Step 3:** Using **B = ∇×A** in cylindrical coordinates:

$$\mathbf{B} = -\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial z} \hat{\mathbf{s}} + \frac{1}{s}\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

**Step 4:** Since **A** has no **z**-dependence, the $\hat{\mathbf{s}}$ component vanishes. So:

$$B_z = \frac{1}{s}\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} = \mu_0 n I$$

**Step 5:** Integrate with respect to **s**:

$$s A_\phi = \mu_0 n I \cdot \frac{s^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 6:** Apply boundary condition: **A** must be finite at **s = 0**, so **C₁ = 0**:

$$A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I s}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Outside the solenoid (s > R), the magnetic field is zero, so:

$$\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} = 0 \implies s A_\phi = C_2 \implies A_\phi = \frac{C_2}{s}$$

**Step 8:** Matching at **s = R**: $\frac{\mu_0 n I R}{2} = \frac{C_2}{R}$, giving **C₂ = (μ₀nIR²)/2**:

$$\boxed{A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2 s}, \quad s > R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Outside the solenoid, **B = 0** but **A ≠ 0**. This is the classical setup that demonstrates the **Aharonov–Bohm effect** — a question frequently asked in KTU ESE!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Current, Vector Potential, and Magnetic Field

The following Mermaid flow chart depicts the conceptual architecture linking source currents, the vector potential, and the resulting magnetic field in the Coulomb gauge framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Current Distribution J r prime] -->|Biot-Savart Integral| B[Vector Potential A r]
    B -->|Curl Operation B equals curl A| C[Magnetic Flux Density B r]
    B -->|Vector Poisson Eq Laplacian A = mu0 J| D[Inhomogeneous PDE Form]
    C -->|Gauss Law for Magnetism curl B = 0| E[Closed Loop Field Lines]
    C -->|Stokes Theorem Phi = line integral of A dot dl| F[Magnetic Flux Phi]
    G[Gauge Choice: divergence of A = 0] --> B
    H[Physical Constants: mu0 = 4 pi times 10 to power minus 7] --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Vector Potential for Standard Geometries

This Mermaid block diagram maps how **A** is derived for various classical geometries encountered in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source[Current Source Type] -->|Volume Current J r| V1[Volume Integral of J over r prime times 1 over r]
    Source -->|Line Current I dl| V2[Closed Line Integral of I dl over r]
    Source -->|Surface Current K| V3[Surface Integral of K over r prime times 1 over r]
    Source -->|Magnetic Dipole m| V4[mu0 over 4 pi times m cross r hat over r squared]
    Source -->|Infinite Solenoid| V5[mu0 n I s over 2 inside and mu0 n I R squared over 2 s outside]
    V1 --> Result[A r - Vector Potential Field]
    V2 --> Result
    V3 --> Result
    V4 --> Result
    V5 --> Result
    Result -->|Take Curl| BField[B r - Magnetic Flux Density]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Gauge Freedom Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OriginalA[Original Vector Potential A] -->|Add Gradient of psi| NewA[New Vector Potential A prime]
    NewA -->|Take Curl| BCheck[Same Magnetic Field B]
    OriginalA -->|Take Curl| BOriginal[Same Magnetic Field B]
    BCheck --> Compare[Identical B Field - Gauge Invariance]
    BOriginal --> Compare
    Compare --> Choice[Gauge Choice Determines Laplacian Form]
    Choice -->|Coulomb: div A = 0| PoissonA[Laplacian A = minus mu0 J]
    Choice -->|Lorenz for time varying| LorenzA[Box Operator A = minus mu0 J]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define magnetic vector potential. Derive the relation between magnetic flux density **B** and vector potential **A**.**

**Model Answer:**

The magnetic vector potential **A** is a vector field defined such that its curl gives the magnetic flux density **B**:

$$\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$$

**Derivation of the relation:** Since **∇·B = 0** (Gauss's law for magnetism, no magnetic monopoles), and using the vector identity **∇·(∇×A) ≡ 0**, we can always find a vector field **A** such that **B = ∇×A**. This is because the divergence of the curl of *any* vector field is identically zero, guaranteeing **∇·B = 0** is automatically satisfied. **A** has units of **Wb/m**, and gauge freedom allows **A' = A + ∇ψ** without changing **B**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**State and explain the significance of the Coulomb gauge condition in magnetostatics.**

**Model Answer:**

The Coulomb gauge condition is:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$$

**Significance:**
- It is a *valid* choice of gauge allowed by the freedom **A' = A + ∇ψ** (provided **∇²ψ = 0**).
- Under this gauge, the vector Poisson equation simplifies to **∇²A = −μ₀J**.
- Each Cartesian component of **A** then satisfies an independent scalar Poisson equation, which has the well-known solution $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV'$.
- It decouples **A** from the scalar potential **V** in the magnetostatic limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for the magnetic vector potential **A** due to a volume current distribution **J(r')** using the Biot–Savart law.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the Biot–Savart Law:** The magnetic flux density due to a volume current distribution is:

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3} \, dV'$$

**Step 2 — Use the gradient identity:**

$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) = -\frac{\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}'}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3}$$

This allows rewriting the Biot–Savart integrand as:

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = -\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) dV'$$

**Step 3 — Use the curl-of-a-product identity** for a constant vector **J**:

$$\nabla \times \left( \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = -\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \nabla \times \left( \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \right) dV'$$

**Step 4 — Exchange the order of curl and integration:**

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \nabla \times \left[ -\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV' \right]$$

**Step 5 — Identify the bracketed quantity as A:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \, dV'}$$

Hence **B = ∇×A** is verified. ∎

---

**(b) Derive the vector potential for a magnetic dipole of moment **m** located at the origin. Hence obtain **B** at a distant point.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Express the magnetic moment:** A magnetic dipole consists of a small current loop of area **da** with current **I**, such that:

$$\mathbf{m} = I \, d\mathbf{a}$$

**Step 2 — Vector potential of a current loop:** For a small loop at origin, the vector potential at point **r** is:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{m} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Justification (sketch):** Treat the loop as a current **I** flowing in a tiny closed path. Using the formula for line currents and expanding for small loop size (to leading order), the cross product with the position vector picks up the moment **m = I·da**:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{I \, d\mathbf{a} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{r^2} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^2} \, (\mathbf{m} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}})$$

**Step 4 — Compute B = ∇×A:** Using spherical coordinates and the identity **∇ × (m × r̂ / r²)**, after differentiation one obtains the standard dipole field:

$$\mathbf{B} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^3} \left[ 3(\mathbf{m} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}})\hat{\mathbf{r}} - \mathbf{m} \right]$$

This is the classic **magnetic dipole field** at far distances, identical in form to the electric dipole field of electrostatics with **m ↔ p** and **μ₀ ↔ 1/ε₀**. ∎

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) Starting from the magnetostatic Ampère's law, derive the vector Poisson equation for the magnetic vector potential.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ampère's Law (magnetostatic form):**

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute B = ∇×A:**

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the BAC–CAB identity:**

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 4 — Impose the Coulomb gauge ∇·A = 0:**

$$-\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}}$$

This is the **vector Poisson equation**. In Cartesian coordinates, each component (Ax, Ay, Az) satisfies the scalar Poisson equation **∇²Aᵢ = −μ₀Jᵢ**, which has the standard solution given in part (a) of Question A. ∎

---

**(b) Find the vector potential inside and outside a long solenoid of radius **R** having **n** turns per unit length and carrying current **I**.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Symmetry:** By the cylindrical symmetry of an infinitely long solenoid, **A** must be along the azimuthal direction $\hat{\phi}$ and depend only on the radial coordinate **s**:

$$\mathbf{A} = A_\phi(s) \hat{\phi}$$

**Step 2 — Known B field:** Inside (s < R): **B = μ₀nI ẑ**; Outside (s > R): **B = 0**.

**Step 3 — Curl in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{s}\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

(since **A** has no **z** dependence, the $\hat{\mathbf{s}}$ term vanishes).

**Step 4 — Inside the solenoid (s < R):**

$$\frac{1}{s}\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} = \mu_0 n I$$

Integrate:

$$s A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I s^2}{2} \implies \boxed{A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I s}{2}, \quad s < R}$$

(The integration constant vanishes because **A** must remain finite at **s = 0**.)

**Step 5 — Outside the solenoid (s > R):**

$$\frac{1}{s}\frac{\partial (s A_\phi)}{\partial s} = 0 \implies s A_\phi = C_2 \implies A_\phi = \frac{C_2}{s}$$

**Step 6 — Boundary matching at s = R:**

$$\frac{\mu_0 n I R}{2} = \frac{C_2}{R} \implies C_2 = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2 s}, \quad s > R}$$

**Conclusion:** Outside the solenoid, **B = 0** but **A ≠ 0**. This result is the classical demonstration of the physical reality of the vector potential — leading to the Aharonov–Bohm effect. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Coulomb gauge:** When deriving the vector Poisson equation, students often write **∇(∇·A) − ∇²A = μ₀J** and forget to invoke **∇·A = 0**. Without this step, the final equation is *incorrect* (4 marks lost).
> 2. **Sign errors in the Biot–Savart derivation:** The negative sign in $\nabla(1/r) = -(r-r')/r^3$ is frequently missed, leading to a sign flip in the final answer.
> 3. **Units inconsistency:** Writing **A** in Tesla instead of **Wb/m** loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Boundary condition omission:** For the infinite solenoid, students forget that **A** must be finite at **s = 0**, missing the justification for why the constant of integration vanishes inside.
> 5. **Confusing line vs volume current formulas:** Mixing **∮(I dl/r)** with **∫(J/r)dV** is a common error in problems involving finite-length wires vs current-carrying volumes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Magnetic Vector Potential A** is defined via **B = ∇×A**.
- ✅ The definition **exists** because **∇·B = 0** and **∇·(∇×A) ≡ 0**.
- ✅ **Units of A:** Weber per meter (Wb/m).
- ✅ **Volume current formula:** $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} dV'$
- ✅ **Line current formula:** $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \oint \frac{I \, d\mathbf{l}'}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}$
- ✅ **Vector Poisson's equation (Coulomb gauge):** **∇²A = −μ₀J**
- ✅ **Coulomb gauge:** **∇·A = 0**
- ✅ **Gauge transformation:** **A' = A + ∇ψ** (does not change **B**).
- ✅ **Magnetic flux:** $\Phi = \oint_C \mathbf{A} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$ (via Stokes' theorem).
- ✅ **Magnetic dipole:** $\mathbf{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^2} (\mathbf{m} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}})$
- ✅ **Infinite solenoid:** $A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I s}{2}$ inside; $A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2s}$ outside.
- ✅ **Aharonov–Bohm effect:** Demonstrates that **A** is physically real, not just a mathematical convenience — even where **B = 0**, quantum particles are influenced by **A**.
- ✅ **A is not unique** — gauge freedom must be explicitly stated in derivations.
- ✅ **Compare/contrast with V:** Electric potential **V** is a *scalar* (gradient gives **E**); magnetic vector potential **A** is a *vector* (curl gives **B**).
- ✅ **Constant to remember:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetostatics in Material Media: Force on a Charged Particle Due to a Magnetic Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetostatics in Material Media: Force on a Charged Particle Due to a Magnetic Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **magnetic force on a charged particle** moving through a magnetic field in a material medium is governed by the **Lorentz Magnetic Force Law**. When a particle of charge $q$ moves with velocity $\vec{v}$ through a region containing magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ (which, in a material medium, is related to the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ by $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$, where $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ is the permeability of the medium), the force experienced is:

$$\vec{F}_m = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

In a material medium, the **total force** on the particle (combining both electric and magnetic contributions) becomes the **Lorentz Force**:

$$\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In magnetostatics, since $\partial/\partial t = 0$, the electric field $\vec{E} = 0$ for purely magnetic interactions. Therefore, the dominant force on a moving charged particle is purely magnetic: $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$. The presence of the material medium modifies $\vec{B}$ through the permeability $\mu$.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value (SI Units) |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| Relative permeability (vacuum) | $\mu_r$ | 1 (dimensionless) |
| Permeability of medium | $\mu$ | $\mu_0 \mu_r$ H/m |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e$ | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The River & Boat Analogy:** Imagine a stationary charged particle as a **boat tied to the dock**. It feels no push. Now imagine the same boat moving through **still water** — the boat glides without resistance from water. But if the boat moves through a **river with a strong cross-current**, the water pushes the boat sideways. The river current is analogous to the magnetic field $\vec{B}$, and the boat's forward motion is the particle's velocity $\vec{v}$. The sideways push is the magnetic force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.
>
> **Key Insight:** A stationary charge feels **nothing**. A charge moving *parallel* to $\vec{B}$ also feels **nothing**. Only motion *perpendicular* (or at an angle) to $\vec{B}$ produces a magnetic push — and that push is *always perpendicular to both* $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.

## 1.4 Why Material Media Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **vacuum**, $\vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{H}$. But in a **material medium** (ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, diamagnetic), the magnetic field inside the material is amplified or reduced by the relative permeability $\mu_r$:
> $$\vec{B} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H} = \mu \vec{H}$$
> This means the *same* velocity $\vec{v}$ and charge $q$ produce a **stronger or weaker** force depending on whether the medium concentrates (e.g., iron, $\mu_r \gg 1$) or weakens (e.g., bismuth, $\mu_r < 1$) the magnetic flux.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the Magnetic Force — Structured Logic

1. **Velocity Dependence:** A charge at rest ($\vec{v} = 0$) experiences **zero** magnetic force. The force exists only for moving charges.
2. **Directionality:** $\vec{F}_m \perp \vec{v}$ and $\vec{F}_m \perp \vec{B}$. The force is always perpendicular to the plane formed by $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.
3. **No Work Done:** Since $\vec{F}_m \cdot d\vec{r} = \vec{F}_m \cdot \vec{v}\, dt = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{v}\, dt = 0$ (because cross product is orthogonal to its operands), magnetic forces **change direction** but never the **kinetic energy** of the particle.
4. **Right-Hand Rule:** The direction of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ follows the right-hand rule (curling fingers from $\vec{v}$ to $\vec{B}$, thumb gives direction). For a *negative* charge, the force reverses direction.
5. **Material Medium Effect:** In a medium, $\vec{B}$ is amplified by $\mu_r$, so $|\vec{F}_m|$ scales linearly with $\mu_r$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic force on moving charge | $\vec{F}_m = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | Lorentz magnetic force |
| 2 | Lorentz total force | $\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | Full force on charge |
| 3 | B in material medium | $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$ | Constitutive relation |
| 4 | Magnitude of force | $\vert \vec{F}_m \vert = qvB\sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ |
| 5 | Radius of circular motion | $r = \dfrac{mv_{\perp}}{qB}$ | In uniform $\vec{B}$, perpendicular injection |
| 6 | Cyclotron angular frequency | $\omega_c = \dfrac{qB}{m}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Cyclotron frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Period of revolution | $T = \dfrac{2\pi m}{qB}$ | Independent of $v$ and $r$ |
| 9 | Kinetic energy in circular motion | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{q^2 B^2 r^2}{2m}$ | Constant — no work done |
| 10 | Pitch of helix (along $\vec{B}$) | $p = v_{\parallel} T = \dfrac{2\pi m v_{\parallel}}{qB}$ | Distance per revolution |
| 11 | Velocity selector condition | $E = vB \implies v = \dfrac{E}{B}$ | Crossed $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ fields |
| 12 | Hall voltage | $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{qnd}$ | Hall effect in conductors/semiconductors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units:** Force is in Newtons (N), charge in Coulombs (C), velocity in m/s, magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ in Tesla (T). Note that $1\,\text{T} = 1\,\text{V·s/m}^2 = 1\,\text{kg/(A·s}^2\text{)}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cyclotrons & Particle Accelerators:** Charged particles are guided in circular paths by magnetic fields, enabling nuclear physics research and medical isotope production.
- **Mass Spectrometers:** By measuring the radius $r$ of charged ions in a known $\vec{B}$, the mass-to-charge ratio is determined, identifying chemical compositions.
- **Hall Effect Sensors:** Used to measure magnetic field strength, current sensing in power systems, and contactless position sensing in automotive and industrial automation.
- **Magnetic Confinement (Tokamaks):** Plasma in fusion reactors is confined by helical magnetic field lines.
- **Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs) & Electron Microscopes:** Electron beams are steered and focused using magnetic deflection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion in a Uniform Magnetic Field

**Given:** A particle of mass $m$, charge $q$, moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a region of uniform magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$.

**Find:** The trajectory equation.

### Step 1 — Write Newton's Second Law

The magnetic force is the only force acting (assuming no electric field and no gravity):

$$m\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

### Step 2 — Decompose Velocity into Components

Let $\vec{B} = B\hat{z}$ (along z-axis). Decompose $\vec{v}$ into components parallel and perpendicular to $\vec{B}$:

$$\vec{v} = v_x \hat{x} + v_y \hat{y} + v_z \hat{z}$$

$$\vec{v}_{\perp} = v_x \hat{x} + v_y \hat{y}, \quad \vec{v}_{\parallel} = v_z \hat{z}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Cross Product

$$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (v_x \hat{x} + v_y \hat{y} + v_z \hat{z}) \times B\hat{z}$$

Using $\hat{x} \times \hat{z} = -\hat{y}$, $\hat{y} \times \hat{z} = \hat{x}$, $\hat{z} \times \hat{z} = 0$:

$$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = B(-v_x \hat{y} + v_y \hat{x})$$

### Step 4 — Equate Components

$$m\frac{dv_x}{dt} = qBv_y$$

$$m\frac{dv_y}{dt} = -qBv_x$$

$$m\frac{dv_z}{dt} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Integrate the z-Component

Since $dv_z/dt = 0$:

$$v_z = v_{\parallel} = \text{constant}$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Coupled x-y Equations

Differentiate the x-equation and substitute from the y-equation:

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} = \frac{qB}{m}\frac{dv_y}{dt} = -\left(\frac{qB}{m}\right)^2 v_x$$

This is the equation of a **simple harmonic oscillator**:

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} + \omega_c^2 v_x = 0, \quad \text{where } \omega_c = \frac{qB}{m}$$

### Step 7 — General Solution

$$v_x(t) = v_{\perp}\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)$$

$$v_y(t) = -v_{\perp}\sin(\omega_c t + \phi)$$

### Step 8 — Trajectory

Integrating once more with appropriate initial conditions:

$$x(t) = \frac{v_{\perp}}{\omega_c}\sin(\omega_c t + \phi)$$

$$y(t) = \frac{v_{\perp}}{\omega_c}\cos(\omega_c t + \phi)$$

$$z(t) = v_{\parallel} t + z_0$$

This represents **circular motion in the x-y plane** superimposed on **uniform translation along z** — a **helical trajectory**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radius and Frequency of Circular Motion

**Given:** A particle injected perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, so $v_{\parallel} = 0$ and $v = v_{\perp}$.

### Step 1 — Centripetal Force Balance

The magnetic force provides the centripetal acceleration:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Radius

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

### Step 3 — Time Period

The particle travels a circumference $2\pi r$ at speed $v$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v} = \frac{2\pi m}{qB}$$

### Step 4 — Cyclotron Frequency

$$f_c = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{qB}{2\pi m}, \quad \omega_c = 2\pi f_c = \frac{qB}{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Observation:** Notice that the period $T$ is **independent** of the particle's speed and radius. This is why cyclotrons can accelerate particles to high energies without adjusting the frequency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Selector (Wien Filter)

**Setup:** Crossed electric and magnetic fields. A particle entering with velocity $\vec{v}$ experiences $\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.

### Step 1 — Condition for Straight-Line (Force-Free) Motion

For the particle to pass undeflected, total force must vanish:

$$q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) = 0$$

$$\vec{E} = -(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

### Step 2 — Magnitude

If $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$ and $\vec{E} \perp \vec{B}$ with $\vec{E} \perp \vec{v}$:

$$E = vB$$

$$\boxed{v = \frac{E}{B}}$$

Only particles with this exact velocity pass through; others are deflected. This device is the **velocity selector**, used as a pre-filter in mass spectrometers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pitch of Helical Motion

### Step 1 — Definition

The **pitch** is the axial distance traveled per complete revolution:

$$p = v_{\parallel} \cdot T$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Period

$$p = v_{\parallel} \cdot \frac{2\pi m}{qB}$$

$$\boxed{p = \frac{2\pi m v_{\parallel}}{qB}}$$

This is critical for designing **magnetic bottle** confinement geometries and **plasma physics** devices.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Hall Effect (Force in Material Media)

**Setup:** A current-carrying conductor of thickness $d$ placed in a transverse magnetic field $\vec{B}$.

### Step 1 — Drift Velocity

Electrons drift with velocity $v_d = I/(nqA)$ where $n$ is carrier density, $A$ is cross-section.

### Step 2 — Magnetic Force on Carriers

$$F_m = qv_d B$$

### Step 3 — Charge Accumulation → Hall Voltage

Electrons accumulate on one face, creating a transverse electric field $\vec{E}_H$ that opposes further accumulation:

$$qE_H = qv_d B \implies E_H = v_d B$$

### Step 4 — Hall Voltage

$$V_H = E_H \cdot d = v_d B d = \frac{IB}{qnd}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The Hall effect allows direct measurement of carrier type (positive/negative), carrier density $n$, and magnetic field strength. Modern Hall sensors are ubiquitous in brushless DC motors, wheel-speed sensors, and smartphone compasses.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**State and explain the Lorentz force equation for a charged particle moving in an electromagnetic field. How is this equation modified when the particle is in a material medium?**

**Model Answer:**

The Lorentz force on a charged particle $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in the presence of both electric field $\vec{E}$ and magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ is:

$$\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

The first term $q\vec{E}$ is the **electrostatic force**, independent of velocity. The second term $q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ is the **magnetic force**, which depends on the velocity and is perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.

**In a material medium:** The magnetic flux density is modified by the medium's permeability $\mu$:

$$\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H}$$

Hence the magnetic force becomes:

$$\vec{F}_m = q\mu_0 \mu_r (\vec{v} \times \vec{H})$$

In ferromagnetic media ($\mu_r \gg 1$), the force is greatly amplified, while in diamagnetic media ($\mu_r < 1$), it is weakened.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**Show that the magnetic force on a moving charge does no work. Why, then, does a magnetic field influence the trajectory of the particle?**

**Model Answer:**

The instantaneous power delivered by the magnetic force is:

$$P = \vec{F}_m \cdot \vec{v} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{v}$$

Since the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{v}$, their dot product is zero:

$$(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \implies P = 0$$

Therefore, the magnetic force does **no work** and cannot change the **kinetic energy** (speed) of the particle.

However, although speed remains constant, the **direction** of velocity continuously changes because $\vec{F}_m \perp \vec{v}$. This redirection causes the particle to follow a **curved trajectory** (circular or helical), even though no energy is transferred. Work is required to *change direction*, but in magnetic deflection, that work is supplied by whatever agency maintains the current producing $\vec{B}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**A.** (a) Derive an expression for the radius and time period of the circular path traced by a charged particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field. **7 marks**

**(b) An electron is accelerated through a potential difference of 200 V and then enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.01 T perpendicular to its direction of motion. Calculate (i) the speed of the electron, (ii) the radius of its circular path, and (iii) the time period of revolution. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Setup:** Consider a particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ entering a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ with velocity $\vec{v}$ perpendicular to $\vec{B}$.

The magnetic force $qvB$ acts perpendicular to $\vec{v}$ and provides the centripetal force for circular motion:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Solving for the **radius**:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

This shows $r \propto v$ and $r \propto 1/B$.

The **time period** is the time to traverse circumference $2\pi r$ at speed $v$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v} = \frac{2\pi}{v} \cdot \frac{mv}{qB} = \frac{2\pi m}{qB}$$

$$\boxed{r = \frac{mv}{qB}, \quad T = \frac{2\pi m}{qB}}$$

Note: $T$ is **independent** of $v$ and $r$.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- Accelerating potential $V = 200$ V
- Magnetic field $B = 0.01$ T
- Electron: $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**(i) Speed of the electron:**

Work done by accelerating potential = Kinetic energy gained:

$$eV = \frac{1}{2}m_e v^2$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 200}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{6.408 \times 10^{-17}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{7.034 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 8.39 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m/s}}$$

**(ii) Radius of circular path:**

$$r = \frac{m_e v}{eB} = \frac{9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 8.39 \times 10^{6}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.01}$$

$$r = \frac{7.642 \times 10^{-24}}{1.602 \times 10^{-21}} = 4.77 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{r \approx 4.77\ \text{mm}}$$

**(iii) Time period:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi m_e}{eB} = \frac{2\pi \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.01}$$

$$T = \frac{5.724 \times 10^{-30}}{1.602 \times 10^{-21}} = 3.57 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 3.57\ \text{ns}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**B.** (a) Explain the principle and working of a velocity selector. Derive the condition for a charged particle to pass through it undeflected. **7 marks**

**(b) A proton, a deuteron (same charge as proton, mass = $2m_p$), and an alpha particle (charge $2e$, mass $4m_p$) are accelerated through the same potential difference and then enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their motion. Find the ratio of their radii of circular paths. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Principle:** A velocity selector (Wien filter) uses **crossed electric and magnetic fields** such that the electric and magnetic forces on a moving charge are equal and opposite, resulting in **zero net force** for a specific velocity.

**Setup:** $\vec{E}$ along $+y$ axis, $\vec{B}$ along $+z$ axis. A positive charge $q$ moves along $+x$ with velocity $\vec{v}$.

- **Electric force:** $\vec{F}_e = q\vec{E} = qE\hat{y}$
- **Magnetic force:** $\vec{F}_m = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = qvB(\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) = -qvB\hat{y}$

**Condition for zero net force:**

$$\vec{F}_e + \vec{F}_m = 0$$

$$qE - qvB = 0$$

$$\boxed{v = \frac{E}{B}}$$

Only particles with this exact speed travel in a **straight line** and exit through the aperture. Faster particles are deflected one way; slower particles are deflected the other way. This device is used as a pre-filter in Bainbridge mass spectrometers.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:** All particles accelerated through the same potential $V$ and enter $\vec{B}$ perpendicularly.

**Step 1 — Speed after acceleration:**

Energy conservation: $qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, so $v = \sqrt{2qV/m}$.

**Step 2 — Radius of circular path:**

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB} = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}} = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2mV}{q}}$$

Thus $r \propto \sqrt{m/q}$.

**Step 3 — Calculate for each particle:**

| Particle | Mass | Charge | $m/q$ | $r \propto \sqrt{m/q}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proton ($p$) | $m_p$ | $e$ | $m_p/e$ | $\sqrt{m_p/e}$ |
| Deuteron ($d$) | $2m_p$ | $e$ | $2m_p/e$ | $\sqrt{2m_p/e}$ |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | $4m_p$ | $2e$ | $2m_p/e$ | $\sqrt{2m_p/e}$ |

**Step 4 — Ratio:**

$$r_p : r_d : r_\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{m_p}{e}} : \sqrt{\frac{2m_p}{e}} : \sqrt{\frac{2m_p}{e}}$$

$$= 1 : \sqrt{2} : \sqrt{2}$$

$$\boxed{r_p : r_d : r_\alpha = 1 : \sqrt{2} : \sqrt{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Forgetting that the radius depends on $\sqrt{m/q}$, not $m/q$. Many students write $r \propto m/q$ directly — this is wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{m/q}$.
> 2. **Sign of charge:** When computing $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$, students often drop the negative sign for electrons. Always verify: for negative charges, force direction reverses.
> 3. **Units:** Always express $B$ in Tesla, $v$ in m/s, $m$ in kg, $q$ in Coulombs. Mixing CGS or "Weber" units causes numerical errors.
> 4. **Relativistic correction:** For high-speed electrons, $m$ should be replaced by relativistic mass $\gamma m_e$. At $v \approx 8 \times 10^6$ m/s, $\gamma \approx 1.0004$, so non-relativistic is fine. But for $v > 0.1c$, this matters.
> 5. **Direction in cyclotron:** Students sometimes state that $\vec{F}$ causes acceleration along $\vec{v}$. Reminder: $\vec{F} \perp \vec{v}$, so it only changes direction, not speed.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Lorentz magnetic force:** $\vec{F}_m = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ — always perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.
- **Static charge → zero magnetic force.** Motion is required.
- **No work done:** Magnetic forces change *direction* of velocity but not *magnitude*.
- **Material medium effect:** $\vec{B} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H}$; $\mu_r$ scales the force magnitude.
- **Circular motion** occurs when $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$: radius $r = mv/(qB)$, period $T = 2\pi m/(qB)$.
- **Helical motion** when $\vec{v}$ has components both parallel and perpendicular to $\vec{B}$.
- **Cyclotron frequency** $f_c = qB/(2\pi m)$ — independent of particle speed.
- **Pitch of helix:** $p = v_{\parallel} T = 2\pi m v_{\parallel}/(qB)$.
- **Velocity selector:** Crossed $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ fields; only $v = E/B$ passes undeflected.
- **Hall effect:** Moving carriers in $\vec{B}$ accumulate sideways, producing transverse voltage $V_H = IB/(qnd)$ — used for sensors and carrier-type identification.
- **Engineering applications:** Cyclotrons, mass spectrometers, MRI, Hall sensors, plasma confinement, CRT displays, electron microscopes, auroras, Van Allen belts.
- **Right-hand rule** governs the direction of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$; reverse for negative charges.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always include units, draw a clear diagram showing $\vec{v}$, $\vec{B}$, $\vec{F}$ directions, and verify $\vec{F} \perp \vec{v}$ in your final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Between Two Current Carrying Conductors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Force Between Two Current Carrying Conductors**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

When two current-carrying conductors are placed in proximity to one another, each conductor experiences a **mechanical force** due to the magnetic field produced by the other. This phenomenon is known as the **force between two current-carrying conductors**, and it is a direct macroscopic consequence of the **Biot–Savart Law** and the **Lorentz force principle** acting on current elements.

For two infinitely long, straight, parallel conductors separated by a distance $d$, carrying steady currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively, the magnitude of the force per unit length on either conductor is given by:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 \, I_1 \, I_2}{2\pi d}$$

where:
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space $= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$ (**H/m**)
- $I_1, I_2$ = currents in the two conductors (Amperes)
- $d$ = perpendicular distance between the conductors (meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> This topic is foundational for the **SI definition of the Ampere**, the operation of **bus-bars in substations**, **overhead transmission line sag calculations**, and the working principle of the **current balance / Ampere's force experiment**.

## 1.2 Direction of the Force — Right-Hand Rule

The direction of the force depends on the **relative direction of the currents**:

| Current Direction Configuration | Nature of Force | Physical Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Currents **parallel** (same direction) | **Attractive** | Magnetic field of one conductor reinforces current-flow alignment |
| Currents **anti-parallel** (opposite direction) | **Repulsive** | Magnetic field of one conductor opposes current-flow alignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine two long, thin, flexible water hoses lying parallel on the ground, both ejecting water in the same direction. The pressure waves generated by one hose push against the other, drawing them slightly together — this is the "attractive" case. Now reverse the flow in one hose so that the two streams collide head-on: the water jets slam into each other, pushing the hoses apart — this is the "repulsive" case. The magnetic field lines act exactly like these invisible pressure waves, and the current in the wire acts like the moving water jet.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$ (permeability of free space)
> - $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$ (permittivity of free space)
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ (speed of light, where $c^2 = 1/(\mu_0 \epsilon_0)$)
> - $1 \ \text{Ampere} \equiv$ the constant current which, if maintained in two straight parallel conductors of infinite length, of negligible circular cross-section, and placed **1 meter apart in vacuum**, would produce a force of exactly $2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N}$ per meter of length.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Force Originates

The phenomenon unfolds in **two sequential stages**, each of which is a direct consequence of laws covered earlier in Module 3:

1. **Stage 1 — Magnetic Field Generation:**
   The steady current $I_1$ in conductor 1 produces a magnetic field $\vec{B_1}$ in the surrounding space. By the **Biot–Savart Law** (and equivalently, by Ampere's Circuital Law for a long straight wire), the magnitude of this field at a perpendicular distance $d$ is:

   $$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 \, I_1}{2\pi d}$$

   The direction of $\vec{B_1}$ is given by the **right-hand grip rule** — curling the fingers of the right hand in the direction of current flow makes the thumb point along $\vec{B_1}$, which forms concentric circles around the wire.

2. **Stage 2 — Force on Conductor 2:**
   Conductor 2, carrying current $I_2$, lies within this magnetic field $\vec{B_1}$. Each infinitesimal length $d\vec{L}$ of conductor 2 experiences a **Lorentz force** $d\vec{F} = I_2 \, d\vec{L} \times \vec{B_1}$. For a straight conductor of length $L$ lying perpendicular to $\vec{B_1}$ (i.e., $\theta = 90^\circ$):

   $$F_2 = B_1 \, I_2 \, L = \left(\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d}\right) I_2 L$$

   $$\boxed{\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Attractive / Repulsive Behaviour

By **Newton's Third Law**, the force on conductor 1 due to conductor 2 is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the force on conductor 2 due to conductor 1. The qualitative direction can be derived by applying the right-hand rule twice:

- **Parallel Currents:** $\vec{B_1}$ at the location of conductor 2 points "into" or "out of" the page. Applying $I_2 \, \vec{L} \times \vec{B_1}$ (using the right-hand rule for cross products) yields a force vector directed **toward** conductor 1 → **Attraction**.

- **Anti-Parallel Currents:** Reversing $I_2$ flips the cross-product result by 180°, giving a force directed **away** from conductor 1 → **Repulsion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this physically intuitive?**
> Currents flowing in the same direction align their magnetic moments (just like two bar magnets placed end-to-end with the same pole orientation). Aligned magnetic moments attract. Currents in opposite directions create opposing magnetic moments, which repel — analogous to two bar magnets with like poles facing each other.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Symbol | Expression | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Magnetic field due to a long straight wire at distance $d$ | $B$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi d}$ | Tesla (T) |
| 2 | Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field | $F$ | $B I L \sin\theta$ | Newton (N) |
| 3 | Force per unit length between two parallel conductors | $F/L$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$ | N/m |
| 4 | Force between two parallel conductors (full length) | $F$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2\pi d}$ | N |
| 5 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | T·m/A (or H/m) |
| 6 | Relative permeability of medium | $\mu_r$ | $\mu/\mu_0$ (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | Force per unit length in a medium | $F/L$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$ | N/m |
| 8 | SI Definition of 1 Ampere | $F/L$ | $2 \times 10^{-7}$ when $I_1 = I_2 = 1 \ \text{A}$, $d = 1 \ \text{m}$ | N/m |
| 9 | Angle condition for maximum force | $\theta$ | $90^\circ$ (i.e., wire $\perp$ to $B$) | degrees |
| 10 | Force when current is parallel to $B$ | $F$ | $0$ (since $\sin 0^\circ = 0$) | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note — Real-World Utility:**
> This principle is exploited in:
> - **Bus-bar design** in electrical switchyards: the repulsive force during a short-circuit fault can be enormous and must be mechanically contained.
> - **Overhead transmission lines**: parallel conductors in a bundle attract each other; spacers maintain separation.
> - **Railgun and coilgun technology**: magnetic forces accelerate projectiles.
> - **Definition of the Ampere** (tied to $2 \times 10^{-7}$ N/m at 1 m separation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Force Per Unit Length Between Two Parallel Current-Carrying Conductors

### Setup

Consider two **infinitely long, straight, parallel conductors** placed in a homogeneous medium of permeability $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$:

- Conductor **A** carries a steady current $I_1$ (directed upward, $+\hat{z}$)
- Conductor **B** carries a steady current $I_2$ (directed upward, $+\hat{z}$ — same direction for attractive case)
- The perpendicular distance between them is $d$ (measured along the $+\hat{x}$ axis)

**Goal:** Derive the magnitude of the force per unit length on conductor B due to the magnetic field produced by conductor A.

### Step 1 — Magnetic Field at Conductor B Due to Conductor A

Apply the **Biot–Savart Law** for a long straight current-carrying conductor. The general expression for the magnetic field at a perpendicular distance $d$ from a long straight wire carrying current $I_1$ is:

$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r \, I_1}{2\pi d}$$

**Reasoning:** This result is most easily obtained from **Ampere's Circuital Law**:

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{L} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enclosed}}$$

Choosing a circular Amperian loop of radius $d$ centered on conductor A, the field $\vec{B}$ is tangential and uniform in magnitude over the loop. Therefore:

$$B_1 \cdot (2\pi d) = \mu_0 I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d}$$

(We drop $\mu_r$ here and assume free space; it can be inserted if the medium is non-vacuum.)

### Step 2 — Direction of the Magnetic Field

Using the **right-hand grip rule** (or right-hand rule for a straight wire): point the thumb of the right hand in the direction of $I_1$ ($+\hat{z}$). The curled fingers show the direction of $\vec{B_1}$ — it forms **counter-clockwise concentric circles** around conductor A when viewed from above (i.e., looking down the $-\hat{z}$ axis).

At the location of conductor B (which lies along $+\hat{x}$ from conductor A), the field $\vec{B_1}$ points along the $-\hat{y}$ axis.

$$\vec{B_1} = -\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d} \, \hat{y}$$

### Step 3 — Force on a Small Current Element of Conductor B

The differential force on a small current-carrying element $I_2 \, d\vec{L_2}$ of conductor B placed in the external field $\vec{B_1}$ is given by the **Lorentz force on a current element**:

$$d\vec{F} = I_2 \, d\vec{L_2} \times \vec{B_1}$$

The direction of $d\vec{L_2}$ is along $+\hat{z}$ (since $I_2$ flows upward). Therefore:

$$d\vec{L_2} = dz \, \hat{z}, \quad d\vec{F} = I_2 \, dz \, \hat{z} \times \left(-\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d} \, \hat{y}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Cross Product

Recall the right-hand-rule identity: $\hat{z} \times \hat{y} = -\hat{x}$.

Therefore:

$$d\vec{F} = I_2 \, dz \, \left(-\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d}\right) (\hat{z} \times \hat{y}) = I_2 \, dz \, \left(-\frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d}\right) (-\hat{x})$$

$$d\vec{F} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} \, dz \, \hat{x}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Over the Length of Conductor B

Integrate from $z = 0$ to $z = L$ to obtain the total force on a finite segment of length $L$:

$$\vec{F} = \int_0^L \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} \, dz \, \hat{x} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} \cdot L \, \hat{x}$$

The magnitude of the force:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2\pi d}}$$

And the force per unit length:

$$\boxed{\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Direction and the Attractive Nature

The result $\vec{F} = +\hat{x} \cdot (\text{positive scalar})$ means the force on conductor B points **toward conductor A** (which is in the $-\hat{x}$ direction relative to B). Therefore the force is **attractive**, confirming the rule that **parallel currents attract**.

### Step 7 — Generalize to a Medium of Permeability $\mu_r$

If the conductors are immersed in a medium of relative permeability $\mu_r$ (e.g., iron, ferrite), the result becomes:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$$

The direction rules (attractive / repulsive) remain unchanged.

## 3.2 Derivation of the SI Definition of the Ampere

**Given:** $F/L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N/m}$ when $I_1 = I_2 = 1 \ \text{A}$ and $d = 1 \ \text{m}$ in vacuum.

**Substitute into the force equation:**

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot (1)(1)}{2\pi (1)} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2\pi} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N/m} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the exact numerical identity and provides a clean, verifiable link between $\mu_0$ and the SI definition of the Ampere — a frequently asked derivation in KTU university examinations.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Two long parallel conductors, separated by a distance of **5 cm**, carry currents of **10 A** and **15 A** in the **same direction**. Find the force per unit length. Will the force be attractive or repulsive? (Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$.)

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the knowns:

$$I_1 = 10 \ \text{A}, \quad I_2 = 15 \ \text{A}, \quad d = 5 \ \text{cm} = 0.05 \ \text{m}$$

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 15}{2\pi \times 0.05}$$

Step 3 — Cancel $\pi$ and simplify:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 150}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{600 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$$

Step 4 — Determine direction:

Since $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow in the **same direction**, the force is **attractive**.

**Final Answer:** $\dfrac{F}{L} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$ (Attractive)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Interaction Between Two Parallel Conductors

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Conductor A: Current I1 in +Z direction] -->|Biot-Savart Law| B1[Magnetic Field B1 forms CCW circles]
    B1 -->|Field at location of Conductor B| C[Magnetic field vector B1 at distance d]
    C -->|Lorentz Force dF = I2 dL x B1| D[Force on Conductor B in -X direction]
    D -->|Newtons Third Law| E[Equal and Opposite Force on Conductor A in +X direction]
    E --> F[Net Result: Attractive Force between the two conductors]
    A -->|Same direction current rule| F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Logic for Attractive vs. Repulsive Force

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Two Parallel Conductors Separated by distance d] --> Q{Are Currents in the SAME direction?}
    Q -->|YES| R[Apply Right-Hand Rule twice: Force is ATTRACTIVE]
    Q -->|NO| S[Apply Right-Hand Rule twice: Force is REPULSIVE]
    R --> T[Final Formula: F divided by L equals mu0 I1 I2 over 2 pi d]
    S --> T
    T --> U{Medium Vacuum or Material?}
    U -->|Vacuum| V[F divided by L equals 4 pi x 10 power -7 times I1 I2 over 2 pi d]
    U -->|Material with mu_r| W[F divided by L equals mu0 mu_r I1 I2 over 2 pi d]
    V --> X[SI Ampere definition: F over L = 2 x 10 power -7 N per m]
    W --> X
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Engineering Application Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Force Between Two Parallel Conductors Principle] --> B[SI Definition of Ampere]
    A --> C[Bus Bar Short Circuit Force Calculation]
    A --> D[Overhead Transmission Line Bundle Conductors]
    A --> E[Railgun and Coilgun Launch Systems]
    A --> F[Current Balance Experimental Setup]
    A --> G[Tesla Coil Primary Secondary Coupling]
    A --> H[Deflection Yoke in CRT Displays]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Physical Setup (For Visualization)

```
        +Z (I1 = 10 A)
         │
         │  ← Conductor A
         │
         │←————— d = 5 cm —————→│
         │                      │
         │                      │  ← Conductor B
         │                      │
        +Z (I2 = 15 A)         +Z (I2 = 15 A)

    Magnetic field B1 at Conductor B location:
            B1 points INTO the page ( -Y direction )
    Force on Conductor B: F = I2 L x B1 → points TOWARD Conductor A
    Result: ATTRACTIVE FORCE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** In the diagram above, both currents point in the $+Z$ direction. By the right-hand rule, the field at conductor B (which is displaced along $+X$ from conductor A) points in the $-Y$ direction. The cross product $I_2 L \hat{z} \times B_1 (-\hat{y})$ evaluates to a vector in the $-X$ direction — i.e., toward conductor A. This confirms the **attractive** nature of the force.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the force between two infinitely long parallel current-carrying conductors. Hence define one Ampere.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When two infinitely long, straight, parallel conductors carry steady currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively, and are separated by a perpendicular distance $d$ in vacuum, each conductor experiences a force whose magnitude per unit length is given by:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$$

**(1 mark for the formula)**

The force is **attractive** if the currents flow in the **same direction** and **repulsive** if the currents flow in **opposite directions**. This can be verified using the right-hand rule applied twice (once to determine the field direction, once to evaluate the cross product $I \, d\vec{L} \times \vec{B}$).

**(1 mark for direction rule)**

**SI Definition of 1 Ampere:** One Ampere is defined as the constant current which, if maintained in two straight parallel conductors of infinite length and negligible circular cross-section, placed **1 metre apart in vacuum**, would produce a force of exactly $2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N}$ per metre of length between them.

**(1 mark for definition)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Two long parallel straight wires, separated by 10 cm in air, carry currents of 20 A and 30 A in the same direction. Calculate the force per unit length on each wire and specify its nature.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: $d = 10 \ \text{cm} = 0.1 \ \text{m}$, $I_1 = 20 \ \text{A}$, $I_2 = 30 \ \text{A}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$.

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20 \times 30}{2\pi \times 0.1} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 600}{0.2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{N/m}$$

**(2 marks for calculation)**

Since the currents are in the **same direction**, the force is **attractive**.

**(1 mark for direction)**

**Final Answer:** $F/L = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{N/m}$ (Attractive)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **Choice Option 1**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the magnetic field at a point due to an infinitely long straight current-carrying conductor using **Biot-Savart Law** or **Ampere's Circuital Law**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two long parallel conductors A and B, separated by a distance of 8 cm, carry currents of 5 A and 8 A respectively in the **same direction**. Find: **(i)** the force per unit length on each conductor, **(ii)** the direction of the force, and **(iii)** the force on a 2 m length of conductor A. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Magnetic Field of a Long Straight Wire (7 Marks)

**Statement of Biot–Savart Law:**

The magnetic field $d\vec{B}$ at a point P due to a current element $I \, d\vec{l}$ located at distance $r$ is:

$$d\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \frac{d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$$

**(1 mark for stating the law)**

**Setup:** Consider an infinitely long straight wire along the $y$-axis carrying current $I$. Let point P be at perpendicular distance $a$ from the wire (in the $x$-direction). Consider a current element $I \, d\vec{l}$ at position $y$.

**Geometry:** The angle $\theta$ between $d\vec{l}$ (along $\hat{y}$) and the position vector $\hat{r}$ (from element to P) is such that $\sin\theta = \frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 + y^2}}$ and $r = \sqrt{a^2 + y^2}$.

**(1 mark for geometry)**

**Substitution into Biot–Savart:**

$$dB = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \frac{dl \sin\theta}{r^2} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \frac{dy \cdot (a/\sqrt{a^2+y^2})}{a^2 + y^2} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \frac{a \, dy}{(a^2+y^2)^{3/2}}$$

**(2 marks for substitution and simplification)**

**Integration:** Integrate $y$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 I a}{4\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dy}{(a^2 + y^2)^{3/2}}$$

Using the standard integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{dy}{(a^2 + y^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{2}{a^2}$$

We obtain:

$$\boxed{B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi a}}$$

**(2 marks for integration and final result)**

**Direction:** Given by the right-hand grip rule; concentric circles around the wire.

**(1 mark for direction)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 8 \ \text{cm} = 0.08 \ \text{m}$, $I_1 = 5 \ \text{A}$, $I_2 = 8 \ \text{A}$, $L = 2 \ \text{m}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$.

**(i) Force per unit length (3 marks):**

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 8}{2\pi \times 0.08} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 40}{0.16} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$$

**(ii) Direction of the force (1 mark):** Currents are in the **same direction**, so the force is **attractive**.

**(iii) Force on 2 m length of conductor A (3 marks):**

$$F = \frac{F}{L} \times L = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F/L = 1 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$
- Direction: **Attractive**
- Total force on 2 m length: $F = 2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N}$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **Choice Option 2**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **force per unit length** between two long straight parallel current-carrying conductors. Hence, **define one Ampere** based on this force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two long parallel straight conductors are placed 6 cm apart in air. They carry currents of 12 A in the same direction and 8 A in the opposite direction respectively. Determine: **(i)** the magnetic field at a point exactly midway between the two conductors, **(ii)** the force per unit length on each conductor, and **(iii)** whether the force is attractive or repulsive. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Force Per Unit Length (7 Marks)

**Setup:** Consider two infinitely long parallel conductors carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$, separated by distance $d$ in vacuum.

**(1 mark for setup and figure description)**

**Stage 1:** Magnetic field at conductor 2 due to current in conductor 1:

$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d}$$

**(1 mark for field expression)**

**Stage 2:** Force on a small element $dL$ of conductor 2 in this field:

$$dF = B_1 I_2 \, dL = \frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi d} \cdot I_2 \, dL$$

**(2 marks for force on element and substitution)**

**Integrate** over the length $L$ of conductor 2:

$$F = \int_0^L \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} \, dL = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2\pi d}$$

**Per unit length:**

$$\boxed{\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}}$$

**(2 marks for integration and final formula)**

**Definition of 1 Ampere (1 mark):**
One Ampere is the constant current which, when flowing in each of two infinitely long parallel straight conductors of negligible cross-section placed 1 m apart in vacuum, produces a force of $2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N}$ per metre of length between them. This follows directly because:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1)(1)}{2\pi (1)} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N/m}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 6 \ \text{cm} = 0.06 \ \text{m}$, so midpoint distance from each conductor $= 3 \ \text{cm} = 0.03 \ \text{m}$, $I_1 = 12 \ \text{A}$ (say, upward), $I_2 = 8 \ \text{A}$ (downward, opposite direction).

**(i) Magnetic field at the midpoint (3 marks):**

Field due to conductor 1 at the midpoint:

$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi (0.03)} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{2\pi \times 0.03} = \frac{24 \times 10^{-7}}{0.03} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{T}$$

Field due to conductor 2 at the midpoint:

$$B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 I_2}{2\pi (0.03)} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 8}{2\pi \times 0.03} = \frac{16 \times 10^{-7}}{0.03} \approx 5.33 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{T}$$

**Since the currents are in opposite directions, the two magnetic fields at the midpoint point in the SAME direction** (by the right-hand rule). So they add:

$$B_{\text{total}} = B_1 + B_2 = 8 \times 10^{-5} + 5.33 \times 10^{-5} = 13.33 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{T} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{T}$$

**(ii) Force per unit length (2 marks):**

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12 \times 8}{2\pi \times 0.06} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 96}{0.12} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$$

**(iii) Nature of the force (2 marks):** Since $I_1$ and $I_2$ are in **opposite directions**, the force is **repulsive**.

**Final Answers:**
- $B_{\text{midpoint}} = 1.33 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{T}$
- $F/L = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{N/m}$
- Force is **repulsive**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $d$ must always be in **metres** (cm → m). Failure here costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sin\theta$:** Force is maximum when $\theta = 90^\circ$ (i.e., wire perpendicular to $B$). If the problem states an angle, $\sin\theta$ **must appear**.
> 3. **Direction logic error:** Students frequently state "attractive" without referencing the right-hand rule. Always state the **physical reason** — *currents in the same direction align magnetic moments and attract*.
> 4. **$\mu_0$ substitution errors:** Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **explicitly**, then cancel $4\pi$ with $2\pi$ early to avoid arithmetic mistakes.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in midpoint problems:** When currents are in **opposite directions**, the fields at the midpoint **add**; when in the **same direction**, they **subtract**. This is a classic conceptual trap.
> 6. **Mixing up "force on a wire" vs "force per unit length":** Read the question carefully — sometimes $L$ is given, sometimes not.
> 7. **Newton missing in the final answer unit:** Force is in **Newtons (N)**, force per unit length is in **N/m**. Examiners deduct marks for wrong units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ **Core Formula:** $F/L = \mu_0 I_1 I_2 / (2\pi d)$ — memorize verbatim with units.
- ✅ **Permeability constant:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{T·m/A}$.
- ✅ **Direction Rule (must remember):**
  - **Same direction currents** → **Attractive** force.
  - **Opposite direction currents** → **Repulsive** force.
- ✅ **SI Definition of 1 Ampere:** Two parallel wires 1 m apart in vacuum carrying 1 A each experience $2 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{N/m}$.
- ✅ **Maximum force condition:** $F_{\max} = BIL$ when the conductor is **perpendicular** to the magnetic field ($\theta = 90^\circ$).
- ✅ **Zero force condition:** Force is zero when current is **parallel** to $\vec{B}$ ($\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$).
- ✅ **Biot–Savart Law** is the foundation: $d\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \dfrac{d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$.
- ✅ **Ampere's Circuital Law** alternative for symmetric cases: $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}$.
- ✅ **Right-hand grip rule** determines field direction around a straight wire.
- ✅ **Right-hand palm rule / Fleming's left-hand rule** determines force direction on a current-carrying conductor.
- ✅ **Newton's Third Law:** Force on conductor 1 due to 2 = – (Force on conductor 2 due to 1) — always equal in magnitude, opposite in direction.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** bus-bar design, transmission line bundles, current balance (Ampere's experiment), railgun, CRT deflection, motor windings.
- ✅ **Common exam trick:** When wires are in a **medium** (not vacuum), replace $\mu_0$ with $\mu_0 \mu_r$ where $\mu_r$ is the relative permeability.
- ✅ **Numerical check:** Always verify that the order of magnitude of $F/L$ for typical currents (10 A, distances in cm) is around $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ N/m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Torque and Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Torque and Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Dipole Moment (m):** A vector quantity that measures the strength and orientation of a magnetic dipole. For a planar current loop of area **A** carrying steady current **I**, the magnetic dipole moment is defined as
> $$\vec{m} \;=\; I\,\vec{A} \;=\; I\,A\,\hat{n}$$
> where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal vector to the plane of the loop, whose direction is given by the right-hand rule with respect to the current direction.

**SI Unit:** Ampere·metre² (**A·m²**)

**Dimensional Formula:** $[\,M^{0}\,L^{2}\,A^{1}\,T^{0}\,]$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A current loop is the magnetic equivalent of an electric dipole. Any closed current-carrying circuit that is small enough to fit inside a region of uniform magnetic field behaves as a *magnetic dipole* characterized by the vector $\vec{m}$.

**Magnetic Torque ($\vec{\tau}$):** The rotational effect experienced by a magnetic dipole when placed in an external magnetic field, given by
$$\vec{\tau} \;=\; \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **compass needle** floating freely on water. The needle naturally rotates so that its "north" pole points toward the Earth's geographic north (which is actually a magnetic south pole). This rotation is caused by the Earth's magnetic field exerting a **torque** on the needle's magnetic dipole moment.

- The **current loop** is analogous to a tiny bar magnet — it has a *north* and *south* pole separated by a tiny distance.
- Just as a bar magnet experiences a turning force in a magnetic field, a current loop with magnetic moment $\vec{m}$ also experiences a turning force in field $\vec{B}$.
