- The loop is *lazy*: it wants to align itself so that $\vec{m}$ points along $\vec{B}$, reaching its **lowest energy** state.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** A magnetic dipole in a field behaves like a *sleeping cat* — it always wants to curl up (rotate) so that it lies parallel to the field lines, with $\vec{m}$ pointing along $\vec{B}$.

## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Context

| Application | Role of Magnetic Torque |
|---|---|
| **DC Electric Motor** | Torque on rotor windings produces mechanical rotation. |
| **Galvanometer** | Deflection of a coil indicates current strength. |
| **Electric Relays & Circuit Breakers** | Magnetic torque triggers switching action. |
| **MRI Imaging** | Alignment/relaxation of nuclear dipoles in strong B-field. |
| **Hard Disk Drives** | Write heads use dipole alignment principles. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The behavior of a magnetic dipole in an external field can be understood as a four-step logical chain:

- **Step 1 — The Setup:** A current loop of any shape (rectangular, circular, hexagonal) with steady current **I** is placed inside a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$.
- **Step 2 — Force Analysis:** Each infinitesimal current element $I\,d\vec{l}$ experiences a Lorentz force $d\vec{F} = I\,d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}$. When integrated over the entire closed loop, the **net force is zero**, but the forces are not collinear.
- **Step 3 — Net Torque Emerges:** Because the forces on opposite sides of the loop are equal, opposite, and non-collinear, they form a **mechanical couple** with non-zero moment arm. The result is a net torque.
- **Step 4 — Rotational Equilibrium:** The torque rotates the loop until $\vec{m}$ aligns with $\vec{B}$, at which point the system reaches **stable equilibrium** (minimum potential energy).

## 2.2 Why the Net Force Vanishes but Net Torque Exists

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind It:**
> For any closed contour in a uniform field:
> $$\vec{F}_{net} = \oint I\,d\vec{l} \times \vec{B} = \left(\oint I\,d\vec{l}\right) \times \vec{B} = I \cdot \vec{0} \times \vec{B} = \vec{0}$$
> because $\oint d\vec{l} = \vec{0}$ for a closed loop. However, the integrand for torque $\vec{r} \times (I\,d\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$ is **not** a closed-loop derivative, so it survives integration.

## 2.3 Conditions for Stable and Unstable Equilibrium

| Orientation | Angle $\theta$ | Torque $\tau$ | Potential Energy $U$ | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\vec{m} \parallel \vec{B}$ | $\theta = 0°$ | $\tau = 0$ | $U = -mB$ (minimum) | **Stable** |
| $\vec{m} \perp \vec{B}$ | $\theta = 90°$ | $\tau = mB$ (max) | $U = 0$ | Neutral |
| $\vec{m}$ anti-parallel to $\vec{B}$ | $\theta = 180°$ | $\tau = 0$ | $U = +mB$ (maximum) | **Unstable** |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these expressions — they appear in over 80% of KTU board exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Physical Quantity | Vector Form | Magnitude Form | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic Dipole Moment | $\vec{m} = I A \hat{n}$ | $m = I A$ | A·m² |
| 2 | Torque on Dipole | $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$ | $\tau = mB\sin\theta$ | N·m |
| 3 | Maximum Torque | — | $\tau_{max} = mB$ at $\theta = 90°$ | N·m |
| 4 | Potential Energy | $U = -\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B}$ | $U = -mB\cos\theta$ | J (Joule) |
| 5 | Work Done in Rotation | $W = -\Delta U$ | $W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$ | J |
| 6 | Period of Oscillation | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{m}}{mB}}$ | — | s |
| 7 | Force Between Two Dipoles | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\cdot\dfrac{6m_1 m_2}{r^{4}}$ (axial) | — | N |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the notation $\theta$ denotes the angle between $\vec{m}$ and $\vec{B}$ (NOT between the plane of the loop and $\vec{B}$). This is the most common KTU mistake.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Summary

The magnetic torque concept forms the **theoretical foundation** of every electromechanical energy conversion device:

- **Motors & Generators:** Faraday–Lenz law combined with dipole torque governs power conversion.
- **Particle Accelerators:** Magnetic dipole moments of charged particles interact with steering fields.
- **Atomic Physics:** Electron spin and orbital magnetic moments give rise to atomic magnetization ($\vec{M} = n\vec{m}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque on a Rectangular Current Loop

### Setup

Consider a rectangular loop **PQRS** with:
- Side **PQ = RS = $a$** (horizontal sides)
- Side **QR = SP = $b$** (vertical sides)
- Steady current **$I$** flowing in the sense P → Q → R → S → P
- Uniform external magnetic field $\vec{B}$ in the plane of the loop
- Angle between the **normal to the loop** ($\hat{n}$) and $\vec{B}$ is $\theta$

### Step 1 — Force on Side PQ

The current in PQ flows in a direction perpendicular to $\vec{B}$ (chosen such that the length vector $\vec{a}$ is along the current). The force is:
$$\vec{F}_{PQ} = I\,\vec{a} \times \vec{B}$$

Its magnitude is $F_{PQ} = BIa$ and is directed **out of the plane** of the diagram at the position of PQ.

### Step 2 — Force on Side RS

The current in RS is antiparallel to that in PQ. The force has the same magnitude but is directed **into the plane** at the position of RS:
$$F_{RS} = BIa \quad \text{(inward)}$$

### Step 3 — Force on Sides QR and SP

The current in QR and SP is parallel to $\vec{B}$ (by geometry of the problem). Therefore:
$$\vec{F}_{QR} = I\,\vec{b} \times \vec{B} = \vec{0}, \quad \vec{F}_{SP} = \vec{0}$$

### Step 4 — Net Force Calculation

$$\vec{F}_{net} = \vec{F}_{PQ} + \vec{F}_{RS} + \vec{F}_{QR} + \vec{F}_{SP} = \vec{0}$$

**Conclusion:** The net force on a closed current loop in a uniform magnetic field is **zero**.

### Step 5 — Torque Calculation (The Crucial Part)

Even though net force is zero, $\vec{F}_{PQ}$ and $\vec{F}_{RS}$ form a **couple** because they are equal, opposite, and separated by a perpendicular distance.

The perpendicular distance between the lines of action of these two forces is **$b \sin\theta$**, where $\theta$ is the angle between the loop's normal and $\vec{B}$.

The torque of this couple is:
$$\tau = \text{Force} \times \text{Perpendicular distance}$$

$$\tau = (BIa) \times (b \sin\theta)$$

$$\tau = BIab\sin\theta$$

Since the area of the rectangular loop is $A = ab$, we obtain:
$$\tau = BIA \sin\theta$$

In vector form:
$$\vec{\tau} = I\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$$

$$\boxed{\;\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction of $\vec{\tau}$:** Given by the right-hand rule. If you curl the fingers of your right hand from $\vec{m}$ toward $\vec{B}$, the thumb points in the direction of $\vec{\tau}$ (axis of rotation).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque on a Circular Loop (Generalized Proof)

For a circular loop of radius **$R$** with **$N$** turns, area $A = \pi R^2$, and current $I$:

### Step 1 — Identify Magnetic Moment

$$\vec{m} = NIA\hat{n} = NI(\pi R^{2})\hat{n}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the General Result

Using the same physics as the rectangular case, the torque magnitude is:
$$\tau = mB \sin\theta = NI(\pi R^{2})B\sin\theta$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau = N I \pi R^{2} B \sin\theta\;}$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Check with Rectangular Case

For $N = 1$, this matches the rectangular loop result if we set $A = \pi R^{2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Potential Energy of a Magnetic Dipole

### Step 1 — Definition of Work Done

When the dipole is rotated by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ against the torque, the external agent does work:
$$dW_{ext} = \tau\,d\theta = mB\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

### Step 2 — Total Work from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$

$$W = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} mB \sin\theta\, d\theta = mB[-\cos\theta]_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2}$$

$$W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$$

### Step 3 — Equate to Change in Potential Energy

By definition, $W_{ext} = U(\theta_2) - U(\theta_1)$, so:
$$U(\theta_2) - U(\theta_1) = mB\cos\theta_1 - mB\cos\theta_2$$

$$U(\theta) = -mB\cos\theta + C$$

### Step 4 — Choose Reference (Standard Convention)

Setting $U = 0$ at $\theta = 90°$ (i.e., $C = 0$):
$$\boxed{\;U = -\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B} = -mB\cos\theta\;}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Period of Small Oscillations

For small angular displacements ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$):
$$\tau = -mB\theta$$

Comparing with the SHM equation $\tau = -I_m\ddot{\theta}$ where $I_m$ is the moment of inertia of the loop:
$$I_m\ddot{\theta} = -mB\theta$$

Angular frequency:
$$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{mB}{I_m}}$$

Period:
$$\boxed{\;T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I_m}{mB}}\;}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Initial Setup | Key Step | Final Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular loop torque | Loop PQRS, area $ab$ | Couple arm = $b\sin\theta$ | $\tau = BIA\sin\theta$ |
| Circular loop torque | $N$ turns, radius $R$ | Substitute $A = \pi R^{2}$ | $\tau = NI\pi R^{2}B\sin\theta$ |
| Potential energy | $dW = \tau\,d\theta$ | Integrate and set $U(90°)=0$ | $U = -mB\cos\theta$ |
| Work in rotation | $W = \int\tau\,d\theta$ | Evaluate definite integral | $W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$ |
| Oscillation period | Small-angle SHM | $\omega = \sqrt{mB/I_m}$ | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I_m/mB}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Since this topic involves physical drawings of current loops in magnetic fields, the following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps the conceptual interactions of force, torque, and energy components.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Magnetic Dipole Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Loop carrying current I] --> B[Define Magnetic Moment m = I A n]
    B --> C[Place Loop in Uniform B Field]
    C --> D[Compute Force on each element: dF = I dl x B]
    D --> E[Integrate over closed loop: Net Force = 0]
    D --> F[Integrate torque: r x dF]
    F --> G[Net Torque tau = m x B]
    G --> H{Orientation theta}
    H -->|theta = 0 degrees| I[Stable Equilibrium: U minimum]
    H -->|theta = 90 degrees| J[Maximum Torque: tau = mB]
    H -->|theta = 180 degrees| K[Unstable Equilibrium: U maximum]
    G --> L[Loop Rotates to align m with B]
    I --> M[Rest Configuration: No further motion]
    L --> I
    J --> L
    K --> N[Loop Flips to reach Stable State]
    N --> I
```

## 4.2 Torque–Orientation Relationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: m vector] --> Q1[Input: B vector]
    Q1 --> R1[Compute Cross Product]
    P1 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Output: Torque vector tau]
    S1 --> T1[Axis of Rotation]
    T1 --> U1[Mechanical Work Output]
    U1 --> V1[Energy Conversion to Kinetic]
    V1 --> W1[Dissipation or Storage]
    S1 --> X1[Reaches Equilibrium]
    X1 --> Y1[Final State: m parallel to B]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Components of the Torque Expression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SYMBOLS
        direction TB
        s1[m: Magnetic Dipole Moment - A m squared]
        s2[B: Magnetic Flux Density - Tesla]
        s3[theta: Angle between m and B - degrees or radians]
        s4[tau: Torque vector - N m]
    end
    subgraph OPERATION
        direction TB
        o1[Vector Cross Product]
        o2[tau equals m cross B]
        o3[Magnitude: m B sin theta]
    end
    SYMBOLS --> OPERATION
    o3 --> o4[When theta equals 90 deg, tau is maximum = mB]
    o3 --> o5[When theta equals 0 deg, tau is zero = Stable]
    o3 --> o6[When theta equals 180 deg, tau is zero = Unstable]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Tag]**

### Question 1: Define Magnetic Dipole Moment
**[KTU Model Paper - May 2024]**

**Question:** Define magnetic dipole moment. Write its SI unit and dimensional formula. Mention one device that works on the principle of torque on a current-carrying coil.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnetic Dipole Moment ($\vec{m}$):** It is defined as the product of the current $I$ flowing in the loop and the area vector $\vec{A}$ of the loop:
> $$\vec{m} = I\vec{A} = IA\hat{n}$$
> where $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal to the plane of the loop given by the right-hand rule.

- **SI Unit:** Ampere·metre² (**A·m²**)
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[\,M^{0}\,L^{2}\,A^{1}\,T^{0}\,]$
- **Device:** Moving-coil **galvanometer** (or DC motor)

---

### Question 2: Orientations for Maximum and Minimum Torque
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style Tag]**

**Question:** A magnetic dipole is placed in a uniform magnetic field. At what orientations of the dipole with respect to the field is the torque (a) maximum, (b) minimum, and (c) zero? What are the corresponding potential energy values?

**Model Answer:**

Using $\tau = mB\sin\theta$ and $U = -mB\cos\theta$:

| Orientation | Angle $\theta$ | Torque $\tau$ | Potential Energy $U$ | State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\vec{m} \perp \vec{B}$ | $90°$ | $\tau = mB$ (max) | $U = 0$ | Neutral |
| $\vec{m} \parallel \vec{B}$ | $0°$ | $\tau = 0$ | $U = -mB$ (min) | **Stable** |
| $\vec{m}$ anti-parallel | $180°$ | $\tau = 0$ | $U = +mB$ (max) | **Unstable** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Choose **ONE** of the two alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the torque experienced by a rectangular current loop placed in a uniform magnetic field. Hence obtain the general vector form of the torque on a magnetic dipole.

**(b)** A circular coil of **50 turns** and radius **10 cm** carries a current of **2 A**. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field of **0.4 T** with its plane initially parallel to the field. Find the **(i)** magnetic dipole moment of the coil, **(ii)** maximum torque experienced by the coil, and **(iii)** work done to rotate the coil from its initial position to a position where its magnetic moment is anti-parallel to the field.

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Refer to Section 3.1, Derivation 1** for the complete derivation.

**Summary of derivation steps:**
1. Set up loop PQRS with sides $a$ and $b$, current $I$, in uniform $\vec{B}$.
2. Compute forces: $F_{PQ} = F_{RS} = BIa$ (opposite directions, form a couple); $F_{QR} = F_{SP} = 0$.
3. Perpendicular distance between the two equal forces = $b\sin\theta$.
4. Torque of the couple: $\tau = BIa \cdot b\sin\theta = BIA\sin\theta$.
5. Vector form: $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$ where $\vec{m} = IA\hat{n}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $N = 50$, $R = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m, $I = 2$ A, $B = 0.4$ T.

**(i) Magnetic Dipole Moment:**

$$m = NI A = NI\pi R^{2}$$

$$m = 50 \times 2 \times \pi \times (0.1)^{2}$$

$$m = 100 \times \pi \times 0.01 = \pi \approx 3.14 \text{ A·m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Maximum Torque:**

Maximum torque occurs when $\theta = 90°$, i.e., $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$\tau_{max} = mB = 3.14 \times 0.4 = 1.257 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Work Done in Rotation:**

Initial position: plane of coil parallel to $\vec{B}$ → normal is **perpendicular** to $\vec{B}$ → $\theta_1 = 90°$.

Final position: $\vec{m}$ anti-parallel to $\vec{B}$ → $\theta_2 = 180°$.

$$W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$$

$$W = 3.14 \times 0.4 \times (\cos 90° - \cos 180°)$$

$$W = 1.257 \times (0 - (-1)) = 1.257 \times 1$$

$$W \approx 1.257 \text{ J}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Define magnetic dipole moment. Derive the expression for the potential energy of a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field. What is meant by stable and unstable equilibrium?

**(b)** A magnetic dipole of moment **3 A·m²** is placed in a uniform magnetic field of **0.5 T** at an angle of **30°** with the field. Calculate:
   - **(i)** Torque on the dipole
   - **(ii)** Potential energy of the dipole
   - **(iii)** Work done to rotate the dipole to align with the field

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Refer to Section 3.3, Derivation 3** for the complete derivation.

**Key steps:**
1. Define $\vec{m} = I\vec{A}$.
2. External work in rotating by $d\theta$: $dW = \tau\,d\theta = mB\sin\theta\,d\theta$.
3. Integrate from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$: $W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$.
4. By convention, set $U = 0$ at $\theta = 90°$, yielding $U = -mB\cos\theta = -\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B}$.

**Equilibrium Types:**
- **Stable Equilibrium:** $\theta = 0°$ (i.e., $\vec{m} \parallel \vec{B}$). Small disturbance causes restoring torque. $U$ is minimum.
- **Unstable Equilibrium:** $\theta = 180°$ (i.e., $\vec{m}$ anti-parallel to $\vec{B}$). Small disturbance grows; no restoring torque. $U$ is maximum.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $m = 3$ A·m², $B = 0.5$ T, $\theta_1 = 30°$, final $\theta_2 = 0°$.

**(i) Torque:**

$$\tau = mB\sin\theta = 3 \times 0.5 \times \sin 30°$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Potential Energy:**

$$U = -mB\cos\theta = -3 \times 0.5 \times \cos 30°$$

$$U = -1.5 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -1.299 \text{ J}$$

**(iii) Work Done to Align with Field:**

$$W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$$

$$W = 3 \times 0.5 \times (\cos 30° - \cos 0°)$$

$$W = 1.5 \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - 1\right)$$

$$W = 1.5 \times (0.866 - 1) = 1.5 \times (-0.134)$$

$$W \approx -0.201 \text{ J}$$

The negative sign indicates that the **external field does positive work** on the dipole (i.e., energy is released as the dipole aligns).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Angle Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Always use the angle between $\vec{m}$ and $\vec{B}$, NOT the angle between the plane of the loop and $\vec{B}$. When the plane is parallel to the field, $\theta = 90°$ (NOT $0°$).
> 
> 2. **Missing Vector Direction (–1 mark):** For full marks on torque, write the **vector form** $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$ and indicate the direction via the right-hand rule.
> 
> 3. **Sign Error in Work (–2 marks):** Work done **by external agent** vs. **by magnetic field** have opposite signs. Read the question carefully.
> 
> 4. **Units Mismatch (–1 mark):** Always convert cm → m, and express torque in N·m, energy in J.
> 
> 5. **Skipping "Net Force = 0" Proof (–2 marks):** Always include a line stating the net translational force vanishes; the KTU marking scheme explicitly rewards this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Magnetic Torque and Moment**

- **Magnetic Dipole Moment** is a **vector** with magnitude $m = IA$ and direction given by the right-hand rule applied to current flow.
- **SI Unit** is **A·m²**; dimensions are $[\,M^{0}\,L^{2}\,A^{1}\,T^{0}\,]$.
- **Net force** on a closed current loop in a **uniform** $\vec{B}$ is **zero**.
- **Net torque** is **non-zero** (unless $\vec{m} \parallel \vec{B}$ or anti-parallel).
- **Vector torque equation:** $\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$.
- **Magnitude:** $\tau = mB\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{m}$ and $\vec{B}$.
- **Maximum torque** at $\theta = 90°$ → $\tau_{max} = mB$.
- **Zero torque** at $\theta = 0°$ (stable) and $\theta = 180°$ (unstable).
- **Potential energy** of dipole: $U = -\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B} = -mB\cos\theta$.
- **Work done** to rotate from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$: $W = mB(\cos\theta_1 - \cos\theta_2)$.
- **For $N$ turns**, all formulas get multiplied by $N$ (e.g., $m = NIA$, $\tau = NIA B\sin\theta$).
- **Period of small oscillations:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I_m/(mB)}$, where $I_m$ is the moment of inertia of the loop.
- **Energy difference** between unstable and stable equilibrium = $2mB$.
- The dipole **tends to align with the field** to minimize potential energy (principle of least energy).
- **Applications:** DC motors, galvanometers, MRI, mass spectrometers, and relay switches all operate on the principle of magnetic torque on a current loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetization in Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetization in Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetization in materials** describes the macroscopic magnetic response of a medium when subjected to an external magnetic field. It is quantified by the **magnetization vector $\vec{M}$**, defined as the net magnetic dipole moment per unit volume of the material.

$$\vec{M} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N \Delta v} \vec{m}_k}{\Delta v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{A}}{\text{m}}\right]$$

where $\vec{m}_k$ is the magnetic dipole moment of the $k$-th elementary current loop (atom/molecule) and $N$ is the number density of dipoles.

The fundamental field relation linking the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$, the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$, and magnetization $\vec{M}$ is:

$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 \left( \vec{H} + \vec{M} \right)$$

where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ is the **permeability of free space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Magnetization introduces the concept of **bound currents** ($J_b$ and $K_b$) — fictitious currents that model the collective effect of countless microscopic atomic current loops. They are the magnetic analogue of bound charges in dielectrics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom full of spinning tops (atoms acting as tiny current loops). When no external field is present, the tops spin in random directions, so the *classroom* (the material) shows no net magnetic effect — the tiny magnetic moments cancel out. This is an **unmagnetized** state.

Now, switch on a strong bar magnet nearby. Each spinning top tilts and aligns, like a compass needle pointing north. The *collective* alignment of trillions of tiny loops creates a macroscopic magnetic effect that either **aids** (paramagnetic/ferromagnetic) or **opposes** (diamagnetic) the applied field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** Magnetization is a measure of **how strongly a material's microscopic magnetic dipoles align with an applied magnetic field** per unit volume. The greater the alignment, the larger the magnetization vector.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Value/Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | H/m | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ |
| Relative permeability | $\mu_r$ | dimensionless | Material property |
| Magnetic susceptibility | $\chi_m$ | dimensionless | $\chi_m = \mu_r - 1$ |
| Bohr magneton | $\mu_B$ | J/T | $9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ |
| Dipole moment | $m$ | A·m² | $m = I \cdot A$ |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Magnetic Materials

| Type | Susceptibility $\chi_m$ | Behavior | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diamagnetic** | $\chi_m < 0$ (small, $\sim -10^{-5}$) | Weakly repelled by magnets | Bismuth, Copper, Gold, Water |
| **Paramagnetic** | $\chi_m > 0$ (small, $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$) | Weakly attracted; moments align with $\vec{H}$ | Aluminium, Platinum, Oxygen |
| **Ferromagnetic** | $\chi_m \gg 0$ (large, up to $10^5$) | Strongly attracted; spontaneous alignment | Iron, Cobalt, Nickel |
| **Ferrimagnetic** | $\chi_m > 0$ (moderate) | Anti-parallel unequal moments | Ferrites (e.g., $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$) |
| **Antiferromagnetic** | $\chi_m \approx 0$ | Anti-parallel equal moments cancel | MnO, NiO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners frequently ask for the **sign and magnitude of $\chi_m$** for each category, and the relation $\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$ for linear, isotropic media.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Magnetization — Microscopic Picture

Inside any material, electrons orbit the nucleus and also spin on their own axis. Both motions constitute tiny current loops, each carrying a **magnetic dipole moment**:

- **Orbital magnetic moment:** due to electron revolution around the nucleus.
- **Spin magnetic moment:** due to intrinsic electron spin.
- **Nuclear magnetic moment:** usually negligible compared to electronic contributions.

The net effect of all such moments per unit volume defines the magnetization $\vec{M}$.

---

## 2.2 Bound Currents — The Macroscopic Consequence

When a material is magnetized, the aligned microscopic current loops combine to produce two observable macroscopic current distributions:

### 2.2.1 Bound Volume Current Density $\vec{J}_b$

At the interior of the material, neighbouring atomic loops cancel except at regions where the orientation of $\vec{M}$ changes. The net bound volume current density is:

$$\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$$

### 2.2.2 Bound Surface Current Density $\vec{K}_b$

At the material's boundary, the abrupt termination of atomic current loops produces a net current flowing along the surface:

$$\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal to the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** These bound currents allow us to compute the magnetic field of a magnetized body using the same Biot–Savart law as for free currents, **without** tracking individual atoms.

---

## 2.3 Magnetic Susceptibility and Permeability

For **linear, isotropic, homogeneous (LIH)** materials, magnetization is proportional to the applied field:

$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$$

Combining with $\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$:

$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 (1 + \chi_m) \vec{H} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H} = \mu \vec{H}$$

where:
- $\chi_m$ = **magnetic susceptibility** (dimensionless)
- $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ = **relative permeability** (dimensionless)
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ = **absolute permeability** (H/m)

---

## 2.4 Hysteresis & Ferromagnetic Materials (Brief)

For ferromagnetic materials, $\vec{M}$ is **not a single-valued function of $\vec{H}$** — it depends on the magnetic history. The B-H curve shows:
- **Retentivity (remanence):** residual $\vec{B}$ when $\vec{H} = 0$.
- **Coercivity:** reverse $\vec{H}$ required to reduce $\vec{B}$ to zero.
- **Saturation:** maximum $\vec{M}$ when all dipoles are aligned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, focus on the **linear regime** formulas; hysteresis is typically covered as a 3-mark conceptual question.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Magnetic Material Used | Role of Magnetization |
|---|---|---|
| Transformer cores | Silicon steel, ferrites | High $\mu_r$ channels flux efficiently |
| Hard drives | Ferromagnetic thin films | Retentivity stores binary data |
| MRI machines | Superconducting coils | Strong, stable $\vec{B}$ field |
| Magnetic shielding | Mu-metal ($\mu_r \sim 10^5$) | Diverts external field away from sensitive circuits |
| Induction cooktops | Ferromagnetic cookware | Hysteresis/eddy current heating |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA BANK** — Memorize these for KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{M} = \frac{d\vec{m}}{dv}$ | Magnetization = dipole moment density | General |
| 2 | $\vec{B} = \mu_0 (\vec{H} + \vec{M})$ | Fundamental constitutive relation | All media |
| 3 | $\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$ | Linear media relation | Linear, isotropic |
| 4 | $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ | Relating $\mu_r$ to $\chi_m$ | Linear media |
| 5 | $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$ | $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ | Linear media |
| 6 | $\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$ | Bound volume current | General |
| 7 | $\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$ | Bound surface current | At material surface |
| 8 | $\vec{m} = I \cdot A \cdot \hat{a}$ | Loop dipole moment | Single loop |
| 9 | $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 (I_f + I_b)$ | Ampère's law with bound currents | General |
| 10 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ | No magnetic monopoles | Always |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bound Volume Current $\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$

**Goal:** Show that microscopic aligned current loops in a magnetized material produce a macroscopic volume current density equal to $\nabla \times \vec{M}$.

### Step 1 — Consider a small magnetized element

Take an elemental volume $\Delta v$ inside a magnetized body. Each atom has a magnetic dipole moment $\vec{m}_k = I_k \vec{A}_k$ where $I_k$ is the equivalent bound current and $\vec{A}_k$ is the area vector of the $k$-th atomic loop.

The magnetization is:
$$\vec{M} = \frac{1}{\Delta v}\sum_{k} I_k \vec{A}_k$$

### Step 2 — Sum of dipole moments as a current distribution

By the magnetic moment equivalence, a distribution of dipoles produces the same far-field as a current distribution. Using the vector potential of a magnetic dipole:
$$\vec{A}_{\text{dipole}} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \frac{\vec{m} \times \hat{R}}{R^2}$$

For a continuous distribution:
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{M} \times \hat{R}}{R^2} dV'$$

### Step 3 — Convert to a current integral

We recall the identity (Biot–Savart formulation for volume currents):
$$\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}_b}{R} dV'$$

Comparing, the bound volume current density must satisfy:
$$\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the bound surface current

For the surface term, the contribution from an exterior surface element $dS$ with outward normal $\hat{n}$ adds a current that flows along the boundary. The discontinuity of $\vec{M}$ across the boundary gives:

$$\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$ in Linear Media

**Given:** $\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$ and $\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$ (linear media).

**Step 1 — Substitute $\vec{M}$ into the constitutive equation:**

$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 \left( \vec{H} + \chi_m \vec{H} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\vec{H}$:**

$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 (1 + \chi_m) \vec{H}$$

**Step 3 — Define relative permeability $\mu_r$ and absolute permeability $\mu$:**

$$\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H}$$

This is the **magnetic equivalent of $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$** in dielectrics.

---

## 3.3 Solved Analytical Example (Model Problem)

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A magnetic material has $\chi_m = 0.003$ and is placed in a region where $\vec{H} = 250 \ \hat{x} \ \text{A/m}$. Compute $\vec{M}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\mu_r$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find relative permeability:**
$$\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m = 1 + 0.003 = 1.003$$

**Step 2 — Compute magnetization:**
$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H} = 0.003 \times 250 \ \hat{x} = 0.75 \ \hat{x} \ \text{A/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnetic flux density:**
$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1.003)(250) \ \hat{x}$$

$$\vec{B} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(250.75) \ \hat{x}$$

$$\vec{B} = 3.149 \times 10^{-4} \ \hat{x} \ \text{Wb/m}^2 \quad (= \text{T})$$

**Step 4 — Verification using fundamental equation:**

$$\mu_0 (\vec{H} + \vec{M}) = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(250 + 0.75) = 3.149 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{T} \ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bound Currents for Common Geometries

| Geometry | $\vec{M}$ given | $\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$ | $\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniformly magnetized cylinder (along $z$) | $M_0 \hat{z}$ | $0$ | $M_0 \hat{\phi}$ (on curved surface) |
| Uniformly magnetized sphere | $\vec{M}_0$ | $0$ | $\vec{M}_0 \times \hat{n}$ on surface |
| Solenoid core, $\vec{M} = M(z)\hat{z}$ | $M(z)\hat{z}$ | $-\frac{dM}{dz}\hat{\phi}$ (approx.) | At ends: $\pm M \hat{z} \times \hat{n}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** For *uniformly* magnetized bodies, $\nabla \times \vec{M} = 0$ everywhere inside — bound currents exist *only on the surface*, equivalent to a solenoid-like current sheet.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Microscopic Dipoles to Macroscopic $\vec{M}$

The following Mermaid block diagrams the cause-and-effect chain from atomic moments to measurable field quantities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Atomic current loops - electrons orbiting and spinning] --> A2[Each loop has magnetic dipole moment m equals I times A]
    A2 --> A3[Net dipole moment per unit volume = Magnetization M]
    A3 --> A4{External H field applied?}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Atomic moments align - either aid or oppose H]
    A4 -- No --> A6[Random orientations - net M equals 0]
    A5 --> A7[Macroscopic bound currents appear]
    A7 --> A8[Bound volume current Jb equals curl of M]
    A7 --> A9[Bound surface current Kb equals M cross n]
    A8 --> A10[Ampere law modified - B equals mu0 times H plus M]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[For linear media: M equals chi_m times H, B equals mu times H]
    A11 --> A12[Classification: Diamagnetic, Paramagnetic, Ferromagnetic, etc.]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Magnetization Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DOMAIN]
        I1[External H field - source]
        I2[Material properties - chi_m, mu_r]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[MAGNETIZATION SUBSYSTEM]
        P1[Atomic dipole alignment mechanism]
        P2[Magnetization vector M = chi_m H]
        P3[Bound current generator: Jb and Kb]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT DOMAIN]
        O1[Modified B field inside material]
        O2[Auxiliary H field outside]
        O3[Demagnetization field for finite samples]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    O1 --> FEED[Feedback into Amperes law with bound currents]
    FEED --> P2
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Classifying a Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Know chi_m of the material?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is chi_m less than zero?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Measure response to applied H]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[DIAMAGNETIC - weakly repelled]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is chi_m approximately zero?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R2[ANTIFERROMAGNETIC - moments cancel]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is chi_m large and positive?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R3[FERROMAGNETIC - strong attraction, hysteresis]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Is chi_m moderately positive?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> R4[PARAMAGNETIC or FERRIMAGNETIC]
    Q3 --> Q1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define magnetization and magnetic susceptibility. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**Magnetization ($\vec{M}$):** It is defined as the magnetic dipole moment per unit volume of a material.

$$\vec{M} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\sum \vec{m}_k}{\Delta v} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{A}}{\text{m}} \right]$$

**Magnetic Susceptibility ($\chi_m$):** It is the dimensionless quantity that quantifies the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field $\vec{H}$:

$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$$

For diamagnetic materials, $\chi_m < 0$; for paramagnetic and ferromagnetic, $\chi_m > 0$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between diamagnetic and paramagnetic materials. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Diamagnetic | Paramagnetic |
|---|---|---|
| Susceptibility $\chi_m$ | Small and negative ($\sim -10^{-5}$) | Small and positive ($\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$) |
| Atomic dipole moments | No permanent moment; induced opposes $\vec{B}$ | Permanent moments randomly oriented; align weakly with $\vec{H}$ |
| Effect of $\vec{B}$ field | Weakly repelled | Weakly attracted |
| Examples | Bismuth, Copper, Gold | Aluminium, Platinum |
| Temperature dependence | Negligible | Curie law: $\chi_m \propto 1/T$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

### Question A: Bound Currents and Field Computation [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A long cylinder of radius $R$ is uniformly magnetized along its axis with magnetization $\vec{M} = M_0 \hat{z}$.

**(a)** Find the bound volume current density $\vec{J}_b$ and the bound surface current density $\vec{K}_b$. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Using Ampère's law, find the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ inside and outside the cylinder. [7 Marks]

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Bound volume current:**
For uniform magnetization, $\vec{M} = M_0 \hat{z}$ is constant. Hence:
$$\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M} = \nabla \times (M_0 \hat{z}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Bound surface current:**

On the curved surface, the outward normal is $\hat{n} = \hat{\rho}$. Therefore:
$$\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n} = M_0 \hat{z} \times \hat{\rho} = M_0 \hat{\phi}$$

The bound surface current flows **circumferentially**, exactly mimicking a solenoid. The total bound current per unit length is:
$$K_b = M_0 \quad \text{[A/m]}$$

---

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Use Ampère's Law with bound currents:**

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc,total}}$$

where $I_{\text{enc,total}} = I_f + I_b$. With no free currents ($I_f = 0$) and $\vec{J}_b = 0$, the only contribution is from $\vec{K}_b$.

**Step 2 — Choose a circular Amperian loop of radius $\rho < R$ inside the cylinder:**

The enclosed bound current = (current per unit length) × (length) = $M_0 \cdot L$

$$B_{\text{in}} (2\pi \rho) = \mu_0 M_0 L \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{\text{in}} = \mu_0 M_0 \quad (\text{independent of } \rho)$$

$$\vec{B}_{\text{in}} = \mu_0 M_0 \hat{z}$$

**Step 3 — Outside ($\rho > R$):**

The Amperian loop encloses both directions of surface current on opposite sides of the cylinder, so net enclosed current is zero:
$$\vec{B}_{\text{out}} = 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (1-mark loss):** Students often mistakenly write $\vec{B} = \mu_0 M$ as the field *inside a solenoid*. That is correct here because the cylinder behaves as a solenoid with surface current $K_b = M_0$. Make sure to clearly identify the geometry and the Amperian loop.

---

### Question B: Magnetic Susceptibility and B-H Relation [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

A magnetic material has relative permeability $\mu_r = 50$ and is placed in a uniform field of $\vec{H} = 1000 \ \hat{y} \ \text{A/m}$.

**(a)** Find the magnetic susceptibility $\chi_m$ and the magnetization $\vec{M}$. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Compute the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ and the magnetic dipole moment of a unit cube of the material. [7 Marks]

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Susceptibility from relative permeability:**
$$\chi_m = \mu_r - 1 = 50 - 1 = 49$$

**Step 2 — Magnetization:**
$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H} = 49 \times 1000 \ \hat{y} = 49{,}000 \ \hat{y} \ \text{A/m}$$

---

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Absolute permeability:**
$$\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(50) = 6.283 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{H/m}$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic flux density:**
$$\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H} = (6.283 \times 10^{-5})(1000) \ \hat{y} = 6.283 \times 10^{-2} \ \hat{y} \ \text{T}$$

Or, equivalently:
$$\vec{B} = \mu_0 (\vec{H} + \vec{M}) = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1000 + 49000) = 6.283 \times 10^{-2} \ \hat{y} \ \text{T} \ \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Dipole moment of a unit cube:**

For unit volume ($V = 1 \ \text{m}^3$):
$$\vec{m}_{\text{total}} = \vec{M} \cdot V = 49{,}000 \ \hat{y} \ \text{A·m}^2$$

If the unit cube has side $1 \ \text{m}$ and contains $N$ atoms each of moment $m_a$, then $N m_a = 49{,}000 \ \text{A·m}^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (2-mark loss):** Students often forget the factor of 1 when $V$ is unity, or confuse $\mu_0 \vec{H}$ with $\vec{B}$ (the latter includes $\vec{M}$). Always cross-check by computing $\vec{B}$ via both $\mu \vec{H}$ and $\mu_0 (\vec{H} + \vec{M})$.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Common Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $\vec{M}$ is in **A/m**, not A·m². Confusion with dipole moment is frequent.
> 2. **Wrong sign of $\chi_m$:** Diamagnetic materials have $\chi_m < 0$, but many students write positive values.
> 3. **Missing the surface current direction:** $\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$ — the right-hand rule applies strictly. A sign error in cross product loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Linear vs. nonlinear:** Don't apply $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$ to ferromagnetics in the nonlinear regime — examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Bound vs. free currents:** In Ampère's law with magnetization, ALWAYS use total current (free + bound) if $\vec{H}$ is *not* used.
> 6. **Dimensionless check:** $\chi_m$ and $\mu_r$ are dimensionless; writing them with units loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Magnetization in Materials**

- **Magnetization $\vec{M}$** = magnetic dipole moment per unit volume; unit is **A/m**.
- **Fundamental relation:** $\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M})$ — always valid, all media.
- **Linear media:** $\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$, giving $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$ with $\mu = \mu_0(1 + \chi_m)$.
- **Bound volume current:** $\vec{J}_b = \nabla \times \vec{M}$ (curl of M).
- **Bound surface current:** $\vec{K}_b = \vec{M} \times \hat{n}$ (cross product with outward normal).
- **Uniform magnetization** $\Rightarrow$ no volume bound current, only surface bound current.
- **Diamagnetic:** $\chi_m < 0$, weakly repelled, no permanent atomic moments.
- **Paramagnetic:** $\chi_m > 0$ (small), permanent moments align weakly, follows Curie's law ($\chi_m \propto 1/T$).
- **Ferromagnetic:** $\chi_m \gg 0$ (very large, $10^2$ to $10^5$), exhibits hysteresis, retentivity, coercivity.
- **Ferrimagnetic:** Anti-parallel unequal moments — net $\vec{M} \neq 0$; basis of ferrites.
- **Antiferromagnetic:** Anti-parallel equal moments — net $\vec{M} = 0$.
- **Permeability hierarchy:** $\mu > \mu_0$ for para/ferro, $\mu < \mu_0$ for diamagnetic, $\mu \approx \mu_0$ for vacuum.
- **Bohr magneton** $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T is the fundamental unit of atomic magnetic moment.
- **Engineering impact:** Transformers, motors, MRI, magnetic storage, shielding all rely on tailored $\mu_r$.
- **KTU hot topics:** Bound current derivations, classification table, $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ derivations, solenoid-equivalence of uniformly magnetized cylinders.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Magnetic Boundary Conditions** are the physical laws that govern the behaviour of the magnetic flux density vector **B** and the magnetic field intensity vector **H** at the interface separating two distinct magnetic media (e.g., the boundary between a ferromagnetic core and air, or between two different dielectrics carrying magnetisation). These conditions arise directly from Maxwell's equations for static and quasi-static magnetic fields, and dictate exactly how the **normal** and **tangential** components of **B** and **H** transform as they cross such an interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Boundary conditions for **B**, **H**, **D**, and **E** are universally tested. A clear understanding of *why* each component behaves as it does is mandatory for full marks in derivations.

The two fundamental magnetic boundary conditions, in the absence of free surface currents, are:
1. **Continuity of Normal B:** The normal component of the magnetic flux density **B** is continuous across the boundary.
2. **Continuity of Tangential H:** The tangential component of the magnetic field intensity **H** is continuous across the boundary.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine **magnetic flux lines** as an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe. Since magnetic monopoles (sources of magnetic field lines) **do not exist in nature** (Gauss's Law for Magnetism: ∇·**B** = 0), no fluid can be created or destroyed at the interface. As a result, the *number* of flux lines entering medium 1 from the boundary side must exactly equal the number leaving into medium 2. This is why the **normal** component of **B** (the "count" of flux lines crossing a unit area) remains unchanged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with Electric Fields:** Unlike **B** (which is continuous normally), the normal component of the electric flux density **D** is *discontinuous* by the amount of free surface charge σ. This is because electric monopoles *do* exist, but magnetic monopoles do not.

For the **tangential** component, picture a small loop resting on the boundary. Ampere's circuital law (∇×**H** = **J**) tells us that if there are no surface currents, the magnetic "voltage" (**H**·d**l**) around the loop must be zero. Hence, the tangential **H** on both sides must match.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Permeability of free space:** **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** (Henries per metre)
- **Relative permeability of medium:** **μᵣ** (dimensionless)
- **Absolute permeability:** **μ = μᵣμ₀** (H/m)
- **Surface current density:** **K** (A/m)

> [!TIP]
> The relationship **B = μH** is the constitutive relation that ties **B** and **H** inside a linear, isotropic, homogeneous (LIH) medium. This is the key link when going from **B** continuity to **H** discontinuity (or vice versa).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Magnetic Boundary Conditions

Magnetic boundary conditions are not arbitrary; they are **inevitable consequences** of Maxwell's equations applied to infinitesimally small geometrical constructs that straddle the interface between two media. Two fundamental laws from Maxwell's static equations are responsible:

**Law 1 — Gauss's Law for Magnetism:**
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$$
This is the mathematical statement that **magnetic field lines always form closed loops**; they never begin or end. Applying this to a small "pillbox" crossing the boundary yields the **normal B continuity** condition.

**Law 2 — Ampere's Circuital Law (Static / Magnetostatics):**
$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$$
Where **J** is the volume current density. Applying this to a small rectangular loop crossing the boundary yields the **tangential H continuity** condition (modified by surface current if present).

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Case-by-Case)

**Case A — Tangential H with NO free surface current (K = 0):**
- The line integral of **H** around a vanishingly small loop must equal the enclosed current.
- As the loop shrinks, the area → 0, so enclosed volume current → 0.
- Therefore, ∮**H**·d**l** = 0, meaning the tangential **H** is the same on both sides.

**Case B — Tangential H WITH free surface current (K ≠ 0):**
- The shrinking loop still encloses a *finite* surface current **K** in the limit.
- The boundary condition becomes discontinuous: **H₁ₜ − H₂ₜ = K × n̂**

**Case C — Normal B (always, no exceptions):**
- The flux leaving the pillbox's top face must equal the flux entering the bottom face.
- There are no magnetic charges to "absorb" flux. Hence, **B_n** is conserved.

**Case D — Normal H:**
- Since **B** is continuous normally but **B = μH**, we have **H_n1 ≠ H_n2** unless μ₁ = μ₂.
- The relationship is: **H_n1 / H_n2 = μ₂ / μ₁**

**Case E — Tangential B:**
- Since **H** is continuous tangentially but **B = μH**, we have **B_t1 / B_t2 = μ₁ / μ₂**

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Condition | Mathematical Statement | Physical Interpretation |
|---|-----------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Normal **B** continuous | **n̂ · (B₁ − B₂) = 0** ⟹ **B_{n1} = B_{n2}** | No magnetic monopoles exist |
| 2 | Tangential **H** continuous (no K) | **n̂ × (H₁ − H₂) = 0** ⟹ **H_{t1} = H_{t2}** | Ampere's law with no surface current |
| 3 | Tangential **H** with surface current | **n̂ × (H₁ − H₂) = K** | Discontinuity equals surface current density |
| 4 | Normal **H** relation | **H_{n1} / H_{n2} = μ₂ / μ₁** | Refraction-like behaviour |
| 5 | Tangential **B** relation | **B_{t1} / B_{t2} = μ₁ / μ₂** | Direct consequence of H continuity |
| 6 | Constitutive link | **B = μH = μᵣμ₀H** | Connects B and H in LIH medium |
| 7 | Magnetic refraction (Snell's law analog) | **tan θ₁ / tan θ₂ = μ₁ / μ₂** | Field line bending at boundary |
| 8 | Bending of flux lines | **(sin θ₁) / (sin θ₂) = √(μ₁ / μ₂)** | Not true refraction; only field angles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Snell's law for magnetic fields" using sines. The correct magnetic refraction law uses the **tangent** function, *not* the sine function. This is a frequently tested distinction in KTU exams.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Transformer Design:** Magnetic flux must transfer from the iron core to air gaps with minimal loss. The boundary condition at the iron-air interface determines the air-gap reluctance and, therefore, the magnetising current.
- **Magnetic Recording / Hard Disks:** The transition of magnetisation between magnetic grains and the non-magnetic substrate relies on these conditions.
- **MRI Machines:** The patient's body and the bore's magnetic coils form multiple magnetic media; engineers must respect boundary conditions to compute the **B** field inside tissues.
- **Electric Motors:** Ferromagnetic stator/rotor materials have μᵣ >> 1, causing magnetic field lines to bend sharply — critical for designing the air gap flux.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Component of B is Continuous

**Starting Point (Gauss's Law for Magnetism):**
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Integral Form:** Apply the divergence theorem to a small cylindrical "pillbox" of cross-sectional area ΔS and height Δh, oriented so that its axis is along the unit normal **n̂** at the boundary.

$$\oint_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{V} (\nabla \cdot \vec{B}) \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — Surface Decomposition:** The closed surface integral splits into three contributions:
- Top face (in medium 1, normal +n̂)
- Bottom face (in medium 2, normal −n̂)
- Side wall (negligible as Δh → 0)

$$\vec{B_1} \cdot \hat{n} \, \Delta S - \vec{B_2} \cdot \hat{n} \, \Delta S + \underbrace{\vec{B}_{\text{side}} \cdot d\vec{S}}_{\to \, 0} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:** Dividing by ΔS and taking the limit Δh → 0:

$$B_{1n} - B_{2n} = 0$$

$$\boxed{B_{1n} = B_{2n}}$$

**Textual Logic:** The flux entering the pillbox from medium 2 must equal the flux leaving into medium 1 because no magnetic monopoles exist at the boundary to absorb or generate flux. This is the universal magnetic boundary condition for the normal component.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tangential Component of H is Continuous (K = 0)

**Starting Point (Ampere's Circuital Law, static case):**
$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$$

**Step 1 — Integral Form:** Apply Stokes' theorem to a small rectangular loop of length Δl (parallel to the surface) and height Δh (perpendicular to the surface):

$$\oint_{C} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{S} (\nabla \times \vec{H}) \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{S} \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = I_{\text{enc}}$$

**Step 2 — Loop Decomposition:** As Δh → 0, the contributions from the two vertical sides vanish. The remaining two horizontal sides lie just above and just below the boundary:

$$H_{1t} \Delta l - H_{2t} \Delta l + \underbrace{\vec{H}_{\text{vertical}} \cdot d\vec{l}}_{\to \, 0} = I_{\text{enc}}$$

**Step 3 — Enclosed Current in the Limit:** As Δh → 0, the area of the loop goes to zero, so the *volume* current enclosed also goes to zero (assuming **J** is finite). Thus:

$$H_{1t} \Delta l - H_{2t} \Delta l = 0$$

$$\boxed{H_{1t} = H_{2t}}$$

**Textual Logic:** The line integral of **H** around a vanishingly small loop must vanish when no surface current threads it. This enforces the continuity of the tangential **H** field at the interface.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tangential H with a Surface Current K

If a free surface current density **K** (A/m) flows along the boundary, then in the limit of vanishing loop height, the loop encloses a *finite* surface current:

$$I_{\text{enc}} = \vec{K} \cdot \hat{a}_n \Delta l$$

where **â_n** is the unit vector normal to the loop's surface (using right-hand rule). Substituting:

$$H_{1t} \Delta l - H_{2t} \Delta l = K_n \Delta l$$

$$\boxed{H_{1t} - H_{2t} = K_n}$$

In vector form:

$$\boxed{\hat{n} \times (\vec{H_1} - \vec{H_2}) = \vec{K}}$$

**Textual Logic:** A surface current acts as a "magnetic voltage source" that causes a step change in the tangential **H** field as one crosses the boundary. This is directly analogous to how a surface charge σ causes a discontinuity in the normal **D** field in electrostatics.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Magnetic Refraction Law (Field Bending)

**Given:**
- **B_{1n} = B_{2n}** (continuous normal B)
- **H_{1t} = H_{2t}** (continuous tangential H, K = 0)

**Step 1 — Express tangential H in terms of B:**
Since **B = μH**, we have **H = B/μ**. Thus:

$$\frac{B_{1t}}{\mu_1} = \frac{B_{2t}}{\mu_2}$$

$$\frac{B_{1t}}{B_{2t}} = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}$$

**Step 2 — Define angles:** Let θ₁ and θ₂ be the angles **B₁** and **B₂** make with the normal **n̂** at the boundary. Then:
- B_{1n} = B₁ cos θ₁
- B_{1t} = B₁ sin θ₁
- B_{2n} = B₂ cos θ₂
- B_{2t} = B₂ sin θ₂

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of tangential to normal components:**

$$\frac{B_{1t}}{B_{1n}} = \frac{B_1 \sin\theta_1}{B_1 \cos\theta_1} = \tan\theta_1$$

$$\frac{B_{2t}}{B_{2n}} = \tan\theta_2$$

**Step 4 — Divide and substitute:**

$$\frac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \frac{B_{1t}/B_{1n}}{B_{2t}/B_{2n}} = \frac{B_{1t}}{B_{2t}} \cdot \frac{B_{2n}}{B_{1n}}$$

Using **B_{1t}/B_{2t} = μ₁/μ₂** and **B_{1n} = B_{2n}**:

$$\frac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2}}$$

**Textual Logic:** Because μ₁ ≠ μ₂ in general, the magnetic field lines "bend" at the interface. In a ferromagnetic material (μ >> 1), field lines are pulled almost parallel to the surface — this is the principle behind **magnetic shielding** and **flux guiding** in transformer cores.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Boundary Behaviour Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maxwell Equation: div B = 0] --> B[Apply to Pillbox Crossing Boundary]
    B --> C[Normal B Continuous: B1n = B2n]
    C --> C1[Engineering Use: Transformer Core Flux Transfer]
    C --> C2[Engineering Use: MRI Field Calculation]
    C --> C3[Engineering Use: Magnetic Shielding Design]

    D[Maxwell Equation: curl H = J] --> E[Apply to Small Loop Crossing Boundary]
    E --> F{Surface Current K Present?}
    F -- No --> G[Tangential H Continuous: H1t = H2t]
    F -- Yes --> H[Tangential H Discontinuous: H1t minus H2t equals K]
    G --> G1[Engineering Use: Iron-Air Gap in Machines]
    H --> H1[Engineering Use: Superconductor Boundary with K]

    C --> I[Combine with B = mu H]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Magnetic Refraction Law: tan theta1 over tan theta2 equals mu1 over mu2]
    J --> J1[Field Lines Bend at Ferromagnetic Interface]
    J1 --> J2[Flux Confinement in High mu Materials]
```

---

### 4.2 Boundary Interface Diagram (Text Schematic)

```
                MEDIUM 1                    MEDIUM 2
            (mu_r1, perm_1)              (mu_r2, perm_2)
                                          
        \         |  n_hat              /        
         \  B_1n  |  ↑                 /  B_2n   
          \       |  |                /          
           \      |  |               /           
            \  θ₁ |  |              / θ₂         
   ─────────\─────┼──┼─────────────/────────────  Boundary Plane
              \   |  |           /              
               \  |  |          /               
                \ |  |         /                
                 \|  |        /                 
                  \ |  |     /                  
                   \|  |    /                   
                    \  |   /                    
        B_1t ←──────\  |  /                    
                       \| /                     
                        ●                       
                       (B_1 vector tip)         
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **θ₁**: angle **B₁** makes with the normal **n̂** in medium 1
- **θ₂**: angle **B₂** makes with the normal **n̂** in medium 2
- **B_{1n} = B_{2n}**: vertical components are equal
- **tan θ₁ / tan θ₂ = μ₁ / μ₂**: governs the bending

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Which Boundary Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given two media with mu1, mu2 separated by a boundary] --> Q2{What quantity is known?}
    Q2 -- B vector in Medium 1 --> R1[Resolve B1 into normal Bn1 and tangential Bt1]
    R1 --> R2[Apply Bn1 = Bn2 to find Bn2]
    R1 --> R3[Apply Bt1/mu1 = Bt2/mu2 to find Bt2]
    R2 --> R4[Compute angle theta2 = arctan Bt2 over Bn2]
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Compute H2 = B2 over mu2]

    Q2 -- H vector in Medium 1 --> S1[Resolve H1 into normal Hn1 and tangential Ht1]
    S1 --> S2[Apply Hn1 x mu1 = Hn2 x mu2 to find Hn2]
    S1 --> S3[Apply Ht1 = Ht2 to find Ht2]
    S2 --> S4[Compute angle theta2 = arctan Ht2 over Hn2]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Compute B2 = mu2 x H2]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*State and explain the boundary condition for the normal component of the magnetic flux density at the interface between two magnetic media.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The boundary condition for the normal component of **B** is derived from Gauss's law for magnetism, **∇ · B = 0**, which states that the net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero. Applying this to a small pillbox of cross-section ΔS that straddles the interface and taking the limit as the height → 0, the side wall contribution vanishes and we obtain:

$$B_{1n} = B_{2n}$$

That is, the **normal component of the magnetic flux density is continuous** across the boundary between two magnetic media. This continuity is a direct consequence of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles (magnetic charges) in nature. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*What happens to the tangential component of **H** at a magnetic boundary where a surface current of density **K** flows? Write the relevant equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When a free surface current density **K** (in A/m) flows along the interface, the tangential component of the magnetic field intensity **H** becomes discontinuous. The relevant boundary condition, derived from Ampere's circuital law ∇ × **H** = **J**, is:

$$\hat{n} \times (\vec{H_1} - \vec{H_2}) = \vec{K}$$

In scalar form, the magnitude of the discontinuity equals the component of the surface current density normal to the loop: **H_{1t} − H_{2t} = K_n**. Physically, the surface current acts as a magnetic "voltage source" at the boundary, causing a step change in **H**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE** of the two Part-B alternatives. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Derivation + Numerical Application**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Style]**

**(a)** Derive the boundary conditions for the **normal component of B** and the **tangential component of H** at the interface between two magnetic media with permeabilities μ₁ and μ₂. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnetic field **B₁** in medium 1 (μᵣ₁ = 4) makes an angle θ₁ = 30° with the normal to the boundary with medium 2 (μᵣ₂ = 1, free space). Find the angle θ₂ that **B₂** makes with the normal, the ratio of magnitudes B₂/B₁, and the tangential and normal components of **H** in both media. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Normal B — using a pillbox:**
Gauss's law for magnetism: **∇ · B = 0**.
Integral form over a small pillbox of cross-section ΔS straddling the boundary:

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = B_{1n}\Delta S - B_{2n}\Delta S + 0 = 0$$

Thus **B_{1n} = B_{2n}**. Assumption: no magnetic monopoles, no flux absorption. **(3.5 marks)**

**Tangential H — using a small loop:**
Ampere's law (static): **∇ × H = J**.
Integral form over a small rectangular loop of length Δl parallel to the surface, height Δh → 0, with no enclosed surface current:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H_{1t}\Delta l - H_{2t}\Delta l = 0$$

Thus **H_{1t} = H_{2t}**. Assumption: no free surface current at the interface, finite volume current density J. **(3.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** μ₁ = μᵣ₁μ₀ = 4μ₀, μ₂ = μ₀, θ₁ = 30°.

**Step 1 — Find θ₂ using the magnetic refraction law:**

$$\frac{\tan\theta_1}{\tan\theta_2} = \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \frac{4\mu_0}{\mu_0} = 4$$

$$\tan\theta_2 = \frac{\tan 30°}{4} = \frac{0.5774}{4} = 0.1443$$

$$\theta_2 = \arctan(0.1443) \approx 8.21°$$

**Step 2 — Find the ratio of magnitudes:**
Let B₁ = 1 (normalised). Then:
- B_{1n} = B₁ cos 30° = 0.8660
- B_{1t} = B₁ sin 30° = 0.5000

By continuity: B_{2n} = B_{1n} = 0.8660
By tangential H continuity: B_{1t}/μ₁ = B_{2t}/μ₂
- B_{2t} = B_{1t} × (μ₂/μ₁) = 0.5000 × (1/4) = 0.1250

Magnitude of B₂:
$$B_2 = \sqrt{B_{2n}^2 + B_{2t}^2} = \sqrt{0.8660^2 + 0.1250^2} = \sqrt{0.7500 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.7656} \approx 0.8750$$

Ratio: **B₂/B₁ ≈ 0.875 (unitless)**.

**Step 3 — Compute H fields:**
- **H₁ = B₁/μ₁ = 1/(4μ₀) = 1/(4 × 4π × 10⁻⁷) ≈ 1.989 × 10⁵ A/m**
- H_{1n} = H₁ cos 30° = 1.722 × 10⁵ A/m
- H_{1t} = H₁ sin 30° = 9.947 × 10⁴ A/m

- **H₂ = B₂/μ₂ = 0.875/μ₀ ≈ 6.963 × 10⁵ A/m**
- H_{2n} = H₂ cos 8.21° = 6.962 × 10⁵ × 0.9898 ≈ 6.890 × 10⁵ A/m
- H_{2t} = H₂ sin 8.21° = 6.963 × 10⁵ × 0.1428 ≈ 9.943 × 10⁴ A/m ✓ (matches H_{1t} as expected)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constitutive relation B = μH** when going from **B** continuity to **H** discontinuity (or vice versa). This is the #1 error in numerical questions.
> 2. **Using sine instead of tangent** in the refraction law. The KTU marking scheme deducts **1 mark** specifically for writing sin θ₁/sin θ₂.
> 3. **Not converting μᵣ to absolute μ** by multiplying with μ₀. Many students write the ratio as μᵣ₁/μᵣ₂, which is correct numerically, but should be expressed as **μ₁/μ₂ = μᵣ₁/μᵣ₂** with proper justification.
> 4. **Mixing up B and H**: writing B_{1t} = B_{2t} (WRONG) instead of H_{1t} = H_{2t}.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Surface Current + Conceptual Depth**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Style]**

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the boundary condition for the tangential component of **H** at the interface between two magnetic media when a surface current density **K** is present. Discuss the physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long cylindrical conductor of radius *a* carries a uniform current *I* along its axis. The conductor is made of material with relative permeability μᵣ₁ = 100. It is surrounded by free space (μᵣ₂ = 1). Determine the magnetic field **H** just inside and just outside the conductor surface, and verify the boundary condition for the tangential **H** at the surface. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Starting equation:** Ampere's law: **∇ × H = J** (magnetostatics).

**Step 1:** Apply Stokes' theorem to a small rectangular loop of length Δl parallel to the boundary and height Δh straddling it:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2:** In the limit Δh → 0, the two vertical sides contribute zero. The enclosed current becomes the *surface current*:

$$\int_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \vec{K} \cdot \hat{a}_n \Delta l$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$H_{1t}\Delta l - H_{2t}\Delta l = K_n \Delta l$$

$$H_{1t} - H_{2t} = K_n \quad \text{or vectorially} \quad \hat{n} \times (\vec{H_1} - \vec{H_2}) = \vec{K}$$

**Physical significance:** A surface current density acts as the magnetic analogue of a surface charge in electrostatics. It causes a **step discontinuity** in the tangential **H** field, equivalent to a magnetic "voltage" jump. This is critical in designing **superconducting magnets**, **railguns**, and **electromagnetic launchers** where enormous surface currents exist. **(3.5 marks for derivation + 3.5 marks for discussion)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Long cylinder, radius *a*, current *I* along z-axis, μᵣ₁ = 100 (inside), μᵣ₂ = 1 (outside).

**Step 1 — H just inside the conductor (r = a⁻):**
By Ampere's law for an Amperian loop of radius *a*:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H_{\text{in}} \cdot 2\pi a = I_{\text{enc}} = I$$

$$\boxed{H_{\text{in}} = \frac{I}{2\pi a}}$$

Direction: azimuthal (φ̂) by right-hand rule.

**Step 2 — H just outside the conductor (r = a⁺):**
The enclosed current is still *I* (the whole current passes through):

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H_{\text{out}} \cdot 2\pi a = I$$

$$\boxed{H_{\text{out}} = \frac{I}{2\pi a}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify boundary condition:**
The tangential component of **H** (azimuthal direction) just inside = I/(2πa) and just outside = I/(2πa). Therefore:

$$H_{1t} - H_{2t} = \frac{I}{2\pi a} - \frac{I}{2\pi a} = 0 = K$$

**Conclusion:** There is no surface current flowing on the cylindrical surface (the current is distributed within the volume), so the tangential **H** is continuous. **The boundary condition is verified.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Errors in Part B Numericals:**
> 1. **Confusing H and B**: Computing B = μ₀H for *both* sides regardless of medium. Always use **B = μᵣμ₀H** with the correct μᵣ for each region.
> 2. **Direction mistakes**: Forgetting the right-hand rule (φ̂ direction) — KTU deducts **1 mark** for missing direction.
> 3. **Not stating the boundary condition explicitly**: After computing H inside and outside, you *must* write the equation **H_{1t} = H_{2t}** (or H_{1t} − H_{2t} = K) and confirm it holds.
> 4. **Wrong Amperian loop**: Using rectangular loop instead of circular — for a cylindrical conductor, the symmetry demands a circular Amperian loop.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Normal B is always continuous** — B_{1n} = B_{2n} — a direct consequence of **∇ · B = 0** and the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.
- ✅ **Tangential H is continuous ONLY when K = 0** — H_{1t} = H_{2t}. Otherwise, **H_{1t} − H_{2t} = K_n**.
- ✅ **Constitutive relation B = μH** is the bridge between the two conditions. It is the source of "bending" of magnetic field lines.
- ✅ **Magnetic refraction law** uses **tangent**, NOT sine: **tan θ₁ / tan θ₂ = μ₁ / μ₂**.
- ✅ **Analogy with electric fields**: D's normal is discontinuous (by σ), but B's normal is continuous. E's tangential is continuous, and H's tangential is continuous (in absence of K).
- ✅ **Pillbox** is for the *normal* component (uses surface integral, divergence theorem).
- ✅ **Small loop** is for the *tangential* component (uses line integral, Stokes' theorem).
- ✅ **Surface current K** has units of **A/m** and acts as a magnetic "charge" that discontinuously changes H.
- ✅ **Ferromagnetic materials (μᵣ >> 1)** pull magnetic field lines into themselves; the air gap is a region of high reluctance.
- ✅ **KTU exam favourite**: Derive both conditions + solve a numerical involving magnetic refraction.
- ✅ **Memorise**: B_{1n} = B_{2n}, H_{1t} − H_{2t} = K (general), tan θ₁/tan θ₂ = μ₁/μ₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question says "with K = 0", immediately write the two conditions: B_{1n} = B_{2n} and H_{1t} = H_{2t}. These two equations are the *foundation* of every numerical you will encounter. Then, use **B = μH** to switch between **B** and **H** as needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inductance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Inductance

**Inductance** is the property of an electrical conductor by which a change in current flowing through it induces an electromotive force (EMF) in both the conductor itself (self-inductance) and in any nearby conductors (mutual inductance). It is measured in **Henries (H)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Inductance is a fundamental circuit parameter that quantifies the magnetic flux linkage per unit current. For a conductor carrying current $I$, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ is produced, and the ratio of total flux linkage ($\lambda = N\Phi$) to the current defines the inductance.

### 1.1.1 Self-Inductance (L)

Self-inductance is the property of a single coil or circuit element whereby a change in its own current produces a changing magnetic flux that induces an EMF in itself.

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{N\Phi}{I}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Self-inductance (Henries, H)
- $\lambda$ = Total flux linkage (Weber-turns, Wb-turns)
- $N$ = Number of turns
- $\Phi$ = Magnetic flux through one turn (Webers, Wb)
- $I$ = Current through the coil (Amperes, A)

### 1.1.2 Mutual Inductance (M)

Mutual inductance is the property of two coupled coils whereby a current in one coil produces a magnetic flux that links the second coil, inducing an EMF in it.

$$M_{12} = \frac{\lambda_{12}}{I_1} = \frac{N_2 \Phi_{12}}{I_1}$$

Where:
- $M_{12}$ = Mutual inductance between coil 1 and coil 2 (H)
- $\Phi_{12}$ = Flux linking coil 2 due to current in coil 1
- $N_2$ = Number of turns in coil 2

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocity Theorem for Inductance:** $M_{12} = M_{21} = M$. Mutual inductance is symmetric, meaning the flux linkage produced in coil 1 by current in coil 2 equals that produced in coil 2 by the same current in coil 1.

### 1.1.3 Coefficient of Coupling (k)

The fraction of the total flux from one coil that links another coil is called the **coefficient of coupling**.

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}, \quad 0 \leq k \leq 1$$

- $k = 1$: Perfect coupling (all flux links both coils)
- $k = 0$: No coupling (fluxes are independent)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Flywheel:**
> Imagine spinning a heavy flywheel connected to a water pipe. When you suddenly try to stop the water flow, the flywheel keeps pushing water forward for a moment (it "resists" the change). Similarly, an inductor "resists" changes in current. The **larger the inductance**, the more "magnetic inertia" the circuit has — just like a heavier flywheel has more rotational inertia.
>
> **Mutual Inductance Analogy:** Picture two pendulums connected by a spring on the same wall. When you push pendulum A, the spring transfers energy, causing pendulum B to swing too. The spring's stiffness represents mutual coupling — the stiffer the spring, the stronger the coupling.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
> |--------|----------|--------------|
> | $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
> | $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability | Dimensionless |
> | $L$ | Inductance | Henry (H) = Wb/A = V·s/A |
> | $M$ | Mutual Inductance | Henry (H) |
> | $k$ | Coupling coefficient | $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |
> | $W_m$ | Magnetic energy | Joules (J) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal and External Inductance

When current flows through a conductor, the magnetic flux exists both **inside** the conductor (internal inductance) and **outside** in the surrounding space (external inductance).

$$L_{total} = L_{internal} + L_{external}$$

**Internal Inductance** (for a straight wire of radius $a$ and length $l$):

$$L_{int} = \frac{\mu l}{8\pi}$$

**External Inductance** (for two parallel wires or transmission line geometries):

$$L_{ext} = \frac{\mu l}{\pi} \ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \quad \text{(for two parallel wires)}$$

## 2.2 Neumann's Formula for Mutual Inductance

The rigorous general expression for mutual inductance between two circuits is given by the **Neumann Equation**:

$$M_{12} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \oint_{C_1} \oint_{C_2} \frac{d\vec{l}_1 \cdot d\vec{l}_2}{R}$$

Where $R$ is the distance between two differential current elements on the two circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is fundamental for deriving inductances of any arbitrary geometry — from circular loops to solenoids to coaxial cables. The double line integral means we integrate over all pairs of line elements on the two conductors.

## 2.3 Standard Derivation Cases

### 2.3.1 Mutual Inductance Between Two Coaxial Coils (Concentric Solenoids)

For an inner solenoid of radius $r_1$ and $N_1$ turns placed coaxially inside an outer solenoid of $N_2$ turns:

$$M = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 A_1}{l}$$

Where $A_1 = \pi r_1^2$ and $l$ is the common length.

### 2.3.2 Self-Inductance of a Solenoid

For a long solenoid with $N$ turns, length $l$, and cross-sectional area $A$:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{l}$$

### 2.3.3 Self-Inductance of a Toroid

For a toroid with $N$ turns, mean radius $R$, and cross-sectional area $A$:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{2\pi R}$$

### 2.3.4 Inductance of a Coaxial Cable (Per Unit Length)

For a coaxial cable with inner conductor radius $a$, outer conductor inner radius $b$:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \quad \text{[H/m]}$$

## 2.4 Energy Stored in an Inductor

The energy stored in the magnetic field of an inductor carrying current $I$ is:

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

In differential form (energy density):

$$w_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu} = \frac{1}{2} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{H} \quad \text{[J/m}^3\text{]}$$

Total magnetic energy in a volume $V$:

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \vec{B} \cdot \vec{H} \, dV$$

## 2.5 Coupled Circuit Equations

For two coupled inductors with currents $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$\lambda_1 = L_1 I_1 \pm M I_2$$
$$\lambda_2 = L_2 I_2 \pm M I_1$$

The sign of $M$ depends on the **dot convention** — whether the mutual flux aids or opposes the self-flux.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Table**
>
> | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
> |---|----------|---------|------|
> | 1 | Self-Inductance | $L = N\Phi / I$ | H |
> | 2 | Mutual Inductance | $M = N_2 \Phi_{12} / I_1$ | H |
> | 3 | Neumann's Formula | $M = (\mu_0 / 4\pi) \oint \oint (dl_1 \, dl_2) / R$ | H |
> | 4 | Coupling Coefficient | $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | — |
> | 5 | Solenoid Inductance | $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / l$ | H |
> | 6 | Toroid Inductance | $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A / (2\pi R)$ | H |
> | 7 | Coaxial Cable Inductance | $L = (\mu_0 \mu_r / 2\pi) \ln(b/a)$ | H/m |
> | 8 | Two Parallel Wires Inductance | $L = (\mu_0 \mu_r / \pi) \ln(D/a)$ | H/m |
> | 9 | Internal Inductance (wire) | $L_{int} = \mu l / (8\pi)$ | H |
> | 10 | Energy Stored | $W_m = (1/2) L I^2$ | J |
> | 11 | Energy Density | $w_m = B^2 / (2\mu) = (1/2) \vec{B} \cdot \vec{H}$ | J/m³ |
> | 12 | Total Magnetic Energy | $W_m = (1/2) \int_V \vec{B} \cdot \vec{H} \, dV$ | J |
> | 13 | Flux Linkage | $\lambda = L I$ | Wb-turns |
> | 14 | Induced EMF | $\mathcal{E} = -L (dI/dt)$ | V |
> | 15 | Coupled Inductor Flux | $\lambda_1 = L_1 I_1 \pm M I_2$ | Wb-turns |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Inductance Matters in Engineering:**
> 1. **Power Systems:** Transformers rely on mutual inductance to step voltages up or down.
> 2. **RF Engineering:** Coaxial cables, microstrip lines, and inductors are designed using precise inductance calculations.
> 3. **Filters:** Inductors (with capacitors) form LC filters used in signal processing.
> 4. **Energy Storage:** Inductors in SMPS and electric vehicles store magnetic energy.
> 5. **Wireless Charging:** Mutual inductance between primary and secondary coils enables contactless power transfer.
> 6. **Motors and Generators:** Rotating machines use magnetic coupling for energy conversion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Self-Inductance of a Solenoid

**Given:** A long solenoid with $N$ turns, length $l$, cross-sectional area $A$, carrying current $I$ in a medium of permeability $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$.

**Step 1: Magnetic Field Inside a Solenoid**

The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is uniform and given by Ampere's Law:

$$B = \mu \frac{N I}{l}$$

**Step 2: Magnetic Flux Through One Turn**

The flux through a single turn of cross-section $A$ is:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A = \mu \frac{N I A}{l}$$

**Step 3: Total Flux Linkage**

The total flux linkage is the flux through one turn multiplied by the total number of turns:

$$\lambda = N \Phi = N \cdot \mu \frac{N I A}{l} = \frac{\mu N^2 I A}{l}$$

**Step 4: Self-Inductance**

By definition $L = \lambda / I$:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu N^2 A}{l}}$$

This is one of the **most frequently tested derivations** in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mutual Inductance Between Two Coaxial Solenoids

**Given:** A long solenoid 1 (primary) with $N_1$ turns, length $l_1$, radius $r_1$, carrying current $I_1$. Solenoid 2 (secondary) with $N_2$ turns, length $l_2 > l_1$, radius $r_2 > r_1$, placed coaxially around solenoid 1.

**Step 1: Field Due to Solenoid 1**

The magnetic field inside solenoid 1 is uniform:

$$B_1 = \mu_0 \frac{N_1 I_1}{l_1}$$

Outside solenoid 1, the field is essentially zero (ideal solenoid assumption).

**Step 2: Flux Through One Turn of Solenoid 2**

Since solenoid 2 surrounds solenoid 1, the entire field of solenoid 1 links every turn of solenoid 2 (within the region $l_1$):

$$\Phi_{12} = B_1 \cdot A_1 = \mu_0 \frac{N_1 I_1}{l_1} \cdot \pi r_1^2$$

**Step 3: Total Flux Linkage in Solenoid 2**

Since only $N_2$ turns link this flux (assuming all turns lie within $l_1$):

$$\lambda_{12} = N_2 \Phi_{12} = \mu_0 \frac{N_1 N_2 \pi r_1^2 I_1}{l_1}$$

**Step 4: Mutual Inductance**

$$M = \frac{\lambda_{12}}{I_1}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 A_1}{l_1}}$$

**Step 5: Self-Inductance of Solenoid 1 (Verification)**

$$L_1 = \frac{\mu_0 N_1^2 A_1}{l_1}$$

**Step 6: Coupling Coefficient**

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}} = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 A_1 / l_1}{\sqrt{(\mu_0 N_1^2 A_1 / l_1)(\mu_0 N_2^2 A_2 / l_2)}} = \sqrt{\frac{A_1}{A_2}}$$

For perfect coupling, $A_1 = A_2$ (i.e., $r_1 = r_2$), giving $k = 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inductance of a Coaxial Cable (Per Unit Length)
