# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define magnetostatic energy density. Write its expression and state the unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Magnetostatic energy density $w_m$ is the magnetic energy stored per unit volume at a point in a magnetostatic field.

$$w_m = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \quad \text{(Vector form)}$$

For a linear, isotropic medium with permeability $\mu$:

$$w_m = \frac{1}{2} \mu H^2 = \frac{B^2}{2\mu}$$

**SI Unit:** Joules per cubic meter ($\text{J/m}^3$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: State the three equivalent expressions for the total energy stored in a magnetostatic field and identify the variables used in each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three equivalent forms are:
1. **Field form:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV$ — uses field vectors.
2. **Source form:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV$ — uses vector potential and current density.
3. **Circuit form:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ — uses inductance and current.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (INTERNAL CHOICE)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for energy density in a magnetostatic field starting from the work done in establishing a current in an inductor of self-inductance L carrying current I. (7 marks)

**(b)** A long solenoid of length 1 m, radius 5 cm, with 1500 turns, carries a current of 10 A. Compute (i) the energy stored, (ii) the energy density, and (iii) the inductance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Consider an inductor with self-inductance L and current i(t).**

**Step 2 — Back-EMF induced:**

$$e = -L \frac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Work done by external source in time dt:**

$$dW = -e \cdot i \cdot dt = L \, i \, di$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from i = 0 to I:**

$$W = \int_0^I L \, i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $L = N\Phi/I$ and $\Phi = \int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} N I \int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV$$

**Step 6 — Apply $\mathbf{J} = \frac{1}{\mu}\nabla \times \mathbf{B}$ and vector identity:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2\mu} \int_V B^2 \, dV = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV$$

**Step 7 — Energy density:**

$$w_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \quad \text{(J/m}^3\text{)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Energy stored:**

Number of turns $N = 1500$, length $\ell = 1$ m, radius $a = 0.05$ m, $I = 10$ A.

$$B = \mu_0 n I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1500)(10) = 6\pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ T} \approx 0.01885 \text{ T}$$

Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi a^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

$$W_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot \ell = \frac{(0.01885)^2}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1$$

$$W_m = \frac{3.553 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \approx 141.37 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{W_m \approx 1.110 \text{ J}}$$

**(ii) Energy density:**

$$w_m = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} = \frac{(0.01885)^2}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \approx 141.37 \text{ J/m}^3$$

**(iii) Inductance:**

From $W_m = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$:

$$L = \frac{2W_m}{I^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.110}{(10)^2} = 0.0222 \text{ H} = 22.2 \text{ mH}$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for total magnetic energy stored in a system of two magnetically coupled coils with self-inductances L1, L2, mutual inductance M, and currents I1, I2. Discuss both aiding and opposing cases. (7 marks)

**(b)** Two coupled coils have L1 = 0.4 H, L2 = 0.3 H, M = 0.1 H. The currents are I1 = 5 A and I2 = 3 A. Calculate the total magnetic energy in (i) aiding connection and (ii) opposing connection. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Start with I1 = 0, I2 = 0. Raise I1 from 0 to I1 while I2 = 0:**

$$W_1 = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2$$

**Step 2 — Hold I1 constant. Now raise I2 from 0 to I2. Work done by source of coil 2:**

$$dW_2 = (L_2 I_2 + M I_1) dI_2$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to I2:**

$$W_2 = \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 + M I_1 I_2$$

**Step 4 — Total energy:**

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 + M I_1 I_2$$

**Step 5 — For aiding connection (dot convention satisfied):**

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 + \vert M \vert I_1 I_2$$

**Step 6 — For opposing connection:**

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 - \vert M \vert I_1 I_2$$

**Step 7 — Note that $W_m$ must always be positive for passive systems. This requires:**

$$L_1 L_2 \geq M^2 \quad \text{(otherwise, energy becomes negative — unphysical)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.4$ H, $L_2 = 0.3$ H, $M = 0.1$ H, $I_1 = 5$ A, $I_2 = 3$ A.

**(i) Aiding connection ($+M$):**

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2}(0.4)(5)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(0.3)(3)^2 + (0.1)(5)(3)$$

$$W_m = \frac{1}{2}(0.4)(25) + \frac{1}{2}(0.3)(9) + (0.1)(15)$$

$$W_m = 5.0 + 1.35 + 1.5 = \boxed{7.85 \text{ J}}$$

**(ii) Opposing connection ($-M$):**

$$W_m = 5.0 + 1.35 - 1.5 = \boxed{4.85 \text{ J}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL CALLOUT:**
> 1. **Sign of M is the #1 trap:** Students often forget to apply $+$ or $-$ based on aiding/opposing. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** consistently for this.
> 2. **Units:** Always state $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m explicitly in derivations. **No credit** for bare final numbers.
> 3. **Field form vs Source form:** Do not confuse $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J}$ with $\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$. They differ by a factor involving $\nabla \times$ operations.
> 4. **Surface integral vanishing:** In derivations involving divergence theorem, **always mention** that the surface integral vanishes at infinity due to $1/r$ decay — this is the most commonly skipped step costing 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Energy must be non-negative:** If you obtain negative $W_m$ for an opposing-connection problem, double-check the sign of $M$ and confirm $L_1 L_2 \geq M^2$.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these 8 high-yield points before any KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:**

- ✅ **Energy density** in a magnetostatic field: $w_m = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$ — Units: $\text{J/m}^3$.
- ✅ **Total field energy:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV$.
- ✅ **Source form:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV$ (uses $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$).
- ✅ **Circuit form:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.
- ✅ **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- ✅ **Two-coil coupled system:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 \pm M I_1 I_2$; sign of $M$ depends on dot convention.
- ✅ **Force from energy:** $F = +\partial W_m / \partial x \vert_{I=\text{const}}$ — constant current assumption.
- ✅ **Passivity constraint:** $L_1 L_2 \geq M^2$; otherwise stored energy becomes negative (unphysical).
- ✅ **Vector identity used:** $\mathbf{A} \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{B}) = \mathbf{B} \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) - \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B})$.
- ✅ **Surface integral at infinity vanishes** because $\vert \mathbf{A} \vert \sim 1/r$ and $\vert \mathbf{B} \vert \sim 1/r^2$, so the integrand decays as $1/r^4$ and the surface scales as $r^2$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Energy Stored in Magnetostatic Fields** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Induction and Maxwell-Heaviside Equations: Faraday's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Induction and Maxwell-Heaviside Equations: Faraday's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** states that the electromotive force (EMF) induced in any closed conducting loop is directly proportional to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through the surface bounded by that loop, with respect to time. In Maxwell's complete formalism, it is one of the four foundational **Maxwell-Heaviside Equations** that unify electricity and magnetism into a single classical field theory.

Mathematically, in its most general integral form, Faraday's Law is expressed as:

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \frac{d}{dt} \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

The line integral on the left side represents the **induced EMF** $\mathcal{E}$ around the closed contour $\mathcal{C}$, while the surface integral on the right side represents the **magnetic flux** $\Phi$ penetrating the surface $\mathcal{S}$ bounded by $\mathcal{C}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Note (KTU Board Expectation):** The negative sign is **not** an arbitrary choice — it physically represents **Lenz's Law**. Examiners frequently deduct marks if students write Faraday's law without mentioning that the negative sign encodes the *opposition* to the cause of flux change.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a circular water pond with a small paddle wheel floating in it. The wheel is connected to a tiny generator (a propeller in a magnetic field). Now, suppose the pond surface tilts and starts swirling — that "swirl" is analogous to a **changing magnetic flux**. The paddle wheel begins to rotate **opposite** to the swirl, just as the induced current opposes the change in flux that created it.

- The **swirling water** = changing magnetic field $\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$
- The **paddle wheel rotation** = induced EMF and current
- The **opposition direction** = Lenz's Law (the minus sign)
- The **rotational speed of the wheel** = magnitude of induced EMF (proportional to the rate of change)

In essence, Faraday's Law tells us that **magnetic fields that vary in time behave exactly like a non-conservative electric field source**, and this is the bridge that connects electricity to magnetism and gives rise to electromagnetic waves.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are routinely used while solving Faraday's Law problems:

- **Permeability of free space** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ (or $\text{T}\cdot\text{m/A}$)
- **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum** $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$
- **Magnetic flux** $\Phi$ is measured in **webers (Wb)** or equivalently **tesla·meter² (T·m²)**
- **EMF** $\mathcal{E}$ is measured in **volts (V)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever numerical problems are solved, students must specify the **SI unit** of every derived quantity (flux, EMF, induced current) explicitly. Omitting units is a common reason for partial mark deductions in KTU answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Physical Scenarios Producing Induced EMF

Faraday's original observations (1831) can be grouped into two distinct physical situations. KTU examiners often pose a comparison question of this nature.

**Scenario 1 — Transformer EMF (Stationary loop in time-varying field):**
- The conducting loop is **stationary** in space.
- The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is a **function of time**.
- The flux changes because $B$ changes, not because the loop moves.
- The induced electric field is **non-conservative** and circulates around $\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$.

**Scenario 2 — Motional EMF (Moving loop in static field):**
- The conducting loop **moves** through a magnetic field.
- The magnetic field is **static in time**.
- The flux changes because the **area** or **orientation** of the loop changes.
- The induced EMF arises from the magnetic component of the **Lorentz force** on charge carriers, $\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Unified view:** Both scenarios produce the same physical observable (induced EMF) and both are encapsulated in the single differential form $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$. The distinction is only pedagogical.

### 2.2 The Differential Form (Maxwell–Faraday Equation)

Using **Stokes' Theorem**, the integral form transforms into a local, point-wise differential identity. This is one of the four celebrated **Maxwell-Heaviside Equations**.

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Equating with the flux time-derivative:

$$\int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S} = - \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Since this must hold for **any arbitrary surface** $\mathcal{S}$, the integrands themselves must be equal:

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \vec{E} = - \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $\nabla \times \vec{E} \neq 0$:** A non-zero curl means the electric field is **irrotational no longer** — it forms closed loops in space, just like magnetic field lines around a current. This is the deepest insight of Faraday's Law: time-varying $\vec{B}$ fields **generate** $\vec{E}$ fields.

### 2.3 Lenz's Law — Why the Minus Sign?

The negative sign is **Lenz's Law** formalized. It expresses the conservation of energy within the electromagnetic framework:

- If flux is **increasing**, the induced current flows such that its **own magnetic field opposes the increase**.
- If flux is **decreasing**, the induced current flows such that its **own magnetic field tries to maintain the flux**.

If the sign were positive, the system would **violate conservation of energy** by self-amplifying — a runaway process that is physically forbidden.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for problems on this topic. Memorize the differential form, integral form, and the two motional cases.

| **Form** | **Equation** | **When to Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Law (Integral) | $\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ where $\Phi = \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Single loop, total flux known | V (volts) |
| Faraday's Law (Differential) | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ | Field-point analysis, wave equations | V/m and T |
| Motional EMF (Rod) | $\mathcal{E} = \int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Moving conductor in static $\vec{B}$ | V |
| Motional EMF (Sliding Bar) | $\mathcal{E} = B L v$ | Rectangular loop with sliding side of length $L$ | V |
| Transformer EMF | $\mathcal{E} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $N$-turn coil, time-varying flux | V |
| Self-Inductance | $\mathcal{E} = -L \frac{di}{dt}$ where $L = \frac{N\Phi}{i}$ | Coil's response to own current change | V, H |
| Induced Current | $i = \frac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = -\frac{1}{R} \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Ohm's law + Faraday | A |
| Induced Electric Field (Solenoider) | $E = \frac{r}{2} \left\vert \frac{dB}{dt} \right\vert$ for $r \le a$ | Long solenoid, $r$ inside radius $a$ | V/m |
| Induced Electric Field (Solenoider) | $E = \frac{a^{2}}{2r} \left\vert \frac{dB}{dt} \right\vert$ for $r \ge a$ | Long solenoid, $r$ outside radius $a$ | V/m |
| Maxwell-Faraday in Matter | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ (no modification by $\vec{M}$) | Inside magnetic media | V/m, T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** The differential form $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ is **universal** — it is **NOT** modified by the presence of magnetic materials. This contrasts with Ampere's law, where $\vec{H}$ appears. KTU board questions often test this subtlety.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Faraday's Law is the operating principle behind virtually every electrical energy-conversion device in modern engineering:

- **Electric generators** (power plants, wind turbines, dynamos) — mechanical rotation in a magnetic field.
- **Transformers** — stationary coils linked by time-varying flux.
- **Induction motors and induction cooktops** — eddy current heating.
- **Wireless charging pads** — alternating current in a primary coil creates changing flux in a secondary coil.
- **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** scanners — Faraday's law underpins the gradient coil operation.
- **Electromagnetic braking** in trains and roller coasters — induced eddy currents oppose motion.
- **Metal detectors** at airports — distortion of induced flux patterns by metallic objects.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer scripts, ending with a real-world application sentence often earns the examiner's "good impression" mark, especially in 14-mark derivations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Integral to Differential Form (Maxwell-Faraday)

We start with the integral form of Faraday's Law and apply Stokes' theorem rigorously.

**Step 1 — Write the integral Faraday's law with EMF as line integral:**

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \frac{d}{dt} \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This equation says: take the closed path $\mathcal{C}$ bounding any surface $\mathcal{S}$, and the circulation of $\vec{E}$ around $\mathcal{C}$ equals the negative rate of change of flux through $\mathcal{S}$.

**Step 2 — Bring the time derivative inside the surface integral (the loop is fixed in space, so $d\vec{S}$ is time-independent):**

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

The justification is that for a stationary contour, differentiation and integration commute: $\frac{d}{dt} \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$. This is a subtle but essential point.

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' theorem to the line integral on the left side:**

Stokes' theorem in vector calculus states:

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This converts a line integral around a closed loop into a surface integral of the curl of the vector field over any surface bounded by that loop.

**Step 4 — Substitute Stokes' theorem result back into the equation:**

$$\int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S} = - \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Both sides are now surface integrals over the *same* surface $\mathcal{S}$.

**Step 5 — Combine integrals and use locality:**

$$\int_{\mathcal{S}} \left[ (\nabla \times \vec{E}) + \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \right] \cdot d\vec{S} = 0$$

Since this must hold for **any arbitrary** surface $\mathcal{S}$ in space, the integrand itself must vanish point-wise:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = - \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

This is the **Maxwell-Faraday equation**, the local (point-wise) form of Faraday's law.

**Step 6 — Limiting case check (electrostatics):**

If the magnetic field is static in time, $\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} = 0$, and the equation reduces to $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$, which is the familiar condition for a **conservative** electrostatic field. This correctly recovers electrostatics as a special case, confirming the consistency of the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Motional EMF for a Sliding Bar on Rails

Consider a U-shaped conducting rail in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B} = B\hat{z}$, with a conducting bar of length $L$ sliding along the rails at velocity $\vec{v} = v\hat{x}$. The bar, rails, and the connection form a closed rectangular loop whose area changes with time.

**Step 1 — Express the area of the loop as a function of time:**

Let $x(t)$ be the position of the sliding bar from the fixed end of the U-rail. The loop area is:

$$A(t) = L \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnetic flux through the loop:**

$$\Phi(t) = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B \cdot A(t) = B L x(t)$$

The flux is positive when $\vec{B}$ and the area vector point in the same direction.

**Step 3 — Differentiate the flux with respect to time:**

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = B L \frac{dx}{dt} = B L v$$

The rate of change of flux equals the magnetic field strength times the length of the moving bar times its velocity.

**Step 4 — Apply Faraday's Law to obtain the induced EMF:**

$$\mathcal{E} = - \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = - B L v$$

The negative sign indicates the polarity is such that the induced current opposes the increase in flux (Lenz's law).

**Step 5 — Verify using the Lorentz force viewpoint:**

A free electron in the moving bar experiences a magnetic force:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{mag}} = q \vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (-e)(v\hat{x}) \times (B\hat{z}) = (-e)(vB)(\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) = (-e)(vB)(-\hat{y}) = evB\hat{y}$$

Electrons accumulate at the trailing end, leaving the leading end positive. The effective electric field driving current through the external circuit is:

$$\mathcal{E} = \int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l} = vB L$$

The magnitude matches the Faraday result, and the sign convention gives the same answer. The two viewpoints are **physically consistent** — this is a major strength of Maxwell's framework.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Motional vs Transformer EMF

The following matrix is a high-yield comparison table often asked in KTU module tests.

| **Aspect** | **Motional EMF** | **Transformer EMF** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause of flux change | Loop **moves** through static $\vec{B}$ | $\vec{B}$ **changes** in time around stationary loop |
| Origin of force | Magnetic Lorentz force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ | Induced electric field from $\nabla \times \vec{E}$ |
| Equation | $\mathcal{E} = \oint (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$ | $\mathcal{E} = -\int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$ |
| Field involved | $\vec{B}$ only (static) | $\vec{B}(t)$ time-varying |
| Example | DC generator, homopolar disk | AC transformer, induction coil |
| Energy source | Mechanical work done against $\vec{F} = i\vec{L} \times \vec{B}$ | Energy supplied by the source driving $\vec{B}$ change |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Solenoid with Time-Varying Current

A long solenoid of radius $a = 5 \ \text{cm}$ has $n = 1000 \ \text{turns/m}$ carrying a current $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$ where $I_0 = 2 \ \text{A}$ and $\omega = 100\pi \ \text{rad/s}$. Compute the induced electric field at (a) $r = 2 \ \text{cm}$ and (b) $r = 10 \ \text{cm}$.

**Step 1 — Magnetic field inside the solenoid:**

For a long solenoid, $\vec{B}(t) = \mu_0 n i(t) \hat{z}$. Inside the solenoid, this is uniform and parallel to the axis. Outside ($r > a$), the field is approximately zero for an ideal solenoid.

$$B(t) = \mu_0 n I_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Time derivative of the field:**

$$\frac{\partial B}{\partial t} = \mu_0 n I_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply symmetry argument for the induced electric field:**

By cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{E}_{\text{ind}}$ must be tangential, i.e., $\vec{E} = E_\phi(r,t) \hat{\phi}$, and depend only on $r$ and $t$.

**Step 4 — Apply the integral form of Faraday's law on a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$:**

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = E_\phi (2\pi r) = - \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Compute flux for $r \le a$:**

$$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \pi r^2 \frac{\partial B}{\partial t}$$

Solving for $E_\phi$:

$$E_\phi(r,t) = - \frac{r}{2} \frac{\partial B}{\partial t} = - \frac{\mu_0 n I_0 \omega r}{2} \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 6 — Compute flux for $r \ge a$:**

$$\Phi = B \cdot \pi a^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \pi a^2 \frac{\partial B}{\partial t}$$

Solving for $E_\phi$:

$$E_\phi(r,t) = - \frac{a^2}{2r} \frac{\partial B}{\partial t} = - \frac{\mu_0 n I_0 \omega a^2}{2r} \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation at (a) $r = 0.02 \ \text{m}$:**

$$E_\phi = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1000)(2)(100\pi)(0.02)}{2} \cdot \vert \cos(\omega t) \vert$$

$$E_\phi \approx 7.90 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \vert \cos(\omega t) \vert \ \text{V/m}$$

The maximum value is approximately **$7.90 \ \text{mV/m}$**.

**Step 8 — Numerical evaluation at (b) $r = 0.10 \ \text{m}$:**

$$E_\phi = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1000)(2)(100\pi)(0.05)^2}{2(0.10)} \cdot \vert \cos(\omega t) \vert$$

$$E_\phi \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \vert \cos(\omega t) \vert \ \text{V/m}$$

The maximum value is approximately **$0.987 \ \text{mV/m}$**.

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern recognition tip:** Notice that the induced $E$ field **grows linearly** with $r$ inside the solenoid and **decays as $1/r$** outside. This $1/r$ tail is what makes transformer coupling between separate coils possible even when they are not perfectly overlapping.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Faraday Induction System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how a changing magnetic environment produces an induced EMF, summarizing the cause-effect chain Faraday's Law describes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time-Varying Current Source i of t] --> B[Primary Coil with N1 turns]
    B --> C[Time-Varying Magnetic Field B of t]
    C --> D{Faraday Coupling Region}
    D --> E[Secondary Coil with N2 turns]
    D --> F[Moving Conductor in B field]
    D --> G[Stationary Loop in dB by dt field]
    E --> H[Induced EMF epsilon equals minus N2 dPhi by dt]
    F --> I[Induced EMF epsilon equals integral v cross B dot dl]
    G --> J[Induced EMF epsilon equals line integral of E dot dl]
    H --> K[Lenz Opposition: Induced Current Opposes Flux Change]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output: Electrical Signal or Mechanical Force]
    L --> M[Application Layer: Transformer / Generator / Inductive Sensor]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **leftmost block** represents the physical driver — a time-varying current source (alternator) or a moving permanent magnet.
- The **middle branching** shows the three canonical geometries KTU problems use: transformer, motional rod, and solenoid.
- The **rightmost blocks** consolidate the three mathematically equivalent EMF expressions.
- The **Lenz Opposition block** is the unifying physical principle that **selects the sign** of the induced current.
- The **application layer** links the abstract law to engineering practice.

### 4.2 Process Flow: Solving a Faraday's Law Problem

The following Mermaid flowchart provides a **systematic problem-solving procedure** that KTU students should follow during the End Semester Examination (ESE).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> S2[Identify Type: Motional or Transformer]
    S2 -->|Motional| M1[Draw Geometry: rod, rails, area]
    S2 -->|Transformer| T1[Draw Geometry: stationary loop, B of t]
    M1 --> M2[Compute Flux Phi as function of time]
    T1 --> T2[Compute Flux Phi as function of time]
    M2 --> M3[Differentiate Phi with respect to t]
    T2 --> T3[Differentiate Phi with respect to t]
    M3 --> M4[Apply epsilon equals minus dPhi by dt]
    T3 --> T4[Apply epsilon equals minus N dPhi by dt]
    M4 --> M5[Compute Induced Current i equals epsilon by R]
    T4 --> T5[Compute Induced Current i equals epsilon by R]
    M5 --> M6[Check Sign: Does Induced B oppose flux change]
    T5 --> T6[Check Sign: Does Induced B oppose flux change]
    M6 --> M7[State Final Answer with Units]
    T6 --> T7[State Final Answer with Units]
    M7 --> EndA[Finish: Box the Answer]
    T7 --> EndB[Finish: Box the Answer]
```

**Why this flowchart matters for KTU valuation:**

Examiners often report that students lose marks not because they lack formulas, but because they **skip steps** in their solution sheets. Following this structured procedure ensures the valuation key's "step marks" are fully awarded.

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: From Faraday's Experiment to Maxwell's Equations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Faraday 1831: Moving Magnet Induces Current] --> P2[Faraday Law: EMF proportional to minus dPhi by dt]
    P3[Lenz 1834: Sign encodes Energy Conservation] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Maxwell 1865: Generalize using Vector Calculus]
    P4 --> P5[Maxwell-Faraday: Curl E equals minus dB by dt]
    P4 --> P6[Combined with Ampere-Maxwell: Electromagnetic Wave Equation]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Hertz 1887: Experimental Confirmation of EM Waves]
    P7 --> P8[Modern Tech: Wireless, MRI, Generators, Transformers]
```

This conceptual map demonstrates how a single experimental observation by Faraday in 1831 evolved through mathematical generalization by Maxwell into the theoretical prediction of electromagnetic waves, confirmed by Hertz, and ultimately into the foundation of modern electrical engineering and communications technology.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction in both integral and differential forms. Briefly explain the significance of the negative sign.*

**Model Answer:**

Faraday's law in **integral form** is:

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \frac{d}{dt} \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

The left side represents the induced electromotive force (EMF) around a closed conducting path $\mathcal{C}$, and the right side is the negative rate of change of magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the surface $\mathcal{S}$ bounded by $\mathcal{C}$.

In **differential (point-wise) form**, applying Stokes' theorem, it becomes:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = - \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

This states that a time-varying magnetic field produces a curling (non-conservative) electric field in space.

The **negative sign** is the mathematical encoding of **Lenz's Law**: the induced current flows in a direction such that its own magnetic field **opposes** the change in flux that produced it. This sign is essential for the conservation of energy — without it, the system would self-amplify and violate thermodynamics. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between motional EMF and transformer EMF with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Motional EMF** | **Transformer EMF** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Conductor **moves** through a static $\vec{B}$ | $\vec{B}$ changes in time around a **stationary** loop |
| Force origin | Magnetic Lorentz force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ | Induced electric field $\vec{E}$ from changing $\vec{B}$ |
| Example | DC generator, homopolar disk | AC power transformer, induction coil |

**Motional EMF** example: A conducting rod of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B$ generates $\mathcal{E} = BLv$.

**Transformer EMF** example: The secondary coil of a transformer experiences an EMF $\mathcal{E}_2 = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ because the primary's alternating current produces a time-varying magnetic flux. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign errors** are the #1 cause of lost marks. Always re-derive the sign using Lenz's law at the end.
> - **Unit omission** leads to a 0.5–1 mark penalty. Every numerical answer must carry units.
> - **Confusing $\Phi$ with $|\Phi|$**: Always write flux as a **signed scalar** (or vector dot product), not just its magnitude, when applying $d\Phi/dt$.
> - **Stokes' theorem misuse**: The differential form is valid only when the loop is stationary in space. For moving conductors, retain the integral form and use the motional EMF derivation.
> - **Confusing $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{H}$**: The Maxwell-Faraday equation uses $\vec{B}$, not $\vec{H}$. This is a frequently tested subtlety.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Derivation + Application

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Starting from the integral form of Faraday's law, derive the differential form (Maxwell-Faraday equation). Clearly state the mathematical theorem used in each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular conducting loop of dimensions $0.20 \ \text{m} \times 0.30 \ \text{m}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B(t) = 0.50 \sin(60\pi t) \ \text{T}$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The loop has 50 turns and a total resistance of $5 \ \Omega$. Compute:
   - (i) The maximum magnetic flux through the loop.
   - (ii) The maximum induced EMF.
   - (iii) The maximum induced current in the loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

We begin with the integral form of Faraday's law:

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \frac{d}{dt} \int_{\mathcal{S}} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1:** Since the contour $\mathcal{C}$ is fixed in space, the time derivative can be moved inside the surface integral (the differential element $d\vec{S}$ has no explicit time dependence):

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = - \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Stokes' Theorem** to convert the line integral of $\vec{E}$ around $\mathcal{C}$ into a surface integral of $\nabla \times \vec{E}$ over $\mathcal{S}$:

$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3:** Equate the right-hand sides of equations (2) and (3):

$$\int_{\mathcal{S}} (\nabla \times \vec{E}) \cdot d\vec{S} = - \int_{\mathcal{S}} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4:** Combine the integrals:

$$\int_{\mathcal{S}} \left[ (\nabla \times \vec{E}) + \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \right] \cdot d\vec{S} = 0 \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 5:** Since this must hold for **any arbitrary** surface $\mathcal{S}$ in space, the integrand itself must vanish point-wise:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = - \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

This is the **Maxwell-Faraday equation**, the local (differential) form of Faraday's law. It is one of the four Maxwell-Heaviside equations. The negative sign encodes Lenz's law and the conservation of energy.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- Loop dimensions: $0.20 \ \text{m} \times 0.30 \ \text{m}$, so area $A = 0.06 \ \text{m}^2$
- Magnetic field: $B(t) = 0.50 \sin(60\pi t) \ \text{T}$
- Number of turns: $N = 50$
- Resistance: $R = 5 \ \Omega$

**(i) Maximum magnetic flux:**

For an $N$-turn coil, the total flux linkage is $\Lambda = N \Phi$, where $\Phi = B A$.

$$\Phi(t) = B(t) \cdot A = 0.50 \sin(60\pi t) \times 0.06 = 0.03 \sin(60\pi t) \ \text{Wb}$$

$$\Phi_{\max} = 0.03 \ \text{Wb} = 30 \ \text{mWb}$$

For an $N$-turn coil, the **flux linkage** is $\Lambda_{\max} = N \Phi_{\max} = 50 \times 0.03 = 1.5 \ \text{Wb}$.

**(ii) Maximum induced EMF:**

$$\mathcal{E}(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N A \frac{dB}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dB}{dt} = 0.50 \times 60\pi \cos(60\pi t) = 30\pi \cos(60\pi t) \ \text{T/s}$$

$$\mathcal{E}(t) = -50 \times 0.06 \times 30\pi \cos(60\pi t) = -90\pi \cos(60\pi t) \ \text{V}$$

$$\vert \mathcal{E} \vert_{\max} = 90\pi \approx 282.74 \ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Maximum induced current:**

$$i_{\max} = \frac{\mathcal{E}_{\max}}{R} = \frac{282.74}{5} \approx 56.55 \ \text{A}$$

The current oscillates sinusoidally with the same frequency as $B(t)$, lagging in phase by $\pi/2$ (since differentiation introduces a $\pi/2$ phase shift).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the physical origin of motional EMF. Derive the expression for the EMF induced in a straight conducting rod of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long solenoid of radius $a = 4 \ \text{cm}$ with $n = 800 \ \text{turns/m}$ carries a current $i(t) = 5.0 \cos(200\pi t) \ \text{A}$. Calculate the induced electric field at a point:
   - (i) $r = 2 \ \text{cm}$ from the axis
   - (ii) $r = 6 \ \text{cm}$ from the axis

   Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Motional EMF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Physical Origin:** When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, the free charge carriers inside experience a magnetic Lorentz force $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$. This force drives the charges toward one end of the conductor, creating a charge separation and hence an EMF. This EMF can drive a current through an external circuit connected to the rod's ends.

**Diagram description:** A straight conducting rod of length $L$ is shown moving with velocity $\vec{v} = v\hat{x}$ through a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B} = B\hat{z}$ (out of the page or upward). The cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = vB\hat{y}$ points along the rod. Positive charges accumulate at the upper end (if oriented along $\hat{y}$), making it the higher potential terminal.

**Derivation:**

Consider a straight rod of length $L$ moving with constant velocity $\vec{v}$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$, with $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$. The motional EMF is the work done per unit charge in moving a charge from one end of the rod to the other against the magnetic force.

For a small element $d\vec{l}$ of the rod, the force per unit charge is $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$. The work done per unit charge is the integral:

$$\mathcal{E} = \int_{\text{end 1}}^{\text{end 2}} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

If the rod is oriented along the direction of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ (i.e., $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$ and $\vec{L} \parallel \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$), then:

$$\mathcal{E} = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{L} = vBL \cos(0) = BvL$$

**Verification using Faraday's law:** Consider a virtual rectangular loop formed by the rod, two fixed rails, and a sliding contact. As the rod moves, the area of the loop changes at rate $dA/dt = Lv$, so:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = B \frac{dA}{dt} = BLv \Rightarrow \mathcal{E} = BLv$$

Both methods yield the same result, confirming the internal consistency of Faraday's law.

**Part (b) — Solenoid Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- Solenoid radius: $a = 0.04 \ \text{m}$
- Turns per unit length: $n = 800 \ \texttext{turns/m}$
- Current: $i(t) = 5.0 \cos(200\pi t) \ \text{A}$
- Permeability: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$

**Step 1 — Magnetic field inside the long solenoid:**

$$B(t) = \mu_0 n i(t) = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(800)(5.0) \cos(200\pi t)$$

$$B(t) = 1.6\pi \times 10^{-3} \cos(200\pi t) \ \text{T}$$

**Step 2 — Time derivative:**

$$\frac{dB}{dt} = -1.6\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 200\pi \sin(200\pi t)$$

$$\frac{dB}{dt} = -320 \pi^2 \times 10^{-3} \sin(200\pi t) \ \text{T/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply symmetry and Faraday's law on a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$:**

The induced electric field is tangential: $\vec{E} = E_\phi(r,t) \hat{\phi}$.

$$E_\phi (2\pi r) = - \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Case (i) — $r = 0.02 \ \text{m} \le a$ (inside the solenoid):**

$$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \pi r^2 \frac{dB}{dt}$$

$$E_\phi = - \frac{r}{2} \frac{dB}{dt} = - \frac{0.02}{2} \times (-320 \pi^2 \times 10^{-3}) \sin(200\pi t)$$

$$E_\phi = 0.01 \times 0.320 \pi^2 \sin(200\pi t) = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \pi^2 \sin(200\pi t)$$

Numerically: $E_\phi \approx 0.0316 \sin(200\pi t) \ \text{V/m}$

Maximum magnitude: $\vert E_\phi \vert_{\max} \approx 31.6 \ \text{mV/m}$ at $r = 2 \ \text{cm}$.

**Case (ii) — $r = 0.06 \ \text{m} \ge a$ (outside the solenoid):**

The flux is the full solenoid flux $B \cdot \pi a^2$, independent of $r$ for $r > a$.

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \pi a^2 \frac{dB}{dt} = \pi (0.04)^2 \frac{dB}{dt} = 1.6\pi \times 10^{-3} \frac{dB}{dt} \cdot 1 = \pi a^2 \frac{dB}{dt}$$

$$E_\phi = - \frac{a^2}{2r} \frac{dB}{dt} = - \frac{(0.04)^2}{2(0.06)} \times (-320\pi^2 \times 10^{-3}) \sin(200\pi t)$$

$$E_\phi = \frac{0.0016}{0.12} \times 0.320 \pi^2 \sin(200\pi t) = 0.01333 \times 0.320 \pi^2 \sin(200\pi t)$$

Numerically: $E_\phi \approx 4.21 \times 10^{-3} \sin(200\pi t) \ \text{V/m}$

Maximum magnitude: $\vert E_\phi \vert_{\max} \approx 4.21 \ \text{mV/m}$ at $r = 6 \ \text{cm}$.

**Physical interpretation:** The induced $E$ field is much weaker outside the solenoid (decreases as $1/r$), which is why the coupling between two coils of a transformer is best when they share a common iron core that confines the flux.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Faraday's Law & Maxwell-Heaviside Equations**

- ✅ **Faraday's Law (Integral):** $\oint_{\mathcal{C}} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ — global statement over a closed loop.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law (Differential):** $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ — local, point-wise statement. This is the **Maxwell-Faraday equation**, one of the four Maxwell-Heaviside equations.
- ✅ **The negative sign is Lenz's Law** — it physically encodes **energy conservation** and is not a mathematical artifact.
- ✅ **Motional EMF** arises from the **Lorentz force** on charges in a moving conductor: $\mathcal{E} = \int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$.
- ✅ **Transformer EMF** arises from a **time-varying magnetic field** through a stationary loop: $\mathcal{E} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
- ✅ **Sliding bar formula:** $\mathcal{E} = BLv$ (when $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B} \perp \vec{L}$ and all three mutually perpendicular).
- ✅ **Induced $E$ field inside a solenoid** ($r \le a$): $E = \frac{r}{2} \left\vert \frac{dB}{dt} \right\vert$ — **linear growth** with $r$.
- ✅ **Induced $E$ field outside a solenoid** ($r \ge a$): $E = \frac{a^{2}}{2r} \left\vert \frac{dB}{dt} \right\vert$ — **$1/r$ decay** with $r$.
- ✅ **Units to memorize:** $\Phi$ in **Wb** (or T·m²), $\mathcal{E}$ in **V**, $B$ in **T**, $E$ in **V/m**.
- ✅ **Limiting cases:** Static $\vec{B}$ reduces Maxwell-Faraday to $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ (electrostatics); static loop reduces to motional EMF.
- ✅ **The Maxwell-Faraday equation is universal** — it is **not modified** by magnetic materials (no $\vec{H}$ appears, unlike Ampere's law).
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Generators, transformers, induction motors, wireless charging, MRI, electromagnetic braking, metal detectors.
- ✅ **Stokes' Theorem** is the bridge from integral to differential form — must be **explicitly mentioned** in derivations.
- ✅ **Sign convention in KTU valuation:** Always verify that the induced current's $\vec{B}$ opposes $\frac{d\vec{B}}{dt}$ at the location of the loop.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Confusing flux $\Phi$ (scalar) with flux linkage $\Lambda = N\Phi$ (for multi-turn coils).
- ✅ **Faraday's discovery (1831) → Maxwell's synthesis (1865) → Hertz's confirmation (1887) → Modern wireless era** — a complete historical arc worth mentioning in long-answer questions for "good impression" marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer EMF and Motional EMF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformer EMF and Motional EMF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer EMF** is the electromotive force induced in a **stationary closed loop** when the magnetic flux linking the loop **changes with time**. Mathematically, it arises from the **time-varying magnetic field** term of Faraday's Law:
> 
> $$\text{EMF}_{\text{transformer}} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$
> 
> It is the EMF observed by an **observer co-moving with the circuit** (no mechanical motion of the loop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motional EMF** is the electromotive force induced when a **conductor (or circuit element) physically moves** through a region of (static or time-varying) magnetic field. It arises from the **Lorentz magnetic force** acting on the free charges in the moving conductor:
> 
> $$\text{EMF}_{\text{motional}} = \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$
> 
> It is the EMF observed by an **observer in the laboratory frame** (where the source magnet/field is stationary).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine two scenarios at a railway platform:**
> 
> **Scenario 1 — Transformer EMF:** You are **standing still** on the platform, and a powerful magnetic wave (like a moving magnet's field) **sweeps past you**. Even though you didn't move, electrons inside your body (or a loop in your hand) get pushed around because the magnetic field *around* you is changing. This is **Transformer EMF** — the *field* changes, the *circuit* is still.
> 
> **Scenario 2 — Motional EMF:** Now, the magnetic field is **stationary** (a permanent magnet sits quietly), but **you run through it** holding a metal rod. As you sweep the rod across the field lines, the electrons inside the rod feel a sideways push (the Lorentz force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$). This is **Motional EMF** — the *circuit* moves, the *field* is still.
> 
> In reality, the **total EMF** is the sum of both effects, captured by the **generalized Faraday's Law**:
> $$\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Faraday's Constant:** Used in electrochemistry, not directly here, but conceptually the charge of one mole of electrons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Transformer EMF — Operational Breakdown

**When does it arise?**
- The loop/circuit is **stationary** ($\vec{v} = 0$).
- The magnetic flux $\Phi = \int_{S} \vec{B}(t) \cdot d\vec{S}$ **changes with time** because $\vec{B}$ itself is time-varying.
- The driving agent is $\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ (time derivative of flux density).

**How does it work (step by step):**
1. A time-varying current in a primary coil produces a time-varying $\vec{B}(t)$.
2. This $\vec{B}(t)$ passes through the area enclosed by a secondary (stationary) loop.
3. The changing flux $\Phi(t)$ induces a non-conservative electric field $\vec{E}_{\text{ind}}$ curling around $\vec{B}$ (by Maxwell–Faraday equation: $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$).
4. This induced $\vec{E}$ field drives charges around the secondary loop, producing the **transformer EMF**.

**Why does it matter?**
- This is the **working principle of every transformer** (power grid, audio, RF, isolation transformers).
- It is the only EMF mechanism in static (non-moving) circuits with time-varying fields.

### 2.2 Motional EMF — Operational Breakdown

**When does it arise?**
- The conductor/loop **physically moves** with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field.
- The field may be static (steady DC machines) or time-varying (general case).
- The driving agent is the **Lorentz magnetic force** per unit charge: $\vec{f}_m = \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.

**How does it work (step by step):**
1. A straight conductor of length $L$ moves with velocity $\vec{v}$ through a uniform $\vec{B}$.
2. Free electrons inside the conductor experience a force $q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.
3. Charges separate — one end of the rod becomes positive, the other negative.
4. The potential difference between the rod's ends equals $\int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$.

**Why does it matter?**
- This is the **working principle of DC generators, alternators, homopolar generators, and linear motors**.
- It is also the basis of **electromagnetic braking** (eddy current brakes) and **railguns**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Concept | Equation | SI Units | Key Condition |
|---|---------|----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Magnetic Flux | $\Phi = \int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Weber (Wb) | Definition |
| 2 | Faraday's Law (Total) | $\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Volt (V) | General |
| 3 | **Transformer EMF** | $\text{EMF}_{\text{tr}} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Volt (V) | $\vec{v} = 0$ |
| 4 | **Motional EMF (loop)** | $\text{EMF}_{\text{mot}} = \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Volt (V) | $\partial \vec{B}/\partial t = 0$ |
| 5 | **Motional EMF (rod)** | $\text{EMF} = \int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l} = B L v \sin\theta$ | Volt (V) | Straight rod, $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$ |
| 6 | Lorentz Force (per unit charge) | $\vec{f} = \vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ | V/m | Point charge |
| 7 | Maxwell–Faraday Equation | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ | V/m² | Differential form |
| 8 | Generalized Faraday's Law | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Volt | Total (transformer + motional) |
| 9 | Flux Linkage (N-turn coil) | $\lambda = N \Phi$ | Wb-turns | Multi-turn |
| 10 | Induced EMF (N-turn) | $\text{EMF} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Volt | Coil with $N$ turns |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Transformers (11 kV / 400 V step-down at KSEB substations):** Pure transformer EMF operation at **50 Hz**.
- **Induction Generators (wind turbines):** Motional EMF between rotor blades and stator field.
- **Maglev Trains:** Motional EMF is the basis of **linear eddy current braking**.
- **MRI Machines:** Time-varying gradients induce transformer EMF in patient tissue (a safety concern).
- **Electromagnetic Flow Meters:** Motional EMF ($\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$) used to measure blood/pipe flow rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Transformer EMF from Faraday's Law

We start with the **integral form of Faraday's Law** for a stationary loop $C$ bounding surface $S$:

$$\text{EMF} = \oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 1:** Since the loop is **stationary**, the surface $S$ is **not changing in time**. Therefore, the time derivative can be moved **inside** the surface integral:

$$\text{EMF} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \vec{B}(t) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2:** Recognizing that $\vec{B}$ is the only time-dependent quantity (the geometry is fixed), we write:

$$\text{EMF} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 3:** This is the **definition of Transformer EMF**. The negative sign is Lenz's law — the induced EMF opposes the change in flux.

> **Conclusion (Transformer EMF):**
> $$\boxed{\text{EMF}_{\text{transformer}} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Motional EMF from Lorentz Force

Consider a charge $q$ inside a conductor moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$. The total electromagnetic force on the charge is:

$$\vec{F} = q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 1:** The motional EMF is defined as the **work done per unit charge** by the magnetic component of the Lorentz force along the conducting path:

$$\text{EMF} = \frac{1}{q}\int_{a}^{b} \vec{F}_m \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{a}^{b} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 2:** For a **closed loop** $C$ moving in a (possibly time-varying) field:

$$\text{EMF}_{\text{motional}} = \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 3:** Special case — a **straight rod of length $L$** moving with constant velocity $\vec{v}$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$:

- $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ has magnitude $vB$ and is directed along the rod.
- Integrating along the rod's length $L$:

$$\text{EMF} = (vB)(L) = BLv$$

> **Conclusion (Motional EMF for straight rod):**
> $$\boxed{\text{EMF} = BLv \quad \text{(when } \vec{v} \perp \vec{B} \text{ and } \vec{B} \perp \vec{L}\text{)}}$$

### 3.3 Generalized Faraday's Law — Combining Both EMFs

The most general expression of Faraday's law in **non-relativistic form** accounts for both transformer and motional contributions:

$$\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Applying the **convective derivative** (Reynolds transport theorem) to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} + \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

Therefore, the total EMF can be split cleanly into two parts:

$$\text{EMF}_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{-\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}}_{\text{Transformer EMF}} \; + \; \underbrace{\oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}}_{\text{Motional EMF}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- The **first term** requires a time-varying field and a **stationary** loop.
- The **second term** requires a moving loop, regardless of whether the field is static.
- The negative sign on transformer EMF arises from the convention that the contour direction and surface normal follow the **right-hand rule**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A circular loop of radius $r = 0.1 \text{ m}$ lies in the $xy$-plane. The magnetic flux density is $\vec{B}(t) = B_0 \sin(\omega t) \hat{a}_z$ where $B_0 = 0.5 \text{ T}$ and $\omega = 100\pi \text{ rad/s}$. Find the induced (transformer) EMF at $t = 0$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Area of circular loop:
$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.01\pi \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the flux:
$$\Phi(t) = \int_{S} \vec{B}(t) \cdot d\vec{S} = B_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot A = 0.5 \sin(100\pi t) \cdot 0.01\pi$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time (this is the transformer EMF):
$$\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -0.5 \cdot 0.01\pi \cdot 100\pi \cos(100\pi t) = -0.5\pi^2 \cos(100\pi t)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $t = 0$:
$$\text{EMF}(0) = -0.5\pi^2 \cos(0) = -0.5\pi^2 \approx -4.93 \text{ V}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\text{EMF}(0) \approx -4.93 \text{ V}$ (the negative sign indicates opposition to the increasing flux — Lenz's law).

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Motional EMF

**Problem:** A copper rod of length $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$ slides along parallel rails with a uniform velocity $v = 10 \text{ m/s}$ in a uniform magnetic field $B = 0.8 \text{ T}$ directed into the page. The rod, rails, and field are mutually perpendicular. Find the motional EMF.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify geometry — all three vectors $\vec{B}$, $\vec{v}$, $\vec{L}$ are mutually perpendicular, so $\sin\theta = 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply the motional EMF formula:
$$\text{EMF} = BLv = (0.8)(0.5)(10) = 4 \text{ V}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\text{EMF} = 4 \text{ V}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transformer EMF Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Source Vp] --> B[Primary Coil Np]
    B --> C[Time-Varying Current ip]
    C --> D[Time-Varying Magnetic Field B]
    D --> E[Flux Phi through Secondary]
    E --> F[dPhi over dt nonzero]
    F --> G[Induced Electric Field Eind]
    G --> H[Transformer EMF in Secondary Coil]
    H --> I[Load Current Is]
    I --> J[Output Voltage Vs]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motional EMF Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Permanent Magnet or DC Field B] --> Q[Static Magnetic Field Region]
    R[Mechanical Force F] --> S[Conductor Moves with Velocity v]
    S --> Q
    Q --> T[Charges in Conductor Experience Lorentz Force q v cross B]
    T --> U[Charge Separation along Rod]
    U --> V[Potential Difference BLv]
    V --> W[Motional EMF Measured at Terminals]
    W --> X[External Load Current Flows]

    style P fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px
```
