## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Transformer EMF. Under what conditions is it the only induced EMF in a circuit?

**Model Answer:**
Transformer EMF is the electromotive force induced in a stationary closed loop due to a time-varying magnetic field. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{EMF}_{\text{transformer}} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Conditions:** The loop must be **stationary** ($\vec{v} = 0$) and the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ must be a **function of time**. Transformer EMF is the *only* induced EMF when the circuit is at rest in a region where $\vec{B}(t)$ varies. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for conditions)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** A straight conductor of length $L = 0.2$ m moves with velocity $v = 5$ m/s perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B = 1.2$ T. Calculate the motional EMF.

**Model Answer:**
For a straight rod moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field:

$$\text{EMF} = BLv = (1.2)(0.2)(5) = 1.2 \text{ V}$$

(3 marks: 1 for identifying formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for correct answer with units)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\text{EMF} = -d\Phi/dt$ costs **1 full mark** (Lenz's law is mandatory).
> - **Confusing transformer EMF (field-driven) with motional EMF (motion-driven)** is a **2-mark deduction** — write clearly which one applies.
> - **Missing units** in final answers (V, Wb, T, m/s) → **0.5 mark** deduction per occurrence.
> - **Using $\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ instead of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$** for motional EMF is a common conceptual error → **2-mark penalty**.
> - Always state assumptions (e.g., uniform field, perpendicular geometry) before plugging into formulas.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the motional EMF induced in a straight conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ through a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. Clearly state the conditions under which the magnitude of the EMF is $BLv$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lorentz Force on a Charge:** A free charge $q$ inside the moving conductor experiences a magnetic force:

$$\vec{F}_m = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 2 — Force per Unit Charge:** Dividing by $q$ gives the force per unit charge, which has the dimensions of an electric field (V/m):

$$\vec{f}_m = \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of EMF:** EMF is the line integral of the force per unit charge along the conducting path:

$$\text{EMF} = \int_{a}^{b} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 4 — Special Geometry:** If $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$ and the rod of length $L$ is perpendicular to both, then $\vert \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \vert = vB$ and the integrand is constant along the rod:

$$\text{EMF} = (vB)(L) = BLv$$

**Step 5 — Conditions for $BLv$:**
- $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$
- $\vec{L}$ perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ (mutual perpendicularity)
- $\vec{B}$ is uniform over the rod's length
- $\vec{v}$ is constant (no acceleration)

(7 marks distributed: 2 for Lorentz force, 2 for integration, 2 for final $BLv$, 1 for conditions)

---

**(b)** A rectangular loop of dimensions $0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.3 \text{ m}$ is placed in a uniform time-varying magnetic field $B(t) = 0.6 \sin(50\pi t)$ T, with the field normal to the plane of the loop. Determine: (i) the magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$, (ii) the induced (transformer) EMF, and (iii) the magnitude of EMF at $t = 0.01$ s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Area of Loop:**
$$A = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic Flux:**
$$\Phi(t) = \vec{B}(t) \cdot \vec{A} = B(t) \cdot A = 0.12 \cdot 0.6 \sin(50\pi t) = 0.072 \sin(50\pi t) \text{ Wb}$$

**Step 3 — Transformer EMF** (differentiating flux):
$$\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -0.072 \cdot 50\pi \cos(50\pi t) = -3.6\pi \cos(50\pi t) \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluation at $t = 0.01$ s:**
$$50\pi \cdot 0.01 = 0.5\pi \text{ rad} = 90°$$

$$\cos(90°) = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\text{EMF}(0.01) = -3.6\pi \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ V}$$

(7 marks: 1 for area, 2 for flux, 2 for EMF expression, 2 for evaluation at given time)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Starting from Faraday's Law in integral form, derive the expression for transformer EMF and clearly distinguish it from motional EMF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law in Integral Form:**
$$\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2 — Stationary Loop:** If the loop $C$ is at rest, the surface $S$ is **time-invariant**, so the time derivative passes inside:

$$\text{EMF} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{...(Transformer EMF)}$$

**Step 3 — Moving Loop:** If the loop moves with velocity $\vec{v}$ through a **time-varying** field, the flux rate-of-change must include both the explicit time variation *and* the change due to motion:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{S} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} + \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 4 — Generalized Law:** Substituting back:

$$\oint_{C} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} - \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

The first right-hand term is the **transformer EMF** (field-driven, requires $\partial \vec{B}/\partial t \neq 0$). The second is the **motional EMF** (motion-driven, requires $\vec{v} \neq 0$).

(7 marks: 1 for stating Faraday's law, 2 for transformer EMF derivation, 2 for motional EMF identification, 2 for distinction)

---

**(b)** A conducting rod of length $L = 1$ m rotates about one of its ends with constant angular velocity $\omega = 20$ rad/s in a uniform magnetic field $B = 0.5$ T perpendicular to the plane of rotation. Calculate the motional EMF induced between the two ends of the rod. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity at Distance $r$:** Each point on the rod at distance $r$ from the pivot has a linear velocity:

$$v(r) = \omega r$$

This velocity is tangential and perpendicular to $\vec{B}$.

**Step 2 — Differential EMF Contribution:** A small element $dr$ of the rod contributes a differential EMF:

$$d(\text{EMF}) = B \cdot v(r) \cdot dr = B \omega r \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Along the Rod:**
$$\text{EMF} = \int_{0}^{L} B \omega r \, dr = B \omega \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{L} = \frac{1}{2} B \omega L^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values:**
$$\text{EMF} = \frac{1}{2} (0.5)(20)(1)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10 = 5 \text{ V}$$

(7 marks: 2 for velocity expression, 2 for differential element, 2 for integration, 1 for final value)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Module 3**
> 
> - **Transformer EMF** = induced EMF in a **stationary** loop due to a **time-varying** magnetic field.
> - **Motional EMF** = induced EMF due to a conductor **moving** through a magnetic field (Lorentz force on charges).
> - Faraday's Law in integral form: $\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ is the **unified** statement.
> - **Transformer EMF equation:** $\text{EMF}_{\text{tr}} = -\int_{S} \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$ — flux changes because $\vec{B}$ changes.
> - **Motional EMF equation:** $\text{EMF}_{\text{mot}} = \oint_{C} (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$ — flux changes because the loop moves.
> - **Straight rod formula:** $\text{EMF} = BLv\sin\theta$ — remember the angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.
> - **Rotating rod formula:** $\text{EMF} = \frac{1}{2}B\omega L^2$ — derived by integrating $B\omega r \, dr$ from $0$ to $L$.
> - **Lenz's Law:** Always include the **negative sign** — induced EMF opposes the change in flux.
> - **Lenz's law is the reason for the negative sign** in Faraday's equation; examiners will deduct marks if omitted.
> - **Physical constants to remember:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
> - **N-turn coil:** $\text{EMF} = -N\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$ (each turn contributes the same flux).
> - **Units:** $\Phi$ in Wb, $B$ in T, $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s, EMF in V.
> - **Direction of induced current:** Use **right-hand rule** for $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$, then **Lenz's law** to confirm.
> - **Applications to remember:** Transformer (transformer EMF), DC generator (motional EMF), AC generator (motional EMF with rotating coil), induction motor (combined), eddy current brake (motional), induction cooktop (transformer).
> - **When in doubt, ask:** "Is the circuit moving?" If yes → motional component exists. "Is the field varying?" If yes → transformer component exists.
> - **Generalized Faraday's law** combines both: $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\int \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} - \oint (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$.
> - **Maxwell–Faraday differential form:** $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ — note the absence of $\vec{v}$ (this is the stationary-frame form, valid always).
> - **Common KTU board trap:** If the rod moves *parallel* to its length, the motional EMF is **zero** (since $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ would be perpendicular to $d\vec{l}$).
> - **Common KTU board trap 2:** If $\vec{B}$ is parallel to $\vec{v}$, motional EMF is **zero** (since $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = 0$).
> - **Distinction in one line:** Transformer EMF = $\partial \vec{B}/\partial t$ effect; Motional EMF = $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ effect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Displacement Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Displacement Current** is the rate of change of electric flux density (**D**) with respect to time. It is a quantity introduced by **James Clerk Maxwell** to extend **Ampere's Circuital Law** so that it remains valid for **time-varying fields**, particularly in regions where no actual conduction current flows (e.g., the dielectric region between the plates of a charging capacitor).

The displacement current density is mathematically defined as:

$$\vec{J_d} = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

The corresponding total displacement current crossing a surface **S** is:

$$I_d = \int_S \vec{J_d} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_S \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

where:
- $\vec{J_d}$ → Displacement current density (A/m²)
- $\vec{D}$ → Electric flux density (C/m²)
- $I_d$ → Total displacement current (A)
- $t$ → Time (s)
- $\varepsilon_0$ → Permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Although called a "current," displacement current is **not** a flow of real charges. It is a **time-varying electric field effect** that mathematically completes the continuity equation in Maxwell's framework.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water pipe** that has a small **rubber diaphragm** (the capacitor's dielectric) blocking the middle of the pipe. When you push water in one side, even though no actual water molecules pass through the diaphragm, the **diaphragm stretches** and pushes water out the other side. The *flow is continuous*, even though the middle section carries no real water.

| Real Water Pipe Analogy | Electromagnetic Equivalent |
|:---:|:---:|
| Pumping water (continuous flow) | Conduction current $I_c$ in the wire |
| Stretched rubber diaphragm | Time-varying electric field $E(t)$ between plates |
| Push through stretched membrane | Displacement current $I_d$ through dielectric |
| Continuous water flow | Total current = $I_c + I_d$ is **continuous** |

The **"stretching of the diaphragm"** in electromagnetism is the **time-varying electric displacement field** $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$. Without it, the current would appear to "stop" between the capacitor plates — violating charge conservation.

---

## 1.3 The Problem That Demanded Displacement Current

Consider a **parallel plate capacitor** being charged by a time-varying current $I_c$.

| Region | Conduction Current $I_c$ | Electric Field $\vec{E}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Connecting wire | **Present** (charges flow) | Negligible |
| Between plates (dielectric) | **Zero** (no charges cross) | Strong, time-varying |

Apply Ampere's original law to a surface $S_1$ (passing *through* the wire):

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{S_1} \vec{J_c} \cdot d\vec{S} = I_c$$

Apply the same law to a different surface $S_2$ (bulging *between* the plates, not crossing the wire):

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{S_2} \vec{J_c} \cdot d\vec{S} = 0$$

**Contradiction!** Same line integral, two different answers. Ampere's law fails for **non-steady currents**. Maxwell resolved this by adding the **displacement current term**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Displacement current is essential because it guarantees **current continuity** across any surface bounded by the same closed path, preserving the **conservation of charge** (the continuity equation).

---

## 1.4 The Permittivity of Free Space — The Universal Constant

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $\varepsilon_0$ appears everywhere in displacement current calculations. Its numerical value is **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** and it links $\vec{D}$ to $\vec{E}$ in free space via $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$. The displacement current density in free space is therefore:
>
> $$\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

### Step 1 — The Limitation of Ampere's Original Law

The original Ampere's Circuital Law (steady-state form):

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc} = \int_S \vec{J_c} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This requires $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$, which implies $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} = 0$ — only true for **steady currents** ($\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$).

### Step 2 — The Continuity Equation (Conservation of Charge)

The most fundamental equation governing charge conservation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

This is always true, regardless of whether currents are steady or time-varying. Ampere's original law **violates** this in non-steady cases.

### Step 3 — Maxwell's Genius: Add the Displacement Term

Maxwell realized that to satisfy the continuity equation for time-varying fields, Ampere's law must be augmented. The **complete (modified) Ampere-Maxwell Law** is:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

Or in point (differential) form:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

### Step 4 — Verification of Charge Conservation

Taking divergence of both sides:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

Since $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$ (identity):

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

Using Gauss's Law $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The continuity equation is **preserved exactly**. This is the profound justification for Maxwell's correction.

### Step 5 — Physical Meaning of Each Term

- $\vec{J_c}$ → **Conduction current density** (actual flow of free charges, e.g., electrons in a wire)
- $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ → **Displacement current density** (time-varying electric field in dielectric/vacuum)
- Their **sum** is divergence-free, ensuring **closed current loops** even through capacitors

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Equation | Name | When Used |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\vec{J_d} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ | Displacement current density | General definition |
| 2 | $I_d = \dfrac{d}{dt}\displaystyle\int_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Total displacement current | Through any surface S |
| 3 | $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ | In free space/vacuum | When medium is air/vacuum |
| 4 | $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ | In linear dielectric | When medium has $\varepsilon_r$ |
| 5 | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ | Ampere-Maxwell Law (point form) | Maxwell's equation #4 |
| 6 | $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_c + I_d$ | Ampere-Maxwell Law (integral form) | Closed loop with current |
| 7 | $I_d = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt} = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ | Displacement current in a capacitor | Charging capacitor problems |
| 8 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | Continuity equation | Conservation of charge |
| 9 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$ | Gauss's Law (electric) | Relates D to charge density |
| 10 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ | Gauss's Law (magnetic) | No magnetic monopoles |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Do **NOT** confuse $\Phi_E$ (electric flux) with $\Phi_B$ (magnetic flux). For displacement current, you need the **electric flux**, $\Phi_E = \int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Displacement Current

The displacement current concept is the cornerstone of modern electromagnetic engineering:

| Engineering Field | Application of Displacement Current |
|:---|:---|
| **Antenna Design** | Radiation of EM waves from accelerating charges (Hertzian dipole) |
| **Microwave Engineering** | Waveguide analysis, cavity resonators, microstrip lines |
| **Optical Fibers** | High-frequency EM wave propagation through dielectric cores |
| **Wireless Power Transfer** | Resonant inductive coupling uses displacement effects |
| **Capacitor Modeling** | High-frequency behavior where displacement current dominates |
| **Plasma Physics** | Time-varying E-fields in ionized media |
| **Telecommunications** | 5G/6G mmWave propagation through dielectrics |
| **MRI Machines** | Time-varying RF fields in human tissue (displacement currents) |

Without Maxwell's displacement current, **electromagnetic waves would not exist** as a theoretical prediction — and the entire wireless world (radio, TV, Wi-Fi, mobile phones, satellites) would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Displacement Current from the Continuity Equation

We begin with the **continuity equation** for charge conservation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\rho$ using **Gauss's Law for Electricity**.

From Gauss's law in differential form:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left( \nabla \cdot \vec{D} \right) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

Since $\nabla$ and $\partial/\partial t$ commute (for well-behaved fields):

$$\nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this result back into the continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} = -\nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Move all terms to one side:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) = 0$$

$$\nabla \cdot \left( \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the divergence of a curl is always zero.

For any well-behaved vector field $\vec{H}$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0 \quad \text{(vector identity)}$$

**Step 5:** Equate the divergence-free quantity to a curl.

If a vector quantity has zero divergence, it can be expressed as the curl of some vector field:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6:** Define the new term — **Displacement Current Density**:

$$\boxed{\vec{J_d} = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}}$$

This gives us the complete modified Ampere's law:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \vec{J_d} = \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

---

## 3.2 Displacement Current Inside a Parallel Plate Capacitor

Consider a parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$, separation $d$, filled with a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1:** Write the electric field between the plates when charge $Q$ is on the plates:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{A\varepsilon}$$

where $\sigma = Q/A$ is the surface charge density.

**Step 2:** Compute the electric flux density:

$$D = \varepsilon E = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the displacement current density:

$$J_d = \frac{\partial D}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{I_c}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by plate area $A$ to get total displacement current:

$$\boxed{I_d = J_d \cdot A = \frac{dQ}{dt} = I_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The displacement current $I_d$ flowing **between the plates** exactly equals the conduction current $I_c$ flowing **in the wire**. The current loop is **complete and continuous**, satisfying charge conservation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Pattern — Numerical Practice)

**Problem:** A parallel plate capacitor has circular plates of radius **R = 5 cm**, separated by **d = 2 mm** of air. The voltage across it varies as $V(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ where $V_0 = 100$ V and $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s. Find the displacement current between the plates.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Electric field between plates:

$$E(t) = \frac{V(t)}{d} = \frac{V_0 \sin(\omega t)}{d}$$

**Step 2:** Displacement current density in air ($\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$):

$$J_d(t) = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial E}{\partial t} = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{V_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)}{d}$$

**Step 3:** Total displacement current (multiply by plate area $A = \pi R^2$):

$$I_d(t) = J_d(t) \cdot A = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 V_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)}{d}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.

Given:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $R = 0.05$ m
- $V_0 = 100$ V
- $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s
- $d = 0.002$ m

$$A = \pi (0.05)^2 = \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$I_d(t) = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(100)(10^4)}{0.002} \cos(10^4 t)$$

$$I_d(t) = \frac{6.954 \times 10^{-8}}{0.002} \cos(10^4 t)$$

$$I_d(t) = 3.477 \times 10^{-5} \cos(10^4 t) \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_d(t) = 34.77 \cos(10^4 t) \ \mu\text{A}}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum displacement current amplitude:

$$I_{d,\max} = 34.77 \ \mu\text{A at } t = 0$$

---

## 3.4 Maxwell's Equations — Complete Set (For Context)

Displacement current completes the famous **Maxwell's Equations**:

| Equation (Differential Form) | Name | Physical Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$ | Gauss's Law (Electric) | Electric charges create D-field |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ | Gauss's Law (Magnetic) | No magnetic monopoles |
| $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ | Faraday's Law | Time-varying B creates E |
| $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ | Ampere-Maxwell Law | **Displacement current appears here** |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | Continuity Equation | Conservation of charge |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Ampere's Limitation to Maxwell's Correction

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Displacement Current `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Displacement current is the time-rate of change of electric flux density $\vec{D}$, defined as:

$$\vec{J_d} = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

The total displacement current through a surface S is:

$$I_d = \int_S \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

It was introduced by Maxwell to modify Ampere's law for time-varying fields. In free space:

$$\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

In a parallel plate capacitor, $I_d$ equals the conduction current $I_c$ in the external circuit, ensuring current continuity. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Why was Maxwell's Modification to Ampere's Law Necessary? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Ampere's original law, $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \vec{J_c} \cdot d\vec{S}$, is inconsistent with the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \partial\rho/\partial t = 0$ for **time-varying** currents. For a charging capacitor, two different surfaces bounded by the same loop give different answers ($I_c$ vs. $0$).

Maxwell added the displacement current term $\partial \vec{D}/\partial t$ to make Ampere's law universally valid:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This modification satisfies charge conservation and predicts electromagnetic wave propagation. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the continuity equation, derive the expression for displacement current density. Show that Maxwell's modification of Ampere's law satisfies the conservation of charge. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** State the continuity equation (charge conservation):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gauss's Law in differential form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$

**Step 3:** Differentiate Gauss's law with respect to time:

$$\nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} = -\nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$\nabla \cdot \left( \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) = 0$$

**Step 6:** Using the identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$:

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 7:** Define displacement current density:

$$\vec{J_d} = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 8:** Verification: Taking divergence of the modified Ampere's law:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0 \checkmark$$

Hence charge conservation is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A parallel plate capacitor with circular plates of radius **R = 10 cm** and plate separation **d = 5 mm** is connected to an AC source $V(t) = 200\sin(1000t)$ V. Calculate the displacement current between the plates. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Electric field between the plates:

$$E(t) = \frac{V(t)}{d} = \frac{200\sin(1000t)}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^4 \sin(1000t) \text{ V/m}$$

**Step 2:** Displacement current density (air medium, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$):

$$J_d(t) = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial E}{\partial t} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (4 \times 10^4)(1000)\cos(1000t)$$

$$J_d(t) = 3.5416 \times 10^{-4} \cos(1000t) \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Plate area:

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Total displacement current:

$$I_d(t) = J_d(t) \cdot A = 3.5416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0314 \times \cos(1000t)$$

$$I_d(t) = 1.113 \times 10^{-5} \cos(1000t) \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_d(t) = 11.13 \cos(1000t) \ \mu\text{A}}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State Maxwell's equations in differential form. Explain the physical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| # | Equation | Physical Significance |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho$ | Gauss's law: Electric charges are sources of $\vec{D}$. Net flux out of a closed surface = enclosed charge. |
| 2 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ | Gauss's law for magnetism: Magnetic field lines are continuous (no monopoles). |
| 3 | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ | Faraday's law: Time-varying $\vec{B}$ induces a circulating $\vec{E}$ (electromagnetic induction). |
| 4 | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ | Ampere-Maxwell law: Conduction + displacement current produces circulating $\vec{H}$. |
| 5 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ | Continuity: Charge is conserved. |

**Significance of Displacement Current Term in Eq. (4):**
- Makes the equation consistent with charge conservation.
- Predicts that time-varying electric fields generate magnetic fields.
- This is the **mathematical foundation of electromagnetic wave propagation**.
- Without it, electromagnetic waves (light, radio, X-rays) would not exist. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** In a dielectric medium with $\varepsilon_r = 4$, the electric field is $\vec{E} = E_0 \sin(\omega t) \hat{x}$, where $E_0 = 10^5$ V/m and $\omega = 10^6$ rad/s. Find the displacement current density. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Permittivity of the medium:

$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 = 3.5416 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$$

**Step 2:** Displacement current density formula:

$$\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the time derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} = E_0 \omega \cos(\omega t) \hat{x} = (10^5)(10^6)\cos(10^6 t) \hat{x}$$

$$\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} = 10^{11} \cos(10^6 t) \hat{x} \text{ V/(m·s)}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by permittivity:

$$\vec{J_d} = (3.5416 \times 10^{-11})(10^{11})\cos(10^6 t) \hat{x}$$

$$\boxed{\vec{J_d} = 3.54 \cos(10^6 t) \ \hat{x} \ \text{A/m}^2}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_r$**: When the problem states a dielectric medium with $\varepsilon_r > 1$, students often use $\varepsilon_0$ instead of $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$. This loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in cos vs sin**: Differentiating $\sin(\omega t)$ gives $\omega \cos(\omega t)$, not $-\omega \cos(\omega t)$. Watch the sign.
> 3. **Confusing units**: $J_d$ is in A/m², while $I_d$ is in A. Always multiply by surface area.
> 4. **Missing area calculation**: For circular plates, $A = \pi R^2$. Forgetting $\pi$ loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the continuity verification**: KTU loves asking *"show that charge conservation is satisfied"* — you must take the divergence and explicitly write $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$.
> 6. **Confusing $E$ with $D$**: $D = \varepsilon E$. In displacement current formulas, both forms are correct, but consistency matters.
> 7. **No units in final answer**: Always write units explicitly. Missing units can lose up to **0.5 mark** in numerical problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Displacement Current**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\vec{J_d} = \partial \vec{D}/\partial t$ — rate of change of electric flux density
- ✅ **In vacuum:** $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \partial \vec{E}/\partial t$
- ✅ **In medium:** $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \partial \vec{E}/\partial t$
- ✅ **Total current density:** $\vec{J}_{total} = \vec{J_c} + \vec{J_d}$ — always divergence-free
- ✅ **Ampere-Maxwell Law (point form):** $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J_c} + \partial \vec{D}/\partial t$
- ✅ **Ampere-Maxwell Law (integral form):** $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_c + I_d$
- ✅ **In a capacitor:** $I_d = I_c = dQ/dt$ — current continuity maintained
- ✅ **Origin:** Required to satisfy the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{J_c} + \partial \rho/\partial t = 0$
- ✅ **Conducted by:** Time-varying electric field (NOT by moving charges)
- ✅ **Predicts:** Existence of electromagnetic waves (Maxwell's 1865 prediction, Hertz confirmed 1887)
- ✅ **Constant to remember:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- ✅ **Applications:** Antenna design, waveguides, optical fibers, microwave engineering, wireless comms
- ✅ **Displacement current ≠ real current**: It is a *mathematical* current that completes the electromagnetic circuit
- ✅ **KTU Hot Topics**: Derivation from continuity equation, capacitor charging problems, modified Ampere's law, comparison with conduction current

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maxwell-Heaviside Equations** (also known as the Hertz-Heaviside or modern vector form of Maxwell's equations) are a set of four compact vector differential equations that completely describe the classical behavior of electric and magnetic fields. They were reformulated by **Oliver Heaviside** (and independently by **Heinrich Hertz**) in the 1880s-1890s from James Clerk Maxwell's original 20 equations in 20 unknowns (published in *A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism*, 1873) into the elegant four-equation form universally used today.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Module 3 requires students to derive, interpret, and apply these four equations in both **differential (point) form** and **integral form**, along with the associated **constitutive relations** and **continuity equation**.

The four Maxwell-Heaviside equations are:

| # | Name | Differential Form | Integral Form |
|---|------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Gauss's Law (Electric) | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v$ | $\oint_S \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = Q_{enc}$ |
| 2 | Gauss's Law (Magnetic) | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$ | $\oint_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = 0$ |
| 3 | Faraday's Law | $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$ | $\oint_C \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$ |
| 4 | Ampère-Maxwell Law | $\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}$ | $\oint_C \mathbf{H} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = \int_S \left(\mathbf{J} + \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}\right) \cdot d\mathbf{S}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
>
> Imagine the universe as a vast **invisible fabric** woven from two interacting fields — the **electric field (E)** and the **magnetic field (B)**. The Maxwell-Heaviside equations are the **four "rules of the game"** that dictate how this fabric stretches, swirls, and ripples.
>
> 1. **Rule 1 (Gauss-Electric):** Electric field lines *begin* on positive charges and *end* on negative charges — like rivers flowing from mountains to oceans.
> 2. **Rule 2 (Gauss-Magnetic):** Magnetic field lines are *endless loops* — they never start or stop (no magnetic monopoles exist).
> 3. **Rule 3 (Faraday):** A *changing* magnetic field *whirls* an electric field into existence (the principle behind every electric generator and dynamo).
> 4. **Rule 4 (Ampère-Maxwell):** Both electric currents *and* a *changing* electric field *whirl* a magnetic field into existence (the principle behind every antenna, transformer, and EM wave).

The genius of Heaviside was to condense Maxwell's sprawling 20 equations into these four beautifully symmetric statements using **vector calculus notation** — a framework largely invented by Heaviside himself (alongside Gibbs).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Field Vectors and Their Units (SI):**
> - $\mathbf{E}$ — Electric Field Intensity, measured in **Volts per meter (V/m)**
> - $\mathbf{D}$ — Electric Flux Density, measured in **Coulombs per square meter (C/m²)**
> - $\mathbf{H}$ — Magnetic Field Intensity, measured in **Amperes per meter (A/m)**
> - $\mathbf{B}$ — Magnetic Flux Density, measured in **Tesla (T)** or **Weber per square meter (Wb/m²)**
> - $\mathbf{J}$ — Current Density, measured in **Amperes per square meter (A/m²)**
> - $\rho_v$ — Volume Charge Density, measured in **Coulombs per cubic meter (C/m³)**
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** (permittivity of free space)
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (permeability of free space)
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (speed of light in vacuum)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Equation

### Equation 1: Gauss's Law for Electric Field ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v$)

- **Why it exists:** Electric charges are sources/sinks of electric flux. The net electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge.
- **How it works:** The **divergence** of $\mathbf{D}$ at a point equals the local charge density. In regions free of charge ($\rho_v = 0$), the field is **solenoidal** (no net source/sink).
- **KTU interpretation:** It expresses conservation of electric charge at the field level.

### Equation 2: Gauss's Law for Magnetic Field ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$)

- **Why it exists:** No magnetic monopoles have ever been observed. Magnetic field lines always form closed loops.
- **How it works:** The divergence of $\mathbf{B}$ is *identically zero* everywhere — every field line entering a closed surface must exit it.
- **KTU interpretation:** This is a statement of **non-existence of magnetic charges**.

### Equation 3: Faraday's Law of Induction ($\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$)

- **Why it exists:** A time-varying magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF (electromotive force) in that loop.
- **How it works:** The **curl** of $\mathbf{E}$ is proportional to the *negative* rate of change of $\mathbf{B}$. The negative sign (Lenz's Law) ensures opposition to the change.
- **Engineering application:** Generators, transformers, induction motors, wireless charging.

### Equation 4: Ampère-Maxwell Law ($\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}$)

- **Why it exists:** Maxwell's *correction* to Ampère's original law. A magnetic field is produced both by conduction currents ($\mathbf{J}$) AND by a time-varying electric field ($\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}$, called **displacement current**).
- **How it works:** The curl of $\mathbf{H}$ equals total current density. The displacement current term is what *predicts* the existence of **electromagnetic waves** in free space.
- **Engineering application:** Antennas, waveguides, RF circuits, optical fibers.

## 2.2 Constitutive Relations (Medium-Dependent Equations)

These link the four fundamental field vectors ($\mathbf{E}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{B}$) and **J** to the properties of the material medium:

$$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E}$$

$$\mathbf{B} = \mu \mathbf{H} = \mu_0 \mu_r \mathbf{H}$$

$$\mathbf{J} = \sigma \mathbf{E} \quad \text{(Ohm's Law in point form)}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = permittivity of medium (F/m)
- $\mu$ = permeability of medium (H/m)
- $\sigma$ = conductivity of medium (S/m)
- $\varepsilon_r, \mu_r$ = relative permittivity and permeability (dimensionless)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every entry is fair game for KTU 2024 ESE questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | SI Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Gauss's Law (Diff.) | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v$ | C/m³ | Charge creates E-field divergence |
| 2 | Gauss's Law (Magnetic) | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$ | T | No magnetic monopoles |
| 3 | Faraday's Law (Diff.) | $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\partial \mathbf{B}/\partial t$ | V/m² | Changing B induces E |
| 4 | Ampère-Maxwell (Diff.) | $\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \partial \mathbf{D}/\partial t$ | A/m² | Currents + changing E induce H |
| 5 | Gauss's Law (Integral) | $\oint_S \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = Q_{enc}$ | C | Total flux = enclosed charge |
| 6 | Faraday's Law (Integral) | $\oint_C \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = -d\Phi_B/dt$ | V | EMF = negative rate of flux change |
| 7 | Ampère-Maxwell (Integral) | $\oint_C \mathbf{H} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = I_{enc} + d\Psi_D/dt$ | A | Circuital H = conduction + displacement |
| 8 | Continuity Equation | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \partial \rho_v/\partial t = 0$ | A/m³ | Conservation of charge |
| 9 | Wave Equation (E) | $\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \mu \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{E}}{\partial t^2}$ | V/m | EM wave propagation |
| 10 | Wave Equation (H) | $\nabla^2 \mathbf{H} = \mu \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{H}}{\partial t^2}$ | A/m | EM wave propagation |
| 11 | Displacement Current | $I_d = \varepsilon \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$ | A | "Current through capacitor" |
| 12 | Speed of Light | $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ | m/s | EM waves in vacuum |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Love These Equations:**
> 1. **Antenna Design** — Equation 4 (with displacement current) predicts that accelerating charges radiate EM waves.
> 2. **PCB Design** — Equation 3 governs **crosstalk** between adjacent traces.
> 3. **Microwave Engineering** — All four equations (in phasor form) form the basis of **Maxwell's equations in frequency domain**.
> 4. **Optical Fiber Communication** — Boundary conditions derived from these equations dictate total internal reflection.
> 5. **MRI Machines** — Faraday's law governs the gradient coils that create spatial encoding.
> 6. **Transformer Design** — Equation 3 forms the foundation of mutual inductance calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Continuity Equation from Maxwell-Heaviside Equations

**Goal:** Derive the **equation of continuity** ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \dfrac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$) — the local statement of charge conservation — purely from the four Maxwell-Heaviside equations.

**Step 1:** Take the divergence of both sides of **Ampère-Maxwell Law** (Equation 4).

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{H}) = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \nabla \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{F}) = 0$ for any vector field $\mathbf{F}$. The left-hand side vanishes identically.

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \nabla \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Swap the spatial divergence and time derivative (they are independent operations, so the order can be exchanged — both are linear operators).

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute **Gauss's Law** ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_v$) from Equation 1.

$$0 = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into the standard form of the **continuity equation**.

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** This derivation proves that the four Maxwell-Heaviside equations are *internally consistent* with the conservation of electric charge. If any of the four equations were missing or wrong, charge would not be conserved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electromagnetic Wave Equation in Free Space

**Goal:** Derive the **wave equation for the electric field** $\mathbf{E}$ in a source-free, lossless medium ($\rho_v = 0$, $\mathbf{J} = 0$).

**Step 1:** Start with Faraday's Law (Equation 3). Take the curl of both sides.

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \mathbf{B})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E}$.

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \mathbf{B})$$

**Step 3:** In a source-free region, **Gauss's Law** gives $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = 0 \Rightarrow \nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$ (assuming homogeneous medium). So the first term vanishes.

$$-\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \mathbf{B})$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the sign.

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \mathbf{B})$$

**Step 5:** Replace $\mathbf{B} = \mu \mathbf{H}$ and use the source-free **Ampère-Maxwell Law** ($\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \partial \mathbf{D}/\partial t$).

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu(\nabla \times \mathbf{H}) = \mu \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute this back.

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}\right) = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 7:** Final result — the **vector Helmholtz wave equation** for $\mathbf{E}$.

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{E}}{\partial t^2}}$$

**Step 8:** Compare with the standard 1D wave equation $\partial^2 y/\partial x^2 = (1/v^2)\partial^2 y/\partial t^2$ to identify the wave speed.

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$$

In vacuum: $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ — the **speed of light**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Result:** This derivation proves that light *is* an electromagnetic wave — the greatest unification in classical physics.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Displacement Current Density

**Goal:** Show how Maxwell introduced the **displacement current term** to fix a contradiction in the original Ampère's Law.

**Step 1:** Take the divergence of the original (pre-Maxwell) Ampère's Law: $\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J}$.

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf{H}) = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} \Rightarrow 0 = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}$$

**Step 2:** This implies $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} = 0$ always, which **contradicts** the continuity equation when charge is accumulating (e.g., between the plates of a charging capacitor, where $\partial \rho_v/\partial t \neq 0$).

**Step 3:** Maxwell's resolution: Add a correction term $\mathbf{J}_d$ such that $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_d = 0$.

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_d = -\frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D}) = -\nabla \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Identify the displacement current density.

$$\mathbf{J}_d = \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5:** The corrected Ampère's Law (Ampère-Maxwell Law) becomes:

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Maxwell called this term the "displacement current" because $\mathbf{D}$ arises from the displacement of bound charges in a dielectric. The term $\partial \mathbf{D}/\partial t$ has units of **A/m²** (current density), despite not being a true current of moving charges.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Equation Forms

| Property | Differential (Point) Form | Integral (Global) Form | When to Use |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| Domain | Holds at every *point* in space | Holds for any *arbitrary* surface/loop | Differential → field analysis; Integral → symmetry problems |
| Math Tools | Vector calculus: $\nabla \cdot, \nabla \times$ | Surface & line integrals | Differential → derive wave equation; Integral → Gauss's law symmetry |
| Physical Meaning | Local source/sink of field | Net flux/circuital value | Differential → point-by-point; Integral → total enclosed |
| KTU Exam Weight | High (derivations) | High (numerical problems) | Both equally important |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
