Thus: $$\nabla(\nabla\cdot\vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})$$ $$0 - \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})$$ $$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H}) \quad (3)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Ampère–Maxwell Law (2)** into the RHS of (3): $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$ $$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\varepsilon\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Since $\mu$ and $\varepsilon$ are constants** (linear, homogeneous medium), they come out of the derivative: $$\boxed{\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}}$$ This is the **3D vector wave equation** for the E-field. A fully symmetric derivation starting with $\nabla \times (2)$ yields the same equation for $\vec{H}$. --- ### Derivation 2: 1D Wave Solution — Helmholtz Equation and the Phasor Form **Step 1 — Assume time-harmonic (sinusoidal steady-state) fields:** $$\vec{E}(z,t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{E}}(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the wave equation.** Note that $\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2} \to (j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$ in the phasor domain. $$\nabla^2 \tilde{\vec{E}} = -\mu\varepsilon\omega^2 \tilde{\vec{E}}$$ $$\nabla^2 \tilde{\vec{E}} + \omega^2\mu\varepsilon \tilde{\vec{E}} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Define the wavenumber** $k^2 = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon$: $$\boxed{\nabla^2 \tilde{\vec{E}} + k^2 \tilde{\vec{E}} = 0 \quad \text{(Helmholtz Equation)}}$$ **Step 4 — 1D Reduction** (assume uniform plane wave traveling in +z, E polarized in x): $$\frac{d^2 E_x}{dz^2} + k^2 E_x = 0$$ **Step 5 — General solution** (second-order ODE with characteristic roots $\pm jk$): $$E_x(z) = E_0^+ e^{-jkz} + E_0^- e^{+jkz}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to time-domain** for the forward wave: $$E_x(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz) \quad \text{(uniform plane wave in +z)}$$ **Step 7 — Recover H-field** using $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu \vec{H}$: $$\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z} \hat{a}_y = -j\omega\mu H_y \hat{a}_y$$ $$H_y = \frac{k}{\omega\mu} E_x = \frac{1}{\eta} E_x$$ where $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ for the lossless case. --- ### Derivation 3: Skin Depth in a Good Conductor **Step 1 — Use the general propagation constant** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ in a lossy medium: $$\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$$ **Step 2 — For a good conductor** $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$, so $\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon \approx \sigma$: $$\gamma^2 \approx j\omega\mu\sigma = j\omega\mu\sigma\left(\frac{1+j}{2} + \frac{1-j}{2}\right)$$ Using the identity $j = e^{j\pi/2} = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$, we get $\sqrt{j} = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\gamma \approx (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Read off attenuation constant:** $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Skin depth definition** (distance for field to drop to $1/e$): $$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}}$$ **Step 5 — Frequency dependence check:** Higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → more surface concentration. This is why **DC flows through the entire conductor cross-section** but **AC at 50 Hz in copper** is mostly near the surface ($\delta \approx 9$ mm). --- ### Derivation 4: Intrinsic Impedance in a Good Conductor Starting from $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$ and assuming $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$: $$\eta_c = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{\sigma}} \cdot \sqrt{j} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{\sigma}} \cdot \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_c = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sigma\delta}(1+j)}$$ The magnitude is $\vert\eta_c\vert = \frac{1}{\sigma\delta}$ and the **phase angle is exactly 45°** — a signature property of waves in good conductors, heavily tested in KTU exams. --- ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 100 MHz plane wave propagates in a medium with $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$, $\sigma = 10^{-3}$ S/m. Find: (a) loss tangent, (b) attenuation constant $\alpha$, (c) phase constant $\beta$, (d) intrinsic impedance $\eta$, (e) skin depth $\delta$. **Solution:** **(a) Loss tangent:** $$\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2\pi(10^8)(4)(8.854\times10^{-12})}$$ $$\tan\delta = \frac{10^{-3}}{2.225\times10^{-2}} \approx 0.0449$$ Since $\tan\delta < 0.01$? No, it is ~0.045. This is a **lossy dielectric** (use full $\alpha, \beta$ formulas). **(b) Attenuation constant:** $$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+\tan^2\delta}-1\right]}$$ $$\alpha = 2\pi(10^8)\sqrt{\frac{(4\pi\times10^{-7})(4)(8.854\times10^{-12})}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+0.0020}-1\right]}$$ $$\alpha \approx 2\pi(10^8)\sqrt{(2.222\times10^{-17})(0.001)}$$ $$\alpha \approx 2\pi(10^8)(1.49\times10^{-10}) \approx 0.0937 \ \text{Np/m}$$ **(c) Phase constant:** $$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+\tan^2\delta}+1\right]}$$ $$\beta \approx 2\pi(10^8)\sqrt{(2.222\times10^{-17})(1.001)}$$ $$\beta \approx 2\pi(10^8)(4.71\times10^{-9}) \approx 2.96 \ \text{rad/m}$$ **(d) Intrinsic impedance:** $$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-j\tan\delta}}$$ $$|\eta| = \frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt[4]{1+\tan^2\delta}} \approx \frac{120\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{1.001}} \approx 188.4 \ \Omega$$ **(e) Skin depth:** $$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.0937} \approx 10.67 \ \text{m}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Logic:** Notice how the loss tangent is the "switch" that routes you to the correct formula set. Always compute it **first** in your KTU answer scripts to claim full methodology marks. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Electromagnetic Wave Structure & Orthogonality ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Propagation_Direction_Plus_Z["Propagation Axis +z"] A["Origin of Wave
Source at z=0"] end subgraph Electric_Field_Axis["E-field Polarization +x"] B["Ex oscillates
E0 cos wt minus beta z"] end subgraph Magnetic_Field_Axis["H-field Polarization +y"] C["Hy oscillates
E0 over eta cos wt minus beta z"] end subgraph Power_Flow_Direction["Poynting Vector S = E cross H along +z"] D["Average Power Density
S avg = E0 squared over 2 eta"] end B -. "Right-Hand Rule
E x H = k" .-> D C -. "E and H are
mutually perpendicular" .-> D A --> B A --> C D -- "Wave travels
at phase velocity u = omega over beta" --> D ``` > **Figure Explanation:** In a uniform plane wave, the $\vec{E}$ field, the $\vec{H}$ field, and the direction of propagation form a **right-handed orthogonal triad**. The energy travels in the direction of $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ (the Poynting vector). ### Diagram 2: KTU Decision Flow — Classifying the Medium ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Given: omega, sigma, epsilon, mu"] --> Step1["Compute Loss Tangent
tan delta = sigma divided by omega epsilon"] Step1 --> Decision1{"Is tan delta
much less than 1?"} Decision1 -- "Yes
Lossless Dielectric" --> Branch1["alpha equals 0
beta equals omega sqrt mu epsilon
eta equals sqrt mu over epsilon"] Decision1 -- "Comparable to 1
Lossy Dielectric" --> Branch2["Use full alpha and beta formulas
with square root of 1 plus tan squared delta"] Decision1 -- "Much greater than 1
Good Conductor" --> Branch3["alpha equals beta equals sqrt pi f mu sigma
eta equals 1 plus j over sigma delta
delta equals 1 over alpha"] Branch1 --> Final["Apply Boundary
Conditions"] Branch2 --> Final Branch3 --> Final ``` ### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wave-Matter Interaction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Block1["SOURCE STAGE"] B1["Time-Varying Current
or Accelerating Charge"] end subgraph Block2["FIELD GENERATION STAGE"] B2["Maxwell-Ampere Law
Produces H around J"] end subgraph Block3["WAVE LAUNCH STAGE"] B3["Faraday Law
Time varying H induces E"] end subgraph Block4["PROPAGATION STAGE"] B4["Wave Equation Solution
E and H self sustain
travelling at velocity u"] end subgraph Block5["INTERACTION STAGE"] B5["Material Response
Determined by sigma, epsilon, mu"] end subgraph Block6["DELIVERY STAGE"] B6["Receiving Antenna
Poynting Vector Captures
Power Density S"] end B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 ``` > **Figure Explanation:** This block diagram captures the complete lifecycle of an EM wave from generation to reception — the conceptual framework KTU expects you to articulate in long-answer questions. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define the term "Intrinsic Impedance of a medium" and obtain its expression for a lossless dielectric.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The intrinsic impedance $\eta$ of a medium is defined as the ratio of the phasor electric field intensity to the phasor magnetic field intensity of a uniform plane wave travelling in the medium. $$\eta = \frac{\tilde{E}_x}{\tilde{H}_y}$$ For a lossless dielectric ($\sigma = 0$), starting from $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$ and substituting $\sigma = 0$: $$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{j\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$$ It is a **real quantity** (no phase shift between E and H). For free space, $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega$. **(3 Marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *What is skin depth? Write its expression for a good conductor.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Skin depth $\delta$ is defined as the distance into a conductor at which the amplitude of an electromagnetic wave falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its value at the surface. $$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$ For a good conductor ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$): $$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$ Physical significance: at this depth, the wave is effectively attenuated to zero; conduction currents flow only within one skin depth of the surface. **(3 Marks)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Choice Pattern #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the wave equation for the electric field $\vec{E}$ in a source-free, lossless medium. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 50 MHz uniform plane wave travels in a lossless medium with $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 9$. Calculate the phase velocity, wavelength, and intrinsic impedance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** 1. Start with Maxwell's curl equations in a source-free region ($\rho_v = 0$, $\vec{J} = 0$): $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu\frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$ and $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$. (1 Mark) 2. Take the curl of Faraday's Law: $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\mu\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})$. (1 Mark) 3. Use the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. (1 Mark) 4. Apply $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ (source-free) to get $-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\mu\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{H})$. (1 Mark) 5. Substitute Ampère–Maxwell Law for $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$. (1 Mark) 6. Since $\mu, \varepsilon$ are constants: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$. (1 Mark) 7. State that this is the 3D wave equation with phase velocity $u = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$. (1 Mark) **Model Solution (b):** Given $f = 50$ MHz, $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 9$. (1 Mark) Phase velocity: $u = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{9}} = 1 \times 10^8$ m/s. (2 Marks) Wavelength: $\lambda = \dfrac{u}{f} = \dfrac{10^8}{50 \times 10^6} = 2$ m. (2 Marks) Intrinsic impedance: $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \eta_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_r}{\varepsilon_r}} = 120\pi \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \ \Omega$. (2 Marks) --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the attenuation constant $\alpha$ and phase constant $\beta$ of a plane wave propagating in a lossy dielectric medium. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of skin effect and calculate the skin depth of copper ($\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$) at 1 kHz and 1 MHz. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** 1. Start with the wave equation in a conducting medium: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\sigma\frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} + \mu\varepsilon\frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$. (1 Mark) 2. For a time-harmonic field $\vec{E} = \tilde{\vec{E}} e^{j\omega t}$, the equation becomes $\nabla^2 \tilde{\vec{E}} = (j\omega\mu)(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\tilde{\vec{E}}$. (1 Mark) 3. Define the propagation constant $\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$. (1 Mark) 4. Separate real and imaginary parts by setting $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ and squaring: $\gamma^2 = \alpha^2 - \beta^2 + j2\alpha\beta$. (1 Mark) 5. Equate real parts: $\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon$. (1 Mark) 6. Equate imaginary parts: $2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma$. (1 Mark) 7. Solve simultaneously: $\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2}-1\right]}$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2}+1\right]}$. (1 Mark) **Model Solution (b):** Skin effect is the phenomenon wherein alternating current in a conductor tends to flow predominantly near the surface, with current density decaying exponentially into the conductor. (1 Mark) **At 1 kHz:** $\omega = 2\pi(10^3) = 6.283 \times 10^3$ rad/s. (1 Mark) $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu_0\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{(6.283\times10^3)(4\pi\times10^{-7})(5.8\times10^7)}}$$ $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.576\times10^5}} = \sqrt{4.37\times10^{-6}} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.09 \text{ mm}$$ (2 Marks) **At 1 MHz:** $\omega = 2\pi(10^6) = 6.283 \times 10^6$ rad/s. $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{(6.283\times10^6)(4\pi\times10^{-7})(5.8\times10^7)}}$$ $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.576\times10^8}} = \sqrt{4.37\times10^{-9}} \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.066 \text{ mm}$$ (2 Marks) **Comment:** Skin depth decreases as $\sqrt{f}$. At 1 MHz, current is confined to a much thinner surface layer than at 1 kHz. This is why high-frequency circuits use **plated conductors** (e.g., silver over copper) and why transformer laminations are used at 50 Hz. (1 Mark) --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks (Top 5 Mistakes):** > 1. **Forgetting $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$** when applying the vector identity. Without it, the derivation of the wave equation is **incomplete** and you will lose 2–3 marks. Always *state* the source-free condition explicitly. > 2. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$ formulas.** The signs inside the square brackets differ — $\alpha$ has $[\sqrt{1+x^2} - 1]$ while $\beta$ has $[\sqrt{1+x^2} + 1]$. Memorize them as a **pair** with different inner signs. > 3. **Ignoring units.** $\alpha$ is in **Np/m** (Nepers per meter) and $\beta$ in **rad/m**. The dimension of $\gamma$ is $m^{-1}$. Examiners will deduct marks if units are absent. > 4. **Confusing intrinsic impedance units** — for good conductors $\eta_c$ is complex with magnitude $\vert\eta_c\vert = \frac{1}{\sigma\delta}$, NOT $120\pi$ (which is only for free space). > 5. **Skipping the right-hand rule statement.** KTU awards marks for explicitly stating that $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ points in the direction of propagation. Saying "the wave travels" is not enough. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 - 🔑 **The wave equation is born by coupling Faraday's Law and Ampère–Maxwell Law** — taking the curl of one and substituting the other. - 🔑 **The displacement current term** $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ is the *only* modification by Maxwell that allows wave propagation in free space. - 🔑 **Free space**: $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$. - 🔑 **Loss tangent** $\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ is the **master switch** for choosing formulas. - 🔑 **In a good conductor**, $\eta_c$ has a $45^\circ$ phase angle and skin depth $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$. - 🔑 **EM waves are transverse** — $\vec{E}$, $\vec{H}$, and $\hat{k}$ form a right-handed orthogonal system. - 🔑 **Phase velocity** $u = \omega/\beta = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$. - 🔑 **Poynting vector** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ gives instantaneous power flow; its time-average is $\frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*\}$. - 🔑 **Helmholtz equation** $\nabla^2 \vec{E} + k^2 \vec{E} = 0$ is the phasor-domain form of the wave equation. - 🔑 **Wavenumber** $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ in lossless media; becomes $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ in lossy media. - 🔑 **Boundary conditions** (covered in detail in Module 5) will be needed to solve reflection/transmission problems at interfaces between two media.

topics.title: Wave Propagation in Lossy Dielectrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wave Propagation in Lossy Dielectrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Lossy Dielectric** is a medium that exhibits two simultaneous electromagnetic energy dissipation mechanisms:

1. **Conduction Losses** — caused by a finite, non-zero electrical conductivity **σ** (S/m), allowing free current flow.
2. **Dielectric Losses** — caused by the lag between the applied electric field **E** and the induced polarization **D** in the material.

When an **electromagnetic (EM) wave** propagates through such a lossy medium, its amplitude **exponentially decays** with distance due to ohmic heating of the medium, while simultaneously oscillating at a phase rate. The medium is mathematically described using a **complex propagation constant** $\gamma$ and a **complex intrinsic impedance** $\eta$, both of which contain real and imaginary parts that govern attenuation and phase behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates the derivation of the wave equation from Maxwell's curl equations for a conducting (lossy) medium, identification of the **attenuation constant (α)** and **phase constant (β)**, and the analysis of the two limiting cases: **good dielectric** and **good conductor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a swing that has a rusty chain (the lossy dielectric) instead of a frictionless one.

* Every time the swing oscillates (the **alternating electric field E**), the rust slightly resists the motion, converting some of the swing's mechanical energy into **heat** (this is **ohmic/dielectric loss**, governed by **σ** and the loss tangent **tan δ**).
* The swing's **amplitude** gradually shrinks as you watch it (this is **attenuation**), but the swing still completes its back-and-forth cycles at a specific **rate** (this is the **phase velocity / wavelength**, governed by **β**).
* The ratio of how much energy is **stored** vs how much is **lost per cycle** is captured by the **Loss Tangent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Loss Tangent** $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$ is the single most important figure-of-merit in lossy dielectric analysis. It tells us whether the material behaves more like an **insulator** (lossy dielectric, $\tan\delta \ll 1$) or a **conductor** (good conductor, $\tan\delta \gg 1$).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

* **Free-space permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
* **Free-space permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
* **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
* **Typical σ values:** Seawater ≈ **4 S/m**, Dry earth ≈ **10⁻³ S/m**, Copper ≈ **5.8 × 10⁷ S/m**, Glass ≈ **10⁻¹² S/m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations for a Lossy Medium

For a **homogeneous, isotropic, source-free lossy medium** with parameters $\varepsilon$, $\mu$, and conductivity $\sigma$:

$$
\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu\mathbf{H}
$$

$$
\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E} = j\omega\varepsilon_c \mathbf{H}
$$

where $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - \dfrac{j\sigma}{\omega}$ is the **complex permittivity** (a KTU-favorite reformulation that converts the lossy dielectric into an "equivalent lossless" medium).

## 2.2 Deriving the Wave Equation in Conductive Media

Taking the curl of Faraday's law and substituting Ampere's law (with conduction current):

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -j\omega\mu \,(\nabla \times \mathbf{H}) = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E}
$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E}$, and noting $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$ in a source-free region:

$$
\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}
$$

This is the **complex Helmholtz equation** for lossy media, where $\gamma$ is the **propagation constant**.

## 2.3 Propagation Constant Components

The propagation constant is **complex**:

$$
\gamma = \alpha + j\beta
$$

where:
* **α** = **Attenuation constant** (Np/m) — controls the exponential decay of the wave.
* **β** = **Phase constant** (rad/m) — controls the oscillation/wavelength.

Squaring and matching real-imaginary parts:

$$
\gamma^2 = (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) + j(2\alpha\beta) = j\omega\mu\sigma - \omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

Solving the system of two equations:

$$
\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

$$
2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma
$$

Solving simultaneously (the final expressions are the heart of this topic):

$$
\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

## 2.4 Intrinsic Impedance of Lossy Dielectric

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{j\omega\varepsilon\left(1 + \dfrac{\sigma}{j\omega\varepsilon}\right)}}
$$

This is **complex**, indicating that the E and H fields are **out of phase** in lossy media.

## 2.5 Skin Depth (Depth of Penetration)

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \;\; \text{(meters)}
$$

Defined as the distance at which the wave amplitude drops to **1/e (≈ 36.8%)** of its original value. It governs the **shielding effectiveness** of conductors and the **depth of ocean communication**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — these are the formulas you will reproduce in the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Complex permittivity | $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - j\sigma/\omega$ | F/m | Equivalent lossless representation |
| 2 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | 1/m | Combined attenuation + phase |
| 3 | Attenuation constant | $\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2}\,\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}-1\right]^{1/2}$ | Np/m | Amplitude decay |
| 4 | Phase constant | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2}\,\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}+1\right]^{1/2}$ | rad/m | Phase rotation |
| 5 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = \sigma/\omega\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Dielectric vs conductor test |
| 6 | Intrinsic impedance | $\eta = \sqrt{j\omega\mu/(\sigma+j\omega\varepsilon)}$ | Ω | E/H field ratio |
| 7 | Skin depth | $\delta = 1/\alpha$ | m | Penetration distance |
| 8 | Good dielectric (σ ≪ ωε) | $\alpha \approx (\sigma/2)\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | Np/m | Low-loss approximation |
| 9 | Good dielectric β | $\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | rad/m | Same as lossless |
| 10 | Good conductor (σ ≫ ωε) | $\alpha = \beta \approx \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$ | 1/m | High-loss approximation |
| 11 | Good conductor η | $\eta \approx (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/2\sigma}$ | Ω | Phase-shifted by 45° |
| 12 | Phase velocity | $v_p = \omega/\beta$ | m/s | Signal propagation speed |
| 13 | Wavelength in medium | $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ | m | Distance per cycle |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of α and β from γ²

### Step 1: Set up the governing equation

We start with the Helmholtz equation derived in Section 2.2:

$$
\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}
$$

where $\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$.

### Step 2: Expand the square of γ

Let $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$. Then:

$$
\gamma^2 = (\alpha + j\beta)^2 = \alpha^2 + 2j\alpha\beta + (j\beta)^2 = (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) + j(2\alpha\beta)
$$

### Step 3: Expand the RHS independently

$$
\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon) = j\omega\mu\sigma + j^2\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon + j\omega\mu\sigma
$$

### Step 4: Equate Real and Imaginary parts

Since both sides are complex, the real parts must equal and the imaginary parts must equal:

$$
\text{Real:} \quad \alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon \quad \cdots (1)
$$

$$
\text{Imaginary:} \quad 2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma \quad \cdots (2)
$$

### Step 5: Solve the system — find α² and β²

Square both equations:

$$
(\alpha^2 - \beta^2)^2 = \omega^4\mu^2\varepsilon^2
$$

$$
(2\alpha\beta)^2 = \omega^2\mu^2\sigma^2
$$

**Add** the two squared equations:

$$
(\alpha^2 - \beta^2)^2 + 4\alpha^2\beta^2 = \omega^4\mu^2\varepsilon^2 + \omega^2\mu^2\sigma^2
$$

The LHS is a perfect square: $(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2$. So:

$$
(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 = \omega^2\mu^2(\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2)
$$

Take the square root (positive root since α, β are positive physical quantities):

$$
\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} \quad \cdots (3)
$$

### Step 6: Extract α and β

**Subtract (1)² from (3):**

$$
(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 - (\alpha^2 - \beta^2)^2 = 4\alpha^2\beta^2
$$

But we already have $4\alpha^2\beta^2$ from the imaginary equation, and $(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 = \omega^2\mu^2(\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2)$, so:

$$
4\alpha^2\beta^2 = \omega^2\mu^2\sigma^2
$$

This is consistent — it confirms equation (2). Now solve for the individual values.

**Subtract (1) from (3):**

$$
(\alpha^2 + \beta^2) - (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) = 2\beta^2 = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} + \omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta^2 = \dfrac{\omega^2\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}\;}
$$

**Add (1) to (3):**

$$
(\alpha^2 + \beta^2) + (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) = 2\alpha^2 = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} - \omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha^2 = \dfrac{\omega^2\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}\;}
$$

### Step 7: Sanity-check using Limiting Cases

> **Case A — Perfect Dielectric (σ = 0):**
> The bracket $[\sqrt{1+0}-1]^{1/2} = 0$, so $\alpha = 0$ (no attenuation). And $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ — exactly the lossless medium result. ✓

> **Case B — Good Conductor (σ/ωε → ∞):**
> The bracket $[\sqrt{(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}-1]^{1/2} \approx \sigma/(2\omega\varepsilon)$, so $\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon/2}\cdot\sqrt{\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)} = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$. ✓ Matches the conductor result.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Intrinsic Impedance for Good Conductor

### Step 1: Start with the general expression

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the good-conductor condition (σ ≫ ωε)

Since $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$, the denominator simplifies: $\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon \approx \sigma\,(1 + j\omega\varepsilon/\sigma) \approx \sigma$. Thus:

$$
\eta \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}}
$$

### Step 3: Resolve the complex operator $j = e^{j\pi/2}$

$$
\sqrt{j} = \sqrt{e^{j\pi/2}} = e^{j\pi/4} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + j)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute and simplify

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{\sigma}} \cdot \sqrt{j} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{\sigma}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + j)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}\;}
$$

The **equal real and imaginary parts** confirm that in a good conductor, E leads H by **45°** and the magnitude of the impedance is very small (high conductivity → small η).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Attenuation Constant in a Good Dielectric

### Step 1: Apply condition σ ≪ ωε

When $\sigma/\omega\varepsilon \ll 1$, the term $\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2$ is small. Use the binomial expansion:

$$
\sqrt{1 + x} \approx 1 + \dfrac{x}{2} \quad \text{for } x \ll 1
$$

### Step 2: Substitute x = (σ/ωε)²

$$
\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} \approx 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2
$$

### Step 3: Insert into the α formula

$$
\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\omega^2\varepsilon^2}\right) - 1\right]^{1/2} = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\omega^2\varepsilon^2}\right]^{1/2}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$
\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha \approx \dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\;}
$$

This is a **frequency-independent** constant for good dielectrics — a famous KTU result.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 100 MHz plane wave propagates through a medium with $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$, $\mu_r = 1$, $\sigma = 0.01$ S/m. Compute α, β, η, and skin depth.

**Solution:**

Compute loss tangent: $\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon) = 0.01/(2\pi \times 10^8 \times 2.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 0.72$.

So this is a **transitional** case (neither pure dielectric nor pure conductor), and we must use the **full formulas**.

Using $\mu = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$, $\varepsilon = 2.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-11}$ F/m:

$$
\alpha = 2\pi(10^8)\sqrt{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(2.2135 \times 10^{-11})/2}\left[\sqrt{1+0.72^2}-1\right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\approx 2.32 \;\text{Np/m}, \quad \beta \approx 4.97 \;\text{rad/m}
$$

$$
\delta = 1/\alpha \approx 0.43 \;\text{m}, \quad v_p = \omega/\beta \approx 1.26 \times 10^8 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\eta = \sqrt{j\omega\mu/(\sigma+j\omega\varepsilon)} \approx 47.6\angle 20.2^\circ \;\Omega
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lossy Dielectric Wave Propagation

The diagram below maps the **logical flow** of a plane EM wave interacting with a lossy dielectric medium, from the source through propagation, attenuation, and final field-state extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Time-Harmonic EM Wave E0 cos wt] --> B[Enter Lossy Dielectric Medium]
    B --> C[Complex Permittivity eps_c = eps minus j sigma over w]
    C --> D[Maxwell Curl Equations Applied]
    D --> E[Helmholtz Equation nabla squared E equals gamma squared E]
    E --> F[Propagation Constant gamma equals alpha plus j beta]
    F --> G1[Attenuation Constant alpha computed]
    F --> G2[Phase Constant beta computed]
    F --> H[Intrinsic Impedance eta computed]
    G1 --> I[Skin Depth delta equals 1 over alpha]
    G2 --> J[Phase Velocity vp equals w over beta]
    G2 --> K[Wavelength lambda equals 2 pi over beta]
    H --> L[Limiting Case Selector]
    L --> M1[Good Dielectric: sigma much less than w eps]
    L --> M2[Good Conductor: sigma much greater than w eps]
    M1 --> N1[alpha approx sigma over 2 times sqrt mu over eps]
    M1 --> N2[beta approx w sqrt mu eps]
    M1 --> N3[eta approx sqrt mu over eps]
    M2 --> P1[alpha equals beta approx sqrt w mu sigma over 2]
    M2 --> P2[eta equals 1 plus j times sqrt w mu over 2 sigma]
    M2 --> P3[E leads H by 45 degrees]
    I --> Q[Output: Decaying and Oscillating Wave]
    J --> Q
    K --> Q
    N1 --> Q
    N2 --> Q
    P1 --> Q
    P2 --> Q
    Q --> R[Applications: Submarine Cables, RF Heating, EM Shielding, Antenna Design]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Identifying the Medium Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Given sigma, eps, w] --> T[Compute Loss Tangent = sigma over w eps]
    T --> U{Loss Tangent Value}
    U -->|less than 0.01| V1[Good Dielectric]
    U -->|between 0.01 and 100| V2[Quasi-Conductor or Transitional]
    U -->|greater than 100| V3[Good Conductor]
    V1 --> W1[Use low-loss approximations for alpha and beta]
    V2 --> W2[Use full expressions for alpha and beta]
    V3 --> W3[Use conductor approximations for alpha and beta]
    W1 --> X[Final: alpha, beta, eta, delta]
    W2 --> X
    W3 --> X
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — E/H Field Behavior in Lossy Dielectrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident E Field] --> B[Conduction Current Jc = sigma E]
    A --> C[Displacement Current Jd = j w eps E]
    B --> D[Total Current Jt = sigma + j w eps E]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Has Finite Phase Lag}
    E -->|Yes| F[E and H out of phase]
    F --> G[Complex Intrinsic Impedance eta]
    G --> H[Wave Attenuates and Propagates Simultaneously]
    H --> I[Exponential Decay: e to the power minus alpha z]
    H --> J[Phase Rotation: e to the power minus j beta z]
    I --> K[Final Field: E of z equals E0 e to the power minus gamma z]
    J --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with respect to wave propagation in a lossy dielectric medium:**
**(a) Attenuation constant (α)**
**(b) Phase constant (β)**
**(c) Loss tangent**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Attenuation constant (α):** It is the **real part** of the complex propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$. It measures the rate at which the amplitude of the EM wave **decays per unit distance** as it propagates through the medium, due to ohmic (conduction) and dielectric losses. **Unit: Np/m (Neper per meter).**

**(b) Phase constant (β):** It is the **imaginary part** of $\gamma$. It represents the rate of change of phase of the wave per unit distance and determines the **wavelength** in the medium: $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$. **Unit: rad/m.**

**(c) Loss tangent:** It is defined as $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$, the ratio of conduction current density to displacement current density. It is a **dimensionless** figure-of-merit that classifies the medium as a good dielectric ($\tan\delta \ll 1$) or a good conductor ($\tan\delta \gg 1$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**A 50 MHz EM wave travels through a medium with $\sigma = 0$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$. What is the attenuation constant, phase constant, and intrinsic impedance?**

**Model Answer:**

Given $\sigma = 0$, this is a **perfect (lossless) dielectric**. The medium has $\varepsilon = 4\varepsilon_0$, $\mu = \mu_0$.

$$
\alpha = 0 \;\; \text{(no losses)}
$$

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
\beta = \pi \times 10^8 \times \sqrt{4 \times 4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-18}} = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times \dfrac{2}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \;\text{rad/m}
$$

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon} = \sqrt{\mu_0/(4\varepsilon_0)} = \dfrac{377}{2} \approx 188.5 \;\Omega
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students attempt **ONE of TWO** alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's curl equations, derive the wave equation for the electric field in a lossy dielectric medium and obtain the expressions for the **propagation constant**, **attenuation constant**, and **phase constant**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a **good dielectric**, derive the simplified expressions for α, β, and the intrinsic impedance η. State the condition under which a medium is called a good dielectric. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Maxwell's Curl Equations (phasor form):**

$$
\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu\mathbf{H} \quad \cdots (i)
$$

$$
\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J}_c + j\omega\varepsilon\mathbf{E} = \sigma\mathbf{E} + j\omega\varepsilon\mathbf{E} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E} \quad \cdots (ii)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the curl of (i):**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \mathbf{H})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (ii):**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply vector identity** $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E}$, and $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$ (source-free):

$$
-\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify propagation constant:**

$$
\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon) = j\omega\mu\sigma - \omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Split γ into real and imaginary parts:**

Let $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$. Then $\gamma^2 = (\alpha^2 - \beta^2) + j(2\alpha\beta)$.

Equating real and imaginary parts:

$$
\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon
$$

$$
2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma
$$

**Step 7 — Solve simultaneously** (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$
\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Good Dielectric Condition:**

A medium is called a **good dielectric** when the **conduction current is much smaller than the displacement current**:

$$
\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \ll 1
$$

**Step 2 — Apply binomial expansion** to $\sqrt{1 + x}$ for $x = (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2 \ll 1$:

$$
\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} \approx 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into α:**

$$
\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2\right]^{1/2} = \dfrac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into β:**

$$
\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[2\right]^{1/2} = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 — Intrinsic Impedance** (good dielectric limit):

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{j\omega\varepsilon}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma}{j\omega\varepsilon}\right)} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \;\; \text{(real, lossless-like)}
$$

**Conclusion:** In a good dielectric, the wave attenuates **slowly** (small α, frequency-independent), propagates at $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$, and the intrinsic impedance is **approximately real**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** For a **good conductor**, derive the expressions for the **propagation constant, attenuation constant, phase constant, intrinsic impedance, and skin depth**. State the condition that defines a good conductor. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 1 MHz EM wave propagates in copper with $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$. Calculate α, β, η, and skin depth δ. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Step 1 — Good Conductor Condition:**

$$
\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \gg 1
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify α and β** using $\sqrt{1 + (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2} \approx \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$:

$$
\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} - 1\right]^{1/2} \approx \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} + 1\right]^{1/2} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

So in a **good conductor**: $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$.

**Step 3 — Intrinsic Impedance** (as derived in Section 3.2):

$$
\eta = (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}
$$

The real and imaginary parts are **equal**, indicating a **45° phase difference** between E and H.

**Step 4 — Skin Depth:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}
$$

**Physical meaning:** EM waves penetrate only a thin outer layer of the conductor. All current effectively flows within a depth of $\delta$ from the surface.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Given:** $f = 10^6$ Hz, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 1 — Check the good-conductor condition:**

$\omega\varepsilon = 2\pi \times 10^6 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \approx 5.56 \times 10^{-5}$ S/m.

Since $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7 \gg 5.56 \times 10^{-5}$, copper is an **excellent conductor**. ✓

**Step 2 — Compute α and β:**

$$
\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\pi \times 10^6 \times 7.297 \times 10^{1}} = \sqrt{2.293 \times 10^{8}} \approx 1.514 \times 10^4 \;\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Intrinsic Impedance:**

$$
\eta = (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi f \mu}{\sigma}} = (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{5.8 \times 10^7}}
$$

$$
= (1 + j) \times 8.27 \times 10^{-4} \;\Omega
$$

Magnitude: $\vert \eta \vert = \sqrt{2} \times 8.27 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-3} \;\Omega$ at angle **45°**.

**Step 4 — Skin Depth:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{1.514 \times 10^4} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} = 66 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 inside the bracket** when simplifying the good-conductor expressions — leads to wrong α.
> 2. **Mixing up α and β**: students often write $\beta$ for $\alpha$. In a good conductor, they are **equal**, but in a good dielectric, $\alpha$ is very small and $\beta$ dominates.
> 3. **Dropping the imaginary part of η**: in lossy media, η is always **complex**. Writing only the real part loses marks.
> 4. **Numerical errors in unit conversion**: Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz or mm to m.
> 5. **Missing the factor $\sqrt{2}$** in skin-depth formula derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Lossy Dielectrics**

- **Helmholtz Equation in lossy medium:** $\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}$.
- **Propagation constant:** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$.
- **Master formulas for α and β:** Always derive them by splitting $\gamma^2$ into real and imaginary parts, equating to $\alpha^2 - \beta^2$ and $2\alpha\beta$.
- **Loss Tangent** $\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ is the **master classifier**:
  * $\tan\delta \ll 1$ → **Good Dielectric**
  * $\tan\delta \gg 1$ → **Good Conductor**
  * $\tan\delta \approx 1$ → **Transitional** (use full formulas).
- **Good Dielectric Results:** $\alpha \approx (\sigma/2)\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ (frequency-independent), $\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$, $\eta \approx \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ (real).
- **Good Conductor Results:** $\alpha = \beta \approx \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$ (frequency-dependent, increases with $\sqrt{f}$), $\eta = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/2\sigma}$ (small, 45° phase).
- **Skin Depth:** $\delta = 1/\alpha$. For copper at 1 MHz, $\delta \approx 66$ µm. For seawater at 1 kHz, $\delta \approx 250$ m.
- **Intrinsic Impedance is ALWAYS complex** in a lossy medium — except in the perfect-dielectric limit.
- **Phase velocity** $v_p = \omega/\beta$ is **less than** the speed of light in lossy media.
- **Engineering Applications:**
  * **Submarine Communication** — uses ELF/VLF waves because seawater is a lossy medium.
  * **EM Shielding** — relies on skin depth being very small at high frequencies.
  * **Microwave Ovens** — operate at 2.45 GHz to penetrate food (lossy dielectric) and heat water.
  * **Antenna Ground Planes** — conductor behavior is essential.
  * **Bioelectromagnetics** — SAR calculations require α, β, η in human tissue.
- **For Numerical Problems:** Always compute loss tangent first to decide which formula set to apply.
- **For Derivation Problems:** The 7-step derivation of α and β (Section 3.1) is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Wave Propagation in Lossy Dielectrics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plane Waves in Free Space and Conductors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plane Waves in Free Space and Conductors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **electromagnetic plane wave** is a wave in which the electric field vector $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field vector $\vec{H}$ are:
- **Perpendicular to each other** (orthogonal, $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H}$)
- **Perpendicular to the direction of propagation** (transverse, hence **TEM – Transverse ElectroMagnetic**)
- **Uniform** over every plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A *uniform plane wave* is a wave whose $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields at any instant are uniform over every plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation. The wave propagates through unbounded homogeneous media such as **free space (vacuum)**, **lossless dielectrics**, **lossy dielectrics**, and **good conductors** with fundamentally different behaviors governed by the constitutive parameters $(\mu, \varepsilon, \sigma)$.

A plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction (in source-free, lossless, charge-free medium) has fields of the form:

$$\vec{E} = \hat{a}_x \, E_0 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$

$$\vec{H} = \hat{a}_y \, \frac{E_0}{\eta} \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$

where $\beta$ is the **phase constant (rad/m)** and $\eta$ is the **intrinsic impedance of the medium**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – The Pond Ripple vs. The Stadium Wave:**
> Imagine dropping a pebble in a perfectly still pond. The ripples spread outward as concentric circles. Now imagine you are standing far away from the pebble – the ripple fronts hitting you look almost like **straight lines**, not curves. That is essentially what a "plane wave" is: a slice of the wavefront so far from the source that its curvature is negligible, and the wavefront can be approximated as a flat plane.
>
> - The **height of the ripple** is the electric field $\vec{E}$.
> - The **horizontal motion of water particles** is the magnetic field $\vec{H}$.
> - The **outward propagation** is along the direction of $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ (Poynting vector $\vec{P}$).
>
> In **free space**, the wave never dies down (like an ideal, frictionless pond). In **conductors**, the wave is rapidly absorbed (like ripples in thick honey or mud – the medium "resists" the wave and converts it to heat).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (KTU Board Examinations)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta_0$ | $120\pi \approx 377$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for examiners:** A common 3-mark KTU question is *"Define intrinsic impedance of free space and state its value."* Memorize $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ – board examiners love this number.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown – Logic Steps

### Step 1: Source-Free Maxwell's Equations (Foundation)

For a **linear, isotropic, homogeneous, source-free** medium ($\vec{J} = 0$, $\rho_v = 0$), the four Maxwell's equations reduce to:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega \varepsilon \vec{E}$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$$

### Step 2: Derive the Helmholtz Wave Equation

Taking the curl of Faraday's law and substituting Ampère's law:

$$\nabla \times \nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$ and since $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega \mu \, (j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E} = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \, \vec{E}$$

This is the **vector Helmholtz equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \, \vec{E} = 0$$

### Step 3: Identify the Propagation Constant

Define $\gamma = j\omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon} = j\beta$ for lossless media, so the wave equation is $\nabla^2 \vec{E} - \gamma^2 \vec{E} = 0$.

In **lossless free space / dielectric**:

$$\beta = \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon} = \frac{\omega}{v_p}$$

where $\beta$ = phase constant (rad/m) and $v_p$ = phase velocity (m/s).

### Step 4: Intrinsic Impedance — Ratio of E to H

$$\eta = \frac{\vert \vec{E} \vert}{\vert \vec{H} \vert} = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega \mu}{\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon}}$$

For **free space** ($\sigma = 0$):

$$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \approx 377\ \Omega$$

### Step 5: General Solution for Lossy Media

For a lossy dielectric or conductor, the propagation constant is **complex**:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon)}$$

- $\alpha$ = **attenuation constant** (Np/m) – how fast amplitude decays
- $\beta$ = **phase constant** (rad/m) – how fast phase shifts

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\text{abs}(\cdot)$ in table cells – **NEVER** use the pipe character $\vert$ for absolute value as it breaks the table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Applicable Medium |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave equation | $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$ | – | Lossless |
| 2 | Phase velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$ | m/s | Lossless |
| 3 | Phase constant (lossless) | $\beta = \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}$ | rad/m | Free space / dielectric |
| 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | m | Any |
| 5 | Intrinsic impedance (general) | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega \mu}{\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon}}$ | $\Omega$ | Any |
| 6 | Intrinsic impedance (free space) | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi$ | $\Omega$ | Vacuum |
| 7 | Intrinsic impedance (lossless) | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ | $\Omega$ | Dielectric |
| 8 | Propagation constant (general) | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon)}$ | m$^{-1}$ | Any |
| 9 | Attenuation in good conductor | $\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Np/m | $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$ |
| 10 | Phase constant in good conductor | $\beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | rad/m | Good conductor |
| 11 | Skin depth | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | m | Good conductor |
| 12 | Intrinsic impedance (good conductor) | $\eta_c = (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu}{2\sigma}}$ | $\Omega$ | Good conductor |
| 13 | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | All |
| 14 | Average Poynting vector | $\vec{P}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{Re}[\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*]$ | W/m$^2$ | Any |

### Loss Medium Classification Rule (KTU Examiner Favorite):

> [!TIP]
> - **Lossless dielectric:** $\sigma \ll \omega \varepsilon \Rightarrow \tan \delta \ll 1$
> - **Lossy dielectric:** $\sigma \approx \omega \varepsilon \Rightarrow \tan \delta \approx 1$
> - **Good conductor:** $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon \Rightarrow \tan \delta \gg 1$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Antenna Design & Radio Wave Propagation** – Free space plane-wave analysis predicts signal coverage, link budgets for satellite communication, Wi-Fi, 5G.
2. **Microwave Ovens & Radar** – Behavior inside a conductor (cavity walls) demands understanding of skin depth.
3. **Shielded Cables & EMI/EMC** – Knowing how fast fields attenuate in copper helps design effective shields at 50 Hz to 10 GHz.
4. **PCB Trace Design** – At GHz frequencies, copper traces act like lossy transmission lines with skin-effect losses.
5. **Optical Fiber Communication** – Although waveguided, the fundamental transverse-plane-wave theory originates from free-space propagation concepts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Helmholtz Wave Equation for E (in Source-Free Medium)

**Starting Point:** Maxwell's curl equations in phasor form (time convention $e^{j\omega t}$).

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step A:** Take the curl of equation (i):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

**Step B:** Substitute equation (ii) into the right-hand side:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E}$$

**Step C:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. Since the medium is source-free, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$, so:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E}$$

**Step D:** Rearrange to get the standard **vector Helmholtz wave equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} - j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}}$$

where $\gamma^2 = j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon)$, and $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ is the complex propagation constant.

**Step E:** Specialization to **free space / lossless dielectric** ($\sigma = 0$):

$$\gamma^2 = j\omega \mu \cdot j\omega \varepsilon = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon$$

$$\gamma = j\omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon} = j\beta$$

Therefore the equation simplifies to:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \, \vec{E} = 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Intrinsic Impedance of Free Space

**Starting Point:** From Faraday's law, for a plane wave with $\vec{E} = \hat{a}_x E_x$ propagating in $+z$:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}$$

Compute the curl in Cartesian:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ E_x & 0 & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$= \hat{a}_y \frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z}$$

For a wave $E_x = E_0 e^{-j\beta z}$:

$$\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z} = -j\beta E_0 e^{-j\beta z} = -j\beta E_x$$

So:

$$\hat{a}_y (-j\beta E_x) = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}$$

$$\vec{H} = \frac{\beta}{\omega \mu} \hat{a}_y E_x = \frac{1}{\eta} \hat{a}_y E_x$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\omega \mu}{\beta} = \frac{\omega \mu}{\omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}}$$

**For free space:**

$$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.412 \times 10^{5}} \approx 376.73 \approx 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Plane Wave in a Good Conductor (Skin Depth)

**Setup:** For $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$, the propagation constant simplifies:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon)} \approx \sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma}$$

Using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, so $\sqrt{j} = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Equating real and imaginary parts:**

$$\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Penetration depth (Skin Depth):** Defined as the distance $\delta$ at which the field amplitude falls to $1/e$ ($\approx 36.8\%$) of its surface value:

$$E_x(z) = E_0 e^{-\alpha z} e^{-j\beta z}$$

Setting $e^{-\alpha \delta} = e^{-1}$:

$$\alpha \delta = 1$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$

**Intrinsic impedance of a good conductor:**

$$\eta_c = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega \mu}{\sigma}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu}{2\sigma}} = \frac{1+j}{\sigma \delta}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Average Power Density (Poynting Vector)

For a plane wave $\vec{E} = \hat{a}_x E_0 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ and $\vec{H} = \hat{a}_y \dfrac{E_0}{\eta} \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$:

$$\vec{P}(z,t) = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = \hat{a}_z \frac{E_0^2}{\eta} \cos^2(\omega t - \beta z)$$

Using the identity $\cos^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$\vec{P}(z,t) = \hat{a}_z \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta}\left[1 + \cos(2\omega t - 2\beta z)\right]$$

Time-averaged (over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$, the cosine term averages to zero):

$$\boxed{\vec{P}_{avg} = \hat{a}_z \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta}}$$

In phasor notation:

$$\vec{P}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\left[\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*\right]$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Across Media (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Free Space | Lossless Dielectric | Lossy Dielectric | Good Conductor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ (S/m) | $0$ | $0$ | Small | Very large |
| $\tan \delta = \sigma/(\omega \varepsilon)$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\approx 1$ | $\gg 1$ |
| Propagation constant $\gamma$ | $j\beta$ | $j\beta$ | $\alpha + j\beta$ | $(1+j)\alpha$ |
| $\alpha$ (Np/m) | $0$ | $0$ | $\omega\sqrt{\tfrac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega \varepsilon)^2} - 1\right]}$ | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ |
| $\beta$ (rad/m) | $\omega\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}$ | same form as $\alpha$ (above) | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ |
| Intrinsic impedance $\eta$ | $120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ | $\sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | Complex | $(1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$ |
| Phase velocity $v_p$ | $c$ | $c/n$ | $\omega/\beta$ | $\omega/\beta$ |
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | $c/f$ | $c/(nf)$ | $2\pi/\beta$ | $2\pi/\beta$ |
| Power at depth $z$ | Constant | Constant | $\propto e^{-2\alpha z}$ | $\propto e^{-2z/\delta}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A: Plane Wave Architecture – E, H and Direction of Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SOURCE PLANE z EQUALS 0"] --> B["WAVEFRONT PROPAGATES IN PLUS Z DIRECTION"]
    B --> C["ELECTRIC FIELD E ALONG X AXIS"]
    B --> D["MAGNETIC FIELD H ALONG Y AXIS"]
    B --> E["POYNTING VECTOR P ALONG Z AXIS"]
    C --> F["E CROSS H GIVES DIRECTION OF ENERGY FLOW"]
    D --> F
    F --> G["AVERAGE POWER DENSITY P AVG EQUALS E0 SQUARED DIVIDED BY 2 ETA"]
    C --> H["TRANSVERSE NATURE OF PLANE WAVE"]
    D --> H
    H --> I["NO E OR H COMPONENT ALONG DIRECTION OF PROPAGATION"]
```

```mermaid
    A0["INPUT: MEDIUM WITH MU EPSILON SIGMA AND FREQUENCY F"] --> B0["COMPUTE LOSS TANGENT TAN DELTA EQUALS SIGMA DIVIDED BY OMEGA EPSILON"]
    B0 --> C0{"DECISION: COMPARE TAN DELTA WITH UNITY"}
    C0 -->|"TAN DELTA LESS THAN 0 POINT 01"| D0["LOSSY DIELECTRIC APPROXIMATION 1: ALPHA LESS THAN BETA"]
    C0 -->|"TAN DELTA APPROXIMATELY 1"| E0["TRANSITION REGION USE EXACT FORMULA"]
    C0 -->|"TAN DELTA GREATER THAN 100"| F0["GOOD CONDUCTOR ALPHA EQUALS BETA EQUALS SQRT PI F MU SIGMA"]
    D0 --> G0["COMPUTE ETA AND BETA USING APPROXIMATION"]
    E0 --> G0
    F0 --> H0["COMPUTE SKIN DEPTH DELTA EQUALS 1 DIVIDED BY ALPHA"]
    G0 --> I0["COMPUTE E AND H FIELDS AND POWER FLOW"]
    H0 --> I0
    I0 --> J0["OUTPUT: COMPLETE WAVE DESCRIPTION IN THE MEDIUM"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C: Skin Depth Penetration Concept (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["CONDUCTOR SURFACE z EQUALS 0"] --> B1["FIELD E0 AT SURFACE"]
    B1 --> C1["DEPTH z EQUALS DELTA: AMPLITUDE IS E0 DIVIDED BY E"]
    C1 --> D1["DEPTH z EQUALS 3 DELTA: AMPLITUDE BELOW 5 PERCENT"]
    D1 --> E1["DEPTH z EQUALS 5 DELTA: AMPLITUDE BELOW 1 PERCENT"]
    E1 --> F1["EFFECTIVE CURRENT FLOW IS ONLY WITHIN FEW SKIN DEPTHS"]
    F1 --> G1["APPLICATION: SHIELDING, COAX CABLE LOSS, INDUCTION HEATING"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A common question is *"At what frequency is the skin depth of copper equal to 0.1 mm?"* You set $\delta = 10^{-4}$ m and solve for $f$. This diagram flow summarizes the chain of reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define intrinsic impedance of a medium. Derive its expression for free space and state its value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Intrinsic impedance $\eta$ of a medium is the ratio of the magnitudes of the electric field intensity $E$ to the magnetic field intensity $H$ of a uniform plane wave propagating through that medium, i.e., $\eta = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{\vert H \vert}$. It is an inherent property of the medium, measured in ohms ($\Omega$).

**Derivation (1.5 marks):** For a plane wave in lossless medium with $\vec{E} = \hat{a}_x E_0 e^{-j\beta z}$ and $\vec{H} = \hat{a}_y \dfrac{E_0}{\eta} e^{-j\beta z}$, applying Faraday's law:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}$$

$$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Value for free space (0.5 marks):**

$$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}} \approx 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$$

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What is skin effect? Define skin depth and write the expression for skin depth of a good conductor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Skin Effect (1.5 marks):** Skin effect is the phenomenon in which an alternating current (AC) flowing through a conductor tends to concentrate near the surface of the conductor rather than being uniformly distributed across its cross-section. The current density decreases exponentially with depth from the surface.

**Skin Depth Definition (0.5 marks):** Skin depth $\delta$ is defined as the depth below the surface of a conductor at which the magnitude of the electric field (or current density) falls to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its value at the surface.

**Expression (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} \text{ meters}$$

where $f$ = frequency (Hz), $\mu$ = permeability (H/m), $\sigma$ = conductivity (S/m).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the wave equation for the electric field in a source-free, homogeneous medium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform plane wave of frequency 1 GHz propagates in a lossless dielectric medium with $\varepsilon_r = 4$ and $\mu_r = 1$. Find:
- (i) The phase velocity
- (ii) The phase constant
- (iii) The wavelength
- (iv) The intrinsic impedance of the medium. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the Wave Equation (7 marks):**

In a source-free, homogeneous, linear, isotropic medium ($\vec{J} = 0$, $\rho_v = 0$), Maxwell's curl equations in phasor form are:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 1:** Take the curl of equation (1):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

**Step 2:** Use the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. For source-free media, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute equation (2):

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} - j\omega \mu (\sigma + j\omega \varepsilon) \vec{E} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Define propagation constant $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}}$$

For lossless medium ($\sigma = 0$), this reduces to:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \, \vec{E} \implies \nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \, \vec{E} = 0$$

**Score distribution:** (1) Initial equations (1 mark), (2) Curl operations (2 marks), (3) Vector identity + Gauss law (2 marks), (4) Final result (2 marks).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 1\ \text{GHz} = 10^9\ \text{Hz}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$.

**Step 1: Compute $\omega$:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^9 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1 \times 4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2}$$

$$\boxed{v_p = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Phase constant:**

$$\beta = \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon} = \frac{\omega}{v_p} = \frac{2\pi \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^8}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{4\pi}{0.3} = \frac{40\pi}{3} \approx 41.89 \text{ rad/m}}$$

**Step 4: Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{41.89} = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{10^9}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 0.15 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ cm}}$$

**Step 5: Intrinsic impedance:**

$$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \eta_0 \sqrt{\frac{\mu_r}{\varepsilon_r}} = 120\pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}} = \frac{120\pi}{2} = 60\pi$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 60\pi \approx 188.5\ \Omega}$$

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative Question)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the propagation of a uniform plane wave in a good conductor. Derive the expression for the propagation constant, attenuation constant, phase constant, and skin depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For copper at $f = 10\ \text{MHz}$, given $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$, calculate:
- (i) Skin depth
- (ii) Intrinsic impedance of copper
- (iii) The propagation constant $\gamma$
- (iv) Distance at which the wave amplitude drops to 1% of its surface value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Plane Wave in Good Conductor (7 marks):**

A good conductor is a medium where $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$. Under this condition, the displacement current $\partial \vec{D}/\partial t$ is negligible compared to the conduction current $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$.

**Step 1: Derive $\gamma$:**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)} \approx \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}$$

Using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, $\sqrt{j} = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Step 2: Equating real and imaginary parts:**

$$\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} \quad \text{m}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Attenuation constant $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} \quad \text{Np/m}$$

**Step 4: Phase constant $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} \quad \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 5: Skin depth $\delta$:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} \quad \text{m}}$$

**Step 6: Field behavior:** Inside a good conductor, the field decays rapidly as $e^{-\alpha z}$. Within just a few $\delta$, the wave is essentially extinguished – all energy is dissipated as heat (Joule heating, $I^2R$).

**Step 7: Intrinsic impedance:**

$$\eta_c = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}} = \frac{(1+j)}{\sigma \delta}$$

**Score distribution:** (1) Good conductor definition (1 mark), (2) Derivation of $\gamma$ and $\alpha,\beta$ (3 marks), (3) Skin depth + intrinsic impedance (2 marks), (4) Physical interpretation (1 mark).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 10\ \text{MHz} = 10^7\ \text{Hz}$, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$.

**Step 1: Compute the constant $\pi f \mu \sigma$:**

$$\pi f \mu \sigma = \pi \times 10^7 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7$$

$$= 4\pi^2 \times 5.8 \times 10^7$$

$$= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 5.8 \times 10^7$$

$$= 228.97 \times 10^7 = 2.2897 \times 10^9$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$:**

$$\sqrt{2.2897 \times 10^9} = \sqrt{22.897 \times 10^8} \approx 4.785 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (i) Skin depth:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{4.785 \times 10^4} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 20.9\ \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 4 (ii) Intrinsic impedance:**

$$|\eta_c| = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \times 10^7 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{8\pi^2 \times 1}{11.6 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{\frac{78.957}{11.6 \times 10^7}}$$

$$= \sqrt{6.807 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 8.25 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = (1+j) \times 8.25 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega = 8.25 \times 10^{-4} \angle 45^\circ\ \Omega}$$

**Step 5 (iii) Propagation constant:**

$$\boxed{\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = (4.785 \times 10^4)(1 + j) \text{ m}^{-1}}$$

$$\alpha = \beta = 4.785 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 (iv) Distance for 1% amplitude:**

We want $e^{-\alpha z} = 0.01$. Taking natural log:

$$-\alpha z = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$z = \frac{4.6052}{\alpha} = \frac{4.6052}{4.785 \times 10^4}$$

$$\boxed{z \approx 9.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 96.2\ \mu\text{m}}$$

This is approximately $4.6 \times \delta$, confirming the rule that "within 5 skin depths, the field is reduced to less than 1%."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board Examiners):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** Students frequently write $\alpha$ in dB/m when it should be in Np/m. Conversion: $1\ \text{Np} = 8.686\ \text{dB}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Power Loss:** Power decays as $e^{-2\alpha z}$ while field decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$. Many students erroneously write power also as $e^{-\alpha z}$.
> 3. **Mixing up intrinsic impedance formulas:** Using $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ for a *good conductor* is a critical mistake. Always use $\eta_c = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$.
> 4. **Loss Tangent misclassification:** Before declaring "good conductor," you must verify $\tan \delta = \sigma/(\omega \varepsilon) \gg 1$, usually $\geq 100$.
> 5. **Sign convention for $e^{j\omega t}$ vs $e^{-j\omega t}$:** KTU uses $e^{j\omega t}$ (engineer's convention). Students using $e^{-j\omega t}$ will get wrong signs in $\beta$ direction.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify source-free condition:** The wave equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}$ applies ONLY when $\rho_v = 0$ and $\vec{J} = 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Plane Waves):**

- [x] Plane waves are **TEM** (Transverse ElectroMagnetic): $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp$ direction of propagation.
- [x] **Wave equation** in phasor form: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}$ with $\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$.
- [x] **Free space** wave equation: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2 \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} = 0$.
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$.
- [x] **Phase velocity:** $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$; in free space $v_p = c$.
- [x] **Wavelength:** $\lambda = v_p / f = 2\pi/\beta$.
- [x] **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ classifies media.
- [x] **Good conductor:** $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$.
- [x] **Skin depth:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. Frequency dependence: $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$.
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance of good conductor:** $\eta_c = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$ — magnitude is very small, phase $45^\circ$.
- [x] **Average Poynting vector:** $\vec{P}_{avg} = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2\eta}\hat{a}_z$ (lossless), $\dfrac{E_0^2}{2 \vert \eta \vert} e^{-2\alpha z} \hat{a}_z$ (lossy).
- [x] In free space: **No attenuation**, only phase shift. In conductors: **rapid attenuation**, fields vanish within a few skin depths.
- [x] Standard $e^{j\omega t}$ convention is used by KTU. Wave propagates as $e^{-j\beta z}$ in $+z$ direction.
- [x] For copper at 10 MHz: $\delta \approx 21\ \mu$m; at 1 GHz: $\delta \approx 2.1\ \mu$m.
- [x] **Key relation in good conductors:** $\alpha \delta = 1$, $\beta \delta = 1$, $|\eta_c| = 1/(\sigma \delta)$.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4 — Plane Waves in Free Space and Conductors**
