topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Skin Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Skin Effect** is a high-frequency electromagnetic phenomenon observed in conductors carrying alternating current (AC), in which the **current density is non-uniformly distributed across the cross-section of the conductor**. Specifically, the current density is maximum at the **outer surface (skin)** of the conductor and decays exponentially toward the **center (core)**.

Mathematically, the current density $J(z)$ at a depth $z$ from the surface of a semi-infinite conducting slab is given by:

$$J(z) = J_{0}\,e^{-z/\delta}$$

where $J_{0}$ is the current density at the surface, $z$ is the depth measured from the surface inward, and $\delta$ is the **skin depth** (or depth of penetration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Skin Effect is a direct consequence of the **time-varying magnetic field** ($\partial \mathbf{B}/\partial t \neq 0$) inside the conductor, which induces opposing **eddy currents** (Faraday's Law) that suppress the current flow in the central region of the conductor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):** "Skin effect is the phenomenon in electromagnetic theory wherein the alternating current flowing through a conductor tends to concentrate near the surface of the conductor, with the current density decreasing exponentially with depth into the conductor, characterized by a decay constant known as the skin depth $\delta$."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **river flowing through a muddy channel**. The water in the middle flows faster and is more turbulent, dragging mud with it. But if you imagine an **opposing swirling current** at the center — like a hidden whirlpool pushing outward — the net forward flow at the center gets cancelled, and the water effectively flows only along the **sides (banks)** of the river.

Now translate this to electricity:
- The **river** = the conductor
- The **forward current** = the alternating current (AC)
- The **swirling whirlpool** = induced **eddy currents** generated by the conductor's *own* changing magnetic field (self-induction)
- The **river banks** = the outer surface of the conductor

### The Real Physics Behind the Analogy:
When AC flows, it produces a **time-varying magnetic field** ($\mathbf{B}$). By **Lenz's Law**, this changing $\mathbf{B}$ induces **eddy currents** *inside* the conductor that **oppose the change**. The result:

- At the **center**: induced e.m.f. is maximum → strong opposing eddy currents → net current is **minimum**
- At the **surface**: the induced e.m.f. is minimum (less flux linkage) → weaker opposing currents → net current is **maximum**

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Intuition Test:** Ask yourself — "Why is skin effect negligible at DC (0 Hz)?"  
> **Answer:** At DC, $\partial \mathbf{B}/\partial t = 0$ (steady magnetic field), so no eddy currents are induced. Current distributes **uniformly** across the cross-section. Hence, skin effect is a **purely AC phenomenon**, becoming significant above ~1 kHz for copper.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\text{H/m}$ |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{F/m}$ |
| Conductivity of Copper | $\sigma_{Cu}$ | $5.8 \times 10^{7} \;\text{S/m}$ |
| Conductivity of Aluminum | $\sigma_{Al}$ | $3.5 \times 10^{7} \;\text{S/m}$ |
| Relative Permeability (Copper) | $\mu_{r,Cu}$ | $\approx 1$ |
| Typical skin depth of Cu at 60 Hz | $\delta$ | $\approx 8.5 \;\text{mm}$ |
| Typical skin depth of Cu at 1 MHz | $\delta$ | $\approx 0.066 \;\text{mm}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The complete physics of skin effect can be broken down into the following rigorous, sequential steps:

### Step 1: Time-Varying Current Produces Time-Varying Magnetic Flux
An alternating current $i(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ flowing through a conductor establishes a **time-varying magnetic field** $\mathbf{B}(t)$ around and within the conductor.

### Step 2: Internal Flux Linkage with Conductor Cross-Section
The inner flux linkages of the conductor are *not* zero. The magnetic flux lines pass through the **interior cross-section** of the conductor itself. This is a critical distinction from an ideal solenoid, where the field is confined externally.

### Step 3: Induced Eddy Currents (Faraday's Law)
According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, a changing magnetic flux $\Phi$ through any closed path inside the conductor induces an EMF:

$$\text{EMF} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

This induced EMF drives **eddy currents** in closed loops *within* the conductor volume.

### Step 4: Lenz's Law — Opposition to Cause
By **Lenz's Law**, the eddy currents circulate in a direction such that their magnetic field **opposes** the original flux change. This effectively **cancels** the current density in the central core of the conductor.

### Step 5: Non-Uniform Current Density
The result is an **exponential distribution** of current density $J(z)$, with peak value at the surface ($z = 0$) and minimum at the center ($z = r$, the radius).

### Step 6: Effective Resistance Increase
Because the effective cross-sectional area for current flow **decreases**, the **AC resistance ($R_{ac}$)** becomes greater than the **DC resistance ($R_{dc}$)**. This is the most important engineering consequence of skin effect.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE list for KTU board exams. Every formula is examinable.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J(z) = J_0\,e^{-z/\delta}$ | Current density variation with depth $z$ |
| 2 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Skin depth (frequency-domain form) |
| 3 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | Skin depth (angular-frequency form) |
| 4 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r \sigma}}$ | Skin depth with relative permeability |
| 5 | $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$ | Attenuation & phase constant (good conductor) |
| 6 | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}$ | Intrinsic impedance of good conductor |
| 7 | $\dfrac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \dfrac{\pi r^2}{2\pi r \delta} = \dfrac{r}{2\delta}$ | Ratio of AC to DC resistance (for $r \gg \delta$) |
| 8 | $R_{ac} = \dfrac{l}{2\pi r \delta \sigma}$ | AC resistance of solid cylindrical conductor |
| 9 | $L_{i,ac} = \dfrac{\mu_0 l}{8\pi}$ | Internal inductance per unit length (high-frequency limit) |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{cond}} = (1+j)\;\dfrac{1}{\sigma \delta}$ | Surface impedance of conductor |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** In KTU answer sheets, students often write $\mu$ instead of $\mu_0 \mu_r$. Always specify whether the formula uses **absolute permeability ($\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$)** or **free-space permeability ($\mu_0$)**. The examiner **will deduct a half-mark** for ambiguity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Skin effect is not just a theoretical curiosity — it is foundational to modern electrical engineering design:

1. **Power Transmission Lines:** At 50/60 Hz, skin effect is small in copper (δ ≈ 8.5 mm) but causes measurable $I^2R$ losses. **ACSR (Aluminum Conductor Steel Reinforced)** cables are used to mitigate this.
2. **High-Frequency Electronics:** At MHz–GHz frequencies (RF, microwave circuits), skin depth is **micrometers** thick. Conductors are often **silver-plated** (highest $\sigma$) or made into **hollow tubes** to save weight (e.g., waveguides).
3. **Induction Heating & Surface Hardening:** Industrial induction furnaces **deliberately exploit** skin effect — high-frequency AC only heats the *surface layer* of metal, perfect for surface hardening of gears.
4. **Litz Wire Design:** To combat skin effect, Litz wire uses many fine, individually insulated strands woven together, each strand carrying a portion of the current.
5. **Telecommunications:** Coaxial cables are designed considering skin loss, which is a major attenuation mechanism above 1 MHz.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Skin Depth ($\delta$) from Maxwell's Equations

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 4. Master every line.

### Setup
Consider a **semi-infinite conducting medium** occupying the region $z > 0$, with a plane electromagnetic wave incident normally from the free-space side ($z < 0$).

The medium has conductivity $\sigma$, permeability $\mu$, and permittivity $\varepsilon$.

### Step 1: Maxwell's Equations Inside the Conductor

In a source-free, linear, isotropic conductor, Maxwell's curl equations are:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t} = -j\omega \mu \mathbf{H} \quad \text{(phasor form)}$$

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t} = \sigma \mathbf{E} + j\omega \varepsilon \mathbf{E} = j\omega \varepsilon_c \mathbf{E}$$

where the **complex permittivity** is defined as:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon - \frac{j\sigma}{\omega}$$

### Step 2: Wave Equation in the Conductor

Taking the curl of the first equation and substituting the second:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -j\omega\mu\,(\nabla \times \mathbf{H}) = -j\omega\mu\,(j\omega\varepsilon_c \mathbf{E})$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E}$, and since the conductor is source-free ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$ for a uniform medium):

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = j\omega\mu\,(j\omega\varepsilon_c)\,\mathbf{E} = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon_c\,\mathbf{E}$$

Thus, the **Helmholtz wave equation** in the conductor is:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}$$

where the **propagation constant** is:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Good-Conductor Approximation

For a **good conductor**, the conduction current $\sigma \mathbf{E}$ dominates the displacement current $j\omega\varepsilon \mathbf{E}$. This is valid when:

$$\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \gg 1$$

For copper at 1 MHz: $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon) \approx 5.8 \times 10^{7} / (2\pi \times 10^6 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 10^9 \gg 1$. ✓

Under this approximation:

$$\gamma \approx \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}$$

Using the identity $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, we have $\sqrt{j} = e^{j\pi/4} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Therefore:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma}\,e^{j\pi/4} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$

### Step 4: Extract Attenuation & Phase Constants

Equating real and imaginary parts of $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$:

$$\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$

### Step 5: Define Skin Depth

The **skin depth** $\delta$ is defined as the reciprocal of the attenuation constant $\alpha$:

$$\delta \equiv \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$$

Since $\omega = 2\pi f$, this can also be written as:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

This is the **master formula** for skin depth in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Current Density Distribution $J(z) = J_0 e^{-z/\delta}$

### Step 1: One-Dimensional Wave Solution

For a plane wave traveling in the $+z$ direction inside the conductor, the phasor electric field is:

$$\mathbf{E}(z) = \mathbf{E}_0\,e^{-\gamma z} = \mathbf{E}_0\,e^{-\alpha z}\,e^{-j\beta z}$$

### Step 2: Use Ohm's Law (Point Form)

Inside the conductor, the current density is related to the electric field by the **microscopic Ohm's law**:

$$\mathbf{J}(z) = \sigma \mathbf{E}(z) = \sigma \mathbf{E}_0\,e^{-\alpha z}\,e^{-j\beta z}$$

### Step 3: Express with Skin Depth

Substituting $\alpha = 1/\delta$:

$$\mathbf{J}(z) = \sigma \mathbf{E}_0\,e^{-z/\delta}\,e^{-j\beta z}$$

### Step 4: Magnitude Form

The **magnitude** of the current density at depth $z$ is:

$$J(z) = J_0\,e^{-z/\delta}$$

where $J_0 = \sigma E_0$ is the current density at the surface.

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

- At $z = 0$ (surface): $J(0) = J_0$ (maximum)
- At $z = \delta$: $J(\delta) = J_0/e \approx 0.368\,J_0$ (about 36.8% of surface value)
- At $z = 3\delta$: $J(3\delta) \approx 0.05\,J_0$ (negligible — only 5% remains)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** For practical engineering purposes, current flows within a depth of **$3\delta$ to $5\delta$** from the surface. Beyond $5\delta$, the current is less than 1% of the surface value.

## 3.3 Derivation of AC Resistance $R_{ac}$ for a Solid Cylindrical Conductor

### Step 1: Total Current Through Effective Cross-Section

For a solid cylindrical conductor of radius $r$ and length $l$ with $r \gg \delta$ (the high-frequency limit):

$$I = \int_0^r J(z)\,(2\pi r)\,dz \approx J_0 \int_0^\infty e^{-z/\delta}\,(2\pi r)\,dz$$

$$I = 2\pi r J_0 \delta$$

### Step 2: Surface Power Loss

The power dissipated per unit length is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma \delta} \cdot (2\pi r)$$

This uses the **surface resistance** $R_s = 1/(\sigma\delta)$.

### Step 3: AC Resistance

Using $P = \frac{1}{2}I^2 R_{ac}$:

$$\frac{1}{2}I^2 R_{ac} = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi r}{\sigma \delta}$$

Substituting $I = 2\pi r J_0 \delta$:

$$\frac{1}{2}(2\pi r J_0 \delta)^2 R_{ac} = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi r}{\sigma \delta}$$

Simplifying:

$$R_{ac} = \frac{1}{2\pi r \delta \sigma}$$

### Step 4: DC Resistance for Comparison

$$R_{dc} = \frac{l}{\sigma \pi r^2}$$

### Step 5: Resistance Ratio

$$\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{l/(2\pi r \delta \sigma)}{l/(\sigma \pi r^2)} = \frac{\pi r^2}{2\pi r \delta} = \frac{r}{2\delta}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** At high frequencies where $r \gg \delta$, the AC resistance grows linearly with $\sqrt{f}$, since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$. This is why **RF coils are often silver-plated** — to push the high-current region into the most conductive material.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define skin effect. Mention the factors on which skin depth depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Skin effect is the phenomenon in which alternating current does not distribute uniformly across the cross-section of a conductor, but instead concentrates near the surface (skin) of the conductor, with the current density decreasing exponentially with depth.

**Factors on which skin depth depends (1.5 marks):** From the formula $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$, the skin depth depends on:
1. **Frequency $f$** of the AC signal — $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$
2. **Permeability $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$** of the conductor material — $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\mu}$
3. **Conductivity $\sigma$** of the conductor material — $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\sigma}$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the expression for skin depth. What is its value for copper at 1 MHz? Given $\mu = \mu_0$, $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Expression (1 mark):**
$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 \times 5.8 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5.8 \times 10^{6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2408.3}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} = 66.1 \;\mu\text{m}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for skin depth starting from Maxwell's equations for a good conductor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** In a source-free, linear, isotropic conductor, Maxwell's curl equations in phasor form are:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu \mathbf{H}$$

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \sigma \mathbf{E} + j\omega\varepsilon \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Taking the curl of the first equation:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \mathbf{E}) = -j\omega\mu\,(\nabla \times \mathbf{H})$$

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E}) - \nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\mathbf{E}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For a source-free uniform medium, $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0$, giving the wave equation:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}, \quad \gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** For a **good conductor**, $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ (i.e., conduction current dominates displacement current). Therefore:

$$\gamma \approx \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Using the identity $\sqrt{j} = e^{j\pi/4} = (1+j)/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \alpha + j\beta$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Equating real and imaginary parts:

$$\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 7 (0.5 marks):** The skin depth is defined as the reciprocal of the attenuation constant:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}}$$

---

**(b) For a copper conductor ($\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$) at 50 Hz, calculate (i) the skin depth, and (ii) the ratio $R_{ac}/R_{dc}$ for a conductor of radius 1 cm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Skin depth (3.5 marks):**

Using the formula:
$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r \sigma}}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 50 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 \times 50 \times 5.8 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-7}}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 \times 50 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-1}}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 \times 29}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.385} = \frac{1}{33.83}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.02956 \;\text{m} \approx 2.96 \;\text{cm}}$$

**(ii) Resistance Ratio (3.5 marks):**

The condition $r \gg \delta$ does **not** hold here ($r = 1$ cm, $\delta = 2.96$ cm), so the formula $R_{ac}/R_{dc} = r/(2\delta)$ is **not strictly valid** in this problem. However, as per KTU's standard textbook treatment, we apply the formula:

$$\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{r}{2\delta}$$

$$\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 2.956 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{1}{5.912}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} \approx 0.169}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note for Students:** The fact that this ratio is **less than 1** indicates that the operating frequency (50 Hz) is **too low** for significant skin effect in this conductor radius. At higher frequencies, this ratio would exceed 1.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Explain the concept of skin effect in conductors. Discuss the variation of current density with depth, and show that the AC resistance of a solid cylindrical conductor of radius $r$ (with $r \gg \delta$) is $R_{ac} = l/(2\pi r \delta \sigma)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Skin Effect (2 marks):** Skin effect is the phenomenon in which alternating current tends to flow near the surface of a conductor rather than uniformly across its cross-section, due to the self-induced eddy currents from the conductor's own time-varying magnetic field.

**Current Density Variation (2.5 marks):** The current density at depth $z$ from the surface follows an exponential decay:

$$J(z) = J_0\,e^{-z/\delta}$$

This arises from the wave equation solution in a good conductor, where the attenuation constant $\alpha = 1/\delta$ governs the spatial decay of the field. At $z = \delta$, the current density drops to $J_0/e \approx 36.8\%$ of the surface value.

**Derivation of $R_{ac}$ (2.5 marks):**

For a cylindrical conductor with $r \gg \delta$ (high-frequency limit), the current is essentially confined to a thin annular region of thickness $\delta$ at the surface. The effective cross-sectional area is $A_{\text{eff}} = 2\pi r \delta$.

Total current:
$$I = \int_0^\infty J_0 e^{-z/\delta}(2\pi r)\,dz = 2\pi r J_0 \delta$$

The power dissipated per unit length using the surface resistance $R_s = 1/(\sigma\delta)$:
$$P/l = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 R_s (2\pi r) = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi r}{\sigma\delta}$$

Setting $P/l = \frac{1}{2}I^2 R_{ac}/l$ and substituting $I = 2\pi r J_0 \delta$:

$$\frac{1}{2}(2\pi r J_0 \delta)^2 \cdot \frac{R_{ac}}{l} = \frac{1}{2}|J_0|^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi r}{\sigma\delta}$$

$$R_{ac} = \frac{l}{2\pi r \delta \sigma} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) A copper wire of diameter 2 mm carries a 1 MHz signal. Calculate: (i) skin depth, (ii) the AC resistance per km, and (iii) the ratio $R_{ac}/R_{dc}$. Given $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Skin depth (2.5 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r \sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{5.8 \times 10^6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2408.3}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} = 66.1 \;\mu\text{m}}$$

**(ii) AC resistance per km (2.5 marks):**

For $r = 1$ mm and $\delta = 66.1\;\mu\text{m}$, we have $r \gg \delta$, so:

$$R_{ac} = \frac{l}{2\pi r \delta \sigma} = \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}$$

$$R_{ac} = \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}$$

$$R_{ac} = \frac{1000}{24.10}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ac} \approx 41.5\;\Omega/\text{km}}$$

**(iii) Resistance Ratio (2 marks):**
$$\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{r}{2\delta} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2 \times 6.61 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.322 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} \approx 7.56}$$

The AC resistance is **7.56 times** the DC resistance, illustrating severe skin effect losses at high frequencies.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> **1. Forgetting to convert $f$ to $\omega$ (or vice versa):** Students often mix $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ and $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ in the same problem. Pick **one** form and **stick to it**. Mixing them is an automatic **0.5 mark deduction**.
> 
> **2. Using $\mu_0$ instead of $\mu_0\mu_r$ for ferromagnetic materials:** For iron or steel conductors, $\mu_r$ can be **hundreds or thousands**. Using $\mu_0$ will give a wildly wrong skin depth. **Always check the problem statement** for the material's permeability.
> 
> **3. Misapplying the resistance ratio formula:** The formula $R_{ac}/R_{dc} = r/(2\delta)$ is valid **only when $r \gg \delta$**. If the problem gives you a thin wire or low frequency where this condition is violated, you must use the **Bessel function formulation** (advanced topic — usually out of KTU scope, but state the assumption clearly).
> 
> **4. Confusing skin depth with skin "thickness":** Skin depth is the depth at which $J$ falls to $1/e$ — it is **not** the total current-carrying depth. Engineers often use $3\delta$ or $5\delta$ as the effective thickness.
> 
> **5. Unit Errors:** $\sigma$ is in **S/m** (not m/$\Omega \cdot$mm²). Convert if the problem gives resistivity in $\Omega \cdot$m. Resistivity $\rho = 1/\sigma$.
> 
> **6. Skipping the Good Conductor Approximation:** When deriving $\delta$, students often write $\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$ but skip the step of justifying $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$. The examiner expects this justification for **full marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section!**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] Skin effect is the **non-uniform distribution of AC current** across a conductor's cross-section, with concentration at the surface.
- [x] It is caused by **self-induced eddy currents** (Faraday + Lenz) due to the conductor's own time-varying magnetic field.
- [x] It is **absent at DC** ($\partial B/\partial t = 0$) and **increases with frequency**.

### 🔑 Key Formulae (Memorize!)
- [x] **Skin depth:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$
- [x] **Attenuation constant:** $\alpha = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2} = 1/\delta$
- [x] **Current density:** $J(z) = J_0 e^{-z/\delta}$
- [x] **Resistance ratio:** $R_{ac}/R_{dc} = r/(2\delta)$ (valid for $r \gg \delta$)
- [x] **AC resistance:** $R_{ac} = l/(2\pi r \delta \sigma)$
- [x] **Intrinsic impedance of good conductor:** $\eta = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$

### 🔑 Trends to Remember
- [x] $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$ — doubling frequency reduces skin depth by factor $\sqrt{2}$
- [x] $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\sigma}$ — better conductors have *smaller* skin depth (counterintuitive!)
- [x] $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\mu_r}$ — ferromagnetic materials (iron, steel) have much smaller skin depth

### 🔑 Practical Engineering Values (Memorize for Estimation)
- [x] Copper at 60 Hz: $\delta \approx 8.5$ mm
- [x] Copper at 1 MHz: $\delta \approx 66 \;\mu$m
- [x] Copper at 1 GHz: $\delta \approx 2.1 \;\mu$m

### 🔑 Distinguish Carefully
- [x] **Skin effect** ≠ **Proximity effect** — Proximity effect is due to nearby conductors, not the conductor's own field.
- [x] **Skin depth** ≠ **Depth of complete current penetration** — Always use $3\delta$ to $5\delta$ for practical current-carrying depth.
- [x] **Good conductor** condition: $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon) \gg 1$

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps
- [x] Always state the **good conductor approximation** before deriving $\delta$.
- [x] Convert all units to **SI** before plugging into formulas.
- [x] Watch for $\mu_r$ in problems with iron/steel conductors.
- [x] Draw a small sketch of $J(z)$ vs $z$ in derivations — examiners love this!
