topics.title: Power and Poynting Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power and Poynting Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poynting Vector ($\vec{S}$):** The instantaneous power density (power per unit area) flowing through a surface in an electromagnetic field is given by the cross product of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$.
>
> $$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Poynting Theorem:** It is the statement of conservation of energy in an electromagnetic field. It states that the net power flowing out of a given volume at any instant equals the time rate of increase of energy stored in the volume minus the ohmic power dissipated in the volume.
>
> $$-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V w \, dV + \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} \, dV$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "River of Energy" Analogy**
> Imagine an electromagnetic wave traveling through empty space as a flowing river of energy. The electric field $\vec{E}$ is like the speed of the water flowing across the river, and the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is like the depth/height of the river. The Poynting vector $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ tells you **how much water (energy) is flowing per second per square meter** at any point, and **which way** the river is flowing. The right-hand rule (curl of fingers from $\vec{E}$ to $\vec{H}$, thumb gives $\vec{S}$) tells you the direction of energy flow — perpendicular to both fields, like the water flowing at right angles to the boat's width and the river's depth.
>
> Even though no physical "stuff" moves through space, **energy genuinely flows** — and Poynting's theorem is the bookkeeping that proves it.

**Key Physical Constants (KTU Exam Ready):**

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| $\eta_0$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\approx 377 \ \Omega$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |

### 1.3 Units & Dimensional Check

- $[\vec{S}] = [\vec{E}][\vec{H}] = (\text{V/m})(\text{A/m}) = \text{W/m}^2$ ✓
- The direction of $\vec{S}$ is given by the **right-hand rule**, perpendicular to both $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Poynting Theorem

The Poynting theorem is derived directly from **Maxwell's curl equations** in the time-domain (differential/point form):

- Faraday's Law: $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$
- Ampere-Maxwell Law: $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$

The logical flow is:

1. Take the dot product of $\vec{H}$ with Faraday's Law and $\vec{E}$ with Ampere-Maxwell's Law.
2. Subtract the two scalar equations.
3. Use the **vector identity**: $\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H})$
4. Recognize the energy-storage term: $\vec{E} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} + \vec{H} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}$
5. Where $w = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ is the total electromagnetic energy density (J/m³).

### 2.2 Why the Poynting Vector Matters — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why** does energy flow? Because time-varying electric and magnetic fields store energy, and a dynamic interchange occurs as the wave propagates.
- **How** is energy transferred? Through the **cross product** $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ — meaning both fields must be present and non-parallel.
- **Real-world utility:**
  - **Antenna design:** Determines radiated power in radio/wireless systems.
  - **Microwave engineering:** Waveguide power transmission calculations.
  - **Optical fibers:** Light energy propagation analysis.
  - **Transformer cores:** Identifies power losses due to leakage flux.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes **every key equation** you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. The notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ has been replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Equation | Description / Name |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ | Instantaneous Poynting Vector (W/m²) |
| 2 | $-\nabla \cdot \vec{S} = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$ | Differential form of Poynting's Theorem |
| 3 | $-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V w \, dV + \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} \, dV$ | Integral form of Poynting's Theorem |
| 4 | $w_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | Electric energy density (J/m³) |
| 5 | $w_m = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ | Magnetic energy density (J/m³) |
| 6 | $w = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ | Total EM energy density |
| 7 | $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^{*}\right)$ | Time-average Poynting vector (phasor form) |
| 8 | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}\right) \cdot d\vec{s}$ | Average power through a surface |
| 9 | $\vec{S} = \dfrac{\vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}}{\eta}\,\hat{a}_k$ for a plane wave | Plane-wave power flow along $\hat{a}_k$ |
| 10 | $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} = \sigma \vert \vec{E} \vert^{2}$ | Ohmic (conduction) power density loss |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Poynting's theorem** is the foundation of all wireless power transfer, satellite communication link budgets, RF heating (microwave ovens), and even explains the **radiation pressure** of light on solar sails.
- For a **lossy medium**, the term $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} = \sigma E^2$ represents the **Joule heating** (ohmic loss) — this is the principle behind microwave heating of food.
- The **time-average** version is what engineers use to size **transmitters, antennas, and transmission lines**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Poynting's Theorem (Differential Form)

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Curl Equations:**

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
$$

$$
\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 1:** Take the dot product of $\vec{H}$ with the first equation:

$$
\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the dot product of $\vec{E}$ with the second equation:

$$
\vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} + \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Step 2 from Step 1 (i.e., Step 1 minus Step 2):

$$
\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = -\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} - \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **vector identity** on the left-hand side:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = -\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} - \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 5:** In linear, isotropic media, $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ and $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$. Assuming fields are switched in/out at a constant rate, the order of differentiation w.r.t. time and space is interchangeable. Therefore:

$$
\vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2}\right) = \frac{\partial w_e}{\partial t}
$$

$$
\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right) = \frac{\partial w_m}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting Step 5 into Step 4:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right) - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}
$$

**Step 7:** Define the **Poynting vector** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ and total energy density $w = w_e + w_m$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{S} = -\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}
$$

**Step 8:** Rearranging gives the standard form of Poynting's theorem (differential):

$$
\boxed{\,-\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \vec{S} + \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of each term:**
> - $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}$ — Time rate of change of stored EM energy inside a volume (J/m³·s).
> - $\nabla \cdot \vec{S}$ — Net power flowing **out** of the volume per unit volume (W/m³).
> - $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$ — Power dissipated as heat (ohmic/conduction loss) per unit volume (W/m³).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Integral Form of Poynting's Theorem

**Step 1:** Take the volume integral of both sides of the differential form:

$$
-\int_V \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\, dV = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{S})\, dV + \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}\, dV
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the second term on the right:

$$
\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{S})\, dV = \oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Bring the time derivative outside the volume integral (volume is time-independent):

$$
\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V w\, dV - \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}\, dV
$$

**Step 4:** Final integral form (note the negative sign on the left for power leaving the surface):

$$
\boxed{\,-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V w\, dV + \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}\, dV\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Time-Average Poynting Vector (Phasor Form)

**Step 1:** Express fields as **phasors**: $\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{E}} \, e^{j\omega t}\}$, $\vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{H}} \, e^{j\omega t}\}$

**Step 2:** Instantaneous Poynting vector is:

$$
\vec{S}(t) = \vec{E}(t) \times \vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{E}}\, e^{j\omega t}\} \times \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{H}}\, e^{j\omega t}\}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the trigonometric identity: $\text{Re}\{A\}\text{Re}\{B\} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{AB^{*}\} + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{AB\,e^{j2\omega t}\}$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
\vec{S}(t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}\, e^{j2\omega t}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** The second term oscillates at $2\omega$ and averages to zero over a full cycle. So the **time-average Poynting vector** is:

$$
\boxed{\,\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}\right) \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complex Poynting vector $\tilde{\vec{S}} = \tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}$, and $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{S}}\}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A plane wave in free space has $\vec{E} = 100 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m. Find the instantaneous and average power density in the wave.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Intrinsic impedance of free space:

$$
\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Magnetic field (using $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_0}\hat{a}_k \times \vec{E}$, with propagation along $+\hat{a}_z$):

$$
\vec{H} = \frac{100}{120\pi}\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_y \ \text{A/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Instantaneous Poynting vector:

$$
\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = (100\cos(\omega t - \beta z))\,\hat{a}_x \times \left(\frac{100}{120\pi}\cos(\omega t - \beta z)\right)\,\hat{a}_y
$$

$$
\vec{S} = \frac{10000}{120\pi}\cos^{2}(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\vec{S} = 265.26 \cos^{2}(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Time-average. Using $\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{10000}{120\pi}\,\hat{a}_z = 13.26\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\vec{S}_{avg} = 13.26\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2\,}
$$

**Verification using phasor form:** $\tilde{\vec{E}} = 100\,\hat{a}_x$, $\tilde{\vec{H}} = \dfrac{100}{120\pi}\,\hat{a}_y$

$$
\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(100\,\hat{a}_x \times \frac{100}{120\pi}\,\hat{a}_y\right) = \frac{10000}{240\pi}\,\hat{a}_z = 13.26\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2 \ \checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow in Electromagnetic Fields

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Time-Varying Fields E t and H t"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Poynting Vector"]
    B --> C["S = E cross H"]
    C --> D{"Medium Type?"}
    D -- Free Space --> E["S avg = E0 H0 over 2 along propagation direction"]
    D -- Lossy Medium --> F["S avg attenuates by factor exp to power minus 2 alpha z"]
    E --> G["Output: Power Flow Direction and Magnitude"]
    F --> G
    H["Energy Storage Term"] --> I["dW by dt = half epsilon E squared plus half mu H squared"]
    I --> J["Combined with Power Flow Term"]
    J --> K["Poynting Theorem: Power Balance"]
    C --> K
    K --> L["Net power leaving volume = Rate of decrease of stored energy plus Ohmic loss"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow Through Different Media

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source: Antenna or Waveguide"] --> trans["Transmitted EM Wave"]
    trans --> mediumA["Lossless Dielectric"]
    trans --> mediumB["Lossy Conductor"]
    trans --> mediumC["Free Space"]
    mediumA --> sA["S avg = 0.5 E0 squared by eta"]
    mediumB --> sB["S avg decays exponentially"]
    mediumC --> sC["S avg = 13.26 W per m squared for 100 V per m plane wave"]
    sA --> load["Delivered to Load"]
    sB --> heat["Ohmic Dissipation as Heat"]
    sC --> rad["Radiated into Space"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Poynting Vector Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Ex["E field along X axis"] --> cross["Cross Product Operation"]
    Hy["H field along Y axis"] --> cross
    cross --> Sz["S along Z axis = E x H direction"]
    note["Right Hand Rule: Curl fingers from E to H, thumb gives S"] -.- Sz
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a logical step or physical component in the energy-flow analysis. The arrows indicate directional flow of computation or power. The dashed line in the last diagram shows a **side annotation** explaining the right-hand rule, which is essential for direction-of-flow problems in KTU exams.

### 4.4 Comparison Table: Poynting Vector in Lossless vs. Lossy Media

| Property | Lossless Dielectric | Lossy Conductor |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Intrinsic Impedance | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ (real) | $\eta_c = \sqrt{j\omega\mu/(\sigma+j\omega\varepsilon)}$ (complex) |
| $E$ and $H$ phase relation | In phase | Out of phase (complex) |
| Average power flow | $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2\eta}\hat{a}_k$ | $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2 \vert \eta_c \vert^{2}}\,\text{Re}(\eta_c^{*})\,\hat{a}_k$ |
| Attenuation | None | Exponential decay $e^{-2\alpha z}$ |
| Power dissipation | Zero | $\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J} = \sigma E^2 > 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define Poynting vector. What is its physical significance?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The Poynting vector is defined as the cross product of the electric field $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field $\vec{H}$:
>
> $$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$
>
> **Physical Significance:** It represents the **instantaneous power density** (power flowing per unit area) at any point in an electromagnetic field. Its direction (given by the right-hand rule) gives the **direction of energy flow**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State Poynting's theorem. Mention the physical meaning of each term in its differential form.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The differential form of Poynting's theorem is:
>
> $$-\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \vec{S} + \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$$
>
> **Meaning of each term:**
> - $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}$: Time rate of change of stored electromagnetic energy density.
> - $\nabla \cdot \vec{S}$: Net power flux diverging out of a point (W/m³).
> - $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$: Power dissipated per unit volume as heat (ohmic loss).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **forget the factor of 1/2** in the time-average Poynting vector formula. This is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> - Many confuse the **instantaneous** Poynting vector with the **average** one. Read the question stem carefully!
> - When deriving Poynting's theorem, **always show the vector identity step** explicitly — it carries 2–3 marks.
> - In numerical problems, the unit of $\vec{S}$ **must be W/m²**; missing units = -1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the differential form of Poynting's theorem from Maxwell's equations. Clearly state the physical meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plane wave propagating in free space has the electric field $\vec{E} = 50\sin(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_x$ V/m. Calculate the **instantaneous** and **time-average** power density. Given $\eta_0 = 120\pi \ \Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with Maxwell's curl equations:
$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}, \qquad \nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2:** Dot-multiply the first with $\vec{H}$ and the second with $\vec{E}$:
$$
\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
$$
$$
\vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} + \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract:
$$
\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = -\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} - \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply $\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H})$ identity:
$$
\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2\right) - \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ and $w = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$:
$$
-\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \vec{S} + \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}
$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}$: Rate of change of stored EM energy.
- $\nabla \cdot \vec{S}$: Net power flow out per unit volume.
- $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$: Power lost as heat per unit volume.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Magnetic field, with $\eta_0 = 120\pi \ \Omega$ and propagation along $+\hat{a}_z$:
$$
\vec{H} = \frac{50}{120\pi}\sin(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_y \ \text{A/m}
$$

**Step 2:** Instantaneous Poynting vector:
$$
\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = \frac{2500}{120\pi}\sin^{2}(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z
$$
$$
\boxed{\,\vec{S} = 6.63\sin^{2}(\omega t - \beta z)\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2\,}
$$

**Step 3:** Time-average (using $\langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$):
$$
\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2500}{120\pi}\,\hat{a}_z = 3.315\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2
$$
$$
\boxed{\,\vec{S}_{avg} \approx 3.32\,\hat{a}_z \ \text{W/m}^2\,}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Starting from the time-domain Maxwell equations, derive the **time-average Poynting vector** in phasor form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coaxial cable of length 1 m carries a current $I = 5\cos(\omega t)$ A with an applied voltage of $V = 50\cos(\omega t)$ V between inner and outer conductors. The electric field between the conductors is radial with magnitude $E = V/r\ln(b/a)$, where $a$ and $b$ are the inner and outer radii. The magnetic field is azimuthal with magnitude $H = I/2\pi r$. Using $a = 1$ mm, $b = 4$ mm, compute the **total time-average power** flowing through the cross-section of the dielectric. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of time-average Poynting vector (phasor form):**

**Step 1:** Express fields as phasors: $\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{E}}\,e^{j\omega t}\}$, $\vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{H}}\,e^{j\omega t}\}$.

**Step 2:** Instantaneous Poynting vector:
$$
\vec{S}(t) = \vec{E}(t) \times \vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{E}}\,e^{j\omega t}\} \times \text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{H}}\,e^{j\omega t}\}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply product identity: $\text{Re}\{A\}\text{Re}\{B\} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{AB^{*}\} + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{AB\,e^{j2\omega t}\}$.

**Step 4:** The $e^{j2\omega t}$ term averages to zero:
$$
\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Define the complex Poynting vector $\tilde{\vec{S}} = \tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}$, hence $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\tilde{\vec{S}}\}$. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Coaxial Cable:**

**Step 1:** Compute the phasor magnitudes. RMS values: $V_{rms} = 50/\sqrt{2}$, $I_{rms} = 5/\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 2:** Magnitudes of fields (using peak values, with $E_0 = 50/\ln(4)$):
$$
E_0(r) = \frac{50}{r\,\ln(4)} \ \text{V/m}, \qquad H_0(r) = \frac{5}{2\pi r} \ \text{A/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Direction of $\vec{S}$: $\hat{a}_r \times \hat{a}_\phi = \hat{a}_z$ (power flows along the cable).

**Step 4:** Instantaneous Poynting vector magnitude:
$$
S_z(r,t) = E_0(r) \cdot H_0(r) \cos^{2}(\omega t) = \frac{50 \cdot 5}{2\pi r^{2}\ln(4)}\cos^{2}(\omega t)
$$

**Step 5:** Time-average $\vec{S}_{avg}(r) = \dfrac{1}{2}E_0(r)\,H_0(r)\,\hat{a}_z = \dfrac{125}{2\pi r^{2}\ln(4)}\,\hat{a}_z$.

**Step 6:** Total average power through the annular cross-section:
$$
P_{avg} = \int_{a}^{b} S_{avg}(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{125}{2\pi r^{2}\ln(4)} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$
$$
P_{avg} = \frac{125}{\ln(4)}\int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{125}{\ln(4)} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{125}{\ln(4)} \cdot \ln(4) = 125 \ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{avg} = 125 \ \text{W}\,}
$$

**Sanity check:** $V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} = \dfrac{50}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{250}{2} = 125$ W ✓ — Matches the textbook relation $P = VI$ for a lossless transmission line.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition (must remember):** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$, unit is **W/m²**, direction given by **right-hand rule**.
- **Poynting Theorem (Differential):** $-\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \vec{S} + \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}$
- **Poynting Theorem (Integral):** $-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{s} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_V w\,dV + \int_V \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}\,dV$
- **Energy Densities:** $w_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$, $w_m = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$, $w = w_e + w_m$
- **Time-Average (Phasor):** $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\!\left(\tilde{\vec{E}} \times \tilde{\vec{H}}^{*}\right)$ — **do not forget the 1/2**.
- **Plane-wave power density:** $\vec{S}_{avg} = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2\eta}\,\hat{a}_k$
- **Ohmic loss term:** $\vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} = \sigma E^2$ — appears only in **lossy** media.
- **Common exam traps:**
  1. Confusing $\vec{S}$ (instantaneous) with $\vec{S}_{avg}$ — read the question!
  2. Forgetting the $\dfrac{1}{2}$ factor in time-average problems.
  3. Using $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ instead of $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*$ in phasor domain.
  4. Missing the negative sign convention in integral form of Poynting's theorem.
- **Final takeaway:** Poynting's theorem is the **energy-conservation law** of electromagnetics — energy in = energy stored + energy dissipated + energy out. Treat it exactly like Kirchhoff's energy law in circuit analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection of Plane Wave at Normal Incidence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reflection of Plane Wave at Normal Incidence ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reflection of a Plane Wave at Normal Incidence** is the electromagnetic phenomenon that occurs when a uniform plane wave propagating through one medium strikes a planar interface separating it from a second medium, with the wave vector **k** oriented perpendicular (90°) to the boundary surface. At this interface, the incident wave energy is partially or completely partitioned into a **reflected wave** (returning into medium 1) and a **transmitted wave** (refracted into medium 2), governed strictly by the intrinsic impedances of the two media. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, we study three distinct boundary configurations: 1. **Conductor–Conductor interface** (general lossy case) 2. **Lossless Dielectric–Lossless Dielectric interface** (σ₁ = σ₂ = 0) 3. **Dielectric–Perfect Conductor interface** (total reflection) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool with a flashlight, pointing the beam **straight down** onto the water surface (this is "normal incidence" — perpendicular to the surface). Two things happen: - A portion of the light **bounces back** to your eyes — this is the **reflected wave**, described mathematically by the reflection coefficient **Γ (Gamma)**. - The remaining light **enters the water** and travels deeper — this is the **transmitted wave**, described by the transmission coefficient **τ (Tau)**. Why does the light split instead of just passing through or just bouncing back? Because the two media (air and water) have **different "electrical resistances" to the wave's electric and magnetic fields**, called **intrinsic impedance (η)**. When these impedances are mismatched, the boundary "pushes back" on the wave, creating a reflection. The greater the impedance mismatch, the stronger the reflection — exactly like throwing a ball against a soft cushion (energy absorbed/reflected) versus a hard wall (energy reflected almost completely). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 376.73 \; \Omega \approx 120\pi \; \Omega$ - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m > [!NOTE] > The intrinsic impedance $\eta$ is the single most important quantity governing reflection — it is the **ratio of the transverse electric field to the transverse magnetic field** in a plane wave, and has units of Ohms ($\Omega$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Problem Setup & Wave Decomposition Consider a uniform plane wave traveling in the **+z direction** in **medium 1** with parameters $(\varepsilon_1, \mu_1, \sigma_1, \eta_1)$ approaching a planar boundary at $z = 0$. **Medium 2** occupies $z > 0$ with parameters $(\varepsilon_2, \mu_2, \sigma_2, \eta_2)$. The electric field is assumed to lie along the **x-axis**, and propagation is along **z-axis** (which is normal to the interface). The three wave components are: - **Incident wave** (medium 1, traveling +z): $$\mathbf{E}_i = \hat{a}_x \, E_0 \, e^{-\gamma_1 z}$$ - **Reflected wave** (medium 1, traveling –z): $$\mathbf{E}_r = \hat{a}_x \, \Gamma E_0 \, e^{+\gamma_1 z}$$ - **Transmitted wave** (medium 2, traveling +z): $$\mathbf{E}_t = \hat{a}_x \, \tau E_0 \, e^{-\gamma_2 z}$$ The associated magnetic fields (using $\mathbf{H} = \frac{1}{\eta} \hat{k} \times \mathbf{E}$): $$\mathbf{H}_i = \hat{a}_y \, \frac{E_0}{\eta_1} \, e^{-\gamma_1 z}$$ $$\mathbf{H}_r = \hat{a}_y \, \left(-\frac{\Gamma E_0}{\eta_1}\right) e^{+\gamma_1 z}$$ $$\mathbf{H}_t = \hat{a}_y \, \frac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2} \, e^{-\gamma_2 z}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The minus sign on $\mathbf{H}_r$ arises because when propagation reverses from +z to –z, the cross product $\hat{k} \times \hat{a}_x$ flips from $\hat{a}_y$ to $-\hat{a}_y$. This sign is critical for correct application of boundary conditions. ### 2.2 Application of Boundary Conditions at z = 0 The tangential components of $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ must be continuous across the boundary: **Condition 1 — Continuity of tangential $\mathbf{E}$:** $$E_i + E_r = E_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + \Gamma = \tau$$ **Condition 2 — Continuity of tangential $\mathbf{H}$:** $$\frac{E_i}{\eta_1} - \frac{E_r}{\eta_1} = \frac{E_t}{\eta_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{\tau}{\eta_2}$$ ### 2.3 Solving for the Coefficients Substituting $\tau = 1 + \Gamma$ into the second boundary condition: $$\frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{1 + \Gamma}{\eta_2}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$\eta_2 (1 - \Gamma) = \eta_1 (1 + \Gamma)$$ $$\eta_2 - \eta_2 \Gamma = \eta_1 + \eta_1 \Gamma$$ $$\eta_2 - \eta_1 = \Gamma (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$$ This yields the celebrated **Reflection Coefficient**: $$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}}$$ And the **Transmission Coefficient**: $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they form the backbone of every KTU numerical on this topic.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---| | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}$ | Fraction of $E$ reflected back | | Transmission Coefficient | $\tau = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$ | Fraction of $E$ transmitted forward | | Power Reflection Coefficient | $R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Fraction of power reflected | | Power Transmission Coefficient | $T = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \vert \tau \vert^2$ | Fraction of power transmitted | | Energy Conservation Check | $R + T = 1$ | Verifies no power is lost at the interface | | Intrinsic Impedance (General) | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$ | Universal form for any medium | | Intrinsic Impedance (Lossless) | $\eta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ | Used when $\sigma = 0$ | | Intrinsic Impedance (Good Conductor) | $\eta \approx (1 + j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu}{2\sigma}}$ | Used when $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ | | Intrinsic Impedance (Perfect Conductor) | $\eta_2 = 0$ | Total reflection case | | Phase Velocity (Lossless) | $u = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ | Speed of wave in lossless medium | | Wavenumber (Lossless) | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | Phase shift per unit length | | Wavelength (Lossless) | $\lambda = \dfrac{u}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}$ | Spatial period of the wave | | Standing Wave Ratio (SWR) | $S = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Measure of mismatch; $S \geq 1$ | | Skin Depth | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$ | Depth of penetration in good conductor | ### 2.5 Special Case Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** These special cases appear every semester — memorize them cold. **Case A: Perfect Conductor boundary ($\eta_2 = 0$)** $$\Gamma = \frac{0 - \eta_1}{0 + \eta_1} = -1 \quad ; \quad \tau = \frac{2 \cdot 0}{\eta_1 + 0} = 0$$ - The entire wave is reflected with a **180° phase inversion** in the electric field. - The transmitted wave vanishes ($\tau = 0$). - Power reflection: $R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = 1$; Power transmission: $T = 0$. - Standing wave ratio: $S \to \infty$ (perfect mismatch). **Case B: Identical media ($\eta_1 = \eta_2$)** $$\Gamma = 0 \quad ; \quad \tau = 1$$ - No reflection; the wave continues unchanged as if the boundary did not exist. - Standing wave ratio: $S = 1$ (perfect match — desired in transmission line design). **Case C: Same $\eta$ but different $\beta$ (both lossless, $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ or $\varepsilon_1 \neq \varepsilon_2$ matched)** If $\eta_1 = \eta_2$ but materials differ, $\Gamma = 0$ but **partial transmission and reflection of power still occur** in transient cases. For time-harmonic steady state, $\tau = 1$. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Antenna design:** Engineers use the reflection coefficient concept to design impedance-matching networks (e.g., quarter-wave transformers) that minimize $\Gamma$ and maximize power transfer from a transmitter to an antenna. - **Radar stealth technology:** Military aircraft are coated with radar-absorbing materials (RAM) engineered to have $\eta_2 \approx \eta_1$ (typically free space), so that $\Gamma \to 0$ and incident radar waves are absorbed rather than reflected back to the receiver. - **Optical coatings:** Anti-reflection coatings on camera lenses (e.g., $\text{MgF}_2$ layer) are designed so that destructive interference of reflected waves reduces glare. - **Microwave ovens:** The metal walls act as perfect conductors with $\Gamma = -1$, ensuring that microwave energy is contained within the cavity and forms standing waves for uniform cooking. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Reflection & Transmission Coefficients **Given:** A uniform plane wave with electric field $\mathbf{E}_i = \hat{a}_x E_0 e^{-\gamma_1 z}$ is incident normally on the boundary $z = 0$ between medium 1 ($z < 0$, parameters $\eta_1$) and medium 2 ($z > 0$, parameters $\eta_2$). **Step 1 — Write the total field in medium 1:** The total electric field in medium 1 is the superposition of incident and reflected waves: $$\mathbf{E}_1 = \mathbf{E}_i + \mathbf{E}_r = \hat{a}_x E_0 \left( e^{-\gamma_1 z} + \Gamma e^{+\gamma_1 z} \right)$$ The total magnetic field in medium 1 (recall the sign flip on $\mathbf{H}_r$): $$\mathbf{H}_1 = \mathbf{H}_i + \mathbf{H}_r = \hat{a}_y \frac{E_0}{\eta_1} \left( e^{-\gamma_1 z} - \Gamma e^{+\gamma_1 z} \right)$$ **Step 2 — Write the total field in medium 2:** The total field in medium 2 is purely the transmitted wave: $$\mathbf{E}_2 = \mathbf{E}_t = \hat{a}_x \tau E_0 e^{-\gamma_2 z}$$ $$\mathbf{H}_2 = \mathbf{H}_t = \hat{a}_y \frac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2} e^{-\gamma_2 z}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition on tangential $\mathbf{E}$ at $z = 0$:** The tangential component of $\mathbf{E}$ (the x-component, since the boundary is in the xy-plane and the normal is $\hat{a}_z$) must be continuous: $$\left. E_{1x} \right\vert_{z=0} = \left. E_{2x} \right\vert_{z=0}$$ Substituting the field expressions evaluated at $z = 0$: $$E_0 (1 + \Gamma) = \tau E_0$$ Dividing both sides by $E_0$ (assuming $E_0 \neq 0$): $$1 + \Gamma = \tau \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition on tangential $\mathbf{H}$ at $z = 0$:** The tangential component of $\mathbf{H}$ (the y-component) must be continuous: $$\left. H_{1y} \right\vert_{z=0} = \left. H_{2y} \right\vert_{z=0}$$ Substituting at $z = 0$: $$\frac{E_0}{\eta_1} (1 - \Gamma) = \frac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2}$$ Dividing both sides by $E_0$: $$\frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{\tau}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$ **Step 5 — Solve the system of two equations for $\Gamma$ and $\tau$:** From Equation A: $\tau = 1 + \Gamma$. Substitute into Equation B: $$\frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{1 + \Gamma}{\eta_2}$$ Cross-multiply by $\eta_1 \eta_2$: $$\eta_2 (1 - \Gamma) = \eta_1 (1 + \Gamma)$$ Expand both sides: $$\eta_2 - \eta_2 \Gamma = \eta_1 + \eta_1 \Gamma$$ Collect $\Gamma$ terms on the right and constants on the left: $$\eta_2 - \eta_1 = \eta_1 \Gamma + \eta_2 \Gamma$$ Factor $\Gamma$: $$\eta_2 - \eta_1 = \Gamma (\eta_1 + \eta_2)$$ Solve for $\Gamma$: $$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}}$$ Now substitute back into Equation A to find $\tau$: $$\tau = 1 + \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$$ Combine the terms over a common denominator: $$\tau = \frac{\eta_1 + \eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} + \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$$ $$\tau = \frac{(\eta_1 + \eta_2) + (\eta_2 - \eta_1)}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$$ $$\tau = \frac{\eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} = \frac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}}$$ **Step 6 — Verification: Note that $1 + \Gamma = \tau$:** Check: $1 + \Gamma = 1 + \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} = \dfrac{\eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} = \tau$ ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation: Power Reflection and Transmission Coefficients **Time-average Poynting vector** for a plane wave is: $$\langle \mathbf{S} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\left[ \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}^* \right]$$ **Power densities at $z = 0$:** - Incident power density: $S_i = \dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_1}$ - Reflected power density: $S_r = \dfrac{\vert \Gamma \vert^2 \vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_1}$ - Transmitted power density: $S_t = \dfrac{\vert \tau \vert^2 \vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_2}$ **Power Reflection Coefficient:** $$R = \frac{S_r}{S_i} = \frac{\dfrac{\vert \Gamma \vert^2 \vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_1}}{\dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_1}} = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$$ $$\boxed{R = \left\vert \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} \right\vert^2}$$ **Power Transmission Coefficient:** $$T = \frac{S_t}{S_i} = \frac{\dfrac{\vert \tau \vert^2 \vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_2}}{\dfrac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{2 \eta_1}} = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \vert \tau \vert^2$$ $$\boxed{T = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \left\vert \frac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} \right\vert^2 = \frac{4 \eta_1 \eta_2}{\vert \eta_1 + \eta_2 \vert^2}}$$ **Conservation Check ($R + T = 1$):** $$R + T = \frac{\vert \eta_2 - \eta_1 \vert^2}{\vert \eta_1 + \eta_2 \vert^2} + \frac{4 \eta_1 \eta_2}{\vert \eta_1 + \eta_2 \vert^2}$$ Expand the numerator of $R$: $$R = \frac{(\eta_2 - \eta_1)(\eta_2^* - \eta_1^*)}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)(\eta_1^* + \eta_2^*)}$$ For lossless (real) impedances, $\eta_1^* = \eta_1$ and $\eta_2^* = \eta_2$: $$R = \frac{(\eta_2 - \eta_1)^2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2} = \frac{\eta_2^2 - 2\eta_1\eta_2 + \eta_1^2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2}$$ $$T = \frac{4\eta_1 \eta_2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2}$$ Adding: $$R + T = \frac{\eta_1^2 - 2\eta_1\eta_2 + \eta_2^2 + 4\eta_1\eta_2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2} = \frac{\eta_1^2 + 2\eta_1\eta_2 + \eta_2^2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2} = \frac{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A plane wave traveling in free space ($\eta_1 = 120\pi \; \Omega$) is normally incident on a lossless dielectric with $\varepsilon_{r2} = 4$, $\mu_{r2} = 1$. Find $\Gamma$, $\tau$, $R$, $T$, and the standing wave ratio in medium 1. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\eta_2$:** $$\eta_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_2}{\varepsilon_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{4\varepsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} = \frac{120\pi}{2} = 60\pi \; \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Gamma$:** $$\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1} = \frac{60\pi - 120\pi}{60\pi + 120\pi} = \frac{-60\pi}{180\pi} = -\frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\tau$:** $$\tau = 1 + \Gamma = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $R$ and $T$:** $$R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$ $$T = 1 - R = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889$$ **Step 5 — Compute SWR:** $$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 1/3}{1 - 1/3} = \frac{4/3}{2/3} = 2$$ > [!TIP] > **Result Interpretation:** About 11.11% of the incident power is reflected back into free space, 88.89% is transmitted into the dielectric, and the standing wave ratio of 2 indicates a moderate mismatch. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Geometry of Normal Incidence The Mermaid block below illustrates the geometric configuration, field orientations, and wave interactions at the boundary between two media. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["MEDIUM 1
eps1, mu1, sigma1, eta1
z less than 0"]:::m1 nodeB["INTERFACE
z = 0
Tangential E and H continuous"]:::boundary nodeC["MEDIUM 2
eps2, mu2, sigma2, eta2
z greater than 0"]:::m2 nodeD["INCIDENT WAVE Ei
Travels in plus z direction
E along x axis"]:::wave nodeE["REFLECTED WAVE Er
Travels in minus z direction
E along x axis, scaled by Gamma"]:::wave nodeF["TRANSMITTED WAVE Et
Travels in plus z direction
E along x axis, scaled by tau"]:::wave nodeG["Gamma equals eta2 minus eta1 over eta1 plus eta2"]:::coeff nodeH["tau equals 2 eta2 over eta1 plus eta2"]:::coeff nodeI["R plus T equals 1
Energy conservation"]:::coeff nodeA --- nodeB nodeB --- nodeC nodeD --- nodeB nodeE --- nodeB nodeF --- nodeB nodeG --- nodeE nodeH --- nodeF nodeI --- nodeG classDef m1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef m2 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef boundary fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef wave fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef coeff fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Reflection Behavior Based on Medium 2 ```mermaid flowchart TB startA["Plane Wave Strikes Boundary at Normal Incidence"]:::start q1{"What is Medium 2?"}:::qst caseA["PERFECT CONDUCTOR
eta2 equals 0"]:::case1 caseB["LOSSY DIELECTRIC
eta2 is complex"]:::case2 caseC["LOSSY CONDUCTOR
eta2 has large imaginary part"]:::case3 caseD["SAME AS MEDIUM 1
eta1 equals eta2"]:::case4 outA["Gamma equals minus 1
tau equals 0
Total reflection with 180 phase flip
R equals 1, T equals 0
SWR approaches infinity"]:::out1 outB["Gamma is complex
Partial reflection and transmission
Magnitude less than 1"]:::out2 outC["Gamma close to minus 1
Most power reflected
SWR very large"]:::out3 outD["Gamma equals 0
tau equals 1
No reflection, perfect match
R equals 0, T equals 1
SWR equals 1"]:::out4 startA --> q1 q1 -->|eta2 is zero| caseA q1 -->|eta2 real and finite| caseB q1 -->|eta2 highly complex| caseC q1 -->|eta1 equals eta2| caseD caseA --> outA caseB --> outB caseC --> outC caseD --> outD classDef start fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef qst fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#997300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef case1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef case2 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef case3 fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef case4 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef out1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef out2 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef out3 fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef out4 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Standing Wave Formation in Medium 1 ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA["Source
Plane Wave Generator"]:::blk incF["Incident Field
E_i = E0 exp minus gamma1 z"]:::blk refF["Reflected Field
E_r = Gamma E0 exp plus gamma1 z"]:::blk sumN["Superposition Node
E_total = E_i + E_r"]:::blk bdryN["Boundary Node z=0
Apply BCs to find Gamma and tau"]:::blk standW["Standing Wave Pattern
Nodes and Anti Nodes form in M1"]:::blk transW["Transmitted Wave
E_t = tau E0 exp minus gamma2 z"]:::blk lossR["Return Loss
RL = minus 20 log10 of Gamma magnitude in dB"]:::blk srcA --> incF incF --> bdryN refF --> bdryN bdryN -->|reflects| refF bdryN -->|transmits| transW incF --> sumN refF --> sumN sumN --> standW bdryN --> lossR classDef blk fill:#ddeeff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram maps the spatial geometry (where each wave lives in 3D space). The second is a decision tree for predicting the reflection behavior based on what medium 2 is. The third traces the signal flow from source to standing-wave formation and return-loss calculation — useful for transmission-line analogy. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **Define the term "Reflection Coefficient" for a plane wave normally incident on the boundary between two media. Derive its expression in terms of intrinsic impedances.** **Model Answer:** The reflection coefficient ($\Gamma$) is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the reflected electric field to that of the incident electric field at the boundary of two media, when a uniform plane wave strikes the interface at normal incidence. It is a complex quantity whose magnitude indicates the fraction of the incident field strength that is reflected, and whose argument indicates the phase shift upon reflection. **Derivation:** Consider a plane wave $E_i = E_0 e^{-\gamma_1 z}$ incident normally on the boundary $z = 0$ between two media with intrinsic impedances $\eta_1$ and $\eta_2$. The total fields in media 1 and 2 are: $$E_1 = E_0 \left( e^{-\gamma_1 z} + \Gamma e^{+\gamma_1 z} \right), \quad H_1 = \frac{E_0}{\eta_1}\left( e^{-\gamma_1 z} - \Gamma e^{+\gamma_1 z} \right)$$ $$E_2 = \tau E_0 e^{-\gamma_2 z}, \quad H_2 = \frac{\tau E_0}{\eta_2} e^{-\gamma_2 z}$$ Applying continuity of tangential $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ at $z = 0$: $$1 + \Gamma = \tau \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$\frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{\tau}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ Solving (i) and (ii): $$\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$$ --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **What is the Standing Wave Ratio (SWR)? Write its expression. What is its value for a perfect dielectric-dielectric boundary and a perfect conductor boundary?** **Model Answer:** The Standing Wave Ratio (SWR) is defined as the ratio of the maximum value to the minimum value of the magnitude of the total electric (or magnetic) field along a transmission line or in a region where incident and reflected waves superimpose. **Expression:** $$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$ **Value for perfect dielectric-dielectric boundary:** - $\eta_1 = \eta_2 \Rightarrow \Gamma = 0 \Rightarrow S = \dfrac{1+0}{1-0} = 1$ **Value for perfect conductor boundary:** - $\eta_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \Gamma = -1 \Rightarrow \vert \Gamma \vert = 1 \Rightarrow S = \dfrac{1+1}{1-1} = \infty$ **Physical interpretation:** $S = 1$ indicates a perfectly matched line (no reflection). $S = \infty$ indicates total reflection (e.g., open or short-circuited termination). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > **(a)** A uniform plane wave of frequency **100 MHz** propagating in free space strikes normally a lossless dielectric slab of thickness $d$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 4$. Derive the expressions for the reflection coefficient and transmission coefficient when the wave is incident from free space onto the dielectric. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Calculate the numerical values of $\Gamma$, $\tau$, $R$, $T$, and the standing wave ratio in free space. Also find the wavelength inside the dielectric. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Given: $f = 100$ MHz, medium 1 is free space ($\eta_1 = 120\pi \approx 377 \; \Omega$), medium 2 is a lossless dielectric with $\varepsilon_{r2} = 4$, $\mu_{r2} = 1$. **Intrinsic impedance of medium 2:** $$\eta_2 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_2}{\varepsilon_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{4\varepsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} = 60\pi \; \Omega$$ The incident wave is: $\mathbf{E}_i = \hat{a}_x E_0 e^{-\gamma_1 z}$. The reflected and transmitted waves are: $\mathbf{E}_r = \hat{a}_x \Gamma E_0 e^{+\gamma_1 z}$ and $\mathbf{E}_t = \hat{a}_x \tau E_0 e^{-\gamma_2 z}$. Boundary conditions at $z = 0$: $$1 + \Gamma = \tau \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$\frac{1 - \Gamma}{\eta_1} = \frac{\tau}{\eta_2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ Solving: $$\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2} = \frac{60\pi - 120\pi}{60\pi + 120\pi} = \frac{-60\pi}{180\pi} = -\frac{1}{3}$$ $$\tau = 1 + \Gamma = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$ **(b) Numerical values:** $$\Gamma = -\frac{1}{3}, \quad \tau = \frac{2}{3}$$ $$R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 \; (11.11\%)$$ $$T = 1 - R = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889 \; (88.89\%)$$ $$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{4/3}{2/3} = 2$$ **Wavelength inside the dielectric:** Free-space wavelength: $$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{100 \times 10^6} = 3 \; \text{m}$$ In a medium with refractive index $n = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r} = \sqrt{4} = 2$: $$\lambda_2 = \frac{\lambda_0}{n} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \; \text{m}$$ --- > **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]** > **(a)** Derive the boundary conditions for the tangential components of electric and magnetic fields at the interface of two dielectric media. State the four boundary conditions in total (two tangential and two normal) for a source-free boundary. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A plane wave of amplitude $E_0 = 10$ V/m and frequency 1 GHz is normally incident from air onto a perfect conductor. Determine: (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the transmission coefficient, (iii) the standing wave ratio in air, and (iv) the maximum electric field amplitude in medium 1. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of boundary conditions:** The boundary conditions emerge from applying **Maxwell's equations** in integral form to a small pillbox or rectangular loop straddling the interface $z = 0$ between two source-free media. **Tangential $\mathbf{E}$ (continuity):** From Faraday's law $\oint \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$. As the loop height $h \to 0$, the flux vanishes, giving $\mathbf{E}_{1t} = \mathbf{E}_{2t}$. **Normal $\mathbf{D}$ (continuity):** From Gauss's law $\oint \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = Q_{\text{enc}}$. As the pillbox height $h \to 0$, the enclosed charge is zero (source-free), giving $\mathbf{D}_{1n} = \mathbf{D}_{2n}$, i.e., $\varepsilon_1 E_{1n} = \varepsilon_2 E_{2n}$. **Tangential $\mathbf{H}$ (continuity):** From Ampere's law $\oint \mathbf{H} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = I_{\text{enc}} + \frac{d}{dt}\int \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S}$. With no surface current, the RHS reduces to zero as $h \to 0$, giving $\mathbf{H}_{1t} = \mathbf{H}_{2t}$. **Normal $\mathbf{B}$ (continuity):** From Gauss's law for magnetism $\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = 0$, giving $\mathbf{B}_{1n} = \mathbf{B}_{2n}$, i.e., $\mu_1 H_{1n} = \mu_2 H_{2n}$. **Summary of 4 boundary conditions (source-free):** $$\mathbf{E}_{1t} = \mathbf{E}_{2t}, \quad \varepsilon_1 \mathbf{E}_{1n} = \varepsilon_2 \mathbf{E}_{2n}, \quad \mathbf{H}_{1t} = \mathbf{H}_{2t}, \quad \mu_1 \mathbf{H}_{1n} = \mu_2 \mathbf{H}_{2n}$$ **(b) Numerical computation for perfect conductor:** Given: $E_0 = 10$ V/m, $f = 1$ GHz, medium 1 = air ($\eta_1 = 120\pi \; \Omega$), medium 2 = perfect conductor ($\eta_2 = 0$). **(i) Reflection coefficient:** $$\Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1} = \frac{0 - 120\pi}{0 + 120\pi} = -1$$ **(ii) Transmission coefficient:** $$\tau = 1 + \Gamma = 1 + (-1) = 0$$ **(iii) Standing wave ratio:** $$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 1}{1 - 1} = \frac{2}{0} = \infty$$ **(iv) Maximum electric field in medium 1:** The total field in medium 1 is: $$\mathbf{E}_1 = E_0 \left( e^{-j\beta_1 z} + \Gamma e^{+j\beta_1 z} \right) \hat{a}_x = E_0 \left( e^{-j\beta_1 z} - e^{+j\beta_1 z} \right) \hat{a}_x$$ Using the identity $e^{-j\theta} - e^{+j\theta} = -2j\sin\theta$: $$\mathbf{E}_1 = -2j E_0 \sin(\beta_1 z) \hat{a}_x$$ The maximum magnitude occurs when $\sin(\beta_1 z) = \pm 1$: $$\vert \mathbf{E}_1 \vert_{\max} = 2 E_0 = 2 \times 10 = 20 \; \text{V/m}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Sign error in $\mathbf{H}_r$:** Forgetting the minus sign on the reflected magnetic field. This propagates into wrong boundary condition and wrong $\Gamma$. **Remember:** $\mathbf{H}_r = \hat{a}_y (-\Gamma E_0 / \eta_1) e^{+\gamma_1 z}$. > > 2. **Confusing $R$ with $\vert \Gamma \vert$:** $R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ (power ratio), NOT $\vert \Gamma \vert$ (field ratio). Many students write $R = \Gamma$ and lose 2–3 marks instantly. > > 3. **Wrong transmission coefficient formula:** The standard form is $\tau = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$. Some textbooks use $\tau = \dfrac{2\eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$ — **check which impedance is in the numerator** based on direction of incidence. With the convention used in KTU syllabus, $\tau$ uses $\eta_2$ in the numerator. > > 4. **Forgetting the factor $\eta_1/\eta_2$ in $T$:** The power transmission coefficient is $T = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \vert \tau \vert^2$, NOT just $\vert \tau \vert^2$. > > 5. **SWR with $\Gamma = -1$:** When $\eta_2 = 0$, $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$, so the denominator of SWR becomes zero — state explicitly $S = \infty$ rather than writing "undefined". > > 6. **Units of $\eta$:** Always express in Ohms ($\Omega$), and remember $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \; \Omega$ for free space. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma = \dfrac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$ — single most important equation of this module. - **Transmission Coefficient:** $\tau = 1 + \Gamma = \dfrac{2\eta_2}{\eta_1 + \eta_2}$. - **Power Reflection Coefficient:** $R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$. - **Power Transmission Coefficient:** $T = \dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \vert \tau \vert^2 = \dfrac{4\eta_1 \eta_2}{\vert \eta_1 + \eta_2 \vert^2}$. - **Energy conservation:** $R + T = 1$ (always verify in numerical problems). - **Perfect conductor:** $\Gamma = -1$, $\tau = 0$, $S = \infty$, $R = 1$, $T = 0$, total reflection with 180° phase inversion in $\mathbf{E}$. - **Perfect dielectric match:** $\eta_1 = \eta_2 \Rightarrow \Gamma = 0$, $\tau = 1$, $S = 1$, $R = 0$, $T = 1$, no reflection. - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \; \Omega \approx 377 \; \Omega$. - **Lossless dielectric:** $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$, $\eta \propto 1/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ when $\mu_r = 1$. - **Boundary conditions (source-free):** Tangential $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are continuous; normal $\mathbf{D}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are continuous. - **Standing Wave Ratio (SWR):** $S = (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) \geq 1$ — indicates degree of mismatch. - **Wavelength in medium:** $\lambda = u/f = 1/(f\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon})$; wavelength decreases inside denser media. - **Engineering applications:** Antenna matching networks, radar-absorbing materials, anti-reflection coatings, microwave cavity design, fiber-optic coupling. - **Memory trick:** "**Bigger η₂ relative to η₁ → positive Γ; smaller η₂ → negative Γ**." A perfect conductor has $\eta_2 = 0$, the smallest possible, giving $\Gamma = -1$.

topics.title: Transmission Lines: Transmission Line Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Lines: Transmission Line Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Line Equations (Telegrapher's Equations)** are a set of coupled first-order partial differential equations that govern the propagation of voltage and current waves along a distributed parameter network. They form the foundational mathematical framework for analysing **high-frequency** and **long-distance** electrical signal transfer.

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **transmission line** is a pair of parallel conductors designed to convey electromagnetic energy (or signals) from one point to another. When the physical length of the line becomes comparable to the wavelength of the operating signal (typically when $l \geq \lambda/10$ or when frequencies exceed **$\mathbf{30 \text{ MHz}$**), the line can no longer be treated as a "lumped" circuit. Instead, its electrical behaviour is described by four **distributed parameters** spread uniformly along its entire length:

- $\mathbf{R}$ – Series resistance per unit length (Ω/m)
- $\mathbf{L}$ – Series inductance per unit length (H/m)
- $\mathbf{G}$ – Shunt conductance per unit length (S/m)
- $\mathbf{C}$ – Shunt capacitance per unit length (F/m)

The differential equations that relate the spatial rate of change of voltage ($V$) and current ($I$) to these parameters and to the time rate of change are collectively known as the **Telegrapher's Equations**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Pressure Waves in a Long Pipe with a Leaky Joint**
> Imagine a long, slightly leaky garden hose connected to a pump. As water (current) flows, there is friction along the walls (resistance $R$) and inertia in the moving column of water (inductance $L$). At the same time, the hose is slightly porous, allowing water to seep out (conductance $G$), and the elastic walls of the hose can store water under pressure (capacitance $C$).
> 
> If you suddenly change the pump pressure at one end, the new pressure and flow rate don't appear instantly at the other end — they travel as a **wave**. The pressure difference between two infinitesimally close points in the hose depends on how much water leaks out and how much the walls stretch between them. This is *exactly* how voltage and current waves travel on an electrical transmission line.

### 1.3 Why Transmission Line Theory is Necessary

At low frequencies, a wire simply connects two points with negligible phase shift. However, at high frequencies:
- The signal wavelength $\lambda = c/f$ becomes very small.
- The finite propagation time ($\approx 3.33 \text{ ns/m}$ for $\mathbf{c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$) can no longer be ignored.
- **Reflections, standing waves, and impedance mismatches** become dominant effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PCEET502 explicitly requires the derivation of transmission line equations using **Maxwell's equations** and from **circuit theory**, the solution of these equations for waves travelling in the $+z$ and $-z$ directions, and the identification of the **Characteristic Impedance** and **Propagation Constant** (which will be covered in detail in the next sub-topic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Equivalent Circuit of an Infinitesimal Line Element

A transmission line is modelled as an infinite cascade of identical differential sections, each of length $\Delta z$. Each section contains a series impedance $Z' \Delta z$ and a shunt admittance $Y' \Delta z$.

For a **two-wire transmission line**, applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to the elemental section $\Delta z$ and then taking the limit $\Delta z \to 0$ gives the **generalised telegrapher's equations**.

### 2.2 Time-Domain Form (General)

The equations in the time domain ($t$ and $z$ as independent variables) are:

$$-\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial z} = R \, i(z,t) + L \frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

$$-\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial z} = G \, v(z,t) + C \frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

### 2.3 Phasor (Frequency-Domain) Form

Assuming **time-harmonic** sinusoidal steady-state operation $v(z,t) = \text{Re}\{V(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$, the time derivatives become algebraic ($j\omega$):

$$-\frac{dV(z)}{dz} = (R + j\omega L) \, I(z) = Z' \, I(z)$$

$$-\frac{dI(z)}{dz} = (G + j\omega C) \, V(z) = Y' \, V(z)$$

where $Z' = R + j\omega L$ is the **series impedance per unit length** and $Y' = G + j\omega C$ is the **shunt admittance per unit length**.

### 2.4 Second-Order Wave Equations

Differentiating the first phasor equation with respect to $z$ and substituting the second yields the **one-dimensional Helmholtz equation**:

$$\frac{d^2 V(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2 V(z)$$

$$\frac{d^2 I(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2 I(z)$$

where the **complex propagation constant** is defined as:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{Z' Y'} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$$

The general solution is:

$$V(z) = V_0^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

$$I(z) = I_0^+ e^{-\gamma z} + I_0^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question in Module 4 is built from these equations.**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series Impedance / unit length | $Z'$ | $R + j\omega L$ | Resistance + Inductive reactance per metre |
| Shunt Admittance / unit length | $Y'$ | $G + j\omega C$ | Conductance + Capacitive susceptance per metre |
| Propagation Constant | $\gamma$ | $\sqrt{Z'Y'} = \alpha + j\beta$ | $\alpha$ = attenuation (Np/m), $\beta$ = phase (rad/m) |
| Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{Z'/Y'} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ | Ratio of voltage to current for an infinite line |
| Phase Velocity | $v_p$ | $\omega / \beta$ | Speed of wave propagation along the line |
| Wavelength on line | $\lambda$ | $2\pi / \beta$ | Distance for one full phase cycle |
| Voltage (general solution) | $V(z)$ | $V_0^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^- e^{+\gamma z}$ | Forward + reflected waves |
| Current (general solution) | $I(z)$ | $\frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma z}$ | Forward − reflected waves |
| Input Impedance (lossless) | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta \ell)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta \ell)}$ | Depends on $Z_L$, $Z_0$, and $\ell$ |
| Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma$ | $\dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Ratio of reflected to incident voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Lossless Lines:** For the special case of a **lossless line** ($R = 0$, $G = 0$), the propagation constant simplifies to $\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ and the characteristic impedance becomes purely real: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. These results will be heavily used in the KTU problems.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PCB Trace Design:** High-speed digital circuits (DDR, PCIe, USB 3.0) require controlled-impedance traces to prevent signal distortion.
> - **Antenna Feed Lines:** Coaxial cables delivering RF power to antennas must be matched in $Z_0$ (typically **$50 \ \Omega$** or **$75 \ \Omega$**) to avoid standing waves.
> - **Power Transmission:** The Telegrapher's equations also model long overhead power lines, predicting surges and fault transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Telegrapher's Equations from Circuit Theory (KTU Favourite)

**Step 1 — Model the infinitesimal section.**
Consider a section of two-wire line of length $\Delta z$ with distributed parameters $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ per unit length.

The series branch contains a resistance $R\Delta z$ and inductance $L\Delta z$.
The shunt branch contains a conductance $G\Delta z$ and capacitance $C\Delta z$.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL to the loop.**
Let the voltage at position $z$ be $v(z,t)$ and at $z+\Delta z$ be $v(z+\Delta z, t)$. The current entering the section is $i(z,t)$.

$$v(z,t) = R\Delta z \, i(z,t) + L\Delta z \, \frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t} + v(z+\Delta z, t)$$

Rearranging:

$$v(z+\Delta z, t) - v(z,t) = -\left[R \, i(z,t) + L \frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}\right]\Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit $\Delta z \to 0$.**
The left-hand side becomes the partial derivative of $v$ with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial z} = -R \, i(z,t) - L \frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

This is the **first telegrapher's equation**.

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the upper node.**
The current entering node $z+\Delta z$ is $i(z,t)$; current leaving through the shunt admittance is $(G\Delta z) v(z+\Delta z,t) + (C\Delta z) \dfrac{\partial v(z+\Delta z, t)}{\partial t}$.

$$i(z,t) = i(z+\Delta z, t) + G\Delta z \, v(z+\Delta z,t) + C\Delta z \, \frac{\partial v(z+\Delta z, t)}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange and take the limit.**

$$i(z+\Delta z, t) - i(z,t) = -\left[G \, v(z+\Delta z, t) + C \frac{\partial v(z+\Delta z, t)}{\partial t}\right]\Delta z$$

As $\Delta z \to 0$:

$$\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial z} = -G \, v(z,t) - C \frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

This is the **second telegrapher's equation**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: From Maxwell's Equations to Telegrapher's Equations

**Step 1 — Set up Maxwell's curl equations for the 1-D case.**
For a TEM mode propagating in the $+z$ direction, the fields are transverse ($\mathbf{E}$ in $\hat{x}$, $\mathbf{H}$ in $\hat{y}$). Faraday's law in complex phasor form:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega \mu \mathbf{H}$$

In 1-D, this reduces to:

$$-\frac{\partial E_x}{\partial z} = j\omega \mu H_y$$

**Step 2 — Relate $E$ and $H$ to $V$ and $I$ via geometry.**
For a parallel-plate or two-wire line with plate separation $d$ and plate width $w$:

$$V = E_x \cdot d \quad \text{and} \quad I = H_y \cdot w$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$-\frac{1}{d}\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \cdot d = j\omega \mu \frac{I}{w} \cdot w \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = j\omega \mu \frac{I}{w} d = j\omega L' I$$

where $L' = \mu d / w$ is the **distributed inductance per unit length** (this identifies $L$ from EM principles).

**Step 4 — Apply Ampère's law similarly.**

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = (\sigma + j\omega \epsilon) \mathbf{E}$$

For a lossy dielectric with conductivity $\sigma$:

$$\frac{\partial H_y}{\partial z} = (\sigma + j\omega \epsilon) E_x$$

Using the same $V, I$ relations and noting $G' = \sigma w/d$, $C' = \epsilon w/d$:

$$-\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = (G' + j\omega C') V$$

This recovers the **two telegrapher's equations** with full physical identification of $L$, $C$, $G$ in terms of $\mu$, $\epsilon$, $\sigma$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: General Solution (Wave Solution)

Starting from the phasor form:

$$-\frac{dV}{dz} = Z' I \quad ; \quad -\frac{dI}{dz} = Y' V$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the first equation w.r.t. $z$.**

$$-\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = Z' \frac{dI}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\dfrac{dI}{dz} = -Y' V$.**

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = Z' Y' V = \gamma^2 V$$

where $\gamma^2 = Z'Y'$. This is the **1-D Helmholtz / wave equation**.

**Step 3 — Write the general solution.**
The general solution to a second-order linear ODE with constant coefficients is a sum of two exponentials:

$$V(z) = A e^{-\gamma z} + B e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 4 — Obtain $I(z)$ from $I = -\dfrac{1}{Z'}\dfrac{dV}{dz}$.**

$$I(z) = -\frac{1}{Z'} \frac{dV}{dz} = -\frac{1}{Z'}(-A\gamma e^{-\gamma z} + B\gamma e^{+\gamma z})$$

$$I(z) = \frac{A\gamma}{Z'} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{B\gamma}{Z'} e^{+\gamma z} = \frac{A}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{B}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma z}$$

where we defined $Z_0 = Z'/\gamma = \sqrt{Z'/Y'}$. The minus sign on the second term physically represents a wave propagating in the $-z$ direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sign Convention:** A wave travelling in the $+z$ direction produces a current in the $+z$ direction (a positive $A/Z_0$). A wave travelling in the $-z$ direction produces a current in the $-z$ direction (a *negative* $B/Z_0$ coefficient). This sign convention is what defines the reflection coefficient at a load.

### 3.4 Special Case: Lossless Line

If $R = 0$ and $G = 0$:

$$Z' = j\omega L, \quad Y' = j\omega C$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{(j\omega L)(j\omega C)} = j\omega\sqrt{LC} = j\beta$$

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$$

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad (\text{purely real!})$$

The voltage and current are purely propagating waves with no attenuation:

$$V(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Infinitesimal Equivalent Circuit of a Transmission Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Z = z"] -->|"i(z,t) enters"| node2["Series Branch: R*dz + L*dz"]
    node2 --> node3["Shunt Node: z + dz"]
    node3 -->|"G*dz parallel C*dz"| node4["Return Conductor"]
    node3 --> node5["Z = z + dz"]
    node5 -->|"v(z+dz,t) and i(z+dz,t) leave"| node6["Next Section dz"]

    style node1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node2 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node4 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node5 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node6 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Flow of Derivation from Maxwell to Telegrapher

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Maxwell Curl Equations"] --> stepB["Assume TEM mode in z direction"]
    stepB --> stepC["Apply Faraday Law and Ampere Law"]
    stepC --> stepD["Convert E, H to V, I via geometry"]
    stepD --> stepE["Identify L, C, G from mu, epsilon, sigma"]
    stepE --> stepF["Obtain Phasor Telegrapher Equations"]
    startG["Circuit Theory of Element dz"] --> stepH["Apply KVL and KCL"]
    stepH --> stepI["Take limit dz to zero"]
    stepI --> stepF
    stepF --> stepJ["Differentiate to get Helmholtz Equation"]
    stepJ --> stepK["General Solution: V = V+ e to the minus gamma z + V- e to the plus gamma z"]
    stepK --> stepL["Identify Z0 = sqrt Z prime over Y prime"]
    stepK --> stepM["Identify gamma = alpha + j beta"]

    style startA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style startG fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    style stepK fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style stepL fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepM fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Physical Interpretation of Forward and Reflected Waves

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Source at z = 0"] -->|"V plus travels in +z direction"| fw["Forward Wave"]
    fw -->|"attenuation alpha, phase shift beta"| load["Load ZL at z = L"]
    load -->|"V minus travels in -z direction"| rw["Reflected Wave"]
    rw -->|"Reflection Coefficient Gamma = ZL minus Z0 over ZL plus Z0"| src
    fw -.->|"carries incident power"| obs1["Matched Load: Gamma = 0"]
    rw -.->|"standing waves if not matched"| obs2["Open or Short Circuit: Gamma = ±1"]

    style src fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style fw fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
Define the four primary distributed parameters of a transmission line and state their SI units.

**Model Answer:**
The four primary (line) constants of a transmission line are:
1. **$R$** — Series resistance per unit length, in **Ω/m**.
2. **$L$** — Series inductance per unit length, in **H/m**.
3. **$G$** — Shunt conductance per unit length, in **S/m**.
4. **$C$** — Shunt capacitance per unit length, in **F/m**.
These parameters model the resistive, magnetic, leakage, and electric field effects distributed continuously along the conductors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
State the two telegrapher's equations in their phasor form. What is the physical meaning of the minus sign on the left-hand side?

**Model Answer:**
The phasor telegrapher's equations are:

$$-\frac{dV(z)}{dz} = (R + j\omega L) \, I(z) = Z' I(z)$$

$$-\frac{dI(z)}{dz} = (G + j\omega C) \, V(z) = Y' V(z)$$

The **minus sign** indicates that the voltage (or current) *decreases* in the direction of propagation due to the voltage drop across the series impedance $Z'$ (or current loss through the shunt admittance $Y'$).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 4 Instruction (KTU 2024):** Answer any **one** full question from each module. All sub-parts carry equal marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Transmission Line Equations & General Solution**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Set B]*

**(a)** Starting from the equivalent circuit of an infinitesimal section $\Delta z$ of a two-wire transmission line, derive the **telegrapher's equations** in the time domain and the **phasor domain**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the phasor telegrapher's equations, derive the **one-dimensional wave equation** for $V(z)$ and hence obtain the **general solution** for voltage and current. Clearly identify the **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ and **propagation constant** $\gamma$ in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Time-domain derivation:**
Consider an elemental length $\Delta z$ with series impedance $(R + j\omega L)\Delta z$ and shunt admittance $(G + j\omega C)\Delta z$.

Applying **KVL** to the loop:
$v(z,t) - v(z+\Delta z, t) = R\Delta z \, i(z,t) + L\Delta z \, \dfrac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$

Dividing by $\Delta z$ and taking $\Delta z \to 0$:

$$-\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial z} = R \, i(z,t) + L \frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

Applying **KCL** at the upper node:
$i(z,t) - i(z+\Delta z, t) = G\Delta z \, v(z+\Delta z, t) + C\Delta z \, \dfrac{\partial v(z+\Delta z, t)}{\partial t}$

Taking $\Delta z \to 0$:

$$-\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial z} = G \, v(z,t) + C \frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

Assuming sinusoidal steady-state ($v = \text{Re}\{V e^{j\omega t}\}$, $i = \text{Re}\{I e^{j\omega t}\}$), we get the **phasor form**:

$$-\frac{dV}{dz} = (R + j\omega L) I, \qquad -\frac{dI}{dz} = (G + j\omega C) V$$

**(b) Wave equation derivation:**
Differentiating the first phasor equation w.r.t. $z$:

$$-\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = (R + j\omega L)\frac{dI}{dz} = (R + j\omega L)[-(G + j\omega C) V] = -Z'Y' V$$

Thus:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} - \gamma^2 V = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \sqrt{Z'Y'} = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$$

The general solution is:

$$V(z) = V_0^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

Using $I = -\dfrac{1}{Z'}\dfrac{dV}{dz}$:

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma z}, \quad \text{where} \quad Z_0 = \frac{Z'}{\gamma} = \sqrt{\frac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$$

The first term $V_0^+ e^{-\gamma z}/Z_0$ is the **forward-travelling wave**; the second term with the minus sign is the **reflected wave** travelling in $-z$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Lossless Line Specialisation**
*[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set C]*

**(a)** Derive the telegrapher's equations from **Maxwell's equations** for a two-conductor transmission line carrying a **TEM wave**. Identify how the parameters $L$ and $C$ relate to $\mu$ and $\epsilon$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **lossless** transmission line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$), obtain the simplified expressions for the propagation constant $\gamma$, the characteristic impedance $Z_0$, the phase velocity $v_p$, and the wavelength $\lambda$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) From Maxwell's equations:**
For a TEM mode in a two-conductor line, the fields are transverse to the direction of propagation $z$: $\mathbf{E} = E_x \hat{x}$, $\mathbf{H} = H_y \hat{y}$.

**Faraday's Law** ($\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -j\omega\mu \mathbf{H}$) in 1-D:
$-\dfrac{\partial E_x}{\partial z} = j\omega\mu H_y$

Relating fields to voltages and currents via geometry ($V = E_x d$, $I = H_y w$, where $d$ = separation, $w$ = width):

$$-\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = j\omega\left(\frac{\mu d}{w}\right) I = j\omega L' I$$

where $L' = \mu d/w$ is the inductance per unit length.

**Ampere's Law** ($\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = j\omega\epsilon \mathbf{E}$):
$\dfrac{\partial H_y}{\partial z} = j\omega\epsilon E_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial z} = j\omega\epsilon \dfrac{w}{d} V = j\omega C' V$

where $C' = \epsilon w/d$ is the capacitance per unit length. Combining with finite conductivity $\sigma$ of dielectric gives the $G' = \sigma w/d$ term.

**(b) Lossless case:**
With $R = 0$, $G = 0$:
$$Z' = j\omega L', \quad Y' = j\omega C'$$
$$\gamma = \sqrt{Z'Y'} = \sqrt{(j\omega L')(j\omega C')} = j\omega\sqrt{L'C'} = j\beta$$
$$\alpha = 0, \quad \beta = \omega\sqrt{L'C'}$$
$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L'}{C'}} \quad (\text{purely real})$$
$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L'C'}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\epsilon}}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega\sqrt{L'C'}} = \frac{v_p}{f}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors** in deriving telegrapher's equations: KVL gives $v(z) - v(z+\Delta z) = \text{drop}$, so the spatial derivative has a *minus* sign. Reversing this changes the sign of $Z_0$ and $\gamma$.
> 2. **Confusing the propagation direction sign**: The reflected current component has a *minus* sign relative to the reflected voltage — forgetting this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ incorrectly** for lossless lines: Students often write $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ without setting $\alpha = 0$ explicitly. Always state $\alpha = 0$ for $R = G = 0$.
> 4. **Forgetting units** of $L$, $C$, $R$, $G$ per unit length — KTU expects H/m, F/m, Ω/m, S/m in definitions.
> 5. **Mixing up $\gamma$ vs $Z_0$ formulas**: $\gamma = \sqrt{Z'Y'}$ but $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z'/Y'}$. This is a classic 1-mark trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for KTU ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ A transmission line is described by **four distributed parameters**: $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ per unit length.
- ✅ The **telegrapher's equations** are the *fundamental* governing equations of any transmission line.
- ✅ Two forms exist: **Time domain** (PDEs in $t$ and $z$) and **Phasor domain** (ODEs in $z$ with $j\omega$).
- ✅ The phasor equations lead to the **1-D Helmholtz (wave) equation** with general solution $V(z) = V_0^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^- e^{+\gamma z}$.
- ✅ **$\gamma = \sqrt{Z'Y'} = \alpha + j\beta$** controls attenuation and phase shift.
- ✅ **$Z_0 = \sqrt{Z'/Y'}$** is the ratio of forward voltage to forward current; it is the natural impedance of the line.
- ✅ The reflected current term has a **minus sign** to reflect its opposite direction of travel.
- ✅ For a **lossless line**: $R = G = 0 \Rightarrow \gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (real), $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
- ✅ The line must be analysed using these equations whenever **length $\geq \lambda/10$** (or $f \geq 30$ MHz as a rule of thumb).
- ✅ Derivation from **Maxwell's equations** identifies $L' = \mu d/w$ and $C' = \epsilon w/d$ — these connect circuit theory to field theory.
- ✅ **Reflection coefficient** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ — set $Z_L = Z_0$ to eliminate reflections (impedance matching).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
