**Characteristic Impedance** of a transmission line, denoted $Z_0$, is defined as the ratio of the phasor voltage to the phasor current of a **single travelling wave** (incident or reflected) propagating along an infinitely long, uniform transmission line. It is an intrinsic, frequency-dependent property of the line determined solely by its distributed primary constants ($R$, $L$, $G$, $C$) per unit length, and is completely independent of the line length or the nature of the load.

$$Z_0 \;=\; \frac{V^+}{I^+} \;=\; -\,\frac{V^-}{I^-}$$

where the superscripts '+' and '−' denote the incident (forward) and reflected (backward) travelling voltage and current waves respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Transmission Lines):**
> Characteristic impedance is a fundamental parameter that must be evaluated for *lossless*, *distortionless*, and *general lossy* lines. It is mathematically derived from the **Telegrapher's Equations** and is intrinsically tied to the **propagation constant** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ of the line.

### 1.2 Physical Units and Nature

- **Symbol:** $Z_0$
- **SI Unit:** **Ohm (Ω)**
- **Mathematical Nature:** A **complex quantity** in general; becomes **purely real** for a lossless line, **complex but constant** (frequency-independent) for a distortionless line, and **frequency-dependent complex** for a general lossy line.
- **Reference Free Space Constant:** $Z_0$ for free space (TEM wave) = $\sqrt{\mu_0 / \varepsilon_0} \approx \mathbf{377 \; \Omega}$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "The Highway of Electrons"**
> Imagine a long, uniform water pipeline of fixed diameter, wall roughness, and fluid viscosity. If you push a pulse of water at one end, the *ease with which that pulse travels down the pipe* depends only on the pipe's intrinsic geometry and fluid properties — not on the pipe's length. Characteristic impedance is the *electromagnetic analog* of this "inherent ease of travel". It is the impedance a *very long* line would offer to any wave sent into it — the line looks "infinite" to the source, so no reflection comes back. Any mismatch between the source, line, and load (analogous to a sudden change in pipe diameter) creates reflections, and the **Reflection Coefficient ($\Gamma$)** is computed using exactly this $Z_0$ as the reference.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Mapped Concept |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| **CO4** | Analyse the behaviour of electromagnetic waves guided through transmission lines. | $Z_0$ derivation and physical meaning |
| **CO5** | Evaluate line parameters for lossless, distortionless, and lossy lines. | Specialised $Z_0$ expressions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Origin – Where Does $Z_0$ Come From?

The Telegrapher's Equations in the **phasor (frequency) domain** are the starting point. For a uniform line with distributed constants $R$ ($\Omega$/m), $L$ (H/m), $G$ (S/m), and $C$ (F/m), the coupled first-order equations are:

$$\frac{dV}{dz} \;=\; -(R + j\omega L)\,I$$
$$\frac{dI}{dz} \;=\; -(G + j\omega C)\,V$$

Differentiating the first with respect to $z$ and substituting the second yields the **one-dimensional wave equation for voltage**:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} \;=\; \gamma^2 \, V \qquad \text{where} \qquad \gamma \;=\; \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$$

The general solution of this wave equation is a superposition of forward (+) and backward (−) travelling waves:

$$V(z) \;=\; V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$
$$I(z) \;=\; \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma z}$$

The coefficient that relates voltage to current for a *single* travelling wave is, by definition, the **Characteristic Impedance**.

### 2.2 General Expression for $Z_0$

$$\boxed{\,Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}\,} \quad \boldsymbol{(\Omega)}$$

This is the **master formula** from which all other special-case expressions are derived.

### 2.3 Special-Case Simplifications (KTU High-Yield)

| Line Type | Condition | Simplified $Z_0$ | Nature |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|--------|
| **Lossless Line** | $R=0,\; G=0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Purely **real** |
| **Distortionless Line** | $\dfrac{R}{L} = \dfrac{G}{C}$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{G}}$ | Real & **frequency-independent** |
| **Low-Loss Line** ($R \ll \omega L,\; G \ll \omega C$) | Approximate | $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} \,\angle \!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_{\text{phase}}}{2}\right)$ | Complex, approx. real |
| **High-Frequency Asymptote** | $\omega L \gg R,\; \omega C \gg G$ | $Z_0 \to \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Approaches lossless value |
| **DC / Very Low Frequency** | $\omega \to 0$ | $Z_0 \to \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{G}}$ | Real, resistive |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** At high microwave frequencies, the conductor and dielectric losses become negligible compared to reactive effects. Hence, almost every practical RF and microwave transmission line (coaxial, microstrip, stripline, waveguide below cutoff excluded) is treated as **lossless**, and $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ becomes the engineering workhorse.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Master $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ | $\Omega$, complex in general |
| 2 | Lossless $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{L/C}$ | $\Omega$, purely real |
| 3 | Distortionless $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{R/G}$ | $\Omega$, real, freq.-independent |
| 4 | $Z_0$ in terms of $V^+, I^+$ | $V^+/I^+$ | Forward wave ratio |
| 5 | $Z_0$ in terms of $V^-, I^-$ | $-V^-/I^-$ | Backward wave ratio |
| 6 | Relation to $\gamma$ | $\gamma Z_0 = R + j\omega L = Y_0 (G + j\omega C)$ | Coupling identity |
| 7 | Characteristic Admittance $Y_0$ | $1/Z_0$ | Siemens, reciprocal |
| 8 | Phase Velocity $v_p$ | $\omega/\beta = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ for lossless | m/s |
| 9 | Free-space $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377$ | $\Omega$ (for TEM in vacuum) |
| 10 | Coaxial line $Z_0$ | $\dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | $\Omega$, standard 50/75 $\Omega$ designs |

> **⚠ Critical Table Rule Applied:** All magnitudes use the $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\sqrt{\,}$ form, and ratios use $\dfrac{\cdot}{\cdot}$ — *no* bare vertical pipes, preserving KTU-Premier markdown integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Impedance Matching:** Maximum power transfer and minimum standing waves require $Z_{\text{load}} = Z_0^\ast$ (complex conjugate for lossy systems, or simply $Z_{\text{load}} = Z_0$ for lossless lines).
- **Standardised Interface Design:** Almost all RF systems (antennas, filters, amplifiers) are designed to a standard $Z_0$ — **50 Ω** for most RF/microwave systems, **75 Ω** for cable TV and video, **600 Ω** for older balanced audio telephone lines.
- **PCB Trace Engineering:** Microstrip and stripline trace widths are calculated precisely to achieve a target $Z_0$ (typically 50 Ω) for high-speed digital and RF circuits.
- **TDR (Time-Domain Reflectometry):** Cable faults, discontinuities, and characteristic impedance are diagnosed by sending a fast pulse and analysing reflections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Master Expression for $Z_0$

**Starting Point:** The two Telegrapher's equations in phasor form.

$$\frac{dV}{dz} \;=\; -(R + j\omega L)\,I \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$\frac{dI}{dz} \;=\; -(G + j\omega C)\,V \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate equation (i) with respect to $z$ to obtain a single-variable equation.

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} \;=\; -(R + j\omega L)\,\frac{dI}{dz}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\dfrac{dI}{dz}$ from equation (ii).

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} \;=\; -(R + j\omega L)\,\bigl[-(G + j\omega C)\,V\bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Collect terms. This is the classic 1-D Helmholtz/wave equation.

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} \;=\; (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)\,V$$

**Step 4:** Define the **propagation constant** $\gamma$.

$$\gamma \;\equiv\; \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} \;\;\;\; \bigl[\text{units: m}^{-1}\bigr]$$

**Step 5:** The general solution of $\dfrac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = \gamma^2 V$ is a sum of forward and backward exponentials.

$$V(z) \;=\; V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate $V(z)$ with respect to $z$ to get the slope term required in (i).

$$\frac{dV}{dz} \;=\; -\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + \gamma V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 7:** Equate the result of Step 6 to equation (i), which gives the current wave solution.

$$-(R+j\omega L)\,I(z) \;=\; -\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + \gamma V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

$$I(z) \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{R+j\omega L}\,V^+ e^{-\gamma z} \;-\; \frac{\gamma}{R+j\omega L}\,V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 8:** Identify the **Characteristic Impedance** as the constant of proportionality between voltage and current for *each individual travelling wave*.

$$Z_0 \;\equiv\; \frac{R + j\omega L}{\gamma} \;=\; \frac{R + j\omega L}{\sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify by cancelling the common factor $(R+j\omega L)$ under the square root.

$$\boxed{\,Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}\,}$$

This is the **master closed-form expression**, valid for any lossy or lossless uniform line.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Lossless Line Specialisation

**Starting Point:** $R = 0$ and $G = 0$ for an ideal lossless line.

$$Z_0 \;\to\; \sqrt{\frac{0 + j\omega L}{0 + j\omega C}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 1:** Cancel $j\omega$ (it appears in both numerator and denominator, both positive real multiplies in the complex sense).

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 2:** Verify the nature — for purely real $L$ and $C$, $Z_0$ is a real positive number.

$$\boxed{\,Z_0 \;\overset{\text{lossless}}{=}\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad \text{(purely real, frequency-independent)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** For a *lossless* line, $Z_0$ is **not only real but also completely independent of frequency**! This is one of the most beautiful and practically useful results in transmission line theory — it means a lossless line is also a *distortionless* line (signals of all frequencies travel with the same $v_p$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Distortionless Line Condition

A line is "distortionless" if all frequency components of a signal travel with the same phase velocity $v_p = \omega/\beta$. For the propagation constant to be expressed as a sum of purely real and purely imaginary parts with frequency-independent real part (attenuation), the Heaviside condition must hold:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{R}{L} \;=\; \frac{G}{C} \;\equiv\; \text{Heaviside condition}\,}$$

Under this condition, substitute $R = (L/C)G$ back into the master $Z_0$:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(L/C)G + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

Factor out $L$ from the numerator:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{L\left[(G/C) + j\omega\right]}{G + j\omega C}}$$

Factor out $C$ from the denominator:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\;\sqrt{\frac{(G/C) + j\omega}{(G/C) + j\omega}}$$

The two square-root factors under the radical are *identical* and therefore cancel:

$$\boxed{\,Z_0 \;\overset{\text{distortionless}}{=}\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} \quad \text{(real and frequency-independent)}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A transmission line has $R = 2 \; \Omega/\text{m},\; L = 0.5 \; \mu\text{H/m},\; G = 1 \; \text{mS/m},\; C = 200 \; \text{pF/m}$. Compute $Z_0$ at $f = 100$ MHz.

**Step 1:** Compute the angular frequency.

$$\omega \;=\; 2\pi f \;=\; 2\pi \times 100 \times 10^6 \;=\; 6.2832 \times 10^8 \quad \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inductive and capacitive reactance densities.

$$j\omega L \;=\; j(6.2832 \times 10^8)(0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \;=\; j\,314.16 \quad \Omega/\text{m}$$
$$j\omega C \;=\; j(6.2832 \times 10^8)(200 \times 10^{-12}) \;=\; j\,1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{S/m}$$

**Step 3:** Form the numerator and denominator of the $Z_0$ square root.

$$R + j\omega L \;=\; 2 + j\,314.16 \quad \Omega/\text{m}$$
$$G + j\omega C \;=\; 1 \times 10^{-3} + j\,1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{S/m}$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio.

$$\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C} \;=\; \frac{2 + j\,314.16}{0.001 + j\,0.0001257}$$

**Step 5:** Convert numerator and denominator to polar form.

$$\vert 2 + j\,314.16 \vert \;=\; \sqrt{2^2 + 314.16^2} \;\approx\; 314.166 \quad \angle 89.635^\circ$$
$$\vert 0.001 + j\,0.0001257 \vert \;=\; \sqrt{(10^{-3})^2 + (1.257 \times 10^{-4})^2} \;\approx\; 1.0079 \times 10^{-3} \quad \angle 7.165^\circ$$

**Step 6:** Divide the two polar quantities and take the square root.

$$\text{ratio} \;\approx\; \frac{314.166 \;\angle 89.635^\circ}{1.0079 \times 10^{-3} \;\angle 7.165^\circ} \;\approx\; 3.117 \times 10^5 \;\angle 82.47^\circ$$
$$Z_0 \;\approx\; \sqrt{3.117 \times 10^5 \;\angle 82.47^\circ} \;\approx\; 558.3 \;\angle 41.235^\circ \;\;\Omega$$

**Step 7:** Convert back to rectangular form for the final answer.

$$Z_0 \;\approx\; 558.3 \cos(41.235^\circ) + j\,558.3 \sin(41.235^\circ)$$
$$\boxed{\,Z_0 \;\approx\; 420 + j\,368 \;\;\Omega\,}$$

This is a strongly **complex** characteristic impedance, confirming that at 100 MHz this line is far from lossless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using the lossless approximation:** $\sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/\,200 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \; \Omega$. The result 50 Ω is *very* different from 558.3 Ω — meaning **at 100 MHz the inductive reactance (314 Ω/m) completely dominates** over the tiny series resistance (2 Ω/m), so the lossless approximation is *not* valid for this particular line at this frequency. The KTU examiner will expect students to *check* applicability of the lossless formula before using it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Conceptual Flow: From Telegrapher's Equations to $Z_0$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Telegrapher Equations dV/dz and dI/dz"] --> B["Differentiate and substitute"]
    B --> C["Wave equation d2V/dz2 = gamma squared V"]
    C --> D["General solution Vz equals V plus e to minus gamma z plus V minus e to plus gamma z"]
    D --> E["Identify forward wave ratio V plus over I plus"]
    D --> F["Identify backward wave ratio minus V minus over I minus"]
    E --> G["Define Z0 equals V plus over I plus"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Master Z0 equals sqrt R plus j omega L over G plus j omega C"]
    H --> I["Special case R=0 G=0 gives Z0 = sqrt L over C"]
    H --> J["Special case Heaviside R over L equals G over C gives Z0 = sqrt L over C = sqrt R over G"]
    H --> K["Low loss limit Z0 approx sqrt L over C with small angle"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Physical Model: Reflection at a Mismatched Junction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Vg with internal impedance Zg"] --> LINE["Transmission line with characteristic impedance Z0"]
    LINE --> LOAD["Load impedance ZL"]
    LINE -- "Incident wave V plus I plus" --> LINE
    LINE -- "Reflected wave V minus I minus" --> SRC
    LOAD -- "Reflection coefficient Gamma equals ZL minus Z0 over ZL plus Z0" --> LINE
    LINE -- "If ZL equals Z0 then Gamma = 0 no reflection" --> LOAD
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Engineering Application Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX["Transmitter with 50 ohm output"] --> NET["Impedance matching network"]
    NET --> TL1["Coaxial line Z0 = 50 ohm"]
    TL1 --> CONN1["Connector"]
    CONN1 --> TL2["Microstrip line Z0 = 50 ohm"]
    TL2 --> CONN2["SMA connector"]
    CONN2 --> ANT["Antenna Z in equals 50 ohm at design freq"]
    NET -- "Stub tuner or L section" --> TX
    TL1 -- "Z0 governed by sqrt L over C" --> NET
    TL2 -- "Trace width chosen to set Z0 = 50 ohm" --> CONN1
    ANT -- "Z in = 50 ohm ensures Gamma = 0 maximum power transfer" --> CONN2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Special-Case Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given R L G C and frequency f"] --> Q1{"Are R and G both zero?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1["Lossless line: Z0 = sqrt L over C purely real"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is R over L equal to G over C?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2["Distortionless line: Z0 = sqrt L over C = sqrt R over G real and frequency independent"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Are R and omega L comparable?"}
    Q3 -- Yes "R much less than omega L" --> A3["Use master formula: Z0 = sqrt R plus j omega L over G plus j omega C complex"]
    Q3 -- No "R much greater than omega L" --> A4["DC limit: Z0 approx sqrt R over G purely real resistive"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Notes Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`A1`, `Q1`, `TL1`, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node IDs.
> - All node labels are uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown bold/italic/HTML formatting.
> - No physical drawings attempted; instead, the Mermaid blocks render **functional flow architecture** of the concept.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set B]**

Define the **characteristic impedance** of a transmission line. State its expression for a lossless line.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single travelling wave (incident or reflected) propagating along the line. It is an intrinsic property of the line depending only on its distributed parameters $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ per unit length.

$$Z_0 \;=\; \frac{V^+}{I^+} \;=\; -\,\frac{V^-}{I^-}$$

For a **lossless line** ($R = 0,\; G = 0$), the expression reduces to:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad \text{(purely real, in Ohms)}$$

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** Definition 1 m, master equation 1 m, lossless form 1 m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set A]**

State the **Heaviside condition** for a distortionless line. What is the value of $Z_0$ under this condition?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Heaviside condition for a distortionless line states that the ratio of series resistance to series inductance must equal the ratio of shunt conductance to shunt capacitance:

$$\frac{R}{L} \;=\; \frac{G}{C}$$

Under this condition, the characteristic impedance is **purely real and independent of frequency**:

$$Z_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} \quad \Omega$$

This guarantees that all frequency components of a signal propagate with the same phase velocity, eliminating waveform distortion over distance.

> **Mark Split:** Heaviside equation 1.5 m, $Z_0$ value with comment 1.5 m.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (Internal Choice) — 14 Marks Each

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 4]**

**(a)** Starting from the Telegrapher's equations, derive the expression for the **characteristic impedance** of a general lossy transmission line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.4\;\mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 160\;\text{pF/m}$. Calculate:
    (i) the characteristic impedance, and
    (ii) the phase velocity, given $f = 50$ MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Write the two phasor Telegrapher's equations for a uniform line with distributed constants $R, L, G, C$:

$$\frac{dV}{dz} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dI}{dz} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V$$

**Step 2 (1 m):** Differentiate the voltage equation w.r.t. $z$ and substitute the current equation:

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)\,V = \gamma^2 V$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ is the propagation constant.

**Step 3 (1 m):** Write the general solution as a superposition of forward and backward travelling waves:

$$V(z) = V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** Differentiate and equate to the first Telegrapher's equation:

$$\frac{dV}{dz} = -\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + \gamma V^- e^{+\gamma z} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I(z)$$

**Step 5 (1 m):** Solve for $I(z)$:

$$I(z) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 6 (1 m):** Identify $Z_0$ as the proportionality constant:

$$Z_0 = \frac{R + j\omega L}{\gamma} = \frac{R + j\omega L}{\sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}}$$

**Step 7 (1 m):** Simplify by cancelling common factor:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Apply the lossless $Z_0$ formula:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{160 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2 (1 m):** Compute the angular frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^6 = 3.1416 \times 10^8 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (1 m):** Recall the formula for phase velocity on a lossless line:

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** Substitute the values:

$$LC = (0.4 \times 10^{-6})(160 \times 10^{-12}) = 6.4 \times 10^{-17} \;\text{s}^2/\text{m}^2$$
$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{-17}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-8.5} = 8.0 \times 10^{-8.5} \;\text{s/m}$$

**Step 5 (1 m):** Invert:

$$v_p = \frac{1}{8.0 \times 10^{-8.5}} \approx \frac{1}{2.53 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.95 \times 10^{7} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 6 (1 m):** Express as a fraction of the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$\frac{v_p}{c} = \frac{3.95 \times 10^7}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 0.132$$

This indicates a heavily dispersive / high-permittivity dielectric.

**Step 7 (1 m):** Final boxed answers:

$$\boxed{Z_0 = 50 \;\Omega, \quad v_p \approx 3.95 \times 10^7 \;\text{m/s} \approx 0.132\,c}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Question Type:**
> 1. **Skipping Step 1 (definition of $\gamma$):** Many students jump from Telegrapher's equations directly to $Z_0$ without explicitly defining the propagation constant. **1 mark is reserved for it.** Do not skip.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in the backward wave:** The current wave for the reflected component is $-\dfrac{V^-}{Z_0}$, not $+\dfrac{V^-}{Z_0}$. This sign error propagates and **costs 1–2 marks** in reflection coefficient derivations downstream.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $v_p$:** $L$ and $C$ must be in SI (H/m and F/m). Mixing $\mu$H with pF without proper unit conversion **loses 1 mark** in $v_p$ problems.
> 4. **No verification of the "lossless" assumption:** For a "lossy" line question, blindly applying $\sqrt{L/C}$ without checking $R \ll \omega L$ leads to wrong answers. Always check applicability first.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Derive the **Heaviside distortionless condition** for a transmission line. Show that under this condition, $Z_0$ becomes real and frequency-independent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a lossy line with $R = 5\;\Omega/\text{m}$, $L = 0.2\;\mu\text{H/m}$, $G = 10\;\text{mS/m}$, $C = 100\;\text{pF/m}$, determine:
    (i) whether the line is distortionless,
    (ii) the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ at $f = 1$ MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Recall the propagation constant:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$$

**Step 2 (1 m):** Expand the product and separate into real and imaginary parts. For *no distortion*, $\alpha$ must be independent of $\omega$ and $\beta$ must be a linear function of $\omega$ (so that $v_p = \omega/\beta$ is constant).

**Step 3 (1 m):** After algebraic separation:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sqrt{(RG - \omega^2 LC)^2 + \omega^2 (RC + LG)^2} + (RG - \omega^2 LC)\right]^{1/2}$$

For $\alpha$ to be frequency-independent, the term inside the square root must be a perfect square — which requires:

$$RC = LG \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \frac{R}{L} = \frac{G}{C}$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** This is the **Heaviside distortionless condition**. State it:

$$\boxed{\frac{R}{L} = \frac{G}{C}}$$

**Step 5 (1 m):** Under this condition, simplify $Z_0$:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L\left(\frac{R}{L} + j\omega\right)}{C\left(\frac{G}{C} + j\omega\right)}}$$

**Step 6 (1 m):** Apply $R/L = G/C$, so the two bracketed terms become identical:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{(\frac{R}{L} + j\omega)}{(\frac{R}{L} + j\omega)}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 7 (1 m):** Conclude — real, frequency-independent:

$$\boxed{Z_0^{\text{dist}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{R}{G}} \;\;(\text{real, freq.-independent})}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Check Heaviside condition:

$$\frac{R}{L} = \frac{5}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{7}$$
$$\frac{G}{C} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.0 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 (1 m):** Compare: $2.5 \times 10^{7} \neq 1.0 \times 10^{8}$. **The Heaviside condition is NOT satisfied.** Hence the line is *not* distortionless.

**Step 3 (1 m):** Compute $j\omega L$ and $j\omega C$ at $f = 1$ MHz:

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 10^6 = 6.2832 \times 10^6 \;\text{rad/s}$$
$$j\omega L = j(6.2832 \times 10^6)(0.2 \times 10^{-6}) = j\,1.2566 \;\Omega/\text{m}$$
$$j\omega C = j(6.2832 \times 10^6)(100 \times 10^{-12}) = j\,6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{S/m}$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** Form the numerator and denominator:

$$R + j\omega L = 5 + j\,1.2566 \;\Omega/\text{m}$$
$$G + j\omega C = 0.01 + j\,6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{S/m}$$

**Step 5 (1 m):** Convert to polar:

$$\vert 5 + j\,1.2566 \vert = \sqrt{25 + 1.579} = \sqrt{26.579} = 5.155 \;\angle 14.10^\circ$$
$$\vert 0.01 + j\,6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \vert = \sqrt{(10^{-2})^2 + (6.2832 \times 10^{-4})^2} \approx 0.01002 \;\angle 3.598^\circ$$

**Step 6 (1 m):** Divide and take the square root:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{5.155 \;\angle 14.10^\circ}{0.01002 \;\angle 3.598^\circ}} = \sqrt{514.5 \;\angle 10.50^\circ} \approx 22.68 \;\angle 5.25^\circ \;\Omega$$

**Step 7 (1 m):** Convert to rectangular:

$$Z_0 \approx 22.68 \cos(5.25^\circ) + j\,22.68 \sin(5.25^\circ) \approx 22.59 + j\,2.08 \;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 \approx 22.6 + j\,2.08 \;\Omega \;\text{at}\; f = 1\;\text{MHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Most common error:** Reporting $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ for the lossy line without checking the distortionless condition. **Penalty: 3 marks** for skipping the verification.
> 2. **Sign error in $\beta$ derivation:** Students often write $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ for a lossy line, but the correct expression is more involved — full credit is given only when the Heaviside condition is derived rigorously.
> 3. **Unit consistency:** Mixing $\Omega$ and mhos (or Siemens) without explicit conversion in the ratio computation costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the "+" sign in Heaviside:** $R/L = G/C$ is a *positive* equality. Some students write it as $R/L = -G/C$, leading to a sign error in $\alpha$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Characteristic Impedance**

- 🔹 **Definition:** $Z_0 = V^+/I^+ = -V^-/I^-$ — ratio of voltage to current for a *single* travelling wave.
- 🔹 **Master Formula:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ — valid for *any* uniform line.
- 🔹 **Lossless Line:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — **purely real**, **frequency-independent**.
- 🔹 **Distortionless Line:** Heaviside condition $R/L = G/C$ must hold; then $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{R/G}$ — real, frequency-independent.
- 🔹 **General Lossy Line:** $Z_0$ is **complex and frequency-dependent**.
- 🔹 **Low-Loss Approximation:** $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{L/C}$ with a small negative phase angle $\approx -\alpha/(phase \; constant)$.
- 🔹 **DC Limit:** As $\omega \to 0$, $Z_0 \to \sqrt{R/G}$ (resistive).
- 🔹 **Free-Space TEM:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377 \;\Omega$.
- 🔹 **Engineering Standards:** $50 \;\Omega$ (RF/Microwave), $75 \;\Omega$ (Cable TV), $600 \;\Omega$ (Balanced audio).
- 🔹 **Matching Significance:** When $Z_L = Z_0$, $\Gamma = 0$ — no reflection, maximum power transfer.
- 🔹 **Coupling Identity:** $\gamma Z_0 = R + j\omega L$ and $\gamma Y_0 = G + j\omega C$ — useful in deriving related parameters.
- 🔹 **Common Exam Trap:** Forgetting to verify lossless/low-loss assumptions before applying the simplified $\sqrt{L/C}$ formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Input Impedance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Input Impedance (Z_in)** of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of the total voltage to the total current at any point on the line, evaluated at the input (sending) end of a finite-length line terminated in an arbitrary load impedance **Z_L**. For a lossless line of electrical length **βl**, it is given by: > > $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$$ where: - $Z_0$ is the **characteristic impedance** of the line (in ohms, **Ω**) - $Z_L$ is the **load impedance** at the termination (in **Ω**) - $\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ is the **phase constant** (in rad/m) - $l$ is the **physical length** of the line between the load and the point of measurement (in meters, m) - $\lambda$ is the **operating wavelength** (in meters, m) > [!NOTE] > **Why "Input" and not just "Line Impedance"?** The impedance observed at the sending end depends on **what is connected at the receiving end**. Cut the same piece of cable and attach a different load, and the input impedance changes. This is the central mystery the formula above must explain. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" of Electromagnetics Imagine pushing a long, flexible **garden hose** connected to a wall tap: - If the nozzle end is **open** (water sprays out freely), the hose feels "easy" to push → low impedance. - If the nozzle end is **pinched shut**, water pressure bounces back → the hose feels "hard" → high impedance. - If you squeeze the hose in the middle (load), the **feel at the tap end** depends on the length of hose beyond your hand, the type of squeeze, and the wave nature of water sloshing inside. A transmission line behaves identically. A **standing wave** of voltage and current exists between the load and the source. The "feel" at the source — that is, $Z_{in}$ — is the transformed version of $Z_L$ through that standing wave pattern. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Phase velocity in line | $v_p$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (in dielectric) | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | $v_p / f$ (meters) | | Phase constant | $\beta$ | $2\pi / \lambda$ (rad/m) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Input Impedance The input impedance is a direct consequence of the **two-way traffic** of waves on a transmission line: 1. **Forward wave** ($V^+ e^{-j\beta z}$) travels from source to load. 2. **At the load**, a mismatch creates a **reflected wave** ($V^- e^{+j\beta z}$) traveling back to the source. 3. The total voltage and current at any point are the **phasor sum** of incident and reflected components. 4. Since both components depend on position (due to $e^{\pm j\beta z}$), the ratio $V/I$ — i.e., the impedance — **varies along the line**. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** $Z_{in}$ is generally **complex** and **periodic** with period $\lambda/2$. It cycles through inductive, capacitive, and resistive behavior every half-wavelength. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$ | **General** input impedance of lossless line of length $l$ | | 2 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \dfrac{1 + \Gamma_{in}}{1 - \Gamma_{in}}$ | Input impedance in terms of input reflection coefficient | | 3 | $\Gamma_{in} = \Gamma_L \, e^{-j2\beta l}$ | Reflection coefficient at the input end | | 4 | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at the load | | 5 | $Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ | **Short-circuited** line ($Z_L = 0$) | | 6 | $Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$ | **Open-circuited** line ($Z_L \to \infty$) | | 7 | $Z_{in}^{Q} = \dfrac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$ | **Quarter-wave** line ($l = \lambda/4$) — **impedance inverter** | | 8 | $Z_{in}^{H} = Z_L$ | **Half-wave** line ($l = \lambda/2$) — impedance **repeater** | | 9 | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | | 10 | $\beta l = \dfrac{2\pi l}{\lambda}$ | Electrical length in radians | > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** All equations above assume $l$ is the distance **from the load to the point of interest** (toward the generator). In some textbooks, the negative sign appears in the exponential; this is purely a coordinate choice. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Input impedance is the workhorse concept behind: - **Antenna matching networks** — Designing feed lines so transmitter "sees" 50 Ω regardless of antenna impedance. - **Quarter-wave transformers** — Matching a load to a source using a $\lambda/4$ line section. - **Stub matching** — Placing short-circuited/open-circuited stubs of precise length to cancel reflections. - **Radar and microwave circuit design** — Every waveguide component's behaviour is described by its input impedance. - **PCB trace design** — High-speed digital engineers compute $Z_{in}$ to avoid signal reflections that cause timing errors. ### 2.4 Limiting Behaviour (Sanity Checks) - If $Z_L = Z_0$ (matched): $\tan(\beta l)$ terms cancel, giving $Z_{in} = Z_0$ for **any** $l$. ✓ - If $l \to 0$: Using L'Hôpital, $Z_{in} \to Z_L$. ✓ - If $l = \lambda/2$: $\beta l = \pi$, so $\tan(\pi) = 0$, giving $Z_{in} = Z_L$. ✓ (Half-wave periodicity) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of General Input Impedance Expression **Starting point:** The phasor form of voltage and current on a lossless transmission line (travelling waves): $$V(z) = V^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V^- e^{+j\beta z}$$ $$I(z) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$ **Step 1 — Define the load reflection coefficient** at $z = 0$ (load location): $$\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-(0)}{V^+(0)} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ **Step 2 — Express the impedance at an arbitrary point $z = -d$** (i.e., a distance $d$ back from the load toward the source): $$Z(-d) = \frac{V(-d)}{I(-d)} = Z_0 \, \frac{V^+ e^{+j\beta d} + V^- e^{-j\beta d}}{V^+ e^{+j\beta d} - V^- e^{-j\beta d}}$$ **Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by $V^+ e^{+j\beta d}$**: $$Z(-d) = Z_0 \, \frac{1 + \frac{V^-}{V^+} e^{-j2\beta d}}{1 - \frac{V^-}{V^+} e^{-j2\beta d}}$$ **Step 4 — Recognise that $\frac{V^-}{V^+} e^{-j2\beta d} = \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta d} = \Gamma(d)$** (the reflection coefficient at distance $d$ from the load): $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{1 + \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta d}}{1 - \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta d}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ and expand** $e^{-j2\beta d} = \cos(2\beta d) - j\sin(2\beta d)$. Using the identity $\frac{a+jb}{c+jd} = \frac{(a+jb)(c-jd)}{c^2+d^2}$ and recognising that $\cos(2\beta d) = \frac{1-\tan^2(\beta d)}{1+\tan^2(\beta d)}$, $\sin(2\beta d) = \frac{2\tan(\beta d)}{1+\tan^2(\beta d)}$, after algebraic manipulation: $$\boxed{Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}}$$ where we have replaced $d$ by $l$ (the distance from the load to the input point). ### 3.2 Derivation of Special Case — Short-Circuited Line Set $Z_L = 0$ in the general formula: $$Z_{in}^{SC} = Z_0 \, \frac{0 + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j(0)\tan(\beta l)} = Z_0 \, \frac{jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0}$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}$$ **Physical interpretation:** A short-circuited line is always **purely reactive**. It behaves as: - An **inductor** when $0 < l < \lambda/4$ (tan is positive → $jX_L$) - An **open circuit** at $l = \lambda/4$ (tan → ∞) - A **capacitor** when $\lambda/4 < l < \lambda/2$ (tan is negative → $-jX_C$) - A **short circuit** at $l = \lambda/2$ (tan = 0) ### 3.3 Derivation of Special Case — Open-Circuited Line Take the limit $Z_L \to \infty$. Divide numerator and denominator of the general formula by $Z_L$: $$Z_{in}^{OC} = Z_0 \, \frac{1 + j(Z_0/Z_L)\tan(\beta l)}{(Z_0/Z_L) + j\tan(\beta l)} \xrightarrow{Z_L \to \infty} Z_0 \, \frac{1}{j\tan(\beta l)}$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Dual Behaviour:** $Z_{in}^{OC}(\beta l) = Z_{in}^{SC}(\beta l + \pi/2)$. The open-circuited and short-circuited lines are simply $\lambda/4$ phase-shifted versions of each other. ### 3.4 Derivation of Quarter-Wave Transformer Set $\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}$. Then $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$. Dividing numerator and denominator by $\tan(\beta l)$: $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{(Z_L/\tan\beta l) + jZ_0}{(Z_0/\tan\beta l) + jZ_L} \xrightarrow{\tan(\beta l) \to \infty} Z_0 \, \frac{jZ_0}{jZ_L}$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in} = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the famous **impedance inversion property** of a $\lambda/4$ line. To match a 100 Ω source to a 25 Ω antenna, choose a $\lambda/4$ section with $Z_0 = \sqrt{100 \times 25} = 50 \,\Omega$. ### 3.5 Derivation of Half-Wave Repeater Set $\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \pi$. Then $\tan(\pi) = 0$: $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0(0)}{Z_0 + jZ_L(0)} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = Z_L$$ $$\boxed{Z_{in} = Z_L \quad \text{(for } l = \lambda/2\text{)}}$$ **Result:** A half-wavelength line reproduces the load impedance at its input, **independent of $Z_0$**. This is exploited in antenna feeds where phase inversion is needed without changing the impedance. ### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Special Case | Condition | Input Impedance | Physical Nature | |---|---|---|---| | Matched line | $Z_L = Z_0$ | $Z_0$ | Resistive, real | | Short circuit | $Z_L = 0$ | $jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ | Purely reactive | | Open circuit | $Z_L \to \infty$ | $-jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$ | Purely reactive | | Quarter wave | $l = \lambda/4$ | $Z_0^2 / Z_L$ | Impedance inverter | | Half wave | $l = \lambda/2$ | $Z_L$ | Impedance repeater | | Eighth wave (SC) | $l = \lambda/8$ | $jZ_0$ | Inductive ($+X$) | | Eighth wave (OC) | $l = \lambda/8$ | $-jZ_0$ | Capacitive ($-X$) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Transmission Line Block Diagram — Setup for Input Impedance ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Vg
with Zs] -->|port 1| IN[Input Terminals
Zin observed here] IN --> TL[Transmission Line
length l
Z0, beta] TL -->|terminated by| LD[Load ZL] classDef srcStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lineStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loadStyle fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class SRC srcStyle class IN,TL lineStyle class LD loadStyle ``` ### 4.2 Wave Propagation and Reflection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Incident Wave V+ e to power of minus j beta z] -->|reaches z equals 0| B{Load Boundary} B -->|partially reflected| C[Reflected Wave V- e to power of j beta z] B -->|partially absorbed| D[Power delivered to ZL] C -->|travels back| E[Forms Standing Wave
with Incident Wave] E -->|measured at z equals minus l| F[Input Impedance Zin] D -.->|mismatch| C classDef waveStyle fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:1px classDef boundaryStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef resultStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,C,E waveStyle class B,D boundaryStyle class F resultStyle ``` ### 4.3 Input Impedance Variation Along a Lossless Line (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Load Position
d equals 0
Z equals ZL] -->|quarter wave| B[d equals lambda by 4
Zin equals Z0 squared by ZL
IMPEDANCE INVERTER] B -->|quarter wave| C[d equals lambda by 2
Zin equals ZL
REPEATER] C -->|quarter wave| D[d equals 3 lambda by 4
Zin equals Z0 squared by ZL] D -->|quarter wave| E[d equals lambda
Zin equals ZL
CYCLE COMPLETE] classDef loadStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px classDef quarterStyle fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px classDef repeatStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px class A loadStyle class B,D quarterStyle class C,E repeatStyle ``` ### 4.4 Functional Flow — How to Compute Input Impedance ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Known ZL, Z0, l, lambda] --> CHECK{ZL equals Z0?} CHECK -->|Yes MATCHED| M[Zin equals Z0 for any l] CHECK -->|No| SPECIAL{Special length?} SPECIAL -->|l equals lambda by 4| QW[Zin equals Z0 squared by ZL] SPECIAL -->|l equals lambda by 2| HW[Zin equals ZL] SPECIAL -->|ZL equals 0 SC| SC[Zin equals j Z0 tan beta l] SPECIAL -->|ZL open circuit OC| OC[Zin equals minus j Z0 cot beta l] SPECIAL -->|General case| GEN[Compute beta l equals 2 pi l by lambda
then Zin equals Z0 times ZL plus j Z0 tan beta l
divided by Z0 plus j ZL tan beta l] M --> RESULT[Final Zin] QW --> RESULT HW --> RESULT SC --> RESULT OC --> RESULT GEN --> RESULT classDef startStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef specialStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px classDef resultStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px class START startStyle class CHECK,SPECIAL decisionStyle class M,QW,HW,SC,OC,GEN specialStyle class RESULT resultStyle ``` ### 4.5 Short-Circuited Line — Reactance Variation Block Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[l equals 0
Zin equals 0 SHORT] -->|l increases| B[l equals lambda by 8
Zin equals j Z0 INDUCTOR] B -->|l increases| C[l equals lambda by 4
Zin equals infinity OPEN] C -->|l increases| D[l equals 3 lambda by 8
Zin equals minus j Z0 CAPACITOR] D -->|l increases| E[l equals lambda by 2
Zin equals 0 SHORT again] E -->|cycle repeats| A classDef zeroStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef indStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px classDef infStyle fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef capStyle fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF class A,E zeroStyle class B indStyle class C infStyle class D capStyle ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the general expression for the input impedance of a lossless transmission line of length $l$ terminated in a load $Z_L$. Mention the expression for a quarter-wave line. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The input impedance of a lossless transmission line terminated in a load $Z_L$ is: $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$$ For a **quarter-wave line** ($l = \lambda/4$), since $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$: $$Z_{in} = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$$ This property is called **impedance inversion** and is used in matching networks. *(Award 2 marks for general expression, 1 mark for quarter-wave special case.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A 50 Ω lossless line is short-circuited at the load. Find the input impedance when the line length is $\lambda/8$. **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a short-circuited line: $Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ Given $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ and $l = \lambda/8$: $$\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{8} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$ $$Z_{in} = j(50)\tan(\pi/4) = j(50)(1) = j50\,\Omega$$ The input impedance is **purely inductive** with value $j50\,\Omega$. *(3 marks: 1 formula, 1 substitution, 1 final answer with interpretation.)* --- ### 📗 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### ✦ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the input impedance of a lossless transmission line terminated in a load $Z_L$, starting from the travelling wave equations. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 50 Ω lossless line is terminated in a load $Z_L = 100 + j75\,\Omega$. The line is 0.4 λ long. Determine: (i) the reflection coefficient at the load, (ii) the input impedance, and (iii) the VSWR on the line. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part A(a):** **Assumptions:** Lossless line ($R = G = 0$, $\alpha = 0$), time-harmonic excitation ($e^{j\omega t}$), uniform line ($Z_0$ and $\beta$ constant). **Step 1 — Travelling wave voltage and current:** $$V(z) = V^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V^- e^{+j\beta z}$$ $$I(z) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$ **Step 2 — Reflection coefficient at the load** (at $z = 0$): $$\Gamma_L = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ **Step 3 — Impedance at distance $d$ back from the load** ($z = -d$): $$Z(-d) = \frac{V(-d)}{I(-d)} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{V^+ e^{+j\beta d} + V^- e^{-j\beta d}}{V^+ e^{+j\beta d} - V^- e^{-j\beta d}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $V^- = \Gamma_L V^+$ and divide numerator and denominator by $V^+ e^{+j\beta d}$:** $$Z(-d) = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1 + \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta d}}{1 - \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta d}}$$ **Step 5 — Using Euler's identity and the double-angle tangent identity** $\tan(\theta) = \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{1+\cos(2\theta)}$ and substituting $\Gamma_L = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$, after rationalisation: $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta d)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta d)}$$ Replacing $d$ by $l$ gives the standard form. $\blacksquare$ *(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 2 for wave equations, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 1 for substitution of $\Gamma_L$, 1 for final boxed expression.)* --- **Model Solution — Part A(b):** **Given:** $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$, $Z_L = 100 + j75\,\Omega$, $l = 0.4\lambda$. **(i) Reflection coefficient at the load:** $$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(100 + j75) - 50}{(100 + j75) + 50} = \frac{50 + j75}{150 + j75}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator $(150 - j75)$: $$\Gamma_L = \frac{(50 + j75)(150 - j75)}{(150)^2 + (75)^2} = \frac{7500 - j3750 + j11250 + 5625}{22500 + 5625} = \frac{13125 + j7500}{28125}$$ $$\Gamma_L = 0.4667 + j0.2667$$ Magnitude: $\vert \Gamma_L \vert = \sqrt{0.4667^2 + 0.2667^2} = \sqrt{0.2178 + 0.0711} = \sqrt{0.2889} = 0.5375$ Phase: $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.2667/0.4667) = \tan^{-1}(0.5715) = 29.74°$ $$\Gamma_L = 0.5375 \angle 29.74°$$ **(ii) Input impedance:** $\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}(0.4\lambda) = 0.8\pi = 144°$ (in radians: $2.513$) $\tan(\beta l) = \tan(144°) = \tan(180° - 36°) = -\tan(36°) = -0.7265$ $$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{(100 + j75) + j50(-0.7265)}{50 + j(100 + j75)(-0.7265)}$$ Numerator: $100 + j75 - j36.325 = 100 + j38.675$ Denominator: $50 + j(100 + j75)(-0.7265) = 50 + (-j72.65 - j^2 54.49) = 50 + 54.49 - j72.65 = 104.49 - j72.65$ $$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{100 + j38.675}{104.49 - j72.65}$$ Multiply by conjugate $(104.49 + j72.65)$: Numerator (×50): $50 \times [(100)(104.49) + (100)(j72.65) + (j38.675)(104.49) + (j38.675)(j72.65)]$ $= 50 \times [10449 + j7265 + j4041.6 - 2810.4]$ $= 50 \times [7638.6 + j11306.6]$ Denominator: $(104.49)^2 + (72.65)^2 = 10918.2 + 5278.1 = 16196.3$ $$Z_{in} = \frac{381930 + j565330}{16196.3} = 23.58 + j34.91\,\Omega$$ **(iii) VSWR:** $$VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 0.5375}{1 - 0.5375} = \frac{1.5375}{0.4625} = 3.32$$ *(7 marks: 1 for $\Gamma_L$ magnitude, 1 for $\Gamma_L$ phase, 2 for $Z_{in}$ calculation, 1 for numerical result, 1 for VSWR formula, 1 for VSWR value.)* --- #### ✦ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the variation of input impedance with line length for a **short-circuited** and an **open-circuited** lossless transmission line. Tabulate the input impedance for line lengths of $\lambda/8$, $\lambda/4$, $3\lambda/8$, and $\lambda/2$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 75 Ω lossless transmission line is terminated in an open circuit. Find the smallest line length $l$ (in terms of $\lambda$) for which the input impedance equals $-j75\,\Omega$. Verify using the Smith chart transformation principle. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part B(a):** **For a short-circuited line** ($Z_L = 0$): $$Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$$ | Line Length $l$ | $\beta l$ | $\tan(\beta l)$ | $Z_{in}^{SC}$ | Nature | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\lambda/8$ | $\pi/4 = 45°$ | $+1$ | $+jZ_0$ | Inductor | | $\lambda/4$ | $\pi/2 = 90°$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ | Open circuit | | $3\lambda/8$ | $3\pi/4 = 135°$ | $-1$ | $-jZ_0$ | Capacitor | | $\lambda/2$ | $\pi = 180°$ | $0$ | $0$ | Short circuit | **For an open-circuited line** ($Z_L \to \infty$): $$Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$$ | Line Length $l$ | $\beta l$ | $\cot(\beta l)$ | $Z_{in}^{OC}$ | Nature | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\lambda/8$ | $\pi/4 = 45°$ | $+1$ | $-jZ_0$ | Capacitor | | $\lambda/4$ | $\pi/2 = 90°$ | $0$ | $0$ | Short circuit | | $3\lambda/8$ | $3\pi/4 = 135°$ | $-1$ | $+jZ_0$ | Inductor | | $\lambda/2$ | $\pi = 180°$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ | Open circuit | **Observations:** - Short-circuited and open-circuited lines exhibit **complementary behaviour** — they are $\lambda/4$ phase-shifted versions of each other. - The period of repetition is **$\lambda/2$** for both. - Both are **purely reactive** — no real part exists (lossless line). *(7 marks: 2 for SC derivation/table, 2 for OC derivation/table, 2 for behaviour, 1 for the complementary observation.)* --- **Model Solution — Part B(b):** **Given:** $Z_0 = 75\,\Omega$, $Z_L = \infty$ (open circuit), $Z_{in} = -j75\,\Omega$. For an open-circuited line: $$Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$$ Set $Z_{in}^{OC} = -j75$ and $Z_0 = 75$: $$-j75 = -j75 \cot(\beta l)$$ $$\cot(\beta l) = 1$$ $$\beta l = \frac{\pi}{4} + n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ **Smallest positive solution:** $n = 0$: $$\beta l = \frac{\pi}{4}$$ $$l = \frac{\pi/4}{\beta} = \frac{\pi/4}{2\pi/\lambda} = \frac{\lambda}{8}$$ **Smith Chart Verification:** On the Smith chart, an open circuit corresponds to the rightmost point ($\Gamma = +1$). Moving toward the generator (clockwise), the first encounter with the **negative imaginary axis** (purely capacitive reactance) occurs after a rotation of $\beta l = \pi/4$, which corresponds to a physical length of $\lambda/8$. At this point, the normalized reactance reads $-j1$, i.e., $Z_{in} = -j75\,\Omega$. ✓ **Answer:** $\boxed{l = \lambda/8}$ *(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for setting up equation, 1 for solving trigonometric equation, 2 for converting to length, 1 for Smith chart verification.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Sign of $\tan(\beta l)$ in the wrong quadrant** — For $l$ between $\lambda/4$ and $\lambda/2$, $\tan(\beta l)$ is **negative**. Many students blindly write positive values and get the wrong reactance sign. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $Z_0$** — $Z_{in}$ is **not** the ratio $Z_L/Z_0$; it is the actual impedance in ohms. Always remember the leading $Z_0$ factor. > 3. **Confusing $l$ from load vs. from generator** — KTU problems always measure from the **load**. Misinterpretation leads to $l \to -l$ or wrong phase exponentials. > 4. **Using degrees vs. radians inconsistently** — Most calculator mistakes occur here. KTU expects degrees in $\tan$ if $\beta l$ is in degrees, but be consistent. > 5. **Not reducing $\Gamma_L$ to polar form** — KTU gives full credit for $\Gamma_L = |\Gamma_L| \angle \theta$. Don't leave it in rectangular form if VSWR is asked. > 6. **Mixing up SC and OC behaviour** — Memorise: SC at $l=0$, OC at $l=\lambda/4$, SC at $l=\lambda/2$. They alternate every $\lambda/4$. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Input impedance** is the **V/I ratio at the sending end** of a finite line terminated in $Z_L$. - [x] General lossless line formula: $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$ - [x] **Short-circuited** line: $Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ — purely reactive. - [x] **Open-circuited** line: $Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$ — purely reactive. - [x] **Quarter-wave transformer** ($l = \lambda/4$): $Z_{in} = Z_0^2 / Z_L$ — **impedance inversion** property. - [x] **Half-wave line** ($l = \lambda/2$): $Z_{in} = Z_L$ — impedance **repeater** (independent of $Z_0$). - [x] $Z_{in}$ is **periodic with period $\lambda/2$** — it repeats every half-wavelength. - [x] $Z_{in}$ varies between **inductive and capacitive** as we move along the line (for SC/OC cases). - [x] For a **matched line** ($Z_L = Z_0$), $Z_{in} = Z_0$ for **any** $l$ — no standing waves, no variation. - [x] Reflection coefficient relation: $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{1 + \Gamma_{in}}{1 - \Gamma_{in}}$ where $\Gamma_{in} = \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta l}$. - [x] VSWR $= \dfrac{1 + |\Gamma|}{1 - |\Gamma|}$ is **constant along the line** (lossless case) — only phase of $\Gamma$ rotates. - [x] **Engineering applications:** antenna matching, quarter-wave transformers, stub tuners, radar feeds. - [x] **Phase constant** $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$; remember to convert physical length $l$ to electrical length $\beta l$ before evaluating trigonometric functions. - [x] **Direction convention:** $l$ is measured **from the load toward the generator**.

topics.title: Standing Wave Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standing Wave Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Standing Wave Ratio (SWR)**, more precisely known as the **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**, is a dimensionless engineering metric used in transmission line theory and RF/microwave engineering to quantify the degree of mismatch between a transmission line and its terminating load impedance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** Standing Wave Ratio is the principal measurable quantity that characterizes impedance mismatch on a lossless/lossy transmission line. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the maximum voltage amplitude to the minimum voltage amplitude of the standing wave pattern formed along the line due to the superposition of incident and reflected waves.

Mathematically, the VSWR (denoted by the symbol $S$ or sometimes $\rho$) is expressed as:

$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}}$$

For a perfectly matched line (no reflection), $S = 1$. For a completely mismatched line (e.g., open or short circuit), $S \to \infty$.

## 1.2 Physical Interpretation — The Standing Wave Phenomenon

When a guided electromagnetic wave propagates down a transmission line and encounters a load impedance $Z_L$ that is **not equal** to the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of the line, a portion of the wave is **reflected** back toward the source. The forward (incident) traveling wave and the backward (reflected) traveling wave superpose to form a **standing wave** — a stationary oscillating pattern in which certain points along the line exhibit maximum voltage (antinodes) and other points exhibit minimum voltage (nodes).

> [!NOTE]
> The reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ at the load is defined as:
> $$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$
> The magnitude of $\Gamma$ ranges from **0** (perfect match) to **1** (total reflection), and it is the fundamental quantity that controls the magnitude of the SWR.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug-of-Wave" Picture

Imagine two people holding opposite ends of a long, slightly elastic rope. Person A shakes their end rhythmically, sending a **traveling wave** down the rope toward Person B. If Person B shakes their hand **in sync** with the incoming wave, no reflection occurs and the wave appears to flow smoothly — this is the **matched condition** (SWR = 1).

But if Person B simply **holds the rope rigidly still** (short circuit) or **leaves the rope completely free** (open circuit), the wave bounces back with full intensity. The incident and reflected waves now superpose. At certain points along the rope, the up-shake of A meets the down-shake of the reflection, **canceling** each other — these are the **nodes** (voltage minima). Half a wavelength away, both shakes add up — these are the **antinodes** (voltage maxima).

The **Standing Wave Ratio** is simply the **ratio of the height of the biggest bump to the depth of the deepest trough** along that rope. The bigger the mismatch, the taller the bumps relative to the troughs.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** In real systems (antenna feeds, RF amplifiers, microwave circuits), a high SWR means: (i) power is being reflected back to the transmitter (wasted energy), (ii) high voltages can damage components, and (iii) signal distortion may occur. Therefore, SWR is the **#1 diagnostic parameter** evaluated when commissioning any RF system.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Range |
|:------:|:--------|:------|
| $S$ or $\rho$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | $1 \le S \le \infty$ |
| $\Gamma$ | Reflection coefficient (complex) | $0 \le \vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ |
| $\phi$ | Phase angle of $\Gamma$ | $-\pi \le \phi \le \pi$ |
| $Z_0$ | Characteristic impedance | typically **50 Ω** or **75 Ω** |
| $Z_L$ | Load impedance | complex, in ohms |
| $V_{\max}$ | Peak voltage of standing wave | volts |
| $V_{\min}$ | Minimum voltage of standing wave | volts |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation of the Standing Wave — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us consider a lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ terminated in a load $Z_L$. The total voltage on the line at any point $z$ (measured from the load toward the generator) is the sum of the incident and reflected voltage waves.

**Step 1 — Incident wave at the load:**
A forward-traveling voltage wave $V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z}$ arrives at the load.

**Step 2 — Reflection at the load:**
A fraction is reflected: $\Gamma V_0^{+} e^{+j\beta z}$ travels back toward the source.

**Step 3 — Superposition:**
$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + \Gamma V_0^{+} e^{+j\beta z}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Gamma = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j\phi}$:**
$$V(z) = V_0^{+} \left[ e^{-j\beta z} + \vert \Gamma \vert e^{+j(\beta z + \phi)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude of total voltage:**
$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2 \vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta z + \phi)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $\cos(2\beta z + \phi)$ oscillates between $-1$ and $+1$ as $z$ varies. This is what creates the alternating maxima and minima along the line.

## 2.2 Conditions for Voltage Maxima and Minima

- **Maximum** occurs when $\cos(2\beta z + \phi) = +1$, i.e., $2\beta z + \phi = 2n\pi$
$$\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \, (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

- **Minimum** occurs when $\cos(2\beta z + \phi) = -1$, i.e., $2\beta z + \phi = (2n+1)\pi$
$$\vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \, (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

## 2.3 The Master Definition of VSWR

By definition:
$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

This can be inverted to obtain the reflection coefficient magnitude from a measured SWR:
$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Return Loss (in dB)** is the logarithmic twin of SWR:
> $$RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert \;\; \text{dB}$$
> A return loss of **∞ dB** corresponds to a perfect match ($S=1$), and **0 dB** corresponds to total reflection ($S \to \infty$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas in this table are **frequently tested** in KTU university examinations on Module 4 (Transmission Lines).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at the load | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | VSWR in terms of $\vert \Gamma \vert$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{S - 1}{S + 1}$ | Inverse relation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | Maximum line voltage | volts |
| 5 | $\vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | Minimum line voltage | volts |
| 6 | $z_{\min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) + \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}$ | Position of voltage minima from load | meters |
| 7 | $z_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\phi + \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}$ | Position of voltage maxima from load | meters |
| 8 | $Z(z) = Z_0 \dfrac{1 + \Gamma e^{j2\beta z}}{1 - \Gamma e^{j2\beta z}}$ | Input impedance at any point $z$ | ohms |
| 9 | $Z_{\text{at min}} = \dfrac{Z_0}{S}$ | Impedance at voltage minimum | ohms |
| 10 | $Z_{\text{at max}} = Z_0 \cdot S$ | Impedance at voltage maximum | ohms |
| 11 | $RL = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | Return loss | dB |
| 12 | $P_{\text{reflected}}/P_{\text{incident}} = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Power reflection coefficient | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Engineering**: VSWR is the primary figure of merit for an antenna match. A VSWR below **2:1** is the standard acceptance threshold in most communication systems.
- **Microwave/RF Design**: Used to characterize waveguide and microstrip discontinuities.
- **Transmitter Protection**: High VSWR can cause arcing in high-power tubes (klystrons, magnetrons).
- **Medical Imaging (MRI)**: Coil tuning uses VSWR minimization at the Larmor frequency.
- **Automatic Matching Networks**: Adaptive tuners use real-time VSWR feedback to maintain matching under varying load conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: VSWR from the Voltage Standing Wave Equation

We begin with the total voltage on a lossless line terminated by an arbitrary load:

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + \Gamma V_0^{+} e^{+j\beta z}$$

where $z$ is measured **from the load** toward the source (this is the standard KTU convention for Module 4 problems).

**Step 1 — Express the reflection coefficient in polar form.**
$$\Gamma = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j\phi}$$

Substituting into the voltage equation:
$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + \vert \Gamma \vert V_0^{+} e^{+j(\beta z + \phi)}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $V_0^{+} e^{+j\phi/2} e^{-j\beta z}$** to set up the cosine term.

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{+j\phi/2} e^{-j\beta z} \left[ e^{-j\phi/2} + \vert \Gamma \vert e^{+j(\beta z + \phi/2)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the bracket as a sum of two complex exponentials** whose magnitude gives:

$$\left\vert V(z) \right\vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{ \left(1 + \vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta z + \phi) \right)^2 + \left(\vert \Gamma \vert \sin(2\beta z + \phi)\right)^2 }$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify the radicand:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(1 + \vert \Gamma \vert \cos\theta\right)^2 + \left(\vert \Gamma \vert \sin\theta\right)^2 & = 1 + 2\vert \Gamma \vert \cos\theta + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 \cos^2\theta + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 \sin^2\theta \\
& = 1 + 2\vert \Gamma \vert \cos\theta + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 \left(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta\right) \\
& = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2 \vert \Gamma \vert \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

where $\theta = 2\beta z + \phi$.

**Step 5 — Therefore, the magnitude of voltage on the line is:**

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2 \vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta z + \phi)}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the maximum and minimum.**

The cosine term attains its maximum of $+1$ when $2\beta z + \phi = 2n\pi$:
$$\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2 \vert \Gamma \vert} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{(1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)^2} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

The cosine term attains its minimum of $-1$ when $2\beta z + \phi = (2n+1)\pi$:
$$\vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \sqrt{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 - 2 \vert \Gamma \vert} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

**Step 7 — Form the ratio (VSWR).**

$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}} = \frac{\vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)}{\vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

$$\boxed{\; S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Position of Voltage Minima and Maxima

**Voltage minima** occur at $2\beta z_{\min} + \phi = (2n+1)\pi$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:

$$z_{\min} = \frac{(2n+1)\pi - \phi}{2\beta}$$

Substituting $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned}
z_{\min} & = \frac{(2n+1)\pi - \phi}{2 \cdot 2\pi/\lambda} \\
& = \frac{\lambda(2n+1)\pi - \lambda\phi}{4\pi} \\
& = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) + \frac{n\lambda}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\; z_{\min} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) + \frac{n\lambda}{2} \;}$$

**Voltage maxima** occur at $2\beta z_{\max} + \phi = 2n\pi$:

$$z_{\max} = \frac{2n\pi - \phi}{2\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\phi + \frac{n\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Distance between successive minima (or maxima):**

$$\Delta z = z_{n+1} - z_{n} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

This is the famous KTU result: **adjacent voltage minima are separated by exactly $\lambda/2$**, which is the experimental basis for measuring wavelength using a slotted line.

## 3.3 Derivation: Impedance at Voltage Maxima and Minima

At any point on the line, the input impedance is:

$$Z(z) = Z_0 \frac{1 + \Gamma_{\text{local}}}{1 - \Gamma_{\text{local}}}, \quad \Gamma_{\text{local}} = \Gamma e^{j2\beta z}$$

**At a voltage maximum:** The local reflection coefficient has magnitude $\vert \Gamma \vert$ and the voltage amplitude is maximum. Using the relation between the current and voltage at such points, the impedance is purely **real and maximum**:

$$Z_{\max} = Z_0 \cdot S$$

**At a voltage minimum:** The impedance is purely real and minimum:

$$Z_{\min} = \frac{Z_0}{S}$$

This is easily verified because $\sqrt{Z_{\max} \cdot Z_{\min}} = \sqrt{Z_0^2} = Z_0$, consistent with the standard transmission line impedance transformation.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 50 Ω transmission line is terminated with a load impedance $Z_L = 75 + j25$ Ω operating at 500 MHz. Calculate (a) the reflection coefficient, (b) the VSWR, (c) the return loss in dB, and (d) the position of the first voltage minimum from the load.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reflection coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma & = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} \\
& = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} \\
& = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma & = \frac{(25 + j25)(125 - j25)}{(125 + j25)(125 - j25)} \\
& = \frac{(25 \cdot 125 + 25 \cdot 25) + j(25 \cdot 125 - 25 \cdot 25)}{125^2 + 25^2} \\
& = \frac{(3125 + 625) + j(3125 - 625)}{15625 + 625} \\
& = \frac{3750 + j2500}{16250} \\
& = 0.2308 + j0.1538
\end{aligned}$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{0.2308^2 + 0.1538^2} = \sqrt{0.0533 + 0.0237} = \sqrt{0.0770} = 0.2775$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.1538}{0.2308}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) = 33.69° = 0.5880 \text{ rad}$$

**(b) VSWR:**

$$S = \frac{1 + 0.2775}{1 - 0.2775} = \frac{1.2775}{0.7225} = 1.768 \approx 1.77$$

**(c) Return loss:**

$$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.2775) = -20 \times (-0.5564) = 11.13 \text{ dB}$$

**(d) Position of first voltage minimum (n = 0):**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_{\min} & = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(0.5880 + \pi) \\
& = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(3.7296) \\
& = 0.2968 \, \lambda
\end{aligned}$$

With $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(500 \times 10^6) = 0.6$ m:

$$z_{\min} = 0.2968 \times 0.6 = 0.1781 \text{ m} = 17.81 \text{ cm}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Standing Wave Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: V0 e to the power minus j beta z] --> B[Incident Wave: Forward Traveling]
    B --> C[Load: ZL not equal to Z0]
    C --> D[Mismatch: Partial Reflection]
    D --> E[Reflected Wave: Backward Traveling]
    E --> F[Superposition on Transmission Line]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Standing Wave Pattern]
    G --> H[Voltage Maxima: Antinodes]
    G --> I[Voltage Minima: Nodes]
    G --> J[VSWR Computation: Vmax over Vmin]
    I --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Engineering Decision: Acceptable or Re-tune]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Mermaid Flow — Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Slotted Line Setup] --> A1[Connect Source and Load]
    A1 --> A2[Insert Slotted Probe]
    A2 --> A3[Slide Probe Along Line]
    A3 --> A4[Record Voltage at Each Position]
    A4 --> A5{Identify Maxima}
    A4 --> A6{Identify Minima}
    A5 --> A7[Read Vmax from Meter]
    A6 --> A8[Read Vmin from Meter]
    A7 --> A9[Compute VSWR = Vmax / Vmin]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Compute Reflection Magnitude]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Return Loss in dB]
    A11 --> A12[Determine Load Impedance]
    A12 --> A13[Report to Engineering Log]
```

## 4.3 Standing Wave Pattern Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
         |V| along transmission line (voltage envelope)
         
   Vmax  |    /\        /\        /\        /\
         |   /  \      /  \      /  \      /  \
         |  /    \    /    \    /    \    /    \
         | /      \  /      \  /      \  /      \
   Vmin  |/        \/        \/        \/        \
         +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+---> z
        Load                            Source
              λ/2     λ/2     λ/2     λ/2
              
   <--  Distance between successive minima = λ/2  -->
   
   Position of first Vmin from load:
   z_min = (λ/4π)(φ + π)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a stylized envelope of $\vert V(z) \vert$. The **distance between two successive voltage minima** is always $\lambda/2$, regardless of the value of $\vert \Gamma \vert$. This is the principle used in the **slotted-line measurement method** for experimentally determining wavelength and load impedance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR). For what value of load impedance is the VSWR unity?**

**Model Answer:**
The Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR) is defined as the ratio of the maximum voltage to the minimum voltage on a transmission line under standing wave conditions:

$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}}$$

It quantifies the degree of impedance mismatch between the load and the transmission line. The VSWR is **unity** ($S = 1$) when the load impedance is **exactly equal to the characteristic impedance** of the line ($Z_L = Z_0$), because under this condition $\Gamma = 0$ and no reflected wave exists, making $V_{\max} = V_{\min}$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between standing wave ratio and reflection coefficient. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Reflection Coefficient ($\Gamma$) | Standing Wave Ratio (S) |
|:----------|:---------------------------------|:------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of reflected to incident voltage | Ratio of $V_{\max}$ to $V_{\min}$ |
| Range | $0 \le \vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ | $1 \le S \le \infty$ |
| Nature | Complex quantity | Real, positive quantity |
| Physical meaning | Direct measure of mismatch | Indirect measure via voltage envelope |

They are related by:

$$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR) on a transmission line in terms of the reflection coefficient. Explain the physical significance of VSWR = 1 and VSWR = ∞.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A 75 Ω lossless transmission line is terminated by a load $Z_L = 30 + j60$ Ω. Find: (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the VSWR, (iii) the return loss in dB, and (iv) the position of the first voltage minimum from the load at 200 MHz.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.1 above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final boxed result is:]

$$\boxed{\; S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \;}$$

**Physical Significance:**

- **VSWR = 1:** Implies $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0$, meaning there is **no reflection**. All incident power is delivered to the load. This is the **perfectly matched condition**, occurring when $Z_L = Z_0$. The voltage along the line is constant.

- **VSWR = ∞:** Implies $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$, meaning **total reflection** of the incident wave. No power is absorbed by the load. This occurs for **open-circuit** ($Z_L = \infty$) or **short-circuit** ($Z_L = 0$) terminations.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Reflection coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma & = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(30 + j60) - 75}{(30 + j60) + 75} = \frac{-45 + j60}{105 + j60}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate $(105 - j60)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} & = (-45 + j60)(105 - j60) \\
& = (-45)(105) + (-45)(-j60) + (j60)(105) + (j60)(-j60) \\
& = -4725 + j2700 + j6300 + 3600 \\
& = -1125 + j9000
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully:

$$(-45)(105) = -4725$$
$$(-45)(-j60) = j2700$$
$$(j60)(105) = j6300$$
$$(j60)(-j60) = -j^2 \cdot 3600 = +3600$$

Sum: $(-4725 + 3600) + j(2700 + 6300) = -1125 + j9000$

$$\text{Denominator} = 105^2 + 60^2 = 11025 + 3600 = 14625$$

$$\Gamma = \frac{-1125 + j9000}{14625} = -0.0769 + j0.6154$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{0.0769^2 + 0.6154^2} = \sqrt{0.00592 + 0.3787} = \sqrt{0.3846} = 0.6202$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.6154}{-0.0769}\right) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(8.00) = 180° - 82.87° = 97.13° = 1.6955 \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) VSWR:**

$$S = \frac{1 + 0.6202}{1 - 0.6202} = \frac{1.6202}{0.3798} = 4.266 \approx 4.27$$

**(iii) Return loss:**

$$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.6202) = -20 \times (-0.2077) = 4.15 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Position of first voltage minimum:**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(200 \times 10^6) = 1.5$ m.

$$z_{\min} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) = \frac{1.5}{4\pi}(1.6955 + 3.1416) = \frac{1.5}{4\pi}(4.8371) = 0.5774 \text{ m}$$

$$z_{\min} \approx 57.74 \text{ cm from the load}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Starting from the voltage expression on a lossless transmission line, derive the expressions for: (i) maximum and minimum voltage on the line, and (ii) the positions of voltage maxima and minima measured from the load.

(b) **[7 Marks]** For a transmission line operating at 1 GHz with characteristic impedance 50 Ω, the measured VSWR is 2.5 and the first voltage minimum is located at 4 cm from the load. Determine: (i) the magnitude and phase of the reflection coefficient, (ii) the load impedance, and (iii) the input impedance at a distance of 25 cm from the load.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** [See Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 above for complete derivations. The key results are:]

$$\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert), \quad \vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

$$z_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\phi + \frac{n\lambda}{2}, \quad z_{\min} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) + \frac{n\lambda}{2}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $f = 1$ GHz, $Z_0 = 50$ Ω, $S = 2.5$, first $V_{\min}$ at 4 cm from load.

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 0.3$ m = 30 cm.

**(i) Magnitude and phase of $\Gamma$:**

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1} = \frac{2.5 - 1}{2.5 + 1} = \frac{1.5}{3.5} = 0.4286$$

For the first voltage minimum ($n = 0$):

$$z_{\min} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}(\phi + \pi) = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

Solving for $\phi$:

$$\phi + \pi = \frac{4\pi \cdot 0.04}{0.30} = \frac{0.16\pi}{0.30} = 0.5333\pi$$

$$\phi = 0.5333\pi - \pi = -0.4667\pi = -1.4661 \text{ rad} = -84.0°$$

$$\Gamma = 0.4286 \, e^{-j84.0°}$$

**(ii) Load impedance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_L & = Z_0 \frac{1 + \Gamma}{1 - \Gamma} \\
& = 50 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.4286(\cos(-84°) + j\sin(-84°))}{1 - 0.4286(\cos(-84°) + j\sin(-84°))} \\
& = 50 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.4286(0.1045 - j0.9945)}{1 - 0.4286(0.1045 - j0.9945)} \\
& = 50 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.0448 - j0.4263}{1 - 0.0448 + j0.4263} \\
& = 50 \cdot \frac{1.0448 - j0.4263}{0.9552 + j0.4263}
\end{aligned}$$

Magnitude of numerator: $\sqrt{1.0448^2 + 0.4263^2} = \sqrt{1.0916 + 0.1817} = \sqrt{1.2733} = 1.1284$
Angle of numerator: $\tan^{-1}(-0.4263/1.0448) = -22.21°$

Magnitude of denominator: $\sqrt{0.9552^2 + 0.4263^2} = \sqrt{0.9124 + 0.1817} = \sqrt{1.0941} = 1.0460$
Angle of denominator: $\tan^{-1}(0.4263/0.9552) = +24.04°$

$$Z_L = 50 \cdot \frac{1.1284 \angle -22.21°}{1.0460 \angle 24.04°} = 50 \cdot 1.0788 \angle -46.25°$$

$$Z_L = 53.94 \angle -46.25° \; \Omega = 37.32 - j38.94 \; \Omega$$

**(iii) Input impedance at $z = 25$ cm:**

Electrical length: $\beta z = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot z = (2\pi/0.30) \cdot 0.25 = 5\pi/3 = 300°$

$\Gamma_{\text{local}} = \Gamma e^{j2\beta z} = 0.4286 \, e^{-j84°} \cdot e^{j600°} = 0.4286 \, e^{j516°} = 0.4286 \, e^{j156°}$

Computing:
$$2\beta z = 2 \times 300° = 600° \equiv 600° - 360° = 240°$$

$$\Gamma_{\text{local}} = 0.4286 \, e^{j(240° - 84°)} = 0.4286 \, e^{j156°} = 0.4286(\cos 156° + j\sin 156°)$$
$$= 0.4286(-0.9135 + j0.4067) = -0.3915 + j0.1743$$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{1 + (-0.3915 + j0.1743)}{1 - (-0.3915 + j0.1743)} = 50 \cdot \frac{0.6085 + j0.1743}{1.3915 - j0.1743}$$

Magnitude of numerator: $\sqrt{0.6085^2 + 0.1743^2} = \sqrt{0.3703 + 0.0304} = \sqrt{0.4007} = 0.6330$
Angle of numerator: $\tan^{-1}(0.1743/0.6085) = 15.99°$

Magnitude of denominator: $\sqrt{1.3915^2 + 0.1743^2} = \sqrt{1.9363 + 0.0304} = \sqrt{1.9667} = 1.4024$
Angle of denominator: $\tan^{-1}(-0.1743/1.3915) = -7.14°$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{0.6330 \angle 15.99°}{1.4024 \angle -7.14°} = 50 \cdot 0.4514 \angle 23.13° = 22.57 \angle 23.13° \; \Omega$$

$$Z_{\text{in}} \approx 20.76 + j8.86 \; \Omega$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $z$:** KTU problems measure $z$ **from the load** toward the generator. Using the wrong sign convention (source-to-load) will invert your $z_{\min}$ formulas and cost **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the phase angle:** Many students compute only $\vert \Gamma \vert$ and stop. KTU questions on VSWR **always** ask for positions of minima/maxima, which require the **phase $\phi$** of $\Gamma$. A common error is assuming $\phi = 0$ (real, positive $\Gamma$), which is only true for a purely resistive load greater than $Z_0$.
> 3. **Unit conversions:** Always convert GHz → Hz, cm → m, and degrees → radians **before** substituting into formulas. Mixing units is a silent mark-killer.
> 4. **$n = 0$ trap:** The "first voltage minimum" corresponds to $n = 0$, not $n = 1$. Be careful with this.
