> 5. **The $\pi$ in the $z_{\min}$ formula:** Students often write $z_{\min} = (\lambda/4\pi)\phi + n\lambda/2$, but the correct formula includes a $\pi$ additive constant: $z_{\min} = (\lambda/4\pi)(\phi + \pi) + n\lambda/2$. The two are **NOT** the same. Memorize the correct version.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Standing Wave Ratio**

- ✅ **VSWR** is the ratio $V_{\max} / V_{\min}$ and ranges from **1** (matched) to **∞** (open/short circuit).
- ✅ **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$; always has $\vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ for passive loads.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $S = (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ — memorize both this and its inverse.
- ✅ **Return loss** in dB: $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$; a high return loss = good match.
- ✅ **Positions of minima/maxima** are measured **from the load** in KTU convention.
- ✅ **Voltage minima** are spaced $\lambda/2$ apart; **maxima** are also spaced $\lambda/2$ apart, but offset by $\lambda/4$ from the minima.
- ✅ **Impedance at a voltage minimum** = $Z_0 / S$ (real, minimum value).
- ✅ **Impedance at a voltage maximum** = $Z_0 \cdot S$ (real, maximum value).
- ✅ **Matched line ($Z_L = Z_0$):** $S = 1$, $\Gamma = 0$, no standing wave, pure traveling wave.
- ✅ **Open-circuited line ($Z_L = \infty$):** $S = \infty$, $\Gamma = +1$, voltage max at load.
- ✅ **Short-circuited line ($Z_L = 0$):** $S = \infty$, $\Gamma = -1$, voltage min at load.
- ✅ **Slotted line measurement** is the classical experimental method: read $V_{\max}$ and $V_{\min}$ directly to compute $S$.
- ✅ **Acceptable VSWR threshold** in most RF systems: $S \le 2$ (i.e., $\vert \Gamma \vert \le 0.333$, return loss $\ge 9.54$ dB).
- ✅ Always **retain the phase $\phi$** of $\Gamma$ when computing positions of minima/maxima — the magnitude alone is insufficient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Procedures for Solving Laplace's and Poisson's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET502 - ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Numerical Procedures for Solving Laplace's and Poisson's Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Laplace's Equation — Formal Definition In electrostatics, when a region of space is **free of charge** ($\rho_v = 0$), the electric scalar potential $V$ satisfies **Laplace's Equation**: $$\nabla^2 V = 0$$ In three-dimensional Cartesian coordinates, this expands to: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = 0$$ For a two-dimensional problem in the $xy$-plane: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Laplace's equation governs **source-free, linear, isotropic, homogeneous** regions. The solution $V$ is a **harmonic function** and is unique once the potential (Dirichlet) or its normal derivative (Neumann) is specified on the boundary. ## 1.2 Poisson's Equation — Formal Definition When a **volume charge density** $\rho_v$ exists within a region, the potential satisfies **Poisson's Equation**: $$\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$ In two dimensions: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$ > [!NOTE] > The symbol $\varepsilon$ denotes the **permittivity** of the medium. In free space, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. ## 1.3 Why Numerical Methods? Analytic solutions to Laplace's/Poisson's equation are possible only for **simple geometries** (rectangular, cylindrical, or spherical boundaries) using separation of variables. Real engineering systems — like the cross-section of a transmission line, the field inside a vacuum-tube electrode assembly, or the potential in a semiconductor device — have **irregular boundaries** that defy closed-form analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** Over **90%** of practical boundary-value problems in electromagnetics, thermal conduction, and fluid flow cannot be solved analytically. Numerical methods bridge this gap. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap Film" Intuition Imagine stretching a **thin rubber sheet** over a rectangular wire frame. If you gently push up on a small region of the sheet (simulating a positive charge), the surface deforms into a smooth dome. - The **height** of the sheet at any point is the **electric potential** $V$. - The **dome shape** automatically satisfies Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 V = 0$ because soap films naturally minimize surface area (a variational principle). - If you attach a fixed ring of different heights around the rim, that represents the **boundary conditions**. - Adding a "weight" beneath the sheet at some point corresponds to a **charge density** → this converts the problem to Poisson's equation. This analogy, originally due to **Lord Kelvin (1847)**, beautifully shows why Laplace's equation produces smooth, averaging solutions: **every interior point "feels" the average of its neighbors**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Finite Difference Method (FDM) — Discretization Philosophy The core idea of the FDM is to **replace continuous derivatives with algebraic approximations** built from function values at neighbouring discrete points on a grid. **Operational Steps:** 1. **Overlay a grid** of evenly spaced nodes on the problem region. Let the grid spacing be $h$ in the $x$-direction and $k$ in the $y$-direction. 2. **Label** each node with index $(i, j)$ where $x_i = x_0 + i \cdot h$ and $y_j = y_0 + j \cdot k$. 3. **Approximate** the second partial derivatives using **central differences**. 4. **Replace** the PDE by a system of linear algebraic equations — one per interior node. 5. **Solve** the resulting system either directly (matrix inversion) or iteratively (Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, SOR). ## 2.2 The Five-Point Formula (Most Important KTU Result) For a uniform square grid with spacing $h$, the Laplacian at node $(i, j)$ is approximated as: $$\nabla^2 V \;\Big|_{i,j} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{i+1,j} + V_{i-1,j} + V_{i,j+1} + V_{i,j-1} - 4 V_{i,j}}{h^2}$$ This is the famous **five-point stencil** because it involves $V$ at five points: the center and its four cardinal neighbours. Setting the Laplacian to zero (Laplace) or to $-\rho_v/\varepsilon$ (Poisson) yields the **update rule**: **Laplace's Equation Update:** $$V_{i,j} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j} + V_{i-1,j} + V_{i,j+1} + V_{i,j-1}\right)$$ **Poisson's Equation Update (with $\rho$ constant per node):** $$V_{i,j} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j} + V_{i-1,j} + V_{i,j+1} + V_{i,j-1} + \frac{h^2 \rho_{i,j}}{\varepsilon}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** The potential at an interior point is the **arithmetic average of its four nearest neighbours** (plus a correction for Poisson's case). This is the discrete version of the **mean-value property** of harmonic functions. ## 2.3 Classification of Boundary Conditions | Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | KTU Term | |---|---|---|---| | **Dirichlet** | $V = f(\text{boundary})$ | Potential prescribed on surface | First-kind | | **Neumann** | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial n} = g(\text{boundary})$ | Normal derivative prescribed | Second-kind | | **Mixed / Robin** | $aV + b\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial n} = h$ | Combination of $V$ and its slope | Third-kind | ## 2.4 Iterative Solution Techniques ### A. Jacobi Iteration All updates use values from the **previous iteration** (superscript $n$). Computations are independent, so this method is **parallelizable**. $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n)}\right)$$ ### B. Gauss-Seidel Iteration Updated values are used **as soon as they are computed** — saving memory and accelerating convergence. $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n+1)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n+1)}\right)$$ ### C. Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR) A **relaxation factor** $\omega$ is introduced to **over-correct** each Gauss-Seidel step, dramatically speeding convergence when $\omega$ is chosen well. $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = V_{i,j}^{(n)} + \frac{\omega}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n+1)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n+1)} - 4 V_{i,j}^{(n)}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optimal $\omega$ Range:** For Laplace's equation on a rectangular grid, $\omega_{\text{opt}}$ lies in $(1, 2)$. A value of $\omega = 1$ recovers Gauss-Seidel. The optimum is approximately $\omega_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho(J)^2}}$, where $\rho(J)$ is the spectral radius of the Jacobi iteration matrix. ## 2.5 Convergence Criteria Iterate until the **maximum absolute change** between successive iterations is below a tolerance $\delta$: $$\max_{i,j}\left| V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} - V_{i,j}^{(n)} \right| \;<\; \delta$$ A typical engineering tolerance is $\delta = 10^{-5}$ V. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** | |---|---|---| | Laplace's Equation (2-D) | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} = 0$ | Source-free region | | Poisson's Equation (2-D) | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} = -\dfrac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon}$ | $\rho_v$ in C/m³, $\varepsilon$ in F/m | | Finite-Diff. 2nd Derivative | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} \approx \dfrac{V_{i+1,j} - 2V_{i,j} + V_{i-1,j}}{h^2}$ | Truncation error $O(h^2)$ | | Five-Point Stencil | $\nabla^2 V \approx \dfrac{V_N + V_S + V_E + V_W - 4 V_0}{h^2}$ | Compass notation | | Laplace Update Rule | $V_0 = \dfrac{1}{4}(V_N + V_S + V_E + V_W)$ | Average of neighbours | | Poisson Update Rule | $V_0 = \dfrac{1}{4}\left(V_N + V_S + V_E + V_W + \dfrac{h^2 \rho}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Adds source term | | Jacobi Update | $V^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\sum V_{\text{neighbour}}^{(n)}$ | Old values only | | Gauss-Seidel Update | $V^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\sum V_{\text{updated neighbour}}^{(n+1)} + \text{rest}$ | Mixed old/new | | SOR Update | $V^{(n+1)} = V^{(n)} + \dfrac{\omega}{4}(V_{GS} - V^{(n)})$ | $1 < \omega < 2$ | | Truncation Error Order | $O(h^2)$ per step, $O(h^2)$ globally | Halve $h$ → error quarters | | Convergence Check | $\max \vert \Delta V \vert < \delta$ | Typically $\delta = 10^{-5}$ | | Boundary Types | Dirichlet / Neumann / Mixed | See table above | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transmission Line Design:** Computing capacitance per unit length of a two-wire line with non-circular cross-section. - **Semiconductor Device Physics:** Potential distribution inside a p-n junction or MOSFET channel. - **High-Voltage Engineering:** Electric field intensification near conductor edges and corners. - **Antenna Analysis:** Charge distribution on arbitrarily shaped radiator surfaces. - **Cross-Disciplinary Use:** The same numerical machinery solves **heat conduction** ($\nabla^2 T = 0$), **irrotational fluid flow** ($\nabla^2 \psi = 0$), and **torsion of shafts** in mechanical engineering. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Central-Difference Approximation for the Second Derivative We start with the **Taylor series expansion** of $V$ about the node $(x_i, y_j)$. We expand along the $x$-direction first. **Step 1: Forward expansion** (move to the right neighbour at $x_{i+1} = x_i + h$): $$V(x_i + h, y_j) = V(x_i, y_j) + h \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^2}{2!}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^3}{3!}\frac{\partial^3 V}{\partial x^3}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{4!}\frac{\partial^4 V}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^5)$$ **Step 2: Backward expansion** (move to the left neighbour at $x_{i-1} = x_i - h$): $$V(x_i - h, y_j) = V(x_i, y_j) - h \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^2}{2!}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} - \frac{h^3}{3!}\frac{\partial^3 V}{\partial x^3}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{4!}\frac{\partial^4 V}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} - O(h^5)$$ **Step 3: Add the two expansions.** The odd-derivative terms cancel in pairs, leaving only the even-derivative terms: $$V(x_i + h, y_j) + V(x_i - h, y_j) = 2V(x_i, y_j) + h^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{h^4}{12}\frac{\partial^4 V}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^6)$$ **Step 4: Solve for the second derivative:** $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} = \frac{V(x_i + h, y_j) - 2V(x_i, y_j) + V(x_i - h, y_j)}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12}\frac{\partial^4 V}{\partial x^4}\bigg|_{i,j} + O(h^4)$$ **Step 5: Neglect the higher-order remainder** (valid for smooth $V$ and small $h$): $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{i+1,j} - 2V_{i,j} + V_{i-1,j}}{h^2}}$$ The **truncation error** of this approximation is $O(h^2)$ — meaning the error is proportional to $h^2$, so halving $h$ reduces the error by a factor of 4. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Five-Point Laplacian Formula **Step 1:** Apply the central-difference formula independently to the $x$ and $y$ directions. For the $x$-direction: $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{V_{i+1,j} - 2V_{i,j} + V_{i-1,j}}{h^2}$$ For the $y$-direction (analogous derivation with $k$ replaced by $h$ for a square grid, $k = h$): $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2}\bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{V_{i,j+1} - 2V_{i,j} + V_{i,j-1}}{h^2}$$ **Step 2: Add the two approximations:** $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2}\bigg|_{i,j} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2}\bigg|_{i,j} \approx \frac{V_{i+1,j} + V_{i-1,j} + V_{i,j+1} + V_{i,j-1} - 4V_{i,j}}{h^2}$$ **Step 3: This is the discrete Laplacian operator.** In compact compass notation: $$\nabla^2 V \;\Big|_{0} \;\approx\; \frac{V_E + V_W + V_N + V_S - 4 V_0}{h^2}$$ **Step 4: Setting the discrete Laplacian to zero (Laplace's equation):** $$V_E + V_W + V_N + V_S - 4 V_0 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_0 = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_E + V_W + V_N + V_S\right)$$ **Step 5: Setting the discrete Laplacian to $-\rho_v/\varepsilon$ (Poisson's equation):** $$V_E + V_W + V_N + V_S - 4 V_0 = -\frac{h^2 \rho_v}{\varepsilon}$$ $$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_E + V_W + V_N + V_S + \frac{h^2 \rho_v}{\varepsilon}\right)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the SOR Update Formula **Step 1: Start with the Gauss-Seidel update:** $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1),\, GS} = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n+1)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n+1)}\right)$$ **Step 2: Define the "residual"** — the difference between the Gauss-Seidel estimate and the current value: $$R = V_{i,j}^{(n+1),\, GS} - V_{i,j}^{(n)}$$ **Step 3: Scale the residual by the relaxation factor $\omega$** and add it to the current value to get the SOR update: $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = V_{i,j}^{(n)} + \omega \cdot R$$ **Step 4: Substitute and expand:** $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = V_{i,j}^{(n)} + \omega \left[ \frac{1}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n+1)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n+1)}\right) - V_{i,j}^{(n)} \right]$$ **Step 5: Rearrange to highlight each role of $\omega$:** $$V_{i,j}^{(n+1)} = (1 - \omega)\, V_{i,j}^{(n)} + \frac{\omega}{4}\left(V_{i+1,j}^{(n)} + V_{i-1,j}^{(n+1)} + V_{i,j+1}^{(n)} + V_{i,j-1}^{(n+1)}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Cases:** > - $\omega = 1$ → **Gauss-Seidel** > - $0 < \omega < 1$ → **Under-relaxation** (used when Gauss-Seidel oscillates or diverges) > - $1 < \omega < 2$ → **Over-relaxation** (used to accelerate convergence) > - $\omega \geq 2$ → **Divergence** ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Jacobi Iteration **Problem:** A square region $0 \leq x, y \leq 3$ (units in cm) has the boundary potentials: - $V = 0$ V on three sides (left, right, bottom) - $V = 100$ V on the top side Take $h = 1$ cm and iterate using the **Jacobi method** to find the potential at the four interior nodes: $(1,1), (2,1), (1,2), (2,2)$. **Boundary Setup (numbered nodes):** ``` Top: V = 100 V a(0,3)=100 b(1,3)=100 c(2,3)=100 d(3,3)=100 e(0,2)=0 f(3,2)=0 V1(1,1) V2(2,1) ← Bottom interior g(0,1)=0 h(3,1)=0 i(0,0)=0 j(1,0)=0 k(2,0)=0 l(3,0)=0 Bottom: V = 0 V ``` Renaming interior nodes for clarity: Let $A = V(1,2)$, $B = V(2,2)$, $C = V(1,1)$, $D = V(2,1)$. **Initial guess (Iteration 0):** $A^{(0)} = B^{(0)} = C^{(0)} = D^{(0)} = 0$ V. **Jacobi Update Rule (Laplace):** $$V_0^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left(V_N^{(n)} + V_S^{(n)} + V_E^{(n)} + V_W^{(n)}\right)$$ **Iteration 1:** $$A^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(V_N^0 + V_S^0 + V_E^0 + V_W^0) = \frac{1}{4}(b^{(0)} + C^{(0)} + B^{(0)} + e^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 25.00$$ $$B^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(c^{(0)} + D^{(0)} + f^{(0)} + A^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 25.00$$ $$C^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(A^{(0)} + j^{(0)} + D^{(0)} + g^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0$$ $$D^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}(B^{(0)} + k^{(0)} + h^{(0)} + C^{(0)}) = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0$$ **Iteration 2:** $$A^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 0 + 25 + 0) = \frac{125}{4} = 31.25$$ $$B^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 0 + 0 + 25) = \frac{125}{4} = 31.25$$ $$C^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(25 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 6.25$$ $$D^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4}(25 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 6.25$$ **Iteration 3:** $$A^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 6.25 + 31.25 + 0) = \frac{137.5}{4} = 34.375$$ $$B^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 6.25 + 0 + 31.25) = 34.375$$ $$C^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}(31.25 + 0 + 6.25 + 0) = 9.375$$ $$D^{(3)} = \frac{1}{4}(31.25 + 0 + 0 + 6.25) = 9.375$$ **Iteration 4:** $$A^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 9.375 + 34.375 + 0) = \frac{143.75}{4} = 35.9375$$ $$B^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 9.375 + 0 + 34.375) = 35.9375$$ $$C^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}(34.375 + 0 + 9.375 + 0) = 10.9375$$ $$D^{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}(34.375 + 0 + 0 + 9.375) = 10.9375$$ **Iteration 5:** $$A^{(5)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 10.9375 + 35.9375 + 0) = \frac{146.875}{4} = 36.7188$$ $$B^{(5)} = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 10.9375 + 0 + 35.9375) = 36.7188$$ $$C^{(5)} = \frac{1}{4}(35.9375 + 0 + 10.9375 + 0) = 11.7188$$ $$D^{(5)} = \frac{1}{4}(35.9375 + 0 + 0 + 10.9375) = 11.7188$$ **Iteration Summary Table:** | Iteration | $A$ (V) | $B$ (V) | $C$ (V) | $D$ (V) | $\max \vert \Delta V \vert$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | — | | 1 | 25.0000 | 25.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 25.0000 | | 2 | 31.2500 | 31.2500 | 6.2500 | 6.2500 | 6.2500 | | 3 | 34.3750 | 34.3750 | 9.3750 | 9.3750 | 3.1250 | | 4 | 35.9375 | 35.9375 | 10.9375 | 10.9375 | 1.5625 | | 5 | 36.7188 | 36.7188 | 11.7188 | 11.7188 | 0.7813 | | 6 (extrapolated) | 37.1094 | 37.1094 | 12.1094 | 12.1094 | 0.3906 | | 7 (extrapolated) | 37.3047 | 37.3047 | 12.3047 | 12.3047 | 0.1953 | | 8 (extrapolated) | 37.4023 | 37.4023 | 12.4023 | 12.4023 | 0.0977 | | 9 (extrapolated) | 37.4512 | 37.4512 | 12.4512 | 12.4512 | 0.0488 | | 10 (extrapolated) | 37.4756 | 37.4756 | 12.4756 | 12.4756 | 0.0244 | **Converged Solution (after many iterations):** $$A = B \approx 37.5 \text{ V}, \quad C = D \approx 12.5 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > The slow, monotonic decrease in $\max \vert \Delta V \vert$ by a factor of 2 each iteration is characteristic of the **Jacobi method** on a $4 \times 4$ Laplace problem. SOR with $\omega \approx 1.7$ would cut the iteration count by roughly a factor of 10. ## 3.5 Accuracy of the FDM — Truncation vs. Round-off Two competing error sources exist: $$\text{Total Error} = \underbrace{O(h^2)}_{\text{truncation}} + \underbrace{O(\varepsilon_m / h^2)}_{\text{round-off}}$$ - **Truncation error** decreases as $h \to 0$ (use a finer grid). - **Round-off error** grows as $h \to 0$ (more arithmetic on a finite-precision computer). - The **optimal grid spacing** balances the two — typically $h$ that gives a few hundred to a few thousand nodes. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five-Point Stencil — Compass Map of the Discrete Laplacian The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **5-point stencil** used at every interior node to compute or update the potential. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeN["V at node i, j plus 1
NORTH neighbour"] nodeC["V at node i, j
CENTER node - the point being updated"] nodeS["V at node i, j minus 1
SOUTH neighbour"] nodeE["V at node i plus 1, j
EAST neighbour"] nodeW["V at node i minus 1, j
WEST neighbour"] nodeN --> nodeC nodeS --> nodeC nodeE --> nodeC nodeW --> nodeC nodeC --> eqn["DISCRETE LAPLACIAN EQUALS
V_N plus V_S plus V_E plus V_W minus 4 times V_0
all divided by h squared"] nodeN --> eqn nodeS --> eqn nodeE --> eqn nodeW --> eqn eqn --> lapCase["If LAPLACE: equals 0
V_0 equals 1 over 4 times sum of four neighbours"] eqn --> poisCase["If POISSON: equals minus rho over epsilon
V_0 equals 1 over 4 times sum plus h squared rho over epsilon"] ``` ## 4.2 Iterative Solution Pipeline — Block-Level Architecture This diagram shows the full algorithmic flow for solving a boundary-value problem using FDM with an iterative method. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["STEP 1: Define geometry and PDE type

Jacobi OR Gauss-Seidel OR SOR"] startF["STEP 6: Convergence test

a times V plus b times partial V over partial n equals c
